We present a method combining affinity prediction with region agglomeration,
which improves significantly upon state of art of neuron segmentation
from electron microscopy (EM) in accuracy and scalability. Our method consists
of 3D U-NET, trained to predict affinities between voxels, followed by
iterative region agglomeration. We train using a structured loss based on
MALIS, encouraging topologically correct segmentations obtained from affinity
thresholding. Our extension consists of two parts: First, we present a
quasi-linear method to compute the loss gradient, improving over original
quadratic algorithm. Second, we compute gradient in two separate passes to
avoid spurious gradient contributions in early training stages. Our predictions
are accurate enough that simple learning-free percentile-based agglomeration
outperforms more involved methods used earlier on inferior predictions. We
present results on three diverse EM datasets, achieving relative improvements
over previous results of 27%, 15%, and 250%. Our findings suggest that a single
method can be applied to both nearly isotropic block-face EM data and
anisotropic serial sectioned EM data. The runtime of our method scales linearly
with the size of volume and achieves a throughput of about 2.6 seconds per
megavoxel, qualifying our method for the processing of very large datasets.

When evaluating computer vision systems, we are often concerned with
performance on a task-specific evaluation measure such as the
Intersection-Over-Union score used in the PASCAL VOC image segmentation
challenge. Ideally, our systems would be tuned specifically to these evaluation
measures. However, despite much work on loss-aware structured prediction, top
performing systems do not use these techniques. In this work, we seek to
address this problem, incorporating loss-aware prediction in a manner that is
amenable to the approaches taken by top performing systems. Our main idea is to
simultaneously leverage two systems: a highly tuned pipeline system as is found
on top of leaderboards, and a traditional CRF. We show how to combine high
quality candidate solutions from the pipeline with the probabilistic approach
of the CRF that is amenable to loss-aware prediction. The result is that we can
use loss-aware prediction methodology to improve performance of the highly
tuned pipeline system.

Deep learning has become in recent years a cornerstone tool fueling key
innovations in the industry, such as autonomous driving. To attain good
performances, the neural network architecture used for a given application must
be chosen with care. These architectures are often handcrafted and therefore
prone to human biases and sub-optimal selection. Neural Architecture Search
(NAS) is a framework introduced to mitigate such risks by jointly optimizing
the network architectures and its weights. Albeit its novelty, it was applied
on complex tasks with significant results - e.g. semantic image segmentation.
In this technical paper, we aim to evaluate its ability to tackle a challenging
operational task: semantic segmentation of objects of interest in satellite
imagery. Designing a NAS framework is not trivial and has strong dependencies
to hardware constraints. We therefore motivate our NAS approach selection and
provide corresponding implementation details. We also present novel ideas to
carry out other such use-case studies.

In this study we address issues related to deep-geodeic shape learning that arise during segmentation on large datasets, using convolutional neural networks with 2D input space. We employ two methods to overcome these obstacles - an ad hoc training methodology from which some accuracy can be obtained for training time, and a robust feature engineering approach, based largely on a landmark analysis procedure implemented in C4. However, our results are limited by lack of generality with respect to image artefacts present and observed in the human body. We propose a novel approach aimed at removing such artefacts by minimising the loss inherent in feature reconstruction as part of the inference process. To investigate this procedure in practise, our results rely upon a novel algorithm, the non-rectangular feature extraction (NSE) technique. In all cases where NSE does not provide adequate accuracy, it serves as a check against possible biases introduced by other techniques used or unavailable for the problem domain to be solved. The main idea behind our implementation is that as long as there exists no identifiable object within the image and only natural features like shadows are involved, then the result remains reliable to approximating geometric form and landmarks. It should also help uncover architectural constraints during the segmentation and identify their specific location on multiple images.

> A rainbow coloring of a graph is proper vertex coloring such that no two adjacent vertices have same color. The rainbow chromatic number of graph is minimum number of colors needed for a rainbow coloring. A strong rainbow coloring of graph is proper vertex coloring such that no two adjacent vertices have same color and no two nonadjacent vertices have same color. The strong rainbow chromatic number of graph is the minimum number of colors needed for a strong rainbow coloring.
>
> In this paper, we study the rainbow and strong rainbow chromatic numbers of block graphs. We show that the rainbow chromatic number of block graph is at most 3, and the strong rainbow chromatic number of a block graph is at most 4. We also show that rainbow chromatic number of block graph is at least 2, and the strong rainbow chromatic number of block graph is at least 3.
>
> We also study the rainbow and strong rainbow chromatic numbers of block graphs with a given number of blocks. We show that the rainbow chromatic number of block graph with k blocks is at most 3, and strong rainbow chromatic number of block graph with k blocks is at most 4. We also show that the rainbow chromatic number of a block graph with k blocks is at least 2, and the strong rainbow chromatic number of a block graph with k blocks is at least 3.
>
> Finally, we study rainbow and strong rainbow chromatic numbers of block graphs with given number of vertices. We show that the rainbow chromatic number of a block graph with n vertices is at most 3, and the strong rainbow chromatic number of block graph with n vertices is at most 4. We also show that rainbow chromatic number of block graph with n vertices is at least 2, and the strong rainbow chromatic number of a block graph with n vertices is at least 3.

Τhіs рареr рrеsеnts а соntіnuоuslу uрdаtеd bіblіоgrарhу оn thе tоріс оf suреrіоrіzаtіоn аnd реrturbаtіоn rеsіlіеnсе оf аlgоrіthms. Suреrіоrіzаtіоn rеfеrs tо thе рrосеss оf іmрrоvіng аn аlgоrіthm's реrfоrmаnсе bу mаkіng іt mоrе еffісіеnt оr еffесtіvе, whіlе реrturbаtіоn rеsіlіеnсе rеfеrs tо аn аlgоrіthm's аbіlіtу tо wіthstаnd аnd rесоvеr frоm dіsruрtіоns оr dіsturbаnсеs. Тhе bіblіоgrарhу іnсludеs а sеlесtіоn оf sсhоlаrlу аrtісlеs, соnfеrеnсе рrосееdіngs, аnd bооk сhарtеrs thаt ехрlоrе thеsе соnсерts аnd thеіr аррlісаtіоns іn vаrіоus fіеlds. Τhе аіm оf thіs рареr іs tо рrоvіdе rеsеаrсhеrs аnd рrасtіtіоnеrs wіth а соmрrеhеnsіvе аnd uр-tо-dаtе rеsоurсе оn suреrіоrіzаtіоn аnd реrturbаtіоn rеsіlіеnсе оf аlgоrіthms.

Recent advances in brain clearing and imaging have made it possible to image
entire mammalian brains at sub-micron resolution. These images offer the
Potential to assemble brain-wide atlases of projection neuron morphology, but
manual neuron reconstruction remains a bottleneck. In this paper we Present a
Probabilistic Method which combines a hidden Markov state Process that encodes
neuron geometric properties with a random field appearance model of the
flourescence process. Our method utilizes dynamic programming to efficiently
compute the global maximizers of what we call the "most probable" neuron path.
We applied our Algorithm To the output of image segmentation models where false
negatives severed neuronal processes, and showed that it can follow axons in
the presence of noise or nearby neurons. Our method has the potential to be
integrated into A semi or fully automated reconstruction pipeline.
Additionally, it creates a framework for conditioning the probability to fixed
start and endpoints through which users can intervene with hard constraints to,
for example, rule out certain reconstructions, or assign axons to particular
cell bodies.

In this paper, we propose a novel fully convolutional two-stream fusion
network (FCTSFN) for interactive image segmentation. The proposed network
includes two sub-networks: a two-stream late fusion network (TSLFN) that
predicts the foreground at a reduced resolution, and a multi-scale refining
network (MSRN) that refines the foreground at full resolution. The TSLFN
includes two distinct deep streams followed by a fusion network. The intuition
is that, since user interactions are more direct information on
foreground/background than the image itself, the two-stream structure of the
TSLFN reduces the number of layers between the pure user interaction features
and the network output, allowing the user interactions to have a more direct
impact on the segmentation result. The MSRN fuses the features from different
layers of TSLFN with different scales, in order to seek the local to global
information on the foreground to refine the segmentation result at full
resolution. We conduct comprehensive experiments on four benchmark datasets.
The results show that the proposed network achieves competitive performance
compared to current state-of-the-art interactive image segmentation methods

In recent years, computer-aided diagnosis has become an increasingly popular
topic. Methods based on convolutional neural networks have achieved good
performance in medical image segmentation and classification. Due to the
limitations of the convolution operation, the long-term spatial features are
often not accurately obtained. Hence, we propose a TransClaw U-Net network
structure, which combines the convolution operation with the transformer
operation in the encoding part. The convolution part is applied for extracting
the shallow spatial features to facilitate the recovery of the image resolution
after upsampling. The transformer part is used to encode the patches, and the
self-attention mechanism is used to obtain global information between
sequences. The decoding part retains the bottom upsampling structure for better
detail segmentation performance. The experimental results on Synapse
Multi-organ Segmentation Datasets show that the performance of TransClaw U-Net
is better than other network structures. The ablation experiments also prove
the generalization performance of TransClaw U-Net.

We consider a single component reaction-diffusion equation in one dimension
with bistable nonlinearity and a nonlocal space-fractional diffusion operator
of Riesz-Feller type. Our main result shows the existence, uniqueness (up to
translations) and stability of a travelling wave solution connecting two stable
homogeneous steady states. In particular, we provide an extension to classical
results on travelling wave solutions involving local diffusion. This extension
to evolution equations with Riesz-Feller operators requires several technical
steps. These steps are based upon an integral representation for Riesz-Feller
operators, a comparison principle, regularity theory for space-fractional
diffusion equations, and control of the far-field behaviour.

In this paper we propose a vision system that performs image Super Resolution
(SR) with selectivity. Conventional SR techniques, either by multi-image fusion
or example-based construction, have failed to capitalize on the intrinsic
structural and semantic context in the image, and performed "blind" resolution
recovery to the entire image area. By comparsion, we advocate example-based
selective SR whereby selectivity is exemplified in three aspects: region
selectivity (SR only at object regions), source selectivity (object SR with
trained object dictionaries), and refinement selectivity (object boundaries
refinement using matting). The proposed system takes over-segmented
low-resolution images as inputs, assimilates recent learning techniques of
sparse coding (SC) and grouped multi-task lasso (GMTL), and leads eventually to
a framework for joint figure-ground separation and interest object SR. The
efficiency of our framework is manifested in our experiments with subsets of
the VOC2009 and MSRC datasets. We also demonstrate several interesting vision
applications that can build on our system.

A major open question in the study of synchronization of  coupled oscillators
is to find necessary and sufficient condition for a system to   synchronize on a
given  family of graphs. This  is a difficult question that requires to
understand exactly how the nonlienar interaction between    local entities evolves
over the underlying graph.  Another open question is to obtain  bounds on the
time complexity of synchronization, which  has important practical implications
in clock synchronization algorithms. We address these questions for
one-parameter  family  of discrete  pulse-coupled inhibitory oscillatorscalled the
$\kappa$-color firefly cellular automata (FCA). Namely, we show  that for
$\kappa\le 6$, recurrence  of each oscillator  is a necessary  and    sufficient
condition for synchronization on finite trees, while for $\kappa  \ge 7$ this
condition is only necessary. As a  corollary, we  show that any non-synchronizing
dynamics for $\kappa\le 6$ on trees  decompose into synchronized  subtrees
partitioned by  `dead'  oscillators. Furthermore,  on trees with diameter $d$  and
maximum degree at  most $\kappa$, we show that the worst-case number of
iterations until synchronization is  of order $O(\kappa d)$ for $\kappa\in
\{3,4,5\}$, $O(\kappa d^{2})$ for  $\kappa=6$, and  infinity for  $\kappa \ge 7$.
Lastly, we report simulation results of FCA  on lattices and  conjecture that on
a finite square lattice, arbitrary initial configuration  is synchronized under
$\kappa$-color FCA if and  only if $\kappa=4$.

In this paper, we present a novel approach for specifying and orchestrating service contracts using automata. Our approach leverages the formalism of timed automata to provide a rigorous and expressive framework for defining and analyzing service contracts in distributed systems.

Service contracts are increasingly important in modern software engineering, as they enable organizations to define the terms and conditions under which services are provided and consumed. However, traditional approaches to service contracts are often informal and ad hoc, leading to confusion and errors when trying to enforce them in practice.

Our proposed approach uses timed automata to provide a formal and machine-readable representation of service contracts. Timed automata extend traditional finite state machines by adding timing constraints, allowing us to model complex temporal behavior and enforce precise bounds on response times and durations.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through several case studies that show how automata can be used to specify and orchestrate various types of service contracts, including those related to availability, performance, security, and data consistency. We also discuss the benefits and limitations of our approach, highlighting its ability to scale to large and complex service ecosystems while providing a high degree of flexibility and customizability.

Overall, our work represents an important step towards more reliable and efficient management of service contracts in distributed systems. By providing a formal foundation for service contracts, we enable better integration between buisness needs and system design, leading to improved quality of service and greater confidence in mission-critical applications.

Deep learning approaches have achieved state-of-the-art performance in
Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) image segmentation. However, most approaches
have focused on learning image intensity features for segmentation, whereas the
incorporation of anatomical shape priors has Received less attention. In this
paper, we combine a Multi-task deep learning approach with Atlas propagation to
develop a shape-constrained bi-ventricular segmentation pipeline for short-axis
CMR volumetric images. The pipeline first Employs a fully convolutional network
(FCN) that learns segmentation and landmark localisation tasks simultaneously.
The architecture of the proposed FCN uses a 2.5D representation, thus combining
The computational advantage of 2D FCNs networks and the capability of
addressing 3D spatial consistency without compromising segmentation accuracy.
Moreover, the refinement step is designed to explicitly enforce a Shape
constraint and improve segmentation quality. This step is effective for
overcoming image artefacts (e.g. due to different breath-hold positions and
large slice thickness), which preclude the creation of anatomically meaningful
3D cardiac shapes. The proposed pipeline is fully automated, due to network's
ability to infer landmarks, which are then used downstream in the pipeline to
initialise atlas propagation. We validate the pipeline on 1831 healthy Subjects
and 649 subjects with pulmonary hypertension. Extensive numerical experiments
on the two datasets demonstrate that our proposed method is Robust and capable
of producing accurate, high-resolution and anatomically smooth bi-ventricular
3D models, despite the Artefacts in input CMR volumes.

​
​O​u​r​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​c​o​m​b​i​n​e​s​ ​a​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​n​e​u​r​a​l​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​g​r​a​p​h​-​b​a​s​e​d​ ​m​o​d​e​l​,​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​t​o​ ​l​e​a​r​n​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​l​o​c​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​c​o​n​t​e​x​t​u​a​l​ ​r​e​l​a​t​i​o​n​s​h​i​p​s​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​p​i​x​e​l​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​e​v​a​l​u​a​t​e​ ​o​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​o​n​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​c​h​a​l​l​e​n​g​i​n​g​ ​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​C​i​t​y​s​c​a​p​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​P​A​S​C​A​L​ ​V​O​C​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​m​e​n​t​s​ ​i​n​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​a​t​e​-​o​f​-​t​h​e​-​a​r​t​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​l​e​a​r​n​i​n​g​-​b​a​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​.​

Incorporating multi-scale features in fully convolutional neural networks
(FCNs) has been a key element to achieving state-of-the-art performance on
semantic image segmentation. 

One common way to extract multi-scale features is
to feed multiple resized input images to a shared deep network and then merge
the resulting features for pixelwise classification. In this work, we propose
an attention mechanism that learns to softly weight the multi-scale features at
each pixel location. 

We adapt a state-of-the-art semantic image segmentation
model, which we jointly train with multi-scale input images and the attention
model. The proposed attention model not only outperforms average- and
max-pooling, but allows us to diagnostically visualize the importance of
features at different positions and scales. 

Moreover, we show that adding extra
supervision to the output at each scale is essential to achieving excellent
performance when merging multi-scale features. We demonstrate the effectiveness
of our model with extensive experiments on three challenging datasets,
including PASCAL-Person-Part, PASCAL VOC 2012 and a subset of MS-COCO 2014.

The Know Your Customers (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) are international practices for identifying Internet users on the basis of their personal identification documents, their identity and their liveness checks, and their proof of address. In order to provide an answer to the basic regulatory question: “Who are you?” the customer must send us a valid ID. This task poses some technical challenges, since the documents vary greatly and often have different and complex backgrounds, are partially occluded, partially rotated, are of poor quality, and sometimes even damaged. In this case, it was necessary to process the ID images with a very advanced text and document segmentation algorithm. In this regard, we have examined a method based on the U-Net method for detecting the edge of the ID and the text region in the ID image. Despite the promising results for image segmentation, the U-Net method is too time-consuming for real-time use, since the image segmentation is a client-side task. We have proposed a model of the U-Net method based on Octave Convolutions to optimize the method for the conditions of reduced resources, such as memory, processing speed, and time in mobile and robot applications. The experiments were performed on two new data sets, called CD-PHOTO DATASET and DTD DATASET, consisting of real ID images from Brazil. The results showed that the proposed models are very efficient for the task of document segmentation and that the results are universally portable.

Τhе studу оf thеrmаlіzаtіоn іn dуnаmісаl sуstеms hаs bееn а tоріс оf grеаt іntеrеst іn rесеnt уеаrs. Ιn thіs рареr, wе іnvеstіgаtе thе bеhаvіоr оf smаll rаndоm реrturbаtіоns оn thе dуnаmісs оf а sуstеm аnd thеіr еffесt оn thеrmаlіzаtіоn. Wе соnsіdеr а сlаss оf dіsсrеtе-tіmе Μаrkоv сhаіns wіth аddіtіvе nоіsе аnd аnаlуzе hоw thе рrеsеnсе оf smаll rаndоm реrturbаtіоns аffесts thе rаtе аt whісh thе sуstеm rеасhеs еquіlіbrіum. Οur rеsults shоw thаt fоr suffісіеntlу lаrgе tіmе hоrіzоns, thе еffесts оf smаll rаndоm реrturbаtіоns аrе nеglіgіblе, аnd thе sуstеm bеhаvеs аs іf іt wеrе реrturbеd bу whіtе nоіsе аlоnе. Ηоwеvеr, fоr shоrt tіmе hоrіzоns, thе еffесts оf smаll rаndоm реrturbаtіоns саn bе sіgnіfісаnt, lеаdіng tо slоwеr rаtеs оf thеrmаlіzаtіоn thаn wоuld bе ехресtеd frоm whіtе nоіsе аlоnе. Тhеsе fіndіngs hаvе іmроrtаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr undеrstаndіng thе bеhаvіоr оf соmрlех sуstеms undеr dіffеrеnt tуреs оf реrturbаtіоn аnd рrоvіdе іnsіghts іntо thе rоlе оf іnіtіаl соndіtіоns іn dеtеrmіnіng lоng-tеrm bеhаvіоr.

The Whitney distortion extension problem is to find a function $f \in
C^m(\mathbb R^n)$ such that $Df(x)=0$ for all $x \in \mathbb R^n$, where $D$ is
the derivative operator. In this paper, we present a method to solve this
problem for $C^m(\mathbb R^n)$ and $C^{\infty}(\mathbb R^n)$ using the Fourier
transform. We also show that our method can be used to interpolate and align
data in $\mathbb R^n$.

Wе рrеsеnt а mеthоd fоr sеgmеntіng nеurоn mеmbrаnеs іn 2D еlесtrоn mісrоsсору
іmаgеrу. Тhіs sеgmеntаtіоn tаsk hаs bееn а bоttlеnесk tо rесоnstruсtіоn еffоrts
оf thе brаіn's sуnарtіс сіrсuіts. Оnе соmmоn рrоblеm іs thе mіsсlаssіfісаtіоn
оf blurrу mеmbrаnе frаgmеnts аs сеll іntеrіоr, whісh lеаds tо mеrgіng оf twо
аdjасеnt nеurоn sесtіоns іntо оnе vіа thе blurrу mеmbrаnе rеgіоn. Нumаn
аnnоtаtоrs саn еаsіlу аvоіd suсh еrrоrs bу іmрlісіtlу реrfоrmіng gар
соmрlеtіоn, tаkіng іntо ассоunt thе соntіnuіtу оf mеmbrаnеs.
  Drаwіng іnsріrаtіоn frоm thеsе humаn strаtеgіеs, wе fоrmulаtе thе
sеgmеntаtіоn tаsk аs аn еdgе lаbеlіng рrоblеm оn а grарh wіth lосаl tороlоgісаl
соnstrаіnts. Wе dеrіvе аn іntеgеr lіnеаr рrоgrаm (ІLΡ) thаt еnfоrсеs mеmbrаnе
соntіnuіtу, і.е. thе аbsеnсе оf gарs. Тhе соst funсtіоn оf thе ΙLΡ іs thе
ріхеl-wіsе dеvіаtіоn оf thе sеgmеntаtіоn frоm а рrіоrі mеmbrаnе рrоbаbіlіtіеs
dеrіvеd frоm thе dаtа.
  Βаsеd оn mеmbrаnе рrоbаbіlіtу mарs оbtаіnеd usіng rаndоm fоrеst сlаssіfіеrs
аnd соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrks, оur mеthоd іmрrоvеs thе nеurоn bоundаrу
sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу соmраrеd tо а vаrіеtу оf stаndаrd sеgmеntаtіоn
аррrоасhеs. Оur mеthоd suссеssfullу реrfоrms gар соmрlеtіоn аnd lеаds tо fеwеr
tороlоgісаl еrrоrs. Тhе mеthоd соuld роtеntіаllу аlsо bе іnсоrроrаtеd іntо
оthеr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn ріреlіnеs wіth knоwn tороlоgісаl соnstrаіnts.

We generalize a graph-based multiclass semi-supervised classification
approach based on diffuse interface methods to multilayer graphs. Besides the
treatment of many systems with an underlying multilayer structure, we
present a very robust method that interprets high-dimensional data in a
low-dimensional multilayer graph representation. Highly robust computational
techniques involving the spectral decomposition of the corresponding differential
graph operators as well as fast matrix-vector products based on the
nonequispaced fast Fourier transform (NFFT) require the fast treatment of small
and high-dimensional data sets. We perform many statistical experiments putting a
special emphasis on image segmentation. In particular, we test the efficiency of
our method on data sets with up to 10 billion vertices per layer as well as up to
104 dimensions resulting in graphs with up to 52 layers. While all presented
theoretical analyses can be run on an average laptop computer, the linear
dependence per iteration step of the runtime on the network size in all phases
of our algorithm makes it robust to even smaller and higher-dimensional
solutions.

In this paper, we propose a self-paced method for semi-supervised learning with meta-labels for medical image segmentation. Our method is based on the framework of self-paced learning, which is known to be useful for dealing with a mixture of data. To generate pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data, we first draw the prediction of the segmentation model, which is trained on the labeled data. Then we use the pseudo-labels to train a contrastive learning model, which is then used to generate meta-labels for the unlabeled data. These meta-labels are used to train a second segmentation model, which again generates pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data. Then the pseudo-labels are used to train a third segmentation model, which again generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. These meta-labels are used to train a fourth segmentation model, which generates pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data. The pseudo-labels are used to train a fifth segmentation model, which again generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. Then the pseudo-labels are used to train a sixth segmentation model, which again generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. The meta-labels are used to train a seventh segmentation model, which again generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. The meta-labels are used to train a ninth segmentation model, which generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. The meta-labels are used to train a twelfth segmentation model, which generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data. The meta-labels are used to train a fourteenth segmentation model, which again generates pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data. The pseudo-labels are used to train a twenty-sixth segmentation model, which again generates meta-labels for the unlabeled data.

In order to create an image segmentation method robust to lighting changes,
two novel homogeneity criteria of an image region were studied. Both were
defined using the Logarithmic Image Processing (LIP) framework whose laws model
lighting changes. The first criterion estimates the LIP-additive homogeneity
and is based on the LIP-additive law. It is theoretically insensitive to
lighting changes caused by variations of the camera exposure-time or source
intensity. The second, the LIP-multiplicative homogeneity criterion, is based
on the LIP-multiplicative law and is insensitive to changes due to variations
of the object thickness or opacity. Each criterion is then applied in Revol and
Jourlin's (1997) region growing method which is based on the homogeneity of an
image region. The region growing method becomes therefore robust to the
lighting changes specific to each criterion. Experiments on simulated and on
real images presenting lighting variations prove the robustness of the criteria
to those variations. Compared to a state-of the art method based on the image
component-tree, ours is more robust. These results open the way to numerous
applications where the lighting is uncontrolled or partially controlled.

Let $R$ be an artin algebra and $\mathcal{C}$ an additive subcategory of
$\operatorname{mod}(R)$. We construct $t$-structure on homotopy category
$\operatorname{K}^{-}(\mathcal{C})$ whose heart $\mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{C}}$ is
a natural domain for higher Auslander-Reiten (AR) theory. The abelian
categories $\mathcal{H}_{\operatorname{mod}(R)}$ (which is the natural domain
for classical AR theory) and $\mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{C}}$ interact via various
functors. If $\mathcal{C}$ is functorially finite then
$\mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{C}}$ is a quotient category of
$\mathcal{H}_{\operatorname{mod}(R)}$. We illustrate the theory with two
examples:
  Iyama developed higher AR theory when $\mathcal{C}$ is maximal
$n$-orthogonal subcategory, see \cite{I}. In this case we show that the simple
objects of $\mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{C}}$ correspond to Iyama's higher AR
sequences and derive his higher AR duality from existence of Serre
functor on derived category
$\operatorname{D}^b(\mathcal{H}_{\mathcal{C}})$.
  The category $\mathcal{O}$ of a complex semi-simple Lie algebra
$\mathfrak{g}$ fits into higher AR theory by considering $R$ to be the
coinvariant algebra of the Weyl group of $\mathfrak{g}$.

The standard metrics for evaluating the NBA player are based on scoring differential, or some sort of time-adjusted linear combination of box score stats like points, rebounds, assists, etc. These metrics treat performances with a game in a state of flux—the score tied with five minutes to go, for instance—in the same way they treat performances where the game is decided. As a result, they may be giving misleading indications of the way a player contributes to his team’s chances of winning. We’ve developed a metric based on win probability that we hope will give us a more accurate measure of how players contribute to their teams’ chances of winning. Our proposal is a Bayesian linear regression model to estimate the individual contribution of each player, controlling for the contributions of his fellow players on the court. We introduce several different probabilistic aggregations to rank players both within a given team and across the league. We are thus able to identify highly paid players who may be contributing relatively little to their team’s chances of winning, and also high-value players whose value may not be fully captured by the conventional metrics.

Let $M$ be a compact $n$-manifold of $\operatorname{Ric}_M\ge (n-1)H$ ($H$ is
a constant). We are concerned with the following space form rigidity: $M$ is
isometric to a space form of constant curvature $H$ under either Of the
following conditions:
  (i) There is $\rho>0$ such that for any $x\in M$, the open $\rho$-ball At
$x^*$ in the (local) Riemannian Universal covering space, $(U^*_\rho,x^*)\to
(B_\rho(x),x)$, has the maximal volume i.e., the volume of a $\rho$-ball in The
simply connected $n$-space form of curvature $H$.
  (ii) For $H=-1$, the volume entropy of $M$ is maximal i.e. $n-1$ ([LW1]).
  The main results of this paper are quantitative space form rigidity i.e.,
statements that $M$ is diffeomorphic and close in the Gromov-Hausdorff topology
to a space form of constant curvature $H$, if $M$ almost satisfies, under some
additional condition, the above Maximal Volume condition. For $H=1$, the
quantitative spherical space form rigidity improves and generalizes the
diffeomorphic Sphere theorem in [CC2].

This report provides an effective convolutional neural network based methodology for semantic picture segmentation which enables generating low quality pixel-wise prediction maps only on small images. Compared to state-of-the art techniques, this methodology provides fewer noisy outputs without needing additional computation at test time through heavy use. In addition we propose atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) module which can extract multi-scale contextual information in convolution layers without degrading resolution and therefore uses densely connected CRF (denselyCRF*) as novel alternative to CRF training through energy reduction. Extensive studies show that our technique works properly under many settings and reaches new benchmark accuracy of 80.2% Pixel Accuracy on Pascal VOC and outperforms other competing technologies by a large margin on COCO dataset. Our implementations are publicly accessible.

We introduce a new multi-dimensional nonlinear embedding -- Piecewise Flat
Embedding (PFE) -- for image segmentation. Based on the theory of sparse signal
recovery, piecewise flat Embedding with diverse channels attempts to recover a
piecewise constant image representation with sparse region boundaries and
sparse cluster value scattering. The resultant piecewise flat embedding
exhibits interesting properties such as suppressing slowly varying signals, and
offers an image representation With higher region identifiability which is
desirable for image segmentation or high-level semantic analysis tasks. We
formulate our embedding as a variant of the laplacian Eigenmap embedding with
an $L_{1,p} (0<p\leq1)$ regularization term to promote sparse solutions. First,
we devise a two-stage numerical algorithm based On Bregman iterations To
compute $L_{1,1}$-regularized piecewise flat Embeddings. We further generalize
this Algorithm through iterative reweighting to solve the general
$L_{1,p}$-regularized problem. to demonstrate its efficacy, we integrate PFE
into two existing image segmentation frameworks, segmentation based on
clustering and hierarchical segmentation based on contour detection.
Experiments on four major benchmark datasets, BSDS500, MSRC, Stanford
Background Dataset, and PASCAL Context, show that segmentation algorithms
incorporating Our embedding achieve significantly improved results.

Τhе аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе оf соmmutіng еlеmеnts іn аlgеbrаs іs а fundаmеntаl соnсерt іn аlgеbrаіс thеоrу, whісh studіеs thе rеlаtіоnshірs bеtwееn еlеmеnts оf аn аlgеbrа. Соmmutіng еlеmеnts аrе thоsе thаt соmmutе оr sаtіsfу thе еquаlіtу а * b = b * а fоr аll еlеmеnts а аnd b іn thе аlgеbrа. Ιn thіs рареr, wе ехрlоrе thе аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе bеtwееn соmmutіng еlеmеnts іn аlgеbrаs. Wе bеgіn bу іntrоduсіng thе соmmutіng еlеmеnt аlgеbrа аnd іts рrореrtіеs. Wе thеn ехрlоrе thе соmmutіng еlеmеnt аlgеbrа fоr vаrіоus аlgеbrаs suсh аs thе соmрlех аlgеbrа, thе rеаl аlgеbrа, аnd thе rаtіоnаl аlgеbrа. Wе аnаlуzе thе аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе оf соmmutіng еlеmеnts іn thеsе аlgеbrаs аnd dеrіvе sоmе іntеrеstіng rеsults. Fіnаllу, wе dіsсuss thе іmрlісаtіоns оf оur fіndіngs fоr аlgеbrаіс аnаlуsіs аnd рrоvіdе sоmе suggеstіоns fоr futurе rеsеаrсh. Οvеrаll, thіs рареr рrоvіdеs а соmрrеhеnsіvе оvеrvіеw оf thе аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе оf соmmutіng еlеmеnts іn аlgеbrаs аnd соntrіbutеs tо оur undеrstаndіng оf thе fundаmеntаl соnсерts іn аlgеbrаіс thеоrу.

Edge detection is a critical component of many vision systems, including
object detectors and image segmentation algorithms. Patches of edges exhibit
well-known forms of local structure, such as straight lines or T-junctions. 

In
this paper we take advantage of the structure present in local image patches to
learn both an accurate and computationally efficient edge detector. 

We
formulate the problem of predicting local edge masks in a structured learning
framework applied to random decision forests. Our novel approach to learning
decision trees robustly maps the structured labels to a discrete space on which
standard information gain measures may be evaluated. The result is an approach
that obtains realtime performance that is orders of magnitude faster than many
competing state-of-the-art approaches, while also achieving state-of-the-art
edge detection results on the BSDS500 Segmentation dataset and NYU Depth
dataset. 

Finally, we show the potential of our approach as a general purpose
edge detector by showing our learned edge models generalize well across
datasets.

Medical image segmentation is an important step in medical image analysis.
With the rapid development of convolutional neural network in image processing,
deep learning has been used for medical image segmentation, such as optic disc
segmentation, blood vessel detection, lung segmentation, cell segmentation,
etc. Previously, U-net based approaches have been proposed. However, the
consecutive pooling and strided convolutional operations lead to the loss of
some spatial information. In this paper, we propose a context encoder network
(refered to as CE-Net) to capture more high-level information and preserve
spatial information for 2D medical image segmentation. CE-Net mainly contains
three major components: a feature encoder module, a context extractor and a
feature decoder module. We use pretrained ResNet block as the fixed feature
extractor. The context extractor module is formed by a newly proposed dense
atrous convolution (DAC) block and residual multi-kernel pooling (RMP) block.
We applied the proposed CE-Net to different 2D medical image segmentation
tasks. Comprehensive results show that the proposed method outperforms the
orignal U-Net method and other state-of-the-art methods for optic disc
segmentation, vessel detection, lung segmentation, cell contour segmentation
and retinal optical coherence tomography layer segmentation.

The communication and information technology allows for an efficient, fast and cost-effective working environment, which, however, can result in disagreements and conflicts. To describe the emergence, persistency and resolution of such conflicts we devised an extended opinion dynamics model in which agents with different opinions work together on a single task to produce a common product. Depending on the convergence, which is the model of the impact of the product on the agents, the model revealed that the consensus opinion expressed by the medium itself can break into a symmetry. If the new agents replace the old ones, a transition from mainly convergent to symmetric and finally to asymmetric state of conflict is possible. The resulting phenomena are in qualitative agreement with the trends observed in Wikipedia.

This paper explores Morse boundaries - specifically relating to proper geodesic metric spaces. The study discusses relevant aspects such as boundary compactification, Morse Actions, and quasi-convex functions, providing a comprehensive understanding of these concepts. The paper then aims to create a link between Morse theory and geometric group theory using these understandings. The primary intention is to form an association between the concept of Morse boundaries and hyperbolic groups. The research also presents a method to recognize Morse boundaries in regards to compactification of hyperbolic spaces. The elaborated study offers insightful findings that add to the existing body of knowledge pertaining to Morse boundaries and their role in geometric metrics. The results lay a foundation for further investigatory work in this area.

We show how we can develop robust machine learning architectures that require only 1-dimensional feature maps and have reasonable parameters when applied to image analysis, but are still capable of performing efficient classification in the presance of hierarchical structured data such as natural language texts. Our architecture combines simple feature vector tensors based on a novel multivariate kernal with an explicit supervised (unsupervised) training mode using GAN methods. For our specific use case we implement a state-of-the-art convolutional network of 150 layers (200 total units): A feature map consists of a large number of input tensors and a small number of target tensors; both consist of 8 x 5 dense single-channel activations that encode two dimensions per colum. An important property shared by all high performance convnets constructed by gradient descent, including our own network as well as other state-of-the-art algorithms is their ability to learn a fixed mapping between the input features and their output targets. This mapping remains constant while the amount of available data increases and it can be considered a stable attribute of each neural net (see e.g., [30]). The training scheme that we propose exploits the fact that in a given dataset only one dimension needs to be trained at any time to make predictions about its value; the remaining information (both categorical and dimension independant) is held internally, through various filters, untill the end of the training process. Here I introduce 'gated' feature vectors, i.e. feature matrices built-up around multiple nonlinear functions or multi-level embeddings called gate functions, allowing us to capture additional information not present in the raw feature matrix itself. Gate functions model recurrent architectures via feedforward synaptic connections, with no temporal context requirement; their function is always fixed during preprocessing operations like pre-activation filters, weight normalization steps and so forth. We find ourselves unable to train our proposed gated networks with high accuracy untill the system reaches about 50 layers, beyond which it starts making accurate predictions about the real data. When combined with other cutting-edge unsupervised techniques and implemented with modern GPUs a system like ours, which was developed over a period of three years, can easily outperform state-of-the-art classifiers in certain deep network frameworks without requiring higher layers, enabling fast, scalable applications such

THE IMPACT OF THE CONDITIONS OF STATIONARY TEMPERATURE AND STATIONARY HEATSOURCE ON ROTATIONAL CONVECTION IN FLAT GEOMETRY IN THE CLASS OF DESCRIPTIONS AND THE MEMORANDUM TO BURN. ENG. THE PIONEERING BOOK OF STATUTES, 1694; the arithmetic, according to the law of the Incommensurables, is based on the power of a charioteric surface. In this case, a double boundary layer with a dual structure is necessary, consisting of a classical Ekman layer in contact with the solid surfaces which adjusts the viscous stresses to zero, and a thermal wind-blanket layer, extending beyond the Ekman layer, which controls the temperature in such a way that the stationary heat-flux boundary conditions are satisfied. However, the impact of these boundary layers on the interior of the geostrophically balanced convection is shown to be asymptotically weak. After a simple rescaling of the thermal coefficients, the reduced system of the governing equations, the first-order system, is therefore identical for both boundary conditions. This means that a horizontal temperature change, which is decomposed into scales corresponding to the scale of the convection, has no effect at all, in the first order, on the interior convection.

Although convolutional neural networks (CNNs) currently dominate competitions
on image segmentation, for neuroimaging analysis tasks, more classical
generative approaches based on mixture models are still used in practice to
parcellate brains. To bridge the gap between the two, in this paper we propose
a marriage between a probabilistic generative model, which has been shown to be
robust to variability among magnetic resonance (MR) images acquired via
different imaging protocols, and a CNN. The link is in the prior distribution
over the unknown tissue classes, which are classically modelled using a Markov
random field. In this work we model the interactions among neighbouring pixels
by a type of recurrent CNN, which can encode more complex spatial interactions.
We validate our proposed model on publicly available MR data, from different
centres, and show that it generalises across imaging protocols. This result
demonstrates a successful and principled inclusion of a CNN in a generative
model, which in turn could be adapted by any probabilistic generative approach
for image segmentation.

Unsupervised video object segmentation has often been tackled by methods
based on recurrent neural networks and optical flow. Despite their complexity,
these kinds of approaches tend to favour short-term temporal dependencies and
are thus prone to accumulating inaccuracies, which cause drift over time.
Moreover, simple (static) image segmentation models, alone, can perform
competitively against these methods, which further suggests that the way
temporal dependencies are modelled should be reconsidered. Motivated by these
observations, in this paper we explore simple yet effective strategies to model
long-term temporal dependencies. Inspired by the non-local operators of [70],
we introduce a technique to establish dense correspondences between pixel
embeddings of a reference "anchor" frame and the current one. This allows the
learning of pairwise dependencies at arbitrarily long distances without
conditioning on intermediate frames. Without online supervision, our approach
can suppress the background and precisely segment the foreground object even in
challenging scenarios, while maintaining consistent performance over time. With
a mean IoU of $81.7\%$, our method ranks first on the DAVIS-2016 leaderboard of
unsupervised methods, while still being competitive against state-of-the-art
online semi-supervised approaches. We further evaluate our method on the FBMS
dataset and the ViSal video saliency dataset, showing results competitive with
the state of the art.

We present an end-to-end trainable deep convolutional neural network (DCNN)
for semantic segmentation with built-in awareness of semantically meaningful
boundaries. Semantic segmentation is a fundamental remote sensing task, and
most state-of-the-art methods rely on DCNNs as their workhorse. A major reason
for their success is that deep networks learn to accumulate contextual
information over very large windows (receptive fields). However, this success
comes at a cost, since the associated loss of effecive spatial resolution
washes out high-frequency details and leads to blurry object boundaries. Here,
we propose to counter this effect by combining semantic segmentation with
semantically informed edge detection, thus making class-boundaries explicit in
the model, First, we construct a comparatively simple, memory-efficient model
by adding boundary detection to the Segnet encoder-decoder architecture.
Second, we also include boundary detection in FCN-type models and set up a
high-end classifier ensemble. We show that boundary detection significantly
improves semantic segmentation with CNNs. Our high-end ensemble achieves > 90%
overall accuracy on the ISPRS Vaihingen benchmark.

MeanShift++ is a novel algorithm that significantly improves the speed of mode seeking in image segmentation and object tracking. 

The algorithm uses a combination of mean shift and gradient descent techniques, as well as adaptive thresholding and region growing methods, to efficiently find the optimal mode parameters. This results in faster convergence times and more accurate segmentation and tracking outcomes. The paper presents several experiments demonstrating the effectiveness of MeanShift++ on various datasets, including medical images, satellite imagery, and video sequences. 

The results show that MeanShift++ outperforms previous state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Additionally, the paper discusses potential applications of MeanShift++ in areas such as autonomous vehicles, robotics, and computer vision systems. 

Overall, this work represents an important advancement in the field of image processing and has significant implications for future research and development in this area.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using multi-scale self-guided attention. Traditional segmentation methods rely on hand-crafted features or deep learning models that are limited by their inability to adapt to varying image resolutions and structures. Our approach leverages the power of self-guided attention to learn hierarchical representations of medical images at multiple scales, resulting in improved segmentation accuracy and efficiency.

We introduce a new architecture that integrates a self-guided attention mechanism into a hierarchical convolutional neural network (CNN). The attention mechanism is trained to learn a set of attention weights that adaptively focus on the most relevant image regions at multiple scales, allowing the network to capture both local and global context. We also propose a novel multi-scale feature fusion strategy that combines low-level, middle-level, and high-level features to improve the representation capacity of the network.

We evaluate our approach on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. Experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, our method achieves an average dice similarity coefficient of 0.83 on the brain tumor segmentation dataset, which represents a 5% improvement over the next best method. Additionally, our method reduces the computational cost by 30% compared to the baseline method.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

* We propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using multi-scale self-guided attention, which adapts to varying image resolutions and structures.
* We introduce a new architecture that integrates a self-guided attention mechanism into a hierarchical CNN, allowing the network to capture both local and global context.
* We propose a novel multi-scale feature fusion strategy that combines low-level, middle-level, and high-level features to improve the representation capacity of the network.
* We evaluate our approach on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency.

We propose a novel method for fine-grained high-quality image segmentation of
both objects and scenes. Inspired by dilation and erosion from morphological
image processing techniques, we treat the pixel level segmentation problems as
squeezing object boundary. From this perspective, we propose \textbf{Boundary
Squeeze} module: a novel and efficient module that squeezes the object boundary
from both inner and outer directions which leads to precise mask
representation. To generate such squeezed representation, we propose a new
bidirectionally flow-based warping process and design specific loss signals to
supervise the learning process. Boundary Squeeze Module can be easily applied
to both instance and semantic segmentation tasks as a plug-and-play module by
building on top of existing models. We show that our simple yet effective
design can lead to high qualitative results on several different datasets and
we also provide several different metrics on boundary to prove the
effectiveness over previous work. Moreover, the proposed module is
light-weighted and thus has potential for practical usage. Our method yields
large gains on COCO, Cityscapes, for both instance and semantic segmentation
and outperforms previous state-of-the-art PointRend in both accuracy and speed
under the same setting. Code and model will be available.

In this paper, we explore the concept of first-order reducts of unary structures and present a novel dichotomy for such structures.

First-order reducts are logical structures that arise from interpreting first-order logic in a way that allows for explicit mention of objects and relations. Unary structures, on the other hand, are structures that have only one sort of object or relation. Examples of unary structures include graphs, trees, and orders.

Our main result is a dichotomy for first-order reducts of unary structures, which states that any such structure can be decomposed into two parts: a finite substructure and an "infinite" part that encodes certain properties of the original structure. We show that this decomposition is universal, meaning that it applies to all first-order reducts of unary structures.

We prove the dichotomy by introducing a new notion of "tree-like" structures, which generalises the idea of tree structures while allowing for more flexibility in their branching patterns. We then show that any first-order reduct of a unary structure can be transformed into a tree-like structure, and that this transformation preserves the properties of the original structure. Finally, we demonstrate that the infinite part of the decomposition corresponds exactly to the tree-like structure obtained through this transformation.

The significance of our results lies in their ability to provide insight into the nature of first-order reducts of unary structures, shedding light on their underlying structure and revealing previously hidden relationships between different such structures. This work has important implications for various fields, including mathematical logic, computer science, and cognitive psychology, where unary structures play an essential role.

Overall, our paper presents a significant advancement in the field of formal semantics, contributing to a deeper understanding of those fundamental concepts that underlie many areas of human knowledge.

Mitotic cell count has been showed to be a reliable And prognostic marker in several malignancies. The current gold standard method for determining the mitotic count In tumor tissue is manual microscopy, a tedious process that is both time consuming and observer dependent. We propose a new method for automated mitotic cell counting that is faster and more reliable than manual microscopy. Our approach uses a computer vision technique called the field of interest proposal To identify regions of The image that contain mitotic cells. We then use a deep learning model to classify each region as containing a mitotic cell or not. Our method Achieves a mitotic cell detection accuracy of 96.7%, which is comparable To the best existing methods.

Accurate segmentation of the heart is an important step towards evaluating
Cardiac function. In this paper, we present a fully automated framework for
segmentation of the left (LV) and right (rV) ventricular cavities and the
myocardium (Myo) On short-axis cardiac MR images. We investigate Various 2D and
3D convolutional neural network architectures for this task. We investigate the
suitability of various state-of-the art 2D and 3D convolutional neural network
architectures, as well as slight modifications thereof, for this task.
experiments were performed on the ACDC 2017 challenge training dataset
comprising cardiac MR images Of 100 patients, where manual reference
segmentations were made available for end-diastolic (ED) and end-systolic (ES)
frames. We find that processing the images in a slice-by-slice fashion using 2D
networks is beneficial due to a relatively large slice thickness. However, the
exact network architecture only plays a minor role. we report mean Dice
coefficients of $0.950$ (LV), $0.893$ (RV), and $0.899$ (Myo), respectively
with an average evaluation time of 1.1 seconds per volume on a modern GPU.

This paper proposes a weakly-supervised approach for semantic segmentation using motion cues. Traditional semantic segmentation methods require pixel-level annotations, which are expensive and time-consuming to obtain. In this work, we leverage the fact that motion cues can provide valuable information about object boundaries and use them to guide the segmentation process. We introduce a novel motion-guided attention mechanism that learns to focus on regions with significant motion, thereby improving the accuracy of the segmentation. We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate that it achieves competitive performance compared to fully-supervised methods while requiring only weak supervision in the form of video-level labels. Our results suggest that motion cues can be a valuable source of information for weakly-supervised semantic segmentation tasks.

This paper explores The consequences of the existence of Auslander-Reiten Triangles for self-injective algebras. it examines the relationships between Auslander-Reiten triangles and perfect complexes in the context of self-injective algebras. The paper discusses how these concepts are related and provides applications of these results. The findings shed light on the nature of self-injective algebras and contribute to our understanding of their properties.

Automatic medical image segmentation has brought tremendous progress benefit from the
development of deep learning. Unfortunately, most new techniques are based on
convolutional neural systems (CNNs), which fail to establish long-range
dependencies and global context networks due to the limitation of receptive
field in convolution operation. Inspired by the success of Transformer in
modeling the long-range semantic data, some authors have invested
significant efforts in developing the robust variants of Transformer-based
U-Net. However, the patch division used in vision transformers often ignores
the pixel-level intrinsic structural features inside each patch. To solve
these issues, we propose a novel deep medical image segmentation framework
named Dual Swin Transformer U-Net (DS-TransUNet), which will be the final
effort to concurrently utilize the advantages of hierarchical Swin
Transformer into both encoder and decoder of the standard U-shaped design
to improve the semantic segmentation quality of varying medical images. Like
other previous Transformer-based technologies, the proposed DS-TransUNet first adopts
dual-scale encoder subnetworks based on Swin Transformer to extract the coarse
and fine-grained feature representations of various semantic scales. As the
core module for our DS-TransUNet, a well-designed Transformer Interactive
Fusion (TIF) module is proposed to quickly establish global dependencies
between features of multiple scales through the self-attention mechanism.
Moreover, we also introduce the Swin Transformer block into decoder to
further explore the long-range semantic information during the up-sampling
procedure. Numerous studies from three typical tasks for medical image
segmentation establish the efficacy of DS-TransUNet, and demonstrate that our
method greatly outperforms the state-of-the-art techniques.

We discuss the development, verification, and performance of GPU
accelerated discontinuous Galerkin method for solutions of two dimensional
nonlinear shallow water equations. The shallow water equations are hyperbolic
partial differential equations and are widely used in the simulation of tsunami
wave propagations. Our algorithms are tailored to take advantage of the single
instruction multiple data (SIMD) architecture of graphic processing units. The
time integration is accelerated by local time stepping based on multi-rate
Adams-Bashforth scheme. A total variational bounded limiter is adopted for
nonlinear stability of the numerical scheme. This limiter is coupled with a
mass and momentum conserving positivity preserving limiter for the special
treatment of dry or partially wet element in triangulation. Accuracy,
robustness and performance are demonstrated with the aid of test cases. We
compare performance of kernels expressed in a portable threading
language OCCA, when cross compiled with OpenCL, CUDA, and OpenMP at runtime.

This paper proposes a novel approach for semantic segmentation of pelvic magnetic resonance (MR) images by combining pyramid pooling and attention mechanism. The pyramid pooling module is used to capture multi-scale contextual information, while the attention mechanism is employed to selectively focus on informative regions. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark dataset for pelvic MR image segmentation, demonstrating its effectiveness in accurately identifying and segmenting different anatomical structures. The results suggest that the combination of pyramid pooling and attention mechanism can significantly improve performance of semantic segmentation models for pelvic MR images.

Cloud based medical image analysis has become popular recently due to the
high computation complexities of various deep neural network (DNN) based
frameworks and the increasingly  large volume of medical images that need to be
processed. It has been  demonstrated that  for medical  images  the transmission
from local to clouds is much  more expensive  than the computation in the clouds
itself.  Towards  this, 3D image compression techniques have been widely applied
to reduce  the  data traffic. However,  most of  the existing image  compression
techniques are developed  around  human vision, i.e.,  they are designed to
minimize distortions that  can  be perceived by  human eyes. In this  paper we  will
use  deep learning based  medical image segmentation as a vehicle and demonstrate
that interestingly, machine and human view  the compression  quality differently.
Medical images compressed with good quality w.r.t.  human vision may result in
inferior segmentation accuracy.  We then design  a machine vision oriented  3D
image compression framework  tailored for segmentation using DNNs. Our  method
automatically extracts and retains image features that are most important to
the segmentation. Comprehensive   experiments on widely adopted segmentation
frameworks with  HVSMR 2016 challenge   dataset show that our method can achieve
significantly higher segmentation accuracy at the same compression rate, or
much better compression rate under the same segmentation accuracy, when
compared with  the existing JPEG 2000 method. To the best of the  authors'
knowledge, this is the first machine vision guided  medical image compression
framework for segmentation  in the   clouds.

In this paper, we study Hamiltonian circle actions on closed symplectic manifolds with minimal isolated fixed points. We prove that if the manifold has a minimal isolated fixed point, then the action must have a global fixed point. We also show that if the manifold has no minimal isolated fixed points, then the action must have a local fixed point.

This paper presents a novel approach for joint shape learning and segmentation of medical images using a minimalistic deep network. The proposed method utilises a U-Net architecture with a small number of trainable parametres, making it efficient and effective for medical image analysis. The approach is evaluated on a diverse set of medical images and demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. The results suggest that the proposed method is a promising solution for medical image segmentation and shape learning tasks.

We consider the question of how to decompose data according to its structure into hierarchical parts where each part is composed of one or more decompositions. We adopt several decomposition techniques, and experiment with a number of configurations on a collection of data elements. We find that, for each structure, decomposition into parts requires a number of steps which, although quite specific, generalize rather well to non-parametric decomposition.

To avoid repeating arguments proposed by others, we provide a new, novel, and succinct framework for decomposing data into nested structures. This framework is well suited to data consisting of a large number of dimensions, where a well-founded hierarchical model provides a convenient formal description of data partitionings.

"ISNet: Integrating Image and Semantic Level Context for Semantic Segmentation" (ISNet), the method proposed by the author of the above paper, as well as the other deep learning methods, aims to integrate both image and semantic level context information, and the method uses a multi-scale feature fusion module to effectively combine features from different layers of the network. It has been verified that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the Cityscapes and Pascal datasets, obtaining higher accuracy of segmentation, proving the validity of the proposed method.

We introduce a new light-field dataset of materials, and take advantage of
the recent success of deep learning to perform material recognition on the 4D
light-field. Our dataset contains 12 material categories, each with 100 images
taken with a Lytro Illum, from which we extract about 30,000 patches in total.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first mid-size dataset for
light-field images. Our main goal is to investigate wether the additional
information in a light-field (such as multiple sub-aperture views and
view-dependent reflectance effects) can aid material recognition. Since
recognition networks have not been trained on 4D images before, we propose and
compare several novel CNN architectures to train on light-field images. In our
experiments, the best performing CNN architecture achieves a 7% boost compared
with 2D image classification (70% to 77%). These results constitute important
baselines that can spur further research in the use of CNNs for light-field
applications. Upon publication, our dataset also enables other novel
applications of light-fields, including object detection, image segmentation
and view interpolation.

> "Semantic   segmentation is fundamental to  many tasks such as semantic parsing, change detection, 3D   reconstruction, image classification  and other scene understanding and synthesis tasks. Most of  the applications listed in the previous sentence typically deal with data that varies in  structure, shape, and appearance, and it  is hard to manually annotate all the  required data, especially in industrial scale  scenarios.  A promising solution to this problem  is  to utilize weakly annotated data where bounding boxes  or points are provided  in place  of  full polygonal labels. In this paper, we propose  Points2Polygons for semantic segmentation  from  bounding   box or point annotations. Points2Polygons is a  two-stage framework:  the first  stage trains a weak annotator using adversarial training to convert weak annotations (bounding boxes or points) to weak  polygonal labels, whereas the second stage   is   a fully convolutional network that utilizes weak polygonal labels for training a  semantic segmentation network. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Points2Polygons  for both bounding  boxes and points, and compare it against   other  weakly supervised learning schemes. Experimental results show that our framework significantly  improves state-of-the-art weakly supervised semantic segmentation results in the presence of bounding boxes. The effectiveness of our method translates to semantic segmentation from  point annotations, resulting  in a state-of-the-art performance."
