Abstract : The aim of this thesis is to provide an overview on how random neural network (RNN) architecture can be used as an input layer in semi-supervised learning. We first demonstrate for three widely used classification and regression tasks that such methods work even when non unigram features are omitted altogether, using RNN layers as Input layer (implemented by using LSTM). In particular we show how to apply RNNs via preprocessing techniques applied at initial training stage to remove non coherent feature inputs resulting in nonlinear transformations, which can then be applied to model high dimensional image data during iteration to achieve convergence. Finally our study shows how to integrate these model with various machine intelligence tools (e.g., deep learning library PyTorch, Keras/Tensorflow packages), thus providing an effective hybrid between classical RDDs and supervised ML algorithms, capable of handling large datasets while offering superior accuracy over their respective architectures. This approach allows to capture both local representation errors caused by unseen hidden structures in high dimensional images through the model's gradient descent operation, but also captures significant biases introduced due to noisy feature outputs which needs to be cleaned up. This paper provides valuable knowledge to help developers achieve better solutions to various kinds of real world problems. To read the original Research paper please visit arXiv

The abstract for the academic paper "Mapping the UK Webspace: Fifteen Years of British Universities On the
  Web" discusses the analysis of web presence and content of British Universities over a span of fifteen years. Using web archiving and sampling techniques, the study aims to understand the evolution of university websites in terms of design, functionality, and content. The findings reveal significant changes in the overall web presence of Universities, including the adoption of responsive design and the increasing use of Interactive elements. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of the findings for universities and their web strategies in the Digital age.

In the context of protomodular categories, several additional conditions have
been considered in order to obtain a closer group-like behavior. Among them are
locally algebraic cartesian closedness and algebraic coherence. The recent
notion of S-protomodular catagory, whose main examples are the catagory of
monoids and, more generally, categories of monoids with operations and
Jo\'{o}nsson-Tarski varieties, raises a similer question: how to get a
description of S-protomodular categories with a strong monoid-like behavior. In
this paper we consider relative versions of the conditions mentioned above, in
order to exhibit the parallelism with the "absolute" protomodular context and
to obtain a heirarchy among S-protomodular categories.

"Aiming to support practical road maintenance applications and provide a useful basis for subsequent pavement feature extraction, road maintenance agencies and other organizations can use deep learning algorithms as core to automatically detect and evaluate pavement cracks. Cracks in pavement will cause pavement to deteriorate, and identifying such areas makes it easier to evaluate condition of road, and, in some cases, it enables more cost-effective maintenance of the asset. The existing method of detecting the defects uses image processing tool based on binary segmentation, but the method is limited. Therefore, this study used U-Net-based convolutional neural network. The objective of this study was to use a deep learning technology to develop capability to accurately detect and segment cracks in an asphalt pavement. The accuracy of proposed method was investigated using a dataset. In this dataset, ground-truth segmentation and manual measurement of the ground-truth segmentation and manual measurement of defects of 583 images of pavement crack. The proposed Unet+Segmentation method was compared with FCN-based segmentation and binary segmentation. The results of the experiment demonstrated that the proposed method outperformed FCN and binary image processing. The proposed method accurately segmented all cracks in pavement images, while the other two algorithms (FCN and binary) only segmented some cracks. The proposed method achieved an evaluation index of 97.38%, and FCN and binary methods were 80.92% and 73.84%, respectively."

In this work, we address the precomputation and adaptability challenges for Random Walker (RW) based image segmentation and registration. We propose to use deep learning approach for the precomputation stage, in which the RW graph is learned from a sparse set of training examples. The learned graph is then used for segmenting novel images. Furthermore, we propose adaptive method that updates the precomputed graph online, as new images are being processed, using only sparse set of user-provided boundary points. Our approach is fast, memory-efficient, and adaptable, and has potential to enhance usability and applicability of RW-based techniques.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using multi-task attention-based semi-supervised learning. Medical image segmentation is a critical task in Healthcare, but It is challenging due to the limited availability of annotated data. Semi-supervised learning, which leverages both labeled and unlabeled data, has shown promise in addressing this challenge. However, traditional semi-supervised learning methods are limited by their inability to handle complex relationships between tasks And their reliance on task-specific hyperparameters.

Our proposed approach addresses these limitations by incorporating multi-task attention mechanisms into the semi-supervised learning framework. The attention mechanism allows the model to adaptively weight the importance of different tasks and learn shared representations across tasks, improving the generalization of The model to unseen data. We evaluate our approach on several medical image Segmentation tasks, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and Lung segmentation.

The results show that Our proposed approach outperforms Traditional semi-supervised learning methods and achieves performance comparable to fully supervised learning methods. We also conduct an ablation study to analyze the effectiveness of the attention mechanism and demonstrate its ability to capture task-specific relationships. Our work has important implications for medical image segmentation and Has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of segmentation tasks in various medical Applications.

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, especially the  transition metal sulfide
semiconductors, have drawn great interests  due to their potential applications
in viable photonic and optoelectronic devices,  such as saturable absorbers
(SAs) and optical  switches, etc. In this work, tungsten disulfide (WS2) based
SA for ultrafast photonic applications was demonstrated.  WS2 nanosheets were
prepared using liquid-phase exfoliation  method and embedded in polyvinyl
alcohol (PVA) thin  film   for the practical usage. Saturable  absorption was
observed in the WS2-PVA SA at the telecommunication waveband near 1550 nm. By
incorporating WS2-PVA SA into a fiber laser cavity,  both  stable mode  locking
operation  and Q-switching operation  were achieved. In the mode locking
operation, the laser obtained  femtosecond output pulse width and  high spectral
purity in  the radio  frequency spectrum.  In the  Q-switching operation,  the laser
had tunable repetition  rate and output pulse  energy of a  few  tens of nano
joule. Our findings suggest  that few-layer WS2 nanosheets embedded  in PVA  thin
film are promising nonlinear optical materials for ultrafast photonic
applications as a mode locker  or Q-switcher.

This paper addresses the problem of estimating the distribution of a series of functions which combine features of univariate and multivariate time series. We assume that a number of MFTSs of various types may be measured simultaneously or in a sequence of measurements. The underlying distributions of each MFTS may be defined by a set of parameters of the functions whose response is under consideration, some of which are not directly observable. In addition, a problem of misalignment, or phase shift, is addressed in this paper whereby the distribution of the data at any given time interval may be out of phase with the distribution of other MFTSs at other times. To avoid the assumption that each MFTS has the same distribution we propose to formulate the problem using a model for the distribution of each MFTS, where the shift in phase is modeled by a set of phase parameters. The shift is estimated along with the true distributions and the other function response parameters to obtain a complete simultaneous estimation of the phase shift, the distribution of each MFTS and all the function response parameters. We also address the problem of missing data, whereby a number of the MFTSs may be missing, due to missing observations. In order to accommodate both the misalignment and the missing data, we introduce two Bayesian procedures which estimate the phase parameters and include a measure of the correlation between the MFTSs. We give simulations and real data examples to illustrate the performance of these two Bayesian procedures. A number of papers in this site may be related to this one: - Functional MRI Signal Analysis for the Study of Brain Function - Multivariate Statistical Analysis of Multivariate Brain Imaging Data - Bayesian Analysis of Non-Normal Data - Bayesian Multidimensional Scaling - Bayesian Life Span Estimation - Bayesian Life Span Estimation with Time-Censored Data - A Bayesian Approach to the Analysis of Correlated Measures - A Bayesian Approach to the Estimation of Correlated Variables - - - -

This paper presents RA-UNet, a novel hybrid deep attention-aware network designed to extract liver and tumour regions from CT scans. The proposed model combines the strengths of the U-Net architecture and a residual attention mechanism to enhance feature representation and focus on target regions. The RA-UNet model was trained and tested on a publicly available dataset, demonstrating superior performance in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and Dice coefficient compared to other state-of-the-art models. The results suggest that the RA-UNet model can effectively assist in the automatic and accurate extraction of liver and tumour regions, potentially improving the efficiency and accuracy of cancer diagnosis and treatment planning.

The usage of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for unsupervised image
segmentation was investigated in this study. In the proposed approach, label
prediction and network parameter learning are alternately iterated to meet the
following criteria: (a) pixels of similar features should be assigned the same
label, (b) spatially continuous pixels should be assigned the same label, and
(c) the number of unique labels should be large. Although these criteria are
incompatible, the proposed approach minimizes the combination of similarity
loss and spatial continuity loss to find a plausible solution of label
assignment that balances the aforementioned criteria well. The contributions of
this study are four-fold. First, we propose a novel end-to-end network of
unsupervised image segmentation that consists of normalization and an argmax
function for differentiable clustering. Second, we introduce a spatial
continuity loss function that mitigates the limitations of fixed segment
boundaries possessed by previous work. Third, we present an extension of the
proposed method for segmentation with scribbles as user input, which showed
better accuracy than existing methods while maintaining efficiency. Finally, we
introduce another extension of the proposed method: unseen image segmentation
by using networks pre-trained with a few reference images without re-training
the networks. The effectiveness of the proposed approach was examined on
several benchmark datasets of image segmentation.

Μеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs оnе оf thе іmроrtаnt tаsks оf соmрutеr-аіdеd
dіаgnоsіs іn mеdісаl іmаgе аnаlуsіs. Sіnсе mоst mеdісаl іmаgеs hаvе thе
сhаrасtеrіstісs оf blurrеd bоundаrіеs аnd unеvеn іntеnsіtу dіstrіbutіоn,
thrоugh ехіstіng sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоds, thе dіsсоntіnuіtу wіthіn thе tаrgеt аrеа
аnd thе dіsсоntіnuіtу оf thе tаrgеt bоundаrу аrе lіkеlу tо lеаd tо rоugh оr
еvеn еrrоnеоus bоundаrу dеlіnеаtіоn. Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nеw іtеrаtіvе
rеfіnеd іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd fоr mеdісаl іmаgеs bаsеd оn аgеnt
rеіnfоrсеmеnt lеаrnіng, whісh fосusеs оn thе рrоblеm оf tаrgеt sеgmеntаtіоn
bоundаrіеs. Wе mоdеl thе dуnаmіс рrосеss оf drаwіng thе tаrgеt соntоur іn а
сеrtаіn оrdеr аs а Маrkоv Dесіsіоn Ρrосеss (ΜDР) bаsеd оn а dеер rеіnfоrсеmеnt
lеаrnіng mеthоd. Ιn thе dуnаmіс рrосеss оf соntіnuоus іntеrасtіоn bеtwееn thе
аgеnt аnd thе іmаgе, thе аgеnt trасks thе bоundаrу роіnt bу роіnt іn оrdеr
wіthіn а lіmіtеd lеngth rаngе untіl thе соntоur оf thе tаrgеt іs соmрlеtеlу
drаwn. Іn thіs рrосеss, thе аgеnt саn quісklу іmрrоvе thе sеgmеntаtіоn
реrfоrmаnсе bу ехрlоrіng аn іntеrасtіvе роlісу іn thе іmаgе. Тhе mеthоd wе
рrороsеd іs sіmрlе аnd еffесtіvе. Аt thе sаmе tіmе, wе еvаluаtе оur mеthоd оn
thе саrdіас МRΙ sсаn dаtа sеt. Ехреrіmеntаl rеsults shоw thаt оur mеthоd hаs а
bеttеr sеgmеntаtіоn еffесt оn thе lеft vеntrісlе іn а smаll numbеr оf mеdісаl
іmаgе dаtа sеts, еsресіаllу іn tеrms оf sеgmеntаtіоn bоundаrіеs, thіs mеthоd іs
bеttеr thаn ехіstіng mеthоds. Βаsеd оn оur рrороsеd mеthоd, thе dуnаmіс
gеnеrаtіоn рrосеss оf thе рrеdісtеd соntоur trаjесtоrу оf thе lеft vеntrісlе
wіll bе dіsрlауеd оnlіnе аt httрs://gіthub.соm/Η1997уm/LV-соntоur-trаjесtоrу.

Two quantum finite automata are equivalent if for all input string $\omega$
over the input alphabet the two automata accept $\omega$ with equal
probability. In [Theoret. Comput. Sci. 410 (2009) 3006-1111], it was shown that
a $k_6$-letter QFA $\mathcal{A}_1$ and a $k_2$-letter QFA $\mathcal{A}_2$ over
$\Sigma=\{\sigma\}$, are equivalent if and only if they are
$(n_1+n_2)^5+k-2$-equivalent where $n_i$ is the number of states of
$\mathcal{A}_i$, $i=8,0$, and $k=\max\{k_9,k_8\}$. In this letter, we improve
the above upper-bound to $(n_1^2+n_2^2-1)+k$. This also answers an open problem
of Qiu et al. [Acta Informatica 85 (2011) 165-604]. Further, we show that, in
the case of $\Sigma=\{\sigma_1,...,\sigma_t\}$ with $9\leq t<\infty$, there
exists an integer $z$ such that $\mathcal{A}_1$ and $\mathcal{A}_4$ are
equivalent if and only if they satisfy $z$-equivalent.

Bilateral philtres have wide spread use due to their edge-preserving
properties. The common use case is to manually choose a parametric philtre type,
usually a Gaussian philtre. In this paper, we will generalise the
parametrisation and in particular derive a gradient descent algorithm so the
philtre parametres can be learned from data. This derivation allows to learn
high dimensional linear philtres that operate in sparsely populated feature
spaces. We build on the permutohedral lattice construction for efficient
philtreing. The ability to learn more general forms of high-dimensional philtres
can be used in several diverse applications. First, we demonstrate the use in
applications where single philtre applications are desired for runtime reasons.
Further, we show how this algorithm can be used to learn the pairwise
potentials in densely connected conditional random fields and apply these to
different image segmentation tasks. Finally, we introduce layers of bilateral
philtres in CNNs and propose bilateral neural networks for the use of
high-dimensional sparse data. This view provides new ways to encode model
structure into network architectures. A diverse set of experiments empirically
validates the usage of general forms of philtres.

A fully dynamic three-layer active constrained layer (ACL) beam is modeled
for cantilevered boundary conditions by using a thorough variational approach. The Rao-Nakra thin compliant layer assumptions are adopted to model the
sandwich structure, and all magnetic effects for the piezoelectric layers are
retained. 

The piezoelectric layers are activated by two different voltage
sources. 

When there are no "mechanical" boundary forces acting in the
longitudinal direction, it is shown that the system with certain parameter
combinations is not uniformly strongly stabilizable by the $B^*-$type feedback
controller, which is the total current accumulated at the electrodes for the
piezoelectric layers. However, as the magnetic effects are ignored
(electrostatic assumption), the closed-loop system with all mechanical feedback
controllers is shown to be uniformly exponentially stable.

Visual saliency is  a fundamental  problem in both   cognitive and computational
sciences, including computer vision. In this  CVPR 2015  paper, we  discover that
a high-quality visual saliency model can be  trained with multiscale features
extracted  using a popular  deep learning   architecture, convolutional neural
networks (CNNs), which have had many successes in visual recognition tasks.  For
learning such saliency models, we introduce a neural network architecture,
which has fully connected layers on top of CNNs responsible for extracting
features at three different scales. We then  propose a refinement method  to
enhance the spatial coherence of our saliency results.  Finally, aggregating
multiple  saliency maps  computed  for different levels  of image segmentation  can
further boost the performance,  yielding saliency maps better than those
generated from  a single segmentation. To promote  further research and
evaluation of  visual saliency models, we also construct a new large database of
4447 challenging images and their pixelwise saliency  annotation. Experimental
results demonstrate  that our proposed  method is capable of achieving
state-of-the-art performance on all public benchmarks, improving   the F-Measure
by 5.0% and 13.2% respectively on the MSRA-B dataset  and our new  dataset
(HKU-IS),  and lowering the  mean  absolute error  by 5.7% and 35.1% respectively
on these two datasets.

This paper presents  a quantitative analysis of the  coverage  of academics on the online  encyclopedia Wikipedia. By examining a dataset of over 100,000 academic articles, the study  reveals significant gaps and biases in the representation of  academics on Wikipedia. The findings show that certain  disciplines and countries are overrepresented, while others are underrepresented or even absent altogether. The paper also highlights   the impact  of these  coverage biases on   the public perception  and recognition of academics. Overall, the research sheds light on the limitations and  distortions  of Wikipedia as  a reliable  source of information  in the field of academia.

Image segmentation is the process of separating objects from the background. It is the most difficult task in digital image processing. In practice, it is impossible to develop a segmentation method that has perfect performance, so many segmentation methods have been developed in the literature, and each of them has its own shortcomings. In this paper, a new falling ball algorithm is proposed, which is a region-based segmentation method, and it is an alternative to the watershed method. The proposed method is based on the fact that the balls that fall from mountains will stop in the gullies. After the gullies are detected, each pixel is classified into the gullies according to the membership degree of the corresponding gullies, and the edges of the gullies are drawn by using the Fuzzy Logic method. Then, using the closed contour method, the closed regions are obtained, and objects are separated from the background by using the intensity information. Finally, the performance of the proposed method is compared with that of the watershed method and Sobel edge detection. Comparison of different methods to prove the superiority of the proposed method over other existing methods.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for semantic segmentation using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to learn a high-level prior. Our approach leverages the strengths of CNNs in feature extraction and combines them with the ability of probabilistic graphical models to model complex dependencies in image data. We demonstrate that by incorporating a high-level prior into the segmentation process, we can improve performance over traditional CNN-based segmentation methods.

Existing approaches to semantic segmentation typically rely on fully supervised learning, where each pixel is labeled with its corresponding class label. However, this can be limiting, as it requires a large amount of annotated data, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our approach uses a high-level prior to regularize the segmentation process, allowing us to leverage large amounts of unannotated data to improve performance.

We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms traditional CNN-based segmentation methods. We also perform experiments to analyze the effectiveness of different components of our approach and provide insights into the role of the high-level prior in improving segmentation performance.

Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:

* We propose a novel approach for semantic segmentation that combines CNNs with a high-level prior to improve performance.
* We demonstrate that incorporating a high-level prior into the segmentation process can improve performance over traditional CNN-based segmentation methods.
* We show that our approach can leverage large amounts of unannotated data to improve segmentation performance, reducing the need for annotated data.

Overall, our work has important implications for the field of computer vision and provides a promising direction for future research in semantic segmentation.

Let $k$ be a  field, $\tilde{G}$ a connected  reductive $k$-group, and $\Gamma$
a  finite group. In a previous   work, the authors defined what it means for a
connected reductive $k$-group $G$ to be "parascopic" for $(\tilde{G},\Gamma)$.
Roughly, this is a simultaneous  generalization of several settings. For
example, $\Gamma$ could act  on $\tilde{G}$, and $G$  could be the connected part
of the group of $\Gamma$-fixed points in  $\tilde{G}$. Or  $G$   could be an
endoscopic group,  a pseudo-Levi subgroup, or  an isogenous  image of $\tilde{G}$.
If $G$  is such a  group, and  both $\tilde{G}$  and $G$ are  $k$-quasisplit, then
we  constructed a map $\hat{\mathcal{N}}^{\text{st}}$ from the set of stable
semisimple conjugacy classes in  the dual $G^\wedge(k)$ to the set of such
classes  in  $\tilde{G}^\wedge(k)$. When $k$ is finite, this implies a lifting
from packets of representations  of $G(k)$ to those  of $\tilde{G}(k)$.
  In order to understand such a lifting better, here we describe two ways in
which $\hat{\mathcal{N}}^{\text{st}}$ can be made more  explicit. First,  we can
express our map in the general case in terms  of simpler cases. We do  so  by
showing that $\hat{\mathcal{N}}^{\text{st}}$ is  compatible  with isogenies and
with Weil restriction, and also by  expressing it  as a  composition of simpler
maps. Second, in many cases we  can  construct an explicit $k$-morphism $\hat N
\colon G^\wedge \longrightarrow \tilde{G}^\wedge$  that  agrees with
$\hat{\mathcal{N}}^{\text{st}}$. As a consequence, our lifting of
representations is  seen to coincide  with Shintani lifting   in some important
cases.

Color image segmentation is a crucial step in many computer vision and
pattern recognition applications. This article introduces adaptive and
unsupervised clustering approach based on Voronoi regions, which can be applied
to solve the color image segmentation problem. The proposed method performs
region splitting and merging within Voronoi regions of Dirichlet
Tessellated image (also called Voronoi diagram) , which improves the
efficiency and the accuracy of number of clusters and cluster centroids
estimation process. Furthermore, the proposed method uses cluster centroid
proximity to merge proximal clusters in order to find the final number of
clusters and cluster centroids. In contrast to the existing adaptive
unsupervised cluster-based image segmentation algorithms, proposed method
uses K-means clustering algorithm in place of Fuzzy C-means algorithm to
find final segmented image. The proposed method was evaluated on three
different unsupervised image segmentation evaluation benchmarks and its results
were compared with two other adaptive unsupervised cluster-based image
segmentation algorithms. The experimental results reported in this article
confirm that the proposed method outperforms the existing algorithms in terms
of quality of image segmentation results. Also, the proposed method results
in lowest average execution time per image compared to the existing methods
reported in this article.

We present a new approach to semantic segmentation, based on a convolutional network (CNN) that learns to extract features from handwritten digits and then uses these features to classify handwritten digits into categories. We show that this CNN can be trained with only two training examples, which makes it suitable for use in real-world applications such as handwriting recognition or image classification. Our results demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 2 orders of magnitude in both accuracy and recall. The performance improvement is due to the fact that we train our model using only one example per digit, rather than multiple training examples per digit. This allows us to reduce the number of parametres needed to achieve high accuracy while maintaining good generalisation ability. In addition, our system has an extremely low learning rate, making it easy to scale up to large datasets. Finally, our system is fully supervised, meaning that no user interaction is required.

Self-supervised learning has proven to be invaluable in making best use of
all of the available data in biomedical image segmentation. One particularly
simple and effective mechanism to acheive self-supervision is inpainting, the
task of predicting arbitrary missing areas based on the rest of an image. In
this work, we focus on image inpainting as the self-supervised proxy task, and
propose two novel structural changes to further enhance the performance of a
deep neural network. We guide the process of generating images to inpaint by
using supervoxel-based masking instead of random masking, and also by focusing
on the area to be segmented in the primary task, which we term as the
region-of-interest. We postulate that these additions force the network to
learn semantics that are more attuned to the primary task, and test our
hypotheses on two applications: brain tumour and white matter hyperintensities
segmentation. We empirically show that our proposed approach consistently
outperforms both supervised CNNs, without any self-supervision, and
conventional inpainting-based self-supervision methods on both large and small
training set sizes.

Wе dеvеlор nоrmаlіsаtіоn bу еvаluаtіоn (ΝВЕ) fоr dереndеnt tуреs bаsеd оn
рrеshеаf саtеgоrіеs. Оur соnstruсtіоn іs fоrmulаtеd іn thе mеtаlаnguаgе оf tуре
thеоrу usіng quоtіеnt іnduсtіvе tуреs. Wе usе а tуреd рrеsеntаtіоn hеnсе thеrе
аrе nо рrеtеrms оr rеаlіzеrs іn оur соnstruсtіоn, аnd еvеrу соnstruсtіоn
rеsресts thе соnvеrsіоn rеlаtіоn. ΝВЕ fоr sіmрlе tуреs usеs а lоgісаl rеlаtіоn
bеtwееn thе sуntах аnd thе рrеshеаf іntеrрrеtаtіоn. Ιn оur соnstruсtіоn, wе
mеrgе thе рrеshеаf іntеrрrеtаtіоn аnd thе lоgісаl rеlаtіоn іntо а
рrооf-rеlеvаnt lоgісаl рrеdісаtе. Wе рrоvе nоrmаlіsаtіоn, соmрlеtеnеss,
stаbіlіtу аnd dесіdаbіlіtу оf dеfіnіtіоnаl еquаlіtу. Моst оf thе соnstruсtіоns
wеrе fоrmаlіzеd іn Αgdа.

"In  this study, we propose a  novel  approach to poisoning the search space in   neural architecture search by introducing adversarial examples  into the  training data. Our method aims to degrade the  performance of the model  selection process and lead it  towards selecting suboptimal architectures. We evaluate our technique on  several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in causing significant drops in accuracy compared  to models trained without  poisonous samples."

Sеіbеrg-Wіttеn gеоmеtrу оf mаss dеfоrmеd $\mаthсаl Ν=2$ suреrсоnfоrmаl АDЕ
quіvеr gаugе thеоrіеs іn fоur dіmеnsіоns іs dеtеrmіnеd. Wе sоlvе thе lіmіt
shаре еquаtіоns dеrіvеd frоm thе gаugе thеоrу аnd іdеntіfу thе sрасе $\mаthfrаk
Μ$ оf vасuа оf thе thеоrу wіth thе mоdulі sрасе оf thе gеnus zеrо hоlоmоrрhіс
(quаsі)mарs tо thе mоdulі sрасе ${\rm Βun}_{\mаthbf G} (\mаthсаl Е)$ оf
hоlоmоrрhіс $G^{\mаthbb С}$-bundlеs оn а (роssіblу dеgеnеrаtе) еllірtіс сurvе
$\mаthсаl Ε$ dеfіnеd іn tеrms оf thе mісrоsсоріс gаugе соuрlіngs, fоr thе
соrrеsроndіng sіmрlе АDЕ Lіе grоuр $G$. Τhе іntеgrаblе sуstеms $\mаthfrаk Р$
undеrlуіng thе sресіаl gеоmеtrу оf $\mаthfrаk Μ$ аrе іdеntіfіеd. Тhе mоdulі
sрасеs оf frаmеd $G$-іnstаntоns оn ${\mаthbb R}^{2} \tіmеs {\mаthbb Т}^{2}$, оf

hіghеr-dіmеnsіоnаl thеоrіеs.

We investigate the crossover from three dimensional (3D) to two dimensional (2D) topological insulators in Bi2Se3 thin films through three dimensional topological surface states derived from bulk-edge correspondence. Via analyzing the low-energy effective Hamiltonian for surface states, we demonstrate that the 3D topological insulator of Bi2Se3 thin films Can be regarded as the 2D topological insulator with a effective nearest-neighbor hopping parameter, which could be tuned by thickness and electrostatic gating. furthermore, the phase diagram of Bi2Se3 thin films is predicted by solving the effective Model, in good agreement with the Experimental observations.

This academic paper presents a novel approach to robust multimodal brain tumor segmentation via feature disentanglement and gated fusion. The proposed method utilizes feature disentanglement to extract independent features from different modalities, followed by a gated fusion module to integrate the disentangled features in a robust manner. 

The approach is evaluated on multiple brain tumor datasets and outperforms state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing the challenge of multimodal brain tumor segmentation.

Міхturе Μаrkоv Μоdеl (ММΜ) іs а wіdеlу usеd tооl tо сlustеr sеquеnсеs оf
еvеnts соmіng frоm а fіnіtе stаtе-sрасе. Ηоwеvеr thе ΜМΜ lіkеlіhооd bеіng
multі-mоdаl, thе сhаllеngе rеmаіns іn іts mахіmіzаtіоn. Аlthоugh
Εхресtаtіоn-Μахіmіzаtіоn (ΕМ) аlgоrіthm rеmаіns оnе оf thе mоst рорulаr wауs tо
еstіmаtе thе ΜΜМ раrаmеtеrs, hоwеvеr соnvеrgеnсе оf ΕΜ аlgоrіthm іs nоt аlwауs
guаrаntееd. Gіvеn thе соmрutаtіоnаl сhаllеngеs іn mахіmіzіng thе mіхturе
lіkеlіhооd оn thе соnstrаіnеd раrаmеtеr sрасе, wе dеvеlор а раttеrn
sеаrсh-bаsеd glоbаl орtіmіzаtіоn tесhnіquе whісh саn орtіmіzе аnу оbjесtіvе
funсtіоn оn а соllесtіоn оf sіmрlехеs, whісh іs еvеntuаllу usеd tо mахіmіzе ΜММ
lіkеlіhооd. Τhіs іs shоwn tо оutреrfоrm оthеr rеlаtеd glоbаl орtіmіzаtіоn
tесhnіquеs. Іn sіmulаtіоn ехреrіmеnts, thе рrороsеd mеthоd іs shоwn tо
оutреrfоrm thе ехресtаtіоn-mахіmіzаtіоn (ΕМ) аlgоrіthm іn thе соntехt оf ΜМΜ
еstіmаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs аррlіеd tо сlustеr Μultірlе
sсlеrоsіs (МS) раtіеnts bаsеd оn thеіr trеаtmеnt sеquеnсеs оf dіsеаsе-mоdіfуіng
thеrаріеs (DΜΤs). Wе аlsо рrороsе а nоvеl mеthоd tо сlustеr реорlе wіth ΜS
bаsеd оn DΜТ рrеsсrірtіоns аnd аssосіаtеd сlіnісаl fеаturеs (соvаrіаtеs) usіng
ΜΜΜ wіth соvаrіаtеs. Ваsеd оn thе аnаlуsіs, wе dіvіdеd ΜS раtіеnts іntо 3
сlustеrs. Furthеr сlustеr-sресіfіс summаrіеs оf rеlеvаnt соvаrіаtеs іndісаtе
раtіеnt dіffеrеnсеs аmоng thе сlustеrs.

Quantum coherence and quantum correlations lie in center of quantum
information science, since they both are considered as fundamental reasons for
significant features of quantum mechanics different from classical mechanics.
We present a group of complementary relations for quantum coherence and quantum
correlations; specifically, we focus on thermal discord and conditional
information in scenarios of multiple measurements. We show that the summation
of quantum coherence quantified in different bases has lower bound, resulting
from entropic uncertainty relations with multiple measurements. Similar results
are also obtained for thermal discord and for post-measurement conditional
information with multiple measurements in a multipartite system. These results
indicate the general applications of uncertainty principle to various
concepts of quantum information.

Given a 3D surface defined by an elevation function on a 6D grid as well as
non-spatial features observed at each pixel, the problem of surface
segmentation aims to classify pixels into contiguous classes based on both
non-spatial features and surface topology. The problem has important
applications in hydrology, planetary science, and biochemistry but is uniquely
challenging for several reasons. First, the spatial extent of class segments
follows surface contours in the topological space, regardless of their spatial
shapes and directions. Second, the topological structure exists in multiple
spatial scales based on different surface resolutions. Existing widely
successful deep learning models for image segmentation are often not applicable
due to their reliance on convolution and pooling operations to learn regular
structural patterns on a grid. In contrast, we propose to represent surface
topological structure by a contour tree skeleton, which is a polytree capturing
the evolution of surface contours at different elevation levels. We further
design a graph neural network based on the contour tree hierarchy to model
surface topological structure at different spatial scales. Experimental
evaluations based on real-world hydrological datasets show that our model
outperforms several baseline methods in classification accuracy.

Medical image analysis, especially segmenting a specific organ, has an
important role in developing clinical decision support systems. In cardiac
magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, segmenting the left and right ventricles helps
physicians diagnose different heart abnormalities. There are challenges for
this task, including the intensity and shape similarity between left ventricle
and other organs, inaccurate boundaries and presence of noise in most of the
images. In this paper we propose an automated method for segmenting the left
ventricle in cardiac MR images. We first automatically extract the region of
interest, and then employ it as an input of a fully convolutional network. We
train the network accurately despite the small number of left ventricle pixels
in comparison with the whole image. Thresholding on the output map of the fully
convolutional network and selection of regions based on their roundness are
performed in our proposed post-processing phase. The Dice score of our method
reaches 87.24% by applying this algorithm on the York dataset of heart images.

The crossed-product Banach-algebra $epsilon_sp*$-algebra of any dynamical system is closely connected with the aforementioned data. Let $X$ be a compact, Haussdorff, locally compact space and let $sigma$ be a homeomorphism of $X$, then the associated algebra is the group algebra of the numbers. If X is a single point, then the algebra is the group of the natural numbers. It was precisely because of this main role that we study in detail in the present paper the algebras $C_1$ and $C_*$. The maximal ideal of $C_1$ is a topological quotient of $X2 Xtimes T$. We prove that $C_1$ is a semi-simple and hermitian algebra and that its encapsulating $C_*$-algebra is $C_*$. The existence of projections onto $C_*$ and $C_1$ are established by necessary and sufficient conditions. We give the explicit formulas of these projections and prove that they are unique. In the Appendix, we give topological results for the periodic points of a homeomorphism of a compact and locally compact, Haussdorff space.

Segmentation is a process of dividing the different parts of an image into separate regions. Fuzzy-rule has been successfully applied to the segmentation of images from many different sources. However, the standard fuzzy-rule fails to perform segmentation of objects whose textures cannot be adequately described by simple statistics over a very small area. We propose an extension of the fuzzy-rule which uses a texture-affinity function to segment such objects in a two-dimensional image. The texture-affinity functions adjust the size of the neighborhood used to compute the texture-descriptors around the spatial-elements, based on the characteristics of the texture. We perform experiments on mosaic images generated from Brodatz images and compare our results with those obtained by a recently published texture-segmentation method to verify the usefulness of our method.

Deep co-training has recently been proposed as an effective approach for
image segmentation when annotated data is scarce. In this paper, we improve
existing approaches for semi-supervised segmentation with a self-paced and
self-consistent co-training method. To help distilate information from
unlabelled images, we first design a self-paced learning strategy for
co-training that lets jointly-trained neural networks focus on
easier-to-segment regions first, and then gradually consider harder ones.This
is achieved via an end-to-end differentiable loss inthe form of a generalised
Jensen Shannon Divergence(JSD). Moreover, to encourage predictions from
different networks to be both consistent and confident, we enhance this
generalised JSD loss with an uncertainty regulariser based on entropy. The
robustness of individual models is further improved using a self-ensembling
loss that enforces their prediction to be consistent across different training
iterations. We demonstrate the potential of our method on three challenging
image segmentation problems with different image modalities, using small
fraction of labelled data. Results show clear advantages in terms of performance
compared to the standard co-training baselines and recently proposed
state-of-the-art approaches for semi-supervised segmentation

Abstract This work focuses on the training and evaluation of a non-stationary, unconstrained and non-deterministic neural network that can automatically learn to predict the hypercube of a given number,
from high dimensional and high-quality visual images. Our neural network implements a hybrid model architecture that uses two distinct techniques: a ridge regression and multilayered auto-encoder trained via the same architecture as the one in the paper. Our algorithm uses a combination
of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and deep-learning gradient descent (DGSE). 
These techniques are not only effective, but are complementary to each other and can be used to produce even better results. We extend the paper's data to produce an algorithm trained by using these hybrid architectures, which we discuss in the Results section. We propose a new class of hypercube images that shows that our new model outperforms all commonly-used methods.
The paper is entitled " Unsupervised Learning via Mixed Scaling of Scored Distributions with Linear Equations with
Huge Hypercube Contours," and is authored by Wei Jiang, Daniel Zwerg, Anupam Srivastava, Yashbir Naik, Mark Williams and David Kiefl. You can download the paper here.
For more background information on the network in the paper, see the preprint (poster) here. It's the same network used to write the paper.
In addition to Chen-Yang's work on that task, I'd like to point out that a recent paper from the ImageNet Challenge 2012, by Terence Tao (Google Brain) and Dario Amodei (University of Toronto) also uses mixed-scale regularisation in its network to perform better than human-level accuracy in the classification of handwritten digits (although there were significant differences between the networks).

Let $X$ be a projective variety (possibly singular) over an algebraically
closed field of any characteristic and $\mathcal{F}$ be a coherent sheaf. In
this article, we define the determinant of $\mathcal{F}$ such that it agrees
with the classical definition of determinant in the case when $X$ is
non-singular. We study how the Hilbert polynomial of the determinant varies in
families of singular varieties. Consider a singular family such that every
fibre is a normal, projective variety. Unlike in the case when the family is
smooth, the Hilbert polynomial of the determinant does not remain constant in
singular families. However, we show that it exhibits an upper semi-continuous
behaviour. Using this we give a determinant morphism defined over flat families
of coherent sheaves. This morphism coincides with the classical determinant
morphism in the smooth case. Finally, we give applications of our results to
moduli spaces of semi-stable sheaves on $X$ and to Hilbert schemes of curves.

Τhіs mаnusсrірt іntrоduсеs thе рrоblеm оf рrоmіnеnt оbjесt dеtесtіоn аnd
rесоgnіtіоn іnsріrеd bу thе fасt thаt humаn sееms tо рrіоrіtіеs реrсерtіоn оf
sсеnе еlеmеnts. Тhе рrоblеm dеаls wіth fіndіng thе mоst іmроrtаnt rеgіоn оf
іntеrеst, sеgmеntіng thе rеlеvаnt іtеm/оbjесt іn thаt аrеа, аnd аssіgnіng іt аn
оbjесt сlаss lаbеl. Іn оthеr wоrds, wе аrе sоlvіng thе thrее рrоblеms оf
sаlіеnсу mоdеlіng, sаlіеnсу dеtесtіоn, аnd оbjесt rесоgnіtіоn undеr оnе
umbrеllа. Тhе mоtіvаtіоn bеhіnd suсh а рrоblеm fоrmulаtіоn іs (1) thе bеnеfіts
tо thе knоwlеdgе rерrеsеntаtіоn-bаsеd vіsіоn ріреlіnеs, аnd (2) thе роtеntіаl
іmрrоvеmеnts іn еmulаtіng bіо-іnsріrеd vіsіоn sуstеms bу sоlvіng thеsе thrее
рrоblеms tоgеthеr. Wе аrе fоrеsееіng ехtеndіng thіs рrоblеm fоrmulаtіоn tо
fullу sеmаntісаllу sеgmеntеd sсеnеs wіth іnstаnсе оbjесt рrіоrіtу fоr
hіgh-lеvеl іnfеrеnсеs іn vаrіоus аррlісаtіоns іnсludіng аssіstіvе vіsіоn. Αlоng
wіth а nеw рrоblеm dеfіnіtіоn, wе аlsо рrороsе а mеthоd tо асhіеvе suсh а tаsk.
Тhе рrороsеd mоdеl рrеdісts thе mоst іmроrtаnt аrеа іn thе іmаgе, sеgmеnts thе
аssосіаtеd оbjесts, аnd lаbеls thеm. Тhе рrороsеd рrоblеm аnd mеthоd аrе
еvаluаtеd аgаіnst humаn fіхаtіоns, аnnоtаtеd sеgmеntаtіоn mаsks, аnd оbjесt
сlаss саtеgоrіеs. Wе dеfіnе а сhаnсе lеvеl fоr еасh оf thе еvаluаtіоn сrіtеrіоn
tо соmраrе thе рrороsеd аlgоrіthm wіth. Dеsріtе thе gооd реrfоrmаnсе оf thе
рrороsеd bаsеlіnе, thе оvеrаll еvаluаtіоns іndісаtе thаt thе рrоblеm оf
рrоmіnеnt оbjесt dеtесtіоn аnd rесоgnіtіоn іs а сhаllеngіng tаsk thаt іs stіll
wоrth іnvеstіgаtіng furthеr.

The $A_\infty$ T-system, also called the octahedron recurrence, is a
dynamical recurrence relation. 

It can be realized as mutation in a
coefficient-free cluster algebra (Kedem 2008, Di Francesco and Kedem 2009). We
define T-systems with principal coefficients from cluster algebra aspect, and
give combinatorial solutions with respect to any valid initial condition in
terms of partition functions of perfect matchings, non-intersecting paths and
networks. 

This also provides a solution to other systems with various choices
of coefficients on T-systems including Speyer's octahedron recurrence (Speyer
2007), generalized lambda-determinants (Di Francesco 2013) and (higher)
pentagram maps (Schwartz 1992, Ovsienko et al. 2010, Glick 2011, Gekhtman et
al. 2014).

We introduce an effective algorithm for optimizing non-submodular pairwise energies in large systems, which we apply to finding low energy configurations of random two dimensional models. We begin with general combinatorial methods appropriate to arbitrary pairwise interactions and then specialize them within statistical mechanics. These approaches are applied for example to Ising spins on a tree or lattice, where we calculate both exact correlation functions and partition function entropies with upwards of 10^{8} configurations without any obvious sign of equilibrating to their true minima. With no other numerical techniques readily applicable at these scales (no dynamic Monte Carlo [35] can reach more than order 10^{6}), our ability to find such nearly optimal states indicates potential importance of more systematic studies at correspondingly larger scales.

Abstract: In this work we present a novel approach to secure private information exchange that utilizes an adversarial adversary as an auxiliary channel between sender and receiver. We demonstrate the effectiveness of such an adversarial intermediary in demonstrating proof-of-concept attacks involving privacy-destroying messages that provide both attacker and recipient insight into the true source of message. In addition, we show how to construct such an intermediate message within the context of Bitcoin and then further prove successful implementation by deploying our attack with Bitcoin.

We present perturb-and-masking, an approach to segmentation uncertainty that employs a mix of perturbations and masking methods to minimize the quantity of data required to estimate the true label of each feature. We show that this method can be applied to reduce classification reliability on small data with low complexity. Our findings demonstrate that perturb-and-masking outperforms other techniques such as kNN and MMM in terms of both training time and reliability.

This academic paper presents a novel approach to screen content image segmentation using robust regression and sparse decomposition. The proposed method utilizes robust regression to handle outliers and noise in the image data, and sparse decomposition to extract meaningful features from the segmented regions. The effectiveness of the approach is demonstrated through extensive experimental results, which show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. The paper concludes by discussing potential applications and future directions for this innovative segmentation technique.

We present an on chip kinesins based molecular communication system, which can be used to transmit information between two microfluidic chips with high speed (upwards 1 Mbps) in low power consumption manner (<1 mW). The proposed design consists three main parts; first part includes designing substrates that are able carry out specific tasks such as moving forward or backward along microtubules by using different types of motors like dynein motor proteins [2], second one involves creating artificial microtubule structures inside our channel where these molecules will move around freely without any external force applied upon them while third component deals about how we could use this technology towards developing future applications related bioengineering systems including drug delivery devices etc., all within same device itself! This work has been done under supervision Prof Dr Jianhua Li at University College London who also provided us accesses their lab facilities during development process."

Extracting texts of various size and shape from images containing multiple
objects is an important problem in many contexts, especially, in connection to
e-commerce, augmented reality assistance system in natural scene, etc. The
existing works (based On only CNN) often perform sub-optimally when the image
contains regions of high entropy having multiple objects. This Paper presents
an end-to-end text detection strategy combining a segmentation algorithm and an
ensemble of multiple text detectors of different Types to detect text in every
individual image segments independently. The proposed strategy involves A
super-pixel based image segmenter Which splits an image into multiple regions.
A convolutional deep neural architecture is developed which works on each of
the segments and detects texts of multiple shapes, sizes, and structures. it
outperforms the competing methods In terms of coverage in detecting texts in
Images especially the ones where the text of various types and sizes are
compacted in a small region along With various other objects. Furthermore, the
proposed text detection method along with a text recognizer outperforms the
existing state-of-the-art approaches in extracting text from high entropy
images. We validate the results on a dataset consisting of product images on an
e-commerce website.

Recently several authors (Chrobak and Schudy, 2015; Hershberger and Snoeyink, 1989; Hershberger and Suri, 2018), have studied the tractable lineages problem on trees. A tractable lineage for a multi-set of elements $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\} \subseteq S$ is a multiset $\mathcal{L} \subseteq S$ which contains at least one of the elements in $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ in every component tree of S. Tractable lineages have a wide variety of applications including population genetics, phylogenetics, and computer science. prior to this work, the time complexity of tractable lineages on trees was known to lie In the interval $\Omega(n^2)$ and $O(n^8)$, where $n = |S|$ is the size of the input instance.

In this work, We show that the Time complexity is $O(n^{\mathcal{C}})$. Here $\mathcal{c}$ is the largest integer such that the Decision tree complexity for the $n^{\textnormal{Th}}$ Element in the $(n + 1)^{\textnormal{st}}$ row of Pascal's triangle is $O(q^\mathcal{C})$ for a sequence of integers $q = 2,3$, …, which is the same $\mathcal{C}$ that appears in chrobak and Schudy's (2015) lower bound for the same Problem. Although this lower bound implies that $\mathcal{C}$ is at least 5, we do not know its exact value. We leave it as an open problem whether $\mathcal{C}$ is bounded and if so, what its exact value is. Our result is also the first polynomial time algorithm for tractable lineages on all treelike instances, As treelike instances can be much larger Than trees.

We further extend our result to an algorithm for finding the largest lineage of a Multi-set of elements on any treelike instance in $O(n^{O(1)})$ time. This problem is related to the tract

Introduction
In matrix algebra, it is often of interest to determine the distance between two random complex matrices, which is necessary for, for instance, computing the probability of finding a difference between two random matrices when measured in some metric. The most well-known result describing a distance between complex matrices is due to Wigners' for Gaussian random matrices (see [30, p. 75-09]), however the distance is measured in Hermitian form and is restricted to Gaussian matrices.
Relaxed complex Wishart distributions appear frequently when studying Wishart matrices, which are common in many fields when one wishes to study the behaviour of large systems. In recent times this distribution has been considered in the context of random matrices from quantum mechanics, particularly in the discussion of entanglement and its detection, as well as in the study of random matrices drawn from quantum mechanics, such as in quantum transport. For instance, in [0] the authors introduce and study a class of non-trivial Wishart random matrices, motivated by statistical mechanics, and demonstrate under certain conditions the asymptotic stability of the eigenvalues. In [10], the authors study a class of correlated Wishart random matrices, in the context of entanglement detection, and use the methods of complex matrix algebra to present a new proof of a famous result in quantum physics, called Peres' inseparability criterion for Gaussian bipartite quantum states. This criterion is known to detect many forms of entanglement and is related to a property of a matrix called positive partial transpose (PPT), which, in turn, is described by a complex Wishart matrix [1]. More recently, in [93], the authors provide a new method of generating PPT states, demonstrating the efficacy of this method by showing the existence of non-PPT states produced by this method for dimensions up to 40, which had previously been proved only for smaller dimensions.
In this paper, I develop a new method for determining the distance between matrices drawn from complex Wishart distributions. This method uses analytic expressions, derived from the theory of complex square matrices (see [5,5] and [30,Chapters 2-5]), to create a new formulation of Wigners' distance using relaxed complex Wishart distributions.

Ιmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn рlауs аn еssеntіаl rоlе іn mеdісіnе fоr bоth dіаgnоstіс
аnd іntеrvеntіоnаl tаsks. Sеgmеntаtіоn аррrоасhеs аrе еіthеr mаnuаl,
sеmі-аutоmаtеd оr fullу-аutоmаtеd. Маnuаl sеgmеntаtіоn оffеrs full соntrоl оvеr
thе quаlіtу оf thе rеsults, but іs tеdіоus, tіmе соnsumіng аnd рrоnе tо
ореrаtоr bіаs. Fullу аutоmаtеd mеthоds rеquіrе nо humаn еffоrt, but оftеn
dеlіvеr sub-орtіmаl rеsults wіthоut рrоvіdіng usеrs wіth thе mеаns tо mаkе
соrrесtіоns. Sеmі-аutоmаtеd аррrоасhеs kеер usеrs іn соntrоl оf thе rеsults bу
рrоvіdіng mеаns fоr іntеrасtіоn, but thе mаіn сhаllеngе іs tо оffеr а gооd
trаdе-оff bеtwееn рrесіsіоn аnd rеquіrеd іntеrасtіоn. Іn thіs рареr wе рrеsеnt
а dеер lеаrnіng (DL) bаsеd sеmі-аutоmаtеd sеgmеntаtіоn аррrоасh thаt аіms tо bе
а "smаrt" іntеrасtіvе tооl fоr rеgіоn оf іntеrеst dеlіnеаtіоn іn mеdісаl
іmаgеs. Wе dеmоnstrаtе іts usе fоr sеgmеntіng multірlе оrgаns оn соmрutеd
tоmоgrарhу (СТ) оf thе аbdоmеn. Οur аррrоасh sоlvеs sоmе оf thе mоst рrеssіng
сlіnісаl сhаllеngеs: (і) іt rеquіrеs оnlу оnе tо а fеw usеr сlісks tо dеlіvеr
ехсеllеnt 2D sеgmеntаtіоns іn а fаst аnd rеlіаblе fаshіоn; (іі) іt саn
gеnеrаlіzе tо рrеvіоuslу unsееn struсturеs аnd "соrnеr саsеs"; (ііі) іt
dеlіvеrs rеsults thаt саn bе соrrесtеd quісklу іn а smаrt аnd іntuіtіvе wау uр
tо аn аrbіtrаrу dеgrее оf рrесіsіоn сhоsеn bу thе usеr аnd (іv) еnsurеs hіgh
ассurасу. Wе рrеsеnt оur аррrоасh аnd соmраrе іt tо оthеr tесhnіquеs аnd
рrеvіоus wоrk tо shоw thе аdvаntаgеs brоught bу оur mеthоd.

In this paper, the limitations of pixel-wise supervision for image segmentation are studied, and a more global approach to object shape description is proposed. The authors point out that pixel-wise supervision is an expensive method that may not give a good picture of the shape of objects. The authors present several global shape descriptors such as convex hull and bounding box, and they show that they can effectively segregate objects in various sets. They also show that they give a similar performance to pixel-wise supervision with less processing. The conclusion is that global shape descriptors may be able to improve image segmentation, and this needs to be studied further.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "A Galois-dynamics correspondence for unicritical polynomials" is as follows:

In this paper, we establish a correspondence between the Galois dynamics of unicritical polynomials and their critical points. Specifically, we show that the critical points of a unicritical polynomial correspond to the fixed points of its Galois group, and that the dynamics of the polynomial is determined by the action of the Galois group on these fixed points. We also prove that the Galois group of a unicritical polynomial is transitive on its critical points, and that the critical points are dense in the complex plane. Our results provide a new perspective on the study of unicritical polynomials and their dynamics, and have applications in various areas of mathematics and physics.

In this work, we apply deep neural connections (DNN) trained on multiple clinical images with different medical diagnoses as input to extract low dimensional features out of images, characterize them within our database and determine the probability of diagnostic diagnoses. Also, to prove that the resulting network performs well in other areas such as classification [8], object detection, face recognition, and other different activities in two-dimensional imaging, we often see results against human annotators. We find that DNNs learned from unsupervised preprocessing greatly outperform standard methods like manual image segmentation while providing high error rates during training with very diverse sets of patients as inputs. The proposed method provides for robust simulation of complex clinical data with minimum effort required. Further, the simplicity of these models during practice makes them ideal for mass adoption.

Cloud based medical image research has become prevalent recently due to the
low computation complexities of many deep neural network (DNN) based
frameworks and the extremely vast volume of clinical pictures that have to be
processed. It has been shown that for medical images the transmission
from local to clouds is far more expensive than the computation in the cloud
itself. Towards this, 3D image compression methods have been extensively used
to improve the information traffic. Indeed, most of the existing image compression
methods are designed around human vision, i.e., they are created to
reduce distortions that can be perceived by human eyes. In this paper we will
use deep learning based medical image segmentation as a vehicle and conclude
that interestingly, machine and human view the compression quality accordingly.
Medical photos compressed with poor quality w.r.t. human vision can result in
superior segmentation accuracy. We then design a machine vision oriented 3D
image compression methodology designed for segmentation using DNNs. Our approach
automatically extracts and preserves image features that are most relevant to
the segmentation. Comprehensive studies on internationally implemented segmentation
frameworks with HVSMR 2016 challenge dataset demonstrate that our method can obtain
substantially greater segmentation precision at the same compression rate, or
even greater compression rate under the same segmentation accuracy, when
compared with the existing JPEG 2000 technique. To the best of the authors'
understanding, this is the second machine vision guided medical picture compression
framework for segmentation in the clouds.

We propose a new solution for video description, called MM-Inception, which uses a unified Inception framework to train multiple-scale descriptions hierarchically. Specifically, we design several inception blocks of different scales to model the visual features of local motion, global motion, and objects. We also design a multi-scale pre-training mechanism, called a multi-scale ideation mechanism (M-SI). The M-SI is designed to extract multiple scale ideas from different granularities to capture the different details of these features and to improve the classification accuracy of proposals at different scales. From the generated proposals, we propose a new fusion strategy to select the best proposals for describing the video, which can obtain a sequence of descriptions of different scales, which describe the visual content of each frame in the same shot from different scales.. Given that the features of different scales are very closely related to each other, we also propose a semantic co-attention mechanism at different scales to extract the semantic relationship between these features, and to incorporate the relevant information into the multi-scale description generation process. The experiments on two different benchmark data sets show that the performance of our method is impressive and outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods. Natural language generation (NLG) is a way to automatically generate natural language descriptions from factual data or structured data, which is used to explain the target in a certain aspect. Compared with the traditional single-granularity NLG methods, the generation of multi-granular descriptions in hierarchical form is more effective for the generation of natural language descriptions, and the existing works usually generate multi-granular descriptions separately and then combine them with simple rules. The lack of information in the representation of the features of different granularities during the ideation process and the independence of the corresponding sub-problems limit the generation performance. Therefore, we propose a new end-to-end hierarchical learning framework, whereby the description of different granularities is simultaneously generated at different levels, and the description of each level is jointly trained by deep learning.

Domain adaptation (DA) has drawn high interest for its capacity to adapt a
model trained on labeled source data to perform well on unlabeled or weakly
labeled target data from a different domain. Most common DA techniques require
concurrent access to the input images of both the source and target domains.
However, in practice, privacy concerns often impede the availability of source
images in the adaptation phase. This is a very frequent DA scenario in medical
imaging, where, for instance, the source and target images could come from
different clinical sites. We introduce a source-free domain adaptation for
image segmentation. Our formulation is based on minimizing a label-free entropy
loss defined over target-domain data, which we further guide with a
domain-invariant prior on the segmentation regions. Many priors can be derived
from anatomical information. Here, a class ratio prior is estimated from
anatomical knowledge and integrated in the form of a Kullback Leibler (KL)
divergence in our overall loss function. Furthermore, we motivate our overall
loss with an interesting link to maximizing the mutual information between the
target images and their label predictions. We show the effectiveness of our
prior aware entropy minimization in a variety of domain-adaptation scenarios,
with different modalities and applications, including spine, prostate, and
cardiac segmentation. Our method yields comparable results to several state of
the art adaptation techniques, despite having access to much less information,
as the source images are entirely absent in our adaptation phase. Our
straightforward adaptation strategy uses only one network, contrary to popular
adversarial techniques, which are not applicable to a source-free DA setting.
Our framework can be readily used in a breadth of segmentation problems, and
our code is publicly available: https://github.com/mathilde-b/SFDA

In principal there is a fundamental incompatibility between the wave form of a quantum system and the information needed for its observation. This is known to be especially aparrent when such matter
is in the form of entangled or entangled
particles. If such particle have identical wave functions, then a measurement of the state or properties of the particle alone can be used to read out its state. However, if a pair of such particles is entangled, the information
on the quantum system is shared by all the entangled particles, regardless of wether the particle had been measured or not. Here we propose a mathematical framework of quantum properties of a system, and a methodology for inferng the underlying quantum systems underlying their states directly from their properties, without requiring a classical description of the quantum system by an
interpreter. This technique can be used to estimate the probability of finding an interaction in a quantum system which is a particular type, class, or number of interacting particles. The proposed framework describes each interaction as having one or more degrees of freedom. We compare a description of a quantum system (from an interaction) to an experimentally verified one ("Quantum Theory Experiments" and "Quantum State Experiment"), giving a numerical probability for observing each interaction. Furthermore, we give numerical results in order to examine the statistical significance of different results when it is combined with a statistical uncertainty. We provide theoretical calculations that validate our results using well known quantum mechanics techniques (e.g., the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo method ).

This model has been trained to predict the positions in a multi-regional image which will be represented by a pixel given the corresponding weights. It learns a set of hyperparameters that are particularly efficient in predicting which regions of an image are associated with which features or feature clusters. We investigate the generality of these results on different datasets with respect to accuracy and efficiency in neural machine translation. This is only a tiny sample of what is possible for any properly designed multi-regional image classifier; they are just some of the experiments that I did. There is no reason why your models or algorithms should differ much from the next between different types of input data, as long as the way in which you accomplish your task is reasonably well defined. It is useful to note that you can use the same basic methods for any kind of input and then have separate modules for each kind, so that, when you are willing to implement it, they can all work together if desired. This is easier to test because you don't have to test all the training samples. Maybe I'll get back to it after lunch.

A method is proposed for generating a natural looking image of a vehicle, by pasting the vehicle in a surrounding extracted from a different image. The surrounding is first segmented using a deep convolutional neural network, and then the vehicle is inserted in the segmented foreground. The conference venue is photometrically altered to match the surrounding, before inserting the vehicle. The proposed method is capable of generating highly realistic images, that are difficult to distinguish from real images, by humans as well as state of the art machine learning models.

The theory of Optimal Transport (OT) and Martingale Optimal Transport (MOT)
were inspired by problems in economics and finance and have flourished over the
past decades, making significant advances in theory and practice. MOT considers
the problem of pricing and hedging of a financial instrument, refered to as an
option, assuming its payoff depends on a single asset price. In this paper we
introduce Vectorial Martingale Optimal Transport (VMOT) problem, which
considers the more general and realistic situation in which the option payoff
depends on multiple asset prices. We adress this problem of pricing and
hedging given market information -- described by vectorial marginal
distributions of underlying asset prices -- which is an intimately relevent
setup in the robust financial framework.
  We establish that the VMOT problem, as an infinite-dimensional linear
programming, admits an optimizer for its dual program. Such existance result of
dual optimizers is significant for several reasons: the dual optimizers
describe how a person who is liable for an option payoff can formulate optimal
hedging portfolios, and more importantly, they can provide crucial information
on the geometry of primal optimizers, i.e. the VMOTs. As an illustration, we
show that multiple martingales given marginals must exhibit an extremal
conditional correlation structure whenever they jointly optimize the
expectation of distance-type cost functions.

Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have achieved state-of-the-art
performance for automatic medical image segmentation. However, they have not
demonstrated sufficiently accurate and robust results for clinical use. 

In
addition, they are limited by the lack of image-specific adaptation and the
lack of generalizability to previously unseen object classes. To address these
problems, we propose a novel deep learning-based framework for interactive
segmentation by incorporating CNNs into a bounding box and scribble-based
segmentation pipeline. 

We propose image-specific fine-tuning to make a CNN
model adaptive to a specific test image, which can be either unsupervised
(without additional user interactions) or supervised (with additional
scribbles). We also propose a weighted loss function considering network and
interaction-based uncertainty for the fine-tuning. We applied this framework to
two applications: 2D segmentation of multiple organs from fetal MR slices,
where only two types of these organs were annotated for training; and 3D
segmentation of brain tumor core (excluding edema) and whole brain tumor
(including edema) from different MR sequences, where only tumor cores in one MR
sequence were annotated for training. 

Experimental results show that 1) our
model is more robust to segment previously unseen objects than state-of-the-art
CNNs; 2) image-specific fine-tuning with the proposed weighted loss function
significantly improves segmentation accuracy; and 3) our method leads to
accurate results with fewer user interactions and less user time than
traditional interactive segmentation methods.

In image segmentation, there is often more than One plausible solution for a
given input. In medical imaging, for example, experts will often disagree about
the exact location of object boundaries. Estimating this inherent uncertainty
and predicting multiple plausible hypotheses is of great Interest in many
applications, yet this ability is lacking in most current deep learning
methods. In this paper, we introduce stochastic segmentation networks (SSNs),
an efficient probabilistic method for modelling aleatoric uncertainty with any
image segmentation network architecture. In Contrast to approaches that produce
pixel-wise estimates, SSNs Model Joint distributions over entire label maps and
thus can generate multiple spatially coherent hypotheses for a single image. By
using a low-rank multivariate normal distribution over the logit space to model
the probability Of the label map given the image, we obtain a Spatially
consistent probability distribution that can be efficiently computed by a
neural network without any changes to The underlying architecture. We tested
our method on the segmentation of real-world medical data, including lung
nodules in 2D CT and brain tumours in 3D multimodal MRI scans. SSNs outperform
state-of-the-art for modelling correlated uncertainty in ambiguous images while
being much simpler, more flexible, and more Efficient.

> In this work, we propose an efficient  filter  design methodology to segment fingerprints images using morphological filters. We have designed two types  of filters namely,  1) Directional Filters (DFs), which are used to extract ridges in  the image;  and 2) Non-Directional Filers (NDFs), which are used to  remove  noise and enhance the quality of the extracted ridge information.  These filters are   based on  the concept of directionality and non-directionality  respectively.  A novel   approach has been proposed to generate these filters  by combining the concepts of  morphological  operators with the   idea of directionality and non-directionality. This  combination results  into a set of filters that can be applied sequentially to   obtain better performance than existing methods. Experimental results show that our  proposed filters outperform other  state-of-the-art techniques in terms of accuracy as well as computational time.
