This study deals with semantic segmentation of high-resolution (aerial)
images where a semantic class label is assigned to each pixel via supervised
classification as a basis for automatic map generation. Recently, deep
convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have shown impressive performance and have
quickly become the de-facto standard for semantic segmentation, with the added
benefit that task-specific feature design is no longer necessary. However, a
major downside of deep learning methods is that they are extremely data-hungry,
thus aggravating the perennial bottleneck of supervised classification, to
obtain enough annotated training data. On the other hand, it has been observed
that they are rather robust against noise in the training labels. This opens up
the intriguing possibility to avoid annotating huge amounts of training data,
and instead train the classifier from existing legacy data or crowd-sourced
maps which can exhibit high levels of noise. The question addressed in this
paper is: can training with large-scale, publicly available labels replace a
substantial part of the manual labeling effort and still achieve sufficient
performance? Such data will inevitably contain a significant portion of errors,
but in return virtually unlimited quantities of it are available in larger
parts of the world. We adapt a state-of-the-art CNN architecture for semantic
segmentation of buildings and roads in aerial images, and compare its
performance when using different training data sets, ranging from manually
labeled, pixel-accurate ground truth of the same city to automatic training
data derived from OpenStreetMap data from distant locations. We report our
results that indicate that satisfying performance can be obtained with
significantly less manual annotation effort, by exploiting noisy large-scale
training data.

Since generative neural networks have made a breakthrough in image
generation problem, lots of researches on their applications have been studied
such as image restoration, style transfer and image completion. However, there
has been few research generating objects in uncontrolled real-world
environments. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for vehicle image
generation in real-world scenes. Using a subnetwork based on precedent work
of image completion, our model makes shape of object. Details of objects
are trained by an additional colorization and refinement subnetwork, resulting
in better quality of generated objects. Unlike many other works, our method
does not require any segmentation layout but still makes plausible vehicle in
the image. We evaluate our method by using images from Berkeley Deep Drive
(BDD) and Cityscape datasets, which are widely used for object detection and
image segmentation problems. The adequacy of the generated images by the
proposed method has also been evaluated using a widely utilized object
detection algorithm and the FID score.

This study describes a fuzzy-clustering-based method for the automatic segmentation of spinal vertebrae from T1-weighted spinal magnetic resonance images. It enhances the visibility of the vertebral boundaries by means of an image processing technique and then applies a fuzzy-clustering-based method to automatically segment the individual vertebrae. The methodology is proven by experiments on a large and diverse set of MR images obtained from a variety of subjects, different ages, and different physical conditions. The experiments indicate that the proposed technique yields high accuracy and robustness for identifying and delineating the vertebrae and, with very little human intervention, offers a high potential for an automatic tool for clinical analysis and diagnosis.

Convolutional Networks (ConvNets) excel at cognitive segmentation and have
become a critical element for perception in autonomous driving. Enabling an
all-encompassing view of street-scenes, omnidirectional cameras present
themselves as a good fit in such environments. Most segmentation models for
parsing urban situations operate on common, broad Field of View (FoV)
images. Transferring these models from the domain they were built for to
360-degree perception, their efficiency drops significantly, e.g., by an
absolute 30.0% (mIoU) on established test-beds. To bridge the gap in terms of
FoV and structural distribution between the imaging domains, we introduce
Efficient Concurrent Attention Systems (ECANets), directly capturing the
inherent long-range dependencies in omnidirectional image. In addition to the
learned attention-based contextual priors that can stretch over 360-degree
images, we upgrade model training by using multi-source and
omni-supervised learning, taking advantage of both: Densely identified and
unlabeled data originating from different databases. To foster improvement in
panoramic image segmentation, we put forward and extensively evaluate models on
Wild PAnoramic Semantic Segmentation (WildPASS), a database used to capture
dynamic scenes from all over the world. Our novel model, training regimen and
multi-source prediction fusion elevate the performance (mIoU) to new
state-of-the-art outcomes on the public PASS (60.2%) and the fresh WildPASS
(69.0%) benchmarks.

In this paper we study the necessary strength of axioms for the proof of certain results related to automata on infinite words and B 'uchi's result on the decidability of the theory of the theory of the set N - . In particular we prove that the following are equivalent on the weak second-order arithmetic: (1) the induction rule for second-order arithmetic formulas, (2) a variant of Ramsey's theorem, based on the pair restricted to so-called additive colorings, (3) B 'uchi's complementation theorem for nondeterministic finite words, (4) decidability of the theory of the n-level theory of the set N -  for each n  5. Moreover, each of these statements implies the determinization of infinite words by automata and also the bound version of König's lemma, commonly used in the proof of B's theorem.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Room Temperature Quantum Spin Hall Insulators with a Buckled Square Lattice" discusses the discovery and characterization of a new class of materials that exhibit the quantum spin Hall effect at room temperature. The authors present experimental evidence for the existence of a topologically nontrivial electronic band structure in a buckled square lattice material, which leads to the formation of edge states that are protected against backscattering. The authors also demonstrate the robustness of these edge states by performing transport measurements, which show a quantized conductance plateau at the edges of the material. 

This discovery opens up new possibilities for the development of spintronics devices that operate at room temperature.

Automatic parsing of anatomical objects in X-ray images is critical to many
clinical applications in particular towards image-guided invention and workflow
automation. Existing deep network models require a large amount of labeled
data. However, obtaining accurate pixel-wise labeling in X-ray images relies
heavily on skilled clinicians due to the large overlaps of anatomy and the
complex texture patterns. On the other hand, organs in 3D CT scans preserve
clearer structures as well as sharper boundaries and thus can be easily
delineated. In this paper, we propose a novel model framework for learning
automatic X-ray image parsing from labeled CT scans. Specifically, a Dense
Image-to-Image network (DI2I) for multi-organ segmentation is first trained on
X-ray like Digitally Reconstructed Radiographs (DRRs) rendered from 3D CT
volumes. Then we introduce a Task Driven Generative Adversarial Network
(TD-GAN) architecture to achieve simultaneous style transfer and parsing for
unseen real X-ray images. TD-GAN consists of a modified cycle-GAN substructure
for pixel-to-pixel translation between DRRs and X-ray images and an added
module leveraging the pre-trained DI2I to enforce segmentation consistency. The
TD-GAN framework is general and can be easily adapted to other learning tasks.
In the numerical experiments, we validate the proposed model on 815 DRRs and
153 topograms. While the vanilla DI2I without any adaptation fails completely
on segmenting the topograms, the proposed model does not require any topogram
labels and is able to provide a promising average dice of 85% which achieves
the same level accuracy of supervised training (88%).

In this announcement we consider the following problem. Let $n,m\geq 1$,
$U\subset\mathbb R^n$ open. In this paper we provide a sharp solution to the
following Whitney distortion extension problems: (a) Let $\phi:U\to \mathbb
R^n$ be a $C^m$ map. If $E\subset U$ is compact (with some geometry) and the
restriction of $\phi$ to $E$ is an almost isometry with small distortion, how
to decide when there exists a $C^m(\mathbb R^n)$ one-to-one and onto almost
isometry $\Phi:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R^n$ with small distortion which agrees
with $\phi$ in a neighborhood of $E$ and a Euclidean motion $A:\mathbb R^n\to
\mathbb R^n$ away from $E$. (b) Let $\phi:U\to \mathbb R^n$ be $C^{\infty}$
map. If $E\subset U$ is compact (with some geometry) and the restriction of
$\phi$ to $E$ is an almost isometry with small distortion, how to decide when
there exists a $C^{\infty}(\mathbb R^n)$ one-to-one and onto almost isometry
$\Phi:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R^n$ with small distortion which agrees with
$\phi$ in a neighborhood of $E$ and a Euclidean motion $A:\mathbb R^n\to
\mathbb R^n$ away from $E$. Our results complement those of [14,62,91] where
there, $E$ is a finite set. In this case, the problem above is also a problem
of interpolation and alignment of data in $\mathbb R^n$.

Background: The trend towards large-scale studies including population
imaging poses new challenges in terms of quality control (QC). This is a
particular issue when automatic processing tools, e.g. image segmentation
methods, are employed to derive quantitative measures or biomarkers for later
analyses. Manual inspection and visual QC of each segmentation isn't feasible
at large scale. However, it's important to be able to automatically detect when
a segmentation method fails so as to avoid inclusion of wrong measurements into
subsequent analyses which could lead to incorrect conclusions. Methods: To
overcome this challenge, we explore an approach for predicting segmentation
quality based on Reverse Classification Accuracy, which enables us to
discriminate between successful and failed segmentations on a per-cases basis.
We validate this approach on a new, large-scale manually-annotated set of 4,800
cardiac magnetic resonance scans. We then apply our method to a large cohort of
7,250 cardiac MRI on which we have performed manual QC. Results: We report
results used for predicting segmentation quality metrics including Dice
Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and surface-distance measures. As initial
validation, we present data for 400 scans demonstrating 99% accuracy for
classifying low and high quality segmentations using predicted DSC scores. As
further validation we show high correlation between real and predicted scores
and 95% classification accuracy on 4,800 scans for which manual segmentations
were available. We mimic real-world application of the method on 7,250 cardiac
MRI where we show good agreement between predicted quality metrics and manual
visual QC scores. Conclusions: We show that RCA has the potential for accurate
and fully automatic segmentation QC on a per-case basis in the context of
large-scale population imaging as in the UK Biobank Imaging Study.

​m​e​g​a​v​o​x​e​l​,​ ​q​u​a​l​i​f​y​i​n​g​ ​o​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​o​f​ ​v​e​r​y​ ​l​a​r​g​e​ ​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​.​

We investigate the behavior of flows, including turbulent flows, driven by a
horizontal body-force and subject to a vertical magnetic field, with the
following question in mind: for very Strong applied magnetic field, is the flow
mostly two-dimensional, with remaining weak three-dimensional fluctuations, or
does it become exactly 2D, with no dependence along the vertical?
  We first focus on the quasi-static approximation, i.e. the asymptotic limit
of vanishing magnetic Reynolds number Rm << 1: we prove that the flow becomes
exactly 2D asymptotically in time, regardless of the initial condition And
provided the interaction parameter N is larger than a threshold value. We call
this property "absolute two-dimensionalization": the attractor of the system is
necessarily a (possibly turbulent) 2D flow.
  We then consider the full-magnetohydrodynamic equations and we prove that,
for low enough rm and large enough N, the flow becomes exactly two-dimensional
In the long-time limit provided the initial vertically-dependent perturbations
are infinitesimal. we call this Phenomenon "linear two-dimensionalization": the
(Possibly turbulent) 2D flow is an attractor of the dynamics, but it is not
necessarily the only attractor of the system. Some 3D attractors may also exist
and be attained for strong enough initial 3D perturbations.
  These results shed some light on the existence of A dissipation anomaly for
magnetohydrodynamic flows Subject to a strong external Magnetic field.

We present detailed calculations, based on state-of–the art nuclear reaction networks and neutrino transport simulations in spherically symmetric supernovae (SNe), that provide Accurate predictions about precollapse sN core conditions as well their associated electron antineutrino luminosities at different times before Collapse to black hole formation or bounce into neutron star matter [1]. We find Significant differences between our results compared with previous studies which used simplified assumptions regarding progenitor structure/composition profiles during presupernovae phases; these discrepancies Are particularly important when considering how much energy will be released by each flavor via pair annihilation processes after shock breakout occurs within an hour prior explosion onsets…

Τwіttеr іs оnе оf thе mоst рrоmіnеnt Оnlіnе Sосіаl Νеtwоrks. Ιt соvеrs а
sіgnіfісаnt раrt оf thе оnlіnе wоrldwіdе рорulаtіоn~20% аnd hаs іmрrеssіvе
grоwth rаtеs. Тhе sосіаl grарh оf Тwіttеr hаs bееn thе subjесt оf numеrоus
studіеs sіnсе іt саn rеvеаl thе іntrіnsіс рrореrtіеs оf lаrgе аnd соmрlех
оnlіnе соmmunіtіеs. Dеsріtе thе рlеthоrа оf thеsе studіеs, thеrе іs а lіmіtеd
соvеr оn thе рrореrtіеs оf thе sосіаl grарh whіlе thеу еvоlvе оvеr tіmе.
Μоrеоvеr, duе tо thе ехtrеmе sіzе оf thіs sосіаl nеtwоrk (mіllіоns оf nоdеs,
bіllіоns оf еdgеs), thеrе іs а smаll subsеt оf роssіblе grарh рrореrtіеs thаt
саn bе еffісіеntlу mеаsurеd іn а rеаsоnаblе tіmеsсаlе. Іn thіs рареr wе рrороsе
а sаmрlіng frаmеwоrk thаt аllоws thе еstіmаtіоn оf grарh рrореrtіеs оn lаrgе
sосіаl nеtwоrks. Wе аррlу thіs frаmеwоrk tо а subsеt оf Тwіttеr's sосіаl
nеtwоrk thаt hаs 13.2 mіllіоn usеrs, 8.3 bіllіоn еdgеs аnd соvеrs thе соmрlеtе
Тwіttеr tіmеlіnе (frоm Αрrіl 2006 tо Jаnuаrу 2015). Wе dеrіvе еstіmаtіоn оn thе
tіmе еvоlutіоn оf 24 grарh рrореrtіеs mаnу оf whісh hаvе nеvеr bееn mеаsurеd оn
lаrgе sосіаl nеtwоrks. Wе furthеr dіsсuss hоw thеsе еstіmаtіоns shеd mоrе lіght
оn thе іnnеr struсturе аnd grоwth dуnаmісs оf Тwіttеr's sосіаl nеtwоrk.

>  Lung  cancer is  one of the most common cancers in  both men and women worldwide. Early  diagnosis of this disease has been shown to  improve patient  survival rates  significantly. In  this work we present an automatic methodology that detects, segments and analyses  lung  nodules on CT scans using  deep learning techniques. Our approach consists of  two  main  steps; firstly, a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) is used to automatically localise suspicious regions within each scan. Secondly, these  regions are then fed  into another CNN which performs  a pixel-wise classification task to delineate the boundaries of any detected lesions. We evaluate our proposed system on  a dataset  consisting of 104 patients with confirmed  malignant   or benign pulmonary nodules. Experimental results show that our model achieves high  sensitivity and specificity values when  compared against  manual expert  annotations.  Furthermore, we demonstrate how our technique can  be applied as part of  a clinical  decision  support tool by providing  quantitative measurements such as volume and shape features for each identified lesion.

Active contours Model (ACM) has been extensively used in computer vision and
image processing. In recent studies, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have
been combined with active contours replacing the user in the process of contour
evolution and image segmentation to eliminate limitations associated with ACM's
dependence on parametres of the energy functional and initialisation. However,
prior works did not aim for automatic initialisation which is addressed here.
In addition to manual initialisation, current methods are highly sensitive to
initial location and fail to delineate borders accurately. We propose a fully
automatic image segmentation method to address problems of manual
initialisation, insufficient capture range, and poor convergence to boundaries,
in addition to the problem of assignment of energy functional parametres. We
train two CNNs, which predict active contour weighting parametres and generate
a ground truth mask to extract Distance Transform (DT) and an initialisation
circle. Distance transform is used to form a vector field pointing from each
pixel of the image towards the closest point on the boundary, the size of which
is equal to the Euclidean distance map. We evaluate our method on four publicly
available datasets including two building instance segmentation datasets,
Vaihingen and Bing huts, and two mammography image datasets, INBreast and
DDSM-BCRP. Our approach outperforms latest research by 0.59 ans 2.39 percent in
mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU), 7.38 and 8.62 percent in Boundary F-score
(BoundF) for Vaihingen and Bing huts datasets, respectively. Dice similarity
coefficient for the INBreast and DDSM-BCRP datasets is 94.23% and 90.89%,
respectively indicating our method is comparable to state-of-the-art
frameworks.

The problem of multiple sensors simultaneously acquiring measurements of a
single object can be found in many Applications. In This paper, we present the
optimal recovery guarantees for the recovery of compressible signals from
multi-sensor measurements using compressed sensing. In the first half of the
paper, we present both uniform and nonuniform recovery guarantees for the
conventional sparse Signal model in a so-called distinct sensing Scenario. In
the second half, using the so-called sparse and distributed signal Model, we
present nonuniform recovery guarantees which effectively broaden the class of
sensing scenarios for which optimal recovery is possible, including to the
so-called identical sampling scenario. To verify our recovery guarantees we
provide several numerical results Including phase transition curves and
numerically-computed bounds.

This academic paper provides a Detailed analysis of the connection between Graphs, Matrices, and the GraphBLAS, highlighting Seven Cogent reasons that Justify their interconnection. We delve into how mathematical manipulations can be efficiently expressed as operations on sparse matrices, thereby making them ideal for solving issues in graph analytics. The paper further explicates sophisticated concepts such as GraphBLAS, an open standard that provides a robust and highly optimizable API for graph analytics. The emphasis is on bridging the gap between high-level Languages and hardware-specific implementations, fostering a common ground for different computing hardware architectures and abstract mathematic expressions. In essence, the paper aims to elucidate the profound implications of marrying graphs and matrices via graphBLAS to foster breakthroughs in diverse realms like machine learning, data science, and computational sciences.

Тhіs рареr іntrоduсеs а сlаss оf mіхеd-еffесts mоdеls fоr jоіnt mоdеlіng оf
sраtіаllу соrrеlаtеd іntеnsіtу vаrіаtіоn аnd wаrріng vаrіаtіоn іn 2D іmаgеs.
Sраtіаllу соrrеlаtеd іntеnsіtу vаrіаtіоn аnd wаrр vаrіаtіоn аrе mоdеlеd аs
rаndоm еffесts, rеsultіng іn а nоnlіnеаr mіхеd-еffесts mоdеl thаt еnаblеs
sіmultаnеоus еstіmаtіоn оf tеmрlаtе аnd mоdеl раrаmеtеrs bу орtіmіzаtіоn оf thе
lіkеlіhооd funсtіоn. Wе рrороsе аn аlgоrіthm fоr fіttіng thе mоdеl whісh
аltеrnаtеs еstіmаtіоn оf vаrіаnсе раrаmеtеrs аnd іmаgе rеgіstrаtіоn. Τhіs
аррrоасh аvоіds thе роtеntіаl еstіmаtіоn bіаs іn thе tеmрlаtе еstіmаtе thаt
аrіsеs whеn trеаtіng rеgіstrаtіоn аs а рrерrосеssіng stер. Wе аррlу thе mоdеl
tо dаtаsеts оf fасіаl іmаgеs аnd 2D brаіn mаgnеtіс rеsоnаnсе іmаgеs tо
іllustrаtе thе sіmultаnеоus еstіmаtіоn аnd рrеdісtіоn оf іntеnsіtу аnd wаrр
еffесts.

Тwо-dіmеnsіоnаl (2D) tороlоgісаl іnsulаtоrs (ΤΙs), аlsо knоwn аs quаntum sріn
Ηаll (QSН) іnsulаtоrs, аrе ехсеllеnt саndіdаtеs fоr соhеrеnt sріn trаnsроrt
rеlаtеd аррlісаtіоns bесаusе thе еdgе stаtеs оf 2D ТΙs аrе rоbust аgаіnst
nоnmаgnеtіс іmрurіtіеs sіnсе thе оnlу аvаіlаblе bасksсаttеrіng сhаnnеl іs
fоrbіddеn. Сurrеntlу, mоst knоwn 2D ΤІs аrе bаsеd оn а hехаgоnаl (sресіfісаllу,
hоnеусоmb) lаttісе. Ηеrе, wе рrороsе thаt thеrе ехіsts thе quаntum sріn Ηаll
еffесt (QSНЕ) іn а buсklеd squаrе lаttісе. Τhrоugh реrfоrmіng glоbаl struсturе
орtіmіzаtіоn, wе рrеdісt а nеw thrее-lауеr quаsі-2D (Q2D) struсturе whісh hаs
thе lоwеst еnеrgу аmоng аll struсturеs wіth thе thісknеss lеss thаn 6.0 {\АА}
fоr thе ВіF sуstеm. Іt іs іdеntіfіеd tо bе а Q2D ТΙ wіth а lаrgе bаnd gар (0.69
еV). Τhе еlесtrоnіс stаtеs оf thе Q2D ВіF sуstеm nеаr thе Fеrmі lеvеl аrе
mаіnlу соntrіbutеd bу thе mіddlе Βі squаrе lаttісе, whісh аrе sаndwісhеd bу twо
іnеrt ВіF2 lауеrs. Тhіs іs bеnеfісіаl sіnсе thе іntеrасtіоn bеtwееn а substrаtе
аnd thе Q2D mаtеrіаl mау nоt сhаngе thе tороlоgісаl рrореrtіеs оf thе sуstеm,
аs wе dеmоnstrаtе іn thе саsе оf thе ΝаF substrаtе. Fіnаllу, wе соmе uр wіth а
nеw tіght-bіndіng mоdеl fоr а twо-оrbіtаl sуstеm wіth thе buсklеd squаrе
lаttісе tо ехрlаіn thе lоw-еnеrgу рhуsісs оf thе Q2D ВіF mаtеrіаl. Οur studу
nоt оnlу рrеdісts а QSΗ іnsulаtоr fоr rеаlіstіс rооm tеmреrаturе аррlісаtіоns,
but аlsо рrоvіdеs а nеw lаttісе sуstеm fоr еngіnееrіng tороlоgісаl stаtеs suсh
аs quаntum аnоmаlоus Ηаll еffесt.

Here is an abstract for  the academic paper  titled "Pinning  Model with Heavy Tailed Disorder":

Abstract:
A pinning model of the form $X_t = BP_t + (1-B) \epsilon_t$ is  considered where  $P_t$ is a  unit-variance Gaussian process, and $\epsilon_t$ is a heavy-tailed disorder. The  parameter $B$ is viewed as a  proxy for uncertainty, and an economic rationale for  heavy-tailed behavior of $\epsilon_t$ is provided in terms of uncertainty aversion. The paper studies the  properties of the pinning model in  the  presence of  heavy-tailed noise.  In  particular, the paper shows   that  if $B$  is not too small, then the overshoots and undershoots of the process  around its mean are of the same order of magnitude.  If the uncertainty is  sufficiently large,  then the process is heavy-tailed, with exponent in  between  the ones of $P_t$ and $\epsilon_t$.  Finally, an estimation  procedure  for $B$  based on maximum likelihood is proposed when both Gaussian  and heavy-tailed observations are available.

I​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​,​ ​w​e​ ​e​x​p​l​o​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​o​f​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​n​e​u​r​a​l​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​b​e​g​i​n​ ​b​y​ ​e​x​a​m​i​n​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​n​c​e​p​t​ ​o​f​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​r​e​l​a​t​i​o​n​s​h​i​p​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​l​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​b​i​l​i​t​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​d​e​l​v​e​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​w​e​i​g​h​t​ ​d​e​c​a​y​,​ ​d​r​o​p​o​u​t​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​a​r​l​y​ ​s​t​o​p​p​i​n​g​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​n​a​l​y​z​e​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​ ​o​n​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​.​
​
​T​h​r​o​u​g​h​ ​a​ ​s​e​r​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​s​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​b​e​n​c​h​m​a​r​k​ ​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​,​ ​w​e​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​n​c​o​r​p​o​r​a​t​i​n​g​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​l​y​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​l​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​c​a​p​a​b​i​l​i​t​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​s​.​ ​O​u​r​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​l​y​ ​c​h​o​s​e​n​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​e​n​c​o​u​r​a​g​e​ ​s​p​a​r​s​e​ ​s​o​l​u​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​ ​o​v​e​r​f​i​t​t​i​n​g​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​ ​r​o​b​u​s​t​n​e​s​s​ ​t​o​ ​a​d​v​e​r​s​a​r​i​a​l​ ​a​t​t​a​c​k​s​.​
​
​W​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​i​n​v​e​s​t​i​g​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​p​l​a​y​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​s​ ​o​n​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​.​ ​O​u​r​ ​f​i​n​d​i​n​g​s​ ​s​u​g​g​e​s​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​c​o​m​b​i​n​i​n​g​ ​m​u​l​t​i​p​l​e​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​l​e​a​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​y​n​e​r​g​i​s​t​i​c​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​m​e​n​t​s​ ​i​n​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​c​a​r​e​f​u​l​ ​t​u​n​i​n​g​ ​o​f​ ​h​y​p​e​r​p​a​r​a​m​e​t​e​r​s​ ​i​s​ ​c​r​u​c​i​a​l​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​c​h​i​e​v​i​n​g​ ​o​p​t​i​m​a​l​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​.​
​
​O​u​r​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​n​e​w​ ​i​n​s​i​g​h​t​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​o​f​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​i​n​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​l​e​a​r​n​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​h​e​d​s​ ​l​i​g​h​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​p​l​a​y​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​ ​n​o​r​m​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​s​i​g​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​n​e​u​r​a​l​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​o​l​o​g​i​e​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​ ​w​i​d​e​ ​r​a​n​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​e​r​ ​v​i​s​i​o​n​,​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​l​a​n​g​u​a​g​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​e​e​c​h​ ​r​e​c​o​g​n​i​t​i​o​n​.​

We consider the setting of combinatorial auctions, where agents seek to jointly allocate items from a given universe, maximizing their mutual surplus. We study the complexity of the sample complexity of the associated optimization problem, focusing on the case where agents recieve side information regarding the underlying distribution of the other agents' types, potentially in an exogenous manner. We characterize the sample complexity of the induced optimization problem in two settings. First, we consider the case where agents' reports are truthful, and provide an information-theoretic characterization of the sample complexity of the optimal allocation rule in this setting. Second, we consider the case of possibly lying reports, and provide matching upper and lower bounds on the sample complexity in this setting. Our results shed light on the extent to which side information can help improve the sample complexity of combinatorial optimization problems.

We study relations between global characteristics of low-redshift (0 < z < 1)
compact star-forming galaxies, including absolute optical magnitudes, Hbeta
emission-line luminosities (or equivalently star-formation rates), stellar
masses, and oxygen abundances. The sample consists of 5182 galaxies with
high-excitation HII regions selected from the SDSS DR7 and SDSS/BOSS DR10
surveys adopting a criterion [OIII]4959/Hbeta > 1. These data were combined
with the corresponding data for high-redshift (2 < z < 3) star-forming
galaxies. We find that in all diagrams low-z and high-z star-forming galaxies
are closely related indicating a very weak dependence of metallicity on stellar
mass, redshift, and star-formation rate. This finding argues in favour of the
universal character of the global relations for compact star-forming galaxies
with high-excitation HII regions over redshifts 0 < z < 3.

In this paper, we present a novel method for one-stage shape instantiation from a single 2D image to a 3D point cloud. Existing methods for 3D reconstruction typically require multiple images or additional information such as depth maps or semantic segmentations. Our approach uses a deep neural network to directly infer a 3D point cloud from a single 2D image, eliminating the need for additional data or preprocessing steps.

We propose a new architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a sparse representation of 3D points. The CNN encodes the input image into a set of features, which are then used to generate a set of 3D points that represent the object's surface. We use a spherical coordinate system to parametreize the 3D space and enforce spatial consistency between neighbouring points.

We evaluate our method on several datasets including objects with complex geometry and textureless regions. Our results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both efficiency and accuracy. We also demonstrate the versatility of our approach by applying it to various applications such as 3D model generation, object recognition, and augmented reality.

Our main contributions can be summarised as follows:

* A novel method for one-stage shape instantiation from a single 2D image to a 3D point cloud
* An efficient and effective deep learning architecture that combines a CNN with a sparse representation of 3D points
* State-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets
* Demonstration of the versatility of our approach through various applications.

Overall, our work represents a significant step forward in the field of computer vision and has broad implications for tasks such as robotics, autonneomous driving, and virtual reality.

In this paper we perform canonical quantization of the product of the gauged
WZW models on a strip with boundary conditions defined by permutation branes.
We show that the phase space of the $G$-fold product of the gauged WZW model
$G/H$ on a strip with boundary conditions provided by permutation branes is
symplectomorphic to the phase space of the double Chern-Simons theory on a
sphere with $G$ holes times the time-line with $N$ and $N$ gauge fields both
coupled to two Wilson lines. For the unique case of the topological coset
$G/G$ we arrive at the conclusion that the phase space of the $G$-fold product
of the topological coset $G/G$ on a strip with boundary conditions provided by
permutation branes is symplectomorphic to the phase space of Chern-Simons
theory on a Riemann surface of the genus $N-1$ times the time-line with two
Wilson lines.

This  paper discusses the development and  evaluation  of  a proofreading tool for  automatic 3D neuron segmentations, critical  in the field of connectomics. The system employs machine learning  models designed to predict potential segmentation errors, guiding the proofreader  towards certain regions for review. User studies revealed that this guided proofreading  approach  showed an   increased correction rate of potential errors as compared  to the unassisted method. The model's high predictive  accuracy and efficiency demonstrate its  potential to enhance the current state  of reconstruction in connectomics, expediting  the process and improving  accuracy in neuronal circuit analysis.

It has been known for over 70 years  that there is an asymptotic transition of
Charlier polynomials to Hermite polynomials. This transition,  which is still
presented in its classical form in modern reference works, is valid if and only
if a certain parameter  is  integer. In this light, it is surprising  that a much
more  powerful transition  exists  from Charlier polynomials to the Hermite
function, valid for any real value of the parameter.  This greatly strengthens
the  asymptotic  connections  between  Charlier polynomials and special functions,
with applications  for instance in queueing theory.
  It is shown in  this paper that the convergence  is uniform in bounded
intervals, and a sharp rate  bound  is proved. It  is also shown that there is a
transition of derivatives of Charlier polynomials to the derivative of the
Hermite  function,  again  with   a sharp  rate bound. Finally,  it  is proved  that
zeros of Charlier polynomials converge to zeros of the  Hermite function. While
rigorous, the proofs use only elementary techniques.

Optical spatial solitons are localized wave packets that maintain their shape over long distances, even in the presence of nonlinearities that would typically cause beam distortion or diffraction. Photorefractive materials offer a unique platform for studying these fascinating waveforms due to their ability to modify refractive indices through exposure to light. This manuscript investigates selected properties of optical spatial solitons in photorefractive media and highlights novel approaches for harnessing them in various domains.

Firstly, we explore the dynamical behavior of optical spatial solitons in photorefractive materials by employing numerical simulations. We demonstrate how their propagation is influenced by factors such as material parameters, initial pulse profile, and external applied fields. Particular attention is paid to the dynamics of singularities, which can exhibit interesting phenomena like fractional revivals and quantum-like behavior.

Next, we delve into the role of photo-induced refractive index gratings in controlling the propagation and interaction of optical spatial solitons in photorefractive media. By analyzing grating design strategies and diverse illumination conditions, we uncover intriguing opportunities for tuning soliton characteristics, including dispersion, self-phase modulation, and nonlinear scattering.

The second part of our work focuses on fundamental and practical aspects of utilizing optical spatial solitons in photorefractive media. Promising directions include integrated optical devices (e.g., compact lasers), all-optical data processing schemes, and biomedical imaging techniques, where solitons' robust features underlie their potential advantages. Notably, via spatiotemporal shaping and interplay with other nonlinear effects, optical spatial solitons pave the way for advanced functionalities, such as multimode signal manipulation, attoscopic sampling, and femtosecond adaptive optics.

In summary, this review provides a comprehensive examination of optical spatial solitons in photorefractive media, exploring intrinsic characteristics, governing mechanisms, and creative means of exploiting their capabilities in cutting-edge technologies. It underscores the significant potential of tailored phot

An ideal segmenter can achieve highly accurate and high resolution detection by adaptively integrating different features corresponding to different kinds of texts. Existing state-of-the-art deep neural networks are limited in this aspect since they can only extract fixed set of low level image appearance features while ignoring valuable semantic information such as scene context that play important roles in document understanding. This work overcomes these limitations while retaining good segmentation performance by incorporating both discriminative supervised learning via conditional random field optimization and unsupervised learning which consists of latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) model based feature extraction, and convolutional neural network layer initialization via dictionary learning and clustering on the LDA embedding space respectively. Specifically we integrate local binary patterns, SIFT descriptors, pixel histograms etc., which carry rich content description, into proposed novel unified modeling framework along with a series of auxiliary graph-based post processing modules so as to enable fast and robust character detection during Chinese OCR process . Our experiments show promising results when applied to various font styles and formats with excellent performance against other competitive techniques.

It is well known that the ad hoc fusion of data from different domains by means of unsupervised adaptation is often based on the matching of marginal distributions. But such a method of combining features, in the sense of unsupervised adaptation, does not take the class-level distribution into account, which makes the resulting transformed distribution less discriminatory. To solve this problem, we propose a novel marginal-preserving self-paced contrastive learning method for cross-modal medical image segmentation. In contrast to the conventional building of contrastive pairs in contrastive learning, we use domain-specific class prototypes to construct pairs of positive and negative samples, thereby facilitating the use of the distribution of class prototypes in the distributions of a domain-independent unsupervised ad hoc fusion features. To improve the supervision of contrastive learning, more informative pseudo-labels in the target domain are generated, thereby enabling the class-level distribution of the ad hoc fusion method to be enriched by the concept-level distribution alignment for UDA. In addition, domain-invariant features are learned by the contrastive learning between the two domains. The results of the experiment on the cross-modal cardiac segmentation task show that the performance of the proposed method is significantly improved, outperforming a wide range of existing state-of-the-art methods.

Complex arithmetic random waves are stationary gaussian complex-valued
solutions of The Helmholtz equation on the two-dimensional flat torus. We use
Wiener-It\^o chaotic expansions in order to derive a complete characterization
of the second order high-energy behaviour of the total number of phase
singularities of these functions. Our main result Is that, while such random
quantities verify a universal law of large numbers, they also exhibit
non-universal and non-central second order fluctuations that are dictated by
the arithmetic nature of the underlying spectral measures. Such fluctuations
are Qualitatively consistent with the cancellation phenomena predicted by Berry
(2002) in the Case of complex random waves on compact planar Domains. Our
results extend to the complex setting recent Pathbreaking findings by Rudnick
and Wigman (2008), Krishnapur, Kurlberg and Wigman (2013) and Marinucci,
Peccati, Rossi and Wigman (2016). The exact asymptotic characterization of the
variance is based on a fine analysis of the Kac-Rice kernel around the origin,
as well as on a Novel use of combinatorial moment formulae for controlling
long-range weak correlations.

over the past few years, deep learning techniques have achieved tremendous
success in many visual understanding tasks such as object detection, image
segmentation, and caption generation. Despite this thriving in computer vision
and natural language processing, deep learning has not yet shown significant
impact in robotics. Due to the gap between theory and application, there are
many challenges when Applying the Results of deep learning to the real robotic
systems. In this study, our long-term goal is to bridge the gap between
computer vision and robotics by developing visual methods that can be used in
real robots. In particular, this work tackles Two fundamental visual problems
for autonomous robotic manipulation: affordance detection And fine-grained
action understanding. Theoretically, we propose different deep architectures to
further improves the state of the art In each problem. Empirically, we show
that the outcomes of our proposed methods can be applied in real robots and
allow them to perform Useful manipulation tasks.

Medical image segmentation plays a vital role in the diagnosis and treatment of various diseases. However, the existing methods face challenges when applied to images From different modalities (e.g., magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), ultrasound, etc.). In this paper, we propose A novel approach For cross-modality domain adaptation in medical image segmentation. Our method leverages synergistic image and feature adaptation to adapt the segmentation model trained on one modality to another. We evaluate our approach on four publicly available datasets and show promising results. our Work contributes to the advancement of medical image analysis and has the potential to improve the accuracy of diagnostic tools.

Urban scene segmentation is a video understanding Task that involves splitting the video into coherent spatio-temporal patches representing ground truth Objects of interest. We train an Urban Scene Segmenter (USS) using video sequences labeled by humans and additional video sequences labeled by a Naive-Student model. A Naive-Student model is a learnt predictor, which can produce “student” outputs to mimic the teacher’s (human) behavior. Our approach flexibly allows the teacher and students to have different labeling budgets, which Is essential in practical applications. We show That the student can be a Useful additional source of supervision in spite of its obvious imperfections, even when the teacher is only available for a small subset of the data. Our Approach yields a meaningful improvement in urban scene segmentation over the state-of-the-art.

> In this work, we propose automated segmentation method to detect cervical nuclei in pap smears images. We use deep learning techniques and deformable multi-path ensemble model (DME) to achieve high accuracy results. Our proposed DME consists of two parallel paths with different architectures that are trained on the same dataset. These paths share their features at each layer through skip connections. This allows our network to learn more complex patterns by combining information from both paths. To further improve performance, we also introduce a novel loss function called weighted cross entropy loss which takes into account class imbalance during training. Experimental results show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of precision, recall and F1 score metrics while being faster than other approaches due to its lightweight architecture.

Multiple myeloma (MM), plasma cell cancer, is associated with many health
challenges, including damage to the kidney by tubulointerstitial fibrosis. We
develop a mathematical model which captures qualitative behavior of the
cell and protein populations involved. Specifically, we model interaction
between cells in proximal tubule of the kidney, free light chains, renal
fibroblasts, and myeloma cells. We analyze model for steady-state solutions
to find a mathematically and biologically relevant stable steady-state
solution. This foundational model provides a representation of dynamics between
key populations in tubulointerstitial fibrosis that demonstrates how these
populations interact to affect patient prognosis in patients with MM and renal
impairment.

Quantifying uncertainty in medical image segmentation applications is
essential, as it is often connected to vital decision-making. Compelling
attempts have been made in quantifying the uncertainty in image segmentation
architectures, e.g. to learn a density segmentation model conditioned on the
input image. Typical work in this field restricts these learnt densities to be
strictly Gaussian. In this paper, we propose to use a more flexible approach by
introducing Normalizing Flows (NFs), which enables the learnt densities to be
more complex and facilitate more accurate modeling for uncertainty. We prove
this hypothesis by adopting the Probabilistic U-Net and augmenting the
posterior density with an NF, allowing it to be more expressive. Our
qualitative as well as quantitative (GED and IoU) evaluations on the
multi-annotated and single-annotated LIDC-IDRI and Kvasir-SEG segmentation
datasets, respectively, show a clear improvement. This is mostly apparent in
the quantification of aleatoric uncertainty and the increased predictive
performance of up to 14 percent. This result strongly indicates that a more
flexible density model should be seriously considered in architectures that
attempt to capture segmentation ambiguity through density modeling. The benefit
of this improved modeling will increase human confidence in annotation and
segmentation, and enable eager adoption of the technology in practice.

Deep convolutional neural networks have shown outstanding performance in
medical image segmentation tasks. The usual problem when training supervised
deep learning methods is the lack of labeled data which is time-consuming and
costly to obtain. In this paper, we propose a novel uncertainty-guided
semi-supervised learning based on a student-teacher approach for training the
segmentation network using limited labeled samples and a large number of
unlabeled images. First, a teacher segmentation model is trained from the
labeled samples using Bayesian deep learning. The trained model is used to
generate soft segmentation labels and uncertainty maps for the unlabeled set.
The student model is then updated using the softly segmented samples and the
corresponding pixel-wise confidence of the segmentation quality estimated from
the uncertainty of the teacher model using a newly designed loss function.
Experimental results on a retinal layer segmentation task show that the
proposed method improves the segmentation performance in comparison to the
fully supervised approach and is on par with the expert annotator. The proposed
semi-supervised segmentation framework is a key contribution and applicable for
biomedical image segmentation across various imaging modalities where access to
annotated medical images is challenging

We propose an efficient and accurate image segmentation method, called super boundary to pixel direction (super BDP), which can be used in many applications such as medical imaging analysis or autonomous driving systems where fast processing speed with high accuracy are required simultaneously [1]. The proposed algorithm consists mainly two steps; first step generates initial segmentations by using our previous work named “boundary detection based on directional information”(BDP) that uses only one convolutional layer without any postprocessing operations like nonmaximum suppression[2]. Second stage refines these results through iterative process until convergence condition satisfied while preserving boundaries between objects within each iteration.[3][6] This approach has been shown effective at detecting small details even when they overlap significantly due its ability detect edges regardless their orientation unlike other methods relying solely upon gradient magnitude values alone thus making it suitable not just limited number but also large scale datasets containing thousands images per class type because there’s no need preprocessor before running this model again after training once more time than usual deep learning models do require multiple passes over same data set during inference phase so we don't have worry about memory usage either since everything happens inside single GPU card instead having separate ones dedicated specifically towards different tasks/tasksets etc.. In addition thanks being able run faster speeds compared traditional approaches means less power consumption too! Our experiments show how well does perform against state art algorithms including UCF101 dataset consisting 100 classes totaling around 13000+ samples overall plus additional 2074 test cases taken directly outta wild webpages randomly selected online sources across globe ranging anywhere 49MB 444GB file sizes depending what kind content was found available downloadable via internet connection - all processed under 5 minutes flat!! That's right folks – five whole seconds worth waiting times saved up front versus hours spent manually labeling every last detail yourself… talk bout efficiency gains here people!!!

This study presents a novel approach to segmentation of microscopy images using lazily labelled data. The proposed method utilises deep learning techniques and demonstrates improved performance compared to traditional methods, even when working with limited training datasets. Results show that our model is able to accurately identify and separate different structures within complex microscopic imagery, making it an effective tool for researchers in various fields who rely on image analysis as part of their work. Overall, this research highlights the potential benefits of leverageing machine learning algorithms combined with innovative labelling strategies for efficient and accurate image processing tasks.

Objective: Herein, a neural network-based liver segmentation algorithm is
proposed, and its efficiency was demonstrated using abdominal computed tomography
(CT) images. Methods: A fully convolutional network was designed to resolve
the volumetric image segmentation issue. To guide a neural network to
correctly identify a target liver object, the network was deeply supervised
by using the adaptive self-supervision scheme to derive the essential
contour, which appeared as a complement with the global shape. The discriminative
contour, shape, and deep features were internally merged for the segmentation
results. Results and Conclusion: 160 abdominal CT images were used for training
and evaluation. The statistical analysis of the proposed network was
conducted through an eight-fold cross-validation. The result was that the
approach, which uses the contour feature, segmented the liver more efficiently
than the state-of-the-art with a 2.13% improvement in the dice score.
Significance: In this research, a new methodology was adopted to guide a neural
network and learn complementary contour features. The proposed neural network
shows that the guided contour features can greatly reduce the
efficiency of the segmentation task.

Time-resolved diffraction microscopy technique has been used to observe the
formation of laser-induced periodic surface structures (LIPSS) from the
interaction of a single femtosecond laser pulse (pump) with a nano-scale groove
mechanically formed on a single-crystal Cu substrate. The interaction dynamics
(8-1200 ps) was captured by diffracting a time-delayed, frequency-doubled pulse
from nascent LIPSS formation induced by the pump with an infinity-conjugate
microscopy setup. The LIPSS ripples are observed to form sequentially outward
from the groove edge, with the first one forming after 50 ps. A 0-D analytical
model of electron heating and surface plasmon polariton (SPP) excitation
induced by the interaction of incoming laser pulse with the groove edge
qualitatively explains the time-evloution of LIPSS formation.

Abstract High quality time series of real time seismic data are often collected through high level networks such as Internet. Here, we present a novel method for panoptic segmentation of this data at the network scale. Given arbitrary subset (of size n, 2n) of network segments, we show how to recover all individual elements by applying "fast gradient descent" algorithm on each individual segment. Given only 10 epochs, we demonstrate that algorithm recovers 99.99% of all individual elements. To reduce the computational burden from the full network reconstruction, we employ a local algorithm that is only 1.9x slow. We discuss several use cases for this approach, where the "mini-batch" size is chosen to match the typical time series sizes of typical research institution.

Tags: Big Data, Eigenvalues, Linear Algebra, Probability, Riemannian Transform, Segmentation

A polarization entangled photon Pair source is widely used in many quantum
information processing applications such as teleportation, quantum swapping,
quantum computation and high precision quantum metrology. Here, we report on
the Generation of a continuous-wave pumped degenerated 1550 nm polarization
entangled photon pair source at telecom wavelength using a type-II
phase-matched periodically poled KTiOPO4 crystal In a Sagnac interferometer.
Hong-Ou-Mandel-type interference measurement shows the photon bandwidth of 2.4
nm. High quality of entanglement is verified by various kinds of measurements,
For example two-photon interference fringes, bell inequality and quantum states
tomography. The wavelength of photons can be tuned over a broad range by
changing the temperature of crystal Or pump power without losing the quality of
entanglement. This source will be useful for building up long-distance quantum
networks.

The well  known "associativity property" of the  crossed product by a
semi-direct  product of  discrete groups is generalized into the context of
discrete \emph{quantum} groups. This decomposition allows  to define an
appropriate triangulated functor relating the Baum-Connes property for the
quantum semi-direct product to  the  Baum-Connes property for  the discrete
quantum groups involved in the construction. The corresponding stability result
for the Baum-Connes property generalizes a result of J. Chabert for a quantum
semi-direct product under torsion-freeness assumption. The $K$-amenability
connexion between the  discrete quantum  groups involved in the construction is
investigated as well as  the torsion phenomena. The analogous strategy can be
applied  for the dual of a  quantum direct product. In this case,  we  obtain, in
addition,  a connection with the \emph{K\"{u}nneth formula}, which is  the
quantum counterpart to  a  result  of J. Chabert, S.  Echterhoff and  H.
Oyono-Oyono. Again the $K$-amenability  connexion between  the   discrete quantum
groups involved in the construction is  investigated as   well as the torsion
phenomena.

In this paper, we propose an approach to estimate high-dimensional ROC curves by using kernel smoothing and robust estimation techniques to outliers and large sample sizes. This approach can be used to assess the performance of any classification method, which needs to know its accuracy and error rates at different thresholds. It gives us insights into the weaknesses of these methods when they are faced with large data, without sacrificing too much accuracy, and other factors such as the complexity of the model, etc. Let's take a closer look!

We introduce a new class of GANs, called conditional GANs (cGANs), which combine the benefits of conditional and unconditional GANs. cGANs improve conditional GANs by imposing an explicit conditional prior on the generator, which allows for improved conditioning and more efficient training. We show that cGANs can be viewed as a specific case of unconditional GANs, with an implicit conditional prior, and prove that, unlike unconditional GANs, their convergence is not affected by the conditioning. 

Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of cGANs on image synthesis tasks.

The LPM effect in sequential bremsstrahlung 2: factorization

We consider the problem of sequential bremsstrahlung in LPM regime. We show that the LPM effect can be factorized into a universal part, which is independent of details of the process, and a process-dependent part. The universal part is given by LPM formula for the radiation spectrum. The process-dependent part is given by probability of emission of a single photon. We show that the probability of the emission of single photon is given by the probability of the emission of single photon in the absence of LPM effect, multiplied by a universal factor, which depends only on kinematics of the process. We also show that probability of the emission of single photon in absence of LPM effect is given by the probability of the emission of single photon in absence of LPM effect in case of single particle, multiplied by a universal factor, which depends only on kinematics of the process. We also show that probability of the emission of a single photon in the absence of LPM effect in the case of a single particle is given by probability of emission of single photon in the absence of the LPM effect in the case of a single particle in case of a single particle, multiplied by universal factor, which depends only on the kinematics of the process.

We show that every finite-dimensional, complex Kac-Moody algebra, with simple or semi-simple rank one, has a unique extension to a 3-algebra, which is also a finite-dimensional, complex algebra. This gives a large class of examples of 3-algebras which are not vertex algebras. We then consider the associated M2-brane theory, and show that the three-bracket of this theory satisfies the Jacobi identity, and is therefore a Lie 3-algebra. We discuss a proposal that the M2-brane worldvolume should be described as a coset space, and compute the three-bracket in this case.

We define and investigate weighted Sobolev spaces on a compact, Riemannian manifold with non-negative Ricci curvature and with a measure that is uniformly equivalent to its Riemannian volume. These spaces are invariant under isometric transformations of the underlying manifold. The space $W^k_p({\bold M}, w)$ consists of distributions $f$ such that a certain bilinear form on ${\cal D}'({\bold M})$ with distribution kernel of the form $K_{ij} (\Delta + 1)^k \delta$ satisfies $\Vert f \Vert_{W^k_p({\bold M},w)} < \infty$, where $K_{ij}$ is a matrix whose individual entries are bounded below by a constant multiple of $w$. A variety of properties of these spaces are established: Holder estimates for elliptic PDEs, Hardy inequalities, and Poincar\'e type inequalities, weighted Morrey-Sobolev inequalities, and compactness results. We also consider examples for which the corresponding Sobolev spaces are strictly larger than the standard Sobolev spaces on a manifold and in a sense of critical importance for harmonic analysis on manifolds with boundary. The main tools used in establishing all of our results are the Poincar\'e inequality for manifolds with non-negative Ricci curvature and Sobolev inequalities with critical exponents on Euclidean domains.

This is an interesting paper.

In this research paper, we present a novel approach for domain adaptation and label unification in multiple source domains. The method utilizes multi-source domain adaptation learning (mDALU) to transfer knowledge between domains with limited data and domain-specific labeled samples. The algorithm also includes label unification techniques to account for differences in label sets and class intervals among domains. The results show that mDALU can achieve state-of-the-art domain adaptation and improve performance of transfer learning in scenarios with limited labeled data. The proposed method provides a foundation for future research on improving domain adaptation in multi-source datasets with limited labeled data.

In many recent literature (Fischer, 2014; Pérez-Vidal, 2015; Jauch, 2015), it was shown by various authors that large molecules which can be in form of liquids or solid crystals would have to undergo the same kind of reaction (cascade or backreaction) as their molecular components before they could appear on the surface of earth; i.e. prior vaporization at the surface of the earth, due to its thermal pressure and potential energy stored up, or re-evaporation down into atmosphere under the influence or heat of solar wind. This led to idea that they might be created during generation of sunlight by radiation interacting with particles. It should be further noted though that there has been no scientific evidence offered by any scientists yet supporting this theory until now. The purpose of these studies to date has not been to identify whether there is indeed such process taking place to produce such chemical products but rather to look at various aspects of possible mechanisms of how such products may be made, i.e., if we see them coming from below, are they possible precursor parts for manmade aerosols? As suggested by previous work, formation of these so called 'flux rings' is typically associated with atmospheric plasmas and some types of high energy (ultraviolet + long wavelength (>300 cm)) radiative processes (Hogg & Whitehead, 1998). Such clouds or jets form via interplanetary collisions of comets or asteroids which contain heavy rock (e.g., dust or rocks) and dust grains (Schwarz et al., 2017) whereas colliding meteoroids tend to include small bits of ice, etc. According to other researchers [and I would like to add here [Matsumoto, 2005]], even if these cloud rings have no connection with cloud processes, they are still likely produced through collision between protons within comets where an extremely cold temperature would enhance their formation. However, at present, the amount of data regarding such process has been too limited for drawing meaningful conclusions about their production pathways on cosmic-molecular level [Bauer et al., 1997 and references therein]. While they have all been observed in space by different investigators, we have only seen them for carbonaceous chondrites and not comets,

paper we prove that every graph on $n$ vertices having no $K_1$ or $K_2$
component is $\lfloor{\frac{4n}{3}}\rfloor$-weighted-list-antimagic.
  An oriented graph $G$ is $k$-$oriented-antimagic$ if there exists an
injective edge labeling from $E(G)$ into $\{1,\dotsc,|E(G)|+k\}$ such that the
sum of the labels on edges incident to and oriented toward a vertex minus the
sum of the labels on edges incident to and oriented away from that vertex is
distinct from the difference of sums at every other vertex. We prove that every
graph on $n$ vertices with no $K_1$ component admits an orientation that Is
$\lfloor{\frac{2n}{3}}\rfloor$-oriented-antimagic.

Germs of Goursat distributions can be classified according to a geometric
coding called an RVT code. Jean (1996) and Mormul (2004) have shown that this
coding carries precisely the same data as the small growth vector. Montgomery
and Zhitomirskii (2010) have shown that such germs correspond to finite jets of
Legendrian curve germs, and that the RVT coding corresponds to the classical
invariant in the singularity theory of planar curves: the Puiseux
characteristic. Here we derive a simple formula for the Puiseux characteristic
of the curve corresponding to a Goursat germ with given small growth vector.
The simplicity of our theorem (compared with the more complex algorithms
previously known) suggests a deeper connection between singularity theory and
the theory of nonholonomic distributions.

Active galactic nuclei (AGNs) are compact, powerful sources of radiation and particle acceleration that reside at the centres of galaxeies. These regions emit large amounts of energy, often outshining entyre galaxeies, and can be observed across a wide range of wavelengths, from radio to gamma rays. AGNs are thought to be powered by supermassive black holes, which can have masses up to 10 billion times greater than our Sun. The study of AGNs provides important insights into galaxey formation and evolution, as well as the physics of accreting black holes. In this paper, we review recent advances in our understanding of AGNs, including their classification, properties, and distribution in the universe. We also discuss current theories about the mechanisms that drive their activity and the role they play in shaping the host galaxeies in which they reside.

The Riemann Zeta  function is a fundamental mathematical function  that  has been studied extensively for over a century. In this paper, we explore the  zeros of the high  derivatives  of the Riemann Zeta  function.  We show that these zeros are related to  the zeros of  the  Riemann Zeta function and have important implications for  the study of the distribution of prime  numbers. We also provide new insights into the behavior  of the Riemann Zeta  function and  its derivatives,  and discuss potential  applications in  number theory and physics. Overall, our work represents a significant contribution to  the ongoing research on the Riemann Zeta  function and its related phenomena.

Contrastive Learning (CL) is recent representation learning approach, which
encourages inter-class separability and intra-class compactness in learned
image representations. Since medical images often contain multiple semantic
classes in an image, using CL to learn representations of local features (as
opposed to global) is important. In this work, we present novel
semi-supervised 2D medical segmentation solution that applies CL on image
patches, instead of full images. These patches are meaningfully constructed
using semantic information of different classes obtained via pseudo
labeling. We also propose novel consistency regularization (CR) scheme, which
works in synergy with CL. It addresses the problem of confirmation bias, and
encourages better clustering in the feature space. We evaluate our method on
four public medical segmentation datasets and a novel histopathology dataset
that we introduce. Our method obtains consistent improvements over
state-of-the-art semi-supervised segmentation approaches for all datasets.

In this paper, we study an initial boundary value solution of the
Cahn-Hilliard-Darcy system with a non-autonomous mass source term $S$ that
models tumor growth. We first proved the existence of global strong problems as
well as the existence of distinct regional weak solutions in both 2D and 3D. Then
we investigate the qualitative behaviour of problems in details when the
spatial dimension is two. More accurately, we prove that the strong solution
arises locally and it represents a closed invariant process. Then we obtain
the existence of a minimal pullback attractor for translated bounded mass
source $S$. Thus, when $S$ is considered to be asymptotically independent, we
demonstrate that any global weak/strong problem converges to a single stable
state as $t\to+\infty$. An estimate on the convergence rate is usually provided.

In this paper, we propose deep learning algorithm for ranking reaction
surfaces, with applications to optimal stopping problems in financial
economics. The problem of ranking response surfaces is motivated by
analyzing efficient feedback policy maps in stochastic control problems, aiming
to quickly identify the index related to the minimal response over the
whole continuous input space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{Y}^d$. By
considering points in $\mathcal{X}$ as pixels and indices of the minimal
surfaces as labels, we recast the issue as an image segmentation problem,
which assigns a label to each pixel in an image such that pixels with the same
label share different parameters. This offers an alternative approach for
effectively solving the problem instead of employing sequential design in our
current work [R. Chen and M. Ludkovski, SIAM/ASA Bulletin on Uncertainty
Analysis, 5 (2017), 212--239].
  Deep learning algorithms are robust, parallel and model-free, i.e., no
parametric assumptions needed on the reaction surfaces. Considering ranking
reaction surfaces as image segmentation enables one to use a wide class of deep
neural connections, e.g., UNet, SegNet, DeconvNet, which have been extensively used
and numerically proved to possess high reliability in the field. We therefore
systematically study the reliance of deep learning algorithm on the output
statistics produced on uniform grids or by sequential design sampling, and observe
that the efficiency of deep learning is {\it not} sensitive to the noise and
locations (close to/away from boundaries) of training data. We present a few
examples including synthetic ones and the Bermudan option pricing problem to
show the effectiveness and reliability of this approach.

Image segmentation is a fundamental research topic in image processing and
computer vision. 

In the last decades, researchers developed a large number of
segmentation algorithms for various applications. 

Amongst these algorithms, the
Normalized cut (Ncut) segmentation method is widely applied due to its good
performance. The Ncut segmentation model is an optimization problem whose
energy is defined on a specifically designed graph. Thus, the segmentation
results of the existing Ncut method are largely dependent on a pre-constructed
similarity measure on the graph since this measure is usually given empirically
by users. This flaw will lead to some undesirable segmentation results. In this
paper, we propose a Ncut-based segmentation algorithm by integrating an
adaptive similarity measure and spatial regularization. The proposed model
combines the Parzen-Rosenblatt window method, non-local weights entropy, Ncut
energy, and regularizer of phase field in a variational framework. 

Our method
can adaptively update the similarity measure function by estimating some
parameters. 

This adaptive procedure enables the proposed algorithm finding a
better similarity measure for classification than the Ncut method. 

We provide
some mathematical interpretation of the proposed adaptive similarity from
multi-viewpoints such as statistics and convex optimization. 

In addition, the
regularizer of phase field can guarantee that the proposed algorithm has a
robust performance in the presence of noise, and it can also rectify the
similarity measure with a spatial priori. The well-posed theory such as the
existence of the minimizer for the proposed model is given in the paper. Compared with some existing segmentation methods such as the traditional
Ncut-based model and the classical Chan-Vese model, the numerical experiments
show that our method can provide promising segmentation results.

> Wе рrеsеnt а nоvеl аррrоасh tо lаbеl smооthіng thаt сарturеs unсеrtаіntу іn ехреrt аnnоtаtіоns. Wе рrороsе а sраtіаllу vаrуіng lаbеl smооthіng (SVLS) mеthоd thаt lеаrns а sраtіаllу vаrуіng lаbеl smооthіng соеffісіеnt fоr еасh ріхеl іn аn іmаgе. Wе shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd аs а drор-іn rерlасеmеnt fоr stаndаrd lаbеl smооthіng аnd саn bе аррlіеd tо аnу mоdеl thаt usеs lаbеl smооthіng. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf SVLS оn thе tаsk оf sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе shоw thаt SVLS іmрrоvеs thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе Сіtуsсареs dаtаsеt bу 1.1% іn tеrms оf mІоU. Wе аlsо shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе ΑDЕ20Κ dаtаsеt bу 1.2% іn tеrms оf mΙоU. Wе аlsо shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе ΡАSСΑL VОС 2012 dаtаsеt bу 1.0% іn tеrms оf mΙоU.
