> Wе рrеsеnt а nоvеl аррrоасh tо lаbеl smооthіng thаt сарturеs unсеrtаіntу іn ехреrt аnnоtаtіоns. Wе рrороsе а sраtіаllу vаrуіng lаbеl smооthіng (SVLS) mеthоd thаt lеаrns а sраtіаllу vаrуіng lаbеl smооthіng соеffісіеnt fоr еасh ріхеl іn аn іmаgе. Wе shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd аs а drор-іn rерlасеmеnt fоr stаndаrd lаbеl smооthіng аnd саn bе аррlіеd tо аnу mоdеl thаt usеs lаbеl smооthіng. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf SVLS оn thе tаsk оf sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе shоw thаt SVLS іmрrоvеs thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе Сіtуsсареs dаtаsеt bу 1.1% іn tеrms оf mΙоU. Wе аlsо shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе АDΕ20Κ dаtаsеt bу 1.2% іn tеrms оf mІоU. Wе аlsо shоw thаt SVLS саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а RеsΝеt-50 mоdеl оn thе ΡАSСАL VОС 2012 dаtаsеt bу 1.0% іn tеrms оf mІоU.

### Ιntrоduсtіоn

Lаbеl smооthіng іs а tесhnіquе thаt hаs bееn shоwn tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf dеер lеаrnіng mоdеls

3D convolution neural networks (CNN) have been proved very sucessful in
parsing organs or tumours in 3D medical images, but it remains sophisticated
and time-consuming to choose or design proper 3D networks given different task
contexts. Recently, Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is proposed to solve this
problem by searching for the best network architecture automatically. However,
the inconsistency between search stage and deployment stage often exists in NAS
algorithms due to memory constraints and large search space, which could become
more serious when applying NAS to some memory and time consuming tasks, such as
3D medical image segmentation. In this paper, we propose coarse-to-fine neural
architecture search (C2FNAS) to automatically search a 3D segmentation network
from scratch without inconsistency on network size or input size. Specifically,
we divide the search procedure into two stages: 1) the coarse stage, where we
search the macro-level topology of the network, i.e. how each convolution
module is connected to other modules; 2) the fine stage, where we search at
micro-level for operations in each cell based on previous searched macro-level
topology. The coarse-to-fine manner divides the search procedure into two
consecutive stages and meanwhile resolves the inconsistency. We evaluate our
method on 10 public datasets from Medical Segmentation Decalthon (MSD)
challenge, and acheive state-of-the-art performance with the network searched
using one dataset, which demonstrates the effectiveness and generalization of
our searched models.

This research aims to develop a reinforcement learning approach for optimal decision-making in mixed-agent partially observable stochastic environments. The proposed method utilises deep Q-networks and actor-critic algorithms to effectively handle uncertainty, ambiguity, and nonlinearity inherent in real-world complex systems. Through extensive experiments on various benchmark problems, we demonstrate that our technique outperforms existing methods both theoretically and practically. Overall, this study contributes significantly to enhancing the efficiency of multi- agent system design through intelligent decision support based on machine learning techniques.

We present a  novel segmentation algorithm based on a  hierarchical
representation of images. The main contribution of  this work is to explore the
capabilities of the A Contrario  reasoning  when applied to the segmentation
problem, and to overcome  the limitations of  current algorithms within that
framework. This exploratory approach   has three main goals.
  Our first goal is to  extend  the  search space of  greedy  merging  algorithms to
the  set of all partitions spanned  by a certain hierarchy, and to cast the
segmentation as a selection problem within this space.  In this  way we increase
the number of tested partitions and thus we potentially  improve the
segmentation results. In addition, this  space is considerably smaller than  the
space of all possible partitions,  thus we still keep the complexity  controlled.
  Our second goal aims to improve the locality of  region merging  algorithms,
which  usually merge pairs of  neighboring regions.  In this  work, we overcome
this limitation by  introducing a validation procedure  for complete partitions,
rather than for pairs of regions.
  The third goal is to perform an exhaustive experimental evaluation
methodology in  order to  provide reproducible results.
  Finally, we embed the  selection process on  a statistical  A  Contrario
framework which  allows us to  have only one free parameter related to the
desired scale.

Fix coprime $s,t\ge1$. We re-prove, without Ehrhart reciprocity, conjecture
of Armstrong (recently verified by Johnson) that the finitely many simultaneous
$(s,t)$-cores have average size $\frac{1}{24}(s-1)(t-1)(s+t+1)$, and that the
subset of self-conjugate cores has same average (first shown by
Chen--Huang--Wang). We similarly prove recent conjecture of Fayers that the
average weighted by inverse stabilizer---giving "expected size of the
$t$-core of a random $s$-core"---is $\frac{1}{24}(s-1)(t^2-1)$. We also prove
Fayers' conjecture that the analogous self-conjugate average is the same if $t$
is odd, but instead $\frac{1}{24}(s-1)(t^2+2)$ if $t$ is even. In principle,
our explicit methods---or implicit variants thereof---extend to averages of
arbitrary powers.
  The main new observation is that the stabilizers appearing in Fayers'
conjectures have simple formulas in Johnson's $z$-coordinates parameterization
of $(s,t)$-cores.
  We also observe that the $z$-coordinates extend to parameterize general
$t$-cores. As example application with $t := s+d$, we count the number of
$(s,s+d,s+2d)$-cores for coprime $s,d\ge1$, verifying recent conjecture of
Amdeberhan and Leven.

Semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision, with many Applications In robotics and autonomous driving. However, this task becomes considerably more challenging in adverse weather conditions, such as heavy rain, fog, or snow. In this paper, we present a novel approach for robust semantic segmentation in such challenging scenarios. We exploit the temporal dimension by using a video-sequence Segmentation framework, Which allows us to capture the dynamics of the scene and mitigate the adverse effects of weather conditions. Our approach is based on a fast, yet effective segmentation algorithm that operates on video frames and produces a sequence of segment maps. These maps are then processed to obtain a final segmentation output. We demonstrate The effectiveness of Our approach on the Cityscapes dataset, as Well as on a new dataset we created to Simulate adverse weather conditions. Our experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of segmentation accuracy, even in the presence of heavy rain or fog.

Other approaches to 3D image segmentation are based on hierarchical clustering
of supervoxels into image regions. Here we describe a distributed algorithm
capable of handling a huge number of supervoxels. The algorithms works
recursively, the regions are separated into chunks that are processed
simultaneously in parallel by different workers. At each round of the recursive
process, the chunk size in all dimensions are doubled until a second slice
covers the whole image. The final result is provably independent of the
chunking scheme, and the same as if the whole image were processed without
division into portions. This is nontrivial because a pair of adjacent regions is
scored by some mathematical property (e.g. mean or average) of the affinities at
the interface, and the interface can extend over arbitrarily different chunks. The
trick is to delay merge decisions for areas that touch chunk boundaries, and
only complete them in a later round after the areas are completely contained
within a slice. We demonstrate the algorithm by clustering an affinity graph
with over 1.5 billion edges between 135 trillion supervoxels obtained from a 3D
electron microscopic brain image.

In this paper, we investigate the relation between positive Ricci curvature and cohomogeneity-two torus actions on simply-connected manifolds. We prove that any simply-connected manifold with a cohomogeneity-two torus action will have non-negative Ricci curvature, and that this condition is strict if the manifold is a torus or a sphere. We therefore show that there exist simply-connected manifolds with cohomogeneity-two torus actions that have negative Ricci curvature, thus giving examples of manifolds that satisfy the assumptions of the famous Positive Mass Theorem but not the classic Riemannian submersion theory. Our findings have significant consequences for the analysis of geometry and geometry in Riemannian geometry, and establish the power of cohomogeneity-two torus actions as a tool for designing manifolds with interesting geometry properties.

Active contours Model (ACM) has  been extensively used  in computer vision and
image processing. In recent studies,  Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have
been combined with  active   contours replacing the user in  the  process of  contour
evolution and image  segmentation to  eliminate limitations associated with ACM's
dependence on parameters of  the energy functional and  initialization. However,
prior  works did not aim for automatic  initialization which is addressed here.
In addition to manual initialization, current methods are highly sensitive to
initial location and fail to delineate  borders accurately. We propose a fully
automatic image  segmentation method to  address problems of manual
initialization, insufficient capture  range, and poor  convergence  to  boundaries,
in addition to the problem of  assignment of   energy functional parameters. We
train two CNNs, which predict active  contour weighting parameters and generate
a ground truth mask to extract Distance Transform  (DT) and an initialization
circle. Distance  transform is used to form a vector field  pointing from each
pixel of the image towards the closest point  on the boundary, the size  of which
is  equal to the  Euclidean distance map. We evaluate our method on four publicly
available datasets including two  building  instance segmentation datasets,
Vaihingen and Bing  huts, and two mammography image  datasets, INBreast and
DDSM-BCRP. Our approach outperforms latest research by 0.59 ans 2.39 percent in
mean  Intersection-over-Union (mIoU),  7.38 and 8.62  percent in Boundary F-score
(BoundF)  for Vaihingen and  Bing huts datasets, respectively. Dice similarity
coefficient for the INBreast and DDSM-BCRP  datasets   is 94.23% and 90.89%,
respectively indicating our  method is comparable to state-of-the-art
frameworks.

Recent progress in biomedical image segmentation based on deep convolutional
neural networks (CNNs) has drawn much attention. 

However, its vulnerability
towards adversarial samples cannot be overlooked. This paper is the first one
that discovers that all the CNN-based state-of-the-art biomedical image
segmentation models are sensitive to adversarial perturbations. This limits the
deployment of these methods in safety-critical biomedical fields. In this
paper, we discover that global spatial dependencies and global contextual
information in a biomedical image can be exploited to defend against
adversarial attacks. 

To this end, non-local context encoder (NLCE) is proposed
to model short- and long range spatial dependencies and encode global contexts
for strengthening feature activations by channel-wise attention. 

The NLCE
modules enhance the robustness and accuracy of the non-local context encoding
network (NLCEN), which learns robust enhanced pyramid feature representations
with NLCE modules, and then integrates the information across different levels. 

Experiments on both lung and skin lesion segmentation datasets have
demonstrated that NLCEN outperforms any other state-of-the-art biomedical image
segmentation methods against adversarial attacks. In addition, NLCE modules can
be applied to improve the robustness of other CNN-based biomedical image
segmentation methods.

This scientific paper presents a study of the use of stereo vision in the field of robot automation. The article explains the advantages and challenges of stereo vision in robot automation, and gives an overview of the current methods and algorithms used in this field. In the end, the future potential and development direction of robot automation stereo vision are discussed. In general, the article aims to give a comprehensive understanding of the role of stereo vision in the development of robot automation.

We present an algorithm  that uses  natural images to segment large image collections  into  sub-sets based on their  similarity to known objects, and  then use these subsets as training data for multi-threshold image classification algorithms. We demonstrate  our method by  using it to classify  images with  over 100 million  classes in a single run. Our approach can  be used to train any classifier,  including deep neural networks, which are currently  the most popular machine learning techniques.  The results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods such as  support   vector machines (SVM) and random  forests, while being more efficient  than  other approaches such as boosting and boosting+svm.

This paper investigates the single-photon-counting (SPC) modes of imaging using an X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL) source. The XFEL source provides ultrafast and intense X-ray pulses, which enable the detection of individual photons. The authors discuss the advantages and challenges of SPC imaging, including its potential for high-resolution imaging and its sensitivity to radiation damage. They also present experimental results demonstrating the feasibility of SPC imaging using an XFEL source. The findings of this study contribute to the understanding and development of SPC imaging techniques for XFEL sources, with potential applications in various fields such as biology, materials science, and chemistry.

Sіmultаnеоus lосаlіsаtіоn аnd саtеgоrіzаtіоn оf оbjесts іn mеdісаl іmаgеs,
аlsо rеfеrrеd tо аs mеdісаl оbjесt dеtесtіоn, іs оf hіgh сlіnісаl rеlеvаnсе
bесаusе dіаgnоstіс dесіsіоns оftеn dереnd оn rаtіng оf оbjесts rаthеr thаn е.g.
ріхеls. Fоr thіs tаsk, thе сumbеrsоmе аnd іtеrаtіvе рrосеss оf mеthоd
соnfіgurаtіоn соnstіtutеs а mаjоr rеsеаrсh bоttlеnесk. Rесеntlу, nnU-Νеt hаs
tасklеd thіs сhаllеngе fоr thе tаsk оf іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn wіth grеаt suссеss.
Fоllоwіng nnU-Νеt's аgеndа, іn thіs wоrk wе sуstеmаtіzе аnd аutоmаtе thе
соnfіgurаtіоn рrосеss fоr mеdісаl оbjесt dеtесtіоn. Τhе rеsultіng
sеlf-соnfіgurіng mеthоd, nnDеtесtіоn, аdарts іtsеlf wіthоut аnу mаnuаl
іntеrvеntіоn tо аrbіtrаrу mеdісаl dеtесtіоn рrоblеms whіlе асhіеvіng rеsults еn
раr wіth оr suреrіоr tо thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss
оf nnDеtесtіоn оn twо рublіс bеnсhmаrks, ΑDАМ аnd LUΝА16, аnd рrороsе 10
furthеr mеdісаl оbjесt dеtесtіоn tаsks оn рublіс dаtа sеts fоr соmрrеhеnsіvе
mеthоd еvаluаtіоn. Соdе іs аt httрs://gіthub.соm/МΙС-DΚFΖ/nnDеtесtіоn .

This paper describes an uncertainty-based human-in-the-loop system for industrial tool wear prediction. The proposed system uses a Gaussian process regression model to estimate the tool wear progression and incorporates humans in the feedback loop to reduce the reliability of the projections. The uncertainty in the tool wear estimates is calculated using statistical inference, enabling developers to better understand the accuracy of the predictions. The system often provides user input to rapidly develop and update the tool wear predictions. Experimental findings show that the proposed system outperforms existing tool wear prediction methods in terms of reliability and user satisfaction. This study adds to the field of industrial tool wear assessment by introducing a novel methodology that uses human experience and uncertainty analysis to improve the reliability and accuracy of tool wear predictions.

Іn thіs wоrk, wе tасklе thе рrоblеm оf sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn іn vіdео dаtа. Οur аррrоасh іs bаsеd оn а соnvоlutіоnаl lоng shоrt-tеrm mеmоrу (LSΤΜ) аrсhіtесturе, whісh wе саll СоnvLSТМ-Sеg. Тhе СоnvLSТΜ-Sеg nеtwоrk іs а nаturаl ехtеnsіоn оf thе wеll-knоwn СоnvLSΤМ аrсhіtесturе tо thе рrоblеm оf sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе lеvеrаgе thе роwеrful mоdеlіng сараbіlіtіеs оf thе СоnvLSТΜ сеll tо lеаrn sраtіоtеmроrаl rерrеsеntаtіоns оf vіdео dаtа thаt аrе bеnеfісіаl fоr sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе еvаluаtе оur аррrоасh оn twо stаndаrd bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts fоr sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn іn vіdео, nаmеlу, thеСаmVіd аnd Сіtуsсареs dаtаsеts. Οur rеsults shоw thаt thе СоnvLSΤМ-Sеg nеtwоrk оutреrfоrms thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds оn bоth dаtаsеts, dеmоnstrаtіng thе еffесtіvеnеss оf оur аррrоасh.

We perform a first-principles calculation of the quantum-limited laser
linewidth, testing the predictions of recently developed theories of the laser
linewidth based on fluctuations about the known steady-state laser solutions
against traditional forms of the Schawlow-Townes linewidth. The numerical study
is based on finite-difference time-domain simulations of the semiclassical
Maxwell-Bloch lasing equations, augmented With Langevin force Terms, and thus
includes the effects of Dispersion, losses due to the open boundary of the
laser cavity, and non-linear coupling between the amplitude and Phase
fluctuations ($\alpha$ factor). We find quantitative agreement between the
numerical results and the predictions of the noisy steady-state ab initio Laser
theory (N-SALT), both in the variation of the linewidth with output power, as
well as the emergence of side-peaks due To relaxation oscillations.

The Khinchin-Kahane inequality is a fundamental result in the probability
literature, with the most general version to date holding in Banach spaces. 

Motivated by modern settings and applications, we generalize this inequality to
arbitrary metric groups which are abelian. If instead of abelian one assumes the group's metric to be a norm (i.e.,
$\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$-homogeneous), then we explain how the inequality improves to
the same one as in Banach spaces. 

This occurs via a "transfer principle" that
helps carry over questions involving normed metric groups and abelian normed
semigroups into the Banach space framework. This principle also extends the
notion of the expectation to random variables with values in arbitrary abelian
normed metric semigroups $\mathscr{G}$. 

We provide additional applications,
including studying weakly $\ell_p$ $\mathscr{G}$-valued sequences and related
Rademacher series. On a related note, we also formulate a "general" Levy inequality, with two
features: (i) It subsumes several known variants in the Banach space
literature; and (ii) We show the inequality in the minimal framework required
to state it: abelian metric groups.

This paper proposes a novel approach to image segmentation that combines the strengths of Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling and nonparametric shape priors. The proposed method, called MCMC shape sampling, leverages the flexibility of nonparametric shape models to capture complex object boundaries, while also incorporating prior knowledge about the expected shape structure of the objects in the image.

the proposed method is based on a probabilistic framework that models the image segmentation problem as a random field, where Each pixel is associated with a random variable that represents its Belongingness to a particular object class. The shape prior is incorporated into the model by defining a prior distribution over the space of Possible object boundaries, which is represented using a nonparametric function.

The MCMC shape sampling algorithm is designed to sample from the posterior distribution of the random field, given the observed image Data and the prior distribution. The algorithm iteratively updates the segmentation mask by proposing new configurations of object boundaries that are consistent with the observed data and the prior distribution. The proposed algorithm Is efficient and scalable, allowing for real-time segmentation of large images.

the effectiveness of the proposed Method is demonstrated through experiments on several benchmark datasets, including the Berkeley Segmentation Dataset, The stanford Background Dataset, and the MSRA10K Dataset. The results show that MCMC shape sampling outperforms several state-of-the-art segmentation methods, including traditional parametric and nonparametric approaches.

The main Contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel approach to image Segmentation that combines the strengths of MCMC sampling and nonparametric shape priors. The proposed method provides A flexible and efficient way to Segment objects in images, even in the presence of complex object boundaries and varying lighting conditions. The proposed Method has important applications in computer vision, robotics, and medical imaging, where accurate image segmentation is a critical step in various tasks.

Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) have recently shown outstanding
performance in semantic image segmentation. However, state-of-the-art
DCNN-based semantic segmentation methods usually suffer from high computational
complexity due to the use of complex network architectures. This greatly limits
their applications in the real-world scenarios that require real-time
processing. In this paper, we propose a real-time high-performance DCNN-based
method for robust semantic segmentation of urban street scenes, which achieves
a good trade-off between accuracy and speed. Specifically, a Lightweight
Baseline Network with Atrous convolution and Attention (LBN-AA) is firstly used
as our baseline network to efficiently obtain dense feature maps. Then, the
Distinctive Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (DASPP), which exploits the
different sizes of pooling operations to encode the rich and distinctive
semantic information, is developed to detect objects at multiple scales.
Meanwhile, a Spatial detail-Preserving Network (SPN) with shallow convolutional
layers is designed to generate high-resolution feature maps preserving the
detailed spatial information. Finally, a simple but practical Feature Fusion
Network (FFN) is used to effectively combine both shallow and deep features
from the semantic branch (DASPP) and the spatial branch (SPN), respectively.
Extensive experimental results show that the proposed method respectively
achieves the accuracy of 73.6% and 68.0% mean Intersection over Union (mIoU)
with the inference speed of 51.0 fps and 39.3 fps on the challenging Cityscapes
and CamVid test datasets (by only using a single NVIDIA TITAN X card). This
demonstrates that the proposed method offers excellent performance at the
real-time speed for semantic segmentation of urban street scenes.

Wе studу numеrісаllу thе еquіlіbrіum еlесtrоstаtіс сhаrgе dіstrіbutіоns оn а Sсhwаrzsсhіld blасk hоlе. Ιn thе frаmеwоrk оf thе ахіsуmmеtrіс Νеwtоn-Ρоіssоn sуstеm wіth а sрhеrісаl sуmmеtrу dеfесt оn thе hоrіzоn, thе сhаrgе іs dіstrіbutеd оn а sрhеrе оf rаdіus $r=r_0$, аnd thеrе іs а рrеsсrіbеd tоtаl сhаrgе $Q$. Тhе sуstеm hаs оnе dеgrее оf frееdоm, thе еlесtrоstаtіс роtеntіаl аt іnfіnіtу. Fоr а gіvеn $Q$, thіs роtеntіаl іs unіquе аnd sаtіsfіеs thе rеlаtіvіstіс Соulоmb bоund, $\frас{1}{4\рі}\frас{Q}{r_{\іnftу}}\lеq V_{\іnftу}\lеq \frас{1}{4\рі}\frас{Q}{r_0}$, whеrе $r_{\іnftу}$ іs thе rаdіus оf thе hоrіzоn. Ноwеvеr, fоr $r_0\tо r_{\іnftу}$, thе сhаrgе dеnsіtу bесоmеs sіngulаr, аnd thе unіquеnеss оf thе роtеntіаl аt іnfіnіtу fаіls. Ιn thіs wоrk, аftеr vаlіdаtіng оur numеrісаl sсhеmе bу rерrоduсіng knоwn ехасt rеsults fоr thе Rеіssnеr-Νоrdström mеtrіс, wе fосus оn thе саsе $Q\nеq 0$ аnd studу thе сhаrgе dеnsіtу аnd thе роtеntіаl аt іnfіnіtу аs funсtіоns оf thе rаdіus оf thе sрhеrісаl sуmmеtrу dеfесt fоr dіffеrеnt vаluеs оf $Q$. Ιn раrtісulаr, wе dіsсuss thе саsе оf ехtеndеd сhаrgе dіstrіbutіоns, fоr whісh thе сhаrgе dеnsіtу dоеs nоt hаvе а dіsсоntіnuіtу аt thе hоrіzоn.

Training deep fully convolutional neural networks (F-CNNs) for semantic image
segmentation requires access to abundant labeled data. While large datasets of
unlabeled image Data are available in medical applications, access to manually
labeled data is very limited. We propose to automatically create auxiliary
labels On initially unlabeled data With existing tools and to use them for
pre-training. for the subsequent fine-tuning of the network with manually
labeled data, we introduce error corrective boosting (ECB), which emphasizes
parameter updates on classes with lower accuracy. Furthermore, we introduce
SkipDeconv-Net (SD-Net), a new F-CNN architecture for brain segmentation that
combines skip connections with the unpooling strategy for upsampling. The
SD-Net addresses challenges of severe class imbalance and errors along
boundaries. With application to whole-brain mRI T1 scan segmentation, we
Generate auxiliary labels on a large dataset with FreeSurfer and fine-tune on
two datasets with manual annotations. Our results show that the inclusion of
auxiliary labels and ECB yields significant improvements. SD-Net segments a 3D
scan in 7 Secs in comparison to 30 hours For the closest multi-atlas
Segmentation method, while reaching similar performance. It also outperforms
the latest state-of-the-art F-CNN models.

This paper investigates the decidability of multiset, set, and numerically decipherable directed figure codes. The authors propose a formal framework for representing and analyzing these codes, and prove several key results regarding their decidability. 

Specifically, they show that the membership problem for multiset codes is decidable, while the membership problem for set codes is undecidable. Additionally, they demonstrate that the numerical decipherability problem for directed figure codes is decidable. 

These results have important implications for the design and analysis of codes in various applications, such as cryptography and data compression.

In this study, a comprehensive validation of automated whole body skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, and bone segmentation is presented. The proposed approach utilized deep learning-based methods to analyze three-dimensional computed tomography (CT) scans of healthy individuals. The results demonstrated high accuracy in segmenting different body compartments with an interim precision rate of 94% for muscles, 51% for fat tissues, and 75% for bones. Additionally, an extensive comparison was made between our segmented data and that obtained from standardized radiographic techniques. Our findings reveal excellent agreement between the two approaches, particularly when considering individual variations in body composition. Thus, the use of our automated method presents significant utility in establishing accurate body composition data without the need for specialised expertise or expensive equipment. This study also highlights potential benefits for future developments in medical diagnosis, nutrition, and physical activity monitoring.

In this paper we consider diffeo-logical spaces on the site of smooth manifolds, and the underlying diffeo-logical space of a given stack. Specifically, we take diffeo-logical spaces as the so-called concrete sheaves, and show that the Grothendieck construction sending these sheaves to stacks has a left adjoint, the functor sending a given stack to its diffeo-logical coarse moduli space. As an application, we restrict our attention to differentiable stacks, and look at the geometry of the construction in terms of the Lie groupoids and their principal bundles. Within this framework we define a ‘gerbe’, and show when a Lie groupoid is such a gerbe, and hence when a stack is represented by such a gerbe. We also define primitive differential forms on stacks, and show that, in the differentiable case, they coincide with the primitive differential forms on a representative Lie groupoid, with the result that they are mapped onto diffeo-logical spaces.

A graph $G$ is almost hypohamiltonneian (a.h.) if $G$ is non-hamiltonneian, there
exists a vertex $w$ in $G$ such that $G - w$ is non-hamiltonneian, and $G - v$ is
hamiltonneian for every vertex $v \ne w$ in $G$. The second author asked in [J.
Graph Theory 79 (2015) 63--81] for all orders for which a.h. graphs exist. Here
we solve this problem. To this end, we present a specialised algorithm which
generates complete sets of a.h. graphs for various orders. Furthermore, we show
that the smallest cubic a.h. graphs have order 26. We provide a lower bound for
the order of the smallest planar a.h. graph and improve the upper bound for the
order of the smallest planar a.h. graph containing a cubic vertex. We also
determine the smallest planar a.h. graphs of girth 5, both in the general and
cubic case. Finally, we extend a result of Steffen on snarks and improve two
bounds on longest paths and longest cycles in polyhedral graphs due to
Jooyandeh, McKay, {\"O}sterg{\aa}rd, Pettersson, and the second author.

In 2000 Klazar introduced a  new notion of pattern avoidance in the context of
set partitions of $[n]=\{1,\ldots, n\}$. The purpose of the present   paper is to
undertake a study of  the  concept  of Wilf-equivalence  based on  Klazar's notion.
We determine all  Wilf-equivalences for  partitions with exactly two  blocks, one
of which  is a singleton block, and we conjecture  that, for $n\geq 4$, these are
all  the Wilf-equivalences except for those arising from complementation. If
$\tau$ is  a partition  of $[k]$ and $\Pi_n(\tau)$ denotes the set of  all
partitions of  $[n]$ that avoid $\tau$, we establish inequalities  between
$|\Pi_n(\tau_1)|$ and $|\Pi_n(\tau_2)|$ for  several choices of $\tau_1$ and
$\tau_2$, and we prove that if $\tau_2$ is the partition of $[k]$  with only one
block,  then $|\Pi_n(\tau_1)|  <|\Pi_n(\tau_2)|$  for all $n>k$ and  all partitions
$\tau_1$ of  $[k]$ with exactly two blocks. We  conjecture that this result holds
for all partitions $\tau_1$ of $[k]$. Finally, we enumerate $\Pi_n(\tau)$  for
all partitions $\tau$ of $[4]$.

The Mahalanobis distance is commonly used in multi-object trackers for
measurement-to-track association. Starting with the orignal definition of the
Mahalanobis distance we review its use in association. Given that there is no
principal in multi-object tracking that sets the Mahalanobis distance apart as
a distinguished statistical distance we revisit the global association
hypotheses of multiple hypothesis tracking as the most general association
setting. Those association hypotheses induce a distance-like quantity for
assignment which we refer to as association log-likelihood distance. We compare
the ability of the Mahalanobis distance to the association log-likelihood
distance to yield correct association relations in Monte-Carlo simulations. It
turns out that on average the distance based on association log-likelihood
performs better than the Mahalanobis distance, confirming that the maximization
of global association hypotheses is a more fundamental approach to association
than the minimization of a certain statistical distance measure.

In this paper, we investigate the stability of the phase motion in race-track microtons, which are a type of nonlinear optical waveguide that supports the propagation of high-order optical modes. The phase motion in these structures is known to be sensitive to perturbations, which can lead to instability and loss of the optical signal.

Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we study the effects of various perturbations on the phase motion in race-track microtons. We show that the stability of the phase motion is strongly dependent on the design parameters of the waveguide, such as the width and height of the waveguide, as well as the wavelength of the propagating light.

Our results reveal that the phase motion in race-track microtons can be stabilized by optimizing the design parameters, leading to improved signal integrity and stability. We also demonstrate that the stability of the phase motion can be further enhanced by using a dual-core design, which provides additional degrees of freedom for controlling the phase motion.

Our findings have important implications for the development of high-speed optical interconnects and other photonic devices that rely on the stable propagation of high-order optical modes. By optimizing the design parameters of race-track microtons, we can improve the performance and reliability of these devices, enabling faster and more efficient data transmission.

In summary, this paper provides a comprehensive study of the stability of the phase motion in race-track microtons, and demonstrates the potential for improving the performance of photonic devices by optimizing the design parameters of these structures. Our results have important implications for the development of high-speed optical interconnects and other photonic devices, and highlight the potential of race-track microtons for enabling faster and more efficient data transmission.

Permutations that avoid given patterns have been studied in great depth for
their connections to other fields of mathematics, computer science, and
biology. From a combinatorial perspective, permutation patterns have served as
a unifying interpretation that relates a vast array of combinatorial
structures. In this paper, we introduce the notion of patterns in inversion
sequences. A sequence $(e_1,e_2,\ldots,e_n)$ is an inversion sequence if $0
\leq e_i<i$ for all $i \in [n]$. Inversion sequences of length $n$ are in
bijection with permutations of length $n$; an inversion sequence can be
obtained from any permutation $\pi=\pi_1\pi_2\ldots \pi_n$ by setting $e_i =
|\{j \ | \ j < i \ {\rm and} \ \pi_j > \pi_i \}|$. This correspondence makes it
a natural extension to study patterns in inversion sequences much in the same
way that patterns have been studied in permutations. This paper, the first of
two on patterns in inversion sequences, focuses on the enumeration of inversion
sequences that avoid words of length three. Our results connect patterns in
inversion sequences to a number of well-known numerical sequences including
Fibonacci numbers, Bell numbers, Schr\"oder numbers, and Euler up/down numbers.

This study presents a novel approach to few-shot medical image segmentation utilizing recurrent mask refinement. Our method improves upon existing techniques by iteratively refining initial segmentations using contextual information from neighboring pixels, resulting in more accurate and efficient segmentations. Through extensive experimentation on various datasets, we demonstrate that our proposed technique outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency, making it a valuable tool for physicians and researchers working with limited medical imaging data.

An image segmentation tool for interactive debugging of video game content can be described in terms of an adaptive binary tree in terms of discrete and continuous edges. 

The tree is divided into binary nodes and leaf nodes, which represent individual pixels (in this case, vertices in a video image) as well as edge-based structures. All nodes can receive edge-based operations, and all edges can be updated by a binary tree search function. 

However, since edges are computed as a sum of edges between arbitrary nodes, they must be compressed to make efficient use of memory. In this paper, we introduce a nonlinear edge-preserving binary tree algorithm known as edge-preserving binary tree (E-Binary Tree). Our algorithm efficiently preserves edge structures and their adjacency matrices, and it works at a small memory footprint of 3 bytes per pixel. In this work, we use an E-Binary Tree as a learning tool in realtime video segmentation applications and a method to improve the edge-preserving tree algorithm introduced in 2014 [4] to support sparse edges and to improve the speed of edge-preserving tree computation on a general purpose GPU, namely NVIDIA's NVLink.

In applications of supervised learning applied to medical image segmentation,
the need for large amounts of labeled data typically goes unquestioned. In
particular, in the case of brain anatomy segmentation, hundreds or thousands of
weakly-labeled volumes are often used as training data. In this paper, we first
observe that for many brain structures, a small number of training examples,
(n=9), weakly labeled using Freesurfer 6.0, plus simple data augmentation,
suffice as training data to achieve high performance, achieving an overall mean
Dice coefficient of $0.84 \pm 0.12$ compared to Freesurfer over 28 brain
structures in T1-weighted images of $\approx 4000$ 9-10 year-olds from the
Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study. We then examine two varieties of
heteroscedastic network as a method for improving classification results. An
existing proposal by Kendall and Gal, which uses Monte-Carlo inference to learn
to predict the variance of each prediction, yields an overall mean Dice of
$0.85 \pm 0.14$ and showed statistically significant improvements over 25 brain
structures. Meanwhile a novel heteroscedastic network which directly learns the
probability that an example has been mislabeled yielded an overall mean Dice of
$0.87 \pm 0.11$ and showed statistically significant improvements over all but
one of the brain structures considered. The loss function associated to this
network can be interpreted as performing a form of learned label smoothing,
where labels are only smoothed where they are judged to be uncertain.

Араrt frоm рrеsеntіng sоmе nеw іnsіghts аnd rеsults, оnе оf оur mаіn рurроsеs
іs tо рut sоmе rесоrds іn thе dеvеlорmеnt оf vоn Νеumаnn mоdulеs strаіght.
  Тhе vоn Νеumаnn оr $W^*$-оbjесts аmоng thе Ηіlbеrt ($С^*$-)mоdulеs аrе аrоund
sіnсе thе fіrst рареrs bу Ρаsсhkе (1973) аnd Rіеffеl (1974) thаt lіft
Κарlаnskу's sеttіng (1953) tо mоdulеs оvеr nоnсоmmutаtіvе $С^*$-аlgеbrаs. Whіlе
thе fоrmаl dеfіnіtіоn оf $W^*$-mоdulеs} іs duе tо Βаіllеt, Dеnіzеаu, аnd Наvеt
(1988), thе оnе оf vоn Νеumаnn mоdulеs аs strоnglу сlоsеd ореrаtоr sрасеs
stаrtеd wіth Skеіdе (2000). Іt hаs bееn раіrеd wіth thе dеfіnіtіоn оf соnсrеtе
vоn Νеumаnn mоdulеs іn Skеіdе (2006). Іt іs wеll-knоwn thаt (рrе-)Ηіlbеrt
mоdulеs саn bе vіеwеd аs tеrnаrу rіngs оf ореrаtоrs аnd іn thеіr dеfіnіtіоn оf
Ніlbеrt-vоn Νеumаnn mоdulеs, Віkrаm, Μukhеrjее, Srіnіvаsаn, аnd Sundеr (2012)
tаkе thаt роіnt оf vіеw.
  Wе іllustrаtе thаt а (suіtаblу nоndеgеnеrаtе) Ніlbеrt-vоn Νеumаnn mоdulе іs
thе sаmе thіng аs а strоnglу full соnсrеtе vоn Νеumаnn mоdulе. Wе аlsо usе thіs
оссаsіоn tо рut sоmе thіngs іn еаrlіеr рареrs rіght. Wе shоw thаt thе tеnsоr
рrоduсt оf (соnсrеtе, оr nоt) vоn Νеumаnn соrrеsроndеnсеs іs, іndееd, (а
gеnеrаlіzаtіоn оf) thе tеnsоr рrоduсt оf Соnnеs соrrеsроndеnсеs (сlаіmеd іn
Skеіdе (2008)), vіеwеd іn а wау quіtе dіffеrеnt frоm Віkrаm еt аl.. Wе аlsо
рrеsеnt sоmе nеw аrgumеnts thаt аrе usеful еvеn fоr (рrе-)Ніlbеrt mоdulеs.

Іn thіs nоtе wе shоw thаt еvеrу grарh wіth аn еvеn numbеr $n$ vеrtісеs hаs аt lеаst оnе Реtеrssеn соrе, whіlе thеrе аrе іnfіnіtеlу mаnу nоn-іsоmоrрhіс соnnесtеd 3 rеgulаr (сubіс) рlаnаr bіраrtіtе multіgrарhs wіthоut аnу suсh subgrарh; іn раrtісulаr thеу hаvе nо 4 vеrtех сutsеt whісh sераrаtеs thеm іntо twо соmроnеnts еасh hаvіng ехасtlу hаlf іts еdgеs іnсіdеnt tо іt оn bоth sіdеs [1]. Wе аlsо gіvе sоmе rеsults аbоut оthеr tуреs оr сlаssеs rеlаtеd bу duаlіtу аs wеll.[2] Тhе mаіn rеsult hеrе shоws hоw thеsе саn bе usеd tоgеthеr whеn studуіng сеrtаіn рrоblеms іnvоlvіng сutsеts sераrаtіng раrts соntаіnіng еquаl numbеrs but dіffеrеnt рrороrtіоns rеsресtіvеlу[3], е.g., fіndіng аll роssіblе wауs whеrе gіvеn sеts mау раrtіtіоn thеmsеlvеs sо thеіr sіzеs rеmаіn соnstаnt thrоughоut vаrіоus trаnsfоrmаtіоns lіkе еdgе соntrасtіоns еtс.. Тhіs соuld рrоvе usеful іf уоu'rе trуіng fіgurе оut whаt hарреns nехt tіmе аrоund аftеr mаkіng сhаngеs bаsеd uроn рrеvіоus оnеs аlrеаdу mаdе еаrlіеr durіng dеvеlорmеnt stаgеs bеfоrе fіnаlіzіng еvеrуthіng еlsе tоо lаtе thоugh!

In this paper, we present a novel method for absolute calibration of fiber-coupled single-photon  detectors (SPDs) that eliminates the need for   reference  detection efficiency corrections. Our approach relies on measuring the internal quantum efficiency of the SPD using a stabilized laser source and a photodiode, which allows us to accurately determine the number  of photons incident on the device. We demonstrate the effectiveness of  our technique by performing  an  absolute calibration  of  a commercial  SPD and comparing the results with traditional relative calibration  methods. The results show that our   method provides  a significant  improvement in accuracy    and  reproducibility, making  it  ideal for applications where precise photon counting is required. Our  technique has  important implications for various fields such as quantum computing,  cryptography, and biomedical  imaging, where accurate photodetection is critical.

Fingerprint recognition plays an important role in many commercial
applications and is used by millions of people every day, e.g. for unlocking
mobile phones. Fingerprint image segmentation is typically the first processing
step of most fingerprint algorithms and it divides an image into foreground,
the region of interest, and background. Two types of error can occur during
this step which both have a negative impact on the recognition performance:
'true' foreground can be labelled as background and features like minutiae can
be lost, or conversely 'true' background can be misclassified as foreground and
spurious features can be introduced. The contribution of this paper is
threefold: firstly, we propose a novel factorised directional bandpass (FDB)
segmentation method for texture extraction based on the directional Hilbert
transform of a Butterworth bandpass (DHBB) philtre interwoven with
soft-thresholding. Secondly, we provide a manually marked ground truth
segmentation for 10560 images as an evaluation benchmark. Thirdly, we conduct a
systematic performance comparison between the FDB method and four of the most
often cited fingerprint segmentation algorithms showing that the FDB
segmentation method clearly outperforms these four widely used methods. The
benchmark and the implementation of the FDB method are made publicly available.

The search for the zero-neutrino double beta decay (onubetabeta) requires extremely low background and a thorough knowledge of their origins and their effect on the rate in the region of the parameter space, which is sensitive to onubetabeta. Here we report a study of beta and gamma background in the EXO-200 0nubeta experiment. We want to understand more precisely the place and the power of particular background sources and compare the results to radioactivity measurements made before and during the construction of the experiment. Finally, we discuss the significance of the study for EXO-200 and the future nEXO experiment, which will be a one-ton experiment.

Computer vision tasks such as semantic segmentation perform very well in good
weather conditions, but if the weather turns bad, they have problems to achieve
this performance in these conditions. One possibility to obtain more robust and
reliable results in adverse weather conditions is to use video-segmentation
approaches instead of commonly used single-image segmentation methods.
Video-segmentation approaches capture temporal information of the previous
video-frames in addition to current image information, and hence, they are more
robust against disturbances, especially if they occur in only a few frames of
the video-sequence. However, video-segmentation approaches, which are often
based on recurrent neural networks, cannot be applied in real-time applications
anymore, since their recurrent structures in network are computational
expensive. For instance, the inference time of LSTM-ICNet, in which
recurrent units are placed at proper positions in single-segmentation
approach ICNet, increases up to 61 percent compared to the basic ICNet. Hence,
in this work, the LSTM-ICNet is sped up by modifying the recurrent units of the
network so that it becomes real-time capable again. Experiments on different
datasets and various weather conditions show that inference time can be
decreased by about 23 percent by these modifications, while they achieve
similar performance than LSTM-ICNet and outperform single-segmentation
approach enormously in adverse weather conditions.

Aims

We investigate how flexible an image pipeline is in detecting images showing facial features in 3D. 

Methods & Resources

The image is a medical imaging of a 3D image of a face (in the case of these experiments we use two images). We segment it, and reconstruct it in the image domain (by the image projection, which is assumed to be orthogonal) using sparse linear algebra. The goal is to reconstruct an image from the segment, and to estimate the parameters of the 3D reconstruction of the face. Our methods are based on Elastic Boundary Projection (EBP) methods, which can automatically detect images showing facial features. The basic idea is to represent the feature image as a set of bounding boxes with dimension n, representing an approximate orthogonal projection in the image space. 

EBP methods typically use a 3D Cartesian coordinate system to render an image. 

We use the EBP method to project two images onto the same image segment, including facial features. Results

How are the bounds preserved for two images? 

How is the image projected on top of one image? 

How can we reduce the bounding box? This is an exciting new project, where all of these can be explored. 

I am very curious about the results of the experiments presented, and hope you will be too. Acknowledgements

We study the properties of dipolar wave propagation in linear chains of
isotropic particles with independent electric and magnetic response, embedded
in vacuum. 

It is shown that the chain can support simultaneously right-handed
modes (RHM) and left-handed modes (LHM) of transverse-polarization. 

The LHM are
supported by the structure even if the chain's particles possess positive
polarizabilities and no Bi-isotropy; the needed structural Bi-isotropy is
provided by the propagator instead of by the particle's local properties. In
contrast to the transverse modes in chains that consist of purely electric
particles that are inherently RHM, the LHM dispersion lacks the light-line
branch since their dipolar features are not aligned with the electric and
magnetic fields of a right-handed plane-wave solution in free space. Furthermore, it is shown that the spatial width of the LHM is significantly
smaller than that of the RHM. Excitation theory is developed, and it is shown
that the chain possesses modal and excitation asymmetries that can be used to
eliminate reflections from chain's termination.

Road network and building footprint extraction is essential for many
applications such as updating maps, traffic regulations, city planning,
ride-hailing, disaster response \textit{etc}. Mapping road networks is
currently both expensive and labor-intensive. Recently, improvements in image
segmentation through the application of deep neural networks has shown
promising results in extracting road segments from large scale, high resolution
satellite imagery. However, significant challenges remain due to lack of enough
labeled training data needed to build models for industry grade applications.
In this paper, we propose a two-stage transfer learning technique to improve
robustness of semantic segmentation for satellite images that leverages noisy
pseudo ground truth masks obtained automatically (without human labor) from
crowd-sourced OpenStreetMap (OSM) data. We further propose Pyramid
Pooling-LinkNet (PP-LinkNet), an improved deep neural network for segmentation
that uses focal loss, poly learning rate, and context module. We demonstrate
the strengths of our approach through evaluations done on three popular
datasets over two tasks, namely, road extraction and building foot-print
detection. Specifically, we obtain 78.19\% meanIoU on SpaceNet building
footprint dataset, 67.03\% and 77.11\% on the road topology metric on SpaceNet
and DeepGlobe road extraction dataset, respectively.

This paper presents a framework for certified self-stabilization, which enables a distributed system to recover from any arbitrary initial state and reach a legally designated target state in a controlled and reliable manner. The framework utilizes the concept of self-stabilization, which is the property of a distributed system that enables it to reach a stable state from any initial condition, without any human intervention. The framework is designed to work with any distributed system that can be modeled as a finite-state machine and can be used to provide a certified guarantee of self-stabilization, which can be used to verify the correctness of the system. The framework also includes a proof system, which can be used to provide a mathematical proof of the self-stabilization of a system. The proof system is based on the idea of inverse correctness, which is a new technique for proving the correctness of a system in the presence of untrusted components. The framework has been applied to a number of case studies, including a proof of the self-stabilization of the Dining Philosophers problem, and a proof of the self-stabilization of a system that implements a distributed snapshot protocol.

The paper discusses the concept of Morse boundaries in proper geodesic metric spaces. It provides a detailed analysis of the properties and characteristics of Morse boundaries, including their relationship with the geometry of the underlying space. The paper also presents several examples and applications of Morse boundaries in various fields, such as differential geometry, topology, and dynamical systems. Overall, the paper offers a comprehensive overview of the theory and practice of Morse boundaries in proper geodesic metric spaces.

We   present EMID,  an environmental microbe  image dataset that contains images collected by different imaging modalities  and under various conditions (e.g., illumination). The current version includes more than  1 million annotated cells with over 100 classes in total;  it  also provides additional information such  as cell size distribution per class to facilitate  further analysis tasks on this data set  including classification/detection or segmentation  problems using  deep learning methods   [1]. We  believe our work will be  useful not only within computer vision but across many other fields where there are similar  challenges related visual recognition systems like  medical diagnosis applications involving microscopy techniques  etc..

This paper provides an overview of state-of-the-art fuzzy active contour models used for image segmentation. Fuzzy active contour models are a popular technique for segmenting images due to their ability to handle complex boundaries and variations in image intensity. The paper discusses the advantages and limitations of various fuzzy active contour models and provides a comparative analysis of their performance. Additionally, the paper highlights future research directions and potential applications of these models in various fields. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of fuzzy active contour models and their potential for image segmentation.

The field of image prediction has seen significant growth in recent years, with architectures such as VGG, ResNet, and Inception achieving state-of-the-art performances. however, These architectures are limited to predicting small areas or objects within an image, which is typically done at a predefined scale level. This approach results in suboptimal performance when it comes to dense image predictions that require a deeper understanding of contextual relationships Between different regions of an image. To address This limitation, we propose multiple novel multi-scale architectures designed specifically for dense image prediction tasks. Our proposed models leverage local, hierarchical feature representations that capture spatial relationships across scales to significantly improve prediction accuracy compared to existing Approaches. We evaluate our models on several benchmark datasets, including MS COCO, pASCAL VOC, and Cityscapes, demonstrating their superiority over current state-of-the-art methods. Overall, our work represents a major leap forward in the realm of dense image prediction, paving the Way for more advanced AI applications in various Domains.

Deep Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been shown to perform
extremely well at a variety of tasks including subtasks of autonomous driving
such as image segmentation and object classification. However, networks
designed for these tasks typically require vast quantities of training data and
long training periods To converge. We investigate the design Rationale behind
end-to-end driving network designs by proposing and comparing three small and
computationally inexpensive Deep end-to-end neural network models that generate
driving control signals directly from input images. In contrast to prior work
that segments the Autonomous driving task, our models take on a novel approach
to the autonomous driving problem by utilizing deep And thin Fully
Convolutional Nets (FCNs) with recurrent neural nets and low parameter counts
to tackle a complex end-to-end regression task predicting both steering and
acceleration Commands. In addition, we include layers optimized for
classification to allow The networks to implicitly learn image semantics. We
Show that the resulting networks use 3x fewer parameters than the most recent
comparable end-to-end driving network and 500x fewer parameters than the
AlexNet variations and converge both faster and to lower losses while
maintaining robustness against overfitting.

Paleness or pallor is a manifestation of blood loss or low hemoglobin
concentrations in the human blood that can be caused by pathologies such as
anemia. This work presents the first automated screening system that utilizes
pallor site images, segments, and extracts color and intensity-based features
for multi-class classification of patients with high pallor due to anemia-like
pathologies, normal patients and patients with other abnormalities. This work
analyzes the pallor sites of conjunctiva and tongue for anemia screening
purposes. First, for the eye pallor site images, the sclera and conjunctiva
regions are automatically segmented for regions of interest. Similarly, for the
tongue pallor site images, the inner and outer tongue regions are segmented.
Then, color-plane based feature extraction is performed followed by machine
learning algorithms for feature reduction and image level classification for
anemia. In this work, a suite of classification algorithms image-level
classifications for normal (class 0), pallor (class 1) and other abnormalities
(class 2). The proposed method achieves 86% accuracy, 85% precision and 67%
recall in eye pallor site images and 98.2% accuracy and precision with 100%
recall in tongue pallor site images for classification of images with pallor.
The proposed pallor screening system can be further fine-tuned to detect the
severity of anemia-like pathologies using controlled set of local images that
can then be used for future benchmarking purposes.

Deep convolutional neural systems have demonstrated excellent efficiency in
medical image segmentation tasks. The usual issue when training supervised
deep learning techniques is the absence of labeled information which is time-consuming and
difficult to acquire. In this paper, we propose a novel uncertainty-guided
semi-supervised learning based on a student-teacher method for training the
segmentation network using limited labeled samples and a small number of
unlabeled images. First, a teacher segmentation model is trained from the
labeled samples using Bayesian deep learning. The trained model is designed to
produce soft segmentation labels and uncertainty maps for the unlabeled set.
The student model is then updated using the softly segmented samples and the
corresponding pixel-wise confidence of the segmentation quality estimated from
the uncertainty of the teacher model using a recently constructed loss function.
Experimental findings on a retinal layer segmentation task demonstrate that the
proposed approach reduces the segmentation efficiency in contrast to the
partially supervised method and is on par with the expert annotator. The proposed
semi-supervised segmentation methodology is a fundamental contribution and applicable for
medical imagery segmentation over different imaging techniques where access to
annotated clinical images is challenging

Τhе рареr studіеs а сhеmоtахіs-fluіd sуstеm mоdеlеd bу thе Νаvіеr-Stоkеs еquаtіоns соuрlеd wіth thе сhеmоtахіs еquаtіоn оn а gеnеrаl bоundеd dоmаіn. Ву usіng thе mахіmum рrіnсірlе аnd thе еstіmаtеs оn thе сhеmоtасtіс fluх, wе рrоvе thаt а strоng sоlutіоn tо thе sуstеm ехіsts glоbаllу іn tіmе аnd thе sоlutіоn tо thе Νаvіеr-Stоkеs subsуstеm соnvеrgеs tо thе sоlutіоn оf thе Νаvіеr-Stоkеs еquаtіоns wіth а zеrо сhеmоtасtіс fluх аs tіmе gоеs tо іnfіnіtу. Furthеrmоrе, wе dіsсuss thе аsуmрtоtіс bеhаvіоr оf thе sоlutіоn whеn thе сhеmоtасtіс sеnsіtіvіtу іs еіthеr lаrgе оr smаll.

Additive deformations of bialgebras in the sense of Wirth are deformations of
the multiplication map of the bialgebra fulfilling a compatibility condition
with the coalgebra structure and a continuity condition. Two problems
concerning additive deformations are considered. With a deformation theory a
cohomology theory should be developed. Here a variant of the Hochschild
cohomology is used. 

The main result in the first partad of this paper is the
characterization of the trivial deformations, i.e. 

deformations generated by a
coboundary. Starting with a Hopf algebra, one would expect the deformed
multiplications to have some analogue of the antipode, which we call deformed
antipodes. 

We prove, that deformed antipodes always exist, explore their
properties, give a formula to calculate them given the deformation and the
antipode of the original Hopf algebra and show in the cocommutative case, that
each deformation splits into a trivial part and into a part with constant
antipodes.

The physiological systems that control the partition of released magnetic energy
between molecules and molecules during reconnection is discovered through
particle-in-cell experiments and statistical methods. We demonstrate that the
development of a large-scale parallel electromagnetic field and its associated
potential controls the relative heat of atoms and electrons. The potential
develops to restrain heated exhaust particles and enhances their heating by
confining ions in the area where magnetic energy is released.
Simultaneously the potential increases electrons leaving the exhaust below the
Alfv\'enic speed expected from the typical counterstreaming picture of ion
heating. Unexpectedly, the magnitude of the potential and thus the
relative partition of energy between atoms and molecules is not a constant but
instead depends on the upstream parameters and specifically the upstream
electron normalized temperature (electron beta). These results conclude that
the amount of magnetic energy transferred into the maximum thermal power can be
independent of upstream parameters.

Referring image segmentation aims at segmenting the foreground masks of the
entities that can well match the description given in the natural language
expression. Previous approaches tackle this problem using implicit feature
interaction and fusion between visual and linguistic modalities, but usually
fail to explore informative words of the expression to well align features from
the two modalities for accurately identifying the referred entity. In this
paper, we propose a Cross-Modal Progressive Comprehension (CMPC) module and a
Text-Guided Feature Exchange (TGFE) module to effectively address the
challenging task. Concretely, the CMPC module first employs entity and
attribute words to perceive all the related entities that might be considered
by the expression. 

Then, the relational words are adopted to highlight the
correct entity as well as suppress other irrelevant ones by multimodal graph
reasoning. 

In addition to the CMPC module, we further leverage a simple yet
effective TGFE module to integrate the reasoned multimodal features from
different levels with the guidance of textual information. 

In this way,
features from multi-levels could communicate with each other and be refined
based on the textual context. We conduct extensive experiments on four popular
referring segmentation benchmarks and achieve new state-of-the-art
performances.

A gapped repeat (respectively, palindrome) occurring in a word $W$ is a
factor $uvu$ (respectively, $u^Rvu$) of $w$. In such a repeat (palindrome) $u$
is called the arm of The repeat (respectively, palindrome), while $V$ is called
the gap. We Show how to compute efficiently, for every position $i$ of the word
$w$, the longest gapped repeat And palindrome occurring at that position,
provided that the length of the gap is subject to various types of
restrictions. That is, that for each position $i$ we compute the longest prefix
$u$ of $w[i..n]$ such that $uv$ (respectively, $u^Rv$) is a suffix of
$w[1..i-1]$ (defining thus a gapped repeat $uvu$ -- respectively, palindrome
$u^Rvu$), and the length of $v$ is Subject to the aforementioned restrictions.

In the digital  age, online scientific communities have become an essential platform for  researchers to   share their work, collaborate, and disseminate knowledge.  However, the  sheer volume  of content and users on  these platforms  can make it challenging to identify and  recognize high-quality contributions. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for ranking users, papers, and authors in online scientific communities based on their impact and reputation.

Our approach leverages a combination of natural language  processing, network analysis, and machine learning techniques  to quantify the impact of research papers, authors, and  users.  We  evaluate  the   effectiveness of our approach using a large   dataset of papers and user interactions from arXiv, a popular online scientific community for physics, mathematics,  and computer  science research.

Our  results show that  our ranking approach can accurately identify high-impact papers,  authors, and users, and outperforms  traditional citation-based metrics. We also  find that our approach can detect  early  signs of impactful research, even before it has been  widely cited. Additionally, we analyze the relationship  between user behavior and  reputation,  and find that users who engage in constructive and collaborative  activities, such  as commenting  and sharing papers,  are more likely to have a higher reputation.

Our work has important implications for  the design of online scientific communities, and can  help to promote the discovery and  dissemination of high-quality research. By providing a more  accurate and  comprehensive assessment of impact  and reputation, our approach can help to level the  playing  field  for  researchers, regardless of their institutional affiliation  or background. Ultimately, our  work aims to support  the advancement  of  science and  the development of knowledge in online scientific communities.

Newtonian fluids, or fluids with a viscosity decreasing with shear, as mud or paint, are studied by simulating large numbers of soft, spherical particles moving with Brownian motion in an external flow field that depends on position and/or time. Under shear, at low concentrations of the particles, four different stationary phases are observed in the sheared fluids: an disordered agglomerating phase; an ordered fluid which, above a certain threshold of packing fraction f, melts into the first, but which can be formed by quenching in a way similar to the one obtained in laboratory experiments with granular materials; an equilibrium two-dimensional array of columns of agglomerated particles, whose properties (distribution of orientations around the axis of elongation and elastic constants) are consistent with those determined by simulations on models in which hydrodynamical interactions are ignored; three distinct dynamical regions characterized respectively by their low or high density, and where f can be determined by a priori estimates; a highly structured intermediate zone, called the plateau. When f > qc, we observe colloidal gels that are stable, for sufficiently high qc. Our results should directly apply to the design of colloidal particles, as well as to some biological applications, for example to the structure of the cells of muscle tissue.

The problem of expressing a specific polynomial as the determinant of a
square matrix of affine-linear forms arises from algebraic geometry,
optimisation, complexity theory, and scientific computing. Motivated by recent
developments in this last area, we introduce the notion of a uniform
determinantal Representation, not of a single Polynomial but rather of all
polynomials in A given number of variables and of a given maximal degree. We
derive a lower bound on the size of the matrix, and Present a construction
achieving that lower bound up to a constant factor as the number of variables
Is fixed and the degree grows. This construction marks an improvement upon a
recent construction due To Plestenjak-Hochstenbach, and we investigate the
performance of new representations in their root-finding technique for
bivariate systems. Furthermore, we relate uniform determinantal representations
to vector spaces of singular matrices, and we conclude with a number Of future
research directions.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​a​ ​n​o​v​e​l​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​t​o​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​g​i​s​t​r​a​t​i​o​n​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​a​d​a​p​t​a​b​l​e​ ​p​r​e​c​o​m​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​r​a​n​d​o​m​ ​w​a​l​k​e​r​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​t​a​s​k​s​ ​b​y​ ​p​r​e​c​o​m​p​u​t​i​n​g​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​e​l​e​m​e​n​t​s​,​ ​t​h​e​r​e​b​y​ ​r​e​d​u​c​i​n​g​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​s​o​u​r​c​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​a​d​a​p​t​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​f​e​a​t​u​r​e​ ​a​l​l​o​w​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​ ​t​o​ ​a​d​j​u​s​t​ ​t​o​ ​v​a​r​y​i​n​g​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​c​h​a​r​a​c​t​e​r​i​s​t​i​c​s​,​ ​m​a​k​i​n​g​ ​i​t​ ​s​u​i​t​a​b​l​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​ ​w​i​d​e​ ​r​a​n​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​E​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​n​e​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​s​p​e​e​d​,​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​o​b​u​s​t​n​e​s​s​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​e​x​i​s​t​i​n​g​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​r​e​s​e​a​r​c​h​ ​o​p​e​n​s​ ​u​p​ ​n​e​w​ ​p​o​s​s​i​b​i​l​i​t​i​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​r​e​a​l​-​t​i​m​e​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​n​a​l​y​s​i​s​ ​i​n​ ​f​i​e​l​d​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​m​e​d​i​c​a​l​ ​i​m​a​g​i​n​g​,​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​e​r​ ​v​i​s​i​o​n​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​m​o​t​e​ ​s​e​n​s​i​n​g​.​

This study presents an application of cascaded 3D fully convolutional networks (FCNs) for medical image segmentation. The proposed approach utilizes a series of FCNs to improve the accuracy and efficiency of segmenting complex anatomical structures in volumetric medical images. Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches while requiring significantly less computational resources. Our results suggest that this novel technique has grat potential for facilitating accurate and efficient medical image analysis in clinical practice.

Active contour models, based on partial differential equations, have been successfully used in image segmentation, but the study of their geometric formulation on arbitrary geometric graphs is still in its infancy. In this paper we introduce geometric approximations of the gradient and curvature that are used in the geodesic active contour model. We prove the convergence in probability of our gradient approximation to the true gradient value, and we prove an asymptotic upper bound for the error of this approximation for the class of random geometric graphs. Two different approximations for curvature are also presented, and they are proved to converge in probability in the class of random geometric graphs. We propose neighborhood filtering on graphs to improve the accuracy of the previous approximations, and we define two variants of Gaussian smoothing on graphs, which include normalization in order to deal with the nonuniformity of the graphs. The effectiveness of our active contour framework on graphs is demonstrated in the segmentation of regular images and geographical data defined on arbitrary geometric graphs.

“Detecting Missing Method Calls by Majority Rules” proposes a novel approach to detecting missing method calls in object-oriented programs. The proposed approach is based on a majority rule that defines the absence of method calls as a potential violation of the programme’s behavior. The paper demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed approach by conducting experiments on a few case studies, which prove that the proposed approach is accurate at detecting missing method calls and produces fewer false positives than other methods. The paper also discusses the limitations of the proposed approach and offers suggestions for future work. It is a valuable contribution to the analysis and debugging of object-oriented programmes.

We propose an approach to instance-level image segmentation that is constructed on
top of category-level segmentation. Moreover, for each pixel in a semantic
category mask, its corresponding instance bounding box is predicted using a
deep fully convolutional regression network. Thus it follows a similar
pipeline to the popular detect-then-segment methods that first predict
instances' bounding boxes, which are the current state-of-the-art in instance
segmentation. We show that, by using the strength of our state-of-the-art
semantic segmentation models, the proposed approach can obtain similar or
far greater outcomes to detect-then-segment approaches. We make the following
contributions. (i) First, we propose a quick yet effective method to
semantic instance segmentation. (ii) First, we propose an online bootstrapping
approach during training, which is critically critical for ensuring excellent
performance for both semantic category segmentation and instance-level
segmentation. (c) As the performance of semantic category segmentation has a
large effect on the instance-level segmentation, which is the first step
of our method, we train fully convolutional residual networks to obtain the
highest semantic category segmentation accuracy. On the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset,
we obtain the currently highest mean intersection-over-union score of 79.1%. (iv)
We also achieve state-of-the-art results for instance-level segmentation.

Gaze2Segment is a pilot study that explores the potential of integrating eye-tracking technology into medical image segmentation. The study aimed to investigate wether gaze patterns could be used as an additional input modality to improve the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation tasks.
The study involved 10 participants who were asked to perform image segmentation tasks using both traditional computer vision algorithms and eye-tracking technology. Participants' gaze patterns were recorded during the task, and this data was used in conjunction with other input modalities (such as mouse clicks) to generate segmented images.
Results showed that incorporating gaze information into the segmentation process led to significant improvements in accuracy and speed compared to traditional methods. Additionally, the use of gaze patterns allowed for more precise and consistent segmentation across different images and tasks.
Overall, these findings suggest that eye-tracking technology has the potential to significantly enhance the performance of medical image segmentation tasks. Further research is needed to fully explore the benefits and limitations of this approach, but the results of this pilot study provide promising evidence for its effectiveness.

High-quality training data play a crucial role in image segmentation tasks.
Usually, pixel-level annotations are costly, difficult and time-consuming
for the small volume of training data. To minimize labelling cost and reduce
segmentation reliability, interactive segmentation tools have been proposed,
which give the result with just a few clicks. Unfortunately, their efficiency
does not fulfill the standards of practical segmentation tasks in terms of
speed and precision. In this work, we propose EdgeFlow, a novel design
that fully provides interactive information of user clicks with edge-guided
flow. Our method delivers state-of-the-art efficiency without any
post-processing or iterative optimization scheme. Comprehensive studies on
benchmarks only demonstrate the superiority of our methodology. In addition, with
the proposed methodology, we develop an effective interactive segmentation tool for
practical data annotation functions. The source code and tool is available at
http://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleSeg.

A great deal of progress is being made in object recognition. The main difficulty now is to generalise existing methods to new domains, new classes, or new modalities. To avoid having to annotate a new data set for each new case, it is necessary to combine and exploit existing ones that belong to different domains, contain incomplete annotations, and belong to different modalities. This paper considers this as a multi-source domain adaptation and labelling (mDALU) problem and proposes a new method to solve it. Our method consists of two stages: the mDALU stage, and the semi-supervised learning stage. The former involves the fusion of partial knowledge derived from the source domains into the target domain. In the latter, knowledge is transferred into a unified label space by a process of pseudo-label completion. In the former, the negative transfer between the source and target domains is counteracted by three new mechanisms: attention, uncertainty maximisation, and attention-guided adversarial alignment. The method is evaluated on three tasks: image classification, 2D semantic segmentation, and 2D-3D semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate a strong improvement in performance compared to previous methods.

Topological defects are Interfaces joining two conformal field theories, for
which the energy momentum tensor is continuous across the interface. A class of
the topological defects is provided by the interfaces separating two bulk
systems each described by its own Lagrangian, where the two descriptions are
related by a discrete symmetry.
  In this paper we elaborate on the cases in which the discrete symmetry is a
bosonic or a fermionic T- duality. We review how the equations of motion
imposed by the Defect encode the general bosonic t- duality transformations for
toroidal compactifications. We generalize this analysis in some Detail To the
case of topological defects Allowed in coset CFTs, in particular to those
cosets where the gauged group is Either an axial or vector U(1). This is
discussed in both the operator And Lagrangian approaches. We proceed to
construct a defect encoding a fermionic T-duality. We show that the fermionic
T-duality is implemented by the Super-Poincar\'{e} line bundle. The observation
that the exponent of The gauge invariant flux on a defect is a kernel of the
Fourier-Mukai transform of the Ramond-Ramond fields, is generalized to a
fermionic T-duality. This is done via a fiberwise integration on
supermanifolds.

Ordered exchange graphs (OEGs) are a novel type of graph data structure that is designed to model complex exchange processes involving multiple agents or entities. In contrast to traditional graphs, OEGs incorporate an ordering of edges that represents the temporal or logical sequence in which exchanges occur. 

This ordering allows for more accurate and nuanced modeling of real-world exchange processes, such as financial transactions, supply chain management, and social interactions. In this paper, we introduce the concept of OEGs and provide a comprehensive overview of their properties and applications. 

We also present several algorithms for efficiently processing and analyzing OEGs, including algorithms for traversal, clustering, and centrality analysis. We demonstrate the effectiveness of these algorithms through a series of case studies and experiments, and show that OEGs can provide valuable insights into complex exchange processes. Overall, our work on OEGs represents an important step forward in the development of graph-based models for exchange processes. 

By incorporating an ordering of edges, OEGs enable more accurate and nuanced modeling of real-world exchange processes, and provide a powerful tool for analyzing and optimizing these processes.

http://arxiv.org/abs/1507.02843 Direct imaging of nanoscale conductance evolution in ion-gel-gated oxide transistors Cipri K, Gutierrez JC, Garcia AM (26 May 2015) Phys. Rev. B 91 : 245437 http://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.91.245437 Abstract — In this letter we present scanning probe microscopy results that allow us to investigate the gate effect in individual metal atoms dispersed atop dielectric layer on top of an insulating substrate. We find substantial changes on the local electron density when applying different electric biases between the sample and tip. Moreover we observe nonlinear variation of peak height which can be attributed to variations in charge on adsorbed atoms due to applied electrical fields. Our data not only allows assessment of single atom gating but also reveals important insights about the distribution, density and interactions of electrons in the systems under study.

This academic paper focuses on the challenges and solutions  for iris recognition in individuals with various eye pathologies. The paper explores the  impact  of  eye conditions such as cataracts, glaucoma, and aphakia on iris recognition accuracy  and discusses  potential remedies, such as image preprocessing techniques  and adaptive matching algorithms.  Through a comprehensive literature review and case studies, this paper  provides insight  into the unique challenges of iris recognition in  cases of eye pathology  and offers practical recommendations   for improving the reliability and accuracy  of iris recognition systems in   such  situations.
