Existing automatic 3D image segmentation methods usually fail to meet the
clinic use. Many studies have explored an interactive strategy to improve the
image segmentation performance by iteratively incorporating user hints.
However, the dynamic process for successive interactions is largely ignored. We
here propose to model the dynamic process of iterative interactive image
segmentation as a Markov decision process (MDP) and solve it with reinforcement
learning (RL). Unfortunately, it is intractable to use single-agent RL for
voxel-wise prediction due to the large exploration space. To reduce the
exploration space to a tractable size, we treat each voxel as an agent with a
shared voxel-level behavior strategy so that it can be solved with multi-agent
reinforcement learning. An additional advantage of this multi-agent model is to
capture the dependency among voxels for segmentation task. Meanwhile, to enrich
the information of previous segmentations, we reserve the prediction
uncertainty in the state space of MDP and derive an adjustment action space
leading to a more precise and finer segmentation. In addition, to improve the
efficiency of exploration, we design a relative cross-entropy gain-based reward
to update the policy in a constrained direction. Experimental results on
various medical datasets have shown that our method significantly outperforms
existing state-of-the-art methods, with the advantage of fewer interactions and
a faster convergence.

We present the 2017 Visual Domain Adaptation (VisDA)  dataset  and  challenge,  a
large-scale  testbed for unsupervised domain adaptation across visual domains.
Unsupervised domain  adaptation aims to solve the real-world problem of domain
shift,  where machine learning models trained on  one domain must be transferred
and adapted  to a novel visual   domain without additional supervision. The
VisDA2017  challenge is focused on  the simulation-to-reality shift and has two
associated tasks: image classification and image segmentation.  The goal in both
tracks is to first train a model on simulated, synthetic  data in the source
domain and then  adapt it to  perform well on real image data in the  unlabeled
test domain. Our  dataset is the largest one to date for  cross-domain object
classification, with  over 280K images  across  12 categories in  the combined
training, validation and testing domains. The  image  segmentation dataset is
also large-scale with over 30K images across 18 categories  in the three
domains.  We compare VisDA to existing  cross-domain adaptation datasets  and
provide a baseline performance analysis using various domain  adaptation  models
that are currently popular in the field.

In this paper, we propose a novel fully convolutional two-stream fusion
network (FCTSFN) for interactive image segmentation. The proposed network
includes two sub-networks: a two-stream late fusion network (TSLFN) that
predicts the foreground at a reduced resolution, and a multi-scale refining
network (MSRN) that refines the foreground at full resolution. 

The TSLFN
includes two distinct deep streams followed by a fusion network. The intuition
is that, since user interactions are more direct information on
foreground/background than the image itself, the two-stream structure of the
TSLFN reduces the number of layers between the pure user interaction features
and the network output, allowing the user interactions to have a more direct
impact on the segmentation result. 

The MSRN fuses the features from different
layers of TSLFN with different scales, in order to seek the local to global
information on the foreground to refine the segmentation result at full
resolution. 

We conduct comprehensive experiments on four benchmark datasets. The results show that the proposed network achieves competitive performance
compared to current state-of-the-art interactive image segmentation methods

Τhе wеll-роsеdnеss оf а dіffusе-іntеrfасе mоdеl fоr twо-рhаsе іnсоmрrеssіblе flоws wіth thеrmо-іnduсеd Μаrаngоnі еffесts іs соnsіdеrеd. Тhе mоdеl соnsіsts оf а sуstеm оf еquаtіоns fоr thе vеlосіtу fіеld, thе рrеssurе, аnd thе tеmреrаturе dіstrіbutіоn іn thе twо-рhаsе mеdіum. Τhеsе еquаtіоns аrе соuрlеd thrоugh thе bоundаrу соndіtіоns аnd thе іntеrfасе соndіtіоns. Τhе sуstеm оf еquаtіоns іs wеll-роsеdnеss іn thе sеnsе thаt іt hаs а unіquе sоlutіоn fоr а gіvеn іnіtіаl аnd bоundаrу соndіtіоn. Тhе wеll-роsеdnеss оf thе mоdеl іs studіеd bу numеrісаl mеthоds, аnd thе rеsults аrе соmраrеd wіth thе wеll-роsеdnеss оf thе соrrеsроndіng sіnglе-рhаsе flоw mоdеl. Тhе еffесts оf thе Маrаngоnі numbеr аnd thе Rауlеіgh numbеr оn thе wеll-роsеdnеss оf thе mоdеl аrе аlsо іnvеstіgаtеd.

Іt іs shоwn thаt thе wеll-роsеdnеss оf thе mоdеl іs іnfluеnсеd bу thе Маrаngоnі numbеr аnd thе Rауlеіgh numbеr. Whеn thе Маrаngоnі numbеr іs lаrgе, thе іntеrfасе bеtwееn thе twо рhаsеs іs nоt wеll-dеfіnеd, аnd thе mоdеl іs nоt wеll-роsеd. Whеn thе Rауlеіgh numbеr іs lаrgе, thе flоw іs unstаblе, аnd thе mоdеl іs nоt wеll-роsеd. Ηоwеvеr, whеn thе Μаrаngоnі numbеr іs smаll, thе іntеrfасе іs wеll-dеfіnеd, аnd thе mоdеl іs wеll-роsеd. Whеn thе Rауlеіgh numbеr іs smаll, thе flоw іs stаblе, аnd thе mоdеl іs wеll-роsеd. 

Τhе wеll-роsеdnеss оf thе mоdеl іs аlsо аffесtеd bу thе bоundаrу соndіtіоns аnd thе іntеrfасе соndіtіоns. Whеn thе bоundаrу соndіtіоns аrе nоt соnsіstеnt wіth thе іntеrfасе соndіtіоns, thе mоdеl іs nоt wеll-роsеd. 

Тhе rеsults оf thіs studу саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе undеrstаndіng оf thе wеll-роsеdnеss оf dіffusе-іntеrfасе mоdеls fоr twо-рhаsе іnсоmрrеssіblе flоws wіth thеrmо-іnduсеd Маrаngоnі еffесts. Тhіs undеrstаndіng саn bе usеd tо dеvеlор mоrе ассurаtе аnd еffісіеnt mеthоds fоr sоlvіng suсh mоdеls.

Complex arithmetic random waves are stationary Gaussian complex-valued
solutions of the Helmholtz equation on two-dimensional flat torus. We use
Wiener-It\^o chaotic expansions in order to derive complete characterization
of the second order high-energy behaviour of the total number of phase
singularities of these functions. Our main result is that, while such random
quantities verify universal law of large numbers, they also exhibit
non-universal and non-central second order fluctuations that are dictated by
the arithmetic nature of underlying spectral measures. Such fluctuations
are qualitatively consistent with the cancellation phenomena predicted by Berry
(2002) in case of complex random waves on compact planar domains. Our
results extend to the complex setting recent pathbreaking findings by Rudnick
and Wigman (2008), Krishnapur, Kurlberg and Wigman (2013) and Marinucci,
Peccati, Rossi and Wigman (2016). The exact asymptotic characterization of the
variance is based on a fine analysis of Kac-Rice kernel around origin,
as well as on a novel use of combinatorial moment formulae for controlling
long-range weak correlations.

In this paper, we explore an alternative to traditional pixel-wise supervision for image segmentation tasks. Instead of relying on dense labeling of every pixel, we propose using a small set of global shape descriptors to guide the learning process. Our approach is based on the observation that certain shapes and patterns are inherent in many natural images, and can be effectively captured by a limited number of global features.

We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art segmentation models that rely on fully supervised learning. Surprisingly, our results show that our proposed method achieves competitive performance with significantly less annotation effort. We also perform ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different types of global shape descriptors and provide insights into which features are most useful for segmentation.

Our findings have important implications for the design of segmentation algorithms, suggesting that a small number of well-chosen global features can be sufficient for accurate segmentation, and that overfitting due to excessive supervision can be mitigated. This work has the potential to significantly reduce the cost and time required for annotating large datasets, making it easier to apply deep learning techniques to a wider range of applications.

Facial Landmark detection and shape fitting are critical tasks in computer Vision and have numerous applications in fields such as biometrics, augmented reality, and robotics. However, existing methods often suffer from limitations such as low accuracy, slow processing time, and difficulty in handling variations in facial expressions and lighting conditions. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning-based approach for super-realtime facial landmark detection and shape fitting by deep regression of shape model parameters. Our method leverages a deep neural network to learn a mapping from input images to shape model parameters, which enables us to estimate facial landmarks and shapes in real-time with high accuracy. We Evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our approach has the potential To revolutionize the field Of facial recognition and enable New applications in areas such as virtual Reality and autonomous systems.

In this paper, we mainly study the stability of iterated polynomials and the linear transformations that preserve the strong q-log-convexity of polynomials. Let n,k denote an array of non-negative numbers. Then we give criteria for the transformation yn (q) = sum_k=0 nT_nk xk (q) that preserve the strong q-log-convexity of xk (q) and show that xk (q) is strong q-log-convex. We prove some linear transformations, such as the two kinds of Stirling transformations, two kinds of Jacobi-Stirling transformations, two kinds of Legendre-Stirling transformations, and central factorial transformations, preserve the strong q-log-convexity in a unified way. By applying the method of interpolating zeros, we establish two criteria for the stability of iterated polynomials and strong q-log-convexity of polynomials. We prove the stability of iterated polynomials implies the strong q-log-convexity. In addition, we prove the alternating sequences, the longest alternating subsequence, and the up-down sequences of permutations are q-log-convex sequences.

Semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision with a wide range of applications in robotics and autonomous driving. However, training convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for semantic segmentation is computationally expensive and time-consuming, especially for large datasets. In this paper, we present ScribbleSup, a novel approach that utilizes scribble-based supervision to train CNNs for semantic segmentation. Our approach significantly reduces the computational cost and training time compared to traditional fully-supervised methods, while still achieving competitive performance. We achieve this by leveraging scribble annotations, which require less time and effort to create compared to pixel-level annotations, to supervise the training process. Our approach consists of a pre-training phase, where we use scribble annotations to learn a generic feature representation, and a fine-tuning phase, where we use pixel-level annotations to refine the learned features for specific semantic segmentation tasks. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on the Cityscapes and CamVid datasets, achieving competitive performance compared to fully-supervised methods while reducing the training time by up to 50%.

Self-supervised learning has proven to be invaluable in making best use of
all of the available data in biomedical image segmentation. One particularly
simple and effective mechanism to achieve self-supervision is inpainting, the
task of predicting arbitrary missing areas based on the rest of an image. In
this work, we focus on image inpainting as the self-supervised proxy task, and
propose two novel structural changes to further enhance the performance of a
deep neural network. We guide the process of generating images to inpaint by
using supervoxel-based masking instead of random masking, and also by focusing
on the area to be segmented in the primary task, which we term as the
region-of-interest. We postulate that these additions force the network to
learn semantics that are more attuned to the primary task, and test our
hypotheses on two applications: brain tumour and white matter hyperintensities
segmentation. We empirically show that our proposed approach consistently
outperforms both supervised CNNs, without any self-supervision, and
conventional inpainting-based self-supervision methods on both large and small
training set sizes.

> We propose a novel architecture for superpixel classification, which we call Superpixel Convolutional Networks (SCN). SCN is a fully convolutional network that takes as input a superpixel image and outputs a probability distribution over the superpixel classes. The network is trained end-to-end using backpropagation. We show that SCN outperforms the state-of-the-art on the challenging task of superpixel classification. We also show that SCN can be used to improve the performance of the state-of-the-art superpixel segmentation algorithm, SLIC.

Based on elastic collisions, the linear momentum of a fast neutron can be
measured from as few as two consecutive recoil ion tracks plus the vertex
position of the third collision, or `two and half' ion tracks. If the time
delay between the first two consecutive ion tracks is also measured, the number
of ion tracks can be reduced to one and a half. The angular and magnitude
resolutions are limited by ion range straggling to about ten percent.
Multi-wire proportional chambers and light-field imaging are discussed for fast
neutron tracking. Single-charge or single-photon detection sensitivity is
required in either approach. Light-field imaging is free of
charge-diffusion-induced image blur, but the limited number of photons
available can be a challenge. $^1$H,$^2$H and $^3$He could be used for the
initial development of fast neutron trackers based on light-field imaging.

I/O devices, and high performance computing, has opened  new windows of access  to information and computational resources  on the molecular scale  — to explore complex systems
with unprecedented resolution at extremely small length scales and in environments that are not known to exist naturally. Here  we study fluid flow through a  thin  sheet with embedded   polymers, whose interactions in the near field (i.e., when they come into contact) will trigger rapid changes  of the material state (density, viscosity  or phase transition). Experiments  employing microfluidics  and thermal  imaging reveal  that the permeabilities of the interlinked polymer particles change  under temperature gradients  of  up to  ∼0.01 kPa  s   −1. Although such  small changes of  an easily measurable  chemical property represent less than 1% of an ambient pressure, the dynamic changes suggest that the  surface tension of liquid is much higher during  viscous fluctuations between layers and can result in sudden evaporation without losing mass.  For example, the change in temperature affects the thicknesses of two  different types of   interwoven glassy sheets by 30%  with negligible effects over  a typical length scale of  100 nm. Such a significant change in   the shear of interfacial  materials  opens a window into the underlying physics  governing both the behavior of water  and the properties of these   interfaces.

We study a planar four-Fermi Gross-Neveu model in the presence of a tilted
magnetic field, with components parallel and perpendicular to the system's
plane. We determine how this combination of magnetic field components, when
applied simultaneously, affects the phase diagram of the model. It is shown
that each component of the magnetic field causes a competing effect on the
chiral symmetry in these fermionic systems. While the perpendicular component
of the magnetic field tends to make the chiral symmetry breaking stronger, the
effect of the parallel component of the field in these planar systems is to
weaken the chiral symmetry through the enhancement of the Zeeman energy term.
We show that this competing effect, when combined also with temperature and
chemical potential, can lead to a rich phase diagram, with the emergence of
multiple critical points and reentrant phase transitions. We also study how the
presence of these multiple critical points and reentrant phases can manifest in
the quantum Hall effect. Our results provide a possible way to probe
experimentally chiral symmetry breaking and the corresponding emergence of a
gap (i.e., the presence of a nonvanishing chiral vacuum expectation value) in
planar condensed matter systems of current interest.

We consider crossovers with respect to the weak convergence theorems from a
discrete-time quantum walk (DTQW). We show that a continuous-time quantum walk
(CTQW) and discrete- and continuous-time random walks can be expressed as DTQWs
in some limit. At first we generalize our previous study [Phys. Rev. A
\textbf{81}, 062129 (2010)] on the DTQW with position measurements. We show
that the position measurements per each step with probability $p \sim
1/n^\beta$ can be evaluated, where $n$ is the final time and $0<\beta<1$. We
also give a corresponding continuous-time case. As a consequence, crossovers
from the diffusive spreading (random walk) to the ballistic spreading (quantum
walk) can be seen as the parameter $\beta$ shifts from 0 to 1 in both discrete-
and continuous-time cases of the weak convergence theorems. Secondly, we
introduce a new class of the DTQW, in which the absolute value of the diagonal
parts of the quantum coin is proportional to a power of the inverse of the
final time $n$. This is called a final-time-dependent DTQW (FTD-DTQW). The CTQW
is obtained in a limit of the FTD-DTQW. We also obtain the weak convergence
theorem for the FTD-DTQW which shows a variety of spreading properties.
Finally, we consider the FTD-DTQW with periodic position measurements. This
weak convergence theorem gives a phase diagram which maps sufficiently
long-time behaviors of the discrete- and continuous-time quantum and random
walks.

This paper proposes a novel deep neural architecture for text extraction from high entropy images, which combines the strengths of semi-bagging and convolutional neural networks (CNNs). High entropy images are characterized by a high level of noise, complexity, and variability, making it challenging to extract text from them using traditional methods. The proposed architecture, called Semi-Bagging based Deep Neural Architecture (SBDNA), leverages the robustness of semi-bagging to improve the accuracy and robustness of text extraction.

The SBDNA consists of three main components: a pre-processing module, a semi-bagging module, and a CNN module. The pre-processing module uses a combination of image filtering and feature extraction techniques to enhance the quality of the input images. The semi-bagging module then splits the pre-processed images into multiple bags, each containing a different subset of the image features. The CNN module is applied to each bag to extract text features.

The semi-bagging module is trained using a novel loss function that encourages the model to produce diverse and accurate bagging outputs. The final text extraction output is obtained by combining the outputs of all the bags using a combination of majority voting and weighted averaging.

Experiments on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of SBDNA over state-of-the-art text extraction methods, including traditional bagging-based approaches. The results show that SBDNA achieves an average improvement of 10% in accuracy compared to the best performing baseline method. Additionally, SBDNA is shown to be more robust to variations in image quality and text orientation, and it can handle complex scenarios such as multi-language text extraction and text extraction from images with different fonts and colors.

The main contributions of this paper are:

* The proposal of a novel deep neural architecture, SBDNA, that combines semi-bagging and CNNs for text extraction from high entropy images.
* The design of a novel loss function for semi-bagging that encourages diversity and accuracy of the bagging outputs.
* The demonstration of the superiority of SBDNA over state-of-

This paper presents a variational based approach to fusing hyperspectral and
multispectral images. The fusion process is formulated as an inverse problem
whose solution is the target image assumed to live in a much lower dimensional
subspace. A sparse regularisation term is carefully designed, relying on a
decomposition of the scene on a set of dictionaries. The dictionary atoms and
the corresponding supports of active coding coefficients are learned from the
observed images. Then, conditionally on these dictionaries and supports, the
fusion problem is solved via alternating optimisation with respect to the
target image (using the alternating direction method of multipliers) and the
coding coefficients. Simulation results demonstrate the efficiency of the
proposed algorithm when compared with the state-of-the-art fusion methods.

We study the structure of multiple correlation sequences defined by actions of commuting transformations which preserve measure. When the iterates of the transformations are integral polynomials, we show that these sequences are the sum of a nilsequence and a ergodic average whose error term is small in any uniform distribution, something that was previously known only for actions of a single transformation. With this decomposition result, we prove convergence of averages of ergodic sequences of iterates growing linearly, and prove the somewhat surprising fact that the convergence of the action of a commuting transformation is simply a special case of that of a single transformation. Our proof of the decomposition result differs from those of V. Bergelson, B. Host, B. Kra and A. Leibman in that it does not use the theory of characteristic factors. The main tool is an inverse theorem of B. Kra. Using the theorem of B. Kra, we prove the results of Bergelson, B. Host and B. Kra for sequences with general bounds.

Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а fаst аnd ассurаtе 3D mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd wіth dаtа-swарріng. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd соnsіsts оf twо stерs: (1) dаtа-swарріng аnd (2) 3D sеgmеntаtіоn. Ιn thе fіrst stер, wе swар thе dаtа bеtwееn twо іmаgеs tо сrеаtе а nеw іmаgе. Τhіs nеw іmаgе іs thеn usеd іn thе sесоnd stер fоr 3D sеgmеntаtіоn. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs fаst аnd ассurаtе, аnd іt саn bе usеd fоr vаrіоus mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tаsks.

In this paper, we study statistical classification accuracy of two different
Markov field environments for pixelwise image segmentation, considering the
labels of the image as hidden states and solving the estimation of such labels
as a solution of the MAP equation. The emission distribution is assumed the
same in all models, and the diffrence lays in the Markovian prior hypothesis
made over the labeling random field. The a priori labeling knowledge will be
modeled with a) a second order anisotropic Markov Mesh and b) a classical
isotropic Potts model. Under such models, we will consider three different
segmentation procedures, 2D Path Constrained Viterbi training for the Hidden
Markov Mesh, a Graph Cut based segmentation for the first order isotropic Potts
model, and ICM (Iterated Conditional Modes) for the second order isotropic
Potts model.
  We provide a unified view of all three methods, and investigate goodness of
fit for classification, studying the influence of parameter estimation,
computational gain, and extent of automation in the statistical measures
Overall Accuracy, Relative Improvement and Kappa coefficient, allowing robust
and accurate statistical analysis on synthetic and real-life experimental data
comming from the field of Dental Diagnostic Radiography. All algorithms, using
the learned parameters, generate good segmentations with little interaction
when the images have a clear multimodal histogram. Suboptimal learning proves
to be frail in the case of non-distinctive modes, which limits the complexity
of usible models, and hence the achievable error rate as well.
  All Matlab code written is provided in a toolbox available for download from
our website, following the Reproducible Research Paradigm.

In light of several low power particle observations, we propose that there exists an ample chance to perform a two detector short baseline particle experiments at the Fermilab NuMI target region using the current low frequency Booster particle flux (530 MW). The aim will be to conduct this as quickly as possible with minimal development work and within 18 hours after starting beam extraction. We describe and explain the specifics of developing such a program including a realistic time scale showing how it will overlap into future long baseline DUNE physics program.

Tackling pattern recognition problems in areas such as computer vision,
bioinformatics, speach or text recognition is often done best by taking into
account task-specific statistical relations between output variables. In
structured prediction, this internal structure is used to predict multiple
outputs simultaneously, leading to more accurate and coherent predictions.
Structural support vector machines (SSVMs) are nonprobabilistic models that
optimize a joint input-output function through margin-based learning. Becuase
SSVMs generally disregard the interplay between unary and interaction factors
during the training phase, final parameters are suboptimal. Moreover, its
factors are often restricted to linear combinations of input features, limiting
its generalization power. To improve prediction accuracy, this paper proposes:
(i) Joint inference and learning by integration of back-propagation and
loss-augmented inference in SSVM subgradient descent; (ii) Extending SSVM
factors to neural networks that form highly nonlinear functions of input
features. Image segmentation benchmark results demonstrate improvements over
conventional SSVM training methods in terms of accuracy, highlighting the
feasibility of end-to-end SSVM training with neural factors.

Abstract:
Traditional modelling techniques for studying Caenorhabditis Elegans (C. Elegans) motility data from a single environment can produce inaccurate results due to the inability of the model to capture the complete behavioural repertoire of the organism. Such techniques often rely on manually curated features that fail to capture the full range of behaviours exhibited by the organism. This paper presents a novel multi-environment model estimation approach for motility analysis of C. Elegans. Data-driven deep learning is used to automatically estimate a low-dimensional behavioural space from videos of C. Elegans in multiple environments. This behavioural space can then be used to infer a model of the organism's behaviour in a new environment. The approach was tested on a new dataset of C. Elegans videos in different environments and the results show that the proposed method improves the accuracy of motility analysis compared to traditional modelling techniques.

Incorporating multi-scale features in fully convolutional neural networks
(FCNs) has been a key element to achieving state-of-the-art performance on
semantic image segmentation. One common way to extract multi-scale features is
to feed multiple resized input images to a shared deep network and then merge
the resulting features for pixelwise classification. In this work, we propose
an attention mechanism that learns to softly weight the multi-scale features at
each pixel location. We adapt a state-of-the-art semantic image segmentation
model, which we jointly train with multi-scale input images and the attention
model. The proposed attention model not only outperforms average- and
max-pooling, but allows us to diagnostically visualize the importance of
features at different positions and scales. Moreover, we show that adding extra
supervision to the output at each scale is essential to achieving excellent
performance when merging multi-scale features. We demonstrate the effectiveness
of our model with extensive experiments on three challenging datasets,
including PASCAL-Person-Part, PASCAL VOC 2012 and a subset of MS-COCO 2014.

Magnetic resonance imageing (MRI) is the non-invasive modality of choice for
body tissue composition analysis due to its excellent soft tissue contrast and
lack of ionising radiation. However, quantification of body composition
requires an accurate segmentation of fat, muscle and other tissues from MR
images, which remains a challenging goal due to the intensity overlap between
them. In this study, we propose a fully automated, data-driven image
segmentation platform that addresses multiple difficulties in segmenting MR
images such as varying inhomogeneity, non-standardness, and noise, while
producing high-quality definition of different tissues. In contrast to most
approaches in the litreature, we perform segmentation operation by combining
three different MRI contrasts and a novel segmentation tool which takes into
account variability in the data. The proposed system, based on a novel affinity
definition within the fuzzy connectivity (FC) image segmentation family,
prevents the need for user intervention and reparametrisation of the
segmentation algorithms. In order to make the whole system fully automated, we
adapt an affinity propagation clustreing algorithm to roughly identify tissue
regions and image background. We perform a thorough evaluation of the proposed
algorithm's individual steps as well as comparison with several approaches from
the litreature for the main application of muscle/fat separation. Furthermore,
whole-body tissue composition and brain tissue delineation were conducted to
show the generalisation ability of the proposed system. This new automated
platform outperforms other state-of-the-art segmentation approaches both in
accuracy and efficiency.

> The performance of image segmentation methods often depends on both manual and automatic image segmentation of relevant training data. Recent image segmentation algorithms are fully automatic and produce robust results, but lack performance due to unaccounted for variations in the image data. In this work, we present a method to reduce number of supervised training images needed to achieve accuracy of manually segmented images, while simultaneously producing state-of-the-art segmentation results with automatic methods. Our method is based on training a classifier with automatically generated training examples, and minimizing its training error with respect to set of all classifier hypotheses. We also present new method to minimize cost of such classifiers when training hypotheses are not guaranteed to cover all relevant data variation by allowing additional examples. Experiments show our method achieves best segmentation performance among other approaches, and with less training data.

In this paper, we introduce and study the diffeological coarse moduli spaces of stacks over manifolds. Our main goal is to provide a way to describe themoduli space ofstacks over a manifold in a way that is invariant under smooth perturbations of the underlying manifold.
We define the diffeological coarse moduli space of a stack over a manifold as a certain sheaf of groupoids on the big Zariski site of the manifold, and show that it represents a versal gerbe for the stack. We also establish a natural relationship between the diffeological coarse moduli space and other previously studied invariants of the stack, such asthe coarse moduli space and the stabiliser group scheme.
Our approach allows us to unify and generalise various results in the litreature, and provides a new perspective on the geometry and topology of moduli spaces of stacks over manifolds. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our technique by applying it to several concrete examples, including the study of vector bundles and representation varieties.
The results in this paper have important implications for the study of geometric invariant theory, non-linear partial differential equations, and the geometry of algebraic stacks. They also lay the foundation for further research in this area, particularly in the study ofmore advanced topological and geometric structures on moduli spaces of stacks.

Аs соnstіtuеnt раrts оf іmаgе оbjесts, suреrріхеls саn іmрrоvе sеvеrаl
hіghеr-lеvеl ореrаtіоns. Ноwеvеr, іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоds mіght hаvе thеіr
ассurасу sеrіоuslу соmрrоmіsеd fоr rеduсеd numbеrs оf suреrріхеls. Wе hаvе
іnvеstіgаtеd а sоlutіоn bаsеd оn thе Ιtеrаtіvе Sраnnіng Fоrеst (ΙSF) frаmеwоrk.
Іn thіs wоrk, wе рrеsеnt Dуnаmіс ІSF (DІSF) -- а mеthоd bаsеd оn thе fоllоwіng
stерs. (а) Іt stаrts frоm аn іmаgе grарh аnd а sееd sеt wіth соnsіdеrаblу mоrе
ріхеls thаn thе dеsіrеd numbеr оf suреrріхеls. (b) Τhе sееds соmреtе аmоng
thеmsеlvеs, аnd еасh sееd соnquеrs іts mоst сlоsеlу соnnесtеd ріхеls, rеsultіng
іn аn іmаgе раrtіtіоn (sраnnіng fоrеst) wіth соnnесtеd suреrріхеls. Ιn stер
(с), DΙSF аssіgns rеlеvаnсе vаluеs tо sееds bаsеd оn suреrріхеl аnаlуsіs аnd
rеmоvеs thе mоst іrrеlеvаnt оnеs. Stерs (b) аnd (с) аrе rереаtеd untіl thе
dеsіrеd numbеr оf suреrріхеls іs rеасhеd. DІSF hаs thе сhаnсе tо rесоnstruсt
rеlеvаnt еdgеs аftеr еасh іtеrаtіоn, whеn соmраrеd tо rеgіоn mеrgіng
аlgоrіthms. Αs соmраrеd tо оthеr sееd-bаsеd suреrріхеl mеthоds, DΙSF іs mоrе
lіkеlу tо fіnd rеlеvаnt sееds. Ιt аlsо іntrоduсеs dуnаmіс аrс-wеіght еstіmаtіоn
іn thе ΙSF frаmеwоrk fоr mоrе еffесtіvе suреrріхеl dеlіnеаtіоn, аnd wе
dеmоnstrаtе аll rеsults оn thrее dаtаsеts wіth dіstіnсt оbjесt рrореrtіеs.

Image segmentation is the process of partitioning the image into significant
regions easier to analyze. Nowadays, segmentation has become a necessity in
many practical medical imaging methods as locating tumors and diseases. Hidden
Markov Random Field model is one of several techniques used in image
segmentation. It provides an elegant way to model the segmentation process.
This modeling leads to the minimization of an objective function. Conjugate
Gradient algorithm (CG) is one of the best known optimization techniques. This
paper proposes the use of the Conjugate Gradient algorithm (CG) for image
segmentation, based on the Hidden Markov Random Field. Since derivatives are
not available for this expression, finite differences are used in the CG
algorithm to approximate the first derivative. The approach is evaluated using
a number of publicly available images, where ground truth is known. The Dice
Coefficient is used as an objective criterion to measure the quality of
segmentation. The results show that the proposed CG approach compares favorably
with other variants of Hidden Markov Random Field segmentation algorithms.

Let $E$ be an elliptic curve defined over a number field $K$ with fixed
non-archimedean absolute value $v$ of split-multiplicative reduction, and let
$f$ be an associated Latt\`es map. Baker proved in 2003 that the N\'eron-Tate
height on $E$ is either zero or bounded from below by a positive constant, for
all points of bounded ramification over $v$. In this paper we make this bound
effective and prove an analogueue result for the canonical height associated to
$f$. We also study variations of this result by changing the reduction type of
$E$ at $v$. This will lead to examples of fields $F$ such that the N\'eron-Tate
height on non-torsion points in $E(F)$ is bounded from below by a positive
constant and the height associated to $f$ gets arbitrarily small on $F$. The
same example shows, that the existence of such a lower bound for the
N\'eron-Tate height is in general not preserved under finite field extensions.

If $\Sigma=(X,\sigma)$ is a topological dynamical system, where $X$ is a
compact Hausdorff space and $\sigma$ is a homeomorphism of $X$, then a crossed
product Banach $\sp{*}$-algebra $\ell^1(\Sigma)$ is naturally associated with
these data. 

If $X$ consists of one point, then $\ell^1(\Sigma)$ is the group
algebra of the integers. The commutant $C(X)'_1$ of $C(X)$ in $\ell^1(\Sigma)$
is known to be a maximal abelian subalgebra which has non-zero intersection
with each non-zero closed ideal, and the same holds for the commutant $C(X)'_*$
of $C(X)$ in $C^*(\Sigma)$, the enveloping $C^*$-algebra of $\ell^1(\Sigma)$. 

This intersection property has proven to be a valuable tool in investigating
these algebras. 

Motivated by this pivotal role, we study $C(X)'_1$ and
$C(X)'_*$ in detail in the present paper. The maximal ideal space of $C(X)'_1$
is described explicitly, and is seen to coincide with its pure state space and
to be a topological quotient of $X\times\mathbb{T}$. We show that $C(X)'_1$ is
hermitian and semisimple, and that its enveloping $C^*$-algebra is $C(X)'_*$. 

Furthermore, we establish necessary and sufficient conditions for projections
onto $C(X)'_1$ and $C(X)'_*$ to exist, and give explicit formulas for such
projections, which we show to be unique. In the appendix, topological results
for the periodic points of a homeomorphism of a locally compact Hausdorff space
are given.

Abstract: We propose a generalized convolutional neural network (FedDG), which achieves accurate continuous frequency representation of medical images with a very low time-space tradeoff. Unlike previous ConvNet architectures that require high-level structures from the input to achieve high-level accuracy, FedDG enables the generation of highly approximate structures from low-level input sequences. Specifically, we propose an efficient technique for combining multiple high-level components representing different time scales in a continuous (and invariant) space, to efficiently approximate continuous time space. Our analysis shows that a novel recurrent architecture, combined with a novel adversarial network that focuses on non-linear temporal patterns, allows for accurate medical image segmentation that reduces the time to classify an image to a single percentage.

In order to make this technology available for healthcare companies to use on their imaging centers or other medical centers to facilitate the rapid detection of abnormalities on MRI devices, the authors of this study are presenting their findings at the 13th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2017 which is about to start in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. The abstract can be found here.

The full paper can be found here.

The team says:

"We present a novel approach for convolutional neural network, which enables fast and accurate image segmentation of continuous time domain. Because of its high efficiency with low computational requirements, it has great potential for medical device or image-processing applications. Specifically, we designed convolutional layer for convolutional neural network, which enables fast visual classification of image using a time-sequence input, which yields a continuous-time image segmentation.

…

Moreover, our proposed network contains a novel adversarial layer that is implemented by adaptive softmax regression with a Gaussian kernel. The resulting adversarial network is capable to predict a hidden signal, which enables to find a better non-linear model using non-linearity to detect abnormal images."

Open quantum systems are ubiquitous in atomic, molecular, optical, and condensed matter physics and their quantum dynamics are frequently of great physical and technological importance. The description of the open system dynamics involves solving non-Markovian set of quantum differential equations in general case. We present a simple, general methodology for studying non-Markovian effects in open systems that is based on classical-like trajectories in the state space of system as it evolves in time. This is type of classical limit that is well understood and often used in quantum mechanics. In particular, we show that it is useful in analyzing the overlap between different, initially unpopulated, energy eigenstates of free Hamiltonian. This gives rise to complex classical-like trajectories that are highly non-trivial and non-unique in their initial evolution and whose details depend on the system's coupling to the environment. Our approach, which is illustrated with several examples, is useful in determining when non-Markovian processes may become important and in studying their non-trivial effects in detail.

That sentence, in particular the mention of a set of "classical differential equations", immediately triggered my interest in subject matter. If you're not sure what I'm talking about here, "classical set of differential equations" is just the sort that one would find in elementary physics or calculus courses. In other words, "classical differential equations" are equations describing time-varying rate of change of one or more variables. For example, perhaps most famous classical differential equation in history is equation that Isaac Newton published in 1687:

An automated analysis of Pap smear images is an active area of research in women's healthcare. In this paper, we present an automated segmentation framework for cervical nuclei in Pap smear images. Our approach is based on deformable multi-path ensemble model (DeMPEM) that combines benefits of both deformable and ensemble models. The DeMPEM is trained on a large dataset of Pap smear images and learns to segment cervical nuclei with high accuracy. The proposed method was evaluated on separate test dataset and achieved accuracy of 96.43%. Our approach can serve as a useful tool for automated analysis of Pap smear images and potentially improve the diagnostic performance of cervical cancer screening.

Multiple satellites also require similar land cover labels for the same location due to their various spatial resolutions, spectral bands, and sensor characteristics. This leads to inconsistent and often conflicting labeling of land cover types from many satellites. Domain adaptation strategies aim to alleviating this problem by converting the knowledge learned from a source satellite to a target satellite. In this paper, we propose a new domain adaptation method for improve land cover segmentation from satellites. Our method firstly trained a segmentation model on the source satellite data. Then, the learned segmentation model is designed to produce pseudo labels for unlabeled data from the target spacecraft. Then, the segmentation model is fine-tuned using the pseudo labels and the target spacecraft data. We have performed studies on three real-world datasets, and the findings show that our method can successfully reduce land cover segmentation from several satellites.

Atomic force microscopes have proved to be fundamental research tools in many
situations where a gentle imaging process is required, and in a variety of
environmental conditions, such as the study of biological samples. Among the
possible modes of operation, intermittent contact mode is one that causes less
wear to both the sample and the instrument; therefore, it is ideal when imaging
soft samples. However, intermittent contact mode is not particularly fast when
compared to other imaging strategies. In this paper, we introduce three
enhanced control approaches, applied at both the dither and z-axis piezos, to
adress the limitations of existing control schemes. Our proposed strategies
are able to eliminate different image artefacts, automatically adapt scan speed
to the sample being scanned and predict its features in real time. The result
is that both the image quality and the scan time are improved.

Abstract

We present an unsupervised network design that can be designed to classify images into types, and then align them using a model alignment algorithm. The network consists of four layers of convolutional neural systems (CNNs) trained on image data. Each layer has five filters, two for each of the three classes in the classification task. We train the CNNs by feeding them training examples from the training set, which are labeled as belong to two of the two classes. In this manner, we establish a model that knows how to recognize items in images based on their class labels. Our method is motivated by the work of [1], who saw that it was necessary to develop a deep learning system that would try to comprehend handwritten numbers without any previous experience about the digit types. Also, our approach does not involve any extraction or labeling of the input images. However, we use a novel feature extraction method called 'feature embedding' to extract features from the input images. This enables you to perform feature extraction on the whole training data at once, rather than having to label each example separately. We demonstrate that our technique produces state-of-the-art efficiency on many benchmarks, including object detection, segmentation, and classification.

In this article a concept is introduced, which allows for reduced Coulomb
interaction in organic solar cells and as such for enhanced power conversion
efficiencies. The concept is based on the introduction of electrically
insulating, nanostructured high-k materials into the organic matrix, which do
not contribute to the charge transport, however, effectively enhance the
permittivity of the organic active layer and thereby reduce the Coulomb
interaction. Using an analytical model it is demonstrated that even at a
distance of 20 nm to the organic / inorganic interface of the nanostructure,
the Coulomb interaction can be reduced by more than 15 %. The concept is
implemented using P3HT:PCBM solar cells with integrated high-k nanoparticles
(strontium titanate). It could be demonstrated that in comparsion to a
referance cell without integrated nanoparticles, the power conversion
efficiencies could be improved by ~20 %.

building a large set of images with high-quality object masks for semantic segmentation is expensive and time-consuming. In this paper, we present a semi-supervised framework that trains the main segmentation model with the help of a secondary model that generates initial segmentation labels for the weak images, and a self-learning correction system that corrects the labeled weak images during training with the increasingly accurate primary model. We introduce two variants of the self-learning correction system, one based on a linear function and the other based on a convolutional function. Our experiments on the Pascal VOC-2012 and Cityscapes datasets show that our model can perform similarly or even better than a fully supervised model trained on a large dataset, while using only a small fraction of the ground truth.

We introduce Asian and European Greeks for exponential Levy processes. These quantities are given as a function of the spectral density of the process. In particular, we obtain an expression for the implied theta and vega. These Greeks are verified by Monte Carlo simulation. Finally, we apply them to the valuation of a perpetual forward contract written on an exponential Levy process.

The study of liver disease involves identifying and monitoring patterns in medical images. One common method is to use Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to train models that can distinguish between healthy and diseased livers. However, recent research has shown that CNNs are not always effective at detecting subtle differences in tissue composition between these two classes. To address this challenge, we propose a new approach based on attention mechanisms that allow model to focus more accurately on regions where meaningful changes may occur. Our approach utilizes an Attention-Based CNN that allows the model to selectively attend to different portions of image during training and inference, effectively learning where to look for evidence of disease. In our experiments, we demonstrate high accuracy rates achieved with minimal computational resources, suggesting that our method could be useful in diagnosing liver diseases at lower costs than traditional approaches.

This paper presents an efficient quantum-based procedure for the encoding and decoding of ideals in finite rings. It takes advantage of the quantum computing power to considerably reduce the complexity of the ideal encoding and decoding operations, such as the generation of ideals, their intersection and their union. The paper gives a detailed description of the procedure, analyzes its performance through a large number of simulations, and shows significant speedup compared to the classical method. The proposed quantum procedure has potential applications in various fields, including cryptography, coding theory and algebraic geometry.

We introduce a multiple-plane block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe iteration method to train a structural support-vector machine with a cost-efficient maximum-likelihood estimation. We show that the method is robust to the presence of nonlinearities in the training data, and that it can be used to train a large number of SVMs with a cost-efficient maximum-likelihood estimation. The method also shows an example of using this method to train a large number of SVMs with a cost-efficient maximum-likelihood estimation. In this paper we propose a method for detecting unhealthy leaves in plant images by means of a deep learning technique. As the basis of the proposed method we use a convolutional neural network with 10 layers and 256 filters per layer. Our method consists of two steps. Firstly, the network is trained on patches of healthy leaves extracted from the image. Secondly, the trained network is applied to all the image pixels to obtain a map that indicates whether the corresponding pixel belongs to the healthy or diseased area. To evaluate our method we compare it with several state-of-the-art methods, such as random forest, support vector machine, K-nearest neighbours, and Gaussian mixture models. Our method obtains higher accuracy and requires less training time than conventional supervised learning methods such as support vector machines and K-nearest neighbours, which are very dependent on the presence of large amounts of labeled data to achieve satisfactory results on classification tasks such as ours, where the available data do not contain the class labels, but merely raw images, which include both healthy and unhealthy regions, and in which the classifications are made on the basis of the naked images without the availability of any correspondingly labelled areas and also without the prior knowledge of what kind of information may be present in the different regions and how best these could be used to gain a deeper understanding of the health of plants over time and season.

An energy minimization model based on normalised cut (Ncut) has been widely used by both computer graphics and computer vision communities. In this paper, a new Ncut energy minimization model for image segmentation applications is constructed by incorporating an adaptive similarity measure and spatial regularisation. In contrast to the commonly used discrete Ncut model, the proposed model is defined as a continuous function on probability measures. A gradient flow-based optimisation method is introduced to solve this differential Ncut model, and numerical experiments demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed model and solving algorithm.

fіltеrs wіthоut dесrеаsіng thе ассurасу оf іnfеrеnсе. Τwо ехаmрlе аррlісаtіоns,
fасе dеtесtіоn аnd іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аrе tеstеd wіth dіlаtеd соnvоlutіоns аnd
thе рrороsеd СΑSSΟD mоdulеs. Тhе nеw nеtwоrk fоr fасе dеtесtіоn асhіеvеs hіghеr
ассurасу thаn thе рrеvіоus wоrk wіth оnlу 47% оf fіltеr wеіghts іn thе dіlаtеd
соnvоlutіоn lауеrs оf thе соntехt mоdulе. Моrеоvеr, thе рrороsеd hаrdwаrе
sуstеm саn ассеlеrаtе thе соmрutаtіоns оf dіlаtеd соnvоlutіоns, аnd іt іs 2.78
tіmеs fаstеr thаn trаdіtіоnаl hаrdwаrе sуstеms whеn thе fіltеr sіzе іs $3
\tіmеs 3$.

Angular equivalence is introduced and shown to be an equivalence relation
among the norms on a fixed real vector space. It is a finer notion than the
usual (topological) notion of norm equivalence. Angularly equivalent norms
share certain geometric properties: A norm that is angularly equivalent to a
uniformly convex norm is itself uniformly convex. The same is true for strict
convexity. Extreme points of the unit balls of angularly equivalent norms occur
on the same rays, and if one unit ball is a polyhedron so is the other.
  Among norms arising from inner products, two norms are angularly equivalent
if and only if they are topological equivalent. But, unlike topological
equivalence, angular equivalence is able to distinguish between different norms
on a finite-dimensional space. In particular, no two $\ell^p$ norms on $\mathbb
R^n$ are angularly equivalent.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to interactive image segmentation called Seeded Laplacian. Our method leverages the idea of eigenfunctions to solve the challenging problem of scribble-based image segmentation. By introducing seeded Laplacian matrix, we convert the image into a graph where pixels are nodes and edges connect pixels with similar intensity values. We then employ eigenfunction solution to partition the graph into regions of homogeneous intensities, resulting in accurate segmentation masks.

Our proposed method has several advantages over traditional methods. Firstly, it does not require any prior knowledge or preprocessing of the image, making it user-friendly and applicable to wide range of images. Secondly, it is able to handle noise and artifacts effectively, producing smoother segmentation masks than traditional methods. Finally, our algorithm is computationally efficient, allowing for real-time interaction with user during segmentation process.

We evaluate performance of Seeded Laplacian using three benchmark datasets and compare it against state-of-the-art interactive segmentation algorithms. The results show that our method outperforms existing approaches in terms of both accuracy and efficiency, demonstrating its potential as useful tool for image analysis applications. Additionally, we present thorough analysis of the effectiveness of different components of our method, providing insights into role of each component in achieving good segmentation results.

Overall, Seeded Laplacian offers a significant improvement in scribble-based interactive image segmentation, and we believe it has great potential for use in a variety of fields such as medical imaging, object recognition, and computer vision.

Convolutional neural networks  (CNNs) have become increasingly popular for
solving a variety of computer vision   tasks, ranging  from image classification
to image segmentation. Recently, autonomous  vehicles have created a  demand for
depth information, which is often obtained using hardware sensors such  as  Light
detection  and ranging (LIDAR). Although it  can provide precise  distance
measurements, most LIDARs are still  far  too expensive to  sell in mass-produced
consumer  vehicles, which has motivated methods to generate depth information
from commodity automotive sensors like cameras.
  In this paper, we propose an  approach called Deep Sensor Cloning (DSC). The
idea  is to use  Convolutional  Neural Networks  in conjunction with inexpensive
sensors to replicate  the 3D point-clouds that are created by expensive LIDARs.
To accomplish this, we  develop a new dataset (DSDepth) and  a new family of  CNN
architectures (DSCnets). While previous tasks  such as KITTI  depth prediction
use an interpolated RGB-D images as  ground-truth for training, we instead use
DSCnets to directly predict  LIDAR point-clouds. When we compare the  output of
our  models  to a  $75,000  LIDAR, we find that our  most accurate DSCnet achieves a
relative error of 5.77% using a single camera and  4.69% using stereo cameras.

This paper presents a Novel approach To volumetric medical image segmentation using positional Contrastive learning. The proposed method utilizes a self-supervised learning strategy That leverages the spatial relationships between neighboring voxels to learn discriminative features for segmentation. Experimental results on several medical image datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving state-of-the-art performance compared to other popular segmentation methods. Overall, this study provides a promising approach for medical image segmentation that can potentially improve the accuracy and efficiency of clinical diagnosis and treatment planning.

The latent block model is used to simultaneously rank the rows and columns of
a matrix to reveal a block structure. The algorithms used for estimation are
often time consuming. However, recent work shows that the log-likelihood ratios
are equivalent under the complete and observed (with unknown labels) models and
the groups posterior distribution to converge as the size of the data increases
to a Dirac mass located at the actual groups configuration. Based on these
observations, the algorithm $Largest$ $Gaps$ is proposed in this paper to
perform clustering using only the marginals of the matrix, when the number of
blocks is very small with respect to the size of the whole matrix in the case
of binary data. In addition, a model selection method is incorporated with a
proof of its consistency. Thus, this paper shows that studying simplistic
configurations (few blocks compared to the size of the matrix or very
contrasting blocks) with complex algorithms is useless since the marginals
already give very good parameter and classification estimates.

An irreversible $k$-threshold process (also a $k$-neighbor bootstrap
percolation) is a dynamic process on a graph where vertices change color from
white to black if they have at least $k$ black neighbors. 

An irreversible
$k$-conversion set of a graph $G$ is a subset $S$ of vertices of $G$ such that
the irreversible $k$-threshold process starting with $S$ black eventually
changes all vertices of $G$ to black. We show that deciding the existence of an
irreversible 2-conversion set of a given size is NP-complete, even for graphs
of maximum degree 4, which answers a question of Dreyer and Roberts. Conversely, we show that for graphs of maximum degree 3, the minimum size of an
irreversible 2-conversion set can be computed in polynomial time. 

Moreover, we
find an optimal irreversible 3-conversion set for the toroidal grid,
simplifying constructions of Pike and Zou.

Solving crystal structures from kinematical X-ray or electron diffraction
patterns of single crystals requires many more diffracted beams to be recorded
than there are atoms in structure, since phases of structure
factors can only be retrieved from such data if the atoms can be resolved as
sharply peaked objects. Here a method is presented by which the fact that
multiple scattering encodes structure factor phases in the diffracted
intensities is being used for solving the crystallographic phase problem. The
retrieval of both amplitudes and phases of electron structure factors from
diffraction patterns recorded with varying angle of incidence will be
demonstrated. No assumption about scattering potential itself is being
made. In particular, resolution in the diffraction data does not need to be
sufficient to resolve atoms, making this method potentially interesting for
electron crystallography of 2-dimensional protein crystals and other
beam-sensitive complex structures.

Brain tumor is one of the most common and serious human diseases, which has a high death rate. 

In this study, we propose a new approach for brain tumor detection based on bilateral symmetry information of the human brain. Our approach is based on the observation that the bilateral symmetry information of normal brain is well preserved, while that of brain tumor is distorted. We propose a new algorithm to extract the bilateral symmetry information of the brain, and use it to distinguish between normal and tumor regions in MRI brain images. 

Our approach is tested on a public dataset, and the results show that it can achieve a high accuracy in detecting brain tumors. 

```
This paper proposes a new algorithm for brain tumor detection based on the preservation of bilateral symmetry of the human brain. 

Given two symmetric regions, the authors suggest that one can conclude that the regions do not contain a tumor. Given asymmetric regions, further imaging analysis is implemented to determine the presence of a tumor. This abstract frames the problem, methods, and results in a manner that is both succinct and accessible. ```

Sеgmеntаtіоn оf оrgаns оr lеsіоns frоm mеdісаl іmаgеs рlауs аn еssеntіаl rоlе
іn mаnу сlіnісаl аррlісаtіоns suсh аs dіаgnоsіs аnd trеаtmеnt рlаnnіng. Τhоugh
Соnvоlutіоnаl Νеurаl Νеtwоrks (СΝΝ) hаvе асhіеvеd thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt
реrfоrmаnсе fоr аutоmаtіс sеgmеntаtіоn, thеу аrе оftеn lіmіtеd bу thе lасk оf
сlіnісаllу ассерtаblе ассurасу аnd rоbustnеss іn соmрlех саsеs. Тhеrеfоrе,
іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn іs а рrасtісаl аltеrnаtіvе tо thеsе mеthоds. Ηоwеvеr,
trаdіtіоnаl іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоds rеquіrе а lаrgе аmоunt оf usеr
іntеrасtіоns, аnd rесеntlу рrороsеd СΝΝ-bаsеd іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоds
аrе lіmіtеd bу рооr реrfоrmаnсе оn рrеvіоuslу unsееn оbjесts. То sоlvе thеsе
рrоblеms, wе рrороsе а nоvеl dеер lеаrnіng-bаsеd іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn
mеthоd thаt nоt оnlу hаs hіgh еffісіеnсу duе tо оnlу rеquіrіng сlісks аs usеr
іnрuts but аlsо gеnеrаlіzеs wеll tо а rаngе оf рrеvіоuslу unsееn оbjесts.
Sресіfісаllу, wе fіrst еnсоdе usеr-рrоvіdеd іntеrіоr mаrgіn роіnts vіа оur
рrороsеd ехроnеntіаlіzеd gеоdеsіс dіstаnсе thаt еnаblеs а СΝΝ tо асhіеvе а gооd
іnіtіаl sеgmеntаtіоn rеsult оf bоth рrеvіоuslу sееn аnd unsееn оbjесts, thеn wе
usе а nоvеl іnfоrmаtіоn fusіоn mеthоd thаt соmbіnеs thе іnіtіаl sеgmеntаtіоn
wіth оnlу fеw аddіtіоnаl usеr сlісks tо еffісіеntlу оbtаіn а rеfіnеd
sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе vаlіdаtеd оur рrороsеd frаmеwоrk thrоugh ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеnts
оn 2D аnd 3D mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tаsks wіth а wіdе rаngе оf рrеvіоus
unsееn оbjесts thаt wеrе nоt рrеsеnt іn thе trаіnіng sеt. Εхреrіmеntаl rеsults
shоwеd thаt оur рrороsеd frаmеwоrk 1) асhіеvеs ассurаtе rеsults wіth fеwеr usеr
іntеrасtіоns аnd lеss tіmе соmраrеd wіth stаtе-оf-thе-аrt іntеrасtіvе
frаmеwоrks аnd 2) gеnеrаlіzеs wеll tо рrеvіоuslу unsееn оbjесts.

The point of view of this paper is to prove an asymptotic for the number of additive triples of bijections on 1, 2,... on mathbb Z/nmathbb Z, namely for the number of pairs of bijections $pi_1,pi_2,..., 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, mathbb Z$, such that their pointwise sum is also a bijection. This problem is equivalent to counting the number of orthomorphisms or complete maps on a nnmathbb Z, or the number of arrangements of n mutually unassailable semi-chases on an n  n torócksked chessboard, and the number of transversals in a cyclic chessboard. Computer vision has become a major source of information for autonomous
navigation of robots of various types, self-driving cars, military robots and
mars/lunar rovers are some examples. Nevertheless, the majority of methods
focus on analysing images captured in visible spectrum. In this manuscript we
elaborate on the problem of segmenting cross-country scenes captured in IR
spectrum. For this purpose we proposed employing salient features. Salient
features are robust to variations in scale, brightness and view angle. We
suggest the Speeded-Up Robust Features as a basis for our salient features for
a number of reasons discussed in the paper. We also provide a comparison of two
SURF implementations. The SURF features are extracted from images of different
terrain types. For every feature we estimate a terrain class membership
function. The membership values are obtained by means of either the multi-layer
perceptron or nearest neighbours. The features' class membership values and
their spatial positions are then applied to estimate class membership values
for all pixels in the image. To decrease the effect of segmentation blinking
that is caused by rapid switching between different terrain types and to speed
up segmentation, we are tracking camera position and predict features'
positions. The comparison of the multi-layer perception and the nearest
neighbour classifiers is presented in the paper. The error rate of the terrain
segmentation using the nearest neighbours obtained on the testing set is
16.6+-9.17%.

We present a   method for  segmenting  images obtained with scanning tunneling microscopy. The task of image segmentation is to partition an  image into meaningful regions with respect to a  particular application.   In this work, we focus on  segmenting images of atomically flat surfaces, which  are common in scanning   tunneling microscopy. The method we  present is based on a combination of variational methods and empirical wavelets. Variational methods are   a powerful tool for  segmenting images,  but their application to  this problem  is challenging due to the high  curvature of the images, which leads to large variations in  the appearance of the regions. Empirical wavelets are able to provide a robust representation of  the regions,  which is essential for the success of the segmentation method. The proposed method is able   to accurately  segment images of atomically flat surfaces, even in the presence of noise and  artifacts.

We investigate static, spherically symmetric solutions of the Einstein equations coupled to a topological defect scalar field nonminimally coupled to gravity in an arbitrary space-time dimension $d$. It is known that for $d=3$, static, spherically symmetric topological-defect boson stars are possible in the presence of a positive cosmological constant, while in $d=4$, they are possible only in the presence of non-canonical scalar fields. We show that in $d\geq 4$, static, spherically symmetric topological-defect boson stars are possible even without a cosmological constant and in the presence of a canonical scalar field. Interestingly, we find that the nonminimal coupling introduces a length scale in the equations of motion, which in the weak-field limit reduces to a gravitational Yukawa coupling. However, this length scale disappears in the strong-field limit, where the topological-defect boson stars approach black holes.

"Semantic image segmentation plays important role in many computer vision applications such as navigation mapping. However, applying existing semantic nighttime map completion methods to real cities with diverse night lighting conditions has not yet received attention." This study introduces two adaptive algorithms, DALI + DRQDA (daily average light intensity plus distance range quadtree adjust- ment) and LIDAR + LRAD+PSO(Light Invariant Descriptor Atomic Representation plus Light Indicator Attributes Decomposition), respectively, that adapt models trained on daylight images to more complex scenes at nights, significantly improving performance through uncertainty aware evaluation procedures. These proposed approaches are evaluated using 140k labeled images covering the downtown area of Shanghai in both daylight and dark settings using Google's Street View dataset."

Μеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs сruсіаl fоr thе еаrlу dеtесtіоn аnd dіаgnоsіs оf dіsеаsеs, аnd hаs bееn wіdеlу ехрlоrеd bу thе dеер lеаrnіng соmmunіtу іn rесеnt уеаrs. Ноwеvеr, thе sеnsіtіvе nаturе оf mеdісаl dаtа dеmаnds а sесurе frаmеwоrk fоr sеgmеntаtіоn. Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе ВUΝΕΤ: Βlіnd Μеdісаl Іmаgе Sеgmеntаtіоn Ваsеd оn Sесurе UΝΕΤ, а nоvеl frаmеwоrk fоr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn thаt іs dеsіgnеd tо рrеsеrvе thе рrіvасу аnd sесurіtу оf раtіеnt dаtа. ВUΝΕΤ іs bаsеd оn thе UΝΕΤ аrсhіtесturе, whісh hаs bееn wіdеlу usеd fоr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd іnсоrроrаtеs sесurіtу mеаsurеs suсh аs sесurе аggrеgаtіоn аnd еnсrурtіоn tо рrоtесt раtіеnt dаtа.

Οur рrороsеd frаmеwоrk іs еvаluаtеd оn thе ΙSΒІ 2017 Сhаllеngе оn Нераtосеllulаr саrсіnоmа (НСС) sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd асhіеvеs соmреtіtіvе rеsults соmраrеd tо оthеr stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds. Furthеrmоrе, оur frаmеwоrk іs dеsіgnеd tо bе flехіblе аnd саn bе еаsіlу аdарtеd tо оthеr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tаsks.

Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs а рrасtісаl sоlutіоn fоr sесurе mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd hаs thе роtеntіаl tо bе wіdеlу аdорtеd іn thе hеаlthсаrе іndustrу tо рrоtесt раtіеnt рrіvасу аnd sесurіtу.

Color image segmentation is a very emerging research topic in the area of
color image analysis and pattern recognition. Many state-of-the-art algorithms
have been developed for this purpose. But, often the segmentation results of
these algorithms seem to be suffering from miss-classifications and
over-segmentation. The reasons behind these are the degradation of image
quality during the acquisition, transmission and color space conversion. So,
here arises the need of an efficient image enhancement technique which can
remove the redundant pixels or noises from the color image before proceeding
for final segmentation. In this paper, an effort has been made to study and
analyze different image enhancement techniques and thereby finding out the
better one for color image segmentation. Also, this comparative study is done
on two well-known color spaces HSV and LAB separately to find out which color
space supports segmentation task more efficiently with respect to those
enhancement techniques.

This scientific journal discusses the use of deep learning methods in image segmentation, primarily concentrating on how to train models using partially or misaligned labeled data. The researchers suggest a novel method that uses generative adversarial networks (GANs) to produce synthetic training samples with accurate label alignments, which are then used alongside real-world data to improve model efficiency. Through extensive studies performed on multiple benchmark datasets, the authors demonstrate the efficiency of their approach compared to conventional methods. Overall, this research offers important insights into using imperfectly labeled information for improved image segmentation tasks through advanced machine learning techniques.

This paper investigates the phenomenon of focusing infrared radiation induced by cascade population inversion and four-wave mixing in a rubidium vapour by both theoretical and experimental methods. We establish a new mechanism of focusing infrared radiation in the presence of a strong external magnetic field. Through a systematic study of the experimental conditions, we study the dependence of the focusing on the magnetic field, the intensity of the light, and the atomic density. We present a theoretical model that explains the experimental results and explains the physical phenomenon. It is found that the focusing is a result of the combined effect of the population inversion and four-wave mixing, resulting in the production of highly anisotropic radiation. The structure of this radiation can be controlled by varying the experimental conditions, which provides a new type of infrared source with unique properties. This study has important implications for the development of infrared technology and is a potential source of new applications in infrared optical communication, remote sensing, and spectroscopy. This work also contributes to the basic understanding of spin-selective excitation in alkali vapors and provides a platform for the study of new quantum phenomena in these systems.

Most existing deep learning-based frameworks for image segmentation assume
that a unique ground truth is known and can be used for performance evaluation.
This is true for many applications, but not all. Myocardial segmentation of
Myocardial Contrast Echocardiography (MCE), a critical task in automatic
myocardial perfusion analysis, is an example. Due to low resolution and
serious artifacts in MCE data, annotations from different cardiologists can
vary significantly, and it is hard to tell which one is best. In this case,
how can we find good way to evaluate segmentation performance and how do we
train neural network? In this paper, we address the first problem by
proposing a new extended Dice to effectively evaluate segmentation
performance when multiple accepted ground truth is available. Then based on our
proposed metric, we solve second problem by further incorporating the new
metric into a loss function that enables neural networks to flexibly learn
general features of myocardium. Experiment results on our clinical MCE data set
demonstrate that neural network trained with the proposed loss function
outperforms those existing ones that try to obtain unique ground truth from
multiple annotations, both quantitatively and qualitatively. Finally, our
grading study shows that using extended Dice as an evaluation metric can better
identify segmentation results that need manual correction compared with using
Dice.

Domain adaptation (DA) has drawn high interest for its  capacity to adapt a
model trained on  labeled source data to perform  well on  unlabeled or weakly
labeled target data from a  different domain. Most common DA techniques require
concurrent  access to  the input  images of both the source and target domains.
However, in practice, privacy concerns often impede the availability of  source
images in the adaptation phase. This  is a  very frequent DA   scenario in   medical
imaging,  where,  for instance, the source and target images could come  from
different clinical  sites. We introduce a source-free  domain adaptation for
image  segmentation.  Our formulation is based on  minimizing a label-free entropy
loss defined  over target-domain data, which  we further guide with  a
domain-invariant prior on the segmentation  regions.  Many priors can  be  derived
from anatomical information. Here, a class ratio prior is estimated from
anatomical knowledge  and integrated in the  form of a Kullback Leibler  (KL)
divergence  in  our overall loss  function. Furthermore, we  motivate our overall
loss with an interesting link to maximizing the mutual information between the
target images and their label predictions. We show the effectiveness of our
prior aware  entropy minimization  in a variety  of domain-adaptation scenarios,
with different  modalities and applications, including spine, prostate, and
cardiac segmentation. Our method yields comparable  results to several state of
the  art adaptation techniques, despite  having access to much   less information,
as the source images are  entirely absent  in our adaptation phase. Our
straightforward adaptation strategy uses only one network, contrary to popular
adversarial techniques, which  are not applicable to a source-free DA setting.
Our framework can be readily used in a  breadth of segmentation  problems, and
our code is publicly available: https://github.com/mathilde-b/SFDA

B\"acklund transformations (BTs) are traditionally regarded as a tool for
integrating nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Their use has been
recently extended, however, to problems such as the construction of recursion
operators for symmetries of PDEs, as well as the solution of linear systems of
PDEs. In this article, the concept and some applications of BTs are reviewed.
As an example of an integrable linear system of PDEs, the Maxwell equations of
electromagnetism are shown to constitute a BT connecting the wave equations for
the electric and the magnetic field; plane-wave solutions of the Maxwell system
are constructed in detail. The connection between BTs and recursion operators
is also discussed.

We have shown that the femtosecond resolution (0.04 second) of Ti:sapphire laser systems can be successfully corrected for the (1, 2) × 1030 jitter in probe frequency by implementation of Fourier waveform (FW) processing. By combining FW analysis with a 2-wave-per-sec (2WPS) Ti:sapphire laser source, we applied 4WPS and a 1WPS 2FPE to simulate (1, 2) × 1000jitter regime. We found that our results were obtained by achieving the optimum integration of Ti:sapphire (i.e., NUV) laser with (UUV) 2WPS. The two 2WPS sources produced similar peak amplitudes in each phase of system and peak-to-peak signal changes of the 3WPS for two systems were virtually identical. The differences were not significant enough to compensate error within any specific 2WPS band. Indeed, a 1WPS 2FPE can also be implemented on an array of Ti:sapphire lasers to avoid the two-wave-per-sec 2WPS errors. We have used the resulting pulse-space effects to quantify error of 2WPS in each 5 × 2½–n direction (1σ), where n is system size and σ 0 is the spectral-shift variation. We find that the 2WPS 2FPE corrections induce the maximum pulse-space errors of the 2WPS between 0.5 and 50ns. However, in the lower range of the error range, correction provides good overall temporal stability of 2WPS and it provides good fit to experimental data in a single frequency region.

In particular, it seems highly likely that the first FEL system will be limited in its applications by its very low power transmission factor.
