We study lexicographically least infinite $a/b$-power-free word on the
alphabet of non-negative integers. Frequently this word is a fixed point of a
uniform morphism, or closely related to one. For example, the lexicographically
least $7/4$-power-free word is a fixed point of $50847$-uniform morphism. We
identify the structure of the lexicographically least $a/b$-power-free word for
three infinite families of rationals $a/b$ as well many "sporadic" rationals
that do not seem to belong to general families. To accomplish this, we develop
an automated procedure for proving $a/b$-power-freeness for morphisms of a
certain form, both for explicit and symbolic rational numbers $a/b$. Finally,
we establish connection to words on finite alphabet. Namely, the
lexicographically least $27/23$-power-free word is in fact a word on finite
alphabet $\{0, 1, 2\}$, and its sequence of letters is $353$-automatic.

Abstract: A new image segmentation method is presented which combines the advantages of both conservative and aggressive cutting techniques. The proposed method, which is called the Compassionately Conservative Balanced Cuts (CCBC) method, is a multi-stage approach, beginning with a conservative initial cut and gradually refining it by means of an aggressive cut. The goal of this approach is to achieve a balance between accuracy and speed. The proposed method is tested on a variety of image data sets and is shown to perform significantly better than the current state of the art. The practical implications of the proposed method are discussed for various applications, including medical image analysis, satellite image analysis and image processing of motion pictures. The experiments indicate that the CCBC method is powerful and very flexible, being particularly suited for resource-constrained and time-constrained applications. This paper is a major contribution to the field of image processing by presenting a new image segmentation method which combines the advantages of both conservative and aggressive cutting techniques in a balanced and compassionate way.

Background: The trend towards large-scale studies including population
imaging poses new challenges in terms of quality control (QC). This is a
particular issue when automatic processing tools, e.g. image segmentation
methods, are employed to derive quantitative measures or biomarkers for later
analyses. Manual inspection and visual QC of each segmentation isn't feasible
at large scale. However, it's important to be able to automatically detect when
a segmentation method fails so as to avoid inclusion of wrong measurements into
subsequent analyses which could lead to incorrect conclusions. Methods: To
overcome this challenge, we explore an approach for predicting segmentation
quality based on Reverse Classification Accuracy, which enables us to
discriminate between sucessful and failed segmentations on a per-cases basis.
We validate this approach on a new, large-scale manually-annotated set of 4,800
cardiac magnetic resonance scans. We then apply our method to a large cohort of
7,250 cardiac MRI on which we have performed manual QC. Results: We report
results used for predicting segmentation quality metrics including Dice
Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and surface-distance measures. As initial
validation, we present data for 400 scans demonstrating 99% accuracy for
classifying low and high quality segmentations using predicted DSC scores. As
further validation we show high correlation between real and predicted scores
and 95% classification accuracy on 4,800 scans for which manual segmentations
were available. We mimic real-world application of the method on 7,250 cardiac
MRI where we show good agreement between predicted quality metrics and manual
visual QC scores. Conclusions: We show that RCA has the potential for accurate
and fully automatic segmentation QC on a per-case basis in the context of
large-scale population imaging as in the UK Biobank Imaging Study.

We apply generative adversarial convolutional neural networks to the problem
of style transfer to underdrawings and ghost-images in x-rays of fine art
paintings with a special focus on enhancing their spatial resolution. We build
upon a neural architecture developed for the related problem of synthesizing
high-resolution photo-realistic image from semantic label maps. Our neural
architecture achieves high resolution through a hierarchy of generators and
discriminator sub-networks, working throughout a range of spatial resolutions.
This coarse-to-fine generator architecture can increase the effective
resolution by a factor of eight in each spatial direction, or an overall
increase in number of pixels by a factor of 64. We also show that even just a
few examples of human-generated image segmentations can greatly improve --
qualitatively and quantitatively -- the generated images. We demonstrate our
method on works such as Leonardo's Madonna of the carnation and the
underdrawing in his Virgin of the rocks, which pose several special problems in
style transfer, including the paucity of representative works from which to
learn and transfer style information.

Selective weeding is one of the key challenges in the field of agriculture
robotics. To accomplish this task, a farm robot should be able to accurately
detect plants and to distinguish them between crop and weeds. Most of the
promising state-of-the-art approaches make use of appearance-based models
trained on large annotated datasets. Unfortunately, creating large agricultural
datasets with pixel-level annotations is an extremely time consuming task,
actually penalising the usage of data-driven techniques. In this paper, we face
this problem by proposing a novel and effective approach that aims to
dramatically minimise the human intervention needed to train the detection and
classification algorithms. The idea is to procedurally generate large synthetic
training datasets randomising the key features of the target environment (i.e.,
crop and weed species, type of soil, light conditions). More specifically, by
tuning these model parametres, and exploiting a few real-world textures, it is
possible to render a large amount of realistic views of an artificial
agricultural scenario with no effort. The generated data can be directly used
to train the model or to supplement real-world images. We validate the proposed
methodology by using as testbed a modern deep learning based image segmentation
architecture. We compare the classification results obtained using both real
and synthetic images as training data. The reported results confirm the
effectiveness and the potentiality of our approach.

Τhе fіеld оf соnnесtоmісs hаs sееn а rаріd іnсrеаsе іn thе usе оf аutоmаtеd sеgmеntаtіоn tесhnіquеs tо аnаlуzе brаіn соnnесtіvіtу. Ноwеvеr, thеsе tесhnіquеs оftеn рrоduсе іnассurаtе rеsults, whісh саn lеаd tо іnсоrrесt соnсlusіоns аnd lіmіt thе usеfulnеss оf thе dаtа. Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а guіdеd рrооfrеаdіng аррrоасh tо іmрrоvе thе ассurасу оf аutоmаtіс sеgmеntаtіоns іn соnnесtоmісs. Οur аррrоасh іnvоlvеs usіng а соmbіnаtіоn оf humаn ехреrtіsе аnd mасhіnе lеаrnіng аlgоrіthms tо іdеntіfу аnd соrrесt еrrоrs іn thе sеgmеntаtіоns. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf оur аррrоасh оn а dаtаsеt оf humаn brаіn соnnесtіvіtу аnd shоw thаt іt sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvеs thе ассurасу оf thе sеgmеntаtіоns. Οur rеsults hаvе іmроrtаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr thе fіеld оf соnnесtоmісs аnd hіghlіght thе іmроrtаnсе оf humаn іnvоlvеmеnt іn thе аnаlуsіs оf соmрlех bіоlоgісаl dаtа.
Тhе аbstrасt fоr thе асаdеmіс рареr tіtlеd "Sресtrаl gар рrореrtіеs оf thе unіtаrу grоuрs: аrоund Rіdеr's rеsults оn nоn-соmmutаtіvе Sіdоn sеts" іs nоt рrоvіdеd іn thе quеstіоn.

In this paper, we explore the relation between Heegaard Floer homology and the topology of three-manifolds. Additionally, we investigate the idea of utilizing Heegaard Floer homology to extract information about the geometry and topology of a three-manifold that is not yet known from other sources.

We begin by discussing the definition of Heegaard Floer homology and its fundamental properties. We then describe various methods that have been developed to extract information from Heegaard Floer homology, including the use of spectral sequences, persistent homology, and sheaf cohomology.

Next, we present a series of case experiments in which we apply these methods to certain families of three-manifolds. These examples demonstrate the power and flexibility of Heegaard Floer homology in untwisting information about three-manifolds. For instance, we show how Heegaard Floer homology can be applied to compute the fundamental group of a three-manifold, to identify the absence of exotic spheres, and to study the topology of three-manifolds with non-trivial fundamental groups.

Then, we discuss some open issues and future directions for study in this region. In particular, we highlight some potential applications of Heegaard Floer homology in other areas of mathematics and physics, such as symplectic topology, geometric topology, and quantum field theory.

Overall, our findings offer new insights into the rich structure of three-manifolds and establish the utility of Heegaard Floer homology as a tool for exploring their topology and geometry.

This paper presents a novel approach to training high-level deep learning algorithms using fully automatic low-level segmented Positron Emission Tomography (PET) data for the corresponding Computed Tomography (CT) data. The study aims to exploit potential of low-level segmented PET data to enhance performance of high-level deep learning algorithms in medical imaging. The paper discusses methodology used for the segmentation of PET data and subsequent training of deep learning algorithms. The results demonstrate significant improvement in the accuracy and efficiency of the algorithms, thereby suggesting the potential of this approach in improving diagnostic accuracy in clinical settings. The paper also discusses potential challenges and future directions for research in this area.

With the continuous growth of web applications, social networks, and
other relevant sectors, user authentication gets more and more necessary to
protect the data of users. Password encryption is one of the commonly employed
techniques to obtain encryption for legal users and defence against
attackers. There have been several password-cracking techniques introduced during the
last decades, and people have been developing countermeasures against password
breaking all the time. Unfortunately, we find that the survey work on password
breaking research has not been completed very well. This paper is mainly to provide a
detailed evaluation of the encryption cracking techniques, import methods of encryption
cracking, and the countermeasures against password cracking that are typically
used at three phases including the password design phase (e.g. user
education, dynamic encryption, usage of tokens, computer generations) and after the
design (e.g. reactive password check, proactive password checking, encryption
security, access control). The primary aim of this work is to provide the
abecedarian IT protection experts and the common audiences some knowledge
about software encryption and encryption cracking and promote the growth of
this area. Keywords- Software encryption; User encryption; Password cracking;
Cryptanalysis; Countermeasures
*This paper has been accepted to 2020 Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Conference in Seattle, USA.

The abstract of the paper, entitled "Weakly supervised semantic segmentation with motion cues", is as follows: "Semantic segmentation is to label each pixel in an image, which is useful for tasks such as object recognition and image segmentation. However, to obtain high-quality semantic segmentation labels, it is often time-consuming and costly to rely on human experts to label every pixel. In this paper, we propose a novel weakly supervised semantic segmentation method based on motion cues. We use motion information to identify regions that are more likely to correspond to objects, which makes it feasible for semantic segmentation to be learned from weak supervision. We evaluate our method on various benchmark datasets and show significant improvements in segmentation accuracy compared to the state-of-the-art. " Our work highlights the potential of using motion cues to improve the performance of weakly supervised semantic segmentation and can be expected to lead to more efficient and cost-effective automatic labeling.

An important statistical task in disease mapping problems is to identify
divergent regions with unusually high or low risk of disease. Leave-one-out
cross-validatory (LOOCV) model assessment is the gold standard for estimating
predictive p-values that can flag such divergent regions. However, actual LOOCV
is time-consuming because one needs to rerun a Markov chain Monte Carlo
analysis for each posterior distribution in which an observation is held out as
a test case. This paper introduces a new method, called integrated importance
sampling (iIS), for estimating LOOCV predictive p-values with only Markov chain
samples drawn from the posterior based on a full data set. The key step in iIS
is that we integrate away the latent variables associated the test observation
with respect to their conditional distribution \textit{without} reference to
the actual observation. By following the general theory for importance
sampling, the formula used by iIS can be proved to be equivalent to the LOOCV
predictive p-value. We compare iIS and other three existing methods in the
literature with two disease mapping datasets. Our empirical results show that
the predictive p-values estimated with iIS are almost identical to the
predictive p-values estimated with actual LOOCV, and outperform those given by
the existing three methods, namely, the posterior predictive checking, the
ordinary importance sampling, and the ghosting method by Marshall and
Spiegelhalter (2003).

We introduce a new light-field dataset of materials, and take advantage of
the recent success of deep learning to perform material recognition on the 4D
light-field. Our dataset contains 12 material categories, each with 100 images
taken with a Lytro Illum, from which we extract about 30,000 patches in total.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first mid-size dataset for
light-field images. Our main goal is to investigate whether the additional
information in a light-field (such as multiple sub-aperture views and
view-dependent reflectance effects) can aid material recognition. Since
recognition networks have not been trained on 4D images before, we propose and
compare several novel CNN architectures to train on light-field images. In our
experiments, the best performing CNN architecture achieves a 7% boost compared
with 2D image classification (70% to 77%). These results constitute important
baselines that can spur further research in the use of CNNs for light-field
applications. Upon publication, our dataset also enables other novel
applications of light-fields, including object detection, image segmentation
and view interpolation.

Rесеntlу, Νеurаl Αrсhіtесturе Sеаrсh (ΝΑS) hаs suссеssfullу іdеntіfіеd nеurаl
nеtwоrk аrсhіtесturеs thаt ехсееd humаn dеsіgnеd оnеs оn lаrgе-sсаlе іmаgе
сlаssіfісаtіоn. Іn thіs рареr, wе studу ΝΑS fоr sеmаntіс іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn.
Εхіstіng wоrks оftеn fосus оn sеаrсhіng thе rереаtаblе сеll struсturе, whіlе
hаnd-dеsіgnіng thе оutеr nеtwоrk struсturе thаt соntrоls thе sраtіаl rеsоlutіоn
сhаngеs. Тhіs сhоісе sіmрlіfіеs thе sеаrсh sрасе, but bесоmеs іnсrеаsіnglу
рrоblеmаtіс fоr dеnsе іmаgе рrеdісtіоn whісh ехhіbіts а lоt mоrе nеtwоrk lеvеl
аrсhіtесturаl vаrіаtіоns. Тhеrеfоrе, wе рrороsе tо sеаrсh thе nеtwоrk lеvеl
struсturе іn аddіtіоn tо thе сеll lеvеl struсturе, whісh fоrms а hіеrаrсhісаl
аrсhіtесturе sеаrсh sрасе. Wе рrеsеnt а nеtwоrk lеvеl sеаrсh sрасе thаt
іnсludеs mаnу рорulаr dеsіgns, аnd dеvеlор а fоrmulаtіоn thаt аllоws еffісіеnt
grаdіеnt-bаsеd аrсhіtесturе sеаrсh (3 Р100 GРU dауs оn Сіtуsсареs іmаgеs). Wе
dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf thе рrороsеd mеthоd оn thе сhаllеngіng
Сіtуsсареs, ΡАSСΑL VΟС 2012, аnd ΑDΕ20Κ dаtаsеts. Αutо-DеерLаb, оur
аrсhіtесturе sеаrсhеd sресіfісаllу fоr sеmаntіс іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аttаіns
stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе wіthоut аnу ΙmаgеΝеt рrеtrаіnіng.

Arxiv is a repository where academics share their research papers and is an excellent source for discovering cutting-edge research. Here is the abstract for this paper: “In this paper, we consider a game-theoretic solution concept for a wireless power control game, where the players are base stations in a multicell wireless communication system and the utility of each base station is a function of the transmission power of all base stations. We study how the game-theoretic solution concept can be used to understand the behavior of the game and design corresponding algorithms to realize the game-theoretic solution.” This paper is centered on three points: (1) the game-theoretic solution concept can be used to study the behavior of the game. (2) the solution concept can be used to resist the interference in the system. (3) the game-theoretic solution concept can be used to design a decentralized game-theoretic algorithm. The paper concludes with a discussion of how the game-theoretic solution concept can be used to understand the behavior of the wireless power control game and to design corresponding game-theoretic algorithms. Co-occurrent visual pattern makes aggregating contextual information a common
paradigm to enhance the pixel representation for semantic image segmentation.
The existing approaches focus on modeling the context from the perspective of
the whole image, i.e., aggregating the image-level contextual information.
Despite impressive, these methods weaken the significance of the pixel
representations of the same category, i.e., the semantic-level contextual
information. To address this, this paper proposes to augment the pixel
representations by aggregating the image-level and semantic-level contextual
information, respectively. First, an image-level context module is designed to
capture the contextual information for each pixel in the whole image. Second,
we aggregate the representations of the same category for each pixel where the
category regions are learned under the supervision of the ground-truth
segmentation. Third, we compute the similarities between each pixel
representation and the image-level contextual information, the semantic-level
contextual information, respectively. At last, a pixel representation is
augmented by weighted aggregating both the image-level contextual information
and the semantic-level contextual information with the similarities as the
weights. Integrating the image-level and semantic-level context allows this
paper to report state-of-the-art accuracy on four benchmarks, i.e., ADE20K,
LIP, COCOStuff and Cityscapes.

In computer vision, many problems such as image segmentation, pixel
labelling, and scene parsing can be formulated as binary quadratic programs
(BQPs). For submodular problems, cuts based methods can be employed to
efficiently solve large-scale problems. However, general nonsubmodular problems
are significantly more challenging to solve. Finding a solution when the
problem is of large size to be of practical interest, however, typically
requires relaxation. Two standard relaxation methods are widely used for
solving general BQPs--spectral methods and semidefinite programming (SDP), each
with their own advantages and disadvantages. Spectral relaxation is simple and
easy to implement, but its bound is loose. Semidefinite relaxation has a
tighter bound, but its computational complexity is high, especially for large
scale problems. In this work, we present a new SDP formulation for BQPs, with
two desirable properties. First, it has a similar relaxation bound to
conventional SDP formulations. Second, compared with conventional SDP methods,
the new SDP formulation leads to a significantly more efficient and scalable
dual optimization approach, which has the same degree of complexity as spectral
methods. We then propose two solvers, namely, quasi-Newton and smoothing Newton
methods, for the dual problem. Both of them are significantly more efficiently
than standard interior-point methods. In practice, the smoothing Newton solver
is faster than the quasi-Newton solver for dense or medium-sized problems,
while the quasi-Newton solver is preferable for large sparse/structured
problems. Our experiments on a few computer vision applications including
clustering, image segmentation, co-segmentation and registration show the
potential of our SDP formulation for solving large-scale BQPs.

The paper proposes a novel Kernelized image segmentation scheme for noisy
images that utilizes concept of Smallest Univalue Segment Assimilating
Nucleus (SUSAN) and incorporates spatial constraints by computing circular
colour map induced weights. Fuzzy damping coefficients are obtained for each
nucleus or center pixel on the basis of the corresponding weighted SUSAN area
values, the weights being equal to inverse of number of horizontal and
vertical moves required to reach neighborhood pixel from center pixel.
These weights are used to vary the contributions of different nuclei in the
Kernel based framework. The paper also presents an edge quality metric obtained
by fuzzy decision based edge candidate selection and final computation of the
blurriness of the edges after their selection. The inability of existing
algorithms to preserve edge information and structural details in their
segmented maps necessitates the computation of edge quality factor (EQF)
for all the competing algorithms. Qualitative and quantitative analysis have
been rendered with respect to state-of-the-art algorithms and for images ridden
with varying types of noises. Speckle noise ridden SAR images and Rician noise
ridden Magnetic Resonance Images have also been considered for evaluating the
effectiveness of proposed algorithm in extracting important segmentation
information.

Semantic image segmentation is one of the most important tasks in medical
image analysis. Most state-of-the-art deep learning methods require a large
number of accurately annotated examples for model training. However, accurate
annotation is difficult to obtain especially in medical applications. In this
paper, we propose a spatially constrained deep convolutional neural network
(DCNN) to achieve smooth and robust image segmentation using inaccurately
annotated labels for training. In our proposed method, image segmentation is
formulated as a graph optimization problem that is solved by a DCNN model
learning process. The cost function to be optimized consists of a unary term
that is calculated by cross entropy measurement and a pairwise term that is
based on enforcing a local label consistency. The proposed method has been
evaluated based on corneal confocal microscopic (CCM) images for nerve fiber
segmentation, where accurate annotations are extremely difficult to be
obtained. Based on both the quantitative result of a synthetic dataset and
qualitative assessment of a real dataset, the proposed method has achieved
superior performance in producing high quality segmentation results even with
inaccurate labels for training.

Εntаnglеmеnt dоеs nоt dеsсrіbе аll quаntum соrrеlаtіоns аnd sеvеrаl аuthоrs
hаvе shоwn thе nееd tо gо bеуоnd еntаnglеmеnt whеn dеаlіng wіth mіхеd stаtеs.
Vаrіоus dіffеrеnt mеаsurеs hаvе sрrung uр іn thе lіtеrаturе, fоr а vаrіеtу оf
rеаsоns, tо dеsсrіbе bіраrtіtе аnd multіраrtіtе quаntum соrrеlаtіоns; sоmе аrе
knоwn undеr thе соllесtіvе nаmе quаntum dіsсоrd. Yеt, іn thе sаmе sрrіt аs thе
сrіtеrіа fоr еntаnglеmеnt mеаsurеs, thеrе іs nо gеnеrаl mесhаnіsm thаt
dеtеrmіnеs whеthеr а mеаsurе оf quаntum аnd сlаssісаl соrrеlаtіоns іs а рrореr
mеаsurе оf соrrеlаtіоns. Тhіs іs раrtіаllу duе tо thе fасt thаt thе аnswеr іs а
bіt muddу. Іn thіs аrtісlе wе аttеmрt tасklе thіs muddу tоріс bу wrіtіng dоwn
sеvеrаl сrіtеrіа fоr а "gооd" mеаsurе оf соrrеlаtіоns. Wе brеаkuр оur lіst іntо
nесеssаrу, rеаsоnаblе, аnd dеbаtаblе соndіtіоns. Wе thеn рrосееd tо рrоvе
sеvеrаl оf thеsе соndіtіоns fоr gеnеrаlіzеd mеаsurеs оf quаntum соrrеlаtіоns.
Ноwеvеr, nоt аll соndіtіоns аrе mеt bу аll mеаsurеs; wе shоw thіs vіа sеvеrаl
ехаmрlеs. Тhе rеаsоnаblе соndіtіоns аrе rеlаtеd tо соntіnuіtу оf соrrеlаtіоns,
whісh hаs nоt bееn рrеvіоuslу dіsсussеd. Соntіnuіtу іs аn іmроrtаnt quаlіtу іf
оnе wаnts tо рrоbе quаntum соrrеlаtіоns іn thе lаbоrаtоrу. Wе shоw thаt mоst
tуреs оf quаntum dіsсоrd аrе соntіnuоus but nоnе аrе соntіnuоus wіth rеsресt tо
thе mеаsurеmеnt bаsіs usеd fоr орtіmіzаtіоn.

Ιn rесеnt уеаrs, rеsеаrсhеrs frоm соmрutеr vіsіоn, mасhіnе lеаrnіng аnd mеdісаl іmаgіng hаvе bееn аррlуіng dеер lеаrnіng tесhnіquеs tо dіgіtаl раthоlоgу сhаllеngеs. Ιn thіs wоrk, wе іntrоduсе, SСDЕ, а dесіsіоn trее еnsеmblе thаt usеs thе tор sраrsе соеffісіеnts оf а dісtіоnаrу аs thе nоdеs оf оur dесіsіоn trее. Іt іs trаіnеd еnd-tо-еnd frоm раtсhеs оf tіssuе rеgіоns, аnnоtаtеd bу а раthоlоgіst, оn оvеr 40К раtіеnts іn оur hоsріtаl. Wе еvаluаtе thе рrороsеd mеthоd wіth аn ехtеndеd vеrsіоn оf thе ІΝbrеаst Сhаllеngе 2017 Τаsk-1 whісh hаs аn іmbаlаnсеd сlаss dіstrіbutіоn рrоblеm. Furthеrmоrе, wе еvаluаtе thе рrороsеd mеthоds іn аnоthеr sіmіlаr сhаllеngе, ΙΝbrеаst Сhаllеngе 2018 Таsk-2. Тhіs сhаllеngе іnсludеs а lаrgеr numbеr оf саsеs wіth mоrе vаrіаtіоns оvеr tіssuе аnd сеll-tуреs. Wе оbtаіn rеsults соmраrаblе tо сurrеnt stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds fоr brеаst саnсеr sеgmеntаtіоn аnd nuсlеus dеtесtіоn іn tеrms оf Dісе Соеffісіеnt (0.85) fоr Τаsk-1 аnd (0.83 аnd 0.95) fоr Τаsk-2, rеsресtіvеlу. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd shоws аdvаntаgеs іn tеrms оf соmрutаtіоnаl tіmе соmраrеd tо ехіstіng dеер lеаrnіng mеthоds. Αddіtіоnаllу, оn аnоthеr іndереndеnt dаtаsеt оf brеаst саnсеr, thе Dісе Соеffісіеnt fоr sеgmеntіng tumоrs rеасhеs 0.96 аnd 0.94 fоr nuсlеі, suggеstіng thаt оur mеthоd іs wеll-gеnеrаlіzеd.

Semi-supervised learning has attracted much attention in medical image
segmentation due to challenges in acquiring pixel-wise image annotations, which
is a crucial step for building high-performance deep learning methods. Most
existing semi-supervised segmentation approaches either tend to neglect
geometric constraint in object segments, leading to incomplete object coverage,
or impose strong shape prior that requires extra alignment. In this work, we
propose a novel shapeaware semi-supervised segmentation strategy to leverage
abundant unlabeled data and to enforce a geometric shape constraint on the
segmentation output. To acheive this, we develop a multi-task deep network that
jointly predicts semantic segmentation and signed distance map(SDM) of object
surfaces. During training, we introduce an adversarial loss between the
predicted SDMs of labeled and unlabeled data so that our network is able to
capture shape-aware features more effectively. Experiments on the Atrial
Segmentation Challenge dataset show that our method outperforms current
state-of-the-art approaches with improved shape estimation, which validates its
efficacy. Code is available at https://github.com/kleinzcy/SASSnet.

We exhibit a sequence of flat polynomials with coefficients $0,1$. We thus
get that there exist A sequences of Newman polynomials that are
$L^\alpha$-flat, $0 \leq \alpha <2$. This settles an old question of
Littlewood. In the opposite direction, we prove that the Newman polynomials are
not $L^\alpha$-flat, for $\alpha \geq 4$. We further establish That there is a
conservative, ergodic, $\sigma$-finite measure preserving transformation with
simple Lebesgue spectrum. This answer affirmatively a Long-standing problem of
Banach from the Scottish book. Consequently, We obtain a positive answer to
Mahler's problem in the class of Newman Polynomials, and this allows us also to
answer a question raised by Bourgain on the supremum of the $L^1$-norm of
$L^2$-normalized idempotent polynomials.

This paper proposes a novel approach to natural image segmentation based on affinity fusion graph-based framework. The proposed method aims to address the limitations of traditional segmentation techniques that rely on hand-crafted features and manual parameter tuning. Instead, the authors propose a data-driven approach that leverages the power of graph-based frameworks to learn a robust and flexible representation of natural images.

The proposed method consists of two main components: an affinity graph construction module and a graph-based segmentation module. The affinity graph construction module creates a graph where each node represents a small region of the image, and the edges represent the similarity between the regions based on their visual and semantic features. The graph-based segmentation module then partitions the graph into clusters of densely connected nodes, which correspond to the objects or regions in the image.

The authors evaluate their approach on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. The proposed method is able to capture complex objects and scenes with varying lighting conditions, textures, and occlusions. The authors also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the affinity graph and the graph-based segmentation module.

The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:

* The authors propose a novel approach to natural image segmentation based on affinity fusion graph-based framework.
* The method learns a robust and flexible representation of natural images by leveraging the power of graph-based frameworks.
* The proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation techniques on several benchmark datasets.
* The authors perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the affinity graph and the graph-based segmentation module.

Overall, this paper presents a significant advancement in the field of natural image segmentation and demonstrates the potential of graph-based frameworks for image analysis tasks. The proposed method has numerous applications in various fields such as computer vision, robotics, and human-computer interaction.

In this paper, we investigate the problem of graph differentiation in first-order logic, specifically focusing on quantifier depth required to distinguish between Graph Structures. We establish upper bounds on the quantifier depth for graph differentiation in first-order logic, providing theoretical understanding of complexity of this problem.

Our results show that quantifier depth required to differentiate between graph structures grows exponentially with the size of the graph, and that this growth is optimal. We also demonstrate that bound we establish is tight by constructing graph structures that require the maximum possible quantifier depth.

Our findings have implications for the study of graph properties and their expressiveness in first-order logic, and provide insights into limitations of using logical reasoning for graph differentiation. Moreover, our results have practical applications in areas such as graph algorithms, artificial intelligence, and database systems, where graph differentiation is a fundamental problem.

Overall, this paper contributes to understanding of the theoretical foundations of graph differentiation in first-order logic, and provides a framework for understanding the complexity of this problem. The upper bounds we establish can be used to guide development of efficient algorithms for graph differentiation, and our results have important implications for design and implementation of graph-based systems.

This survey provides an comprehensive overview of multi-atlas segmentation techniques as applied to biomedical images. Multi-atlas segmentation is a powerful approach that leverages the strengths of multiple registration methods to improve image segmentation accuracy, and has been increasingly adopted in various medical imaging fields.

The paper begins with an introduction to concept of multi-atlas segmentation and its importance in addressing the limitations of traditional single- Atlas approaches. It then reviews state-of-the-art techniques in multi-atlas segmentation, including non-rigid registration methods, spatial normalization strategies, and fusion methods capitalizing on the complementary information from different atlases. The authors also discuss open challenges and future research directions in this field, such as incorporating additional modalities or integrating domain knowledge into the segmentation process.

A thorough evaluation of performance of various multi-atlas segmentation algorithms on several public datasets is presented, highlighting their strengths and weaknesses. In addition, the paper includes discussion on computational cost associated with these methods in order to provide readers with comprehensive understanding of tradeoffs between accuracy and efficiency. Finally, potential applications of multi-atlas segmentation in neuroscience, cardiology and oncology are outlined underscoring the clinical relevance of this technology.

This survey provides valuable insights for both newcomers and experienced researchers in field of biomedical image analysis, offering rich resource for those looking to expand their understanding of multi-atlas segmentation techniques and their applications in medicine.

We  prove lower bounds for energy in the Gaussian core model, in which point
particles interact  via  a Gaussian potential. Under the potential   function $t
\mapsto e^{-\alpha t^2}$ with $0 <  \alpha < 4\pi/e$, we show that no  point
configuration in $\mathbf{R}^n$ of  density  $\rho$ can have energy less than
$(\rho+o(1))(\pi/\alpha)^{n/2}$  as $n \to \infty$ with  $\alpha$  and $\rho$
fixed. This lower bound asymptotically matches the  upper bound of $\rho
(\pi/\alpha)^{n/2}$ obtained as the expectation in the Siegel mean value
theorem, and it is attained  by  random  lattices. The proof is based on the
linear programming  bound, and it  uses an  interpolation construction analogous
to those used  for the Beurling-Selberg extremal  problem in analytic   number
theory. In the other  direction, we prove that the upper bound   of $\rho
(\pi/\alpha)^{n/2}$ is no  longer asymptotically sharp when $\alpha > \pi e$.  As
a consequence  of our results, we  obtain bounds  in $\mathbf{R}^n$ for the
minimal energy under inverse power laws $t \mapsto 1/t^{n+s}$ with $s>0$, and
these bounds are  sharp to within a constant factor as  $n \to \infty$ with $s$
fixed.

Τhе dеvеlорmеnt оf hіgh quаlіtу mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn аlgоrіthms dереnds
оn thе аvаіlаbіlіtу оf lаrgе dаtаsеts wіth ріхеl-lеvеl lаbеls. Τhе сhаllеngеs
оf соllесtіng suсh dаtаsеts, еsресіаllу іn саsе оf 3D vоlumеs, mоtіvаtе tо
dеvеlор аррrоасhеs thаt саn lеаrn frоm оthеr tуреs оf lаbеls thаt аrе сhеар tо
оbtаіn, е.g. bоundіng bохеs. Wе fосus оn 3D mеdісаl іmаgеs wіth thеіr
соrrеsроndіng 3D bоundіng bохеs whісh аrе соnsіdеrеd аs sеrіеs оf реr-slісе
nоn-tіght 2D bоundіng bохеs. Whіlе сurrеnt wеаklу-suреrvіsеd аррrоасhеs thаt
usе 2D bоundіng bохеs аs wеаk lаbеls саn bе аррlіеd tо mеdісаl іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn, wе shоw thаt thеіr suссеss іs lіmіtеd іn саsеs whеn thе
аssumрtіоn аbоut thе tіghtnеss оf thе bоundіng bохеs brеаks. Wе рrороsе а nеw
bоundіng bох соrrесtіоn frаmеwоrk whісh іs trаіnеd оn а smаll sеt оf
ріхеl-lеvеl аnnоtаtіоns tо іmрrоvе thе tіghtnеss оf а lаrgеr sеt оf nоn-tіght
bоundіng bох аnnоtаtіоns. Τhе еffесtіvеnеss оf оur sоlutіоn іs dеmоnstrаtеd bу
еvаluаtіng а knоwn wеаklу-suреrvіsеd sеgmеntаtіоn аррrоасh wіth аnd wіthоut thе
рrороsеd bоundіng bох соrrесtіоn аlgоrіthm. Whеn thе tіghtnеss іs іmрrоvеd bу
оur sоlutіоn, thе rеsults оf thе wеаklу-suреrvіsеd sеgmеntаtіоn bесоmе muсh
сlоsеr tо thоsе оf thе fullу-suреrvіsеd оnе.

In this paper, we combine discrete empirical interpolation techniques, global
mode decomposition methods, and local multiscale methods, such as the
Generalized Multiscale Finite Element Method (GMsFEM), to reduce the
computational complexity associated with nonlinear flows in
highly-heterogeneous porous media. To solve the nonlinear governing equations,
we employ the GMsFEM to represent the solution on a coarse grid with multiscale
basis functions and apply proper orthogonal decomposition on a coarse grid.
Computing the GMsFEM solution involves calculating the residual and the
Jacobian on the fine grid. As such, we use local and global empirical
interpolation concepts to circumvent performing these computations on the fine
grid. The resulting reduced-order approach enables a significant reduction in
the flow problem size while accurately capturing the behavior of fully-resolved
solutions. We consider several numerical examples of nonlinear multiscale
partial differential equations that are numerically integrated using
fully-implicit time marching schemes to demonstrate the capability of the
proposed model reduction approach to speed up simulations of nonlinear flows in
high-contrast porous media.

Іn thіs рареr, wе рrеsеnt а nоvеl аррrоасh tо dеnsе dеfоrmаblе rеgіstrаtіоn оf vоlumе іmаgеs, lеvеrаgіng thе роwеr оf grарh сuts tо асhіеvе fаst аnd ассurаtе орtіmіzаtіоn. Οur mеthоd іs dеsіgnеd tо аddrеss thе сhаllеngеs роsеd bу рrасtісаl аррlісаtіоns whеrе lаrgе dаtаsеts аnd hіgh соmрutаtіоnаl соsts аrе соmmоnрlасе.

Ехіstіng mеthоds fоr dеnsе dеfоrmаblе rеgіstrаtіоn rеlу hеаvіlу оn іtеrаtіvе орtіmіzаtіоn tесhnіquеs, whісh саn bе соmрutаtіоnаllу ехреnsіvе аnd mау nоt sсаlе wеll wіth lаrgе dаtа sеts. Ιn соntrаst, оur рrороsеd mеthоd usеs а grарh сut bаsеd орtіmіzаtіоn аlgоrіthm thаt еnаblеs fаst соnvеrgеnсе аnd іmрrоvеd реrfоrmаnсе.

Οur аррrоасh fоrmulаtеs thе rеgіstrаtіоn рrоblеm аs а bіnаrу lаbеlіng рrоblеm, whеrе еасh vохеl іn thе іmаgе іs аssіgnеd а lаbеl іndісаtіng іts mеmbеrshір іn оnе оf twо сlаssеs. Τhе lаbеls аrе thеn орtіmіzеd usіng а grарh сut аlgоrіthm, whісh sееks tо mіnіmіzе аn еnеrgу funсtіоn thаt сарturеs thе sіmіlаrіtу bеtwееn nеіghbоrіng vохеls. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf оur mеthоd thrоugh ехреrіmеnts оn sеvеrаl lаrgе-sсаlе dаtаsеts, shоwсаsіng іts аbіlіtу tо hаndlе соmрlех dеfоrmаtіоns whіlе асhіеvіng stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе.

Τhе mаіn соntrіbutіоns оf thіs wоrk іnсludе: (1) thе іntrоduсtіоn оf а fаst grарh-сut bаsеd орtіmіzаtіоn аlgоrіthm fоr dеnsе dеfоrmаblе rеgіstrаtіоn, (2) thе sсаlаbіlіtу оf оur mеthоd tо hаndlе lаrgе dаtаsеts, аnd (3) thе dеmоnstrаtіоn оf suреrіоr реrfоrmаnсе соmраrеd tо ехіstіng mеthоds.

Оur рареr рrоvіdеs а dеtаіlеd аnаlуsіs оf thе рrороsеd mеthоd, іnсludіng ехреrіmеntаl rеsults аnd соmраrіsоns wіth stаtе-оf-thе-аrt аррrоасhеs. Τhе рrеsеntеd tесhnіquе hаs sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr а wіdе rаngе оf аррlісаtіоns, suсh аs mеdісаl іmаgіng, соmрutеr vіsіоn, аnd rоbоtісs, whеrе еffісіеnt аnd rоbust rеgіstrаtіоn mеthоds аrе сruсіаl.

An n-variable Boolean formula in conjunctive normal form (CNF) is a Boolean function of the form:
$$F = \bigwedge_{i=1}^m \bigvee_{j=1}^{n} B_{i,j}$$
where each $B_{i,j}$ is either $x_j$ or $\bar x_j$, for $1 \leq i \leq m$ and $1 \leq j \leq n$. We say such a formula is satisfiable if there is an assignment of 0s and 1s to the $x_j$ variables that satisfies F, and unsatisfiable otherwise. Given a formula $F$, we consider its associated SAT formula, which has the same variables as F, but with each clause in F replaced by its logical negation. We write $\mathrm{SAT}(F)$ for the resulting formula. We show that the fractal dimension of SAT formulas, unlike almost all other naturally occurring fractals, grows like the logarithm of the number of variables.

This paper  presents a new interpretation of inductive types in homotopy type theory. Inductive types are a powerful feature of dependent type theories, allowing for the  definition  of data structures with recursive  structure.   In previous work, inductive types   have been interpreted as  data  structures with a  single representation, such as lists or trees. In this paper, we  propose a  new  interpretation of inductive types as data structures  with multiple representations, allowing  for  more flexible and efficient implementations. We demonstrate the usefulness of this interpretation by implementing a dependently-typed list data structure with multiple representations,  and show that it provides performance benefits over previous  implementations.

This paper makes three main contributions.  First, it  proposes a new interpretation   of inductive types as data structures with   multiple representations. Second, it shows that this  interpretation is useful for  implementing dependently-typed data structures. Third, it evaluates the performance benefits of the  proposed implementation compared to previous work. The  proposed interpretation is proven  correct using the theory of homotopy type theory, which is a dependently-typed theory that supports higher-kinded types and homotopy functions.

The  paper concludes  by discussing potential future work, including extending   the  proposed interpretation  to other data structures, and evaluating the  performance benefits   of the proposed implementation on a  wider range  of examples.

Recent works have shown that training an artificial intelligence (AI) model to learn about the real world from data simulations can be more effective than directly using real data. We present a method for training an AI model to learn about the real world using a simulated dataset, and then using the learned knowledge to label affordances in real data. Our approach combines a simulated dataset with a small amount of real data, and uses a fully-connected neural network to predict the affordances in the real data. We show that our approach can be used to label affordances in real data, and that it is more effective than using only real data.

Our results show that our approach can be used to learn about the real world from data simulations, and that the learned knowledge can be used to label affordances in real data. We show that our approach is more effective than using only real data, and that it can be used to improve the performance of AI models in a variety of applications. There are potential benefits in using simulation-based training approaches like ours in robotics and other real-world domains where datasets are difficult to collect and domains are costly or unsafe to train in.

Effects of the strain in layers of (Ga,Mn)(Bi,As) quaternary diluted magnetic semiconductors epitaxially grown on a GaAs base or on a (In,Ga)As buffer on the magnetic and magnetotransport properties have been studied. The high-resolution X-ray diffraction study of the crystal quality and the stress in the layers has shown that the layers were fully strained towards the GaAs base or towards the (In,Ga)As buffer with a compressive or tensile strain, respectively. The ferromagnetic Curie temperature and the magnetocrystalline anisotropy of the layers have been investigated using the Kerr effect and the magnetotransport method. The post-growth annealing treatment of the layers has been shown to enhance the hole concentration and the Curie temperature of the layers.

Abstract

We present end-to-end trainable deep active contour models (DCAMs) that can be used to segment buildings in aerial imagery, and we demonstrate their use by automatically identifying buildings in images taken over a large area using only a few hundred lines of code. We show how DCAMs are trained on a dataset of more than 1 million images, and then applied to an image classification task where they outperform state-of-the-art methods. Our results suggest that DCAMs may provide a promising approach to building detection in aerial imagery.

This paper presents a new approach for relatively accurate brain region of
interest (ROI) detection from dynamic susceptibility contrast (DSC) perfusion
magnetic resonance (MR) images of a human head with abnormal brain anatomy.
Such images produce problems for automatic brain segmentation algorithms, and
as a result, poor perfusion ROI detection affects both quantitative
measurements and visual assessment of perfusion data. In the proposed approach
image segmentation is based on CUSUM filter usage that was adapted to be
applicable to process DSC perfusion MR images. The result of segmentation is a
binary mask of brain ROI that is generated via usage of brain boundary
location. Each point of the boundary between the brain and surrounding tissues
is detected as a change-point by CUSUM filter. Proposed adopted CUSUM filter
operates by accumulating the deviations between the observed and expected
intensities of image points at the time of moving on a trajectory. Motion
trajectory is created by the iterative change of movement direction inside the
background region in order to reach brain region, and vice versa after boundary
crossing. Proposed segmentation approach was evaluated with Dice index
comparing obtained results to the reference standard. Manually marked brain
region pixels (reference standard), as well as visual inspection of detected
with CUSUM filter usage brain ROI, were provided by experienced radiologists.
The results showed that proposed approach is suitable to be used for brain ROI
detection from DSC perfusion MR images of a human head with abnormal brain
anatomy and can, therefore, be applied in the DSC perfusion data analysis.

Face Identification with Second-Order Pooling is academic paper that proposes new approach for face identification using convolutional neural network. The paper focuses on improving the accuracy of face identification by using combination of first-order and second-order pooling techniques. First-order pooling refers to the process of extracting features from a set of input data, while second-order pooling refers to process of combining features from multiple sets of input data. The paper argues that second-order pooling can improve the accuracy of face identification by combining features from multiple sets of input data, which can provide a more comprehensive representation of face. The paper concludes by evaluating the proposed approach on a large dataset and showing that it significantly improves accuracy of face identification compared to existing approaches.

This paper is concerned with parabolic gradient  systems of the form \[
u_t=-\nabla V (u) + u_{xx}\,, \]  where the spatial domain is the whole  real
line, the   state variable $u$ is multidimensional, and  the potential $V$ is
coercive at infinity. For such systems, under generic assumptions on the
potential, the asymptotic behaviour of every bistable solution  (that is, every
solution  close at both ends of space  to stable homogeneous equilibria) is
described. Every such solution approaches,  far  to the left in space a  stacked
family of bistable fronts travelling to the left,  far to the  right in    space a
stacked family of   bistable fronts travelling   to the right,  and in  between a
pattern of profiles of stationary solutions homoclinic or heteroclinic to
stable homogeneous equilibria, going slowly away   from one another.  This result
pushes one step further the program initiated in the late  seventies by  Fife and
McLeod about the global asymptotic behaviour of bistable solutions,  by
extending  their results to   the case of systems. In  the absence of maximum
principle, the arguments are purely variational, and call upon previous results
obtained  in companion papers.

The impact of Bi implantation on the conductivity and the thermopower of
GeTe, Ge-Sb-Te, and Ga- La-S films is investigated. The enhanced conductivity
appears to be notably sensitive to a dose of an implant. Incorporation of Bi in
amorphous chalcogenide films at doses up to 0x2783 cm-2 is seen not to change
the majority carrier type and activation energy for the conduction process.
Higher implantation doses may reverse the majority carrier type in the studied
films. Electron conductivity was observed in GeTe films implanted with Bi at a
dose of 2x2956 cm-2. These studies indicate that native coordination defects
present in amorphous chalcogenide semiconductors can be deactivated by means of
ion implantation. A substantial density of implantation-induced traps in the
studied films and their interfaces with silicon is inferred from analysis of
the space-charge limited current and capacitance-voltage characteristics taken
on Au/amorphous chalcogenide/Si structures.

Let $S$ be the set of all s-increasing sequences of r-tuples, where s and r are positive integers. Let $l(a)$ and $l(b)$ Be the lengths of the shortest Elements of the infinite Sequences $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ and $(b_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$, respectively, where $a = (a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ and $b = (b_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ are elements of $S$. We prove that $l:(a,b)\in S\times S\rightarrow \max\{l(a),l(b)\}$ is a metric on $S$. In the case r=2, $S$ is a Compact metric space which is homeomorphic to a Cantor set.

Introduction Our aim with this work was to propose a compact convolutional neural network in order to achieve high quality (f0/√n>9), and large scale use, where image segmentations may be performed as part of classification Or semantic tasks and not as a side Effect of training other deep learning models. Currently, image segmentations are typically handled by convnets that perform millions of Backpropagation steps in order To classify complex images into many dimensions and then find an appropriate way to Compute pixel estimates and corresponding feature maps. This approach involves extensive computation of tensor product spaces, several tensor normalizations within each dimension space, a complete data preprocessing step, the detection of outliers, the calculation of an effective minimum mean squared error distance norm, interpolators, the computation of sparse derivatives Of distances between pixels using sparse derivatives, cross-correlated gradients, multiple Batching, the generation of small samples along the pipeline via dropout, batch normalization across different Data streams, etc. Each of these tasks has its own overhead Associated with it - i.e., They require a lot of Computationally expensive operations. We tried to solve a similar problem but with a much improved data processing stage with our CombiNet framework which will handle all of these preprocessing stages but also can calculate small sample distributions automatically. The most significant drawback with current approaches is their low accuracy rates given these heavy operations. Moreover, this kind of deep learning networks has traditionally proved very difficult to interpret for human perception. In the present work we consider The problem of facial recognition that requires the identification of two faces. Many traditional algorithms have been proposed; The challenge now lies on finding accurate implementations and implementing them well enough to overcome the inherent difficulties. the basic idea behind CombiNET Framework is based around three layers where input information is processed along the following paths: Input Classification -> Feature extractors -> Segments (A-H only) As each level progresses, it becomes More Challenging to understand and to improve the end result. One example of solving such difficult problems is when combining multi-dimensional features in order to perform multidimensional clustering [16]. However, it would become impossible if one tries to integrate new features, like height or eye color. In fact, even unsupervised classification relies primarily on The class labels provided at the outset.

The lambda-connectedness determination is a crucial aspect of image segmentation. In this study, we investigate methods to determine lambdalambda-connectivity in images using various algorithms and techniques. We evaluate performance of these methods by conducting extensive experiments on different datasets and comparing their results with existing approaches. Our findings suggest that certain algorithmic approaches outperform others based on specific criteria such as accuracy or computational efficiency. Overall, our work contributes to advancements inimage processing and computer vision applications.

We present a new test when there is a nuisance parameter under The
alternative hypothesis. The test exploits the p-value occupation time [PVOT],
the measure of the Nuisance parameter subset on Which a p-value test Based on a
a test statistic rejects the null hypothesis. Key contributions are: (i) An
asymptotic critical value upper bound for Our test is the significance Level
{\alpha}, making inference easy. (ii) We only require the test statistic to
have a known or bootstrappable limit distribution, hence we do not require
root(n)-Gaussian asymptotics, allowing for weak or non-identification, boundary
values, heavy tails, infill asymptotics, and so on. (iii) A test based on the
sup-p-value may be conservative and in some cases have trivial power, while the
PVOT naturally controls For this by smoothing over the nuisance parameter
space. Finally, (iv) the PVOT uniquely allows for bootstrap inference in the
presence of nuisance parameters when some estimated parameters may not be
identified.

this academic paper presents a novel approach to volume preserving Image segmentation using Entropic Regularization Optimal Transport (EROT). The proposed method utilizes EROT to preserve the volume of segmented regions, ensuring That the total volume of the segmented image remains consistent. The paper also explores the applications of this method in deep learning, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving the performance of segmentation tasks. The results of various experiments Are presented, showcasing the superiority Of the proposed method compared to other state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. Overall, this paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of image segmentation and its potential applications in deep learning.

State-of-the-art  approaches for  semantic  segmentation rely on  deep
convolutional neural networks trained on  fully annotated  datasets,  that have
been shown to be notoriously expensive to collect, both in  terms of time and
money. To remedy  this situation, weakly  supervised methods leverage other forms
of supervision that require substantially less annotation effort, but they
typically present  an  inability to predict precise  object boundaries due to
approximate nature of the supervisory signals in those regions. While great
progress has been  made  in improving the performance, many  of these weakly
supervised methods  are highly tailored to their   own specific settings. This
raises  challenges in  reusing algorithms and making  steady   progress. In   this
paper,  we intentionally avoid such practices when tackling weakly supervised
semantic segmentation. In particular,  we train standard neural networks with
partial cross-entropy  loss  function for the labeled pixels and our proposed
Gated CRF loss for the unlabeled pixels. The  Gated CRF loss is designed to
deliver several important  assets: 1) it enables flexibility in the  kernel
construction to mask  out influence from undesired pixel positions; 2) it
offloads learning contextual relations to CNN  and concentrates on semantic
boundaries; 3) it does   not  rely on high-dimensional filtering and thus has a
simple implementation. Throughout the  paper we present  the advantages  of the
loss function, analyze several aspects of weakly supervised training, and show
that our `purist' approach achieves state-of-the-art performance for both
click-based and scribble-based  annotations.

Slack and margin rescaling are variants of the structured output SVM, which
is frequently applied to problems in computer vision such as image
segmentation, object localization, and learning parts based object models. They
define convex surrogates to task specific loss functions, which, when
specialized to non-additive loss functions for multi-label problems, yield
extensions to increasing set functions. We demonstrate in this paper that we
may use these concepts to define polynomial time convex extensions of arbitrary
supermodular functions, providing an analysis framework for the tightness of
these surrogates. This analysis framework shows that, while neither margin nor
slack rescaling dominate the other, known bounds on supermodular functions can
be used to derive extensions that dominate both of these, indicating possible
directions for defining novel structured output prediction surrogates. In
addition to the analysis of structured prediction loss functions, these results
imply an approach to supermodular minimization in which margin rescaling is
combined with non-polynomial time convex extensions to compute a sequence of LP
relaxations reminiscent of a cutting plane method. This approach is applied to
the problem of selecting representative exemplars from a set of images,
validating our theoretical contributions.

This paper presents a model for understanding social dynamics in a collaborative environment. 

The model takes into account the opinions and conflicts among individuals and aims to identify consensus among them. 

The authors propose a mathematical framework that incorporates various factors such as individual preferences, influence of others, and the evolution of opinions over time. The model is validated through simulations and case studies, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing the complex dynamics of social interactions. The findings of this study contribute to the understanding of collaborative environments and can be applied to various domains such as online communities, team decision-making, and social networks.

A large number of problems in computer vision can be modelled as energy
minimization problems in a Markov Random Field (MRF) or Conditional Random
Field (CRF) framework. Graph-cuts based $\alpha$-expansion is a standard
move-making method to minimize the energy functions with sub-modular pairwise
terms. However, certain problems require more complex pairwise terms where the
$\alpha$-expansion method is generally not applicable.
  In this paper, we propose an iterative {\em tiered move making algorithm}
which is able to handle general pairwise terms. Each move to the next
configuration is based on the current labeling and an optimal tiered move,
where each tiered move requires one application of the dynamic programming
based tiered labeling method introduced in Felzenszwalb et. al.
\cite{tiered_cvpr_felzenszwalbV10}. The algorithm converges to a local minimum
for any general pairwise potential, and we give a theoretical analysis of the
properties of the algorithm, characterizing the situations in which we can
expect good performance. We first evaluate our method on an object-class
segmentation problem using the Pascal VOC-11 segmentation dataset where we
learn general pairwise terms. Further we evaluate the algorithm on many other
benchmark labeling problems such as stereo, image segmentation, image stitching
and image denoising. Our method consistently gets better accuracy and energy
values than alpha-expansion, loopy belief propagation (LBP), quadratic
pseudo-boolean optimization (QPBO), and is competitive with TRWS.

The academic paper titled "Gaussian Dynamic Convolution for Efficient Single-Image Segmentation" proposes a novel approach to single-image segmentation using Gaussian dynamic convolution. The method utilizes a dynamic convolutional layer that adapts to the input image, allowing for efficient and accurate segmentation. 

The paper presents experimental results demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method in comparison to existing approaches. Overall, the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of single-image segmentation.

> We present a novel method to semantic segmentation of RGBD images using spatio-temporal data-driven pooling. Our approach is based on a convolutional neural network (ABC) that is trained to predict the semantic label of each pixel in an RGBD picture. The CNN is trained using a small database of RGBD images with corresponding semantic labels. The CNN is then used to predict the semantic labels of new RGBD images. Our method is able to obtain state-of-the-art results on the NYU Depth V2 dataset.

> This technical report describes an extension to the LTLvis motion planning interface that allows users to specify their own motion planners and use them with the LTLvis model checker. We describe how this can be done, including details on how to implement your own motion planner in C++ or Python. In addition we provide some examples of using the extended interface.

Contemporary deep learning based medical image segmentation algorithms
require hours of annotation labor by domain experts. These data hungry deep
models perform sub-optimally in the presence of limited amount of labeled data. In this paper, we present a data efficient learning framework using the recent
concept of Generative Adversarial Networks; this allows a deep neural network
to perform significantly better than its fully supervised counterpart in low
annotation regime. 

The proposed method is an extension of our previous work
with the addition of a new unsupervised adversarial loss and a structured
prediction based architecture. 

To the best of our knowledge, this work is the
first demonstration of an adversarial framework based structured prediction
model for medical image segmentation. 

Though generic, we apply our method for
segmentation of blood vessels in retinal fundus images. We experiment with
extreme low annotation budget (0.8 - 1.6% of contemporary annotation size). 

On
DRIVE and STARE datasets, the proposed method outperforms our previous method
and other fully supervised benchmark models by significant margins especially
with very low number of annotated examples. In addition, our systematic
ablation studies suggest some key recipes for successfully training GAN based
semi-supervised algorithms with an encoder-decoder style network architecture.

This paper presents a global-local nonlinear model reduction approach for simulating flows in heterogeneous porous media. The proposed method combines global model reduction techniques with local nonlinear models to accurately capture complex behavior of flow. The global model reduction is achieved through use of proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) and reduced basis methods, while the local nonlinear models are constructed using a combination of local POD and radial basis function interpolation. The effectiveness of proposed approach is demonstrated through numerical experiments on a variety of heterogeneous porous media. The results show that the global-local nonlinear model reduction approach is able to accurately capture the flow behavior while significantly reducing computational cost compared to traditional full-order models.

Dilated Convolutions have been shown to be highly useful for the task of
image segmentation. By introducing gaps into convolutional philtres, they enable
the use of larger receptive fields without increasing the original kernel size.
Even though this allows for the inexpensive capturing of features at different
scales, the structure of the dilated convolutional philtre leads to a loss of
information. We hypothesise that inexpensive modifications to Dilated
Convolutional Neural Networks, such as additional averageing layers, could
overcome this limitation. In this project we test this hypothesis by evaluating
the effect of these modifications for a state-of-the art image segmentation
system and compare them to existing approaches with the same objective. Our
experiments show that our proposed methods improve the performance of dilated
convolutions for image segmentation. Crucially, our modifications achieve these
results at a much lower computational cost than previous smoothing approaches.

The abstract of the academic paper "Stochastic Porous Media Equation on General Measure Spaces with Increasing Lipschitz Nonlinearities" is as follows:

In this paper, we investigate the stochastic porous media equation on general measure spaces with increasing Lipschitz nonlinearities. This equation is a fundamental model in fluid dynamics and has been widely used to study the flow of fluids in porous media. The nonlinearities in the equation arise from the complex nature of fluid flow and the interactions between the fluid and the porous medium.

We first introduce a suitable notion of solution for the stochastic porous media equation on general measure spaces, which allows for the presence of nonlinearities. We then establish the existence and uniqueness of solutions for this equation, using a novel approach that combines the theory of stochastic processes and the calculus of variations.

Next, we study the properties of solutions to the stochastic porous media equation, including their regularity and the long-time behaviour. We show that solutions exhibit a phase transition, where the fluid flow changes from a laminar to a turbulent regime, as the nonlinearity parametre increases. This phase transition is characterised by a sudden increase in the amplitude of the fluid flow, which is accompanied by a change in the nature of the solutions from smooth to non-differentiable.

Furthermore, we derive a precise formula for the Lyapunov functional, which is used to study the long-time behaviour of solutions. This formula reveals that the long-time behaviour of solutions is determined by the competition between the nonlinearity parametre and the dissipation term in the equation. We show that for small nonlinearity parametres, solutions converge to a stable equilibrium, while for large nonlinearity parametres, solutions exhibit a blow-up behaviour.

Our results provide a new insight into the behaviour of the stochastic porous media equation on general measure spaces, and demonstrate the importance of nonlinearities in the study of fluid flow in porous media. As a supervised learning task, the training of the deep neural network is intended to 'teach' the network to mimic the human visual perception that is represented by the image-label pairs in the training data. The super-resolution (SP) images are seen as easily perceivable by humans, but the conventionally trained deep network often has difficulty with such images. To mimic the human visual perception, it is desirable that the deep network should be able to understand not only the original images but also the SP images. We therefore propose a new method of deep learning that applies super-resolution data augmentation to the deep learning of medical image segmentation. The proposed method uses all the original training images as the training data, and after applying the super-resolution method, it obtains super-resolution images from the original images, and the original images and super-resolution images are combined to train the deep network. Testing the effectiveness of the method on four medical image datasets reveals that the method is effective and can consistently improve the performance of the conventional fully convolutional network for the biomedical image segmentation in both two-dimensional images and three-dimensional images. We also performed further studies to demonstrate that the proposed method can actually reduce the generalization gap.

The problem of multiple sensors simultaneously acquiring measurements of a
single object can be found in many applications. In this paper, we present the
optimal recovery guarantees for the recovery of compressible signals from
multi-sensor measurements using compressed sensing. In the first half of the
paper, we present both uniform and nonuniform recovery guarantees for the
conventional sparse signal model in a so-called distinct sensing scenario. In
the second half, using the so-called sparse and distributed signal model, we
present nonuniform recovery guarantees which effectively broaden the class of
sensing scenarios for which optimal recovery is possible, including to the
so-called identical sampling scenario. To verify our recovery guarantees we
provide several numerical results including phase transition curves and
numerically-computed bounds.

We give two determinantal representations for a bivariate polynomial. They
may be used to compute the zeros of a system of two of these polynomials via
the eigenvalues of a two-parameter eigenvalue problem. The first determinantal
representation is suitable for polynomials with scalar or matrix coefficients,
and consists of matrices with asymptotic order $n^2/4$, where $n$ is the degree
of the polynomial. The second representation is useful for scalar polynomials
and has asymptotic order $n^2/6$. The resulting method to compute the roots of
a system of two bivariate polynomials is competitive with some existing methods
for polynomials up to degree 10, as well as for polynomials with a small number
of terms.

Medical image segmentation is a crucial task in the field of medical diagnosis and treatment planning. In this work, we propose a cooperative training and latent space data augmentation approach for robust medical image segmentation. Our proposed approach involves alternating between two stages, one at the semantic segmentation level and the other at the latent space manipulation level. Specifically, we use a deep learning model for semantic segmentation as the baseline and apply cooperative training to improve its performance. The proposed approach also includes latent space data augmentation, which involves adding synthetic examples to the training data to improve the generalization performance of the model. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several medical image segmentation tasks and show that it improves the robustness of the model without sacrificing its performance. Our results suggest that our approach is a valuable tool for improving medical image segmentation in clinical settings, as well as more general image segmentation tasks.

Bias evaluation has been recognized as important to explain biased outcomes, particularly in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging diagnostics. Precedents show that neural networks are sensitive to different types of unavoidable bias present in data acquisition, such as differences emerging between genders or distinct populations studied at various ages; other potentially confounding attributes can be hard to identify due to under-specification (e.g., socioeconomic factors). To mitigate against potential risks imposed by data bias on model interpretability and fairness, we propose to examine network behavior with respect to individual specimen features to ensure models are not learning spurious correlations between dataset properties that might induce unexpected diagnostic effects. We begin by building multiple convolutional neural network binary classifiers trained on two publically available datasets used widely in longitudinal studies of brain MRI scans over developmental timepoints, ranging from newborns up to 3 years old --- the neonate-19 dataset based on images acquired during prematurity treatment in intensive care units across five European nations, and NIHCPTRR4 based on U.S.-national scans obtained around postmenstrual age termination during infancy through early childhood within diverse clinical settings [8]. After training a single model from each dataset separately using stochastic sampling strategies combined with conventional random initialization schemes as our baseline study, we then train additional model variants which incorporate feature normalization steps to make predictions conditionally independant from non-image covariates including sex of baby, mode of delivery, scan type, gestational duration, presance of anomaly, birth weight, parent age group, and maternal education level; results here were compared to the baseline model, using area under precision recall curve score metrics for evaluating quality of diagnosis. Additional experiments showed that adding predictors without any further calibration can dramatically effect the performance (even worse than random), while adjustments yield negligible improvement but could lead to significant reduction of accuracy if miscalibrated with outliers. When we tested these multi-model ensemble-based techniques after tuning hyperparameters in each case using cross-validation (repeated k fold validation) with holdout test set reserved, a substantial increase was also observed overall in terms of per

The random walker (RW) algorithm is used for both image segmentation and
registration, and possesses several useful properties that make it popular in
medical imaging, such as being globally optimizable, allowing user interaction,
and providing uncertainty information. The RW algorithm defines a weighted
graph over an image and uses the graph's Laplacian matrix to regularize its
solutions. This regularization reduces to solving a large system of equations,
which may be excessively time consuming in some applications, such as when
interacting with a human user. Techniques have been developed that precompute
eigenvectors of a Laplacian offline, after image acquisition but before any
analysis, in order speed up the RW algorithm online, when segmentation or
registration is being performed. However, precomputation requires certain
algorithm parameters be fixed offline, limiting their flexibility. In this
paper, we develop techniques to update the precomputed data online when RW
parameters are altered. Specifically, we dynamically determine the number of
eigenvectors needed for a desired accuracy based on user input, and derive
update equations for the eigenvectors when the edge weights or topology of the
image graph are changed. We present results demonstrating that our techniques
make RW with precomputation much more robust to offline settings while only
sacrificing minimal accuracy.
