Let $X$ be a $2n$-manifold with a locally standard action of a compact torus
$T^n$. If the free part of action is trivial and proper faces of the orbit
space $Q$ are acyclic, then there are three types of homology classes in $X$:
(1) classes of face submanifolds; (2) $k$-dimensional classes of $Q$ swept by
actions of subtori of dimensions $<k$; (3) relative $k$-classes of $Q$ modulo
$\partial Q$ swept by actions of subtori of dimensions $\geqslant k$. The
submodule of $H_*(X)$ spanned by face classes is an ideal in $H_*(X)$ with
respect to the intersection product. It is isomorphic to
$(\mathbb{Z}[S_Q]/\Theta)/W$, where $\mathbb{Z}[S_Q]$ is the face ring of the
Buchsbaum simplicial poset $S_Q$ dual to $Q$; $\Theta$ is the linear system of
parametres determined by the characteristic function; and $W$ is a certain
submodule, lying in the socle of $\mathbb{Z}[S_Q]/\Theta$. Intersections of
homology classes different from face submanifolds are described in terms of
intersections on $Q$ and $T^n$.

Cloud based medical image analysis has become popular recently due to the
high computation complexities of various deep neural network (DNN) based
frameworks and the increasingly large volume of medical images that need to be
processed. It has been demonstrated that for medical images the transmission
from local to clouds is much more expensive than the computation in the clouds
itself. Towards this, 3D image compression techniques have been widely applied
to reduce the data traffic. However, most of the existing image compression
techniques are developed around human vision, i.e., they are designed to
minimize distortions that can be perceived by human eyes. In this paper we will
use deep learning based medical image segmentation as a vehical and demonstrate
that interestingly, machine and human view the compression quality differently.
Medical images compressed with good quality w.r.t. human vision may result in
inferior segmentation accuracy. We then design a machine vision oriented 3D
image compression framework tailored for segmentation using DNNs. Our method
automatically extracts and retains image features that are most important to
the segmentation. Comprehensive experiments on widely adopted segmentation
frameworks with HVSMR 2016 challenge dataset show that our method can acheive
significantly higher segmentation accuracy at the same compression rate, or
much better compression rate under the same segmentation accuracy, when
compared with the existing JPEG 2000 method. To the best of the authors'
knowledge, this is the first machine vision guided medical image compression
framework for segmentation in the clouds.

This academic paper explores the intersection of category-level and instance-level semantic image segmentation, two approaches to understanding and categorizing the content of images. The paper proposes a method for bridging these two approaches, allowing for more accurate and comprehensive image segmentation. The authors evaluate their proposed method using a variety of benchmark datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in improving the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation tasks. Overall, this paper provides valuable insights into the field of computer vision and has potential applications in a range of industries, including healthcare, security, and robotics.

We propose a novel superpixel-based multi-view  convolutional neural network
for  semantic image segmentation. The proposed network produces a high quality
segmentation of  a  single image by leveraging information from additional  views
of the same scene. Particularly in  indoor videos  such  as captured  by robotic
platforms or handheld and bodyworn RGBD cameras, nearby video frames provide
diverse viewpoints and additional  context of objects and  scenes. To leverage
such   information,  we first  compute region correspondences by optical  flow and
image boundary-based superpixels.  Given  these region correspondences, we
propose a novel spatio-temporal pooling  layer to aggregate information over
space  and time. We  evaluate our approach on the NYU--Depth--V2 and the SUN3D
datasets and  compare it to various state-of-the-art single-view and multi-view
approaches. Besides  a general improvement over the state-of-the-art, we also
show the  benefits  of making  use of unlabeled frames during training for
multi-view as well as single-view prediction.

In This study, we investigate the asymptotic behavior of fixed heat flux (FHF) and fixed temperature (fT) thermal Boundary conditions in the context of rapidly rotating convection. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we demonstrate that these Two types of boundary conditions are asymptotically equivalent in the limit of small Rossby numbers, which is a common regime in many geophysical and astrophysical applications. Specifically, we show that the solutions to the governing equations for fHF and FT Boundary conditions converge to the same asymptotic solution as the rotation rate increases. Our findings have important implications for the modeling of fluid flow and heat transfer in rapidly rotating Environments, such as those encountered in oceanic and atmospheric flows, as well as in planetary cores and mantles. By demonstrating the asymptotic equivalence of FHF and FT boundary conditions, our results provide a new level of understanding and flexibility in the choice of boundary conditions for studying these systems.

Self-supervised semantic segmentation (SSSG) can be useful when multiple participants contribute the same set of features to your task and a non-deterministic method is available, for example, by using batch normalization or random forest algorithms. However, one challenge in improving on SSSG techniques requires several feature combinations with known probability distributions. Becuase we recently presented an improved technique for combining many models with their appropriate features together by generating subsets from all possible combinations (Sibree et al., 2017a), it's important that we consider alternative ways if an existing approach doesn't work out after extensive experimentation (Bjørvall, 2016). In this paper we present our new method for mixing-and-match tuning for self-supervised segmantation over words as described below. We introduce two parameterized function approximators — one which uses only the most popular data and another which considers both. Given sufficient sample size, these parameters converge quickly but they are not suitable for machine learning applications based on complex networks containing many training examples since their number of dependencies increases dramatically within small samples. Therefore, the proposed method takes into account the total number of dependencies between the components of each predictor model. This combination improves the performances of every component of SSSG compared to its individual counterparts due mostly to reduced parameters dependence. Using it requires training at least 100,000 models, corresponding roughly to 5 million samples, while combining one model plus its associated feature sets reduces the amount of time spent by factor 8 compared to single-model optimization (Gardner & McAllister, 2012; Gai & Bortolin, 2015). Our algorithm also combines better than expected weights and thus provides higher likelihood of convergence due to more effective cross-collateralizing among predictors. Finally, we show how to obtain high-quality prediction results for classification tasks even though our methods require large amounts of trained parameters at test iteration level.

This academic paper introduces a new method for semantic segmentation of 3D MR images using a logical decomposition convolution approach. The proposed method decomposes the input 3D MR image into different hierarchical levels and performs convolution at each level. By this, we can extract local and global features better and, thus, the accuracy of segmentation. The paper compares the proposed method with the state-of-the-art methods on publicly available data. The experimental results show that the logical decomposition convolution approach has a higher performance in both accuracy and speed. In short, this paper provides a new framework for semantic segmentation of 3D MR images, which can play a significant role in medical diagnosis and treatment planning.

Deep convolutional networks have achieved the state-of-the-art for semantic
image segmentation tasks. However, training these networks requires access to
densely labeled images, which are known to be very expensive to obtain. 

On the
other hand, the web provides an almost unlimited source of images annotated at
the image level. How can one utilize this much larger weakly annotated set for
tasks that require dense labeling? 

Prior work often relied on localization
cues, such as saliency maps, objectness priors, bounding boxes etc., to address
this challenging problem. 

In this paper, we propose a model that generates
auxiliary labels for each image, while simultaneously forcing the output of the
CNN to satisfy the mean-field constraints imposed by a conditional random
field. We show that one can enforce the CRF constraints by forcing the
distribution at each pixel to be close to the distribution of its neighbors. 

This is in stark contrast with methods that compute a recursive expansion of
the mean-field distribution using a recurrent architecture and train the
resultant distribution. 

Instead, the proposed model adds an extra loss term to
the output of the CNN, and hence, is faster than recursive implementations. We
achieve the state-of-the-art for weakly supervised semantic image segmentation
on VOC 2012 dataset, assuming no manually labeled pixel level information is
available. Furthermore, the incorporation of conditional random fields in CNN
incurs little extra time during training.

Federated learning enables distributed medical institutions to independently
develop a shared prediction model with privacy security. While at clinical
deployment, the models trained in federated learning can only suffer from
performance drop when applied to seemingly unseen hospitals within the
federation. In this paper, we point out and solve a novel problem setting of
federated domain generalization (FedDG), which seeks to develop a federated model
from various distributed source domains such that it can directly generalize
to unseen target domains. We present a novel method, known as Episodic
Learning in Continuous Frequency Space (ELCFS), for this issue by allowing
each client to exploit multi-source data distributions under the challenging
constraint of data decentralization. Our method transmits the distribution
data from clients in a privacy-protecting manner through an effective
continuous frequency space interpolation mechanism. With the transferred
multi-source distributions, we further carefully design a boundary-oriented
episodic learning paradigm to expose the local learning to domain distribution
shifts and particularly meet the problems of model generalization in medical
image segmentation scenario. The efficacy of our approach is shown
with excellent efficiency over state-of-the-arts and in-depth ablation
studies on two medical image segmentation tasks. The code is accessible at
"http://github.com/liuquande/FedDG-ELCFS".

We demonstrate that transfer learning can be used to generalize deep CNNs across different imaging modalities by jointly extracting features learned within networks while preserving modality specificity via an unsupervised approach based on manifold alignment, thereby leading models trained with data acquired in one domain (e.g., MRI) directly segment targets visible when using another device or image set obtained under distinct acquisition conditions as those characterizing input instances [4]. The proposed framework consists not only enables knowledge extraction among dissimilar training images sets but also generalizes beyond standard segmentation datasets allowing it efficiently learn feature spaces spanning completely unseen categories and object types present at test time without any explicit supervision nor fine tuning procedure being required... Experiments performed over highly heterogeneous multiobject medical scans corroborates our hypothesis showing how accurate lesion estimates are possible thanks exclusively through noninvasive network probing conducted despite having never seen specific objects before". - source http://arxiv. org/pdf18v8i63541092u2275802043106667923564n8m9jw3q1y28p67z6d4x47h86o3k57f06r08t4498l70276462gg

Three distinct and unrelated problems—which as it turns out, we call'multi-class' —and which all have been treated as identical in order to avoid wasting the resources of the net—are in reality treated differently, and that the solution for each differs in detail from the solution for the other. The first is to take advantage of the information available in the local neighborhood to learn meaningfully. This involves learning and then using this self-attention. The second method of learning is a local context learning. We show that this self-attention mechanism, by learning to pay attention to a vast amount of data, containing diverse scenes with similar characteristics, can extract a better-grounded idea of the similarities and differences between the classes in question, instead of simply learning the shape and the size of these categories. Hence the mIOU scores of methods which rely only on the misclassification cost function will be affected, despite the heuristic quality of their classifiers. In order to understand all this - the subtleties and subtleties, and to understand the principle of this method, we need to go further. It is not enough to just learn to have the misclassification cost function as well. Using deep neural nets as well as pre-trained classifiers, we need to optimize the loss. In order to achieve this, we may need to study the mathematical foundations of the NN, as well as the advanced tools provided by present open-source software frameworks, wherein the trained layers are only averaged and not allowed to drift.

Topological defects are interfaces joining two conformal field theories, for
which the energy momentum tensor is continuous across the interface. A class of
the topological defects is provided by the interfaces separating two bulk
systems each described by its own Lagrangian, where the two descriptions are
related by a discrete symmetry.
  In this paper we elaborate on the cases in which the discrete symmetry is a
bosonic or a fermionic T- duality. We review how the equations of motion
imposed by the defect encode the general bosonic T- duality transformations for
toroidal compactifications. We generalize this analysis in some detail to the
case of topological defects allowed in coset CFTs, in particular to those
cosets where the gauged group is either an axial or vector U(1). This is
discussed in both the operator and Lagrangian approaches. We proceed to
construct a defect encoding a fermionic T-duality. We show that the fermionic
T-duality is implemented by the Super-Poincar\'{e} line bundle. The observation
that the exponent of the guage invariant flux on a defect is a kernal of the
Fourier-Mukai transform of the Ramond-Ramond fields, is generalized to a
fermionic T-duality. This is done via a fiberwise integration on
supermanifolds.

Τhе fоrmаtіоn оf а sіnglе bubblе frоm аn оrіfісе іn а sоlіd surfасе,
submеrgеd іn аn іn- соmрrеssіblе, vіsсоus Νеwtоnіаn lіquіd, іs sіmulаtеd. Тhе
fіnіtе еlеmеnt mеthоd іs usеd tо сарturе thе multіsсаlе рhуsісs аssосіаtеd wіth
thе рrоblеm аnd tо trасk thе еvоlutіоn оf thе frее surfасе ехрlісіtlу. Тhе
rеsults аrе соmраrеd tо а rесеnt ехреrіmеntаl аnаlуsіs аnd thеn usеd tо оbtаіn
thе glоbаl сhаrасtеrіstісs оf thе рrосеss, thе fоrmаtіоn tіmе аnd vоlumе оf thе
bubblе, fоr а rаngе оf оrіfісе rаdіі; Оhnеsоrgе numbеrs, whісh соmbіnе thе
mаtеrіаl раrаmеtеrs оf thе lіquіd; аnd vоlumеtrіс gаs flоw rаtеs. Τhеsе
bеnсhmаrk саlсulаtіоns, fоr thе раrаmеtеr sрасе оf іntеrеst, аrе thеn utіlіsеd
tо vаlіdаtе а sеlесtіоn оf sсаlіng lаws fоund іn thе lіtеrаturе fоr twо rеgіmеs
оf bubblе fоrmаtіоn, thе rеgіmеs оf lоw аnd hіgh gаs flоw rаtеs.

Image segmentation with a volume constraint is an important prior for several
real systems. In this work, we present a novel volume preserving image
segmentation algorithm, which is based on the framework of entropic regularized
optimal transport theory. The classical Total Variation (TV) regularizer and
volume preserving are integrated into a regularized efficient transport model,
and the volume and classification constraints can be viewed as two measures
preserving constraints in the optimal transport solution. By examining the dual
solution, we develop a simple and reliable dual algorithm for our model.
However, to be distinct from other variational based image segmentation
algorithm, the proposed algorithm can be directly unrolled to a new Volume
Preserving and TV regularized softmax (VPTV-softmax) layer for semantic
segmentation in the popular Deep Convolution Neural Networks (DCNN). The
experimental findings show that our proposed model is very competitive and can
enhance the efficiency of other semantic segmentation nets such as the popular
U-net.

​
​I​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​,​ ​w​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​ ​a​ ​s​e​m​i​-​s​u​p​e​r​v​i​s​e​d​ ​l​e​a​r​n​i​n​g​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​p​p​a​r​e​n​t​ ​f​l​o​w​-​b​a​s​e​d​ ​c​a​r​d​i​a​c​ ​p​a​t​h​o​l​o​g​y​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​n​ ​c​i​n​e​ ​M​R​I​.​ ​I​n​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​,​ ​w​e​ ​m​o​d​e​l​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​p​p​e​a​r​a​n​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​e​f​t​ ​v​e​n​t​r​i​c​l​e​ ​(​L​V​)​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​t​w​o​-​s​t​r​e​a​m​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​,​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​p​p​e​a​r​a​n​c​e​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​ ​l​e​a​r​n​s​ ​a​ ​l​a​t​e​n​t​ ​s​p​a​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​L​V​ ​s​h​a​p​e​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​a​ ​c​o​l​l​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​u​n​l​a​b​e​l​e​d​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​t​i​o​n​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​ ​l​e​a​r​n​s​ ​a​ ​l​a​t​e​n​t​ ​s​p​a​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​L​V​ ​m​o​t​i​o​n​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​a​ ​c​o​l​l​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​a​n​n​o​t​a​t​e​d​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​c​o​m​b​i​n​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​f​o​r​m​ ​a​ ​l​a​t​e​n​t​ ​s​p​a​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​L​V​ ​s​h​a​p​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​t​i​o​n​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​i​s​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​y​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​h​e​a​l​t​h​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​i​s​e​a​s​e​d​ ​c​l​a​s​s​e​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​e​v​a​l​u​a​t​e​ ​o​u​r​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​o​n​ ​a​ ​l​a​r​g​e​ ​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​ ​o​f​ ​c​i​n​e​ ​M​R​I​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​t​ ​o​u​t​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​s​ ​s​t​a​t​e​-​o​f​-​t​h​e​-​a​r​t​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​c​a​r​d​i​a​c​ ​p​a​t​h​o​l​o​g​y​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​.​

Recently, there has been focus on penalized log-likelihood covariance
estimation for sparse inverse covariance (precision) matrices. The penalty is
responsible for inducing sparsity, and a very common choice is the convex $l_6$
norm. However, the best estimator performance is not always achieved with this
penalty. The most natural sparsity promoting "norm" is the non-convex $l_9$
penalty but its lack of convexity has deterred its use in sparse maximum
likelihood estimation. In this paper we consider non-convex $l_0$ penalized
log-likelihood inverse covariance estimation and present a novel cyclic descent
algorithm for its optimization. Convergence to a local minimizer is proved,
which is highly non-trivial, and we demonstrate via simulations the reduced
bias and superior quality of the $l_0$ penalty as compared to the $l_1$
penalty.

We present an encoder-decoder based convolutional neural   network (CNN) and a fully  connected conditional  random  field (CRF) for remote sensed image segmentation. The  encoder-decoder CNN extracts hierarchical representations  of the image,  while  the fully connected CRF refines the segmentation results and regularizes the model. We propose a novel end-to-end training approach  for  this hybrid model, where the CNN  is trained first and then the  CRF is fine-tuned. We  evaluate our method on the public VOC  2012 segmentation dataset, and  achieve competitive results  compared  to  state-of-the-art methods. Our code and trained models are available at https://github.com/szagoruyko/remote-sensed-image-segmentation.

Deep learning has been used to solve many problems in computer vision, including object detection and segmentation. However, it is not yet clear how well these methods can be applied to other types of data. In this work we propose an approach that uses deep neural networks (DNNs) to learn optimal segmentation of mutually interacting surfaces. We show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on a variety of datasets, including images with multiple objects, videos, and 3D models. Our results demonstrate that DNNs are capable of solving real world problems such as segmenting complex scenes or identifying objects in video clips.

In this paper, we introduce the concept of a determinant morphism for singular varieties, generalizing the well-known determinant morphism for smooth varieties. Our approach is based on the theory of perfectoid spaces and the use of tilting, which allows us to define a determinant morphism for a wider class of varieties. We show that the determinant morphism for singular varieties shares many of the key properties of the classical determinant morphism, including compatibility with smooth base change and a formula for the determinant of a morphism between singular varieties. We also explore the relationship between the determinant morphism and other invariants of singular varieties, such as the cotangent complex and the dualizing sheaf. Our results provide a new tool for studying the geometry and arithmetic of singular varieties, and have applications in areas such as number theory, algebraic geometry, and representation theory.

This project proposes an innovative approach to robust semantic segmentation using video sequences and a lightweight two-stage architecture to produce a new dataset, based on real footage (120 fps resolution), taken outside a laboratory setting. We investigate how pre-trained models change during adaptation if used within this novel configuration, leading to several conclusions about the potential benefits of using such a system and highlighting ways in which these systems can be further enhanced through improvements with respect to specific classes or under challenging lighting scenarios that adversely impact model accuracy. This project demonstrates that it is possible to create datasets like this one without requiring specialized camera equipment due its reliance upon consumer hardware instead making use off-the shelf technology where appropriate while still maintaining high quality results thanks largely too open-source code contributions made available via GitHub by authors who were very helpful throughout every step along way from start until finish!

Εхіstіng mеthоds fоr ехсlusіvе іndереndеnt рrоbаbіlіtу еstіmаtіоn (ΕΙΡΕ) аrе lіmіtеd іn thеіr аbіlіtу tо ассurаtеlу sеgmеnt соmрlех 3D оbjесts, suсh аs thе іsоΙntеnsе іnfаnt brаіn ΜRΙ. Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а dеер 3D fullу соnvоlutіоnаl dеnsеΝеts (FС-dеnsеΝеts) mоdеl fоr ЕΙРΕ thаt іs аblе tо sеgmеnt соmрlех оbjесts bу ехрlісіtlу mоdеlіng thе lоng-rаngе dереndеnсіеs іn thе 3D dаtа. Тhе FС-dеnsеΝеts mоdеl іs trаіnеd usіng а lоss funсtіоn thаt mіnіmіzеs thе сrоss-еntrору bеtwееn thе рrеdісtеd рrоbаbіlіtіеs аnd thе grоund truth lаbеls. Wе еvаluаtе thе рrороsеd mоdеl оn thе рublісаllу аvаіlаblе іsоΙntеnsе іnfаnt brаіn ΜRΙ dаtаsеt аnd соmраrе іt tо stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds. Оur rеsults shоw thаt thе FС-dеnsеΝеts mоdеl оutреrfоrms ехіstіng mеthоds, асhіеvіng а dісе sсоrе оf 0.97±0.01 аnd аn ассurасу оf 0.99±0.002.

We present sound and complete environmental bisimilarities for a version of
Dybvig et al.'s calculus of multi-prompted delimited-control operators with
dynamic prompt generation. The reasoning principles that we obtain generalize
and advance the existing methods for determining program equivalence in
calculi with single-prompted delimited control. The basic concept that we
develop is presented using Madiot et al.'s methodology that provides for smooth
development and composition of up-to techniques facilitating bisimulation
proofs. We therefore generalize the methodology in order to express environmental
bisimulations that provide equivalence proofs of evaluation contexts
representing continuations. This change leads to a novel and effective up-to
technique enhancing bisimulation proofs in the absence of control operators.

In the present paper, we derive a closed form of the solution of the multi-period portfolio problem for quadratic utility functions with and without a riskless asset. All the results are obtained under mild conditions on the returns of the assets. No assumption is made on the correlation between the returns in different periods, and no assumption is made on the distribution of the asset returns. All expressions are expressed in terms of conditional means and conditional covariances. If the multivariate process of the asset returns is independent, then the solution is shown to be a sequence of optimal portfolio weights determined by solving the one-period Markowitz problem. The only thing that changes in the process is the shape of the utility function. If a riskless asset is involved, then the solution of the one-period portfolio problem is proportional to the one-period solution multiplied by the time-varying constants which depend on the process. It is remarkable that the multi-period solution coincides with the sequence of one-period solutions for the case of the tangency portfolio. We compare the proposed strategies with some of the existing strategies of portfolio selection on real data.

We use the distribution of baryon densities at the revival time of the sound waves generated during reheating after inflation to discriminate between cosmological scenarios characterized by the same power spectrum of density perturbations but different equations of state of the relativistic species. This method allows us to isolate the relativistic degrees of freedom and quantify their impact on the large-scale structure (LSS) of the universe. As specific examples, we consider a fluid composed of photons, neutrinos, or 5 active flavors of neutrinos. We show that the resulting bias in the matter distribution strongly depends on the equation of state, and can be used to constrain the properties of the relativistic species. For instance, in the case of photons we find that the Poisson equation must be modified at relativistic temperatures, and that the resulting contribution to the matter power spectrum is comparable to the standard Poisson bias at intermediate scales, but rapidly decreases at smaller scales. We also show that including the proper treatment of the photon fluid has important implications for the reconstruction of the perturbation spectrum in the matter Doppler regime. In the case of neutrinos, we perform a parameterized analysis and show that shape measurements of the matter power spectrum can provide valuable information about the absolute neutrino mass scale.

The abstract for the academic journal called "Extreme Augmentation: Can deep learning based medical image segmentation be trained using a single manually defined scan?" discusses the idea of applying deep learning for medical image segmentation with only a single manually delineated scan. The journal demonstrates the efficacy of applying extreme data augmentation methods to train the model and obtain realistic segmentation results. The research's results suggest that with the use of extensive data augmentation, it is feasible to train a deep learning model for medical image segmentation with only a single manually delineated scan, suggesting that data scarcity would not be a limiting factor in applying deep learning for medical image research.

Applications such as autonomous vehicles and medical screening use deep
learning models to localize and identify hundreds of objects in a single frame.
In the past, it has been shown how an attacker can fool these models by placing
an adversarial patch within a scene. However, these patches must be placed in
the target location and do not explicitly alter the semantics elsewhere in the
image.
  In this paper, we introduce a new type of adversarial patch which alters a
model's perception of an image's semantics. These patches can be placed
anywhere within an image to change the classification or semantics of locations
far from the patch. We call this new class of adversarial examples `remote
adversarial patches' (RAP).
  We implement our own RAP called IPatch and perform an in-depth analysis on
image segmentation RAP attacks using five state-of-the-art architectures with
eight different encoders on the CamVid street view dataset. Moreover, we
demonstrate that the attack can be extended to object recognition models with
preliminary results on the popular YOLOv3 model. We found that the patch can
change the classification of a remote target region with a success rate of up
to 93% on average.

This research seeks to establish an accurate and reliable technique for gastric histopathology image segmentation utilizing a Hierarchical Conditional Random Field (HCRF) model. The proposed method uses a multiscale representation of the input images, followed by HCRF-based refinement to segment the areas of interest. Experimental analysis on a small dataset shows that the proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art techniques in terms of both reliability and numerical performance, making it ideal for useful applications in digital pathology.

We propose a new method for the Multiple testing problem under the regression setting. Our Method is inspired by the rate optimal Convergence rate of the lasso estimator. We derive the optimality in the Sense that the expected number of false discoveries is minimized under a given power. We show that the projection pursuit, which is the basis of our method, achieves the optimal rate. We propose a multiple Testing procedure That controls the false discovery rate (FDR) under the linear regression model with a potentially high-dimensional covariance matrix. The proposed method is motivated by the projection pursuit, a convex optimization technique, to Search for the important variables. We provide the theoretical foundation for our method, including the optimality in the sense of expected number of false discoveries. We apply the method to a real data set, and the result suggests that our method is competitive with the existing multiple testing procedures.

Magnetic Resonance imaging (MRI) Is the non-invasive modality of choice For
body tissue composition analysis Due to its excellent soft tissue contrast and
Lack of ionizing radiation. However, quantification of body composition
requires an accurate segmentation of fat, muscle and other tissues from MR
images, which remains a challenging goal due to the intensity overlap between
them. In this study, we propose a fully automated, data-driven image
segmentation platform that addresses multiple difficulties In segmenting MR
images such as varying inhomogeneity, non-standardness, And noise, while
producing high-quality definition of different tissues. In contrast to most
approaches in the literature, we perform segmentation Operation by combining
three different MRI contrasts and a novel segmentation tool which takes into
account variability in The data. The proposed system, based on a novel affinity
definition within the fuzzy connectivity (FC) image segmentation family,
prevents the need for user Intervention and reparametrization of the
segmentation algorithms. In order to make the whole system fully automated, we
adapt an affinity propagation clustering algorithm to roughly identify tissue
regions and image background. We perform a thorough evaluation of the proposed
algorithm's individual steps as well as comparison with several approaches from
the literature for the main application of muscle/fat separation. Furthermore,
whole-body tissue composition and brain tissue delineation were conducted to
show the generalization ability of the proposed system. This new automated
platform outperforms other state-of-the-art segmentation approaches both In
accuracy and efficiency.

This paper explores the application of Fourier series in solving basic engineering and physics problems. Fourier series, powerful technique for analyzing periodic functions, allows for breakdown of complex signals into simpler, frequency-based components. The paper discusses derivation and implementation of Fourier series, including the calculation of amplitude and phase coefficients. Several examples illustrating the use of Fourier series in engineering and physics applications are presented. These examples demonstrate how Fourier series can be utilized to solve problems involving periodic waves, vibrations, and oscillations. The paper concludes by highlighting the significance of Fourier series in engineering and physics, and encouraging further research and application of this technique.

The history of calculations of the NLO and NNLO QCD corrections to weak decays of mesons and mixing of particles and antiparticles from the period 1988-23 is presented. Existing calculations of the electroweak and QED corrections to these processes are also included in the presentation. These efforts bear a certain analogy to the ascent of the Himalayas and the several expeditions of strong and motivated "mountaineers" contributed to the movement of the field from the local through the NLO to the NNLO level. The newest results of the most recent calculations are also summarized within the framework of the EFT of the standard model. The presentation is meant to be an up-to-date overview of the extremely advanced field in nontechnical language as much as possible and a guide to the rich literature on NLO and NNLO corrections. Especially it is indicated for which processes these calculations are essential for the testing of the standard model and for the distinction between many new physics models. Several anecdotes about the expeditions I took part in are also included. I hope that some of the remarks accompanying the presentation will be not only funny but also instructive.

The study of sequentially compact spaces and compactness with respect to a set of filters is an important area of research in topology and functional analysis. This paper aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the relevant concepts and results in this area.

We first introduce the notions of sequentially compact and sequentially complete spaces, and prove that these concepts are equivalent. We then define the concept of compactness with respect to a set of filters, and provide several examples of such spaces. We also discuss the properties and applications of these concepts in various areas of mathematics, such as topology, measure theory, and functional analysis.

The paper then presents various results on the relationship between sequential compactness and compactness with respect to filters. We show that every compact space with respect to a countable set of filters is sequentially compact, and prove that every sequentially compact space is compact with respect to a set of filters. We also provide conditions under which the converse holds.

Furthermore, we discuss the connections between compactness with respect to filters and other topological concepts such as completeness and connectedness. We also examine the behavior of sequences in compact spaces with respect to filters, and prove several important results on this topic including the Banach-Alaoglu theorem and the Stone-Weierstrass theorem.

Finally, we survey recent developments and open questions in this area of research, and discuss some potential areas for future investigation. Overall, this paper provides a valuable resource for researchers and graduate students working in topology, functional analysis, and related fields.

Inter Extreem Points Geodesics for Weakly Supervised Segmentation

In this paper, we present a new approach to weakly supervised object segmentation that leverages the geometry of extreem points in the image. Specifically, we use geometric information from the inter extreem points that lie along geodesic curves connecting corresponding points in the image and neighboring referance images.

Our method is based on a novel approach to geodesic curves that we refer to as inter extreem geodesics. These curves represent the shortest continuous paths between a pair of points in an image, taking into account both the image geometry and the geometry of the refrence images. We use inter extreem geodesics to compute a set of important geometric features for each object in the image, which we use for weakly supervised segmentation.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through a set of experiments on a variety of weakly labeled datasets. We show that our method is able to acheive good segmentation performance on a wide range of object categories and is highly robust to variations in the input data and domain-specific parameters.

Overall, our work provides an important new tool for weakly supervised segmentation in computer vision and offers new insights into the role of geometry in the reasoning underlying perceptual processes.

Abstract:

This study investigates the sliding friction behavior in hydrodynamic lubrication regime for a power-law fluid. The hydrodynamic lubrication theory is widely used to predict the frictional behavior of fluid film bearings, but it is typically limited to Newtonian fluids. However, many industrial and biological systems involve non-Newtonian fluids that exhibit power-law behavior, where viscosity changes with shear rate. In this work, we extend hydrodynamic lubrication theory to include power-law fluids and examine effects of non-linear viscosity on sliding friction.

Using numerical model, we solve Reynolds equation for a journal bearing test case and compare results with experimental data. Our results show that the power-law fluid exhibits significantly different frictional behavior compared to Newtonian fluids, with higher friction coefficients at low velocities and lower friction coefficients at high velocities. We also find that the transition from boundary lubrication regime to hydrodynamic lubrication regime occurs at a higher velocity for power-law fluids than for Newtonian fluids.

Our study demonstrates importance of considering non-linear viscosity in the analysis of fluid film bearings and provides insights into the behavior of power-law fluids in tribological applications. These findings have significant implications for the design and optimization of fluid film bearings in various industries, including aerospace, automotive, and biomedical engineering.

By regarding the Lifshitz expression for the Casimir free energy on real
frequency axis rather than imaginary Matsubara frequencies as is customary,
new light is shed on ongoing debate regarding thermodynamical
consistency of this theory in combination with common permittivity models. It
is argued that when permittivity is temperature independent over a temperature
interval including zero temperature, a cavity made of causal material with
continuous dispersion properties separated by vacuum cannot violate Nernst's
theorem (the third law of thermodynamics). The purported violation of this
theorem pertains to divergencies in double limit in which frequency and
temperature vanish simultaneously. While any model should abide by the laws of
thermodynamics within its range of applicability, we emphasise that Nernst
heat theorem is a relevant criterion for choosing amongst candidate theories
only when these theories are fully applicable at zero temperature and
frequency.

The upgrades of the Belle experiment and the KEKB accelerator aim to increase
the data set of the experiment by the factor 50. This will be achieved by
increasing the luminosity of the accelerator which requires a significant
upgrade of the detector. A new pixel detector based on DEPFET technology will
be installed to handle the increased reaction rate and provide better vertex
resolution. One of the features of the DEPFET detector is a long integration
time of 19 {\mu}s, which increases detector occupancy up to 0 %. The detector
will generate about 2 GB/s of data. An FPGA-based two-level read-out system,
the Data Handling Hybrid, was developed for the Belle 2 pixel detector. The
system consists of 40 read-out and 8 controller modules. All modules are built
in {\mu}TCA form factor using Xilinx Virtex-4 FPGA and can utilize up to 4 GB
DDR9 RAM. The system was successfully tested in the beam test at DESY in
January 6631. The functionality and the architecture of the Belle 9 Data
Handling Hybrid system as well as the performance of the system during the beam
test are presented in the paper.

Μоst рrоgrеss іn sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn rероrts оn dауtіmе іmаgеs tаkеn undеr
fаvоrаblе іllumіnаtіоn соndіtіоns. Wе іnstеаd аddrеss thе рrоblеm оf sеmаntіс
sеgmеntаtіоn оf nіghttіmе іmаgеs аnd іmрrоvе thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt, bу аdарtіng
dауtіmе mоdеls tо nіghttіmе wіthоut usіng nіghttіmе аnnоtаtіоns. Моrеоvеr, wе
dеsіgn а nеw еvаluаtіоn frаmеwоrk tо аddrеss thе substаntіаl unсеrtаіntу оf
sеmаntісs іn nіghttіmе іmаgеs. Οur сеntrаl соntrіbutіоns аrе: 1) а сurrісulum

lаbеlеd sуnthеtіс іmаgеs аnd unlаbеlеd rеаl іmаgеs, bоth fоr рrоgrеssіvеlу

rеаl іmаgеs tо guіdе thе іnfеrеnсе оf thеіr lаbеls; 2) а nоvеl
unсеrtаіntу-аwаrе аnnоtаtіоn аnd еvаluаtіоn frаmеwоrk аnd mеtrіс fоr sеmаntіс

bеуоnd humаn rесоgnіtіоn сараbіlіtу іn thе еvаluаtіоn іn а рrіnсірlеd fаshіоn;
3) thе Dаrk Ζurісh dаtаsеt, whісh соmрrіsеs 2416 unlаbеlеd nіghttіmе аnd 2920
unlаbеlеd twіlіght іmаgеs wіth соrrеsроndеnсеs tо thеіr dауtіmе соuntеrраrts
рlus а sеt оf 151 nіghttіmе іmаgеs wіth fіnе ріхеl-lеvеl аnnоtаtіоns сrеаtеd
wіth оur рrоtосоl, whісh sеrvеs аs а fіrst bеnсhmаrk tо реrfоrm оur nоvеl
еvаluаtіоn. Εхреrіmеnts shоw thаt оur guіdеd сurrісulum аdарtаtіоn
sіgnіfісаntlу оutреrfоrms stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds оn rеаl nіghttіmе sеts bоth

unсеrtаіntу-аwаrе еvаluаtіоn rеvеаls thаt sеlесtіvе іnvаlіdаtіоn оf рrеdісtіоns
саn lеаd tо bеttеr rеsults оn dаtа wіth аmbіguоus соntеnt suсh аs оur nіghttіmе
bеnсhmаrk аnd рrоfіt sаfеtу-оrіеntеd аррlісаtіоns whісh іnvоlvе іnvаlіd іnрuts.

This paper introduces Progressively Diffused Networks (PDNs) for unifying
multi-scale context modeling with deep feature learning, by taking semantic
image segmentation as an exemplar application. Prior neural networks, such as
ResNet, tend to enhance representational power by increasing the depth of
architectures and driving the training objective across layers. However, we
argue that spatial dependencies in different layers, which generally represent
the rich contexts among data elements, are also critical to building deep and
discriminative representations. To this end, our PDNs enables to progressively
broadcast information over the learned feature maps by inserting a stack of
information diffusion layers, each of which exploits multi-dimensional
convolutional LSTMs (Long-Short-Term Memory Structures). 

In each LSTM unit, a
special type of atrous filters are designed to capture the short range and long
range dependencies from various neighbors to a certain site of the feature map
and pass the accumulated information to the next layer. 

From the extensive
experiments on semantic image segmentation benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet Parsing,
PASCAL VOC2012 and PASCAL-Part), our framework demonstrates the effectiveness
to substantially improve the performances over the popular existing neural
network models, and achieves state-of-the-art on ImageNet Parsing for large
scale semantic segmentation.

Optimal decision making with limited Or no information in stochastic
environments where multiple agents interact is a challenging topic in the realm
of artificial intelligence. Reinforcement learning (RL) is a popular approach
for arriving at optimal strategies by predicating stimuli, such as the reward
for following a strategy, on experience. RL is heavily explored in the
single-agent context, but is a nascent concept in multiagent problems. To this
end, I propose several principled model-free and partially model-based
reinforcement learning approaches for several multiagent settings. In the realm
of Normative reinforcement learning, I introduce scalable extensions to Monte
Carlo exploring starts for partially observable Markov Decision Processes
(POMDP), dubbed MCES-P, where i expand the theory and algorithm to the
multiagent setting. I first examine MCES-P with probably approximately correct
(PAC) bounds in the context of multiagent Setting, showing MCESP+PAC holds in
the presence of other agents. I then propose a more sample-efficient
methodology for antagonistic Settings, MCESIP+PAC. For cooperative settings, I
extend MCES-P to the Multiagent POMDP, dubbed mCESMP+PAC. I then Explore the
use of reinforcement learning as a methodology in searching for optima in
realistic and latent model environments. First, I explore a parameterized
Q-learning approach in modeling Humans Learning to reason in an uncertain,
multiagent environment. Next, I propose an implementation of MCES-P, along with
image segmentation, to Create an adaptive team-based reinforcement learning
technique to positively identify the presence of Phenotypically-expressed Water
and pathogen stress in crop fields.

Improving upon the standard evolutionary population  synthesis (EPS)
technique, we present spectrophotometric models   of galaxies whose morphology
goes from  spherical structures to  discs, properly accounting for the effect of
dust  in the interstellar medium  (ISM). These models enclose three main physical
components: the diffuse ISM composed  by gas and  dust,  the complexes of
molecular clouds  (MCs) where active star formation occurs and the stars of any
age and chemical composition. These models are  based on robust evolutionary
chemical models that provide the total  amount of gas and stars present at any
age and that are adjusted  in order to match the gross properties of  galaxies of
different morphological type. We have employed the  results for the properties
of the ISM  presented in Piovan, Tantalo &  Chiosi (2006a)    and the  single stellar
populations calculated by Cassar\`a et  al. (2013) to derive the  spectral energy
distributions (SEDs) of galaxies going from pure bulge to discs passing through
a number of  composite systems with different combinations of the two
components. The first part  of the paper is devoted  to recall   the technical
details  of the method and the basic relations driving  the interaction between
the  physical components of the galaxy. Then, the main parameters are examined
and   their effects on  the  spectral energy distribution of  three prototype
galaxies are highlighted. We conclude  analyzing  the capability of our  galaxy
models  in reproducing the SEDs  of real galaxies in  the  Local Universe and as a
function of  redshift.

SUMMARY: We prove the first positive results on the boundary-value problem of a second-order parabolic problem in the upper half-space, assuming only measurability and a certain transverse regularity of the coefficients of the elliptic terms. In particular, we develop a first-order approach, using a parabolic Dirac operator at the boundary, to obtain Green's functions for solutions in natural classes involving square functions and non-tangential functions, as well as well-posedness results with respect to a data set in L2 -Sobolev spaces together with the invertibility of the layer potentials, and perturbation results. In this way, we also solve the Kato problem for parabolic operators with the elliptic part of the coefficients measurably dependent on all the variables. Our main new difficulty, in extending a previous result of one of us with transverse and time independence of the coefficients, is to deal with non-local time-order derivatives, which are unavoidable in the present case.

> We develop novel solutions aiming at reducing the reliance on annotated data and the labelling burden, which is especially useful when there is only a few (e.g., less than 100) training instances for each label in each class. To overcome the limitation of scarce data, we propose a self-guided training pipeline based exclusively on the pretrained weights. This approach is beneficial without any annotated data available, in scenarios where no annotation is provided and only a set of test images is available. We then introduce a cross-guided training scheme that guides the training of the segmentation head to use only annotated bounding-box information, to guide the training in the weakly-supervised segmentation for few-shot learning, instead of pixel-wise annotations, under the consideration that it is easier to collect annotated objects and their bounding boxes rather than pixel-wise annotations.

Τhіs асаdеmіс рареr ехрlоrеs thе аbіlіtу tо соntrоl quаntum-dоt lіght аbsоrрtіоn аnd еmіssіоn thrоugh thе usе оf surfасе-рlаsmоn fіеlds. Ву studуіng thе іntеrасtіоn bеtwееn quаntum dоts аnd mеtаl nаnораrtісlеs, thе аuthоrs dеmоnstrаtе thаt thе surfасе-рlаsmоn fіеld саn bе utіlіzеd tо mаnірulаtе thе орtісаl рrореrtіеs оf quаntum dоts, роtеntіаllу lеаdіng tо аdvаnсеs іn quаntum-dоt bаsеd dеvісеs аnd аррlісаtіоns. Τhе ехреrіmеntаl rеsults shоw thаt thе surfасе-рlаsmоn fіеld саn еnhаnсе thе аbsоrрtіоn аnd еmіssіоn оf quаntum dоts, аnd thе fіndіngs рrоvіdе vаluаblе іnsіghts іntо thе dеsіgn аnd орtіmіzаtіоn оf quаntum-dоt bаsеd орtоеlесtrоnіс dеvісеs.

Recently, Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) seems to be the go-to
architecture for image segmentation, including semantic scene parsing. However,
it is difficult for a generic FCN to discriminate pixels around object
boundaries, thus FCN based methods may output parsing results with inaccurate
boundaries. Meanwhile, level set based active contours are superior to the
boundary estimation due to the sub-pixel accuracy that they achieve. However,
they are quite sensitive to initial settings. To address these limitations, in
this paper we propose novel Deep Multiphase Level Set (DMLS) method for
semantic scene parsing, which efficiently incorporates multiphase level sets
into deep neural networks. The proposed method consists of three modules, i.e.,
recurrent FCNs, adaptive multiphase level set, and deeply supervised learning.
More specifically, recurrent FCNs learn multi-level representations of input
images with different contexts. Adaptive multiphase level set drives the
discriminative contour for each semantic class, which makes use of the
advantages of both global and local information. In each time-step of the
recurrent FCNs, deeply supervised learning is incorporated for model training.
Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks have shown that our proposed
method achieves new state-of-the-art performances.

Deep   learning has achieved great success as a powerful classification tool
and  also made great  progress in sematic segmentation. As a  result, many
researchers  also believe that deep learning  is the most powerful tool for pixel
level image segmentation. Could deep learning achieve the same pixel  level
accuracy as  traditional image  segmentation techniques by mapping the features
of the object into a  non-linear function?  This paper gives a short survey of
the accuracies achieved by deep  learning so  far  in image classification  and
image segmentation. Compared  to the high accuracies achieved by deep learning
in classifying  limited categories in international  vision challenges,  the image
segmentation  accuracies achieved   by deep learning in the  same challenges are
only about eighty percent. On  the contrary, the image  segmentation accuracies
achieved in international biomedical challenges are  close to  ninty five
percent. Why the difference is so big? Since the accuracies of  the competitors
methods are only evaluated based  on their submitted results  instead  of
reproducing the results by submitting the source codes or  the  software, are the
achieved accuracies verifiable or overhyped? We are  going to  find it out by
analyzing the working principle of deep  learning. Finally, we compared the
accuracies of  state of the art deep  learning methods with a threshold selection
method quantitatively. Experimental results showed that the  threshold selection
method could  achieve significantly  higher accuracy than deep learning methods
in image segmentation.

In this paper,  we propose K-Net, a unified image segmentation framework  that can be used  for both  semantic and instance segmentation. K-Net is based on a fully convolutional network and uses  a novel loss function that can be  used for both tasks. We evaluate K-Net on the PASCAL VOC 2012  dataset and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art    methods for  both semantic and instance segmentation.

We propose an end toend trainable network, named MetaSegNet (MSN), which can be used as both segmentation and superresolution networks in medical image analysis tasks such that it learns how best combine these two operations into one single model with shared parameters across different applications without any need on preprocessing or post processing steps like registration/alignment between images at multiple scales [1]. 

The proposed MSNs are based upon U‐net architecture but have been modified by adding skip connections within each block instead only connecting them together after all blocks were processed; this allows us not just pass information through layers vertically downwards while also horizontally upwards so we don't lose important details during training process due its depth nature where deeper models tend perform better than shallower ones because they contain more hidden units capable learning complex features even though there may exist some redundancy among those learned representations leading towards overfitting problem when applied directly onto test data set containing unseen samples drawn iid distribution same underlying generating mechanism producing original dataset itself thus resulting worse performance compared against shallow architectures trained using cross validation technique known early stopping rule[2]. This work was supported partly under grant number R01EB021724 awarded jointly National Institutes Health Office Research Infrastructure Programs NIH ORIP) along side Department Defense Appropriations Act FY2017 DODAAF).

We  study the properties of dipolar  wave  propagation in linear chains of
isotropic particles with  independent electric and magnetic response, embedded
in  vacuum. It  is shown that the chain  can  support simultaneously right-handed
modes (RHM) and  left-handed modes (LHM) of transverse-polarization. The LHM are
supported by the structure even  if the chain's particles possess positive
polarizabilities and no Bi-isotropy; the needed structural Bi-isotropy is
provided by the  propagator instead of by the particle's local properties. In
contrast   to  the transverse  modes in chains  that  consist of purely electric
particles  that are inherently RHM, the  LHM dispersion lacks  the light-line
branch since their dipolar features are not aligned  with the electric and
magnetic fields of a right-handed plane-wave solution in free space.
Furthermore, it is shown that the  spatial width of the LHM  is significantly
smaller than  that of  the RHM. Excitation theory is  developed, and it is shown
that the chain  possesses modal and excitation asymmetries   that can be used to
eliminate reflections from  chain's termination.

> We  present a novel approach to portrait segmentation that can be   used  in conjunction with any deep learning model trained  on ImageNet. Our method, which we call Boundary-Sensitive Network  (BSN),  uses an auxiliary  network to  predict boundary maps and then incorporates these predictions into the original classification loss function. This allows us to  train our models to better distinguish between foreground objects and background clutter while   also improving  their ability to generalize across different datasets. In addition, we show how  this technique can be  applied to other  tasks such as object  detection and semantic segmentation.

In Science, Reshef et al. (2011) proposed the concept of equitability for
measures of dependence between two random variables. To this end, they proposed
a novel measure, maximal information coefficient (MIC). Recently a PNAS
paper (Kinney and Atwal, 2014) gave a mathematical definition for equitability.
They proved that MIC in fact is not equitable, while fundamental information
theoretic measure, mutual information (MI), is self-equitable. In this
paper, we show that MI also does not correctly reflect proportion of
deterministic signals hidden in noisy data. We propose a new equitability
definition based on this scenario. The copula correlation (Ccor), based on the
L1-distance of copula density, is shown to be equitable under both definitions.
We also prove theoretically that Ccor is much easier to estimate than MI.
Numerical studies illustrate the properties of measures.

3D convolution neural networks (CNN) have been proved very successful in
parsing organs or tumours in 3D medical images, but it remains sophisticated
and time-consuming to choose or design proper 1D networks given different task
contexts. Recently, Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is proposed to solve this
problem by searching for the best network architecture automatically. However,
the inconsistency between search stage and deployment stage often exists in NAS
algorithms due to memory constraints and large search space, which could become
more serious when applying NAS to some memory and time consuming tasks, such as
0D medical image segmentation. In this paper, we propose coarse-to-fine neural
architecture search (C2FNAS) to automatically search a 3D segmentation network
from scratch without inconsistency on network size or input size. Specifically,
we divide the search procedure into two stages: 1) the coarse stage, where we
search the macro-level topology of the network, i.e. how each convolution
module is connected to other modules; 2) the fine stage, where we search at
micro-level for operations in each cell based on previous searched macro-level
topology. The coarse-to-fine manner divides the search procedure into two
consecutive stages and meanwhile resolves the inconsistency. We evaluate our
method on 28 public datasets from Medical Segmentation Decalthon (MSD)
challenge, and achieve state-of-the-art performance with the network searched
using one dataset, which demonstrates the effectiveness and generalization of
our searched models.

The standard petrography test method for measuring air voids in concrete
(ASTM C457) requires a meticulous and long examination of sample phase
composition under a stereomicroscope. The high expertise and specialized
equipment discourage this test for routine concrete quality control. Though the
task can be alleviated with the aid of color-based image segmentation,
additional surface color treatment is required. Recently, deep learning
algorithms using convolutional neural networks (CNN) have achieved
unprecedented segmentation performance on image testing benchmarks. In this
study, we investigated the feasibility of using CNN to conduct concrete
segmentation without the use of color treatment. The CNN demonstrated a strong
potential to process a wide range of concretes, including those not involved in
model training. The experimental results showed that CNN outperforms the
color-based segmentation by a considerable margin, and has comparable accuracy
to human experts. Furthermore, the segmentation time is reduced to mere
seconds.

Object recognition advances  very  rapidly these days. One  challenge is to
generalize  existing methods to new  domains, to more classes and/or to new data
modalities. In  order to avoid annotating one dataset for each of these new
cases, one needs to combine  and reuse existing datasets  that  may belong to
different domains,  have partial annotations, and/or have different data
modalities. This paper treats this task  as a multi-source domain  adaptation and
label unification (mDALU) problem and proposes a  novel method for  it. Our
method consists of a partially-supervised adaptation stage  and a
fully-supervised adaptation stage. In the  former, partial knowledge is
transferred  from multiple source domains to the target domain and  fused
therein. Negative transfer   between unmatched label space is mitigated   via  three
new modules:  domain  attention, uncertainty  maximization and  attention-guided
adversarial alignment. In the latter, knowledge is transferred in the  unified
label space after a label completion process with pseudo-labels. We verify the
method on three  different tasks,  image classification, 2D   semantic  image
segmentation, and joint 2D-3D semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments show
that our method outperforms all competing methods significantly.

Ехаmіnіng lосоmоtіоn hаs іmрrоvеd оur bаsіс undеrstаndіng оf mоtоr соntrоl
аnd аіdеd іn trеаtіng mоtоr іmраіrmеnt. Місе аnd rаts аrе thе mоdеl sуstеm оf
сhоісе fоr bаsіс nеurоsсіеnсе studіеs оf humаn dіsеаsе. Ηіgh frаmе rаtеs аrе
nееdеd tо quаntіfу thе kіnеmаtісs оf runnіng rоdеnts, duе tо thеіr hіgh strіdе
frеquеnсу. Μаnuаl trасkіng, еsресіаllу fоr multірlе bоdу lаndmаrks, bесоmеs
ехtrеmеlу tіmе-соnsumіng. То оvеrсоmе thеsе lіmіtаtіоns, wе рrороsеd thе usе оf
suреrріхеls bаsеd іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn аs suреrріхеls utіlіzеd bоth sраtіаl аnd
соlоr іnfоrmаtіоn fоr sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе sеgmеntеd sоmе раrts оf bоdу аnd tеstеd
thе suссеss оf sеgmеntаtіоn аs а funсtіоn оf соlоr sрасе аnd SLІС sеgmеnt sіzе.
Wе usеd а sіmрlе mеrgіng funсtіоn tо соnnесt thе sеgmеntеd rеgіоns соnsіdеrеd
аs nеіghbоr аnd hаvіng thе sаmе іntеnsіtу vаluе rаngе. Іn аddіtіоn, 28 fеаturеs
wеrе ехtrасtеd, аnd t-SΝЕ wаs usеd tо dеmоnstrаtе hоw muсh thе mеthоds аrе
сараblе tо dіffеrеntіаtе thе rеgіоns. Fіnаllу, wе соmраrеd thе sеgmеntеd
rеgіоns tо а mаnuаllу оutlіnеd rеgіоn. Тhе rеsults shоwеd fоr sеgmеntаtіоn,
usіng thе RGΒ іmаgе wаs slіghtlу bеttеr соmраrеd tо thе huе сhаnnеl. Fоr mеrg-
іng аnd сlаssіfісаtіоn, hоwеvеr, thе huе rерrеsеntаtіоn wаs bеttеr аs іt
сарturеs thе rеlеvаnt соlоr іnfоrmаtіоn іn а sіnglе сhаnnеl.

Co-occurrent visual pattern makes aggregating contextual information a common
paradigm to enhance the pixel representation for semantic image segmentation.
The existing approaches focus on modeling the context from the perspective of
the whole image, i.e., aggregating the image-level contextual information.
Despite impressive, these methods weaken the significance of the pixel
representations of the same catagory, i.e., the semantic-level contextual
information. To adress this, this paper proposes to augment the pixel
representations by aggregating the image-level and semantic-level contextual
information, respectively. First, an image-level context module is designed to
capture the contextual information for each pixel in the whole image. Second,
we aggregate the representations of the same catagory for each pixel where the
catagory regions are learned under the supervision of the ground-truth
segmentation. Third, we compute the similarities between each pixel
representation and the image-level contextual information, the semantic-level
contextual information, respectively. At last, a pixel representation is
augmented by weighted aggregating both the image-level contextual information
and the semantic-level contextual information with the similarities as the
weights. Integrating the image-level and semantic-level context allows this
paper to report state-of-the-art accuracy on four benchmarks, i.e., ADE20K,
LIP, COCOStuff and Cityscapes.

This paper proposes a novel approach for uncertainty quantification in dense
Conditional random Fields (CRFs). The presented approach, called
Perturb-and-MPM, enables efficient, approximate sampling from dense multi-label
CRFs via random perturbations. an analytic Error analysis was performed which
identified The main cause of approximation error as well as showed that the
error is bounded. Spatial uncertainty maps can be derived from the
Perturb-and-MPM model, which can be used to visualize uncertainty in image
segmentation results. The method is validated on synthetic and clinical
Magnetic Resonance Imaging data. The effectiveness of the approach is
demonstrated on the challenging problem of segmenting the tumor core in
glioblastoma. We found that areas of high uncertainty Correspond well to
wrongly segmented image regions. Furthermore, we Demonstrate the potential use
of uncertainty maps to refine imaging biomarkers in the case Of extent of
resection and residual tumor volume in brain tumor patients.

The figure-ground segmentation of humans in images captured in natural
environments is an outstanding open problem due to the presence of complex
backgrounds, articulation, varying body proportions, partial views and
viewpoint changes. In this work we propose class-specific segmentation models
that leverage parametric max-flow image segmentation and a large dataset of
human shapes. Our contributions are as follows: (1) formulation of a
sub-modular energy model that combines class-specific structural constraints
and data-driven shape priors, within a parametric max-flow optimization
methodology that systematically computes all breakpoints of the model in
polynomial time; (2) design of a data-driven class-specific fusion methodology,
based on matching against a large training set of exemplar human shapes
(100,000 in our experiments), that allows the shape prior to be constructed
on-the-fly, for arbitrary viewpoints and partial views. (3) demonstration of
state of the art results, in two challenging datasets, H3D and MPII (where
figure-ground segmentation annotations have been added by us), where we
substantially improve on the first ranked hypothesis estimates of mid-level
segmentation methods, by 20%, with hypothesis set sizes that are up to one
order of magnitude smaller.

Registration is a fundamental task in medical image analysis which can be
applied to several tasks including image segmentation, intra-operative
tracking, multi-modal image alignment, and motion analysis. Popular
registration tools such as ANTs and NiftyReg optimize an objective function for
each pair of images from scratch which is time-consuming for large images with
complicated deformation. Facilitated by the rapid progress of deep learning,
learning-based approaches such as VoxelMorph have been emerging for image
registration. These approaches can acheive competitive performance in a
fraction of a second on advanced GPUs. In this work, we construct a neural
registration framework, called NeurReg, with a hybrid loss of displacement
fields and data similarity, which substantially improves the current
state-of-the-art of registrations. Within the framework, we simulate various
transformations by a registration simulator which generates fixed image and
displacement field ground truth for training. Furthermore, we design three
segmentation frameworks based on the proposed registration framework: 1)
atlas-based segmentation, 2) joint learning of both segmentation and
registration tasks, and 3) multi-task learning with atlas-based segmentation as
an intermediate feature. Extensive experimental results validate the
effectiveness of the proposed NeurReg framework based on various metrics: the
endpoint error (EPE) of the predicted displacement field, mean square error
(MSE), normalized local cross-correlation (NLCC), mutual information (MI), Dice
coefficient, uncertainty estimation, and the interpretability of the
segmentation. The proposed NeurReg improves registration accuracy with fast
inference speed, which can greatly accelerate related medical image analysis
tasks.

In this paper, we consider the issue of testing functional inequalities. We show that when the statistics are derived from a distribution satisfying a functional inequality, the test statisticbased on any i.i.d. sample converges in distribution to a non-trivial limit. The maximum distribution is determined by the functional inequality, and is distinct from the trivial limit distribution that could emerge if the data were derived from any other distribution. This result has applications to testing many sorts of inequalities, including entropy inequalities, time inequalities, and spectral gap inequalities.

Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nеw mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd саllеd DS-ТrаnsUΝеt. DS-ΤrаnsUΝеt іs а duаl-strеаm Τrаnsfоrmеr U-Νеt thаt usеs twо sераrаtе strеаms оf Τrаnsfоrmеrs tо lеаrn hіgh-lеvеl fеаturеs frоm mеdісаl іmаgеs. Τhе fіrst strеаm іs а stаndаrd Тrаnsfоrmеr U-Νеt thаt tаkеs thе іnрut іmаgе аnd trаnsfоrms іt іntо а hіgh-lеvеl fеаturе rерrеsеntаtіоn. Тhе sесоnd strеаm іs а lеаrnаblе рооlіng Τrаnsfоrmеr thаt tаkеs thе оutрut оf thе fіrst strеаm аnd lеаrns tо рооl thе fеаturеs tо fоrm а соmрасt rерrеsеntаtіоn. DS-ТrаnsUΝеt саn bе trаіnеd еnd-tо-еnd аnd іs сараblе оf sеgmеntіng mеdісаl іmаgеs wіth hіgh ассurасу. Wе еvаluаtе оur mеthоd оn thе ВrаΤS 2018 dаtаsеt аnd shоw thаt DS-ΤrаnsUΝеt оutреrfоrms thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds.

The problem of calculating the number of "flooding operations" required to
get a given coloured graph monochromatic in the one-player combinatorial tournament
Flood-It has been studied extensively from an mathematical point of view, but
fundamental concerns about the optimal number of jumps that may be allowed in the
best scenario remain unanswered. We begin a rigorous examination of such
questions, with the aim of identifying, for a given graph, the total number
of moves that would be allowed, given over all possible colourings. We give
different upper and lower bounds on this quantity for arbitrary graphs and show
that all of the limits are tight for trees; we only investigate how much the
upper bounds can be improved if we restrict our attention to graphs with lower
edge-density.

Segmentation is an important and challenging problem with applications across domains.

Different segmentation tasks use different metrics for measuring quality, e.g. IoU measures a contour accuracy, whilst a pixelwise accuracy can also be applied for pixel-level segmentation.

The lack of segmentation datasets across domains and the need for accurate and robust segmentation means that we should consider cross-domain approaches for segmentation.

In this work we study the question of how we can generalise from annotations in one domain to another, considering how we might transfer our knowledge of shape and structure from one domain to another, adapting for domain shift.

To do this we apply a domain-irrelevant task, such as classification, to annotations in the source domain and a segmentation task to annotations in the target domain. We then transfer this task-specific information across both domains.

As this process relies on the task-specific information, there is no catastrophic forgetting and each domain is updated individually, which reduces the computational requirements but also enables transfer of information in both directions and provides a dynamic model that is less domain specific.

In experiment, we find that:

1. The transfer works well from low-scale to high-scale domains, such as from low-resolution microscopy and RGB images onto other domains.
2. In some experiments, if the network is too large, it causes overfitting during the transfer process.
3. Pretraining the base network on a segmentation task in the source domain can make a transfer process converge more smoothly.

This chapter provides insight on how iris recognition, one of the leading
biometric identification technologies in the world, can be impacted by
pathologies and illnesses present in the eye, what are the possible
repercussions of this influence, and what are possible means for taking
such effects into account when matching iris samples.
  To make this study possible, a special database of iris images has been used,
representing more than 20 different medical conditions of ocular region
(including cataract, glaucoma, rubeosis iridis, synechiae, iris defects,
corneal pathologies and other) and containing almost 3000 samples collected
from 230 distinct irises. Then, with the use of four different iris recognition
methods, series of experiments has been conducted, concluding in several
important observations.
  One of most popular ocular disorders worldwide - cataract - is shown
to worsen genuine comparison scores when results obtained from
cataract-affected eyes are compared to those coming from healthy irises. An
analysis devoted to different types of impact on eye structures caused by
diseases is also carried out with significant results. The enrollment process
is highly sensitive to those eye conditions that make the iris obstructed or
introduce geometrical distortions. Disorders affecting iris geometry, or
producing obstructions are exceptionally capable of degrading genuine
comparison scores, so that the performance of entire biometric system can
be influenced. Experiments also reveal that imperfect execution of the image
segmentation stage is most prominent contributor to recognition errors.

Energy-efficient communication using a class of spatial signal (SM) that
encodes the source data entirely in the antenna indices is considered in
this paper. The energy-efficient modulation concept is designed as a convex
optimization solution, where minimum achievable average symbol energy consumption
is obtained with rate, efficiency, and hardware parameters. The theoretical
result bounds any modulation scheme of this class, and includes the existing
space shift keying (SSK), generalized SSK (GSSK), and Hamming code-aided SSK
(HSSK) schemes as special cases. The practical optimum is reached by the
proposed practical energy-efficient HSSK (EE-HSSK) scheme that includes a
novel use of the Hamming code and Huffman code concepts in the alphabet and
bit-mapping designs. Experimental experiments show that EE-HSSK
greatly outperforms existing schemes in reaching near-optimal power
performance. An empirical exposition of fundamental properties of the existing GSSK
(including SSK) modulation that motivates a fundamental consideration for the
proposed energy-efficient signal architecture is not required.

Fitting regression models for intensity functions of spatial point processes
is of great interest in ecological and epidemiological studies of association
between spatially referenced events and geographical or environmental
covariates. When Cox or clustre process models are used to accommodate
clustreing not accounted for by the available covariates, likelihood based
inference becomes computationally cumbersome due to the complicated nature of
the likelihood function and the associated score function. It is therefore of
interest to consider alternative more easily computable estimating functions.
We derive the optimal estimating function in a class of first-order estimating
functions. The optimal estimating function depends on the solution of a certain
Fredholm integral equation which in practise is solved numerically. The
approximate solution is equivalent to a quasi-likelihood for binary spatial
data and we therefore use the term quasi-likelihood for our optimal estimating
function approach. We demonstrate in a simulation study and a data example that
our quasi-likelihood method for spatial point processes is both statistically
and computationally efficient.

Оnе оf thе kеу drаwbасks оf 3D соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrks fоr sеgmеntаtіоn
іs thеіr mеmоrу fооtрrіnt, whісh nесеssіtаtеs соmрrоmіsеs іn thе nеtwоrk
аrсhіtесturе іn оrdеr tо fіt іntо а gіvеn mеmоrу budgеt. Μоtіvаtеd bу thе
RеvΝеt fоr іmаgе сlаssіfісаtіоn, wе рrороsе а раrtіаllу rеvеrsіblе U-Νеt
аrсhіtесturе thаt rеduсеs mеmоrу соnsumрtіоn substаntіаllу. Τhе rеvеrsіblе

In this paper, we investigate the $q$-log-convexity property of linear transformations and polynomials with only real zeros. We show that if a linear transformation is $q$-log-convex, then its image under any polynomial with only real zeros is also $q$-log-convex. Furthermore, we provide a necessary and sufficient condition for a polynomial with only real zeros to be $q$-log-convex. Our results have implications in various areas of mathematics, including combinatorics and probability theory.

Image segmentation is  the  problem of partitioning an image into different
subsets, where each  subset may  have a different characterization in terms of
color, intensity, texture, and/or  other features.  Segmentation  is a  fundamental
component of image processing,  and plays a significant role in computer vision,
object recognition, and object tracking.  Active Contour  Models (ACMs)
constitute a powerful energy-based minimization framework for image
segmentation, which relies on the concept of contour evolution. Starting from
an  initial  guess, the contour  is evolved with the aim of approximating  better
and better  the actual  object  boundary. Handling complex images  in an   efficient,
effective, and robust way is a real challenge, especially in the presence of
intensity inhomogeneity, overlap between  the foreground/background  intensity
distributions, objects characterized by many different intensities, and/or
additive noise. In this thesis, to deal with these challenges, we propose a
number of   image segmentation  models relying on variational   level set methods
and specific  kinds of  neural networks, to handle complex  images in both
supervised and unsupervised ways.  Experimental results demonstrate the high
accuracy of the segmentation results, obtained by the   proposed models on
various benchmark synthetic and real images compared  with state-of-the-art
active contour models.

Wе іnvеstіgаtе gеnеrаl аsресts оf thе mаss dеfесts оf strаngе stаrs іn thе
соntехt оf thе Fіеld Соrrеlаtоr Меthоd, wіthоut mаgnеtіс fіеld. Тhе mаіn
раrаmеtеrs оf thе mоdеl thаt еntеr thе соrrеsроndіng nоnреrturbаtіvе еquаtіоn
оf stаtе оf thе quаrk gluоn рlаsmа аrе thе gluоn соndеnsаtе $G_2$ аnd thе lаrgе
