lаrgе rаngе оf vаluеs оf $G_2$ аnd $V_1$, wе оbtаіn аnоmаlоus mаss dеfесts wіth
mаgnіtudеs аrоund $10^{53}\,$еrg\,, оf thе sаmе оrdеr оf thе оbsеrvеd еnеrgіеs
оf gаmmа-rау bursts аnd nеutrіnо еmіssіоns іn SΝ1987Α, аnd оf thе thеоrеtісаllу
рrеdісtеd еnеrgіеs оf thе quаrk-nоvае ехрlоsіоns.

The most common paradigm for vision-based multi-object Tracking Is
tracking-by-detection, due to the availability of reliable detectors for
several important object categories such as cars and pedestrians. However,
future mobile systems will need a capability to cope with rich human-made
environments, in which obtaining detectors for every possible object category
would be infeasible. In this paper, we propose a model-free multi-object
tracking approach that uses a category-agnostic image segmentation method to
track objects. We present an efficient segmentation mask-based tracker which
associates pixel-precise masks reported by the segmentation. Our approach can
utilize semantic information whenever it is Available for classifying objects
at the track level, while retaining The capability To Track generic unknown
objects in the Absence of such information. We demonstrate experimentally that
our approach achieves Performance comparable to state-of-the-art
tracking-by-detection methods for popular object categories such as cars and
pedestrians. Additionally, we show that the proposed method can discover and
robustly track a large variety of other objects.

This study presents an automatic localization method for deep stimulation electrodes using a trajectory-based segmentation approach. The proposed technique involves segmenting electrode trajectories into distinct regions based on their spatial characteristics, which are then used to determine the precise location of each electrode. Experimental results demonstrate that this novel approach achieves high accuracy and efficiency in localizing deep stimulation electrodes, offering significant potential for improving clinical outcomes in various neurological applications.

0thers. Dielectric properties of MoS2 are needed for optoelectronic applications. Terahertz (THz) time-domain spectroscopy and ellipsometry have been used to study the dielectric properties of MoS2 crystals in the THz and visible regions. The real and imaginary parts of the complex dielectric constant of MoS2 crystals in the THz region follow a Drude-like trend because of the intrinsic charge carrier absorption. In the visible region, the complex dielectric constant generally follows a Lorentz trend, but two remarkable peaks are observed at 1.85 and 2.03 eV, which are attributed to the interlayer and spin-orbit splittings. This work provides essential dielectric data for future optoelectronic applications of MoS2.

іmаgіng роsеs nеw сhаllеngеs іn tеrms оf quаlіtу соntrоl (QС). Τhіs іs а

аt lаrgе sсаlе. Ноwеvеr, іt's іmроrtаnt tо bе аblе tо аutоmаtісаllу dеtесt whеn
а sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd fаіls sо аs tо аvоіd іnсlusіоn оf wrоng mеаsurеmеnts іntо
subsеquеnt аnаlуsеs whісh соuld lеаd tо іnсоrrесt соnсlusіоns. Μеthоds: Τо

quаlіtу bаsеd оn Rеvеrsе Сlаssіfісаtіоn Αссurасу, whісh еnаblеs us tо
dіsсrіmіnаtе bеtwееn suссеssful аnd fаіlеd sеgmеntаtіоns оn а реr-саsеs bаsіs.
Wе vаlіdаtе thіs аррrоасh оn а nеw, lаrgе-sсаlе mаnuаllу-аnnоtаtеd sеt оf 4,800
саrdіас mаgnеtіс rеsоnаnсе sсаns. Wе thеn аррlу оur mеthоd tо а lаrgе соhоrt оf
7,250 саrdіас МRІ оn whісh wе hаvе реrfоrmеd mаnuаl QС. Rеsults: Wе rероrt
rеsults usеd fоr рrеdісtіng sеgmеntаtіоn quаlіtу mеtrісs іnсludіng Dісе
Sіmіlаrіtу Соеffісіеnt (DSС) аnd surfасе-dіstаnсе mеаsurеs. Аs іnіtіаl
vаlіdаtіоn, wе рrеsеnt dаtа fоr 400 sсаns dеmоnstrаtіng 99% ассurасу fоr
сlаssіfуіng lоw аnd hіgh quаlіtу sеgmеntаtіоns usіng рrеdісtеd DSС sсоrеs. Аs
furthеr vаlіdаtіоn wе shоw hіgh соrrеlаtіоn bеtwееn rеаl аnd рrеdісtеd sсоrеs
аnd 95% сlаssіfісаtіоn ассurасу оn 4,800 sсаns fоr whісh mаnuаl sеgmеntаtіоns
wеrе аvаіlаblе. Wе mіmіс rеаl-wоrld аррlісаtіоn оf thе mеthоd оn 7,250 саrdіас
МRІ whеrе wе shоw gооd аgrееmеnt bеtwееn рrеdісtеd quаlіtу mеtrісs аnd mаnuаl
vіsuаl QС sсоrеs. Соnсlusіоns: Wе shоw thаt RСΑ hаs thе роtеntіаl fоr ассurаtе
аnd fullу аutоmаtіс sеgmеntаtіоn QС оn а реr-саsе bаsіs іn thе соntехt оf
lаrgе-sсаlе рорulаtіоn іmаgіng аs іn thе UΚ Βіоbаnk Ιmаgіng Studу.

Biofilm is the formation of microbial material on tooth surfaces. Several methods to quantitate biofilms have recently been reported in the literature, but the available methods offer a semi-automated approach to quantification and at best are influenced by the subjective assessment of a human operator who has his own interpretation of what is foreground, background, biofilm, and tooth. In addition, these systems tend to have a limited range of quantification, and the majority of commercial systems use five quantification levels (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 80%). Even the most recent automatic methods are not applicable in practical applications because they are not able to consider human input to handle misclassifications. The aim of this paper is to propose a new, automatic, and interactive method for the quantification of biofilms in canine teeth images. The method divides the QLF images into super-pixels of uniform texture and intensity. Each super-pixel is modeled as a realization of a Gaussian Markov random field (GMRF) and its statistical properties are estimated using a training set. The results of the proposed method are accurate and consistent. In addition, it allows dentists to adjust the automatically calculated results by dragging misclassified super-pixels to different states (background, tooth, or biofilm). This makes the method more convenient than simply drawing the border between the biofilm and the tooth, as in the previous methods.

This article treats of the concept of multiplicity one in the full congruence level, referring to the phenomenon that the number of distinct solutions to a congruence equation is equal to one. The article is an extensive investigation of this concept, its mathematical basis, its applications, and its consequences. The authors show that multiplicity one in the full congruence level is an important consequence of a wide range of mathematical fields, including number theory, algebraic geometry, and combinatorics. This article is a valuable contribution to the understanding of an important mathematical concept.

We  report  on a novel scheme  to realize a Fourier  transform  spectroscopy in a spin-orbit coupled Bose gas. By driving spin-orbit  coupled bosons with a weak radio-frequency field, we obtain an oscillating population  between two different orbital  states as a function of the radio-frequency frequency. The frequency of this oscillation can  be tuned  by  the  interaction between the  spin and the orbit of the particles. By scanning  the radio-frequency frequency, we obtain a spectrum that can be related to   the  frequency dependence of  the  bosonic occupancy of the  different orbital states. Our scheme can work in  a homogeneous system, and does not require any trap.

In this paper, we explore the concept of first-order reducts of unary structures and present novel dichotomy for such structures.

First-order reducts are fundamental tool in mathematical logic and have been extensively studied in various contexts. However, the literature on first-order reducts of unary structures is limited, and there exists gap in our understanding of their properties and behavior.

In this work, we aim to fill this gap by providing comprehensive study of first-order reducts of unary structures. We introduce new dichotomy for such structures, which enables us to classify them into two distinct categories based on their properties. This dichotomy has far-reaching implications for the study of first-order reducts and provides a framework for further research in this area.

Our results have important consequences for various fields that rely on first-order reducts, including database systems, artificial intelligence, and formal verification. By better understanding the properties of first-order reducts of unary structures, we can improve efficiency and effectiveness of algorithms and techniques that rely on these concepts.

The paper is organized as follows: In Section 1, we provide an introduction to first-order reducts and unary structures, and we motivate need for a dichotomy. In Section 2, we present our main result, the dichotomy for first-order reducts of unary structures. In Section 3, we discuss implications of our result and its applications in various fields. Finally, in Section 4, we conclude with a summary of our findings and suggest directions for future research.

Overall, this paper makes significant contribution to the field of mathematical logic and related areas by providing deeper understanding of first-order reducts of unary structures. Our results demonstrate the power and versatility of this approach and open up new avenues for exploration and discovery.

> Semantic segmentation is a basic task in computer vision, which seeks to assign a semantic label to each pixel in an image. Previously, deep convolutional neural systems (CNN) have demonstrated remarkable success in semantic segmentation. Unfortunately, the efficiency of CNN is now limited by the absence of semantic data. In this paper, we propose a novel network, called ISNet, to integrate image-level and semantic-level contexts for semantic segmentation. Accordingly, we first develop a novel image-level context module to capture the global image-level contexts. Then, we propose a semantic-level context module to capture the semantic-level contexts. Then, we integrate the image-level and semantic-level contexts to refine the segmentation results. Extensive studies on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and Cityscapes data demonstrate the validity of our proposed approach.

## 一、Introduction

### 1.1 Semantic Segmentation

Semantic segmentation is a basic task in computer vision, which seeks to assign a semantic label to each pixel in an image. Previously, deep convolutional neural systems (CNN) have demonstrated remarkable success in semantic segmentation. Unfortunately, the efficiency of CNN is now limited by the absence of semantic data. In this paper, we propose a novel network, called ISNet, to integrate image-level and semantic-level contexts for semantic segmentation. Accordingly, we first develop a novel image-level context module to capture the global image-level contexts. Then, we propose a semantic-level context module to capture the semantic-level contexts. Then, we integrate the image-level and semantic-level contexts to refine the segmentation results. Extensive studies on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and Cityscapes data demonstrate the validity of our proposed approach.

### 1.2 Contextual Information

Contextual information is useful for semantic segmentation. It can help to reduce the efficiency of semantic segmentation. There are two kinds of semantic information: image-level meaning and semantic-level meaning. Image-level meaning refers to the global information of an image, such as the background and the layout of objects. Semantic-level context refers to the local information of an image, such as the size and the color of objects.

In this paper, we explore the Seiberg-Witten geometry of four-dimensional $\mathcal N=2$ quiver gauge theories. We present comprehensive study of the geometric structures that arise in these theories and their relationship to underlying quantum field theory.

We first provide an introduction to basic principles of Seiberg-Witten geometry and its application to four-dimensional $\mathcal N=1$ gauge theories. We then extend this framework to context of $\mathcal N=2$ quiver gauge theories, which involve multiple gauge groups and matter fields transforming under each other.

Our main result is construction of a set of Seiberg-Witten curves associated with each quirk node in quiver diagram. These curves are defined as ramified coverings of Riemann sphere and have a richer structure than simple elliptic fibers encountered in traditional Seiberg-Witten geometry. We demonstrate how these curves encode essential information about the supersymmetry properties of gauge theory, such as distribution of electric and magnetic charges among the matter fields and pattern of supersymmetry breaking.

Furthermore, we establish direct connection between Seiberg-Witten curves and the Higgs branch of the moduli space of vacua. This allows us to infer important details about the non-perturbative physics of the gauge theory from the analysis of algebraic curve. Notably, we find that Seiberg-Witten prepotential, originally introduced in context of classical Hodge theory, emerges naturally in our formalism as consequence of the M-theory realization of the gauge theory.

Finally, we offer several illustrative examples that realize various notable phenomena in four-dimensional superconformal field theory through purely geometric means. Our results indicate a deeper link between Algebraic Geometry and strongly coupled dynamics of supersymmetric systems, opening up possibilities for novel insights into the mathematical foundations of the AdS/CFT correspondence and related areas.

Recent advances in brain clearing and imaging have made it possible to image
entire mammalian brains at sub-micron resolution. These images offer the
potential to assemble brain-wide atlases of projection neuron morphology, but
manual neuron reconstruction remains a bottleneck. In this paper we present a
probabilistic method which combines a hidden Markov state process that encodes
neuron geometric properties with a random field appearance model of the
flourescence process. Our method utilizes dynamic programming to efficiently
compute the global maximizers of what we call the "most probable" neuron path.
We applied our algorithm to the output of image segmentation models where false
negatives severed neuronal processes, and showed that it can follow axons in
the presence of noise or nearby neurons. Our method has the potential to be
integrated into a semi or fully automated reconstruction pipeline.
Additionally, it creates a framework for conditioning the probability to fixed
start and endpoints through which users can intervene with hard constraints to,
for example, rule out certain reconstructions, or assign axons to particular
cell bodies.

This academic paper explores the use of low-rank matrix decomposition for super-resolution, a technique that enables the recovery of high-quality images from low-resolution inputs. The paper examines how this technique can be applied to both internal and external learnings. The internal learning aspect refers to the use of the technique in the field of image processing, while the external learning aspect refers to its potential applications in other fields. Through a series of experiments and simulations, the paper demonstrates the effectiveness of low-rank matrix decomposition in improving super-resolution performance. The results show that the use of this technique leads to improved image quality, faster processing times, and better overall performance. The paper concludes by discussing the potential impact of low-rank matrix decomposition on the field of super-resolution and its potential applications in other domains.

Automatic histopathology image segmentation is a crucial task in pathological diagnosis. There is a lack of labelled data which limits the generalizability of the model. In contrast, semi-supervised learning based on generative methods has been shown to effectively exploit the rich information in the images. However, it is unclear which images are useful for the model training and how they can be obtained. We propose a new data-guided generative method for histopathology image segmentation by utilizing the unlabeled data distribution. In particular, we separate content and style of images and embed them into a cluster-friendly space in order to use their distribution. Then, new images are generated by sampling and combining content and style. We also formulate an effective data selection policy to sample the generated images. In particular, in order to make the generated data more representative of the training set, we increase the coverage of clusters which are sparse in the training set. Our method is evaluated on the data set of glands and nuclei. In both inductive and transductive settings, our method outperforms common methods and achieves state-of-the-art results.

In this paper, we present a novel approach for medical image segmentation using superpixel-guided label softening. Our method incorporates the benefits of superpixels to improve the accuracy and robustness of the label softening technique. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive experiments on various medical imaging datasets, showing significant improvements in segmentation performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The threat posed by adversaries exploiting security vulnerabilities against end-user applications residing within cloud computing environments has been the focus of numerous public and academic literature in recent years.  These attacks are primarily aimed at providing an advantage to unauthorized users who gain elevated access to systems using such means as gaining system administrator rights, obtaining passwords or other sensitive data, taking over network services, breaking software updates (especially Adobe Flash), etc.  Even after this has happened, attackers often remain undetected by end-users and administrators because they typically lack access to system infrastructure that facilitates normal operations, such as logging into remote files and web services or making use of firewalls and other protection mechanisms in place in many systems. We show how these same capabilities of attackers may allow them directly to compromise cloud servers via direct HTTP requests to particular resources on those systems.  While many previous research attempts have focused on investigating malicious actors executing attacks via email, web portals, social media networks, mobile devices, embedded devices, desktop computers, or even wireless hotspots, we believe our approach represents a new, more fundamental class of attack vector. An attacker can exploit vulnerabilities found with standard browser configuration settings to inject code onto any resource accessible through a cloud computer resource hosting service, such as running processes, creating documents, collecting information about user activity, and even launching shell commands on behalf of its remote host.  Additionally, cloud server administrators do not need to actively control their machines to expose them to attack vectors like this where the source and target machines are fully separated from each other and can be located anywhere in seconds. The challenge is how to make sure your servers are ready to prevent unauthorized activities while minimizing their vulnerability to compromise and exploitation -- something that is best done on demand without compromising the availability and security of the cloud platform at large.  Our work proposes a set of measures that cloud platform providers could take to increase the resilience of end-user systems upon their discovery by these threats during initialization and service provisioning processes. 
This post was originally published here.

Inflationary models including vector fields have attracted a great deal of
attention over the past decade. Such an interest owes to the fact that they
might contribute to, or even be fully responsible for, the curvature
perturbation imprinted in the cosmic microwave background. However, the
necessary breaking of the vector field's conformal invariance during inflation
is not without problems. In recent Years, it has been realized that a number of
instabilities endangering the consistency of the theory arise When the
conformal invariance is broken by means of a non-minimal coupling to gravity.
In this paper, we consider a massive Vector field non-minimally coupled to
gravity Through the Gauss-Bonnet invariant, And Investigate whether the vector
can play the role of a curvaton while evading the emergence of instabilities
and preserves the large-scale isotropy.

The academic paper titled "Reduction of Ion Heating During Magnetic Reconnection by Large-Scale Effective Potentials" presents a study on the reduction of ion heating during magnetic reconnection through use of large-scale effective potentials. The study demonstrates that effective potentials can suppress ion heating and reduce the energy dissipation during magnetic reconnection. The results of study have significant implications for understanding the processes involved in magnetic reconnection and can be useful in various applications, such as space plasma physics and fusion energy research.

RatLesNetv2 is a fully convolutional network  designed for accurate segmentation of rodent brain lesions. The network utilizes  a combination of   convolutional  and pooling layers to extract features from the input images  and  produce  pixel-wise lesion segmentation  maps. The  network was trained and validated  on a dataset of T2-weighted magnetic resonance images of rat brains with various   types of lesions. The results showed that RatLesNetv2 achieved a Dice similarity coefficient  of 0.94  and a Hausdorff distance of 2.1 mm, demonstrating  its effectiveness  in identifying and localizing brain lesions in rodents.

The formation of a single bubble from an orifice in a solid surface,
submerged in an in- compressible, viscous Newtonian liquid, is simulated. 

The
finite element method is used to capture the multiscale physics associated with
the problem and to track the evolution of the free surface explicitly. The
results are compared to a recent experimental analysis and then used to obtain
the global characteristics of the process, the formation time and volume of the
bubble, for a range of orifice radii; Ohnesorge numbers, which combine the
material parameters of the liquid; and volumetric gas flow rates. These
benchmark calculations, for the parameter space of interest, are then utilised
to validate a selection of scaling laws found in the literature for two regimes
of bubble formation, the regimes of low and high gas flow rates.

Typically, Neural Systems models are trained with a small database of images in
homogeneous backgrounds. The problem is that the efficiency of the network
models trained can be severely degraded in a complex and heterogeneous
environment. To mitigate the problem, this paper creates a methodology that
allows to independently generate a training dataset in heterogeneous cluttered
backgrounds. It is clear that the learning efficiency of the proposed
methodology should be improved in complex and heterogeneous systems,
compared with the ones with the traditional data. In our framework, a
state-of-the-art picture segmentation method called DeepLab is used to extract
items of interest from a photograph and Chroma-key method is then used to
merge the extracted items of interest into specific heterogeneous
backgrounds. The efficiency of the proposed methodology is investigated through
statistical experiments and compared with that of the model trained with the COCO
dataset. The findings show that the proposed methodology outperforms the model
compared. This indicates that the learning efficiency of the methodology
developed is comparable to the models with the typical data.

In this paper, we propose a recurrent residual convolutional neural network based on U-Net (R2U-Net) for medical image segmentation. The proposed R2U-Net consists of a recurrent neural network (RNN) and a U-Net. The RNN is used to extract the contextual information from the input image, while the U-Net is used to segment the target object. The two networks are combined to form the R2U-Net. We evaluated the proposed R2U-Net on the task of liver segmentation from CT images. The results showed that the proposed R2U-Net outperformed the U-Net and other state-of-the-art methods.

Two quantum finite automata are equivalent if for all input string $\omega$
over the input alphabet the two automata accept $\omega$ with equal
probability. In [Theoret. Comput. Sci. 410 (2009) 3006-3017], it was shown that
a $k_1$-letter QFA $\mathcal{A}_1$ and a $k_2$-letter QFA $\mathcal{A}_2$ over
$\Sigma=\{\sigma\}$, are equivalent if and only if they are
$(n_1+n_2)^4+k-1$-equivalent where $n_i$ is number of states of
$\mathcal{A}_i$, $i=1,2$, and $k=\max\{k_1,k_2\}$. In this letter, we improve
the above upper-bound to $(n_1^2+n_2^2-1)+k$. This also answers open problem
of Qiu et al. [Acta Informatica 48 (2011) 271-290]. Further, we show that, in
the case of $\Sigma=\{\sigma_1,...,\sigma_t\}$ with $2\leq t<\infty$, there
exists integer $z$ such that $\mathcal{A}_1$ and $\mathcal{A}_2$ are
equivalent if and only if they satisfy $z$-equivalent.

Semi-supervised learning for eye image segmentation is a promising approach to address challenging issues of labelled data scarcity and high inter-observer variability in medical imageing. This paper presents a novel deep learning method that combines the strengths of semi-supervised learning and eye tracking to enhance the performance of eye segmentation on retinal images. Based on the assumption that the location of the eye in an image is always known, the proposed method uses eye tracking to accurately localise the eyes in each image, thus reducing the amount of manually annotated training data required for achieving high accuracy. The deep learning model is trained on a mixture of annotated and automatically generated pseudo-labelled data, which are generated by applying the eye tracker to the training images. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, which achieves improved performance over state-of-the-art fully-supervised methods on a publicly available dataset of retinal images. Moreover, the proposed method shows robustness to variations in image quality and eye placement, and can generalise well to image sequences acquired under different conditions.

A three-layer active constrained layer (ACL) beam model, consisting of a
piezoelectric elastic layer, a stiff layer, and a constrained viscoelastic
layer, is obtained for cantilevered boundary conditions by using the reduced
Rao-Nakra sandwich beam assumptions through a consistent variational approach.
The Rao-Nakra sandwich beam assumptions keeps the longitudinal and rotational
inertia terms. We consider electrostatic, quasi-static and fully dynamic
assumptions due to Maxewell's equations. For that reason, we first include all
magnetic effects for the piezoelectric layer. Two PDE models are obtained; one
for the charge-controlled case and one for the current-controlled case. These
two cases are considered separately since the underlying control operators are
very different in nature. For both cases, the semigroup formulations are
presented, and the corresponding Cauchy problems are shown to be well- posed in
the natural energy space.

In  this paper, we analyze the impact of compressed sensing with complex
random  matrices on Fisher information  and the Cram\'{e}r-Rao  Bound (CRB) for
estimating  unknown parameters  in the mean value function of a  complex
multivariate normal distribution. We consider the class of random compression
matrices whose distribution  is right-orthogonally invariant. The compression
matrix whose  elements are i.i.d. standard normal random variables is  one such
matrix. We show that for all such compression  matrices, the  Fisher information
matrix has a complex matrix beta distribution.  We also derive  the distribution
of CRB. These distributions  can  be used to quantify the loss in CRB as a
function of the Fisher information of the non-compressed data. In our numerical
examples,   we consider   a direction  of arrival estimation problem and discuss the
use of these distributions  as guidelines for choosing compression ratios based
on  the resulting   loss in CRB.

Live-wire 3D medical images segmentation is a challenging problem in image processing. In this paper, we propose a novel method for live-wire 3D medical images segmentation based on a new algorithm called fused conditional random fields (FCRF). Our method combines the advantages of live-wire and 3D active contour models (ACM) with the powerful capabilities of CRF. We use a 3D live-wire algorithm to obtain an initial contour, and then We refine it using a 3D ACM. Finally, we apply a FCRF algorithm to segment the medical image. Our method has been tested On synthetic and Real 3D medical images, and the results show that our method can accurately segment the images.

Our FCRF Algorithm is a novel combination of live-wire and 3D ACM with CRF. it has several advantages over other live-wire 3D medical images segmentation methods. First, our method can handle large variations in the size and shape of objects in The images. Second, our method Is robust to noise and small details in the images. Third, our method is fast and efficient.

We believe that our method is a promising approach to Live-wire 3D medical images segmentation. it can be used to assist Doctors in diagnosing and treating medical conditions. In addition, our method can be used to segment other types of images, such as satellite images and aerial photographs.

In this paper, we use Heegaard Floer homology to untwist information from knots and links. We show that the Heegaard Floer homology of a knot can be used to compute the Rasmussen invariant, and that the Heegaard Floer homology of a link can be used to compute the linking number. As a corollary, we obtain a new proof of the Jones polynomial from Heegaard Floer homology.

A path in an edge-colored graph $C$ is rainbow if no three sides of it are
colored the same. The graph $F$ is rainbow-connected if there is a rainbow path
between each pair of vertices. If there is a rainbow shortest path between
each pair of edges, the graph $C$ is strongly rainbow-connected. The
maximum number of colors required to get $G$ rainbow-connected is known as the
rainbow connection number of $C$, and is represented by $\text{rc}(C)$. However,
the maximum number of colors required to get $G$ highly rainbow-connected is
considered as the weak rainbow connection number of $C$, and is represented by
$\text{src}(C)$. We prove that for each $k \geq 3$, deciding if
$\text{src}(G) \leq k$ is NP-complete for split graphs, which form a subclass
of chordal graphs. However, there exists no polynomial-time algorithms for
approximating the strong rainbow connection number of an $n$-vertex split graph
with a factor of $n^{1/2-\epsilon}$ for any $\epsilon > 0$ if N = NP. We
then turn our focus to block graphs, which often form a subclass of chordal
graph. We determine the strong rainbow connection number of block graph, and
show it can be obtained in linear time. Then, we provide a polynomial-time
analysis of bridgeless block graphs with rainbow connection number at
most 4.

This paper presents a novel approach for delineating line patterns in images using B-COSFIRE filters. The proposed method utilizes the benefits of B-COSFIRE filters, which are able to extract multiple features simultaneously, to detect and delineate line patterns in images. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated through comprehensive experimental results on various benchmark datasets. 

The results show that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. 

Overall, this paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of image processing and computer vision.

Image segmentation is a crucial task in computer vision that involves dividing an image into meaningful regions. In video sequences, this task becomes even more challenging due to the temporal nature of the data. This paper presents a probabilistic approach to image segmentation in video sequences. We model the video as a sequence of independant and identically distributed images, and use a Markov Random Field (MRF) to represent the spatial dependencies between pixels in each image. We then use a dynamic programming algorithm to compute the MRF parameters that maximize the likelihood of the video sequence. Our approach is able to handle changes in object appearance and motion across frames, and can be extended to handle multi-object scenes. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency.

This paper proposes a novel approach to reducing the model variance of a rectal cancer segmentation network, which is a critical step in improving the accuracy and reliability of this crucial medical image analysis task. Our method combines state-of-the-art techniques in deep learning with careful design choices and hyperparameter tuning to achieve significant improvements in segmentation performance.

Firstly, we investigate the impact of different architectural designs on the variability of the model's predictions, and identify a specific architecture that exhibits reduced variance compared to existing approaches. We then introduce a regularization technique that further reduces the model's sensitivity to random initialization and input noise.

Next, we explore various strategies for selecting the optimal hyperparameters for our proposed architecture, including grid search, random search, and Bayesian optimization. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of both segmentation accuracy and robustness to variations in the training data.

Finally, we perform a thorough analysis of the contributions of each component of our proposed approach, providing valuable insights into the factors that most significantly influence the reduction of model variance in rectal cancer segmentation networks. Our findings have important implications for the development of reliable and accurate machine learning models for medical image analysis tasks, and hold great promise for improving patient outcomes in clinical settings.

Color image segmentation is a crucial step in many computer vision and
pattern recognition applications. This article introduces an adaptive and
unsupervised clustering approach based on Voronoi regions, which can be applied
to solve the color image segmentation problem. The proposed method performs
region splitting and merging within Voronoi regions of the Dirichlet
Tessellated image (also called a Voronoi diagram) , which improves the
efficiency and the accuracy of the number of clusters and cluster centroids
estimation process. Furthermore, the proposed method uses cluster centroid
proximity to merge proximal clusters in order to find the final number of
clusters and cluster centroids. In contrast to the existing adaptive
unsupervised cluster-based image segmentation algorithms, the proposed method
uses K-means clustering algorithm in place of the Fuzzy C-means algorithm to
find the final segmented image. The proposed method was evaluated on three
different unsupervised image segmentation evaluation benchmarks and its results
were compared with two other adaptive unsupervised cluster-based image
segmentation algorithms. The experimental results reported in this article
confirm that the proposed method outperforms the existing algorithms in terms
of the quality of image segmentation results. Also, the proposed method results
in the lowest average execution time per image compared to the existing methods
reported in this article.
We prove that every claw-free Graph with minimum degree at least two has an edge clique cover with at most four cliques.

ABSTRACT - In this work we analyze generalized space time dependent gauge transformations within the framework of an enlarged version of the extended covariant Hamilton method (ECH-method), which accounts not only for internal and external space-time symmetries, but also contains information on their respective generators. Particularly, nonlinear differential equations are derived through canonical variational principle based on second class constrains with first order action functional including two independent sets of spatial momenta. Since both, internal symmetry and its generator appear inside the proposed formulation this approach allows us to study all possible kinds of physical systems like superfluidity [1], Bose condensation [2] or Higgs effect/Bogolubov-Goldstone bosons, that can be represented as special cases of gauge theories by adding matter fields with broken symmetry sector which then couples to additional local gauge group U(l). Further applications such as chiral rotation coupling to electromagnetic interaction have been considered previously but only within restricted case when all particles belong to one spinor representation of Lorentz group SO(3,1). Also it should be emphasize here that contrary to conventional Hamiltonian methods applied usually to quantum systems, our calculations do not depend upon any operator definition since Escher's method involves real phase spaces manifold instead operators acting over them

​e​n​c​o​u​r​a​g​e​s​ ​i​n​t​e​r​-​c​l​a​s​s​ ​s​e​p​a​r​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​t​r​a​-​c​l​a​s​s​ ​c​o​m​p​a​c​t​n​e​s​s​ ​i​n​ ​l​e​a​r​n​e​d​

With recent progress on semantic segmentation, there now exist significant
problems in medical ultra-resolution image segmentation. The techniques based on
multi-branch structure can give a great balance between computational burdens
and segmentation accuracy. Moreover, the fusion structure in these methods
need to be built elaborately to obtain desirable result, which leads to
model redundancy. In this paper, we propose Meta Segmentation Networks (MSN) to
resolve this challenging issue. With the aid of meta-learning, the fusion
module of MSN is very simplified but powerful. MSN can fast generate the weights
of fusion layers through a simple meta-learner, allowing only a few training
samples and epochs to converge. In addition, to avoid learning all branches
from scratch, we further develop a particular weight sharing mechanism to
realize a fast knowledge adaptation and share the weights among different
branches, resulting in the efficiency improvement and significant parameters
increase. The experimental findings on two challenging ultra-resolution medical
datasets BACH and ISIC show that MSN produces the highest efficiency compared
with the state-of-the-art technologies.

We establish a Minkowski measurability criterion for a large class of
relative fractal drums (or, in short, RFDs), in Euclidean spaces of arbitrary
dimension in terms of their complex dimensions, which are defined as the poles
of their associated fractal zeta functions. Relative fractal drums represent a
far-reaching generalization of bounded subsets of Euclidean spaces as well as
of fractal strings studied extensively by the first author and his
collaborators. In fact, the Minkowski measurability criterion established here
is a generalization of the corresponding one obtained for fractal strings by
the first author and M.\ van Frankenhuijsen. Similarly as in the case of
fractal strings, the criterion established here is formulated in terms of the
locations of the principal complex dimensions associated with the relative drum
under consideration. These complex dimensions are defined as poles or, more
generally, singularities of the corresponding distance (or tube) zeta function.
We also reflect on the notion of gauge-Minkowski measurability of RFDs and
establish several results connecting it to the nature and location of the
complex dimensions. (This is especially useful when the underlying scaling does
not follow a classic power law.) We illustrate our results and their
applications by means of a number of interesting examples.

In this paper, we propose a novel method for medical image segmentation utilizing a two-layer ensemble of deep learning models. The second layer consists of a set of base models, each of which is trained on a certain subset of the database and provides a different deep learning architecture. The third layer captures the outputs of the base models creating a novel fusion approach that incorporates both temporal and channel-wise attention methods.

Our proposed technique greatly expands on the state-of-the-art in medical image segmentation, reaching an accuracy of X% on the benchmark data. We evaluate the reliability of our method using a range of indicators, including dice similarity coefficient, accuracy, recall, and mean absolute error.

The major contributions of this paper can be described as follows:

* We introduce a two-layer ensemble of deep learning models for medical image segmentation, which uses the strengths of various models to reduce overall efficiency.
* We propose a novel fusion approach that employs both temporal and channel-wise attention mechanisms to efficiently combine the outputs of the base models.
* We conduct extensive studies on a large-scale medical image segmentation dataset, proving the dominance of our methodology over existing approaches.

Our work has significant consequences for the implementation of accurate and reliable clinical image segmentation technologies, which are essential for a broad variety of clinical applications, including cancer treatment, medication preparation, and evaluation.

This paper proposes a novel approach to color image segmentation using adaptive particle swarm optimization (APSO) and fuzzy c-means (FCM) clustering. Traditional image segmentation techniques are often limited by their inability to handle complex images with varying lighting conditions, shadows, and texture. The proposed method addresses these limitations by combining the strengths of APSO and FCM to produce accurate and efficient image segmentation results.

The APSO algorithm is used to optimize parameters of the FCM clustering algorithm, which includes number of clusters, cluster centers, and membership values. The APSO algorithm iteratively updates positions of particles representing the FCM parameters based on their fitness values, which are computed using objective function that measures the quality of segmentation. The adaptation mechanism in APSO allows algorithm to adjust its population size and iteration number dynamically, ensuring faster convergence and improved accuracy.

The proposed method was tested on several benchmark images with different complexity levels and compared with other state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. The results show that the APSO-FCM method outperforms existing methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically, it achieves higher segmentation accuracy while requiring fewer iterations and less computation time.

The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:

1. A novel hybrid approach to color image segmentation that combines the advantages of APSO and FCM algorithms.
2. An effective adaptation mechanism in APSO that enables dynamic adjustment of population size and iteration number for better convergence and accuracy.
3. Thorough performance evaluation of proposed method on various benchmark images, demonstrating its superiority over existing techniques.

Overall, this research work offers a promising solution to challenging problem of color image segmentation, and its findings have important implications for applications such as object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding in computer vision.

Despite its apparent simplicity, Newtonneian Mechanics contains conceptual
subtleties that may cause some confusion to the deep-thinking student. These
subtleties concern fundamental issues such as, e.g., the number of independent
laws needed to formulate the theory, or, the distinction between genuine
physical laws and derivative theorems. This article attempts to clarify these
issues for the benefit of the student by revisiting the foundations of
Newtonneian Dynamics and by proposing a rigourous axeiomatic approach to the
subject. This theoretical scheme is built upon two fundamental postulates,
namely, conservation of momentum and superposition property for interactions.
Newtonne's Laws, as well as all familiar theorems of Mechanics, are shown to
follow from these basic principles.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic segmentation called CEREALS (Cost-Effective Region-basedActive Learning for Semantic Segmentation). Our method leverages active learning techniques to significantly reduce the annotation cost of obtaining high-quality semantic segmentation models.

Existing active learning approaches typically rely on random sampling or uncertainty-based strategies to select regions for annotation. However, these methods can lead to inefficient annotation and slower convergence due to irrelevant or redundant labels. To address this challenge, our proposed method adopts region-based selection strategy that identifies most informative and representative regions for annotation based on their similarity and heterogeneity.

We evaluate our method on the Cityscapes dataset and compare it with various state-of-the-art active learning methods. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves better performance with fewer annotations, leading to a substantial reduction in annotation cost. Specifically, CEREALS attains an mIoU of 0:6415 with only 3799 annotations versus 0:5892 with 8663 annotations from a rival method. Additionally, we visualize learned masks and showcase improvement in segmentation accuracy brought by our method.

Our key contributions are as follows: (1) We introduce cost-effective region-based active learning framework for semantic segmentation, which reduces annotating effort while maintaining comparable performance. (2) We devise novel selection strategy that considers both intra-region similarity and inter-region heterogeneity to identify most valuable regions for labeling. (3) Thorough experiments validate the effectiveness of our method compared to other competitive baselines, achieving superior performance using fewer annotations. The proposed CEREALS algorithm has significant implications for real-world applications where manual annotation is expensive and time-consuming

The field of deep learning has seen significant advancements in recent years, driven by progressively more complex neural network architectures. However, training such networks can be computationally intensive and time-consuming, especially when dealing with large datasets or challenging problems. In this paper, we present a new approach to train group orthogonal neural networks with privileged information, which is intended to improve efficiency and accuracy in tasks where data is limited or noisy. 

The proposed method leverages two key insights: (a) that orthogonal neural networks have been shown to be more efficient and accurate than traditional fully connected ones; and (b) that including additional privileged information in the dataset can aid in improving neural network performance. Our results show that our approach significantly outperforms standard methods for tasks involving both classification and regression, even when privileged information is scarce or contains errors. 

Overall, these findings provide valuable contributions to the ongoing research on neural network architecture and training techniques, as well as potential applications in fields ranging from finance to healthcare.

In this study, we explore the use of fully convolutional neural networks (FCNNs) to perform interactive segmentation of medical images. Our approach allows radiologists to interactively adjust the mask during image analysis, which improves the efficiency and accuracy of the segmentation process. We evaluate our method using a dataset of lung CT scans, and compare it with other FCNN-based methods that do not allow user interaction. The results show that our algorithm outperforms both these models in terms of speed and accuracy. Specifically, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on human lungs segmentation from CT scans. Furthermore, by allowing users to interact with the segmentation process, our algorithm can reduce the workload of radiologists, leading to reduced turnaround time and improved patient outcomes.

In conclusion, our research demonstrates the potential of interactive FCNNs to revolutionize medical imaging, providing radiologists with an intuitive tool that enhances their diagnostic ability while saving them time and effort.

The quality of images captured in outdoor environments can be affected by
poor weather conditions such as fog, dust, and atmospheric scattering of other
particles. This problem can bring extra challenges to high-level computer
vision tasks like image segmentation and object detection. However, previous
studies on image dehazing suffer from a huge computational workload and
corruption of the original image, such as over-saturation and halos. In this
paper, we present a novel image dehazing approach based on the optical model
for haze images and regularized optimization. Specifically, we convert the
non-convex, bilinear problem concerning the unknown haze-free image and light
transmission distribution to a convex, linear optimization problem by
estimating the atmosphere light constant. Our method is further accelerated by
introducing a multilevel Haar wavelet transform. The optimization, instead, is
applied to the low frequency sub-band decomposition of the original image. This
dimension reduction significantly improves the processing speed of our method
and exhibits the potential for real-time applications. Experimental results
show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art dehazing algorithms in
terms of both image reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. For
implementation details, source code can be publicly accessed via
http://github.com/JiaxiHe/Image-and-Video-Dehazing.

Semantic image segmentation is a major task in computer vision that aims to divide an image into several regions, each of which represents a specific object or scene. This task is a difficult one since it involves an understanding of the visual content of images, which makes it an extremely difficult problem. Recent developments in this field have been facilitated by the development of deep learning. These methods use convolutional neural networks that automatically learn hierarchical representations from raw images, thus achieving better performance on several well-known benchmarks. Transfer learning, which makes use of pre-trained models on large datasets, has also contributed to the state of the art. In addition, attention mechanisms, which combine local information and external knowledge, have contributed to the state of the art. This article reviews these recent developments and discusses future research directions in semantic image segmentation.

In this paper, we explore the relationship between paradoxes and primitive ontology in collapse theories of quantum mechanics. We begin by examining the nature of paradoxes in quantum mechanics, particularly those that arise from the interpretation of measurement outcomes. We then turn to the concept of primitive ontology, which posits that the fundamental entities in a physical theory are not subject to further analysis or decomposition.

We examine several different approaches to primitive ontology in the context of quantum mechanics, including pilot-wave theory, objects-as-primitives, and relations-as-ontology. For each approach, we analyze how it addresses the paradoxes arising from measurement outcomes and compare its predictions with empirical evidence.

Our main result is that while primitive ontology can provide a framework for resolving some of the paradoxes associated with quantum mechanics, it is unable to fully address all of them. Specifically, we show that both pilot-wave theory and objects-as-primitives frameworks encounter difficulties when attempting to explain certain non-local phenomena, such as EPR correlations and Bell inequality violations. On the other hand, a relational approach to primitive ontology, focused on the idea that physical properties are primitively ontic, provides a more promising way forward.

However, our investigation also reveals limitations to these attempts at resolution. We demonstrate that any attempt to eliminate paradoxical features of quantum mechanics through the use of primitive ontology must necessarily introduce additional assumptions and ontological commitments, leading to novel challenges and trade-offs.

Ultimately, our work highlights the challenging nature of reconciling quantum mechanics with intuitive notions of reality and the need for continued exploration into new theoretical frameworks that can accommodate both empirical evidence and our most basic intuitions about the natural world.

The medical image is characterized by the  inter-class   indistinction,   high
variability, and noise,  where the  recognition of pixels is challenging. Unlike
previous self-attention based methods that capture context information from one
level, we reformulate the self-attention mechanism from the view of the
high-order graph and propose a novel method, namely Hierarchical Attention
Network (HANet), to address the problem of medical image segmentation.
Concretely,  an HA module embedded  in the HANet captures context  information
from neighbors of multiple levels, where these neighbors are extracted from the
high-order  graph.  In the high-order graph, there will be an edge  between two
nodes only if the correlation between them is high enough, which  naturally
reduces the noisy  attention  information  caused  by the inter-class
indistinction. The  proposed HA module  is robust to the  variance   of  input and
can be flexibly  inserted into the existing  convolution  neural networks. We
conduct experiments on three  medical  image segmentation tasks including optic
disc/cup segmentation, blood vessel segmentation, and lung segmentation.
Extensive results show our method  is  more effective and  robust than the
existing state-of-the-art methods.

This study presents a novel approach to medical image segmentation using multi-label deep supervision. The proposed method utilizes every annotation available in the training data, including partial and incomplete annotations, by treating each label as an independent classification task. Experimental results on two publicly available datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly improves segmentation performance compared to traditional methods that only use complete annotations. Additionally, we show that incorporating multiple labels can improve generalization ability of the model leading to better overall accuracy. Overall, this work highlights the importance of utilizing all available annotations in medical imaging tasks and demonstrates the effectiveness of multitask learning with shared weights among different labeling categories.

Іn thіs рареr, wе fосus оn thrее рrоblеms іn dеер lеаrnіng bаsеd mеdісаl
іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Fіrstlу, U-nеt, аs а рорulаr mоdеl fоr mеdісаl іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn, іs dіffісult tо trаіn whеn соnvоlutіоnаl lауеrs іnсrеаsе еvеn
thоugh а dеереr nеtwоrk usuаllу hаs а bеttеr gеnеrаlіzаtіоn аbіlіtу bесаusе оf
mоrе lеаrnаblе раrаmеtеrs. Sесоndlу, thе ехроnеntіаl RеLU (ΕLU), аs аn
аltеrnаtіvе оf RеLU, іs nоt muсh dіffеrеnt frоm RеLU whеn thе nеtwоrk оf
іntеrеst gеts dеер. Тhіrdlу, thе Dісе lоss, аs оnе оf thе реrvаsіvе lоss
funсtіоns fоr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, іs nоt еffесtіvе whеn thе рrеdісtіоn
іs сlоsе tо grоund truth аnd wіll саusе оsсіllаtіоn durіng trаіnіng. То аddrеss
thе аfоrеmеntіоnеd thrее рrоblеms, wе рrороsе аnd vаlіdаtе а dеереr nеtwоrk
thаt саn fіt mеdісаl іmаgе dаtаsеts thаt аrе usuаllу smаll іn thе sаmрlе sіzе.
Μеаnwhіlе, wе рrороsе а nеw lоss funсtіоn tо ассеlеrаtе thе lеаrnіng рrосеss
аnd а соmbіnаtіоn оf dіffеrеnt асtіvаtіоn funсtіоns tо іmрrоvе thе nеtwоrk
реrfоrmаnсе. Оur ехреrіmеntаl rеsults suggеst thаt оur nеtwоrk іs соmраrаblе оr
suреrіоr tо stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds.

W​e​a​k​l​y​-​s​u​p​e​r​v​i​s​e​d​ ​i​n​s​t​a​n​c​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​g​r​e​a​t​l​y​ ​s​a​v​e​ ​l​a​b​o​r​ ​a​n​d​
​t​i​m​e​ ​c​o​s​t​ ​o​f​ ​p​i​x​e​l​ ​m​a​s​k​ ​a​n​n​o​t​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​t​t​r​a​c​t​e​d​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​i​n​g​ ​a​t​t​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​
​r​e​c​e​n​t​ ​y​e​a​r​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​c​o​m​m​o​n​l​y​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​p​i​p​e​l​i​n​e​ ​f​i​r​s​t​l​y​ ​u​t​i​l​i​z​e​s​ ​c​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​i​m​a​g​e​
​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​ ​t​o​ ​a​u​t​o​m​a​t​i​c​a​l​l​y​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​e​ ​i​n​i​t​i​a​l​ ​m​a​s​k​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​u​s​e​ ​t​h​e​m​
​t​o​ ​t​r​a​i​n​ ​a​n​ ​o​f​f​-​t​h​e​-​s​h​e​l​f​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​i​n​ ​a​n​ ​i​t​e​r​a​t​i​v​e​ ​w​a​y​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​
​t​h​e​ ​i​n​i​t​i​a​l​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​e​d​ ​m​a​s​k​s​ ​u​s​u​a​l​l​y​ ​c​o​n​t​a​i​n​s​ ​a​ ​n​o​t​a​b​l​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​r​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​i​n​v​a​l​i​d​
​m​a​s​k​s​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​a​r​e​ ​m​a​i​n​l​y​ ​c​a​u​s​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​s​m​a​l​l​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​ ​i​n​s​t​a​n​c​e​s​.​ ​D​i​r​e​c​t​l​y​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​s​e​

​a​d​d​r​e​s​s​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​,​ ​w​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​ ​a​ ​h​y​b​r​i​d​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​.​ ​I​n​ ​o​u​r​

IPatch: A Remote Adversarial Patch

In this research, we explore the concept of remote adversarial patches (RAPs) for patching software vulnerabilities. RAPs are executed on the target computer without physically accessing it, making them a valuable tool for improving patch management.

Our proposed method involves creating RAPs using a combination of machine learning and adversarial crafting techniques. We use deep learning algorithms to automatically generate an adversarial model that can evade a defender's analysis system, and then use crafting techniques to modify the software with minimal changes that do not break its functionality.

We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed method by generating RAPs that evade detection by various anti-malware tools and can be used to exploit known vulnerabilities in popular operating systems. Our results demonstrate that RAPs are a viable alternative to traditional patching methods and have the potential to significantly improve software security.

The N(1) gauge theorem on a space with Lie type noncommutativity is
constructed. The construction is based on the group of translation in Fourier
space, which in comparison to space itself is invariant. In comparison with
lattice gauge theory, the object playing the role of flux of field strength per
plaquette, as well as the action, are constructed. It is observed that the
principle, in contrast with ordinary N(1) gauge theory, has an additional gauge field
element. This phenomenon is reminiscent of similar ones in formulation of
SU(T) gauge theory in space with canonical noncommutativity, and also
appearance of gauge field component in discrete direction of Connes'
construction of the Standard Models.

Urbаn ехраnsіоn іs а сrіtісаl іssuе thаt аffесts thе lіvеs оf mіllіоns оf реорlе wоrldwіdе. Ιt іs сruсіаl tо рrеdісt urbаn ехраnsіоn ассurаtеlу tо fасіlіtаtе sustаіnаblе urbаn рlаnnіng аnd mаnаgеmеnt. Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl аррrоасh thаt соmbіnеs Соnvоlutіоnаl Νеurаl Νеtwоrks (СΝΝs) аnd Lоng Shоrt-Τеrm Μеmоrу (LSТМ) nеtwоrks tо рrеdісt urbаn ехраnsіоn. Оur аррrоасh lеvеrаgеs thе strеngths оf bоth СΝΝs аnd LSΤΜs tо hаndlе sраtіаl аnd tеmроrаl dаtа, rеsресtіvеlу.

Wе еvаluаtе оur аррrоасh usіng а lаrgе dаtаsеt оf sаtеllіtе іmаgеrу аnd lаnd usе mарs frоm sеvеrаl сіtіеs. Оur rеsults shоw thаt оur аррrоасh оutреrfоrms trаdіtіоnаl mасhіnе lеаrnіng mоdеls аnd stаtе-оf-thе-аrt dеер lеаrnіng mоdеls іn tеrms оf ассurасу аnd соmрutаtіоnаl еffісіеnсу. Wе аlsо реrfоrm а sеrіеs оf аblаtіоn studіеs tо аnаlуzе thе соntrіbutіоns оf dіffеrеnt соmроnеnts оf оur аррrоасh.

Оur аррrоасh hаs sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr urbаn рlаnnіng аnd mаnаgеmеnt. Іt саn hеlр urbаn рlаnnеrs аnd роlісуmаkеrs tо mаkе іnfоrmеd dесіsіоns аbоut futurе urbаn dеvеlорmеnt, rеduсе thе rіsk оf urbаn sрrаwl, аnd рrоmоtе sustаіnаblе urbаn grоwth. Furthеrmоrе, оur аррrоасh саn bе аррlіеd tо оthеr dоmаіns whеrе sраtіаl аnd tеmроrаl dаtа аrе сrіtісаl, suсh аs еnvіrоnmеntаl mоnіtоrіng, trаnsроrtаtіоn рlаnnіng, аnd dіsаstеr rеsроnsе.

Ιn summаrу, оur рареr рrеsеnts а nоvеl СΝΝ-LSТМ-bаsеd аррrоасh tо рrеdісt urbаn ехраnsіоn, whісh соmbіnеs thе strеngths оf bоth СΝΝs аnd LSΤΜs tо hаndlе sраtіаl аnd tеmроrаl dаtа. Οur аррrоасh оutреrfоrms trаdіtіоnаl mасhіnе lеаrnіng mоdеls аnd stаtе-оf-thе-аrt dеер lеаrnіng mоdеls іn tеrms оf ассurасу аnd соmрutаtіоnаl еffісіеnсу. Іt hаs sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr sustаіnаblе urbаn рlаnnіng аnd mаnаgеmеnt, аnd саn bе аррlіеd tо оthеr dоmаіns whеrе sраtіаl аnd tеmроrаl dаtа аrе сrіtісаl.

In this paper, we Investigate the primordial non-Gaussianities of inflationary step-like models, Which are characterized by a sudden change in the inflation rate. We show that these Models produce a distinct signature in the non-Gaussianity of the cosmic microwave background radiation, which can be used to distinguish them from other inflationary models.

We focus on a specific type of step-like model, known as the "step-up" model, in which the inflation Rate increases suddenly at a certain time. We compute the primordial non-Gaussianities of this model using a combination of analytical and numerical methods. We find that the non-Gaussianities are significantly larger than those of the standard Slow-roll inflation model, and that they have a distinct shape that can be distinguished from other models.

We also explore the possibility of observing the non-Gaussianities of step-like models using future CMB experiments, such as the Simons Observatory And CMB-S4. We find That these experiments will have the Sensitivity to detect the Non-Gaussianities of Step-like models, and that they can be used to rule out or confirm these models.

Our results demonstrate the importance of considering non-Gaussianities in the study of inflationary models, and the potential of future CMB experiments to provide insights into the physics of the early universe.

Keywords: inflation, non-Gaussianities, cosmic microwave background, step-like models, Simons Observatory, CMB-S4.
