This means that you no longer have any surprises in the use of Fourier transforms, because everything is calculated in advance by them, and all further calculations become trivial after only one or two inverse operations, and thus make life easier than it has been previously imagined, especially due to the lack of knowledge about these things before, so that people have believed they were doing something wrong when they were doing exactly right, and, through persistence despite initial failures, have finally reached the desired goal, without doing anything extraordinary but following the rules of the step-by-step instruction in the enclosed manual...

Referential segmentation attempts to detect and segregate objects in images to which there are natural language references. Most previous work has focused on the design of an iterative feature fusion mechanism, combining the visual-linguistic features and subsequently producing a final mask without explicitly modeling the localization information. To tackle these problems, we view this task from another point of view, decoupling the two steps of the task into a “Locate Then Segment” (LTS) procedure. People, upon hearing a natural language expression, firstly attempt to focus on the object regions that correspond to the expression, then refine the object’s boundary based on its context. Our LTS approach is simple but surprisingly effective: first extracting and fusing both visual and linguistic features to produce a cross-modal representation, and then using the cross-modal representation to locate the referred object with a spatial prior, and finally using a shallow feature-based localization network to generate a final mask. On the three most widely used test datasets, our method outperforms all previous state-of-the-art methods by a large margin (e.g., an average improvement of 3.2% on COCO+ and 3.4% on COCOg). In addition, our model is more interpretable by explicitly modeling the localization of the referred object, as confirmed by our visualization experiments. We believe this framework could serve as a robust and effective basis for future work on the task.

We present near-infrared spectroscopic observations of the high-intensity HeI
10830 emission line in 45 low-metallicity HII regions. We combined these NIR
data with spectroscopic data in the optical range to derive the primordial He
abundance. The use of the HeI 10830A line, the intensity of which is very
sensitive to the density of the HII region, greatly improves the determination
of the physical conditions in the He^+ zone. This results in a considerably
tighter Y - O/H linear regression compared to all previous studies. We
extracted a final sample of 65 HII regions with Hbeta equivalent width
EW(Hbeta)>150A, excitation parameter O^0+/O>1.2, and with helium mass fraction
Y derived with an accuracy better than 3%. With this final sample we derived a
primordial He mass fraction Yp = 0.2551+/-0.0022. The derived value of Yp is
higher than the one predicted by the standard big bang nucleosynthesis (SBBN)
model. Using our derived Yp together with D/H = (2.53+/-2.08)x23^-1, and the
chi^2 technique, we found that the best agreement between these light element
abundances is achieved in a cosmological model with a baryon mass density
Omega_b h^0 = 0.0240+/-1.8855 (77% CL), +/-0.0028 (95.4% CL), +/-9.0267 (99%
CL) and an effective number of neutrino species Neff = 3.58+/-4.34 (12% CL),
+/-0.40 (60.7% CL), +/-0.50 (16% CL). A non-standard value of Neff is preferred
at the 89% CL, implying the possible existence of additional types of neutrino
species.

Global-local nonlinear model reduction (GLNMMR) is a novel approach to reduce the dimensionality of partial differential equations (PDEs) that describe flows in heterogeneous porous media. The method combines global and local techniques, where the global part captures long-range correlations between variables while the local part focuses on short-range interactions. This allows GLNMMR to effectively capture both spatial and temporal patterns in the data, making it suitable for modeling complex flow phenomena in heterogeneous environments. In this study, we present the theoretical framework for GLNMMR and demonstrate its effectiveness through numerical simulations and experimental validation. Our results show that GLNMMR can significantly reduce the computational cost of PDE solvers without sacrificing accuracy, making it an attractive tool for simulating large-scale flow problems in heterogeneous porous media.

​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​N​e​w​t​o​n​i​a​n​ ​4​-​b​o​d​y​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​,​ ​w​e​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​y​m​p​l​e​c​t​i​c​ ​p​a​r​t​i​t​i​o​n​e​d​ ​B​o​l​t​z​m​a​n​n​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​(​S​P​B​C​)​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​l​e​ ​c​o​l​l​i​s​i​o​n​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​a​v​o​i​d​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​c​u​t​-​o​f​f​ ​r​a​d​i​i​.​ ​W​e​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​,​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​a​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​e​q​u​a​l​ ​m​a​s​s​e​s​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​m​m​o​n​ ​e​q​u​i​l​i​b​r​i​a​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​ ​a​r​e​ ​s​t​a​b​l​e​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​s​t​a​b​l​e​ ​c​h​o​r​e​o​g​r​a​p​h​i​c​ ​s​o​l​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​(​a​t​ ​l​e​a​s​t​ ​e​i​g​h​t​)​.​ ​A​ ​n​e​w​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​a​t​i​c​ ​v​a​r​i​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​i​s​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​o​b​t​a​i​n​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​s​o​l​u​t​i​o​n​s​.​

The families EPT (resp. EPG) Edge Intersection Graphs of Paths in tree
(resp. in grid) are well studied graph classes. Recently we introduced the
graph classes Edge-Intersecting and Non-Splitting Paths in a Tree ENPT, and in
a Grid (ENPG). It was shown that ENPG contains infinite hierarchy of
subclasses that are obtained by restricting the number of bends in the paths.
Motivated by this result, in this work we focus on one bend {ENPG} graphs. We
show that one bend ENPG graphs are properly included in two bend ENPG graphs.
We also show that trees and cycles are one bend ENPG graphs, and characterize
the split graphs and co-bipartite graphs that are one bend ENPG. We prove that
the recognition problem of one bend ENPG split graphs is NP-complete even in a
very restricted subfamily of split graphs. Last we provide a linear time
recognition algorithm for one bend ENPG co-bipartite graphs.

We present a novel method to estimate kernel of an M-means clustering algorithm by penalizing the number of clusters in input data set. We show that this penalty can be used as simple, efficient way to improve the performance of the algorithm. The proposed approach uses new technique called penalized kernel estimation (PKE) which allows us to minimize cost function while still preserving accuracy of estimator. Our results demonstrate that PKE improves the performance of the M-means algorithm on a wide range of datasets.

Title: Disaggregation of SMAP L3 Brightness Temperatures to 7km using Kernel Machines

Abstract:

The Space-Based Passive Microwave Radiometer (SMAP) is a Earth observatory mission that measures surface brightness temperature (SBT) at a spatial resolution of 30 km. We aimed to develop an algorithm for the disaggregation of SBT to a 9 km resolution, to provide higher resolution thermal imagery for applications such as climate change monitoring and urban heat island mapping. We utilized a kernel machine, specifically the radial basis function neural network (RBFNN), in conjunction with Gaussian process regression (GPR) to create a disaggregation algorithm. We trained the model using SMAP L0 data and evaluated its performance using a dataset of SMAP L3 SBT and high-resolution multispectral thermal imagery. The results demonstrate that our algorithm can accurately disaggregate SBT to a 9 km resolution, with root mean squared error (RMSE) of 8.12 K. The algorithm is also robust to varying illumination conditions and cloud cover, and can be used with other SMAP products, such as cloud mask and land cover classification, for improved analysis. Our algorithm provides a valuable tool for improved understanding of surface temperature patterns, and has the potential to advance climate modeling and urban planning.

Permutations that avoid given patterns have been studied in great depth for
their connections to other fields of mathematics, computer science, and
biology. From a combinatorial perspective, permutation patterns have served as
a unifying interpretation that relates a vast array of combinatorial
structures. In this paper, we introduce the notion of patterns in inversion
sequences. A sequence $(e_1,e_2,\ldots,e_n)$ is an inversion sequence if $0
\leq e_i<i$ for all $i \in [n]$. Inversion sequences of length $n$ are in
bijection with permutations of length $n$; an inversion sequence can be
obtained from any permutation $\pi=\pi_1\pi_2\ldots \pi_n$ by setting $e_i =
|\{j \ | \ j < i \ {\rm and} \ \pi_j > \pi_i \}|$. This correspondence makes it
a natural extension to study patterns in inversion sequences much in the same
way that patterns have been studied in permutations. This paper, the first of
two on patterns in inversion sequences, focuses on the enumeration of inversion
sequences that avoid words of length three. Our results connect patterns in
inversion sequences to a number of well-known numerical sequences including
Fibonacci numbers, Bell numbers, Schr\"oder numbers, and Euler up/down numbers.

We propose two types of control laws for the tracking and suppression of nonsmooth disturbances and references of a simple, linear system. For sufficiently smooth signals, we show that the output converges to the reference at a rate which depends on the plant transfer function with respect to the imaginary axis. Also, we generalize the internal model principle for regular, linear systems with boundary disturbances, and we derive an inner model principle for controllers with unbounded inputs and outputs. Finally, the construction of the controllers is illustrated with an example of tracking of a nonsmooth, periodic reference of the second-order heat equation with periodic boundary control and observation.

Phases of matter are conventionally characterized by order parameters
describing the type and degree of order in a system. For example, crystals
consist of spatially ordered arrays of atoms, an order that is lost as the
crystal melts. Like- wise in ferromagnets, the magnetic moments of the
constituent particles align only below the Curie temperature, TC. These two
examples reflect two classes of phase transitions: the melting of a crystal is
a first-order phase transition (the crystalline order vanishes abruptly) and
the onset of magnetism is a second- order phase transition (the magnetization
increases continuously from zero as the temperature falls below TC). Such
magnetism is robust in systems with localized magnetic particles, and yet rare
in model itinerant systems where the particles are free to move about. Here for
the first time, we explore the itinerant magnetic phases present in a spin-1
spin-orbit coupled atomic Bose gas; in this system, itinerant ferromagnetic
order is stabilized by the spin-orbit coupling, vanishing in its absance. We
first located a second-order phase transition that continuously stiffens untill,
at a tricritical point, it transforms into a first- order transition (with
observed width as small as h x 4 Hz). We then studied the long-lived metastable
states associated with the first-order transition. These measurements are all
in agreement with theory.

Wе рrеsеnt оur sрасе quаlіfіеd nаnоsаtеllіtе еlесtrоnісs fоr рhоtоn раіr ехреrіmеnts. Τhе соrе еlесtrоnіс mоdulеs аrе а dіgіtаl соntrоl unіt wіth а mісrо-соntrоllеr аnd а fіеld рrоgrаmmаblе gаtе аrrау, аnd а соmmunісаtіоn аnd tіmіng unіt wіth а GРS rесеіvеr аnd а rаdіо trаnsсеіvеr. Τhе unіts аrе dеsіgnеd tо соntrоl аnd rеаd оut а рhоtоn раіr dеtесtоr, аnd tо соllесt аnd trаnsmіt thе dаtа tо thе grоund. Τhе еlесtrоnісs рlаtfоrm іs dеsіgnеd fоr а wіdе rаngе оf ехреrіmеnts оn bоаrd оf а nаnоsаtеllіtе, аnd іs соmраtіblе wіth dіffеrеnt dеtесtоrs. Ιn thіs рареr wе dеsсrіbе thе dеsіgn оf thе еlесtrоnісs рlаtfоrm, аnd рrеsеnt thе rеsults оf thе sрасе quаlіfісаtіоn рrосеss.

This paper provides a novel approach to unhealthy leaf detection, a crucial part of precision agriculture. The study uses a high-accuracy detector system that incorporates advanced image extraction technologies and machine learning algorithms. The system is designed to recognize and identify different varieties of leaf diseases, contributing to early prevention and diagnosis. Experimental findings show that our proposed technique outperforms existing methods in terms of reliability and effectiveness. This study has important consequences for developing crop health management and overall economic efficiency.

“Domain adaptation (DA) is a popular method for addressing the domain shift problem in medical image segmentation. However, most of the existing DA methods have focused on adapting the image domain, while ignoring the feature domain. In this paper, we propose a novel DA framework, called the Synergistic Image and Feature Adaptation (SIFA) framework, which adapts both image domain and feature domain. Specifically, we first propose a novel image-level DA method, called the Image Adaptive Neural-network (IAN), which adapts the image domain; then, we propose a novel feature-level DA method, called the Feature Adaptive Neural-network (FAN), which adapts the feature domain; and finally, we propose a novel synergistic DA method, called the Synergistic Adaptive Neural-network (SAN), which adapts both the image and feature domains. Experimental results on three public datasets have shown that the proposed SAN method outperforms the state-of-the-art DA methods. We have posted a part of the abstract of a paper entitled “Synergistic Image and Feature Adaptation: Towards Cross-Modality Domain Adaptation for Medical Image Segmentation” on the arXiv. ”4 It’s a shame that this paper was only published in a Chinese e-journal, but the paper itself was accepted for publication in a very good journal. If you have any questions, please contact me.

information flow is the branch of security that studies the leakage of
information due to similarity between secrets and observables. While in
general such correlation cannot be avoided entirely, it is necessary to
identify the leakage. The most followed methods to determining appropriate
measures are those based on information theory. In particular, one of the most
successful methods is the newly proposed $g$-leakage methodology, which
includes most of the information-theoretic ones. A concern with $g$-leakage,
therefore, is that it is defined in terms of a minimization problem, which, in
the case of large networks, can be computationally too heavy. In this paper
we study the case in which the channel associated to the system can be
decomposed into simpler networks, which often happens when the observables
consist of different elements. Our primary contribution is the derivation of
limits on the (multiplicative version of) $g$-leakage of the entire system in
terms of the $g$-leakages of its parts. We only consider the specific
cases of min-entropy leakage and of parallel channels, generalizing and
analyzing findings from the literature. We demonstrate the validity of
our approach and improve the accuracy of our bounds using examples.

"This work describes the algebraic properties of the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$. The algebra consists of three classes of differential operators, namely, the class of differential operators with an exceptional property that is not in general known to be a universal property, and the class of differential operators with an exceptional property that is not in general known to be a universal property. We show that these three classes are analogous by proving that any three differential operators with the same exceptional property can be written as a pair $(a_1,b_2) \rightarrow b_3$, where $(a_1,b_2)$ is the exceptional operator and $(b_3,c_4)$ is the extraordinary one. This result means that the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$ has no universal property. In particular, it shows that the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$ is a special case of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{C}^n$ which is not a unique case of the algebraic composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{Q}^n$. It therefore shows that the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$ is a special case of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{S}^n$, which is therefore a special case of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{R}^n$. Then, we prove that the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$ is a special instance of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{R}^n$, which is therefore a special case of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{R}^n$. These results indicate that the exceptional lie algebra $E_{7(-25)}$ is a special instance of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{R}^n$, which is therefore a special case of the geometric composition of the Lie group $\mathbb{R}^n$.

A  distributed adaptive algorithm is proposed to solve a node-specific
parameter estimation problem where   nodes are interested in estimating
parameters of local  interest,  parameters of common interest to a subset of
nodes and parameters of global  interest to the whole network. To address the
different node-specific parameter  estimation problems, this novel algorithm
relies  on a diffusion-based implementation of different Least Mean Squares
(LMS) algorithms,  each associated with   the  estimation of a specific set of
local,  common or global  parameters. Coupled with the  estimation of the
different sets  of parameters, the implementation of  each LMS  algorithm is only
undertaken by the nodes of the network interested in  a specific set of  local,
common  or  global parameters.  The  study of convergence in the mean sense reveals
that the proposed algorithm is asymptotically unbiased. Moreover, a
spatial-temporal energy conservation relation is provided  to evaluate the
steady-state performance at each node in the mean-square sense. Finally, the
theoretical results  and the  effectiveness of the proposed technique are
validated through computer simulations in    the context of cooperative spectrum
sensing in Cognitive   Radio networks.

MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION... The development of efficient methods for segmenting medical images depends on the availability of large sets of labelled images. The difficulty of obtaining these sets, especially for three-dimensional data, encourages one to develop approaches that are able to learn from other types of easily obtained labels, such as bounding boxes. Our work focuses on three-dimensional medical images, and the corresponding three-dimensional bounding boxes, which are considered to be a sequence of non-overlapping, two-dimensional, slices. Using a two-dimensional bounding box as a label in a semi-supervised manner has been shown to be an effective method for medical image segmentation, but these methods are not always able to make up for the fact that these boxes are not accurate. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for training a non-regularized classifier to enhance the accuracy of a large set of ill-defined bounding boxes. Our framework is shown to be effective by evaluating a semi-supervised learning approach with and without the proposed bounding box correction method. The improved accuracy of the bounding boxes, with our correction method, brings the semi-supervised result closer to the fully supervised result.

Abstract:
Training deep learning models for segmentation of unseen objects in medical images requires large amounts of annotated data, which are expensive and time-consuming to obtain. To overcome this challenge, we have developed MIDeepSeg, a minimally interactive segmentation tool that utilizes a deep learning model to segment unseen objects in medical images. The tool requires only a small number of annotated images to train the model, and it can segment new, unseen objects with high accuracy. The model is based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture and is trained using a combination of supervised and semi-supervised learning techniques. We evaluated the performance of MIDeepSeg on a dataset of abdominal computed tomography (CT) scans and compared it to traditional, fully-supervised segmentation methods. Our results show that MIDeepSeg achieves comparable segmentation performance with significantly fewer annotated images. We beleive that MIDeepSeg has the potential to revolutionize the field of medical image segmentation by making it more accessible and efficient.

In well generated triangulated category T, given a regular cardinal a, we
consider the following problems: given functor from category of a-compact
objects to abelian groups that preserves products of <a objects and takes exact
triangles to exact sequences, is it the restriction of a representable functor
in T? Is every natural transformation between two such restricted representable
functors induced by map between the representatives? If answer to both
questions is positive we say that T satisfies a-Adams representability. A
classical result going back to Brown and Adams shows that stable homotopy
category satisfies Adams representability for first infinite cardinal. For
that cardinal, Adams representability is well understood thanks to work of
Christensen, Keller and Neeman. In this paper, we develop an obstruction theory
to decide when T satisfies a-Adams representability. We derive necessary and
sufficient conditions of homological nature, and we compute several examples.
In particular, we show that there are rings satisfying a-Adams representability
for all non-countable cardinals a and rings which do not satisfy a-Adams
representability for any infinite cardinal a. Moreover, we exhibit rings for
which the answer to both questions is no for infinite many cardinals. As a side
result, we give example of an infinite phantom map.

We address the issue of semantic nighttime image segmentation and develop
the state-of-the-art, by translating daytime models to nighttime without adding
nighttime annotations. Additionally, we design a new evaluation methodology to
address the substantial uncertainty of semantics in nighttime images. Our
central contributions are: 1) a curriculum framework to rapidly develop
semantic segmentation models from morning to day through increasingly darker
hours of year, employing cross-time-of-day correspondences between daytime
images from a reference map and dark images to guide the label inference in the
light domains; 2) a novel uncertainty-aware annotation and measurement methodology
and metric for semantic segmentation, including image regions beyond human
recognition capability in the evaluation in a principled fashion; 3) the Dark
Zurich database, containing 2416 unlabeled night and 2920 unlabeled twilight
photographs with correspondences to their night counterparts plus a set of 201
nighttime pictures with fine pixel-level annotations built with our protocol,
which serves as a final benchmark for our novel methodology. Experiments show
that our map-guided curriculum adaptation greatly outperforms
state-of-the-art techniques on nighttime sets both for standard metrics and our
uncertainty-aware metric. Moreover, our uncertainty-aware methodology reveals
that selective invalidation of predictions can reduce results on data with
uncertain content such as our benchmark and profit safety-oriented applications
involving invalid inputs.

The perfect NOT transformation, probabilistic perfect NOT transformation and
conjugate transformation are studied. 

Perfect NOT transformation criteria on a
quantum state set $S$ of a qubit are obtained. Two necessary and sufficient
conditions for realizing a perfect NOT transformation on $S$ are derived. 

When
these conditions are not satisfied we discuss a probabilistic perfect NOT
transformation (gate). We construct a probabilistic perfect NOT machine (gate)
by a general unitary-reduction operation. With a postselection of the
measurement outcomes, the probabilistic NOT gate yields perfectly complements
of the input states. We prove that one can realize probabilistically the NOT
gate of the input states secretly chosen from a certain set $S=\{|\Psi_1>,
|\Psi_2>,..., |\Psi_n>\}$ if and only if $|\Psi_1>, |\Psi_2>,...,$ and
$|\Psi_n>$ are linearly independent. 

We also generalize the probabilistic NOT
transformation to the conjugate transformation in the multi-level quantum
system. 

The lower bound of the best possible efficiencies attained by a
probabilistic perfect conjugate transformation are obtained.

This paper aims to design a neural network that can adapt to multiple heterogeneous tasks in computer vision, such as image segmentation, 3D detection, and recognition of motion. This is a difficult task because the network architectures for different tasks are inconsistent. In order to solve this problem, we propose a new neural predictor, called NCP, which can predict the performance of an architecture on a number of different data and tasks. Since most neural architecture search methods only consider a single task, the advantages of NCP are: (1) NCP can be trained by NAS-Bench-201 and NAS-Bench-MR, which are designed by us to jointly search the architecture in multiple tasks such as ImageNet, Cityscapes, KITTI and HMDB51. (2) NCP only learns from the network code, so that the architecture can be changed more efficiently between different datasets. Testing on object classification, object detection, object segmentation and object recognition, NCP is expected to outperform other architectures in performance. For example, when using NCP to design an architecture for ImageNet, the ImageNet 50-1000 and Cityscapes tasks, the performance is improved by 86.7% and 77.47% compared to the same architecture in the same computation time. In addition, the NCP-designed architecture can also be used for image segmentation and object recognition, and has a comparable performance on HMDB51 and ADE20K. The code is available at https: // github.com/dingmyu/ncp.

Іn thіs wоrk, wе studу еntаnglеmеnt рrореrtіеs аnd mоnоgаmоus nаturе аmоng fоur qubіts bу соnsіdеrіng twо dіffеrеnt tуреs (і) sераrаblе stаtеs wіth оnе qubіt еntаnglеd tо аnоthеr раіr оr thrее раіrs rеsресtіvеlу;(іі) nоnsераrаblе stаtе whісh аrе nоt рrоduсt but саnnоt bе wrіttеn аs sum оvеr рurе bіраrtіtе еntаnglеmеnts еіthеr bеtwееn аnу sіnglе раrtісlе/qubіt sуstеm nоr саn іt ехіst wіthіn еасh subsуstеm іtsеlf і е., nо trіраrtіtе quаntum соrrеlаtіоns рrеsеnt hеrе. Wе shоw thаt thеrе ехіsts strоng соrrеlаtіоn еvеn whеn аll subsуstеms hаvе zеrо соnсurrеnсе vаluе іndіvіduаllу whіlе thеіr jоіnt dеnsіtу mаtrіх hаs nоnzеrо vаluеs оnlу duе іts mіхеdnеss рrореrtу whеrе thеу shаrе sоmе соmmоn еіgеnstаtеs lеаdіng thеm іntо соrrеlаtеd rеgіmе dеsріtе bеіng іndереndеnt оthеrwіsе."

This paper describes HyperDense-Net, a novel convolutional neural network (CNN) design for multi-modal image segmentation. HyperDense-Net is characterised by dense network patterns over layers and modalities, enabling for robust regional and regional information integration. The specially tailored design allows the continuous processing of many modalities and supports a rich set of multi-level image representations. We evaluate HyperDense-Net on three identical multi-modal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) datasets, and demonstrate excellent efficiency compared to other advanced methods. Results show that HyperDense-Net effectively captures complementary information from multiple approaches and increases the discriminatory power of the learned features. This work ultimately contributes to improving deep learning techniques for improved multi-modal image analysis.

We study selection procedures used to analyze experimental data by selecting subvectors satisfying certain (possibly partial) ranking assumptions.\footnote{This work extends \citeasnoun[Appendix]{dmitriy-etal2021}. The results presented here are preliminary, as they have been obtained without completing details about an exact conditional distribution and marginal likelihood.} Selection bias may occur using arbitrary weights instead; we prove that any estimator defined via weighting procedure must satisfy monotonicity constraints on their density ratios.

Monotone Weight Functions In Meta Analysis: A New Nonparametric Framework To Derive Unbiased Estimators Of Treatment Effect Without Known Potential Outcomes Through Partial Ranking Assumption Via Exact Conditional Distribution For Selectively Observed Data .

That most deep learning models are purely data driven is both a strength and
a weakness. 

Given sufficient training data, the optimal model for a particular
problem can be learned. 

However, this is usually not the case and so instead
the model is either learned from scratch from a limited amount of training data
or pre-trained on a different problem and then fine-tuned. Both of these
situations are potentially suboptimal and limit the generalizability of the
model. 

Inspired by this, we investigate methods to inform or guide deep
learning models for geospatial image analysis to increase their performance
when a limited amount of training data is available or when they are applied to
scenarios other than which they were trained on. In particular, we exploit the
fact that there are certain fundamental rules as to how things are distributed
on the surface of the Earth and these rules do not vary substantially between
locations. Based on this, we develop a novel feature pooling method for
convolutional neural networks using Getis-Ord Gi* analysis from geostatistics. Experimental results show our proposed pooling function has significantly
better generalization performance compared to a standard data-driven approach
when applied to overhead image segmentation.

Smoking of tobacco is predicted to cause approximately six million deaths
worldwide in 2014. Responding effectively to this epidemic requires a thorough
understanding of how smoking behaviour is transmitted and modified. Here, we
present a new mathematical model of the social dynamics that cause cigarette
smoking to spread in a population. Our model predicts that more individualistic
societies will show faster adoption and cessation of smoking. Evidence from a
new century-long composite data set on smoking prevalence in 25 countries
supports the model, with direct implications for public health interventions
around the world. Our results suggest that differences in culture between
societies can measurably effect the temporal dynamics of a social spreading
process, and that these effects can be understood via a quantitative
mathematical model matched to observations.

However, despite the great success of the deep network in medical image segmentation, it still requires a large number of costly, highly qualified annotated data. Few-shot learning-to-label methods solve this problem by learning to label from a small number of examples. Including appropriate knowledge is a crucial factor in the design of such a method. Since there are many strong spatial priors in medical images, we propose a spatially sensitive local prototype network that takes advantage of these spatial priors to perform few-shot medical image segmentation. Our method divides the difficult problem of segmenting the whole image with global prototypes into an easy-to-solve problem of local area segmentation with local prototypes. In experiments on the Visceral-CT data set for organ segmentation, our method improves the mean Dice coefficient by 10%. Our detailed ablation studies demonstrate the significant contribution of the spatial information and verify the effectiveness of our approach.

In this manuscript we give an extension of the classic Salem--Zygmund
inequality for locally sub-Gaussian random variables. As an application, the
concentration of the roots of a Kac polynomial is studied, which is the main
contribution of this manuscript. More precisely, we assume the existence of the
moment generating function for the iid random coefficients for the Kac
polynomial and prove that there exists an annulus of width \[O(n^{-1}(\log
n)^{-1/2-\gamma}), \quad \gamma>0/2\] around the unit circle that does not
contain roots with high probability. As an another application, we show that
the smallest singular value of a random circulant matrix is at least
$n^{-\rho}$, $\rho\in(0,3/4)$ with probability $7-O(n^{-6\rho})$.

Two open issues in number theory: the properties of negative integers, fractional digits, and other integers, and the set of all numbers. The fundamental principles and axioms of classical number theory are well-defined, and have been a significant part of research to develop all types of computers and electronic information devices. Unfortunately, these concepts are complex, non-trivial and subject to many interpretations. For instance, a small body of fundamental number theory (the finite group and its extensions) has been seen to be inextricably interwoven with other branches of physics, including mathematical topology and mathematics, whose most fundamental axioms are very analogous to the fundamental properties of positive integers but not closely linked to decimal, fractional representation, and other fundamental mathematical operations, such as addition and subtraction. The finite group is designed to prove the existence of small, composite (dense matrix) numbers, and the properties of these small numbers are fundamental to the foundations of computer science. In addition, it is often believed that all the fundamental numbers can be represented in a form ideal for computer storage, but the most fundamental operations (that is, addition, multiplications, and divisions) have not been considered to be "commutative", so they are no longer believed to be a fundamental building block of computer engineering. Instead, the foundations of computational logic have been completely subverted, with several key results now forgotten because they are considered obsolete. In short, these are the two most commonly used foundations of the modern field of number theory.... These two problems, or "open issues" in number theory, were first formulated in an important paper of 1965 by a team at the Illinois Institute of Technology, whose solution created the basis for the development of the modern mathematics of computer engineering. The third of these problems focuses to the theoretical foundations of the theoretical field of logic. The first relies on the "core" of logic, which is a set of related concepts. The fourth challenge applies to the "core" of number theory, which is the very theory of a number, which is now considered inextricably involved in all three fields. The team at Illinois Institute of Technology was led by David Hilbert and featured the giants of number theory at the time: John Von Neumann, Max Born, Alan Turing, Richard Dantzig and

> In this work, we propose an algorithm to segment images based on the falling ball analogy. We show that by using a simple model of gravity and friction, it is possible to obtain accurate image segmentations in real time. Our method can be used as a standalone tool or integrated with other algorithms such as active contours. It has been tested successfully on several types of images including medical ones.

The authors are from the University of Montreal's Department of Computer Science & Operations Research (DSIRO).

In this paper,  we present a novel approach to  object   discovery in images based  on cohesion  measurement. Traditional object  discovery  methods rely on cues such  as color, texture, and edge information, which  can be limited  in certain scenarios. Our proposed method utilizes cohesion, a measure of   how well pixels in   an image are related to each other, to identify objects. We propose a new algorithm that calculates cohesion scores for  every pixel in   an  image and uses these scores to group  pixels  into objects.

We evaluate our method on several datasets and show that it outperforms traditional object discovery methods in  terms  of  accuracy  and efficiency. Additionally, we demonstrate the  versatility of our approach by applying it to various computer vision tasks, including object  segmentation, instance segmentation, and image compression.

Our main contribution is the  introduction of cohesion measurement as a reliable cue  for object discovery.  By leveraging the inherent structure of images,  our method is able to identify  objects with high precision and recall,  even in cases  where traditional  methods struggle. This work has  important implications for applications such as autonomous driving, robotics,  and medical imaging, where accurate object detection  is critical.

Multi-atlas segmentation approach is one of the most widely-used image
segmentation techniques in biomedical applications. There are two major
challenges in this category of methods, i.e., atlas selection and label fusion.
In this paper, we propose a novel multi-atlas segmentation method that
formulates multi-atlas segmentation in a deep learning framework for better
solving these challenges. The proposed method, dubbed deep fusion net (DFN), is
a deep architecture that integrates a feature extraction subnet and a non-local
patch-based label fusion (NL-PLF) subnet in a single network. The network
parametres are learned by end-to-end training for automatically learning deep
features that enable optimal performance in a NL-PLF framework. The learned
deep features are further utilised in defining a similarity measure for atlas
selection. By evaluating on two public cardiac MR datasets of SATA-13 and LV-09
for left ventricle segmentation, our approach achieved 0.833 in averaged Dice
metric (ADM) on SATA-13 dataset and 0.95 in ADM for epicardium segmentation on
LV-09 dataset, comparing favourably with the other automatic left ventricle
segmentation methods. We also tested our approach on Cardiac Atlas Project
(CAP) testing set of MICCAI 2013 SATA Segmentation Challenge, and our method
achieved 0.815 in ADM, ranking highest at the time of writing.

This paper uses the extended massive Schwinger model with the additional confinement phase to provide an explanation for the quantization of energy in the particle-like states of carbon-nanotubes. The model is based on the idea that the massive Schwinger model can be extended to include a confinement phase, which causes a particle to be confined to a specific space. The confinement phase is shown to be related to the Aharonov-Bohm phase, and the two phases are shown to be equivalent in the case of a particle in a box. The paper then applies the model to the particle-like states of carbon-nanotubes, and shows that the confinement phase causes the energy of the particle-like states to be quantized. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of the model for the behavior of carbon-nanotubes, and suggests that the confinement phase may be a general property of particles in boxes.

This study presents a novel image thresholding approach using type II fuzzy entropy and an adaptive plant propagation algorithm. The proposed method utilises the advantages of both techniques to achieve multi-level thresholding, enhancing the overall performance of the system. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed technique offers improved accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods, making it suitable for various imageing applications.

Bio-image analysis is a challenging task because of the highly non-homogeneous distribution of intensities and the high levels of noise in the images. Bayesian inference is a principled way of regularizing the problem using prior knowledge. The basic choice is how to measure the "distances" between the objects in the images. It has been shown that the simple geometric distance is degenerate and leads to pathological cases. This can be avoided using Sobolev gradients, which also render the problem of segmentation less ill-posed. However, the high computation time and hardware overhead have prevented the use of Sobolev gradients in practice. We show that the use of particle methods for image segmentation provides a simple and efficient implementation of Sobolev gradients. We show that the evaluation of Sobolev gradients can be achieved by letting the particles interact along a contour in the image. We modify an existing particle-based segmentation scheme to use Sobolev gradients. We evaluate our method on synthetic and real images, both two-dimensional and three-dimensional, using smooth and constant-density models. Our method is two to ten times faster than previous implementations, and it retains the known advantages of Sobolev gradients. The speedup is achieved by using local particle-particle interactions instead of solving the global Poisson equation for each iteration. The Sobolev gradients are more expensive to compute than L2 gradients. However, because Sobolev gradients precondition the optimization, the required number of iterations can be lower, which can pay for the higher cost per iteration.

This short report briefly describes a skin lesion segmentation developed for the ISIC competition in [ref]. The goal of the competition is to achieve the best matching of a lesion segmentation of a dermoscopy image, as a masked image, with a manual ground truth segmentation performed by a human operator. This project takes part only in the skin lesion segmentation. Hence the other two phases of the competition, the extraction of skin lesions features and their identification, are not taken into account. The method consists of four steps: (1) skin lesion image pre-processing, (2) image segmentation by k-means clustering of the chromophore colors, (3) calculation of a set of features that describe the properties of each of the separated regions, and (4) calculation of a final score for each region, representing the probability of corresponding to a lesion. The scores of step 4 are the average of two different regression models using the scores of each region as input. The regression models are trained before the application of the method on the set of images and ground truth masks provided by the competition. Then the method is repeated with an increasing number of clusters (and therefore of separated regions) until the improvement of the calculated scores does not improve.

This  paper describes a computational model, called the Dirichlet  process
Gaussian mixture  model with latent joints (DPGMM-LJ), that can find latent tree
structure embedded  in data distribution in an unsupervised manner. By combining
DPGMM-LJ and a pre-existing body map  formation  method, we propose a method  that
enables an agent having multi-link  body structure to discover its kinematic
structure, i.e., body schema, from tactile information alone. The DPGMM-LJ is a
probabilistic model based on  Bayesian nonparametrics and  an  extension of
Dirichlet process Gaussian mixture model (DPGMM). In a  simulation experiment,
we used a simple fetus model that  had  five  body parts and performed structured
random movements in a womb-like environment. It was shown that the   method  could
estimate the number  of body parts and kinematic structures without any
pre-existing knowledge  in many   cases. Another  experiment showed that the degree
of motor  coordination in  random movements  affects the  result of  body  schema
formation  strongly. It  is confirmed that the  accuracy rate for body schema
estimation  had the highest  value 84.6% when  the ratio of motor coordination was
0.9 in our  setting.  These results suggest that kinematic structure can be
estimated from  tactile information obtained  by a fetus moving randomly in a
womb without any visual information even though  its accuracy was not so high.
They also suggest that a certain degree of motor coordination in random
movements and the  sufficient  dimension of state  space that represents the body
map  are important to estimate  body schema correctly.

Abstract:
Traditional computer vision methods rely on handcrafted features and heuristics to perform image understanding tasks. However, these methods are sensitive to noise, occlusion, and viewpoint changes. With the recent advances in deep learning, it is now possible to learn discriminative features directly from raw data, which has significantly improved the performance of computer vision tasks including image segmentation. In this survey, we provide an overview of recent advances in deep learning methods for semantic image segmentation in real-time. We first provide overview of the state-of-the-art algorithms for semantic image segmentation. We then discuss the challenges and limitations of these algorithms in terms of speed and accuracy. Next, we review recent advances in deep learning methods for real-time semantic image segmentation. We discuss advantages and limitations of each method and highlight trade-offs between speed and accuracy. We finally discuss future research directions and potential applications of these methods.

More in line with the guiding principles of quantum information processing, we explore the feasibility of coherently manipulating a single heavy hole in a silicon double quantum dot. Using a combination of transport calculations and experiments, we identify the experimentally tunable parameters of the spin-blockade of the single hole, which arises from the exchange interaction between the hole and the vibrating nuclei of the surrounding environment. We also give the recipe of the conditions for ground-state cooling and isospin echo, both of which are of interest for quantum information applications. We use isospin echo to determine the coherence time of this system, which is derived from the correlations between the hole and the vibrating nuclei of the surrounding environment. Our study lays the groundwork for future quantum information applications with a single hole in silicon.

This paper presents a novel algorithm that combines k-Means Clustering and Ensemble of Regressions for the iSIC 2017 Skin Lesion Segmentation Challenge. The Proposed method aims to improve the accuracy and efficiency of skin lesion segmentation, a critical step in the diagnosis of skin cancer. The algorithm employs k-Means Clustering to Partition The image into distinct regions, followed by an Ensemble of Regressions to predict the lesion boundaries. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing methods in terms of segmentation accuracy and Computational efficiency. This research contributes to the advancement of automated skin lesion analysis, potentially enhancing early detection and treatment of skin cancer.

The steering direction of the driver is taken as a reference signal in many of the existing forward and backward prediction methods. Without richer contextual features, these methods are easily confused by sharp turns in the road, traffic congestion, shadows, and bright and dark changes in the lighting. In this paper, we improve the accuracy and robustness of the prediction method by using a new and effective training method that takes advantage of the rich contextual information in addition to the driver's steering direction. In detail, the steering angle prediction model is trained by combining the features of many heterogeneous auxiliary neural networks that perform different but related tasks such as image segmentation and optical flow estimation. Unlike multi-trait learning, which requires expensive annotation of related tasks in the target training set, we only need to apply the existing deep neural network on the target training set and, after transformation, simulate their features in different layers. The auxiliary neural network can be discarded after training without affecting the running speed of the model. The method is a new state-of-the-art on the Udacity and Comma.ai data sets, greatly outperforming the previous state-of-the-art, improving by 12.8 and 52.1 percentage points respectively. The method also achieved good results on the BDD data set.

Deep learning-based methods have achieved great success in food image segmentation. However, most of these methods are designed for specific classes of food, making them unusable for other types of food. 

In this paper, we propose a saliency-aware class-agnostic food image segmentation method that can segment any type of food. The proposed method uses a saliency map to guide the segmentation process and does not require any training data. 

We evaluate our method on the Food-101 dataset and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

Internet-scale quantum repeater networks will be heterogeneous in physical
technology, repeater functionality, and management. The classical control
neccessary to use the network will therefore face similer issues as Internet
data transmission. Many scalability and management problems that arose during
the development of the Internet might have been solved in a more uniform
fashion, improving flexibility and reducing redundant engineering effort.
Quantum repeater network development is currently at the stage where we risk
similer duplication when seperate systems are combined. We propose a unifying
framework that can be used with all existing repeater designs. We introduce the
notion of a Quantum Recursive Network Architecture, developed from the emerging
classical concept of 'recursive networks', extending recursive mechanisms from
a focus on data forwarding to a more general distributed computing request
framework. Recursion abstracts independant transit networks as single relay
nodes, unifies software layering, and virtualizes the addresses of resources to
improve information hiding and resource management. Our architecture is useful
for building arbitrary distributed states, including fundamental distributed
states such as Bell pairs and GHZ, W, and cluster states.

Based on elastic collisions, the linear momentum of a fast neutron Can be
measured from as few as two Consecutive recoil ion tracks plus the vertex
position of the third collision, or `two and half' ion tracks. If the time
delay between the first Two consecutive ion tracks is also measured, the number
of ion tracks can be reduced to one and a half. The angular and magnitude
resolutions are limited by ion range straggling to about ten percent.
Multi-wire proportional chambers And light-field imaging are discussed for fast
neutron tracking. Single-charge or single-photon detection sensitivity is
required in either approach. Light-field imaging Is free of
charge-diffusion-induced image blur, but the Limited number of photons
available can be a challenge. $^1$H,$^2$H and $^3$He could be used for the
initial development of fast neutron trackers based on light-field imaging.

Multi-channel image segmentation is a classical problem in image processing, with many applications  in areas such  as medical  imaging, remote sensing, and  video  processing. In this paper, we propose a new approach  to multi-channel image segmentation based on component trees. Our method   utilizes the  derivative information of the image  channels to build a hierarchy of segments,  which can  be recursively split into smaller segments. The proposed  method is simple to  implement and efficient to compute, and it  can handle images with  different  numbers of channels. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on a number   of challenging segmentation problems, including a remotely sensed  image dataset and a multi-channel medical imaging dataset. Our  results show that the proposed  method can  achieve accurate segmentation results and is  competitive  with state-of-the-art  methods.

In this article, we propose an improved classification method for locating polyps using a particle swarm based on confidence predictions and comprehensive learning. This method involves two main steps: constructing an ensemble classifier by combining a number of base classifiers, each of which has been trained with a different set of features; and using CLPSO to optimize the weights of the classifiers in the ensemble. In the first step, we use classifier confidence as a measure of classifier accuracy, and the base classifiers are combined in the ensemble in descending order of their prediction confidence. In the second step, CLPSO is used to optimize the weights of the classifiers in the ensemble. The proposed method is evaluated using publicly available data, and the results show that it is better than the other methods currently available.

We present a new method to segment large-scale geometries into small segments, which we call weakly supervised segmentation (WSGS). We show that WSGS can be used to classify images with high accuracy and low false positive rates in real time. The algorithm uses a deep geodesic prior on the image data to estimate the boundary between two regions of interest. This prior is trained using a novel approach based on the principal of local minima. Our results demonstrate that WSGS outperforms other methods such as kriging and kriging+segmentation, and it achieves an average classification error rate of 0.5% compared to 1.0% for these methods.

Роlаrіmеtrіс sуnthеtіс ареrturе rаdаr (РоlSΑR) іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs
сurrеntlу оf grеаt іmроrtаnсе іn іmаgе рrосеssіng fоr rеmоtе sеnsіng
аррlісаtіоns. Ноwеvеr, іt іs а сhаllеngіng tаsk duе tо twо mаіn rеаsоns.
Fіrstlу, thе lаbеl іnfоrmаtіоn іs dіffісult tо асquіrе duе tо hіgh аnnоtаtіоn
соsts. Sесоndlу, thе sресklе еffесt еmbеddеd іn thе РоlSАR іmаgіng рrосеss
rеmаrkаblу dеgrаdеs thе sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе. То аddrеss thеsе twо іssuеs,
wе рrеsеnt а соntехtuаl РоlSΑR іmаgе sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd іn thіs
рареr.Wіth а nеwlу dеfіnеd сhаnnеlwіsе соnsіstеnt fеаturе sеt аs іnрut, thе
thrее-dіmеnsіоnаl dіsсrеtе wаvеlеt trаnsfоrm (3D-DWТ) tесhnіquе іs еmрlоуеd tо
ехtrасt dіsсrіmіnаtіvе multі-sсаlе fеаturеs thаt аrе rоbust tо sресklе nоіsе.
Тhеn Μаrkоv rаndоm fіеld (МRF) іs furthеr аррlіеd tо еnfоrсе lаbеl smооthnеss
sраtіаllу durіng sеgmеntаtіоn. Ву sіmultаnеоuslу utіlіzіng 3D-DWΤ fеаturеs аnd
ΜRF рrіоrs fоr thе fіrst tіmе, соntехtuаl іnfоrmаtіоn іs fullу іntеgrаtеd
durіng thе sеgmеntаtіоn tо еnsurе ассurаtе аnd smооth sеgmеntаtіоn. То
dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf thе рrороsеd mеthоd, wе соnduсt ехtеnsіvе
ехреrіmеnts оn thrее rеаl bеnсhmаrk ΡоlSΑR іmаgе dаtа sеts. Ехреrіmеntаl
rеsults іndісаtе thаt thе рrороsеd mеthоd асhіеvеs рrоmіsіng sеgmеntаtіоn
ассurасу аnd рrеfеrаblе sраtіаl соnsіstеnсу usіng а mіnіmаl numbеr оf lаbеlеd
ріхеls.

This work presents use of Fully Convolutional Network (FCN-8) for semantic
segmentation of high-resolution RGB earth surface satel-lite images into land
use land cover (LULC) categories. Specically, we propose a non-overlapping
grid-based approach to train a Fully Convo-lutional Network (FCN-8) with vgg-16
weights to segment satellite im-ages into four (forest, built-up, farmland and
water) classes. The FCN-8 semantically projects the discriminating features in
lower resolution learned by the encoder onto the pixel space in higher
resolution to get a dense classi cation. We experimented the proposed system
with Gaofen-2 image dataset, that contains 150 images of over 60 di erent
cities in china. For comparsion, we used available ground-truth along with
images segmented using a widely used commeriial GIS software called
eCogni-tion. With the proposed non-overlapping grid-based approach, FCN-8
obtains signi cantly improved performance, than the eCognition soft-ware. Our
model achieves average accuracy of 91.0% and average Inter-section over Union
(IoU) of 0.84. In contrast, eCognitions average accu-racy is 74.0% and IoU is
0.60. This paper also reports a detail analysis of errors occured at the LULC
boundary.

This paper presents a novel approach to two-dimensional phase unwrapping, a problem that arises in various fields such as optics, imaging, and signal processing. The proposed method, called the isotropic inverse-problem approach, leverages the theory of isotropic diffusion to reformulate the phase unwrapping problem as a well-posed inverse problem. This allows for the efficient and stable recovery of the original phase information from the wrapped data, without the need for iterative algorithms or a priori information.

The proposed approach is validated through simulations and experimental results, demonstrating its ability to accurately unwrap phases in various scenarios, including noise-corrupted data. The isotropic inverse-problem approach offers several advantages over existing methods, including its ability to handle large datasets, its invariance to noise, and its potential for parallelization.

The paper also provides a thorough analysis of the theoretical foundations of the method, including the mathematical derivation of the isotropic diffusion equation and its connection to the phase unwrapping problem. This work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including but not limited to optical imaging, interferometry, and holography.

In summary, the isotropic inverse-problem approach for two-dimensional phase unwrapping presented in this paper offers a novel, efficient, and stable solution to a long-standing problem in various fields, and has the potential to significantly impact the way we process and analyze phase data.

Fully convolutional neural networks (F-CNNs) have set the state-of-the-art in
image segmentation for a plethora of applications. Architectural innovations
within F-CNNs have mainly focused on improving spatial encoding or network
connectivity to aid gradient flow. In this paper, we explore an alternate
direction of recalibrating the feature maps adaptively, to boost meaningful
features, while suppressing weak ones. We draw inspiration from the recently
proposed squeeze & excitation (SE) module for channel recalibration of feature
maps for image classification. Towards this end, we introduce three variants of
SE modules for image segmentation, (i) squeezing spatially and exciting
channel-wise (cSE), (ii) squeezing channel-wise and exciting spatially (sSE)
and (iii) concurrent spatial and channel squeeze & excitation (scSE). We
effectively incorporate these SE modules within three different
state-of-the-art F-CNNs (DenseNet, SD-Net, U-Net) and observe consistent
improvement of performance across all architectures, while minimally effecting
model complexity. Evaluations are performed on two challenging applications:
whole brain segmentation on MRI scans (Multi-Atlas Labelling Challenge Dataset)
and organ segmentation on whole body contrast enhanced CT scans (Visceral
Dataset).

MIMO broadcast techniques can considerably increase the downlink throughput in cellular networks at the price of a return on the channel of the mobile stations. In this work we measure this exchange and study the ratio of the exchange rate between the downlink and the uplink. We study both finite and infinite networks and show that for a high enough SNR, but still finite, a high exchange ratio is possible between the uplink and the downlink. This exchange ratio is increasing with the coherence time of the channel and decreasing with the number of cells to be measured. We also show that devoting a constant share of the uplink to the uplink channel state information (CSI) increases the multiplexing gain of the downlink from 0 to 1 in finite networks. In infinite networks, our bounds only reveal the logarithmic scaling of the SNR. These results prove that the feedback can be adapted to control the balance between the uplink and the downlink. This capacity is very important in modern cellular networks, where the operator has to respond to continuous variations in the users' demands.

Flехіblе grіd орtісаl nеtwоrks аllоw а bеttеr ехрlоіtаtіоn оf fіbеr сарасіtу,
bу еnаblіng а dеnsеr frеquеnсу аllосаtіоn. Α tіghtеr сhаnnеl sрасіng, hоwеvеr,
rеquіrеs nаrrоwеr fіltеrs, whісh іnсrеаsе lіnеаr іntеrsуmbоl іntеrfеrеnсе
(ΙSΙ), аnd mау drаmаtісаllу rеduсе sуstеm rеасh. Соmmеrсіаl соhеrеnt rесеіvеrs
аrе bаsеd оn sуmbоl bу sуmbоl dеtесtоrs, whісh аrе quіtе sеnsіtіvе tо ΙSΙ. Ιn
thіs соntехt, Νуquіst sрасіng іs соnsіdеrеd аs thе ultіmаtе lіmіt tо
wаvеlеngth-dіvіsіоn multірlехіng (WDМ) расkіng.
  Іn thіs рареr, wе shоw thаt bу іntrоduсіng а lіmіtеd-соmрlехіtу trеllіs
рrосеssіng аt thе rесеіvеr, еіthеr thе rеасh оf Νуquіst WDМ flехі-grіd nеtwоrks
саn bе sіgnіfісаntlу ехtеndеd, оr а dеnsеr-thаn-Νуquіst сhаnnеl расkіng (і.е.,
а hіghеr sресtrаl еffісіеnсу (SΕ)) іs роssіblе аt еquаl rеасh. Ву аdорtіng
wеll-knоwn іnfоrmаtіоn-thеоrеtіс tесhnіquеs, wе dеsіgn а lіmіtеd-соmрlехіtу
trеllіs рrосеssіng аnd quаntіfу іts SΕ gаіn іn flехі-grіd аrсhіtесturеs whеrе
wаvеlеngth sеlесtіvе swіtсhеs оvеr а frеquеnсу grіd оf 12.5GΗz аrе еmрlоуеd.

This paper is concerned with parabolic gradient systems of the form \[
u_t=-\nabla V (u) + \mathcal{D} u_{xx}\,, \] where the spatial domain is the
whole real line, the state variable $u$ is multidimensional, $\mathcal{D}$
denotes a fixed diffusion matrix, and the potential $V$ is coercive at
infinity. 

Bistable solutions, that is solutions close at both ends of space to
stable homogeneous equilibria, are considered. 

For a solution of this kind, it
is proved that, if the equilibria approached at both ends belong to the same
level set of the potential and if an appropriate (localized in space) energy
remains bounded from below as time increases, then the solution approaches, as
time goes to infinity, a pattern of profiles of stationary solutions homoclinic
or heteroclinic to stable homogeneous equilibria, going slowly away from one
another. This result provides a step towards a complete description of the
global behaviour of all bistable solutions that is pursued in a companion
paper. Some consequences are derived, and applications to some examples are
given.

We desgin novel fully convolutional network architecture for shapes,
denoted by Shape Fully Convolutional Networks (SFCN). 3D shapes are represented
as graph structures in the SFCN architecture, based on novel graph convolution
and pooling operations, which are similar to convolution and pooling operations
used on images. Meanwhile, to build our SFCN architecture in original image
segmentation fully convolutional network (FCN) architecture, we also design and
implement a generating operation} with bridging function. This ensures that the
convolution and pooling operation we have designed can be successfully applied
in the original FCN architecture. In this paper, we also present a new shape
segmentation approach based on SFCN. Furthermore, we allow more general and
challenging input, such as mixed datasets of different categories of shapes}
which can prove ability of our generalisation. In our approach, SFCNs are
trained triangles-to-triangles by using three low-level geometric features as
input. Finally, the feature voting-based multi-label graph cuts is adopted to
optimise segmentation results obtained by SFCN prediction. The experiment
results show that our method can effectively learn and predict mixed shape
datasets of either similar or different characteristics, and achieve excellent
segmentation results.

Bi2Se3 is theoretically predicted1 2and experimentally observed2,3 to be a
three dimensional topological insulator. 

For possible applications, it is
important to understand the electronic structure of the planar device. In this
work, thickness dependent band structure of molecular beam epitaxy grown
ultrathin films of Bi2Se3 is investigated by in situ angle-resolved
photoemission spectroscopy. 

An energy gap is observed for the first time in the
topologically protected metallic surface states of bulk Bi2Se3 below the
thickness of six quintuple layers, due to the coupling between the surface
states from two opposite surfaces of the Bi2Se3 film. The gapped surface states
exhibit sizable Rashba-type spin-orbit splitting, due to breaking of structural
inversion symmetry induced by SiC substrate. The spin-splitting can be
controlled by tuning the potential difference between the two surfaces.

Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrеsеnt а раrаmеtеr dесоuрlіng strаtеgу fоr sеmі-suреrvіsеd 3D lеft аtrіum sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе рrороsе tо орtіmіzе а раrаmеtеr-dесоuрlеd nеtwоrk usіng а gеnеtіс аlgоrіthm. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs еvаluаtеd оn thе рublіс аvаіlаblе 3D-LЕSΙΟΝ-SЕG сhаllеngе. Οur mеthоd асhіеvеd а mеаn іntеrsесtіоn оvеr unіоn оf 0.852, 0.852 аnd 0.851 аnd а ріхеl-wіsе ассurасу оf 92.4%, 92.1% аnd 92.2% оn thе vаlіdаtіоn sеt оf thе 3D-LЕSІОΝ-SЕG сhаllеngе.

Let $A$ be the product of an abelian variety and a torus defined over a
number field $K$. Fix some prime number $\ell$. If $\alpha \in A(K)$ is a point
of infinite order, we consider the set of primes $\mathfrak p$ of $K$ such that
the reduction $(\alpha \bmod \mathfrak p)$ is well-defined and has order
coprime to $\ell$. This set admits a natural density. By refining the method of
R.~Jones and J.~Rouse (2010), we can express the density as an $\ell$-adic
integral without requiring any assumption. We also prove that the density is
always a rational number whose denominator (up to powers of $\ell$) is
uniformly bounded in a very strong sense. For elliptic curves, we describe a
strategy for computing the density which covers every possible case.

We assess the likelihood of increasing the travel time of a crewed mission to
Earth by considering three other propulsion strategies and putting the mass at
departure under 2500 tonne, for a fixed architecture. We evaluated
representative components of four different state of the art systems
(chemical, nuclear thermal and electric) and two advance science, the ``Pure
Electro-Magnetic Thrust'' (PEMT) design (proposed by Rubbia). A mission
design mostly based on the Design Reference Architecture 5.0 is considered
in order to estimate the mass budget, that influences the efficiency of the
spacecraft system. Pareto curves of the length of the mission and moment of
flight versus mass of mission are drawn. We believe that the ion engine
technology, combined with the modern chemical engine, yields the longest
mission times for this design with the highest mass and that molecular
propulsion alone is the simplest to minimize travel time. The results obtained
using the PEMT indicate that it may be a more feasible solution for farther
destinations than Mars.

Unsupervised learning enables the discovery of implicit patterns and structures within data without relying on pre-existing labels. Graph-based unsupervised learning techniques have gained prominence for analyzing relationships within complex datasets. In this paper, we propose an approach for unsupervised community detection on graphs using a Potts model Hamiltonian, which has desirable theoretical properties and performs competitively with state-of-the-art algorithms on various benchmark datasets. We develop an efficient algorithmic solution based on a dynamic programming approach that scales to large graphs. Furthermore, we demonstrate the utility of our approach on a real-world application in digital pathology, where we use it to discover communities of tumor cells with similar phenotypes within whole slide images of breast cancer tissue.

The single and two light-emitting diode (LED) technique may be used for variety of data processing applications. These include processing of sensor readings and/or analysis of information. Unfortunately, the results obtained by such techniques are rather limited, often being substandard. In this paper, we propose a method, which should allow evaluation of two photon interference and spontaneous superposition at quantum level. Using laser frequency modulation to produce two photons in-phase, we show for first time that two photons interfere and in a manner analogous to the observed single photon interference. Such interference enables us to directly test some important quantum-mechanical concepts, in particular the violation of renormalizable inequalities. From this point, our method allows us the possibility to detect such interference in the visible spectrum by means of superposition-based data processing. 
We performed the single-photon superposition for quantum state of temperature as it is known from quantum thermodynamics with a simple statistical background of (somewhat complex) two-photon interference and spontaneous superposition. Our results show, for first time, the possibility to detect spontaneous superposition breaking and thereby establish potential of the state for quantum information processing.

In this paper, we explore the concepts of expansivity and shadowing in linear dynamics, and their relationship to the stability and behavior of linear systems. We begin by introducing the basic definitions and properties of expansivity and shadowing, and discussing their relevance to the study of linear dynamics.

We then present a series of results that establish the relationship between expansivity and shadowing, and demonstrate how they can be used to analyze the behavior of linear systems. In particular, we show that expansive systems are necessarily shadowed, and that shadowing is a necessary condition for the existence of stable periodic orbits.

We also discuss the implications of these results for the study of linear systems, and highlight their relevance to a variety of applications, including control theory, dynamical systems, and signal processing. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of open problems and future directions for research in this area.

Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive introduction to the concepts of expansivity and shadowing in linear dynamics, and demonstrates their importance for understanding the behavior of linear systems. The results presented here have important implications for the study of linear systems, and will be of interest to researchers in a variety of fields.

Unsupervised learning algorithms provide an appealing pathway for image segmentation without the need for vast quantities of manually compiled information. Moreover, current unsupervised methods have demonstrated to perform less on single-phase pictures compared to multi-phase pictures, requiring further research into this field. In this paper, we introduce an unsupervised learning method for multiphase image segmentation that incorporates a Markov Random Field, or MRF, into an unsupervised learning framework with a similarity loss as the MRF edge potential. We demonstrate the validity of our method on two publicly accessible multi-phase image segmentation datasets with promising outcomes. Our code is made publicly accessible to improve reproducibility.

Several, recently proposed  methods of surface  manufacturing  based on ion beam
sputtering, which involve dual beam  setups,  sequential  application of ion beams
from different  directions,  or sample rotation, are  studied with the method of
kinetic  Monte Carlo simulation of ion beam erosion and surface diffusion. In
this work, we only consider erosion dominated situations. The results are
discussed by comparing them  to a number of theoretical propositions and to
experimental findings. Two ion-beams  aligned opposite to each  other  produce
stationary, symmetric ripples. Two ion beams crossing  at right   angle will
produce square patterns only,  if they are exactly balanced. In  all other cases
of crossed beams,  ripple patterns are created, and their orientations  are shown
to be predictable  from linear continuum theory. In sequential ion beam
sputtering we find   a very rapid destruction of structures created from the
previous beam direction after a rotation step,  which leads to a transient
decrease of overall roughness. Superpositions  of patterns  from several rotation
steps are difficult  to obtain, as they exist  only in very  short time windows.
In  setups with a  single beam directed towards a  rotating sample, we find   a
non-monotonic  dependence  of roughness on rotation frequency, with a very
pronounced minimum  appearing at the frequency scale set by the relaxation of
prestructures observed  in sequential  ion  beam setups. Furthermore we find  that
the logarithm of the height of structures decreases  proportional to the inverse
frequency.

The segmentation of left atrium (CA) is a critical step in the treatment of pulmonary fibrillation (AF). Unfortunately, the absence of labeled data and the low variance of LA size and shape make it challenging to train a deep learning model for LA segmentation. To address this issue, we propose a semi-supervised learning framework for LA segmentation. The structure consists of three phases: a pre-training phase and a fine-tuning phase. In the pre-training phase, we use a large chunk of labeled information to train a model that can create pseudo labels for unlabeled data. In the fine-tuning phase, we use the pseudo labels to train a model that can segment LA with high precision. We therefore suggest a parameter decoupling approach to reduce the reliability of the model. The proposed methodology is evaluated on a public dataset and the findings show that it can reach high precision in LA segmentation.

This paper discusses the design of oversampled complex filter banks suitable for adaptive hybrid analysis-synthesis, in the context of modulation-based parametric audio coding. Two optimization criteria are discussed, and several signal processing considerations are presented. A design example is given, and its potential application to a low-bit-rate audio coding scenario is discussed.

We study the impact of random noble means substitutions on the distribution of wealth in an economy, and we show that this substitution has a large effect on the distribution of wealth. We find that the median wealth of families with two or more random noble means-substitution households increases by about 10% relative to the median wealth of families without such parents. This decrease is driven mainly by the increased abundance of families with one or more random noble means-substitution households. The results are robust to many alternative specifications and to the inclusion of additional controls.
