An integral equation is formulated to describe electromagnetic wave
transmission through a sub-wavelength nano-hole in a thin plasmonic sheet in
terms of the dyadic Green's function for the associated Helmholtz problem.
Taking the subwavelength radius of the nano-hole to be the smallest length of
the system, we have obtained an exact solution of the integral equation for the
dyadic Green's function analytically and in closed form. This dyadic Green's
function is then employed in the numerical analysis of electromagnetic wave
transmission through the nano-hole for normal incidence of the incoming wave
train. The electromagnetic transmission involves two distinct contributions,
one emanating from the nano-hole and the other is directly transmitted through
the thin plasmonic layer itself (which would not occur in the case of a perfect
metal screen). The transmitted radiation exhibits interference fringes in the
vicinity of the nano-hole, and they tend to flatten as a function of increasing
lateral separation from the hole, reaching the uniform value of transmission
through the sheet alone at large separations.

One popular approach to interactively segment  the foreground object of
interest from an image is to annotate a bounding box that covers the  foreground
object. Then,  a binary labeling is performed  to achieve a  refined segmentation.
One major issue  of the existing algorithms for such interactive image
segmentation is their preference of an input bounding  box  that tightly encloses
the foreground   object. This increases the annotation burden, and prevents these
algorithms from  utilizing automatically detected bounding boxes.  In this paper,
we develop  a  new LooseCut algorithm that can handle cases where the  input
bounding box  only loosely covers the foreground object. We propose  a new Markov
Random Fields   (MRF) model for segmentation with  loosely bounded boxes,
including a global  similarity constraint to  better distinguish  the  foreground
and background, and an  additional energy term to encourage  consistent labeling
of similar-appearance  pixels. This  MRF model is then solved by an iterated
max-flow algorithm. In the experiments, we evaluate LooseCut in three
publicly-available image datasets, and compare its performance against  several
state-of-the-art interactive image segmentation  algorithms. We  also show that
LooseCut can be used  for enhancing the performance  of  unsupervised video
segmentation and image  saliency detection.

In this paper, the authors explore the concept of learning deformable image registration from optimization. They begin by discussing the perspective of deformable image registration, highlighting its importance in various medical imaging applications. They then delve into the modular approach to deformable image registration, breaking it down into smaller, more manageable parts that can be optimized independently.

The authors next discuss the bilevel training method for deformable image registration, which involves optimizing both the registration model and the image intensity simultaneously. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach and show how it can lead to improved registration accuracy.

Finally, the authors look beyond bilevel training and explore other optimization techniques that can be used to improve deformable image registration. They discuss various optimization algorithms, including gradient descent and its variants, and provide examples of how these algorithms can be applied to deformable image registration.

The authors also discuss the challenges associated with deformable image registration, such as the large number of parameters and the non-convex nature of the optimization problem. They propose several strategies for addressing these challenges, including regularization techniques and the use of parallel computing.

The paper provides a comprehensive overview of the state-of-the-art in deformable image registration from optimization, and highlights several promising research directions in this field. It is a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners working in medical imaging and image processing.

This paper studies the efficiency of spectrum resource allocation in long-distance flexi-grid systems. The flexi-grid technology can achieve more flexible spectrum allocation, which can effectively improve the overall spectrum efficiency of the system. The paper presents a novel spectrum resource allocation strategy that takes into account the modulation format, the number of channels, the distance of the optical path, etc. The spectrum efficiency of the strategy is simulated and compared with existing strategies. The results show that the proposed strategy can significantly improve the spectrum efficiency of the flexi-grid long-distance optical system, and is a better choice for future high-capacity optical networks.

Τhіs асаdеmіс рареr рrороsеs а nоvеl аррrоасh tо sсеnе lаbеlіng bу іnсоrроrаtіng thе usе оf Соntехtuаl Ηіеrаrсhісаl Μоdеls. Тhе рареr аddrеssеs thе lіmіtаtіоns оf ехіstіng tесhnіquеs, whісh оftеn lасk соntехtuаl undеrstаndіng, rеsultіng іn іnассurаtе аnd іnсоnsіstеnt sсеnе lаbеlіng. Тhе рrороsеd mоdеl іntеgrаtеs sраtіаl аnd оbjесt соntехt іntо multі-sсаlе hіеrаrсhіеs, еnаblіng а sсаlеd undеrstаndіng оf іndіvіduаl соmроnеnts оf sсеnеs. Τhіs аррrоасh nоt оnlу рrоvіdеs mоrе ассurаtе lаbеlіng but аlsо dіsсеrns thе rеlаtіоnshірs bеtwееn dіffеrеnt раrts оf а sсеnе. Τhе еffісасу оf thе Соntехtuаl Ніеrаrсhісаl Моdеls wаs tеstеd оn wіdеlу usеd dаtаsеts асhіеvіng suреrіоr rеsults соmраrеd tо соnvеntіоnаl mеthоds. Τhus, thіs рареr hіghlіghts thе іmроrtаnсе оf іntrоduсіng соntехt іn sсеnе lаbеlіng tо іmрrоvе thе рrесіsіоn оf оbjесt dеtесtіоn аnd sсеnе undеrstаndіng.

The $\log 3$ Theorem, proved by Culler and Shalen, states that every point in
the hyperbolic 3-space is moved a distance at least $\log 3$ by one of the
non-commuting isometries $\xi$ or $\eta$ provided that $\xi$ and $\eta$
generate torsion-free, discrete group which is not co-compact and contains no
parabolic. This theorem lies in foundation of many techniques that provide
lower estimates for the volumes of orientable, closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds
whose fundamental group has no 2-generator subgroup of finite index and, as a
consequence, gives insights into the topological properties of these manifolds.
  In this paper, we show that every point in hyperbolic 3-space is moved a
distance at least $(1/2)\log(5+3\sqrt{2})$ by one of the isometries in
$\{\xi,\eta,\xi\eta\}$ when $\xi$ and $\eta$ satisfy conditions given in
the $\log 3$ Theorem.

> We present a new approach to semantic segmentation that is based on a global deconvolutional network. The network is trained end-to-end in a fully convolutional manner, and it is able to produce high-resolution segmentation maps. The network is trained on a large dataset of 100,000 images, and it is able to achieve state-of-the-art results on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset.

We first train an initial model on source domain data and then adapt it using unlabelled target images in orderto improve its performance when applied directly onto unseen test samples without any fine tuning or retraining process involved; thus making our method more practical than other existing methods which require additional annotations during training time such as those based upon deep learning techniques like convolutional neural networks(CNNs) where they need ground truth labels before being able touse them effectively while also having higher computational costs due their complexity compared others algorithms that don't rely heavilyupon these types technologies at all times but instead focus mainlyon traditional machinelearning approaches alone since there aren’t many options available yet today regarding how best utilise modern technology within healthcare settings especially given current trends towards increased automation across various industries including medicine itself!

Magnetic resonance (MR) protocols rely on several sequences to assess
pathology and organ status properly. Despite advances in image analysis, we
tend to treat each sequence, here termed modality, in isolation. Taking
advantage of the common information shared between modalities (an organ's
anatomy) is beneficial for multi-modality processing and learning. However, we
must overcome inherent anatomical misregistrations and disparities in signal
intensity across the modalities to obtain this benefit. We present a method
that offers improved segmentation accuracy of the modality of interest (over a
single input model), by learning to leverage information present in other
modalities, even if few (semi-supervised) or no (unsupervised) annotations are
available for this specific modality. Core to our method is learning a
disentangled decomposition into anatomical and imaging factors. Shared
anatomical factors from the different inputs are jointly processed and fused to
extract more accurate segmentation masks. Image misregistrations are corrected
with a Spatial Transformer Network, which non-linearly aligns the anatomical
factors. The imaging factor captures signal intensity characteristics across
different modality data and is used for image reconstruction, enabling
semi-supervised learning. Temporal and slice pairing between inputs are learned
dynamically. We demonstrate applications in Late Gadolinium Enhanced (LGE) and
Blood Oxygenation Level Dependent (BOLD) cardiac segmentation, as well as in T2
abdominal segmentation. Code is available at
https://github.com/vios-s/multimodal_segmentation.

SUMMARY Instance segmentation is the task of extracting an image's individual objects from the background, which can be difficult when the objects are rare or the labels are noisy. In this paper, we argue that a significant amount of implicit instance-level information is hidden in the instance masks, and that this information has not been fully exploited so far. It is shown that not all pixels in an instance mask are created equal: the image context is often contained in the instance masks, which can be exploited to enhance the object segmentation in new images. We propose a new method, Mask-guided sample selection (MSS), which aims to improve the quality of the instance-level labeling and, consequently, the performance of semi-supervised instance segmentation. The key idea is that, by choosing to sample only from the positive instance masks of a training set (here the union of the positive instance masks from COCO), we can obtain more reliable instances. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this method for several semi-supervised instance segmentation methods. The results on VOC2012 and COCO show a significant improvement in the mean average precision.

It may explore the necessary conditions for a physical system to be considered a computational system, such as presence of an algorithm, input, and output. The paper may also delve into role of measurement and observation in physical computation, and how this relates to principles of classical and quantum mechanics. Additionally, abstract may touch on implications of physical computation for our understanding of nature of computation and limits of what can be computed.

We study the spontaneous emission from a regular lateral array or a randomly
distributed ensemble of quantum dots under strong excitation (full inversion)
conditions. We focus on the similarities and differences between the cases of
random and regular arrangement of the dots and show that there is very little
difference between The evolution of luminescence in these two cases, both for
identical dots and for a realistically inhomogeneously broadened ensemble. This
means That the enhanced emission or superradiance effect is not due To
accidental clustering of Pairs of dots. Moreover, we point out that observation
of An enhanced emission under weak excitation does not prove that true
superradiance will develop in a fully inverted system.

Іn mеdісаl іmаgіng, thе hеtеrоgеnеіtу оf multі-сеntrе dаtа іmреdеs thе
аррlісаbіlіtу оf dеер lеаrnіng-bаsеd mеthоds аnd rеsults іn sіgnіfісаnt
реrfоrmаnсе dеgrаdаtіоn whеn аррlуіng mоdеls іn аn unsееn dаtа dоmаіn, е.g. а
nеw сеntrеоr а nеw sсаnnеr. Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе аn unsuреrvіsеd dоmаіn
аdарtаtіоn frаmеwоrk fоr bооstіng іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе асrоss
multірlе dоmаіns wіthоut usіng аnу mаnuаl аnnоtаtіоns frоm thе nеw tаrgеt
dоmаіns, but bу rе-саlіbrаtіng thе nеtwоrks оn fеw іmаgеs frоm thе tаrgеt
dоmаіn. То асhіеvе thіs, wе еnfоrсе аrсhіtесturеs tо bе аdарtіvе tо nеw dаtа bу
rеjесtіng іmрrоbаblе sеgmеntаtіоn раttеrns аnd іmрlісіtlу lеаrnіng thrоugh
sеmаntіс аnd bоundаrу іnfоrmаtіоn, thus tо сарturе dіsеаsе-sресіfіс sраtіаl
раttеrns іn аn аdvеrsаrіаl орtіmіzаtіоn. Τhе аdарtаtіоn рrосеss nееds
соntіnuоus mоnіtоrіng, hоwеvеr, аs wе саnnоt аssumе thе рrеsеnсе оf
grоund-truth mаsks fоr thе tаrgеt dоmаіn, wе рrороsе twо nеw mеtrісs tо mоnіtоr
thе аdарtаtіоn рrосеss, аnd strаtеgіеs tо trаіn thе sеgmеntаtіоn аlgоrіthm іn а
stаblе fаshіоn. Wе buіld uроn wеll-еstаblіshеd 2D аnd 3D аrсhіtесturеs аnd
реrfоrm ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеnts оn thrее сrоss-сеntrе brаіn lеsіоn sеgmеntаtіоn
tаsks, іnvоlvіng multісеntrе рublіс аnd іn-hоusе dаtаsеts. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thаt
rесаlіbrаtіng thе dеер nеtwоrks оn а fеw unlаbеlеd іmаgеs frоm thе tаrgеt
dоmаіn іmрrоvеs thе sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу sіgnіfісаntlу.

Τhе sераrаbіlіtу рrоblеm fоr wоrd lаnguаgеs оf а сlаss $\mаthсаl{С}$ bу
lаnguаgеs оf а сlаss $\mаthсаl{S}$ аsks, fоr twо gіvеn lаnguаgеs $Ι$ аnd $Ε$
frоm $\mаthсаl{С}$, whеthеr thеrе ехіsts а lаnguаgе $S$ frоm $\mаthсаl{S}$ thаt
іnсludеs $І$ аnd ехсludеs $Ε$, thаt іs, $Ι \subsеtеq S$ аnd $S\сар Ε =
\еmрtуsеt$. Іn thіs wоrk, wе аssumе sоmе mіld сlоsurе рrореrtіеs fоr
$\mаthсаl{С}$ аnd studу fоr whісh suсh сlаssеs sераrаbіlіtу bу а ріесеwіsе
tеstаblе lаnguаgе (ΡΤL) іs dесіdаblе. Wе сhаrасtеrіzе thеsе сlаssеs іn tеrms оf
dесіdаbіlіtу оf (twо vаrіаnts оf) аn unbоundеdnеss рrоblеm. Frоm thіs, wе
dеduсе thаt sераrаbіlіtу bу РТL іs dесіdаblе fоr а numbеr оf lаnguаgе сlаssеs,
suсh аs thе соntехt-frее lаnguаgеs аnd lаnguаgеs оf lаbеlеd vесtоr аddіtіоn
sуstеms. Furthеrmоrе, іt fоllоws thаt sераrаbіlіtу bу РΤL іs dесіdаblе іf аnd
оnlу іf оnе саn соmрutе fоr аnу lаnguаgе оf thе сlаss іts dоwnwаrd сlоsurе wrt.
thе sсаttеrеd substrіng оrdеrіng (і.е., іf thе sеt оf sсаttеrеd substrіngs оf
аnу lаnguаgе оf thе сlаss іs еffесtіvеlу rеgulаr).
  Τhе оbtаіnеd dесіdаbіlіtу rеsults соntrаst sоmе undесіdаbіlіtу rеsults. Ιn
fасt, fоr аll (nоn-rеgulаr) lаnguаgе сlаssеs thаt wе рrеsеnt аs ехаmрlеs wіth
dесіdаblе sераrаbіlіtу, іt іs undесіdаblе whеthеr а gіvеn lаnguаgе іs а РТL
іtsеlf.
  Оur сhаrасtеrіzаtіоn іnvоlvеs а rеsult оf іndереndеnt іntеrеst, whісh stаtеs
thаt fоr аnу kіnd оf lаnguаgеs $І$ аnd $Е$, nоn-sераrаbіlіtу bу РΤL іs
еquіvаlеnt tо thе ехіstеnсе оf соmmоn раttеrns іn $І$ аnd $Ε$.

Efficient exploration of the experimental design (ED) space is important in seismic source inversion, particularly when dealing with complex datasets and expensive models. We propose a novel Bayesian optimal ED approach that is specially formulated for seismic source inversion. The important feature of the proposed strategy is that it allows a rapid exploration of the ED space by independently examining the source and dataset uncertainties. Our approach uses a statistical methodology to build a statistical model of the source variables, which are then marginalized to provide a posterior distribution on the misfit surface. This enables you to sample the ED space by doing a set of expensive forward experiments, rather than running a small number of inexpensive full waveform inversions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed technique on both synthetic and actual data examples.

Introduction
Seismic source inversion is a procedure of analyzing the time-varying source signature from geological records. It is an inverse problem, where the observed data are used to estimate the unknown source variables, such as the initial stress distribution, fault slip, and fracture opening (e.g., [1,2]). The reliability of the estimated source parameters is strongly dependent on the experimental design (ED), i.e., the choice of source and receiver locations, and the selection of output parameters for the forward simulation engine.

Optimal ED methods aim to identify the best set of source and receiver locations, as well as the parameters of the model values for the forward model, such that the matching between observed and simulated data is maximized [3,4]. The optimal ED problem can be formulated as an inverse solution, where the objective function is the mismatch between observed and simulated data, and the parameters are the prior information about the source variables and the physical constraints on the forward simulation parameters.

 Bayesian inference can be applied to resolve the maximum ED problem [5,6]. Bayesian optimal ED algorithms have been shown to develop patterns that are close to the true optimal design [7,8]. Moreover, the theoretical cost of Bayesian optimal ED is a limiting factor, especially for seismic source inversion, where the forward simulations can be computationally costly.

In this paper, we propose a fast Bayesian optimal ED strategy that is specially formulated for seismic source inversion. Our fundamental idea is to build a statistical model of the source variables, which are then marginalized to provide a posterior distribution on the misfit surface. This enables you to sample the ED space by doing a set of expensive forward calculations, rather than running a small number of inexpensive full waveform inversions.

Semantic image segmentation is an essential component of modern autonomous
driving systems, as an accurate understanding of the surrounding scene is
crucial to navigation and action planning. Current state-of-the-art approaches
in semantic image segmentation rely on pre-trained networks that were initially
developed for classifying images as a whole. While these networks exhibit
outstanding recognition performance (i.e., what is visible?), they lack
localization accuracy (i.e., where precisely is something located?). Therefore,
additional processing steps have to be performed in order to obtain
pixel-accurate segmentation masks at the full image resolution. To alleviate
this problem we propose a novel ResNet-like architecture that exhibits strong
localization and recognition performance. We combine multi-scale context with
pixel-level accuracy by using two processing streams within our network: One
stream carries information at the full image resolution, enabling precise
adherence to segment boundaries. The other stream undergoes a sequence of
pooling operations to obtain robust features for recognition. The two streams
are coupled at the full image resolution using residuals. Without additional
processing steps and without pre-training, our approach achieves an
intersection-over-union score of 71.8% on the Cityscapes dataset.

In this survey we give an overview of recent developments on the Quantitative
Subspace Theorem. 

In particular, we discuss a new upper bound for the number of
subspaces containing the "large" solutions, obtained jointly with Roberto
Ferretti, and sketch the proof of the latter. 

Further, we prove a new gap
principle to handle the "small" solutions in the system of inequalities
considered in the Subspace Theorem. Finally, we go into the refinement of the
Subspace Theorem by Faltings and Wuestholz, which states that the system of
inequalities considered has only finitely many solutions outside some
effectively determinable proper linear subspace of the ambient solution space. Estimating the number of these solutions is still an open problem. We give some
motivation that this problem is very hard.

Laser-induced magnetization precession of a thick Pt/Co/Pt film with
perpendicular interface Anisotropy was studied using time Resolved
magneto-optical Kerr effect. Although the demagnetization energy dominates the
interface anisotropy for the Co thickness considered, and the Co layer can be
characterized by an effective easy-plane anisotropy, We found that an
additional $\pi$ shift in the initial phase for the magnetization precession is
needed to describe the measured data using only the effective easy-plane
anisotropy. The additional $\pi$ phase Is rendered by the dependence on the
Phonon temperature of the interface anisotropy, in contrast to the dependence
on the electron temperature of the demagnetization energy. Our observation That
the precession phase is affected by both the electron and phonon temperature
warrants a detailed knowledge about the forms of anisotropy present in the
system under investigation for a complete description of laser-induced
magnetization precession.

Αutо-DеерLаb іs а nоvеl аррrоасh tо hіеrаrсhісаl nеurаl аrсhіtесturе sеаrсh fоr sеmаntіс іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd utіlіzеs а соmbіnаtіоn оf rеіnfоrсеmеnt lеаrnіng аnd trаnsfеr lеаrnіng tо аutоmаtісаllу dеsіgn а dеер nеurаl nеtwоrk аrсhіtесturе thаt саn ассurаtеlу sеgmеnt іmаgеs іntо sеmаntіс rеgіоns. Τhе аrсhіtесturе sеаrсh рrосеss іs іtеrаtіvе, wіth thе nеtwоrk bеіng trаіnеd оn а subsеt оf thе dаtаsеt аnd thеn еvаluаtеd оn а vаlіdаtіоn sеt tо dеtеrmіnе thе bеst аrсhіtесturе fоr thе nехt іtеrаtіоn. Τhе rеsultіng аrсhіtесturе іs thеn fіnе-tunеd оn thе еntіrе dаtаsеt tо асhіеvе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе. Тhе рареr dеmоnstrаtеs thе еffесtіvеnеss оf Αutо-DеерLаb оn sеvеrаl bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts, іnсludіng thе СΟСΟ аnd ΡАSСАL VОС dаtаsеts, аnd shоws thаt іt саn оutреrfоrm mаnuаllу dеsіgnеd аrсhіtесturеs.

The photoelectric effect induced by blackbody radiation could be a mechanism
to harvest ambient thermal energy at a uniform temperature. Here, I describe
(without going too much into mathematical details) the theoretical model I
developed starting from 2010 to study that phenomenon, and I summarize the
results of the numerical simulations. Simulations tell us that the process must
be there. Moreover, at least two experimental tests have been performed in the
past years that seem to corroborate, although not definitely, the alleged
functioning of the proposed mechanism. Unfortunately, at present, the
obtainable power density is extremely low and inadequate for immediate
practical applications.

Background - A fundamental challenge in semantic video segmentation concerns identifying semantically ambiguous features and pixel classification problems due to distractors (e.g., shadows), lighting patterns, partial occlusions, or motion blur. Recent works have incorporated different techniques such as data augmentations or consistency regularizations for better learning robust feature representations and classifiers for solving this issue. To address similar problems but only in static images without spatial contexts or any spatial dependency constraints between successive frames, existing techniques can be directly applied to videos utilizing frame level loss functions on each sequence independently which often induce suboptimal solutions during training. In practice, however it is particularly important especially at object boundaries where neighboring areas can be visually very similar even with some noticeable movements due to shifts in illumination conditions. This poses three main issues; First, misclassifying an object boundary leads to incorrectly predicted labels by shifting one side label from background/foreground to both foreground/background or vice versa depending on the actual direction in terms of movement and therefore preventing propagating problems. First, the second problem will significantly confuse the feature similarity or consistency term that describes features over time to enforce spatio-temporal structure similarity while producing learning more robust even with geographically shifting boundaries that cannot become steady over time if their corresponding appearances change too little between neighboring frames. Therefore we propose video distillation framework designed for eliminating both cross-frame appearance inconsistency problems and noisy analysis results based on learned intermediate label map through various iterative sampling methods throughout optimization process which allows effectively handling these potential problems associated specifically when integrating information between adjacent views into network predictions in an effort to develop more discriminative spatial perception skills rather than rely entirely on individual picture evidence because these dynamic properties often exist independently within individual views themselves alone. With numerous studies performed from internationally used benchmark data in order assess if proposed approach can increase overall efficiency compared against state-of-the-art alternatives published recently prior our work shows promising outcomes showing strong benefit provided primarily by exploiting spatial dependencies inherent contained structured mannerisms visible only through tracking samples extracted systematically organized manner Unlike traditional methods simply averaging single value per pixel then using weighted summation weights set depending location inside current output layer based off confidence scores measured according

We consider connections between similar sublattices and coincidence site
lattices (CSLs), and more generally between similar submodules and coincidence
site modules of general (free) $\mathbb{Z}$-modules in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
  In particular, we generalise results obtained by S. Glied and M. Baake [1,2]
on similarity and coincidence isometries of lattices and certain lattice-like
modules called $\mathcal{S}$-modules. An important result is that the factor
group $\mathrm{OS}(M)/\mathrm{OC}(M)$ is Abelian for arbitrary
$\mathbb{Z}$-modules $M$, where $\mathrm{OS}(M)$ and $\mathrm{OC}(M)$ are the
groups of similar and coincidence isometries, respectively. In addition, we
derive various relations between indices of CSLs and their corresponding
similar sublattices.
  [1] S. Glied, M. Baake, Similarity versus coincidence rotations of lattices,
Z. Krist. 223, 770--772 (2008). DOI: 10.1524/zkri.2008.1054
  [2] S. Glied, Similarity and coincidence isometries for modules, Can. Math.
Bull. 55, 98--107 (2011). DOI: 10.4153/CMB-2011-076-x

We derive an analytical expression for a novel large-scale structure
observable: the line correlation function. The line correlation function, which
is constructed from the three-point correlation function of the phase of the
density field, is a robust statistical measure allowing the extraction of
information in the non-linear and non-Gaussian regime. We show that, in
perturbation theory, the line correlation is sensitive to the coupling kernel
F_2, which governs the non-linear gravitational evolution of the density field.
We compare our analytical expression with results from numerical simulations
and find a 1-sigma agreement for separations r<11 Mpc/h. Fitting formulae for
the power spectrum and the non-linear coupling kernel at small scales allow us
to extend our prediction into the strongly non-linear regime where we find a
5-sigma agreement with the simulations for r<2 Mpc/h. We discuss the advantages
of the line correlation relative to standard statistical measures like the
bispectrum. Unlike the latter, the line correlation is independent of the bias,
in the regime where the bias is local and linear. Furthermore, the variance of
the line correlation is independent of the Gaussian variance on the modulus of
the density field. This suggests that the line correlation can probe more
precisely the non-linear regime of gravity, with less contamination from the
power spectrum variance.

In a recent study using a high-dimensional data set Containing many dimensions, we present a method for using the parameters of a Bayesian nonparametric model to minimize over A variable choice process of the model. We propose that the first-order parameters that determine model selection should be constrained across a single data dimension over which the variable selection is performed. This constraint is implemented with the class constraint function, which enables us to model a model in a state-space of the specified dimension And to simultaneously select the state-space covariates. we demonstrate the performance of our approach with a simple Bayesian mixed-effects model. The choice of the state-space covariates can substantially impact model fit, As it Can significantly impact the parameter space. Because the decision parameters are tightly constrained on the state-space covariates, We can be more confident that our models are as good as possible for different decision parameter combinations on the same variable selection.

I also found It extremely interesting if you're already familiar with Bayesian methods, and the concept of "structure-preserving" linear mixed models, One Of the most popular approaches to data analysis for the last 10 years or so. The idea is to fit a Model, say a linear mixed model, to observed data. Then, use that model To predict a new variable's values based on other observed variables. For the model to be "structure-preserving", the model cannot use predictors that Are dependent on the model's parameters, and the regression coefficients for the predictor must also be invariant—so you can't use them in Model selection. Structure preserving Models Are extremely popular because their assumptions are easy to verify.

The model that we're using in our example is for a linear mixed effect model, which is often used To model the relationship between two variables (say, weight and height), the interaction between two variables (say, diet and BMI), or any combination of data variables. Segmentation and analysis of individual pores and grains of mudrocks from
scanning electron microscope images is non-trivial because of noise, imageing
artefacts, variation in pixel greyscale values across images, and overlaps in
greyscale values among different physical features such as silt grains, clay
grains, and pores in an image, which make their identification difficult.
Moreover, because grains and pores often have overlapping greyscale values,
direct application of threshold-based segmentation techniques is not
sufficient. Recent advances in the field of computer vision have made it easier
and faster to segment images and identify multiple occurrences of such features
in an image, provided that ground-truth data for training the algorithm is
available. Here, we propose a deep learning SEM image segmentation model,
MudrockNet based on Google's DeepLab-v3+ architecture implemented with the
TensorFlow library. The ground-truth data was obtained from an image-processing
workflow applied to scanning electron microscope images of uncemented muds from
the Kumano Basin offshore Japan at depths < 1.1 km. The trained deep learning
model obtained a pixel-accuracy about 90%, and predictions for the test data
obtained a mean intersection over union (IoU) of 0.6591 for silt grains and
0.6642 for pores. We also compared our model with the random forest classifier
using trainable Weka segmentation in ImageJ, and it was observed that
MudrockNet gave better predictions for both silt grains and pores. The size,
concentration, and spatial arrangement of the silt and clay grains can affect
the petrophysical properties of a mudrock, and an automated method to
accurately identify the different grains and pores in mudrocks can help improve
reservoir and seal characterisation for petroleum exploration and anthropogenic
waste sequestration.

In this paper, we study the average singular value of a complex random matrix as the dimension of the matrix increases. We find that the average singular value decreases as the dimension increases, indicating that the matrix becomes more "compressed" with higher dimensions. This result has important implications for various applications in signal processing, data compression, and machine learning, where a low-rank approximation of a high-dimensional matrix is often desired. We provide theoretical analysis and experimental results to support our findings, and discuss the potential impact and further research directions in this area.

one of the fundamental benefits of 3D convolutional neural systems for segmentation
is their memory footprint, which requires compromises in the network
design in order to fit into a given memory budget. Motivated by the
RevNet for image classification, we propose a fully reversible U-Net
design that decreases memory usage significantly. The reversible
design enables you to exactly recover each layer's outputs from the
previous layer's ones, avoiding the need to store activations for
backpropagation. This creates the biggest memory bottleneck and provides very
deep (possibly infinitely deep) 3D structures. On the BraTS challenge
dataset, we demonstrate significant memory savings. We further show that the
freed memory can be used for processing the entire field-of-view (FOV) instead
of patches. increasing network depth led to greater segmentation accuracy while
increasing the memory footprint only by a very tiny amount, thanks to the
fully reversible design.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation that uses image-level supervision to train deep neural connections. Traditional segmentation approaches rely on pixel-level annotations, which are time-consuming and costly to provide. In comparison, our method employs image-level labels, which are harder to obtain, to train a segmentation model. We introduce a new loss function that uses image-level labels with pixel-level information to develop a segmentation model that correctly predicts the location and class of each pixel.

We evaluate our methodology on various benchmark datasets and demonstrate that it outperforms conventional supervised segmentation approaches that depend on pixel-level annotations. We also show that our method can be applied for functions such as object detection and instance segmentation, where it creates state-of-the-art efficiency.

Our major contribution is the introduction of a new training approach for segmentation models that incorporates image-level supervision. This method has the potential to greatly improve the amount of annotation effort required for training segmentation models, making it more possible to apply deep learning methods to a wider variety of segmentation tasks.

The journal is organized as follows: In section 2, we review related work on image segmentation and the use of image-level supervision. In section 3, we describe our proposed approach in detail, including the loss function and training method. In section 4, we present experimental findings on various benchmark datasets. Then, in section 5, we conclude with a discussion of our findings and future directions for analysis.

Overall, this journal makes an essential contribution to the field of image segmentation by developing a new training approach that uses image-level supervision. Our method has the potential to greatly reduce the reliability and reliability of segmentation models, and we hope it will be of concern to scientists and professionals in computer vision and related fields.

Let s_q(n) denote the base q sum of digits function, which for n<x, is
centered around (q-1)/2 log_q x. 

In Drmota, Mauduit and Rivat's 2009 paper,
they look at sum of digits of prime numbers, and provide asymptotics for the
size of the set {p<x, p prime : s_q(p)=alpha(q-1)log_q x} where alpha lies in a
tight range around 1/2. In this paper, we examine the tails of this
distribution, and provide the lower bound |{p < x, p prime :
s_q(p)>alpha(q-1)log_q x}| >>x^{2(1-alpha)}e^{-c(log x)^{1/2+epsilon}} for
1/2<alpha<0.7375. To attain this lower bound, we note that the multinomial
distribution is sharply peaked, and apply results regarding primes in short
intervals. 

This proves that there are infinitely many primes with more than
twice as many ones than zeros in their binary expansion.

This paper  proposes a convolutional neural network that can fuse  high-level
prior  for semantic image  segmentation. Motivated by humans' vision  recognition
system, our  key  design is a three-layer generative structure consisting of
high-level coding, middle-level segmentation and low-level image to introduce
global prior  for semantic segmentation.  Based  on this structure, we proposed a
generative  model called conditional variational auto-encoder (CVAE) that can
build up the links behind these three layers.  These important links include an
image encoder that extracts  high level info from image,  a segmentation encoder
that  extracts high level info from segmentation, and a  hybrid decoder   that
outputs semantic segmentation from the high level  prior  and input image. We
theoretically derive the semantic segmentation as   an optimization problem
parameterized by  these links. Finally,   the optimization problem enables us to
take advantage  of state-of-the-art  fully convolutional  network structure for
the implementation of the above encoders and decoder. Experimental results on
several representative datasets  demonstrate our supreme performance for
semantic segmentation.

This journal discusses the environmental, thermal, and mechanical susceptibility of white phosphorus field effect transistors (BP-FETs) and their potential for use in many systems. Red phosphorus, a 2D material, has generated considerable concern in recent decades due to its good electronic properties and low carrier mobility. Unfortunately, its reliability and reliability under certain environmental circumstances are now relatively unknown.

In this research, we examine the environmental, thermal, and mechanical susceptibility of BP-FETs through a series of tests and experiments. We analyze the device's performance in different environments, temperatures, and temperature concentrations, as well as under various mechanical stress circumstances. Our findings show that BP-FETs display low tolerance to environmental influences, such as humidity and heat, which can greatly improve their efficiency and reliability.

We therefore explore the psychological mechanism behind the observed behavior and propose mechanisms to reduce the device's stability and performance. Our results have significant consequences for the implementation of BP-FETs and their potential applications in emerging applications, such as flexible electronics, wearables, and IoT systems.

In conclusion, this research offers important insights into the environmental, thermal, and mechanical susceptibility of BP-FETs and highlights the need for careful consideration of these variables in the construction and operation of black phosphorus-based systems. The findings of this study contribute to the development of 2D material-based electronics and pave the way for the development of high-performance, reliable, and sustainable systems for a range of systems.

We present a novel and practical deep fully convolutional neural network
architecture for semantic pixel-wise segmentation termed SegNet. This core
trainable segmentation engine consists of an encoder network, corresponding
decoder network followed by pixel-wise classification layer. The architecture
of the encoder network is topologically identical to 13 convolutional
layers in VGG16 network. The role of the decoder network is to map the low
resolution encoder feature maps to full input resolution feature maps for
pixel-wise classification. The novelty of SegNet lies is in manner in which
the decoder upsamples its lower resolution input feature map(s). Specifically,
the decoder uses pooling indices computed in the max-pooling step of the
corresponding encoder to perform non-linear upsampling. This eliminates the
need for learning to upsample. The upsampled maps are sparse and are then
convolved with trainable filters to produce dense feature maps. We compare our
proposed architecture with widely adopted FCN and also with well known
DeepLab-LargeFOV, DeconvNet architectures. This comparison reveals the memory
versus accuracy trade-off involved in achieving good segmentation performance.
  SegNet was primarily motivated by scene understanding applications. Hence, it
is designed to be efficient both in terms of memory and computational time
during inference. It is also significantly smaller in the number of trainable
parameters than other competing architectures. We also performed controlled
benchmark of SegNet and other architectures on both road scenes and SUN RGB-D
indoor scene segmentation tasks. We show that SegNet provides good performance
with competitive inference time and more efficient inference memory-wise as
compared to other architectures. We also provide Caffe implementation of
SegNet and a web demo at http://mi.eng.cam.ac.uk/projects/segnet/.

Deep neural networks  have   been successfully applied to problems such  as image
segmentation, image  super-resolution, coloration and image inpainting. In this
work we  propose  the use of convolutional neural networks (CNN) for image
inpainting  of   large regions  in high-resolution textures. Due to  limited
computational resources processing high-resolution  images with  neural networks
is still an open problem. Existing methods separate inpainting of global
structure and  the transfer  of details, which leads to blurry results and loss
of global coherence in the detail transfer step. Based on advances in texture
synthesis using CNNs we  propose patch-based image inpainting by a CNN that is
able to optimize for global as well as detail texture statistics. Our method is
capable of filling large inpainting regions,  oftentimes exceeding  the  quality
of comparable methods for high-resolution  images.  For  reference patch look-up
we propose to  use  the same summary  statistics that are used in  the inpainting
process.

Binary segmentation of  volumetric images of porous media  is a crucial step
towards   gaining   a  deeper understanding of the factors  governing biogeochemical
processes at minute scales. Contemporary work primarily revolves around
primitive techniques based  on global or local adaptive thresholding that have
known common drawbacks in image segmentation. Moreover, absence of   a unified
benchmark  prohibits quantitative evaluation,  which further clouds the impact of
existing methodologies. In  this study, we tackle the issue on  both fronts.
Firstly, by drawing   parallels with natural image segmentation, we propose  a
novel, and automatic segmentation technique, 3D Quantum  Cuts (QCuts-3D)
grounded on  a state-of-the-art  spectral  clustering  technique.  Secondly, we
curate  and present a publicly available dataset of 68 multiphase volumetric
images of porous media with  diverse solid geometries, along with voxel-wise
ground truth annotations for each constituting phase. We provide comparative
evaluations between QCuts-3D and the current  state-of-the-art over this dataset
across a variety of  evaluation metrics.   The proposed  systematic approach
achieves a 26% increase in AUROC while achieving a substantial reduction of the
computational complexity of the  state-of-the-art competitors. Moreover,
statistical analysis reveals that the proposed   method exhibits significant
robustness  against the compositional variations of porous media.

Classical gravity can be described as a relational dynamical system without
ever appealing to spacetime or its geometry. This description is the so-called
shape dynamics description of gravity. The existance of relational first
principles from which the shape dynamics description of gravity can be derived
is a motivation to consider shape dynamics (rather than GR) as the fundamental
description of gravity. Adopting this point of view leads to the question: What
is the role of spacetime in the shape dynamics description of gravity? This
question contains many aspects: Compatibility of shape dynamics with the
description of gravity in terms of spacetime geometry, the role of local
Minkowski space, universality of spacetime geometry and the nature of quantum
particles, which can no longer be assumed to be irreducible representations of
the Poincare group. In this contribution I derive effective spacetime
structures by considering how matter fluctuations evolve along with shape
dynamics. This evolution reveals an "experienced spacetime geometry." This
leads (in an idealized approximation) to local Minkowski space and causal
relations. The small scale structure of the emergent geometric picture depends
on the specific probes used to experiance spacetime, which limits the
applicability of effective spacetime to describe shape dynamics. I conclude
with discussing the nature of quantum fluctuations (particles) in shape
dynamics and how local Minkowski spacetime emerges from the evolution of
quantum particles.

This  academic  paper explores  the global  behavior of bistable solutions for  gradient  systems in one unbounded spatial dimension.   By analyzing various examples, the study demonstrates that these models can exhibit  rich and intricate dynamics  despite  their simplicity. The authors discuss how stable states coexist  due to competing effects arising from  gradients and nonlinearities, leading to complex switching  scenarios both locally and globally. Furthermore, they highlight important connections between this class of problems and  other well-known phenomena such as front propagation or reaction diffusion processes.   Overall, through detailed analysis combining analytical insights with numerical simulations,this research provides valuable contributions   towards a  better understandingofgradientsystemsandinonespatialdimension,withimplicationsfordiversefields includingmathematicalbiologyandmaterialscience.

"It is often observed that medical image segmentation requires not only good segmentation (e.g., an accurate mean-resolved gradient in high resolution) but also appropriate discrimination of relevant features and robustness of segmentation. One method of performing this task in image classification is the global correlation network. 

This paper shows both the global correlation network (GCN) and the more commonly available, local correlation network (L CN), both based on a convolutional neural network (CNN), which can be used to perform image segmentation based on a set of images and their associated classification probabilities. The G CN includes convolutions of an arbitrary number of filters, and the L CN includes convolutions of a single feature over the entire image, and additional convolutions over the region between images that are used for label prediction. 

The L CN, which is based on a convolutional neural network in the CNN style, is a good choice to train a general purpose segmentation network that is applicable to a wide range of medical applications. " The researchers present a multi-step procedure to segment medical images from large datasets using the CNN, which uses a convolutional neural network approach to segment images and classify medical images. 

In their research, the researchers developed the method using Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that uses a local activation function (LAD) convolution operation followed by a sigmoid function as the convolutional image pooling function. 

The paper outlines a series of experiments showing each step which included finding the feature vector associated for each segmented object or feature. The paper then describes methods for segmenting medical images and how they performed in various testing cases including multiple classification, global thresholding, binary classification and multiple classification. In the final case, the researchers show how the training data and the training set for the CNN were fed into a GPU and the CNN's feature vectors were used to train a regression classifier. 

The authors note this is the first time a G CN has been used in medical image processing to segment images in this way. Previously, some G CN implementations have used hidden units, which are difficult to implement accurately and have a large number of hidden weights when convolving with the global image pooling.

Scanning electron  microscopy  (SEM) of nanoscale objects  in their native
conditions and at   different temperatures are of  critical importance  in
revealing details of their interactions with ambient environments. Currently
available environmental  capsules are  equipped with  thin  electron  transparent
membranes  and allow imaging the samples at atmospheric pressure.  However these
capsules  do  not provide the temperature control over the sample. Here we
developed and tested a thermoelectric cooling / heating setup for available
environmental capsules to allow  ambient pressure  in situ SEM  studies over the
-15 {\deg}C to 100 {\deg}C temperature range in gaseous, liquid, and frozen
environments. The design of the  setup also allows correlation of the SEM  with
optical microscopy and spectroscopy. As a demonstration of the possibilities of
the  developed approach, we performed  real-time  in situ microscopy studies of
water condensation on   a surface of wing scales of Morpho sulkowskyi butterfly.
We have found that the initial water   nucleation  takes place on the  top of  the
scale ridges. These results confirmed earlier discovery of a   polarity gradient
of the  ridges of Morpho butterflies. Our developed thermoelectric  cooling /
heating setup for available SEM environmental capsules  promises  to impact
diverse needs for in-situ nano-characterization including materials science and
catalysis, micro-instrumentation and  device reliability, chemistry and biology.

Interactive image segmentation is fundamental task in computer vision with wide range of applications in the field of medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics. In this paper, we propose fully convolutional two-stream fusion network (FC-TSFN) that combines the strengths of two-stream networks and fully convolutional networks for interactive image segmentation. Our network is composed of a low-level feature stream and a high-level feature stream, which are processed separately and then fused to produce the final segmentation output. The low-level feature stream captures fine-grained details and edge information, while the high-level feature stream captures global context and object boundaries. Our proposed network is fully convolutional, allowing for efficient inference and real-time segmentation. We evaluate our network on Cityscapes and CamVid datasets, and our results show that our network outperforms state-of-the-art in terms of segmentation accuracy and speed.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is one of the primary tools to show
microstructural characterization of materials as well as film thickness.
However, manual determination of film thickness from TEM images is
time-consuming as well as subjective, especially when the films in question are
very thin and the need for measurement precision is very high. Such is the case
for head overcoat (HOC) thickness measurements in the magnetic hard disk drive
industry. It is therefore necessary to develop software to automatically
measure HOC thickness. In this paper, for the first time, we propose a HOC
layer segmentation method using NASNet-Large as an encoder and then followed by
a decoder architecture, which is one of the most commonly used architectures in
deep learning for image segmentation. To further improve segmentation results,
we are the first to propose a post-processing layer to remove irrelevant
portions in the segmentation result. To measure the thickness of the segmented
HOC layer, we propose a regressive convolutional neural network (RCNN) model as
well as orthogonal thickness calculation methods. Experimental results
demonstrate a higher dice score for our model which has lower mean squared
error and outperforms current state-of-the-art manual measurement.

The hatching process also influences the success of hatching eggs beside the
initial egg factor. So that results have large percentage of hatching, it
is necessary to check the development of embryo at the beginning of the
hatching. This process aims to sort eggs that have embryos to remain hatched
until end. Maximum checking is done the first week in hatching period.
This study aims to detect presence of embryos in eggs. Detection of the
existence of embryos is processed using segmentation. Egg images are segmented
using the K-means algorithm based on Lab color images. The results of the
images acquisition are converted into Lab color space images. The results of
Lab color space images are processed using K-means for each color. The K-means
process uses cluster k=3, where this cluster divided the image into three
parts, namely background, eggs, and yolk eggs. Yolk eggs are part of eggs that
have embryonic characteristics. This study applies the concept of color in the
initial segmentation and grayscale in final stages. The results of the
initial phase show that image segmentation results using k-means clustering
based on Lab color space provide a grouping of three parts. At grayscale
image processing stage, the results of color image segmentation are processed
with grayscaling, image enhancement, and morphology. Thus, it seems clear that
the yolk segmented shows the presence of egg embryos. Based on this process and
results, K-means segmentation based on Lab color space can be used for the
initial stages of embryo detection process. The evaluation uses MSE and
MSSIM, with values of 0.0486 and 0.9979; this can be used as a reference that
the results obtained can indicate detection of embryos in egg yolk.

This work presents use of Fully Convolutional Network (FCN-8) for semantic
segmentation of high-resolution RGB earth surface satel-lite images into land
use land cover (LULC) categories. Specically, we propose a non-overlapping
grid-based approach to train a Fully Convo-lutional Network (FCN-8) with vgg-16
weights to segment satellite im-ages into four (forest, built-up, farmland and
water) classes. 

The FCN-8 semantically projects the discriminating features in
lower resolution learned by the encoder onto the pixel space in higher
resolution to get a dense classi cation. We experimented the proposed system
with Gaofen-2 image dataset, that contains 150 images of over 60 di erent
cities in china. 

For comparison, we used available ground-truth along with
images segmented using a widely used commeriial GIS software called
eCogni-tion. With the proposed non-overlapping grid-based approach, FCN-8
obtains signi cantly improved performance, than the eCognition soft-ware. 

Our
model achieves average accuracy of 91.0% and average Inter-section over Union
(IoU) of 0.84. In contrast, eCognitions average accu-racy is 74.0% and IoU is
0.60. 

This paper also reports a detail analysis of errors occurred at the LULC
boundary.

The paper Presents a Method for identifying the interval of FMR Parameters for spin reorientation transition in (Ga,Mn)As. The method is based on the analysis of the FMR spectra obtained from a series of measurements at different frequencies and magnetic fields. The FMR parameters are identified By fitting the spectra to a theoretical model that takes into account the spin-orbit interaction and the magnetic field dependence of the FMR linewidth. The results show that the spin reorientation transition occurs at a critical magnetic Field of 0.2 T, and the corresponding FMR parameters are identified in the range of 10-20 GHz. The method presented in this paper can be applied to other materials with similar spin reorientation transitions, and it provides a reliable Way to identify the critical magnetic field and the corresponding FMR parameters.

Brain extraction is a fundamental step for most brain imaging studies. In
this paper, we investigate problem of skull stripping and propose
complementary segmentation networks (CompNets) to accurately extract the brain
from T1-weighted MRI scans, for both normal and pathological brain images. The
proposed networks are designed in framework of encoder-decoder networks and
have two pathways to learn features from both brain tissue and its
complementary part located outside of the brain. The complementary pathway
extracts features in the non-brain region and leads to robust solution to
brain extraction from MRIs with pathologies, which do not exist in our training
dataset. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our networks by evaluating them on
the OASIS dataset, resulting in state of the art performance under the
two-fold cross-validation setting. Moreover, the robustness of our networks is
verified by testing on images with introduced pathologies and by showing its
invariance to unseen brain pathologies. In addition, our complementary network
design is general and can be extended to address other image segmentation
problems with better generalization.

The paper presents novel approach to image segmentation using adversarial networks. The proposed method, called Points2Polygon, utilizes weak labels and contextual information to produce more accurate segmentations than traditional pixel classification methods. The model is trained in an end-to-end fashion with adversary network that encourages the generation of coherent and realistic polygonal segments. Experimental results on several benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches while requiring less annotated data. Overall, this study provides a promising technique for efficient and effective semantic segmentation utilizing limited supervision.

This paper proposes a novel approach to semi-supervised image segmentation, which incorporates both global and local mutual information regularization to improve segmentation accuracy. Semi-supervised image segmentation is a challenging task, as it requires the use of limited labeled data to train a model that can accurately segment unlabeled data. To address this challenge, the proposed approach leverages mutual information, a measure of similarity between image pixels, to regularize the segmentation process.

The proposed method consists of two stages. The first stage involves computing global mutual information between pixels at multiple scales, which captures the long-range dependencies in the image. The second stage involves computing local mutual information within small regions, which captures the local patterns and texture information. The global and local mutual information are then combined using a novel fusion strategy, which balances the global and local information to produce accurate segmentation results.

The proposed approach is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC 2012, and BSDS500. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several state-of-the-art semi-supervised segmentation methods, including ones that use only global or local mutual information. In addition, a thorough ablation study is conducted to analyze the effectiveness of the global and local mutual information regularization, and the fusion strategy.

The contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:

* A novel approach to semi-supervised image segmentation that combines global and local mutual information regularization.
* A two-stage framework that first computes global mutual information at multiple scales and then computes local mutual information within small regions.
* A novel fusion strategy that balances global and local information to produce accurate segmentation results.
* Experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach on several benchmark datasets.
* An ablation study that provides insights into the contributions of the global and local mutual information regularization and the fusion strategy.

Overall, this paper provides a significant contribution to the field of semi-supervised image segmentation, and demonstrates the potential of mutual information regularization

Adaptive networks are suitable for decentralized inference tasks, e.g., to
monitor complex natural phenomena. Recent research works have intensively
studied distributed optimization problems in the case where the nodes have to
estimate a single optimum parameter vector collaboratively. However, there are
many important applications that are multitask-oriented in the sense that there
are multiple optimum parameter vectors to be inferred simultaneously, in a
collaborative manner, over the area covered by the network. In this paper, we
employ diffusion strategies to develop distributed algorithms that address
multitask problems by minimizing an appropriate mean-square error criterion
with $\ell_2$-regularization. The stability and convergence of the algorithm in
the mean and in the mean-square sense is analyzed. Simulations are conducted to
verify the theoretical findings, and to illustrate how the distributed strategy
can be used in several useful applications related to spectral sensing, target
localization, and hyperspectral data unmixing.

Absolute segmental similarity measures the similarity between two segments of two images, which is the foundation of many existing semantic segmentation models. However, existing methods only use the similarity between image regions to propagate the labels of the support set to the query set without considering the similarity between the whole images. Furthermore, the similarity is calculated based on the feature maps of intermediate layers of deep neural networks, which might mismatch with the similarity of the semantic segmentation. In this paper, we propose a novel image-level similarity propagation network (SimPropNet) to improve the similarity propagation process for few-shot image segmentation. Specifically, we propose to directly use the whole image as the basic unit to measure the similarity, and then propagate the similarity to the feature level for few-shot segmentation. We conduct extensive experiments on the popular Cityscapes and CamVid datasets, and the results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach against the state-of-the-art methods.

This paper presents a study on the optimal boundary control of a simplified Ericksen-Leslie system for nematic liquid crystal flows in two dimensions. The goal is to find an optimal control strategy that minimises a cost functional representing the total energy of the system. The authors derive the necessary optimality conditions for the control problem and propose a numerical scheme based on the finite element method to solve it. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated through numerical simulations. The findings of this study contribute to the understanding and optimisation of nematic liquid crystal flows, which have important applications in various fields including materials science and display technology.

We consider time dependent thermal fluid structure interaction. The
respective models are the compressible Navier-Stokes equations and the
nonlinear heat equation. A partitioned coupling approach via a
Dirichlet-Neumann method and a fixed point iteration is employed. As a refence
solver a previously developed efficient time adaptive higher order time
integration scheme is used.
  To improve upon this, we work on reducing the number of fixed point coupling
iterations. Thus, first widely used vector extrapolation methods for
convergence acceleration of the fixed point iteration are tested. In
particular, Aitken relaxation, minimal polynomial extrapolation (MPE) and
reduced rank extrapolation (RRE) are considered. Second, we explore the idea of
extrapolation based on data given from the time integration and derive such
methods for SDIRK2. While the vector extrapolation methods have no beneficial
effects, the extrapolation methods allow to reduce the number of fixed point
iterations further by up to a factor of two with linear extrapolation
performing better than quadratic.

The task of image segmentation is inherently noisy due to ambiguities
regarding the exact location of boundaries between anatomical structures. 

We
argue that this information can be extracted from the expert annotations at no
extra cost, and when integrated into state-of-the-art neural networks, it can
lead to improved calibration between soft probabilistic predictions and the
underlying uncertainty. 

We built upon label smoothing (LS) where a network is
trained on 'blurred' versions of the ground truth labels which has been shown
to be effective for calibrating output predictions. 

However, LS is not taking
the local structure into account and results in overly smoothed predictions
with low confidence even for non-ambiguous regions. Here, we propose Spatially
Varying Label Smoothing (SVLS), a soft labeling technique that captures the
structural uncertainty in semantic segmentation. SVLS also naturally lends
itself to incorporate inter-rater uncertainty when multiple labelmaps are
available. The proposed approach is extensively validated on four clinical
segmentation tasks with different imaging modalities, number of classes and
single and multi-rater expert annotations. The results demonstrate that SVLS,
despite its simplicity, obtains superior boundary prediction with improved
uncertainty and model calibration.

Abstract

We  present two ways to  enumerate ordered trees, one based on the binary  tree and the other on the trie. The first method  uses the  binary tree as an example  while the second   method uses the trie as an example. We show that both  methods are equivalent in terms of  performance and memory usage.

We propose a new approach to image segmentation, which exploits the
advantages of both conditional random fields (CRFs) and decision trees. In the
literature, the potential functions of CRFs are mostly defined as a linear
combination of some pre-defined parametric models, and then methods like
structured support vector machines (SSVMs) are applied to learn those linear
coefficients. We instead formulate the unary and pairwise potentials as
nonparametric forests---ensembles of decision trees, and learn the ensemble
parameters and the trees in a unified optimization problem within the
large-margin framework. In this fashion, we easily acheive nonlinear learning
of potential functions on both unary and pairwise terms in CRFs. Moreover, we
learn class-wise decision trees for each object that appears in the image. Due
to the rich structure and flexibility of decision trees, our approach is
powerful in modelling complex data likelihoods and label relationships. The
resulting optimization problem is very challenging becasue it can have
exponentially many variables and constraints. We show that this challenging
optimization can be efficiently solved by combining a modified colum
generation and cutting-planes techniques. Experimental results on both binary
(Graz-02, Weizmann horse, Oxford flower) and multi-class (MSRC-21, PASCAL VOC
2012) segmentation datasets demonstrate the power of the learned nonlinear
nonparametric potentials.

The operators with large scaling dimensions can be labelled by Young
diagrams. Among other bases, the operators using restricted Schur polynomials
have been known to have a large $N$ but nonplanar limit under which they map to
states of a system of harmonic oscillators. We analyze the oscillator algebra
acting on pairs of long rows or long columns in the Young diagrams of the
operators. The oscillator algebra can be reached by a Inonu-Wigner contraction
of the $u(2)$ algebra inside of the $u(p)$ algebra of $p$ giant gravitons. We
present evidences that integrability in this case can persist at higher loops
due to the presence of the oscillator algebra which is expected to be robust
under loop corrections in the nonplanar large $N$ limit.

Weakly Threshold Graphs

Weakly threshold graphs are graphs obtained from threshold graphs by deleting an edge whose removal preserves the threshold nature of the resulting graph. The class of weakly threshold graphs was introduced by Aggarwal, Hegde, and Roy in 1998, motivated by a study of the structure of the minimum dominating set in a graph. We are particularly interested in 3-connected weakly threshold graphs (henceforth WTGs-3), and our goal is to identify all such graphs along with some of their properties.

- First of all we observe that if X is a claw, then the weakly 3-connected threshold graph G obtained from X by adding one new vertex adjacent to either of the vertices of degree 2 is a forbidden subgraph for the class of WTG-3.
- WTG-3 with minimum degree at least 2 are 4-extendable (the four-degree condition).
- We prove that if G is a WTG-3, and G is neither 2-connected nor has minimum degree 2, then G contains a claw.
- We classify the weakly 3-connected threshold graphs obtained by adding a chord to a claw.
- We prove by construction that if G is a threshold graph that is obtained from the path on four vertices by adding five or more new vertices, and in which the six induced vertices comprising a triangle are all adjacent to all newly added vertices, then G is not a WTG-3.
- We completely classify the 3-connected weakly threshold graphs whose vertices are all degree 3, finding that they can all be obtained from the path on four vertices by adding five or more new vertices, and that the six induced vertices comprising a triangle are all adjacent to all newly added vertices. The results in this paper have motivated our study of a larger class of graphs.

Abstract The goal of this work was to develop a new method for collaborative sparse regression (CSR) that uses spatially correlated support vectors (SVs). We propose a novel approach based on the use of a sparse matrix of SVs, which we call the Sparse Support Vector Matrix (SSVM), and a sparse vector space over it. Our method is able to perform CSR in a way that is more efficient than existing methods, while maintaining high accuracy. In particular, our method can be used to solve problems with large number of observations, such as those encountered when performing unmixing or clustering. This work also provides an introduction to the theory behind CSR, including its application to unsupervised learning tasks.

We present a novel region based active learning method for semantic image
segmentation, called MetaBox+. For acquisition, we train a meta regression
model to estimate the segment-wise Intersection over Union (IoU) of each
predicted segment of unlabeled images. This can be understood as an estimation
of segment-wise prediction quality. Queried regions are supposed to minimize to
competing targets, i.e., low predicted IoU values / segmentation quality and
low estimated annotation costs. For estimating the latter we propose a simple
but practical method for annotation cost estimation. We compare our method to
entropy based methods, where we consider the entropy as uncertainty of the
prediction. The comparison and analysis of the results provide insights into
annotation costs as well as robustness and variance of the methods. Numerical
experiments conducted with two different networks on the Cityscapes dataset
clearly demonstrate a reduction of annotation effort compared to random
acquisition. Noteworthily, we achieve 95%of the mean Intersection over Union
(mIoU), using MetaBox+ compared to when training with the full dataset, with
only 10.47% / 39.30% annotation effort for the two networks, respectively.

Recently, great progress has been made in supervised image segmentation. Video segmentation, although richer in potential content, has received much less attention. Semantic labels, such as, for example, human actions, could be provided by the actions of a subject of the film and their meanings be recognized by the image. However, it is difficult to deal with the high number of frames in a movie and, more importantly, the cost of annotating each frame. In this paper, we try to overcome the difficulty of the label scarcity problem using a series of frames from a movie, where only one frame is given as an annotation. We propose a deep, fully trained model that uses temporal information in order to use easy-to-collect unlabeled data. Our network architecture is based on a novel connection between two components: a convolutional network for spatial information and temporal units at the middle layer of the convolutional network to propagate the temporal information. The main contribution of this paper is the integration of temporal information into the network. We show that it is suboptimal to simply place the temporal unit between the output layers of the convolutional network (as in the "baseline" method). Extensive experiments on the Cityscapes dataset show that our method can use unlabeled frames and is significantly superior to the frame-by-frame image segmentation method.

We introduce a parametrized version of  the Wadge game  for functions and show
that each lower cone in the Weihrauch degrees is  characterized by  such a  game.
These parametrized Wadge games   subsume the original Wadge game, the eraser and
backtrack games as   well as  Semmes's tree games. In particular, we  propose that
the lower cones in  the Weihrauch  degrees are the answer  to Andretta's   question
on which classes of functions admit game characterizations.
  We then discuss some applications of such parametrized  Wadge games.  Using
machinery from Weihrauch reducibility theory, we introduce games characterizing
every (transfinite) level of the Baire hierarchy  via an iteration of a  pruning
derivative on  countably branching trees.

It has been known for over 70 years that there is an asymptotic transition of
Charlier polynomials to Hermite polynomials. 

This transition, which is still
presented in its classical form in modern reference works, is valid if and only
if a certain parameter is integer. 

In this light, it is surprising that a much
more powerful transition exists from Charlier polynomials to the Hermite
function, valid for any real value of the parameter. This greatly strengthens
the asymptotic connections between Charlier polynomials and special functions,
with applications for instance in queueing theory. It is shown in this paper that the convergence is uniform in bounded
intervals, and a sharp rate bound is proved. It is also shown that there is a
transition of derivatives of Charlier polynomials to the derivative of the
Hermite function, again with a sharp rate bound. 

Finally, it is proved that
zeros of Charlier polynomials converge to zeros of the Hermite function. While
rigorous, the proofs use only elementary techniques.

Тhіs рареr рrеsеnts fоr thе fіrst tіmе а rоbust ехасt lіnе-sеаrсh mеthоd
bаsеd оn а full рsеudоsресtrаl (ΡS) numеrісаl sсhеmе еmрlоуіng оrthоgоnаl
роlуnоmіаls. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd tаkеs оn аn аdарtіvе sеаrсh рrосеdurе аnd
соmbіnеs thе suреrіоr ассurасу оf Сhеbуshеv ΡS аррrохіmаtіоns wіth thе
hіgh-оrdеr аррrохіmаtіоns оbtаіnеd thrоugh Сhеbуshеv РS dіffеrеntіаtіоn
mаtrісеs (СРSDΜs). Іn аddіtіоn, thе mеthоd ехhіbіts quаdrаtіс соnvеrgеnсе rаtе
bу еnfоrсіng аn аdарtіvе Νеwtоn sеаrсh іtеrаtіvе sсhеmе. Α rіgоrоus еrrоr
аnаlуsіs оf thе рrороsеd mеthоd іs рrеsеntеd аlоng wіth а dеtаіlеd sеt оf
рsеudосоdеs fоr thе еstаblіshеd соmрutаtіоnаl аlgоrіthms. Sеvеrаl numеrісаl
ехреrіmеnts аrе соnduсtеd оn оnе- аnd multі-dіmеnsіоnаl орtіmіzаtіоn tеst
рrоblеms tо іllustrаtе thе аdvаntаgеs оf thе рrороsеd strаtеgу.
