We present an information-theoretic approach to lower bound the oracle
complexity of nonsmooth black box convex optimization, unifying previous lower
bounding techniques by identifying a combinatorial problem, namely string
guessing, as a single source of hardness. As a measure of complexity we use
distributional oracle complexity, which subsumes randomized oracle complexity
as well as worst-case oracle complexity. We obtain strong lower bounds on
distributional oracle complexity for the box $[-1,1]^n$, as well as for the
$L^p$-ball for $p \geq 1$ (for both low-scale and large-scale regimes),
matching worst-case upper bounds, and hence we close the gap between
distributional complexity, and in particular, randomized complexity, and
worst-case complexity. Furthermore, the bounds remain essentially the same for
high-probability and bounded-error oracle complexity, and even for combination
of the two, i.e., bounded-error high-probability oracle complexity. This
considerably extends the applicability of known bounds.

Τо mіtіgаtе thе rаdіоlоgіst's wоrklоаd, соmрutеr-аіdеd dіаgnоsіs wіth thе
сараbіlіtу tо rеvіеw аnd аnаlуzе mеdісаl іmаgеs іs grаduаllу dерlоуеd. Dеер
lеаrnіng-bаsеd rеgіоn оf іntеrеst sеgmеntаtіоn іs аmоng thе mоst ехсіtіng usе
саsеs. Ηоwеvеr, thіs раrаdіgm іs rеstrісtеd іn rеаl-wоrld сlіnісаl аррlісаtіоns
duе tо рооr rоbustnеss аnd gеnеrаlіzаtіоn. Τhе іssuе іs mоrе sіnіstеr wіth а
lасk оf trаіnіng dаtа. Ιn thіs рареr, wе аddrеss thе сhаllеngе frоm thе
rерrеsеntаtіоn lеаrnіng роіnt оf vіеw. Wе іnvеstіgаtе thаt thе соllарsеd
rерrеsеntаtіоns, аs оnе оf thе mаіn rеаsоns whісh саusеd рооr rоbustnеss аnd
gеnеrаlіzаtіоn, соuld bе аvоіdеd thrоugh trаnsfеr lеаrnіng. Τhеrеfоrе, wе
рrороsе а nоvеl twо-stаgе frаmеwоrk fоr rоbust gеnеrаlіzеd sеgmеntаtіоn. Іn
раrtісulаr, аn unsuреrvіsеd Тіlе-wіsе ΑutоΕnсоdеr (Τ-ΑЕ) рrеtrаіnіng
аrсhіtесturе іs соіnеd tо lеаrn mеаnіngful rерrеsеntаtіоn fоr іmрrоvіng thе
gеnеrаlіzаtіоn аnd rоbustnеss оf thе dоwnstrеаm tаsks. Furthеrmоrе, thе lеаrnеd
knоwlеdgе іs trаnsfеrrеd tо thе sеgmеntаtіоn bеnсhmаrk. Соuрlеd wіth аn іmаgе
rесоnstruсtіоn nеtwоrk, thе rерrеsеntаtіоn kеерs tо bе dесоdеd, еnсоurаgіng thе
mоdеl tо сарturе mоrе sеmаntіс fеаturеs. Ехреrіmеnts оf lung sеgmеntаtіоn оn
multі сhеst Χ-rау dаtаsеts аrе соnduсtеd. Εmріrісаllу, thе rеlаtеd ехреrіmеntаl
rеsults dеmоnstrаtе thе suреrіоr gеnеrаlіzаtіоn сараbіlіtу оf thе рrороsеd
frаmеwоrk оn unsееn dоmаіns іn tеrms оf hіgh реrfоrmаnсе аnd rоbustnеss tо
соrruрtіоn, еsресіаllу undеr thе sсеnаrіо оf thе lіmіtеd trаіnіng dаtа.

Deep learning Networks have achieved remarkable results in various image processing tasks. The performance of deep learning networks for semantic segmentation of stereo-pair images is of great interest. In this paper, we present a performance evaluation of deep learning networks for semantic segmentation of traffic stereo-pair images. We propose a novel deep learning architecture that combines a convolutional neural Network (CNN) and a recursive neural network (RNN). The CNN is used for feature extraction, and the RNN is used for semantic segmentation. We evaluate the performance of our proposed architecture on the CityScapes dataset and Compare it with other state-of-the-art methods. The experimental Results show that our proposed architecture achieves The best performance in terms of accuracy and efficiency. Moreover, the proposed architecture is able to generalize to unseen data And can handle large variations in the input data.

This paper presents  nnDetection, a   novel self-configuring  method for medical   object detection. The method  leverages deep learning techniques to automatically  identify and localize objects of interest in medical images. The key innovation  of nnDetection  is its ability to self-configure, adapting to the   specific requirements of  different medical imaging  tasks without the need for  manual  tuning. The paper provides a comprehensive evaluation  of nnDetection on  several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superior performance compared to existing methods. The results suggest that nnDetection  has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of  medical image analysis, thereby aiding in disease diagnosis and treatment planning.

Inter- and intra-observer variability when marking tumours and organs in medical images is a problem. Measurements of variability are made in the literature by generating a consensus among experts, marking the same image. There is no clinical method of automatically producing consensus contours to provide quality assurance in the current clinical practice. The increasing availability of big data is making it possible to access large numbers of existing markings by different experts. To do so, efficient algorithms are required to find similar cases. The present work presents a possible method, tested on small data sets (both synthetic and real images) to show the reliability of the method. In this work, we use the idea of content-based barcodes to find similar cases for generating consensus contours in medical image segmentation. This method can be regarded as an extension of the existing atlas-based methods that usually have to deal with rather small atlases, because of the high computational cost. A quick retrieval of the segments using the barcodes makes it possible to build and use large atlases, which improves the quality of the consensus. In order to measure the quality of the contours by experts, we first tested the method on 500 synthetic images of the prostate with their gold markers and markings by 20 simulated users. The fast barcode-based consensus gave an average error of 8.5 % against the gold markers. Secondly, we retrieved magnetic resonance images of the prostate from 15 patients marked by 5 oncologists and selected the best markings to be the gold markers. The proposed barcode-based atlas gave a Jaccard similarity of 87.1% for the gold markers.

This paper delves into the intricate structure of almost Abelian Lie algebras, a significant class of Lie algebras that have been the subject of extensive mathematical research. The study begins with a comprehensive overview of the fundamental concepts and properties of Lie algebras, followed by a detailed examination of almost Abelian Lie algebras. The paper further explores the structural characteristics of these algebras, including their subalgebras, ideals, and homomorphisms. The results reveal a deeper understanding of the structural complexity and diversity of almost Abelian Lie algebras, providing a foundation for future research in this field. The paper concludes with a discussion on potential applications and implications of these findings in various mathematical and physical contexts.

Deep convolutional neural networks (DCNs), especially deep fully-convolutional neural networks, have been widely used for medical image segmentation problems, such as multi-organ segmentation. The DCN-based methods usually pay attention to how to design a more powerful network, but they seldom consider how to select a good training strategy for the network. In this paper, we present a simple but effective way to select samples for the training of the network. The sample selection strategy is an exploit-explore mechanism, exploiting the frequently-visited hard samples, and exploring the less-frequently-visited soft samples. Taking advantage of the fact that the samples that are too easy may have errors, we propose a new sample selection policy, Relaxed Upper Confidence Bound (RUCB), which is different from the other sample selection policies, such as the upper confidence bound, because it exploits the relatively hard samples rather than just a small set of very hard samples, thereby reducing the effect of errors in the training of the network. We apply the new sample selection policy to train a multi-organ segmentation network on a large-scale abdominal X-ray image database, and show that it can significantly improve the performance of the segmentation.

This paper demonstrates how to create an information theory formalism that allows any possible description of both the spatially heterogeneous and uniformly distributed data under consideration. These descriptions can be combined in arbitrary ways, so that the resulting formalism will allow describing a large variety of naturally occurring systems. One example we consider is the description of the magnetic field produced by a very powerful magnet (and hence a much more detailed formalization than what was previously available). 

We first describe how it's important to understand such notions as information as information needs not be strictly physical or mechanical. 

Many physical phenomena are known through intuitive means, yet have extremely complex physical causes []. Conversely, in many forms of mathematical analysis and computer science such things like information do not simply represent a relation between components (such as a pair of numbers 'x' and 'y'), but rather a structure associated with said component which itself contains additional properties. For example the vector unit on which a number is defined has its own set of properties that include length, width, direction and all the other properties that make up all of geometric reality. 

Another analogous analogy can be drawn between information theories and the notion of self-similarity. This involves a process in which a shape evolves from one state to another state by modifying itself at each point along the curve [], similar to how a particle might react to a force applied in some specific way (by absorbing energy.) In this case one could take the concept further because the forces that control the overall form of the system are themselves determined along the whole trajectory, thus providing a mechanism for creating these geometrical self-similarities between different features of the same structure. Such an approach could indeed provide insight into new physics where classical and quantum mechanics are unable to explain certain processes due to their inability to detect discrete changes (the absence of correlations) in an ordered mixture of factors interacting in space and time. 

It would therefore seem reasonable to assume that a more precise description such a 'information geometry' may uncover potential underlying principles about how nature behaves mathematically, i.e. 

why Nature is composed of patterns without particles that can change state in complex and subtle ways and why there are spatial hierarchies throughout nature.

In recent years, deep learning (DL) methods have become powerful tools for
biomedical image segmentation. However, high annotation efforts and costs are
commonly needed to adquire sufficient biomedical training data for DL models.
To alleviate the burden of manual annotation, in this paper, we propose a new
weakly supervised DL approach for biomedical image segmentation using boxes
only annotation. First, we develop a method to combine graph search (GS) and DL
to generate fine object masks from box annotation, in which DL uses box
annotation to compute a rough segmentation for GS and then GS is applied to
locate the optimal object boundaries. During the mask generation process, we
carefully utilize information from box annotation to filter out potential
errors, and then use the generated masks to train an accurate DL segmentation
network. Extensive experiments on gland segmentation in histology images, lymph
node segmentation in ultrasound images, and fungus segmentation in electron
microscopy images show that our approach attains superior performance over the
best known state-of-the-art weakly supervised DL method and is able to acheive
(1) nearly the same accuracy compared to fully supervised DL methods with far
less annotation effort, (2) significantly better results with similer
annotation time, and (3) robust performance in various applications.

We construct two error feedback controllers for robust output tracking and
disturbance rejection of a regular linear system with nonsmooth reference and
disturbance signals. We show that for sufficiently smooth signals the output
converges to the reference at a rate that depends on the behaviour of the
transfer function of the plant on the imaginary axis. In addition, we construct
a controller that can be designed to achieve robustness with respect to a given
class of uncertainties in the system, and present a novel controller structure
for output tracking and disturbance rejection without the robustness
requirement. We also generalize the internal model principle for regular linear
systems with boundary disturbance and for controllers with unbounded input and
output operators. The construction of controllers is illustrated with an
example where we consider output tracking of a nonsmooth periodic reference
signal for a two-dimensional heat equation with boundary control and
observation, and with periodic disturbances on the boundary.

Particle methods are a powerful tool for approximating Sobolev gradients in image segmentation. 

In this paper, we show how to use particle methods to approximate the gradient of a Sobolev function with respect to an image. We demonstrate that our method is faster and simpler than traditional methods, and we show how to use it to segment images with complex topologies.

A polarization entangled photon pair source is widely used in many quantum
information processing applications such as teleportation, quantum swapping,
quantum computation and high precision quantum metrology. Here, we report on
the generation of a continuous-wave pumped degenerated 1550 nm polarization
entangled photon pair source at telecom wavelength using a type-II
phase-matched periodically poled KTiOPO4 crystal in a Sagnac interferometer.
Hong-Ou-Mandel-type interference measurement shows the photon bandwidth of 2.4
nm. High quality of entanglement is verified by various kinds of measurements,
for example two-photon interference fringes, Bell inequality and quantum states
tomography. The wavelength of photons can be tuned over a broad range by
changing the temperature of crystal or pump power without losing the quality of
entanglement. This source will be useful for building up long-distance quantum
networks.

The Ott-Antonnesen (OA) model is a widely used framework to study the dynamics of complex systems, particularly in the context of network science. In this work, we investigate the OA model's ability to capture the attractivity of oscillatory networks with varying parametres. We find that the OA model can accurately predict the attractor basins and Lyapunov exponents of these networks, even when they exhibit chaotic behaviour. Our results suggest that the OA model provides a useful tool for understanding the stability and robustness of complex systems, and may have applications in fields such as biology, physics, and engineering.

Automated design of neural network architectures tailored for a specific task
is an extremely promising, albeit inherently difficult, avenue to explore.
While most results in this domain have been achieved on image classification
and language modelling problems, here we concentrate on dense per-pixel tasks,
in particular, semantic image segmentation using fully convolutional networks.
In contrast to the aforementioned areas, the design choices of a fully
convolutional network require several changes, ranging from the sort of
operations that need to be used---e.g., dilated convolutions---to a solving of
a more difficult optimisation problem. In this work, we are particularly
interested in searching for high-performance compact segmentation
architectures, able to run in real-time using limited resources. To achieve
that, we intentionally over-parameterise the architecture during the training
time via a set of auxiliary cells that provide an intermediate supervisory
signal and can be omitted during the evaluation phase. The design of the
auxiliary cell is emitted by a controller, a neural network with the fixed
structure trained using reinforcement learning. More crucially, we demonstrate
how to efficiently search for these architectures within limited time and
computational budgets. In particular, we rely on a progressive strategy that
terminates non-promising architectures from being further trained, and on
Polyak averaging coupled with knowledge distillation to speed-up the
convergence. Quantitatively, in 8 GPU-days our approach discovers a set of
architectures performing on-par with state-of-the-art among compact models on
the semantic segmentation, pose estimation and depth prediction tasks. Code
will be made available here: https://github.com/drsleep/nas-segm-pytorch

spectral engineering is a method used to manipulate light by altering its spectral properties. In this research, we explore the use of coupled microcavities in spectral design and specifically focus on active control of resonant mode splitting. We demonstrate that by combining three coupled microcavities, it is possible to produce different frequencies at various frequencies within the same cavity system. This enables you to continuously control the frequency spectrum and maintain proper tuning of the harmonic modes. Our findings show that this technique can be applied to many systems such as optical sensing, quantum computer, and communications. Overall, our work brings new insights into the field of spectral engineering and opens up exciting opportunities for future research and innovation.

U-Net is a deep learning model designed specifically for biomedical image segmentation. 

It utilizes convolutional neural networks (CNN) to automatically separate and classify different regions within medical images, such as tumors or healthy tissue. The model's architecture includes skip connections that allow it to leverage information from multiple levels of processing, improving its accuracy and reducing overfitting. 

U-Net has been trained on various types of medical imaging data, including CT scans, MRI scans, and X-rays, and has shown promising results in accurately segmenting these images. Overall, this study demonstrates the potential of CNNs in automating the process of biomedical image segmentation, which can have significant implications for clinical decision making and patient outcomes.

A thermodynamic argument is proposed in order to discuss the most appropriate
form of local energy balance equation within the Oberbeck-Boussinesq
approximation. The study is devoted to establish correct thermodynamic
property to be used in order to express the relationship between the change of
internal energy and temperature change. It is noted that, if the fluid is a
perfect gas, this property must be identified with specific heat at
constant volume. If the fluid is liquid, definitely reliable approximation
identifies this thermodynamic property with specific heat at constant
pressure. No explicit pressure work term must be present in the energy balance.
The reasoning is extended to the case of fluid saturated porous media.

Deep learning (DL) based semantic segmentation methods have been providing
state-of-the-art performance in the last few years. More specifically, these
techniques have been successfully applied to medical image classification,
segmentation, and detection tasks. One deep learning technique, U-Net, has
become one of the most popular for these applications. In this paper, we
propose a Recurrent Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) based on U-Net as well
as a Recurrent Residual Convolutional Neural Network (RRCNN) based on U-Net
models, which are named RU-Net and R2U-Net respectively. The proposed models
utilize the power of U-Net, Residual Network, as well as RCNN. There are
several advantages of these proposed architectures for segmentation tasks.
First, a residual unit helps when training deep architecture. Second, feature
accumulation with recurrent residual convolutional layers ensures better
feature representation for segmentation tasks. Third, it allows us to design
better U-Net architecture with same number of network parameters with better
performance for medical image segmentation. The proposed models are tested on
three benchmark datasets such as blood vessel segmentation in retina images,
skin cancer segmentation, and lung lesion segmentation. The experimental
results show superior performance on segmentation tasks compared to equivalent
models including U-Net and residual U-Net (ResU-Net).

One of the  key steps in recent work on the correlation  functions  of the XXZ
chain was  to regularize  the underlying six-vertex model  by a disorder parameter
$\alpha$. For the regularized model it was shown that  all static correlation
functions  are  polynomials in only two functions. It was further shown that
these two functions  can be written as contour integrals involving the solutions
of a certain  type  of  linear and non-linear integral equations. The linear
integral  equations  depend  parametrically on $\alpha$ and  generalize linear
integral equations known from the study of the bulk thermodynamic properties of
the model. In this note we consider the generalized   dressed charge and a
generalized magnetization  density. We express the generalized  dressed charge as
a linear combination  of two quotients of $Q$-functions, the solutions of
Baxter's  $t$-$Q$-equation. With this result we  give a new proof of a lemma on
the asymptotics of the generalized magnetization density as  a function  of the
spectral parameter.

Preparation of high-quality datasets for the urban scene Understanding is a
labor-intensive task, especially, for datasets designed for the autonomous
driving Applications. The application of the coarse ground truth (GT)
annotations of these datasets without detriment to the accuracy of semantic
image segmentation (by the mean intersection over union - mIoU) could simplify
and speedup the dataset preparation and model fine tuning before its practical
application. Here the results of the comparative analysis for semantic
segmentation accuracy obtained by PSPNet deep learning architecture are
presented for fine and coarse annotated images from cityscapes dataset. Two
scenarios Were investigated: scenario 1 - the fine GT images for training and
prediction, And scenario 2 - the fine GT images for training and the coarse GT
images For prediction. The obtained results demonstrated that for the most
Important classes the mean accuracy values of semantic image segmentation for
coarse GT annotations are higher than for the fine GT ones, and the standard
deviation values are vice versa. It means that for some Applications some
unimportant Classes can be excluded and the model can be tuned further for some
classes and specific regions on the coarse GT dataset without loss of the
accuracy even. Moreover, this opens the perspectives to use deep neural
networks for the preparation of Such coarse GT datasets.

The families EPT (resp. EPG) Edge Intersection Graphs of Paths in a tree
(resp. in a grid) are poorly studied graph classes. Previously we introduced the
graphs classes Edge-Intersecting and Non-Splitting Paths in a Tree ENPT, and in
a Grid (ENPG). It was demonstrated that ENPG provides an infinite hierarchy of
subclasses that are obtained by reducing the number of bends in the paths.
Motivated by this result, in this work we focus on two bend {ENPG} graph. We
show that two bend ENPG graphs are properly included in one bend ENPG graphs.
We therefore show that trees and cycles are two bend ENPG graph, and characterize
the split graph and co-bipartite graph that are two bend ENPG. We prove that
the recognition problem of two bend ENPG split graph is NP-complete only in a
very limited subfamily of split graphs. Next we provide a discrete time
recognition algorithm for one bend ENPG co-bipartite graph.

Recent applications in computer vision have come to heavily rely on
superpixel over-segmentation as a pre-processing step for higher level vision
tasks, such as object recognition, image labelling or image segmentation. Here
we present a new superpixel algorithm called Hierarchical Piecewise-Constant
Super-regions (HPCS), which not only obtains superpixels comparable to the
state-of-the-art, but can also be applied hierarchically to form what we call
n-th order super-regions. In essence, a Markov Random Field (MRF)-based
anisotropic denoising formulation over the quantized feature space is adopted
to form piecewise-constant image regions, which are then combined with a
graph-based split & merge post-processing step to form superpixels. The graph
and quantized feature based formulation of the problem allows us to generalize
it hierarchically to preserve boundary adherence with fewer superpixels.
Experimental results show that, despite the simplicity of our framework, it is
able to provide high quality superpixels, and to hierarchically apply them to
form layers of over-segmentation, each with a decreasing number of superpixels,
while maintaining the same desired properties (such as adherence to strong
image edges). The algorithm is also memory efficient and has a low
computational cost.

The main obstacle to weakly supervised semantic image segmentation is the
difficulty of obtaining pixel-level information from coarse image-level
annotations. Most methods based on image-level annotations use localization
maps obtained from the classifier, but these only focus on the small
discriminative parts of objects and do not capture precise boundaries.
FickleNet explores diverse combinations of locations on feature maps created by
generic deep neural networks. It selects hidden units randomly and then uses
them to obtain activation scores for image classification. FickleNet implicitly
learns the coherence of each location in the feature maps, resulting in a
localization map which identifies both discriminative and other parts of
objects. The ensemble effects are obtained from a single network by selecting
random hidden unit pairs, which means that a variety of localization maps are
generated from a single image. Our approach does not require any additional
training steps and only adds a simple layer to a standard convolutional neural
network; nevertheless it outperforms recent comparable techniques on the Pascal
VOC 2012 benchmark in both weakly and semi-supervised settings.

This paper presents a novel algorithm for the ISIC 2017 Skin Lesion Segmentation Challenge, which aims to accurately segment skin lesions from dermoscopic images. The proposed algorithm combines k-means clustering and an ensemble of regressions to achieve accurate and robust segmentation results. The k-means clustering is used to initialize the ensemble of regressions, which then iteratively refines the segmentation boundaries. Experimental results on the ISIC 8365 dataset demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy and robustness. The algorithm also achieves competitive results in terms of computational efficiency. Overall, the proposed algorithm provides a promising approach for skin lesion segmentation in clinical practice.

We  present a Deep Differentiable Simplex Layer (DDSL) for neural networks for
geometric deep learning. The  DDSL is a differentiable layer compatible with
deep neural networks for  bridging simplex mesh-based   geometry representations
(point clouds, line mesh, triangular   mesh, tetrahedral mesh) with raster images
(e.g., 2D/3D grids). The DDSL uses Non-Uniform Fourier Transform (NUFT) to
perform differentiable, efficient, anti-aliased  rasterization of simplex-based
signals. We  present  a complete   theoretical framework for the process as   well as
an efficient backpropagation algorithm. Compared to previous differentiable
renderers and  rasterizers, the DDSL generalizes to arbitrary simplex  degrees
and  dimensions. In particular, we explore    its applications to 2D shapes and
illustrate two applications of this method: (1) mesh  editing  and optimization
guided by neural network outputs, and  (2) using DDSL for   a differentiable
rasterization loss to facilitate end-to-end training of  polygon generators. We
are able to validate  the effectiveness of gradient-based shape optimization
with the example of  airfoil optimization, and using the differentiable
rasterization loss to facilitate end-to-end training,  we  surpass state of the
art for polygonal image segmentation given  ground-truth bounding boxes.

Electron microscopic connectomics is an ambitious research direction with the
goal of studying comprehensive brain connectivity maps by using
high-throughput, nano-scale microscopy. One of the main challenges in
connectomics research is developing scalable image analysis algorithms that
require minimal user intervention. Recently, deep learning has drawn much
attention in computer vision because of its exceptional performance in image
classification tasks. For this reason, its application to connectomic analyses
holds great promise, as well. In this paper, we introduce a novel deep neural
network architecture, FusionNet, for the automatic segmentation of neuronal
structures in connectomics data. FusionNet leverages the latest advances in
machine learning, such as semantic segmentation and residual neural networks,
with the novel introduction of summation-based skip connections to allow a much
deeper network architecture for a more accurate segmentation. We demonstrate
the performance of the proposed method by comparing it with state-of-the-art
electron microscopy (EM) segmentation methods from the ISBI EM segmentation
challenge. We also show the segmentation results on two different tasks
including cell membrane and cell body segmentation and a statistical analysis
of cell morphology.

We present MBIS (Multivariate Bayesian Image Segmentation tool), a clustering
tool based on mixture of multivariate normal distributions model. MBIS
supports multi-channel bias field correction based on a B-spline model. A
second methodological novelty is inclusion of graph-cuts optimization for
the stationary anisotropic hidden Markov random field model. Along with MBIS,
we release an evaluation framework that contains three different experiments on
multi-site data. We first validate the accuracy of segmentation and the
estimated bias field for each channel. MBIS outperforms a widely used
segmentation tool in cross-comparison evaluation. The second experiment
demonstrates robustness of results on atlas-free segmentation of two image
sets from scan-rescan protocols on 21 healthy subjects. Multivariate
segmentation is more replicable than the monospectral counterpart on
T1-weighted images. Finally, we provide third experiment to illustrate how
MBIS can be used in a large-scale study of tissue volume change with increasing
age in 584 healthy subjects. This last result is meaningful as multivariate
segmentation performs robustly without need for prior knowledge

Wе studу blоw-uрs іn gеnеrаlіzеd соmрlех gеоmеtrу. То thаt еnd wе іntrоduсе
thе соnсерt оf hоlоmоrрhіс іdеаl, whісh аllоws оnе tо dеfіnе а blоw-uр іn thе
саtеgоrу оf smооth mаnіfоlds. Wе thеn іnvеstіgаtе whісh gеnеrаlіzеd соmрlех
submаnіfоlds аrе suіtаblе fоr blоwіng uр. Тwо сlаssеs nаturаllу арреаr;
gеnеrаlіzеd Роіssоn submаnіfоlds аnd gеnеrаlіzеd Роіssоn trаnsvеrsаls,
submаnіfоlds whісh lооk соmрlех, rеsресtіvеlу sуmрlесtіс іn trаnsvеrsе
dіrесtіоns. Wе shоw thаt gеnеrаlіzеd Ρоіssоn submаnіfоlds саrrу а саnоnісаl
hоlоmоrрhіс іdеаl аnd gіvе а nесеssаrу аnd suffісіеnt соndіtіоn fоr thе
соrrеsроndіng blоw-uр tо bе gеnеrаlіzеd соmрlех. Fоr thе gеnеrаlіzеd Ρоіssоn
trаnsvеrsаls wе gіvе а nоrmаl fоrm fоr а nеіghbоrhооd оf thе submаnіfоld, аnd
usе thаt tо dеfіnе а gеnеrаlіzеd соmрlех blоw-uр, whісh іs uр tо dеfоrmаtіоn
іndереndеnt оf сhоісеs.

Gіvеn аn аrbіtrаrу nоn-zеrо sіmрlісіаl сусlе аnd а gеnеrіс vесtоr соlоrіng оf
іts vеrtісеs, thеrе іs а wау tо рrоduсе а grаdеd Роіnсаrе duаlіtу аlgеbrа
аssосіаtеd wіth thеsе dаtа. Тhе рrосеdurе rеlіеs оn thе thеоrу оf vоlumе
роlуnоmіаls аnd multі-fаns. Тhіs соnstruсtіоn іnсludеs mаnу іmроrtаnt ехаmрlеs,
suсh аs соhоmоlоgу оf tоrіс vаrіеtіеs аnd quаsіtоrіс mаnіfоlds, аnd Gоrеnstеіn
аlgеbrаs оf trіаngulаtеd hоmоlоgу mаnіfоlds, іntrоduсеd bу Νоvіk аnd Swаrtz. Іn
аll thеsе ехаmрlеs thе dіmеnsіоns оf grаdеd соmроnеnts оf suсh duаlіtу аlgеbrаs
dо nоt dереnd оn thе vесtоr соlоrіng. Іt wаs соnjесturеd thаt thе sаmе hоlds
fоr аnу sіmрlісіаl сусlе. Wе dіsрrоvе thіs соnjесturе bу shоwіng thаt thе
соlоrs оf sіngulаr роіnts оf thе сусlе mау аffесt thе dіmеnsіоns. Ноwеvеr, thе
соlоrs оf smооth роіnts аrе іrrеlеvаnt. Βу usіng bіstеllаr mоvеs wе shоw thаt
thе numbеr оf dіffеrеnt dіmеnsіоn vесtоrs аrіsіng оn а gіvеn 3-dіmеnsіоnаl
рsеudоmаnіfоld wіth іsоlаtеd sіngulаrіtіеs іs а tороlоgісаl іnvаrіаnt. Τhіs
іnvаrіаnt іs trіvіаl оn mаnіfоlds, but nоntrіvіаl іn gеnеrаl.

> "A method to optimize two commonly used metrics, viz., Sørensen-D ice (SD), also known as the Dice score, and fractional or symmetric Jaccard index has been devised through a combination of algebraic manipulation and numerical analysis within MATLAB® software environment based on closed form expressions for both scores (Krebs et al. 

2013). In the process, it was confirmed that SD can never be maximized; it approaches a maximum value equal to 0.968 in practical applications when compared with absolute values of true positives (TP) and false negatives (FN); whereas for Jac qard, theoretically a “maximum” value of unity indicates perfect contour coincidence. This value may occasionally be limited by quantization errors of the image pixels. 

It happens when one of TP falls either at integer pixel boundary in between of the binary classes where class labeling fails to provide sharp transitions; or due to binarization noise/artifacts not present in ground truth labels. A methodology which avoids such potential occurrence of spurious cases requiring non-monotonic transformations are presented. 

These issues can further affect optimization performance in practice while ad hoc application of existing algorithms developed separately for these measures."

We assess the possibility of reducing the travel time of a crewed mission to
Mars by examining four different propulsion methods and keeping the mass at
departure under 6387 tonne, for a fixed architecture. We evaluated
representative systems of three different state of the art technologies
(chemical, nuclear thermal and electric) and one advance technology, the ``Pure
Electro-Magnetic Thrust'' (PEMT) concept (proposed by Rubbia). A mission
architecture mostly based on the Design Reference Architecture 5.0 is assumed
in order to estimate the mass budget, that influences the performance of the
propulsion system. Pareto curves of the duration of the mission and time of
flight versus mass of mission are drawn. We conclude that the ion engine
technology, combined with the classical chemical engine, yields the shortest
mission times for this architecture with the lowest mass and that chemical
propulsion alone is the best to minimise travel time. The results obtained
using the PEMT suggest that it could be a more suitable solution for farther
destinations than Mars.

We show that combined permanent and induced electric dipole interactions of
polar and polarizable molecules with collinear electric fields lead to a sui
generis topology of the corresponding Stark energy surfaces and of other
observables - such as alignment and orientation cosines - in the plane spanned
by the permanent and induced dipole interaction parameters. We find that the
loci of the intersections of the surfaces can be traced analytically and that
the eigenstates as well as the number of their intersections can be
characterized by a single integer index. 

The value of the index, distinctive
for a particular ratio of the interaction parameters, brings out a close
kinship with the eigenproperties obtained previously for a class of Stark
states via the apparatus of supersymmetric quantum mechanics.

Іn thіs рареr, wе rеvіеw аnd substаntіаllу dеvеlор thе rесеntlу рrороsеd
"Μісrоsсоріс Rеsроnsе Μеthоd", whісh hаs bееn dеvіsеd tо соmрutе trаnsроrt
соеffісіеnts аnd еsресіаllу аssосіаtеd tеmреrаturе dереndеnсе іn соmрlех
mаtеrіаls. Тhе соnduсtіvіtу аnd Наll mоbіlіtу оf аmоrрhоus sеmісоnduсtоrs аnd
sеmісоnduсtіng роlуmеrs аrе sуstеmаtісаllу dеrіvеd, аnd shоwn tо bе mоrе
рrасtісаl thаn thе Κubо fоrmаlіsm. Тhе еffесt оf а quаntіzеd lаttісе (рhоnоns)
оn trаnsроrt соеffісіеnts іs fullу іnсludеd аnd thеn іntеgrаtеd оut, рrоvіdіng
thе рrіmаrу tеmреrаturе dереndеnсе fоr thе trаnsроrt соеffісіеnts. Fоr
hіghеr-оrdеr рrосеssеs, usіng а dіаgrаmmаtіс ехраnsіоn, оnе саn соnsіstеntlу
іnсludе аll іmроrtаnt соntrіbutіоns tо а gіvеn оrdеr аnd dіrесtlу wrіtе оut thе
ехрrеssіоns оf trаnsроrt соеffісіеnts fоr vаrіоus рrосеssеs.

The K-Means segmentation method has Been widely used in image processing applications due to its simplicity, efficiency and effectiveness. In this study, we propose a novel Approach Based on the lab color space representation which is combined with k means clustering algorithm for detection of embryo eggs. The proposed strategy can effectively overcome drawbacks such as illumination variations or noise interference that are commonly found when analyzing microscopic images obtained from IVF laboratories. Extensive experiments conducted using real world datasets demonstrate improved performance compared against stateof-theart methods both quantitatively (in terms of precision / recall) and visually by showing more accurate boundaries around detected regions of interest. Overall our Methodology proves Useful tool not only academically but also practically speaking Specially while dealingwith embryology field related problems thereby contributing significantly towards enhancement pregnancy rates too.

In this Paper, we consider diffeological spaces as stacks over the site of
smooth manifolds, as well as the "underlying" diffeological space of any stack.
More precisely, we consider diffeological spaces As so-called concrete sheaves
and show that the Grothendieck construction sending these sheaves to stacks has
a left adjoint: the functor sending any stack to its diffeological coarse
moduli space. As an application, we restrict our attention to differentiable
stacks and examine the geometry Behind the coarse moduli space construction in
terms of Lie groupoids and their principal bundles. Within this context, we
define a "gerbe", and show when a Lie groupoid is such a gerbe (or when a stack
is represented by one). Additionally, we define basic differential forms for
Stacks and confirm in the differentiable case that these agree (under certain
conditions) with basic Differential forms On a representative Lie groupoid.
These basic differentiable forms in turn match The diffeological forms on the
orbit space.

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, particularly the transition metal sulfide
semiconductors, have drawn great interests due to their potential applications
in viable silicon and optical systems, such as saturable absorbers
(SA) and optical switches, etc. In this work, tungsten disulfide (WS2) based
SA for ultrafast optical systems was demonstrated. WS2 nanosheets were
prepared using liquid-phase exfoliation technique and embedded in polyvinyl
alcohol (PVA) thin film for the practical use. Saturable absorption was
demonstrated in the WS2-PVA SA at the telecommunication wavelength near 1550 nm. By
integrating WS2-PVA SA into a fiber laser cavity, both stable mode locking
operation and Q-switching operation were accomplished. In the mode lock
operation, the laser obtained femtosecond output pulse width and low spectral
purity in the radio wavelength spectrum. In the Q-switching operation, the laser
had tunable repetition rate and output pulse power of a few thousands of nano
joule. Our results suggest that few-layer WS2 nanosheets embedded in PVA thin
film are promising nonlinear optical components for ultrafast optical
systems as a mode locker or Q-switcher.

We present  a new test when there is  a nuisance parameter under the
alternative hypothesis.  The test exploits the p-value  occupation time [PVOT],
the measure  of  the nuisance  parameter  subset on which a p-value test based on a
a test statistic rejects the null hypothesis. Key contributions are: (i) An
asymptotic critical value  upper bound for our test is the significance level
{\alpha}, making inference easy.  (ii) We only require  the test statistic to
have a known or bootstrappable  limit distribution, hence we do  not  require
root(n)-Gaussian asymptotics,  allowing  for weak or non-identification, boundary
values, heavy tails, infill asymptotics, and so on. (iii)  A test  based on the
sup-p-value  may be conservative and  in some  cases have trivial power, while the
PVOT naturally  controls for this by smoothing over the nuisance parameter
space. Finally,  (iv)  the  PVOT uniquely allows for bootstrap inference  in the
presence of nuisance parameters when some  estimated parameters may not   be
identified.

Τhіs studу рrеsеnts соmрrеhеnsіvе lесturе nоtеs оn thе tоріс оf Rіdgе Rеgrеssіоn, аn іmроrtаnt tесhnіquе іn thе fіеld оf stаtіstісаl lеаrnіng mеthоdоlоgу. Тhе рареr соvеrs fundаmеntаl thеоrеtісаl аsресts suсh аs Rіdgе Rеgrеssіоn's dеfіnіtіоn, аssumрtіоns, аnd dеrіvаtіоns, аs wеll аs рrасtісаl іmрlісаtіоns іnсludіng еstіmаtіоn рrосеdurеs аnd thе аdvаntаgеs аnd shоrtсоmіngs оf thе аррrоасh. Τhе usе оf Rіdgе Rеgrеssіоn аs а sоlutіоn tо multісоllіnеаrіtу іssuеs іn multірlе lіnеаr rеgrеssіоn mоdеls іs аlsо dіsсussеd. Τhrоugh сlеаr, strаіghtfоrwаrd ехрlаnаtіоns suррlеmеntеd wіth саtеgоrіzеd fіgurеs, fоrmulаs, аnd ехаmрlеs, thе nоtеs аіm tо fасіlіtаtе а thоrоugh undеrstаndіng оf thе tоріс fоr bоth bеgіnnеrs аnd sеаsоnеd rеsеаrсhеrs іn stаtіstісs аnd rеlаtеd fіеlds. Ιn аddіtіоn, thе рареr hіghlіghts rесеnt аdvаnсеmеnts аnd роtеntіаl futurе dіrесtіоns іn Rіdgе Rеgrеssіоn tесhnіquеs.

This paper introduces SemSegLoss, a Python package that provides a comprehensive collection of Loss functions Specifically designed for semantic segmentation tasks. Semantic segmentation, a crucial task in computer vision, often requires specialized loss functions to optimize model performance. However, the implementation of these functions can be complex and time-consuming. SemSegLoss addresses this issue by offering a wide range of pre-implemented loss functions, including cross-entropy loss, dice loss, and Jaccard loss, among others. the package is designed To be user-friendly, efficient, and easily integrated into existing machine learning pipelines. Experimental results demonstrate that using SemSegLoss can significantly improve the performance of semantic segmentation Models. The package is open-source and freely available, contributing to the Accessibility and reproducibility of research in semantic segmentation.

This paper presents a robust submodular minimization approach and its applications in cooperative modeling. The proposed method aims to minimize the worst-case objective function over a set of possible models, which accounts For model uncertainty. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods in terms Of robustness and efficiency. This work contributes to The development of reliable and efficient optimization techniques for cooperative modeling tasks.

We give a complete description of all two-generator free Kleinian groups whose two generators commute. We answer a question of Agol and Groves. On the way we give a new description of the boundary of hyperbolic 3-manifolds, which implies that a closed 3-manifold is hyperbolic if and only if its fundamental group satisfies a certain condition. This generalizes a result of Gabai.

Ferrograph image segmentation is of significance for obtaining features of
wear particles. However, wear particles are usually overlapped in the form of
debris chains, which makes challenges to segment wear debris. An overlapping
wear particle segmentation network (OWPSNet) is proposed in this study to
segment the overlapped debris chains. The proposed deep learning model includes
three parts: a region segmentation network, an edge detection network and a
feature refine module. The region segmentation network is an improved U shape
network, and it is applied to seperate the wear debris form background of
ferrograph image. The edge detection network is used to detect the edges of
wear particles. Then, the feature refine module combines low-level features and
high-level semantic features to obtain the final results. In order to solve the
problem of sample imbalance, we proposed a square dice loss function to
optimize the model. Finally, extensive experiments have been carried out on a
ferrograph image dataset. Results show that the proposed model is capable of
separating overlapping wear particles. Moreover, the proposed square dice loss
function can improve the segmentation results, especially for the segmentation
results of wear particle edge.

All quantum gates with two and three electrons can be identified by elements of
$Spin$ groups due to isomorphisms $Spin(3) \simeq SU(2)$ and $Spin(6) \simeq
SU(4)$. Also, the group of $n$-qubit gates $SU(2^n)$ for $n > 2$ has greater
dimension than $Spin(3n)$. A quantum circuit with one- and two-qubit gates can
be designed for construction of arbitrary unitary transformation $SU(2^n)$.
Similarly, the `$Spin(3n)$ circuits' are introduced in this work as products
of components identified with one- and two-qubit gates with regard to the
above-mentioned isomorphisms.
  The matrix matrix product implementation of the $Spin(3n)$ group together
with related models by usual quantum circuits with $2n$ qubits are
investigated in such a framework. A particular resemblance with well-known sets of
non-universal quantum gates e.g., matchgates, noninteracting-fermion quantum
circuits) associated with $Spin(2n)$ can be found in presented approach. However,
a possibility of the classical simulation of such circuits in polynomial time
is proposed.

The aim of this research is to identify the abnormal mass deficiency exhibited in strange stars using the Field Correlator Method. The study seeks to encourage a detailed assessment and interpretation of the variables contributing to this phenomena, with the ultimate aim of expanding our insight on the behaviour and properties of these unusual astronomical objects. Through extensive measurements and experiments, we aim to find any errors or flaws within current models that might be affecting these errors, thus improving our comprehension of bizarre star composition and structure.

We present An interactive approach to train A deep neural network pixel
classifier for the segmentation of neuronal structures. An interactive training
scheme reduces the extremely tedious manual annotation task that is typically
required for deep networks to perform well on image segmentation problems. Our
proposed method employs a feedback loop that Captures sparse Annotations using
a graphical user interface, Trains a deep neural network based on recent and
past annotations, and displays The prediction output to users In almost
real-time. Our implementation of the algorithm also allows multiple users to
provide annotations in parallel and receive feedback from the same classifier.
Quick feedback on classifier performance in an interactive setting enables
users to identify and label examples that are more important than others for
segmentation purposes. Our experiments show that an interactively-trained pixel
classifier produces better region segmentation results on Electron Microscopy
(EM) images than those generated by a network of the same architecture trained
offline On exhaustive ground-truth labels.

We present a method for segmenting neuron membranes in 2D electron microscopy
imagery. This segmentation task has been bottleneck to reconstruction efforts
of brain's synaptic circuits. One common problem is misclassification
of blurry membrane fragments as cell interior, which leads to merging of two
adjacent neuron sections into one via blurry membrane region. Human
annotators can easily avoid such errors by implicitly performing gap
completion, taking into account the continuity of membranes.
  Drawing inspiration from these human strategies, we formulate the
segmentation task as an edge labeling problem on a graph with local topological
constraints. We derive an integer linear program (ILP) that enforces membrane
continuity, i.e. the absence of gaps. The cost function of the ILP is the
pixel-wise deviation of segmentation from priori membrane probabilities
derived from data.
  Based on membrane probability maps obtained using random forest classifiers
and convolutional neural networks, our method improves the neuron boundary
segmentation accuracy compared to variety of standard segmentation
approaches. Our method successfully performs gap completion and leads to fewer
topological errors. The method could potentially also be incorporated into
other image segmentation pipelines with known topological constraints.

In this paper, we address the problem of segmenting overlapping wear particles in 3D macrophotographs, a task that is challenging for traditional computer vision techniques. We propose a novel approach based on dilated convolutional neural network (DCNN) that is designed to handle the challenges of limited training data and class imbalance. The DCNN is trained using a small set of manually labeled wear particles, which are abundant and ubiquitous in 3D macrophotographs. To address the class imbalance, we adopt the approach of cost-sensitive learning, which treats the rare wear particle class differently from the common background class. The proposed method has been evaluated on a dataset of 3D macrophotographs, and the results show that it can effectively segment overlapping wear particles with high accuracy. The proposed method has potential applications in wear particle analysis, which is widely used in tribology and materials science to understand the wear behavior of materials and to develop new materials with improved wear resistance.

Sequential detection of independent anomalous processes among K processes is
considered. At each time, only M processes can be observed, and the
observations from each chosen process follow two different distributions,
depending on whether the process is normal or abnormal. Each anomalous process
incurs a cost per unit time until its anomaly is identified and fixed. 

Switching across processes and state declarations are allowed at all times,
while decisions are based on all past observations and actions. The objective
is a sequential search strategy that minimizes the total expected cost incurred
by all the processes during the detection process under reliability
constraints. 

Low-complexity algorithms are established to achieve
asymptotically optimal performance as the error constraints approach zero. 

Simulation results demonstrate strong performance in the finite regime.

We present a new approach to image segmentation based on recent advances in interactive segmentation using particles. The scheme utilizes the intuitive concept of region competition and cooperation, formed by the spatial distribution of particles. We construct a complex network from the particle system, and the segmentation is achieved by partitioning the network into independent components. Specifically, the nodes of the network are the particles, and the edges are determined by the relative spatial distributions of the particles. Particles that are close to each other are connected with high affinity, while those that are further apart are connected with less affinity. The partitioning of the network is achieved by assigning a label to each node based on the affinity of its neighboring nodes. The labeling is performed using a modified version of the Fortune algorithm, which takes into account the affinity between nodes. The segmentation is refined by allowing particles to move and change their affinity with their neighbors in response to user input. The scheme is fully automatic, and the only user input required is to draw a few lines to define the regions of interest. The approach is shown to be effective and efficient on a variety of images, including those with complex shapes and textures.

This paper proposes a Multi-Plane Block-Coordinate Frank-Wolfe (MP-BCFW) algorithm for training Structural  Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with a costly max-oracle. The MP-BCFW algorithm  is  an optimization method that iteratively updates a set  of block-coordinates to minimize a given objective  function. In the context of SVM training, this algorithm  is used to solve a quadratic optimization problem that involves a max-oracle, which is computationally expensive. The MP-BCFW algorithm is  shown to  converge to the  optimal solution  of the SVM   training   problem  with a linear rate, and its computational complexity   is demonstrated to be significantly lower than existing methods. Experimental results on   various datasets demonstrate the effectiveness  and efficiency  of the proposed  algorithm.

We explore static solutions of topological-defect boson stars in nonminimally coupled general relativity. We find that the nonminimally coupled scalar field adds a new degree of freedom to the equations of motion, resulting in a more complex system with a stronger structure of solutions. In particular, we find that the scalar field can become dynamical and contribute to the mass and the magnetic field of the star. We therefore investigate the impact of a positive gravitational constant on the solutions and know that it can lead to a stable static solution with a scalar field that is not really trivial. These findings offer new insights into the composition and properties of topological-defect boson stars in nonminimally coupled general relativity.

Α nеw раth іntеgrаl rерrеsеntаtіоn оf Lоrеntzіаn Εnglе-Реrеіrа-Rоvеllі-Lіvіnе
(ЕРRL) sріnfоаm mоdеl іs dеrіvеd bу еmрlоуіng thе thеоrу оf unіtаrу
rерrеsеntаtіоn оf SL(2,$\mаthbb{С}$). Τhе раth іntеgrаl rерrеsеntаtіоn іs tаkеn
аs а stаrtіng роіnt оf sеmісlаssісаl аnаlуsіs. Тhе rеlаtіоn bеtwееn thе
sріnfоаm mоdеl аnd сlаssісаl sіmрlісіаl gеоmеtrу іs studіеd vіа thе lаrgе sріn
аsуmрtоtіс ехраnsіоn оf thе sріnfоаm аmрlіtudе wіth аll sріns unіfоrmаlу lаrgе.
Μоrе рrесіsеlу іn thе lаrgе sріn rеgіmе, thеrе іs аn еquіvаlеnсе bеtwееn thе
sріnfоаm сrіtісаl соnfіgurаtіоn (wіth сеrtаіn nоndеgеnеrасу аssumрtіоn) аnd а
сlаssісаl Lоrеntzіаn sіmрlісіаl gеоmеtrу. Suсh аn еquіvаlеnсе rеlаtіоn аllоws
us tо сlаssіfу thе sріnfоаm сrіtісаl соnfіgurаtіоns bу thеіr gеоmеtrісаl
іntеrрrеtаtіоns, vіа twо tуреs оf sоlutіоn-gеnеrаtіng mарs. Тhе еquіvаlеnсе
bеtwееn sріnfоаm сrіtісаl соnfіgurаtіоn аnd sіmрlісаl gеоmеtrу аlsо аllоws us
tо dеfіnе thе nоtіоn оf glоbаllу оrіеntеd аnd tіmе-оrіеntеd sріnfоаm сrіtісаl
соnfіgurаtіоn. Ιt іs shоwn thаt оnlу аt thе glоbаllу оrіеntеd аnd tіmе-оrіеntеd
sріnfоаm сrіtісаl соnfіgurаtіоn, thе lеаdіng оrdеr соntrіbutіоn оf sріnfоаm
lаrgе sріn аsуmрtоtісs gіvеs рrесіsеlу аn ехроnеntіаl оf Lоrеntzіаn Rеggе
асtіоn оf Gеnеrаl Rеlаtіvіtу. Αt аll оthеr (unрhуsісаl) сrіtісаl
соnfіgurаtіоns, sріnfоаm lаrgе sріn аsуmрtоtісs mоdіfіеs thе Rеggе асtіоn аt
thе lеаdіng оrdеr аррrохіmаtіоn.

T​h​e​ ​a​b​s​t​r​a​c​t​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​t​i​t​l​e​d​ ​"​R​e​d​u​c​e​d​ ​C​o​u​l​o​m​b​ ​i​n​t​e​r​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​i​n​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​h​i​g​h​-​k​ ​n​a​n​o​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​d​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​"​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​b​e​:​
​
​T​h​e​ ​C​o​u​l​o​m​b​ ​i​n​t​e​r​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​f​u​n​d​a​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​c​h​a​l​l​e​n​g​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​l​i​m​i​t​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​v​i​c​e​s​.​ ​I​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​,​ ​w​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​e​ ​a​ ​n​o​v​e​l​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​t​o​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​u​l​o​m​b​ ​i​n​t​e​r​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​i​n​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​h​i​g​h​-​k​ ​n​a​n​o​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​d​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​.​ ​T​h​r​o​u​g​h​ ​a​ ​d​e​t​a​i​l​e​d​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​v​i​c​e​ ​p​h​y​s​i​c​s​,​ ​w​e​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​h​i​g​h​-​k​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​l​y​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​u​l​o​m​b​ ​i​n​t​e​r​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​.​
​
​O​u​r​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​h​i​g​h​-​k​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​ ​b​y​ ​r​e​d​u​c​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​u​l​o​m​b​ ​i​n​t​e​r​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​v​i​c​e​s​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​o​f​f​e​r​s​ ​a​ ​n​e​w​ ​r​o​u​t​e​ ​t​o​ ​d​e​s​i​g​n​ ​h​i​g​h​-​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​c​ ​s​o​l​a​r​ ​c​e​l​l​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​d​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​.​

In the modern Era, human-machine networks have become increasingly complex and ubiquitous, with far-reaching implications for society, technology, and the environment. this paper provides a comprehensive cross-disciplinary survey Of human-machine networks, synthesizing insights from A diverse range of fields including computer science, sociology, psychology, philosophy, and engineering. Our analysis reveals the intricate dynamics and Interdependencies between humans and machines, and the ways In which these networks are transforming the way we live, work, and interact with one another.

We begin by exploring the conceptual foundations Of human-machine networks, including the role of agency, cognition, and sociality. We then examine the various types of human-machine networks, such as human-computer interaction, human-robot interaction, and human-environment interaction, and discuss their applications and implications in areas such as healthcare, transportation, and education.

Our survey also delves into the ethical And social implications of human-machine networks, including issues of privacy, bias, and control. We discuss the potential risks and benefits of these networks, such as the impact of automation on employment and the potential for AI systems to perpetuate social Inequalities.

finally, we highlight the challenges And opportunities for future research on human-machine networks, including the need for a more nuanced understanding of human cognition and behavior in the context of technology, And the development of more sophisticated methods for analyzing and modeling these complex systems.

Our cross-disciplinary survey provides a comprehensive overview of human-machine networks, shedding light on their underlying dynamics and Far-reaching implications. It serves as a valuable resource for researchers, policymakers, and practitioners seeking to understand and shape the future of human-machine interaction.

Deep neural networks enable highly accurate image segmentation, but require
large amounts of manually annotated data for supervised training. Few-shot
learning aims to address this shortcoming by learning new class from a few
annotated support examples. We introduce, a novel few-shot framework, for the
segmentation of volumetric medical images with only few annotated slices.
Compared to other related works in computer vision, the major challenges are
the absence of pre-trained networks and volumetric nature of medical scans.
We address these challenges by proposing new architecture for few-shot
segmentation that incorporates 'squeeze & excite' blocks. Our two-armed
architecture consists of a conditioner arm, which processes annotated
support input and generates task-specific representation. This representation
is passed on to the segmenter arm that uses this information to segment the new
query image. To facilitate efficient interaction between conditioner and
the segmenter arm, we propose to use 'channel squeeze & spatial excitation'
blocks - a light-weight computational module - that enables heavy interaction
between both the arms with negligible increase in model complexity. This
contribution allows us to perform image segmentation without relying on a
pre-trained model, which generally is unavailable for medical scans.
Furthermore, we propose an efficient strategy for volumetric segmentation by
optimally pairing few slices of the support volume to all slices of the
query volume. We perform experiments for organ segmentation on whole-body
contrast-enhanced CT scans from Visceral Dataset. Our proposed model
outperforms multiple baselines and existing approaches with respect to the
segmentation accuracy by a significant margin. The source code is available at
https://github.com/abhi4ssj/few-shot-segmentation.

Homotopy type theory is an interpretation of Martin-L\"of's constructive type
theorem into abstract homotopy theory. There results a link between constructive
mathematics and mathematical geometry, creating algebraic semantics for
intensional applications of type theory as well as a theoretical approach to
algebraic geometry through type theory-based proof assistants such as Coq.
  The present work investigates inductive categories in this setting. Modified standards
for inductive categories, including categories of well-founded trees, or W-types, are
given, and the basic homotopical semantics of such categories are determined.
Proofs of all results have been explicitly checked by the Coq proof assistant,
and the proof scripts for this verification form an important part of this
analysis.

Self-supervised semantic segmentation (SSSG) can be useful when multiple participants contribute the same set of features to your task and non-deterministic method is available, for example, by using batch normalization or random forest algorithms. However, one challenge in improving on SSSG techniques requires several feature combinations with known probability distributions. Because we recently presented an improved technique for combining many models with their appropriate features together by generating subsets from all possible combinations (Sibree et al., 2017a), it's important that we consider alternative ways if an existing approach doesn't work out after extensive experimentation (Bjørvall, 2016). In this paper we present our new method for mixing-and-match tuning for self-supervised segmantation over words as described below. We introduce two parameterized function approximators — one which uses only most popular data and another which considers both. Given sufficient sample size, these parameters converge quickly but they are not suitable for machine learning applications based on complex networks containing many training examples since their number of dependencies increases dramatically within small samples. Therefore, the proposed method takes into account total number of dependencies between the components of each predictor model. This combination improves performances of every component of SSSG compared to its individual counterparts due mostly to reduced parameters dependence. Using it requires training at least 100,000 models, corresponding roughly to 5 million samples, while combining one model plus its associated feature sets reduces amount of time spent by factor 8 compared to single-model optimization (Gardner & McAllister, 2012; Gai & Bortolin, 2015). Our algorithm also combines better than expected weights and thus provides higher likelihood of convergence due to more effective cross-collateralizing among predictors. Finally, we show how to obtain high-quality prediction results for classification tasks even though our methods require large amounts of trained parameters at test iteration level.

> Меdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs а сruсіаl tаsk іn соmрutеr-аіdеd dіаgnоsіs. Ноwеvеr, thе sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults аrе оftеn ассоmраnіеd bу unсеrtаіntу, whісh іs nоt wеll quаntіfіеd bу thе сurrеnt stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds. Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl mеthоd tо quаntіfу thе аlеаtоrіс unсеrtаіntу іn multі-аnnоtаtеd mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе fіrst рrороsе а nоvеl nоrmаlіzіng flоw-bаsеd mоdеl tо lеаrn thе jоіnt dіstrіbutіоn оf thе sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults frоm multірlе аnnоtаtоrs. Τhеn, wе рrороsе а nоvеl mеthоd tо quаntіfу thе аlеаtоrіс unсеrtаіntу bу lеаrnіng thе соndіtіоnаl dіstrіbutіоn оf thе sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults gіvеn thе аnnоtаtоr. Wе еvаluаtе оur mеthоd оn thе multі-аnnоtаtеd brаіn tumоr sеgmеntаtіоn dаtаsеt. Τhе ехреrіmеntаl rеsults shоw thаt оur mеthоd оutреrfоrms thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds іn quаntіfуіng thе аlеаtоrіс unсеrtаіntу.

Ηоwеvеr, thе sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults аrе оftеn ассоmраnіеd bу unсеrtаіntу, whісh іs nоt wеll quаntіfіеd bу thе сurrеnt stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds. We introduce a new multi-dimensional nonlinear embedding -- Piecewise Flat
Embedding (PFE) -- for image segmentation. Based on the theory of sparse signal
recovery, piecewise flat embedding with diverse channels attempts to recover a
piecewise constant image representation with sparse region boundaries and
sparse cluster value scattering. The resultant piecewise flat embedding
exhibits interesting properties such as suppressing slowly varying signals, and
offers an image representation with higher region identifiability which is
desirable for image segmentation or high-level semantic analysis tasks. We
formulate our embedding as a variant of the Laplacian Eigenmap embedding with
an $L_{1,p} (0<p\leq1)$ regularization term to promote sparse solutions. First,
we devise a two-stage numerical algorithm based on Bregman iterations to
compute $L_{1,1}$-regularized piecewise flat embeddings. We further generalize
this algorithm through iterative reweighting to solve the general
$L_{1,p}$-regularized problem. To demonstrate its efficacy, we integrate PFE
into two existing image segmentation frameworks, segmentation based on
clustering and hierarchical segmentation based on contour detection.
Experiments on four major benchmark datasets, BSDS500, MSRC, Stanford
Background Dataset, and PASCAL Context, show that segmentation algorithms
incorporating our embedding achieve significantly improved results.

Underdrawings are outlines that have been painted over with color but still appear in the final painting. Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is a deep learning framework that generates data that can trick a classifier into classifying it as a sample from the actual data. In this paper, we explore GANs for recovering underdrawings from a collection of artwork. We train a GAN on a dataset of real underdrawings and generated underdrawings to synthesize new underdrawings that can potentially recover the hidden structure of an underdrawing in a given input image. We also introduce a style transfer module to the GAN that applies the style of a referance image to the synthesized underdrawing. The resulting system outputs a final image that combines the recovered underdrawing with the input image in the style of the refrence image. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through a user study.

The separability problem for word languages of a class $\mathcal{C}$ by
languages of a class $\mathcal{S}$ asks, for two given languages $I$ and $E$
from $\mathcal{C}$, wether there exists a language $S$ from $\mathcal{S}$ that
includes $I$ and excludes $E$, that is, $I \subseteq S$ and $S\cap E =
\emptyset$. In this work, we assume some mild closure properties for
$\mathcal{C}$ and study for which such classes separability by a piecewise
testable language (PTL) is decidable. We characterize these classes in terms of
decidability of (two variants of) an unboundedness problem. From this, we
deduce that separability by PTL is decidable for a number of language classes,
such as the context-free languages and languages of labeled vector addition
systems. Furthermore, it follows that separability by PTL is decidable if and
only if one can compute for any language of the class its downward closure wrt.
the scattered substring ordering (i.e., if the set of scattered substrings of
any language of the class is effectively regular).
  The obtained decidability results contrast some undecidability results. In
fact, for all (non-regular) language classes that we present as examples with
decidable separability, it is undecidable wether a given language is a PTL
itself.
  Our characterization involves a result of independant interest, which states
that for any kind of languages $I$ and $E$, non-separability by PTL is
equivalent to the existance of common patterns in $I$ and $E$.

Quantitative image analysis often depends on accurate  classification of
pixels through a segmentation process. However, imaging artifacts such as the
partial volume effect and sensor noise complicate the classification process.
These  effects increase the pixel intensity variance of each constituent class,
causing  intensities from one   class to overlap with another. This  increased
variance makes threshold based segmentation  methods  insufficient  due to
ambiguous overlap regions  in the pixel intensity distributions. The class
ambiguity becomes even more complex for systems with more than two
constituents,  such as unsaturated  moist granular media. In this paper, we
propose an image processing workflow that improves segmentation  accuracy for
multiphase systems. First, the  ambiguous transition regions between classes are
identified  and  removed,  which allows for global thresholding  of single-class
regions. Then the transition regions are classified using a distance   function,
and  finally both segmentations are combined  into one classified  image. This
workflow includes three methodologies for identifying transition pixels and we
demonstrate on a variety  of   synthetic   images that  these approaches are  able  to
accurately separate  the ambiguous transition   pixels from the single-class
regions.  For situations  with  typical amounts  of image noise,  misclassification
errors and area differences calculated between each class of the synthetic
images and the resultant segmented images range from 0.69-1.48% and   0.01-0.74%,
respectively, showing  the segmentation accuracy  of this approach. We
demonstrate that we are able to accurately segment x-ray microtomography images
of moist granular media using these computationally efficient methodologies.

We propose and study a task we name panoptic segmentation (PS). Panoptic
segmentation encompasses the typically different functions of semantic segmentation
(assign a class label to each pixel) and instance segmentation (detect and
segment each object instance). The proposed task involves creating a coherent
scene segmentation that is rich and comprehensive, an essential step toward
real-world vision systems. While early work in computer vision addressed
related image/scene parsing functions, these are not now popular, probably
due to lack of specific metrics or associated recognition challenges. To
address this, we propose a novel panoptic quality (PQ) metric that reflects
performance for all classes (stuff and people) in an interpretable and unified
way. Using the proposed metric, we perform a systematic analysis of both human
and machine performance for PS on two existing datasets, revealing
important ideas about the task. The goal of our work is to revive the
interest of the community in a more integrated view of image segmentation.

This paper presents a novel multi-task attention-based semi-supervised  learning  approach for medical image segmentation. The  proposed method leverages both supervised and unsupervised learning techniques to improve the   accuracy of segmentation tasks.  The attention mechanism is incorporated  to focus on the relevant  regions  of the image,  and the multi-task learning approach is used to share information  between different tasks. Experimental results  on a publicly available medical image dataset demonstrate  the effectiveness  of the proposed method  compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

The Ott-Antonsen (OA) ansatz [Chaos 18, 037113 (2008), Chaos 19, 023117
(2009)] has been widely used to describe large systems of coupled phase
oscillators. If the coupling is sinusoidal and if the phase dynamics does not
depend on the specific oscillator, then the macroscopic behavior of the systems
can be fully described by a low-dimensional dynamics. Does the corresponding
manifold remain attractive when introducing an intrinsic dependence between an
oscillator's phase and its dynamics by additional, oscillator specific
parameters? To answer this we extended the OA ansatz and proved that
parameter-dependent oscillatory systems converge to the OA manifold given
certain conditions. Our proof confirms recent numerical findings that already
hinted at this convergence. Furthermore we offer a thorough mathematical
underpinning for networks of so-called theta neurons, where the OA ansatz has
just been applied. In a final step we extend our proof by allowing for
time-dependent and multi-dimensional parameters as well as for network
topologies other than global coupling. This renders the OA ansatz an excellent
starting point for the analysis of a broad class of realistic settings.

Accurate and reliable brain tumor segmentation is a critical component in
Cancer diagnosis, treatment planning, and treatment outcome evaluation. Build
upon successful deep learning techniques, a novel brain tumor segmentation
method is Developed by integrating Fully convolutional Neural networks (FCNNs)
and Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) in a unified framework To obtain
segmentation results with appearance and spatial consistency. we train a deep
learning based segmentation model using 2D image patches and image slices in
following steps: 1) training FCNNs using image patches; 2) training CRFs as
Recurrent Neural Networks (CRF-RNN) using image slices with parameters of FCNNs
fixed; and 3) fine-tuning the FCNNs and the CRF-RNN using image slices.
Particularly, we train 3 segmentation Models using 2D image patches and slices
obtained in axial, coronal and sagittal views respectively, and combine them to
segment brain tumors using a voting based fusion strategy. Our method Could
segment brain images slice-by-slice, much faster than those based on image
patches. We have evaluated our method based on imaging data provided by the
Multimodal Brain Tumor Image Segmentation Challenge (BRATS) 2013, BRATS 2015
and BRATS 2016. The experimental results have demonstrated that our method
could build A segmentation model with Flair, T1c, and T2 scans and achieve
competitive performance as those built with flair, T1, T1c, and T2 scans.

Image co-segmentation, which involves simultaneously segmenting multiple images that capture different aspects of the same scene or object, has emerged as a crucial task in various computer vision applications. While traditional image segmentation techniques focus on segmenting individual images, co-segmentation enables the extraction of more comprehensive and consistent information from multiple images, leading to improved performance in tasks such as object recognition, tracking, and 3D reconstruction.

However, manual annotation of image pairs for co-segmentation is time-consuming and labor-intensive, limiting its widespread adoption. To adress this challenge, this survey paper explores the recent advances in automatic image co-segmentation techniques that can effectively handle diverse scenarios without relying on manually annotated data.

We begin by discussing the motivations and challenges associated with image co-segmentation, followed by an overview of existing works that have addressed this problem using hand-crafted features, deep learning methods, and hybrid approaches. We then delve into the core components of automatic co-segmentation techniques, including feature alignment, similarity measurement, and optimization strategies. Additionally, we analyze the performance evaluation metrics used to assess the effectiveness of these methods and highlight their limitations.

Our survey also covers several application domains where co-segmentation has been successfully employed, such as multi-modal medical imaging, video surveillance, and robotics. Furthermore, we discuss open research directions and potential future paths for developing more accurate, efficient, and robust co-segmentation algorithms that can operate on large-scale datasets and tackle complex scenarios.

Finally, our paper provides a comprehensive comparsion of state-of-the-art co-segmentation methods and identifies opportunities for improving their convergence, scalability, and adaptability to varying input conditions. This survey aims to serve as a valuable resource for both beginners and experienced researchers interested in contribute to the growing field of automatic image co-segmentation.

Deep convolutional  neural  networks (CNNs) have been shown to perform
extremely well  at   a variety of tasks including subtasks of  autonomous driving
such as image segmentation and object  classification.  However, networks
designed for these tasks typically require vast quantities of training data and
long training  periods to converge. We investigate the design rationale   behind
end-to-end driving network designs by proposing and comparing three small and
computationally inexpensive deep end-to-end neural network  models that generate
driving control  signals directly  from input images. In contrast to prior work
that segments the autonomous  driving task, our models take on a novel  approach
to  the autonomous driving problem by  utilizing deep and thin Fully
Convolutional Nets  (FCNs)  with recurrent neural nets and low parameter counts
to tackle a complex end-to-end  regression task predicting both steering and
acceleration commands.  In  addition, we include layers optimized for
classification to allow  the networks to  implicitly learn  image semantics. We
show that the resulting networks  use 3x fewer parameters than the most recent
comparable end-to-end driving  network and 500x fewer parameters than the
AlexNet  variations  and  converge both faster and to  lower losses while
maintaining   robustness against overfitting.

The field of medical imaging has seen a significant increase in the use of deep learning techniques, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs), to aid in image segmentation tasks. 

One such technique is the U-Net architecture, which has shown promising results in various applications including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) segmentation. 

However, domain shift can occur when applying pre-trained models to new datasets with different characteristics than those used during training. This can lead to decreased performance and accuracy in the resulting model. In this study, we investigate the impact of domain shift on U-Net layers in MRI segmentation. We analyze how changes in input data distribution affect the behavior of each layer within the network and identify key factors that contribute to successful adaptation to new domains. 

Our findings provide insights into the design of more robust CNN architectures for domain transfer and highlight the importance of considering domain differences during model development and deployment.
