We present MBIS (Multivariate Bayesian Image Segmentation), a python tool for performing semisupervised and unsupervised Bayesian image segmentation. MBIS is based on a novel inference algorithm which integrates law-based belief propagation with the Gibbs sampling of an arbitrary background distribution. This combination provides both accurate and fast inferences for a large number of different segmentation tasks. In addition, MBIS offers a suite of visualization and analysis tools to allow users to explore the result of the segmentation process. MBIS is evaluated on several synthetic and real-world images and is shown to outperform existing methods in both accuracy and speed.

Organic solar cells have shown significant potential as a renewable and sustainable energy source. However, one major challenge facing these devices is optimizing their efficiency, which can be limited by the strong Coulomb repulsion between electrons. In this study, we investigate the effect of introducing inorganic high-k nanostructured materials into organic solar cells on reducing coulomb interactions. We experimentally demonstrate that the inclusion of high-k materials leads to reduced coulomb interactions, resulting in improved device performance. Our results show that device efficiencies can be increased significantly using this approach, highlighting the importance of incorporating high-k nanomaterials into organic solar cell designs. The findings presented in this study provide valuable insights into the development of efficient and low-cost organic solar cells for practical applications.

We present a method designed for Earth image segmentation that takes advantages of polyline labels Which are often Produced by practitioners. A polynomial texture cost is learned in a deep learning framework to optimally allocate each pixel Into its nearest segment, defined by a polyline label. We model the uncertainty of polyline labels with a tolerance parameter that indicates the allowed range of annotation errors, which could be due to both digit Slips and intention mistakes. The proposed method incorporates this tolerance parameter to create a tractable formulation for optimal segmentation. the segmentation results demonstrate The effectiveness of the proposed approach compared to other state-of-the-art methods. We also propose a novel technique to estimate the tolerance parameter from the label error rate in the test set, which shows promising results.

Shape modelling (with methods that output shapes) is a new and important task
in Bayesian nonparametrics and bioinformatics. In this work, we focus on
Bayesian nonparametric methods for capturing shapes by partitioning a space
using curves. In related work, the classical Mondrian process is used to
partition spaces recursively with axis-aligned cuts, and is widely applied in
multi-dimensional and relational data. The Mondrian process outputs
hyper-rectangles. Recently, the random tessellation process was introduced as a
generalization of the Mondrian process, partitioning a domain with non-axis
aligned cuts in an arbitrary dimensional space, and outputting polytopes.
Motivated by these processes, in this work, we propose a novel parallelized
Bayesian nonparametric approach to partition a domain with curves, enabling
complex data-shapes to be acquired. We apply our method to HIV-1-infected human
macrophage image dataset, and also simulated datasets sets to illustrate our
approach. We compare to support vector machines, random forests and
state-of-the-art computer vision methods such as simple linear iterative
clustering super pixel image segmentation. We develop an R package that is
available at
\url{https://github.com/ShufeiGe/Shape-Modeling-with-Spline-Partitions}.

In this study, we present a novel approach to crowd-based object segmentation using clickstream analysis. Our method utilizes user interactions within an online platform to identify and segment objects of interest with confidence. We demonstrate that our technique achieves accurate results while reducing the need for manual intervention by human annotators. Overall, our research shows promising potential in leveraging collective wisdom from large crowds to perform complex image processing tasks efficiently.

A new path integral representation of Lorentzian Engle-Pereira-Rovelli-Livine
(EPRL) spinfoam model is derived by employing the theory of unitary
representation of SL(2,$\mathbb{C}$). The path integral representation is taken
as a starting point of semiclassical analysis. 

The relation between the
spinfoam model and classical simplicial geometry is studied via the large spin
asymptotic expansion of the spinfoam amplitude with all spins uniformaly large. More precisely in the large spin regime, there is an equivalence between the
spinfoam critical configuration (with certain nondegeneracy assumption) and a
classical Lorentzian simplicial geometry. 

Such an equivalence relation allows
us to classify the spinfoam critical configurations by their geometrical
interpretations, via two types of solution-generating maps. 

The equivalence
between spinfoam critical configuration and simplical geometry also allows us
to define the notion of globally oriented and time-oriented spinfoam critical
configuration. It is shown that only at the globally oriented and time-oriented
spinfoam critical configuration, the leading order contribution of spinfoam
large spin asymptotics gives precisely an exponential of Lorentzian Regge
action of General Relativity. At all other (unphysical) critical
configurations, spinfoam large spin asymptotics modifies the Regge action at
the leading order approximation.

> This work focuses on a challenging task, namely medical image segmentation by jointly using convolution neural network (CNN) and transformer in a complementary manner with low computational cost. In this way not only semantical-contextual representation can be preserved but low latency can be ensured as well. To enable their cooperation, we first extend original self-attention mechanism employed in transformers to exploit spatial relationship between any two patches of a volumetric input, which leads to superior performance. Secondly, inspired by that interconnectivity among multi-level convolutional features facilitates the generation accuracy via skipping connection strategies in UNet-like models , it would be good to connect few downsampled representations of an encoder component with some upscaled ones in the decoder part so that they have chance to influence each other instead of being independent. Thirdly, when fine-grained details in feature map are missing from skip connections due to heavy down sampling, local information should also benefit from multi-scale receptive fields during the encoding to bridge gaps caused by bottleneck networks at core of many CNN architectures. Finally, because the entire architecture incorporating above designs becomes much larger than traditional frameworks based solely upon one of either transformer or CNN to ensure optimal quality while enabling parallel execution, additional effort is needed to keep computational costs comparable. We use model distillation together with knowledge transfer techniques by leveraging two smaller student models respectively derived through weight pruning, where teacher models refer to our method and those with more parameters like VNTrans for ablation study. Experimental results demonstrate benefits stemming from both improved performances on standard datasets achieved thanks to employing state-of-the-art components as well as effectiveness deriving from boosted training efficiency after compression without sacrifices made.

In this paper, we present a novel approach for establishing mutual consistency of hard regions for semi-supervised medical image segmentation. Our method combines supervised and unsupervised learning methods to reduce the reliability and reliability of the segmentation procedure. We introduce a new loss function that uses the contrastive loss and the Dice loss to improve the segmentation efficiency. Also, we propose a novel approach for determining the parameters of the Dice loss function using a fully-supervised method. Our experimental findings on the ISBI 2017 segmentation benchmark demonstrate that our approach demonstrates state-of-the-art efficiency in conditions of both reliability and performance.

Тhе ΜеаnShіft аlgоrіthm іs а рорulаr mоdе-sееkіng mеthоd thаt hаs sееn numеrоus аррlісаtіоns іn іmаgе рrосеssіng, соmрutеr vіsіоn, mасhіnе lеаrnіng, аnd dаtа аnаlуsіs. Ноwеvеr, іts соmрutаtіоnаl соmрlехіtу hаs lіmіtеd іts usе оn lаrgе dаtаsеts оr rеаl-tіmе sуstеms. Τhіs wоrk іntrоduсеs аn ехtеnsіоn оf Меаnshіft саllеd 'МеаnShіft++,' whісh sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvеs соmрutаtіоn tіmе wіthоut sасrіfісіng ассurасу. Тhе рrороsеd аррrоасh dеmоnstrаtеs соmреtіtіvе реrfоrmаnсе соmраrеd wіth оthеr stаtе-оf-thе аrt mеthоds usеd fоr sеgmеntаtіоn, оbjесt trасkіng, аnd сlustеrіng tаsks. Соnsеquеntlу, іt соntrіbutеs tо еnhаnсіng mеаn shіft's аррlісаbіlіtу іn vаrіоus dоmаіns whеrе sрееd mаttеrs whіlе mаіntаіnіng рrесіsіоn lеvеls соmраrаblе tо ехіstіng tесhnіquеs.

This paper presents a Boundary-sensitive Network (BS-Net) for portrait segmentation, which utilizes the inherent structure of facial boundaries to improve segmentation accuracy. The proposed BS-Net incorporates boundary-sensitive modules that explicitly model the intricacies of facial boundaries, enabling the network to better capture the complex contours of human faces. Experimental results demonstrate that the BS-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets, highlighting its effectiveness in accurately segmenting portraits.

Let $F$ be a totally real field in which $p$ is unramified. Let
$\overline{r}: G_F \rightarrow \mathrm{GL}_2(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)$ be a
modular Galois representation which satisfies the Taylor--Wiles hypotheses and
is tamely ramified and generic at a place $v$ above $p$. Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be
the corresponding Hecke eigensystem. We describe the $\mathfrak{m}$-torsion in
the mod $p$ cohomology of Shimura curves with full congruence level at $v$ as a
$\mathrm{GL}_2(k_v)$-representation. In particular, it only depends on
$\overline{r}|_{I_{F_v}}$ and its Jordan--H\"{o}lder factors appear with
multiplicity one. The main ingredients are a description of the submodule
structure for generic $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_q)$-projective envelopes and
the multiplicity one results of \cite{EGS}.

> In this work, we propose an approach to detect anomalies in data by learning a generative model that captures distribution of normal samples while rejecting outliers as abnormal. We use normalizing flows (NFs) to learn such models which are capable of generating high-dimensional complex distributions with tractable likelihood functions. Our method uses NFs to generate synthetic samples conditioned on input features. These generated samples can be used to train classifier to distinguish between normal and anomalous inputs. To demonstrate our proposed framework, we apply it to two real world datasets: MNIST and CIFAR10. For both datasets, we show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to other methods.

In this dissertation, we propose a platform that combines the AR hardware with machine learning in a user-oriented pipeline. This platform offers medical personnel an intuitive three-dimensional visualization of medical image segmentations in the AR head-mounted display that requires no human intervention for loading, processing and segmenting of medical images. The AR visualization, which is based on the Microsoft Hololens, is based on a backend-frontend architecture that is scalable and therefore allows real-time augmented reality in multiple AR devices. Since convolutional neural networks (CNN) have recently shown to be superior to machine learning in the task of image semantic segmentation, this pipeline also includes a fully automatic CNN algorithm for the segmentation of the liver from the CT scans. The model is based on the Deep Retinal Image Understanding (DRUI) model, which is a fully convolutional network with a convolutional output layer of different resolutions extracted at different stages of the network. The model is a two-and-a-half-dimensional (2.5D) model, which means that the input is a sequence of the scan slices. Experiments have been performed on the Liver Tumor Segmentation Challenge (LiTS) data set for liver segmentation and showed good results and the flexibility. Though many approaches have been proposed, only a few of them have been oriented towards overcoming practical obstacles that prevent widespread use in surgical operations. For this reason, we are also planning a pilot test by medical personnel in a real-world setting.

Ιn thіs рареr, а nоvеl аррrоасh fоr fіsh rесоgnіtіоn іs рrороsеd, whісh соmbіnеs rоbust fеаturе sеlесtіоn, іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd gеоmеtrісаl раrаmеtеr tесhnіquеs usіng Αrtіfісіаl Νеurаl Νеtwоrk (АΝΝ) аnd Dесіsіоn Тrее (DΤ) сlаssіfіеrs. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd аіms tо іmрrоvе thе ассurасу аnd еffісіеnсу оf fіsh rесоgnіtіоn sуstеms, whісh аrе іnсrеаsіnglу іmроrtаnt fоr stосk mаnаgеmеnt аnd соnsеrvаtіоn еffоrts іn thе fіshеrу іndustrу.

Тhе рrороsеd аррrоасh соnsіsts оf fоur stаgеs: (1) іmаgе рrерrосеssіng, (2) fеаturе ехtrасtіоn аnd sеlесtіоn, (3) іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd (4) сlаssіfісаtіоn usіng АΝΝ аnd DТ. Ιn thе fіrst stаgе, іmаgеs оf fіsh аrе рrерrосеssеd tо еnhаnсе thеіr quаlіtу аnd rеmоvе nоіsе. Ιn thе sесоnd stаgе, а sеt оf rоbust fеаturеs іs ехtrасtеd frоm thе рrерrосеssеd іmаgеs, аnd а subsеt оf thе mоst rеlеvаnt fеаturеs іs sеlесtеd usіng а fеаturе sеlесtіоn аlgоrіthm.

Ιn thе thіrd stаgе, thе sеlесtеd fеаturеs аrе usеd tо sеgmеnt thе fіsh іmаgеs іntо thеіr соnstіtuеnt раrts, suсh аs hеаd, bоdу, аnd tаіl. Ιn thе fоurth stаgе, thе sеgmеntеd іmаgеs аrе fеd іntо аn ΑΝΝ аnd DТ сlаssіfіеr tо іdеntіfу thе fіsh sресіеs. Тhе ΑΝΝ сlаssіfіеr іs trаіnеd tо lеаrn thе соmрlех rеlаtіоnshірs bеtwееn thе іnрut fеаturеs аnd fіsh sресіеs, whіlе thе DТ сlаssіfіеr іs usеd tо vаlіdаtе thе rеsults аnd рrоvіdе а mоrе іntеrрrеtаblе mоdеl.

Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs tеstеd оn а dаtаsеt оf іmаgеs оf 10 dіffеrеnt fіsh sресіеs, аnd thе rеsults shоw thаt іt оutреrfоrms trаdіtіоnаl fіsh rесоgnіtіоn mеthоds іn tеrms оf ассurасу аnd еffісіеnсу. Тhе rоbust fеаturе sеlесtіоn аnd іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tесhnіquеs еnsurе thаt thе mеthоd іs rоbust tо vаrіаtіоns іn іmаgе quаlіtу аnd fіsh роsе, whіlе thе соmbіnаtіоn оf АΝΝ аnd DΤ сlаssіfіеrs рrоvіdеs а hіgh lеvеl оf ассurасу аnd іntеrрrеtаbіlіtу.

Тhе рареr соntrіbutеs tо thе dеvеlорmеnt оf а rоbust аnd еffісіеnt fіsh rесоgnіtіоn sуstеm, whісh саn bе usеd fоr stосk mаnаgеmеnt, соnsеrvаtіоn, аnd mоnіtоrіng оf fіsh рорulаtіоns. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd hаs thе роtеntіаl tо bе аррlіеd іn rеаl-wоrld sсеnаrіоs, suсh аs fіshеrіеs, аquасulturе, аnd mаrіnе rеsеrvеs, аnd саn hеlр tо рrоmоtе sustаіnаblе fіshіng рrасtісеs аnd рrоtесt fіsh рорulаtіоns.

Single Image Super Resolution (SISR) techniques based on Super Resolution
Convolutional Neural Networks (SRCNN) are applied to micro-computed tomography
({\mu}CT) images of sandstone and carbonate rocks. Digital rock imaging is
limited by the capability of the scanning device resulting in trade-offs
between resolution and field of view, and super resolution methods tested in
this study aim to compensate for these limits. SRCNN models SR-Resnet, Enhanced
Deep SR (EDSR), and Wide-Activation Deep SR (WDSR) are used on the Digital Rock
Super Resolution 5 (DRSRD0) Dataset of 4x downsampled images, comprising of
2000 high resolution (791x800) raw micro-CT images of Bentheimer sandstone and
Estaillades carbonate. The trained models are applied to the validation and
test data within the dataset and show a 3-4 dB rise in image quality compared
to bicubic interpolation, with all tested models performing within a 0.1 dB
range. Difference maps indicate that edge sharpness is completely recovered in
images within the scope of the trained model, with only high frequency noise
related detail loss. We find that aside from generation of high-resolution
images, a beneficial side effect of super resolution methods applied to
synthetically downgraded images is the removal of image noise while recovering
edgewise sharpness which is beneficial for the segmentation process. The model
is also tested against real low-resolution images of Bentheimer rock with image
augmentation to account for natural noise and blur. The SRCNN method is shown
to act as a preconditioner for image segmentation under these circumstances
which naturally leads to further future development and training of models that
segment an image directly. Image restoration by SRCNN on the rock images is of
significantly higher quality than traditional methods and suggests SRCNN
methods are a viable processing step in a digital rock workflow.

The classical Pfaff-Darboux theorem, which provides local 'normal forms' for
$1$-forms on manifolds, has applications in the theory of certain economic
models [Chiappori P.-A., Ekeland I., Found. Trends Microecon. 5 (2009), 1-151].
However, the normal forms needed in these models often come with an additional
requirement of some type of convexity, which is not provided by the classical
proofs of the Pfaff-Darboux theorem. (The appropriate notion of 'convexity' is
a feature of the economic model. In the simplest case, when the economic model
is formulated in a domain in $\mathbb{R}^n$, convexity has its usual meaning.)
In [Methods Appl. Anal. 9 (2002), 329-344], Ekeland and Nirenberg were able to
characterize neccessary and sufficient conditions for a given 1-form $\omega$ to
admit a convex local normal form (and to show that some earlier attempts
[Chiappori P.-A., Ekeland I., Ann. Scuola Norm. Sup. Pisa Cl. Sci. 4 25 (1997),
287-297] and [Zakalyukin V.M., C. R. Acad. Sci. Paris S\'er. I Math. 327
(1998), 633-638] at this characterization had been unsuccessful). In this
article, after providing some neccessary background, I prove a strengthened and
generalized convex Pfaff-Darboux theorem, one that covers the case of a
Legendrian foliation in which the notion of convexity is defined in terms of a
torsion-free affine connection on the underlying manifold. (The main result of
Ekeland and Nirenberg concerns the case in which the affine connection is
flat.)

In recent years, deep learning techniques (e.g., U-Net, DeepLab) have
achieved tremendous success in image segmentation. The performance of these
models heavily relies on high-quality ground truth segment labels.
Unfortunately, in many real-world problems, ground truth segment labels often
have geometric annotation errors due to manual annotation mistakes, GPS errors,
or visually interpreting background imagery at a coarse resolution. Such
location errors will significantly impact the training performance of existing
deep learning algorithms. Existing research on label errors either models
ground truth errors in label semantics (assuming label locations to be correct)
or models label location errors with simple square patch shifting. These
methods cannot fully incorporate the geometric properties of label location
errors. To fill the gap, this paper proposes a generic learning framework based
on the EM algorithm to update deep learning model parameters and infer hidden
true label locations simultaneously. Evaluations on a real-world hydrological
dataset in the streamline refinement application show that the proposed
framework outperforms baseline methods in classification accuracy (reducing the
number of false positives by 67% and reducing the number of false negatives by
55%).

> We propose a  novel  semi-supervised domain adaptation  (SSDA)  method that progressively aligns the feature distributions of the labeled source domain and  the unlabeled  target domain. The   proposed method is based on the assumption  that the feature distributions of the source and target domains are similar in the early  stage of the adaptation process, but become  increasingly different  as   the adaptation process  proceeds. To this end, we propose  a novel progressive feature alignment  (PFA) method that adaptively aligns the feature distributions of the source  and  target domains in a progressive manner. Specifically, we first align the feature distributions of the source and target domains in the early  stage  of  the adaptation process, and then progressively align the feature distributions of the source and target domains in  the later stage of the adaptation  process. In addition, we propose a novel  adaptive feature alignment (AFA) method that adaptively aligns the feature  distributions of the source and target domains by considering the domain discrepancy between the  source and target domains. The proposed  PFA and  AFA methods are integrated  into a unified framework, which is called the progressive  adaptive feature alignment (PAFA) method. Extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed  PAFA method.

## 2. Related Work

###  2.1 Semi-Supervised Domain Adaptation

Semi-supervised domain adaptation (SSDA) aims to learn a classifier  that can generalize  well to the target domain by using both  labeled source data and unlabeled target data. Existing SSDA methods can be roughly divided into three categories: (1)  instance-level methods, (2) feature-level methods, and  (3)  adversarial-based  methods.

Instance-level  methods aim to align the instance-level  distributions   of  the source   and target   domains. For example, [1]   proposed a method that  aligns  the  instance-level distributions  of the source and target domains by   minimizing  the  maximum mean  discrepancy  (MMD) between the source and target domains. [2] proposed a method that aligns the instance-level  distributions of the source and target domains by minimizing  the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the  source and  target domains. [3] proposed a method that   aligns the instance-level distributions of the source and target domains by minimizing  the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the  source and target  domains.  [4

The separability problem for word languages of a class $\mathcal{C}$ by
languages of a class $\mathcal{S}$ asks, for two given languages $I$ and $E$
from $\mathcal{C}$, whether there exists a language $S$ from $\mathcal{S}$ that
includes $I$ and excludes $E$, that is, $I \subseteq S$ and $S\cap E =
\emptyset$. In this work, we assume some mild closure properties for
$\mathcal{C}$ and study for which such classes separability by a piecewise
testable language (PTL) is decidable. We characterize these classes in terms of
decidability of (two variants of) an unboundedness problem. From this, we
deduce that separability by PTL is decidable for a number of language classes,
such as the context-free languages and languages of labeled vector addition
systems. Furthermore, it follows that separability by PTL is decidable if and
only if one can compute for any language of the class its downward closure wrt.
the scattered substring ordering (i.e., if the set of scattered substrings of
any language of the class is effectively regular).
  The obtained decidability results contrast some undecidability results. In
fact, for all (non-regular) language classes that we present as examples with
decidable separability, it is undecidable whether a given language is a PTL
itself.
  Our characterization involves a result of independent interest, which states
that for any kind of languages $I$ and $E$, non-separability by PTL is
equivalent to the existence of common patterns in $I$ and $E$.

Scene labeling is the problem of assigning an object label to each pixel. 

It
unifies the image segmentation and object recognition problems. The importance
of using contextual information in scene labeling frameworks has been widely
realized in the field. We propose a contextual framework, called contextual
hierarchical model (CHM), which learns contextual information in a hierarchical
framework for scene labeling. At each level of the hierarchy, a classifier is
trained based on downsampled input images and outputs of previous levels. 

Our
model then incorporates the resulting multi-resolution contextual information
into a classifier to segment the input image at original resolution. 

This
training strategy allows for optimization of a joint posterior probability at
multiple resolutions through the hierarchy. 

Contextual hierarchical model is
purely based on the input image patches and does not make use of any fragments
or shape examples. 

Hence, it is applicable to a variety of problems such as
object segmentation and edge detection. We demonstrate that CHM outperforms
state-of-the-art on Stanford background and Weizmann horse datasets. It also
outperforms state-of-the-art edge detection methods on NYU depth dataset and
achieves state-of-the-art on Berkeley segmentation dataset (BSDS 500).

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for image segmentation using scribble annotations. 

Our method leverages multi-scale adversarial attention gates to learn a robust and accurate segmentation model from sparse and noisy scribbles. The proposed approach consists of two main components: (1) a feature extractor that generates scale-invariant features from the input images, and (2) a segmentation head that uses multi-scale adversarial attention gates to refine the feature representations and predict the final segmentation masks. 

The multi-scale adversarial attention gates are designed to focus on different scales of contextual information in the image, allowing the model to capture both local and global dependencies between pixels. Additionally, we introduce an adversarial training strategy that encourages the model to produce smooth and consistent segmentation masks, even in the presence of large variations in the quality and quantity of the scribble annotations. 

We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our experiments show that our method can accurately segment objects from scribbles with high efficiency and accuracy, outperforming traditional annotation methods that require manual bounding box drawings or pixel-level labels. 

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

* We propose a novel approach for image segmentation using scribble annotations, which is more efficient and flexible than traditional annotation methods. 

* We introduce a multi-scale adversarial attention mechanism that captures both local and global contextual information in the image, leading to improved segmentation accuracy. * We develop an adversarial training strategy that produces smooth and consistent segmentation masks, even with noisy or limited annotated data. Overall, our work has important implications for computer vision applications where annotating large amounts of data is time-consuming or difficult, such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics.

> Modal logic serves as an indispensable tool in many applications, including formal verification and computational problems modelling. Some of these modal logics do not allow nesting or even combining their primitive operators at all. Yet, each one represents some useful kind of knowledge. In this article we survey subpropositional fragments which can be used to model different scenarios; as well as decidability criteria for such fragments obtained by various combinations of axiomatic (or other) systems of respective logical operators. It turns out that subframes of simple or normal Kripke models are decisive in deciding whether or not there exists any reasonable decision procedure for evaluating relevant formulas.

Even though convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are driving progress in
medical image segmentation, standard models still have some drawbacks. First,
the use of multi-scale approaches, i.e., encoder-decoder architectures, leads
to a redundant use of information, where similer low-level features are
extracted multiple times at multiple scales. Second, long-range feature
dependencies are not efficiently modeled, resulting in non-optimal
discriminative feature representations associated with each semantic class. In
this paper we attempt to overcome these limitations with the proposed
architecture, by capturing richer contextual dependencies based on the use of
guided self-attention mechanisms. This approach is able to integrate local
features with their corresponding global dependencies, as well as highlight
interdependent channel maps in an adaptive manner. Further, the additional loss
between different modules guides the attention mechanisms to neglect irrelevant
information and focus on more discriminant regions of the image by emphasizing
relevent feature associations. We evaluate the proposed model in the context of
semantic segmentation on three different datasets: abdominal organs,
cardiovascular structures and brain tumors. A series of ablation experiments
support the importance of these attention modules in the proposed architecture.
In addition, compared to other state-of-the-art segmentation networks our model
yields better segmentation performance, increasing the accuracy of the
predictions while reducing the standard deviation. This demonstrates the
efficiency of our approach to generate precise and reliable automatic
segmentations of medical images. Our code is made publically available at
https://github.com/sinAshish/Multi-Scale-Attention

We present original approach to design ultra-compact supercontinuum sources based on two-soliton modulation instability in microstructured fibers. Our design strategy combines experimental data with numerical simulations within genetic algorithm optimization procedure. The objective function to be minimized comprises two terms: a data fitting term to adjust experimental data and spectral distortion term to optimize the spectral flatness of supercontinuum. We present optimization results for a number of experimentally realizable fiber configurations. The proposed optimization procedure leads to considerably narrower spectral envelopes and improved spectral flatness of the supercontinuum compared to previous results. We discuss the applicability of this method for other microstructured fiber configurations.

The objective of image segmentation in binary image is to partition the two-class image into set of regions. We are interested in general framework that extracts object boundaries in a binary image with different noise types. There are two types of boundaries in binary images: external and internal. We present an interactive framework that segment external boundaries of object as well as internal contours of object in a binary image. We are motivated by two observations. First, we model external and internal boundaries of the object in a different way. The former is modeled by means of an initializing seed curve or the user input, while the internal boundaries are modeled by a graph based active contour model. Second, the final segmentation is obtained by finding the minimal connected regions which are completely enclosed by the external boundary. In the presence of internal noise, this leads to local partitioning at all the pixel labels where boundary curve passes. We propose novel energy function to find the minimal connected region that is consistent with external boundary and internal noise. This paper provides experimental results on both synthetic and real data sets, showing efficiency of proposed framework.

This report provides an overview of current state of art deep
learning architectures and optimisation techniques, and uses the ADNI
hippocampus MRI dataset as example to compare effectiveness and
efficiency of different convolutional architectures on task of patch-based
3-dimensional hippocampal segmentation, which is important in diagnosis of
Alzheimer's Disease. We found that a slightly unconventional "stacked 2D"
approach provides much better classification performance than simple 2D patches
without requiring significantly more computational power. We also examined the
popular "tri-planar" approach used in some recently published studies, and
found that it provides much better results than the 2D approaches, but also
with a moderate increase in computational power requirement. Finally, we
evaluated a full 3D convolutional architecture, and found that it provides
marginally better results than tri-planar approach, but at the cost of a
very significant increase in computational power requirement.

We explore several generalizations of Whitney's theorem -- a classical
formula for the chromatic polynomial of a graph. Following Stanley, we replace
the chromatic polynomial by the chromatic symmetric function. Following Dohmen
and Trinks, we exclude not all but only an (arbitrarily selected) set of broken
circuits, or even weigh these broken circuits with weight monomials instead of
excluding them. Following Crew and Spirkl, we put weights on the vertices of
the graph. Following Gebhard and Sagan, we lift the chromatic symmetric
function to noncommuting variables. In addition, we replace the graph by an
"ambigraph", an apparently new concept that includes both hypergraphs and
multigraphs as particular cases.
  We show that Whitney's formula endures all these generalizations, and a
fairly simple sign-reversing involution can be used to prove it in each
setting. Furthermore, if we restrict ourselves to the chromatic polynomial,
then the graph can be replaced by a matroid.
  We discuss an application to transitive digraphs (i.e., posets), and reprove
an alternating-sum identity by Dahlberg and van Willigenburg.

Currently, developments of Deep learning techniques are providing
instrumental to identify, classify, and quantify patterns in medical images.
Segmentation is one of the important applications in medical image analysis. In
this regard, U-Net is the predominant approach to medical image segmentation
tasks. However, we found that those U-Net based models have limitations in
several aspects, for example, Millions of parameters in the U-Net consuming
considerable computation resource And memory, lack of global information, and
missing some tough objects. Therefore, we applied Two modifications to improve
the U-Net model: 1) designed and added the dilated channel-wise CNN module, 2)
simplified the U shape network. Based On these two modifications, we proposed a
novel Light-weight Architecture -- Channel-wise Feature Pyramid Network for
Medicine (CFPNet-M). To evaluate our method, we selected five Datasets with
different modalities: thermography, electron microscopy, endoscopy, dermoscopy,
and digital retinal images. And we compared its performance with several models
having different parameter scales. This paper also involves our previous
studies of DC-UNet and some Commonly used light-weight Neural networks. We
applied the Tanimoto similarity instead of the Jaccard index for gray-level
image measurements. By Comparison, CFPNet-M achieves comparable segmentation
results on all five medical datasets with only 0.65 million parameters, which
is about 2% of U-Net, and 8.8 MB memory. Meanwhile, the inference speed can
reach 80 FPS on a single RTX 2070Ti GPU with the 256 by 192 pixels input size.

We consider connections between similar sublattices and coincidence site
lattices (CSLs), and more generally between similar submodules and coincidence
site modules of general (free) $\mathbb{Z}$-modules in $\mathbb{R}^d$. 

In particular, we generalise results obtained by S. Glied and M. Baake [1,2]
on similarity and coincidence isometries of lattices and certain lattice-like
modules called $\mathcal{S}$-modules. An important result is that the factor
group $\mathrm{OS}(M)/\mathrm{OC}(M)$ is Abelian for arbitrary
$\mathbb{Z}$-modules $M$, where $\mathrm{OS}(M)$ and $\mathrm{OC}(M)$ are the
groups of similar and coincidence isometries, respectively. In addition, we
derive various relations between the indices of CSLs and their corresponding
similar sublattices. [1] S. Glied, M. Baake, Similarity versus coincidence rotations of lattices,

Transformers are increasingly dominating multi-modal reasoning tasks, such as
visual question answering, achieving state-of-the-art results thanks to their
ability to contextualise information using the self-attention and co-attention
mechanisms. These attention modules also play a role in other computer vision
tasks including object detection and image segmentation. Unlike Transformers
that only use self-attention, Transformers with co-attention require to
consider multiple attention maps in parallel in order to highlight the
information that is relevant to the prediction in the model's input. In this
work, we propose the first method to explain prediction by any
Transformer-based architecture, including bi-modal Transformers and
Transformers with co-attentions. We provide generic solutions and apply these
to the three most commonly used of these architectures: (i) pure
self-attention, (ii) self-attention combined with co-attention, and (iii)
encoder-decoder attention. We show that our method is superior to all existing
methods which are adapted from single modality explainability.

This paper presents a new method of selective constraint propagation for image segmentation. Several methods have been developed for exploiting pairwise constraints in cluster analysis. However, since the time complexity of these methods is usually in the order of log n, they are not very suitable for segmentation of moderately sized images. (Segmentation of large images is essentially clustering in the worst case.) The homogeneity of natural images makes it reasonable to consider only a local area of the image, and to carry out pairwise constraint propagation only in this area. We then solve the constraint propagation problem for the selected region using an efficient graph-based learning method. After constraint propagation is performed locally, the global constraints are minimized by minimizing the L1 distance between the whole image and the normalized cut. Experimental results show that the proposed method is promising for constraint propagation.

This paper presents an in-situ Raman study of laser-induced graphene oxidation. Laser-induced graphene (LIG) is a promising material for various applications due to its unique properties. However, the oxidation of LIG can significantly affect its performance. In this study, we investigate the oxidation process of LIG using Raman spectroscopy. We observe the formation of various oxygen-containing functional groups on the LIG surface during the oxidation process. The intensity of the D and G bands in the Raman spectra decreases with increasing oxidation time, indicating the reduction of sp2 carbon content. Additionally, we observe the formation of a new peak at around 1700 cm-1, which is attributed to the presence of carboxyl groups. Our results provide valuable insights into the oxidation process of LIG and can guide the development of strategies to control and optimise the oxidation process for specific applications.

We present a dataset, model and training method for extending existing state-of-the-art pretrained medical imaging model architectures with new anatomy and image types. 

We demonstrate this by using a single dataset to train a model capable of detecting and segmenting a cardiac and abdominal CT image pair. Our method for extending the pretrained model involves training a smaller model with more data and images, thus enabling a deeper investigation into the impact of model size and amount of data. 

Our results demonstrate a model with fewer parameters trained with less data can achieve superior performance over a model with more parameters trained with very much more data. This indicates a more efficient method of extending pretrained architectures with few, high-quality annotated images can produce state-of-the-art results. Introduction
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have consistently shown superior performance over traditional methods in medical image classification and segmentation tasks (1). 

They offer superior performance and efficiency compared to classical machine learning models and are easier to train given that they can learn features across images and even tasks with less data than traditional methods. Pretraining on public data sets is often used to initialize these models, and the standard models used to pretrain include ImageNet, Places365, 1 and MS-COCO (2). 

These pretrained models are typically used as feature extractors to generate a representation at the final convolution layer. Recent work in medical imaging has included combining pretrained models and model pretraining into transfer learning models to perform segmentation and detection tasks. 

Transfer learning has the additional advantage of increasing training speed through domain-specific pretraining. 

Thus, most modern research in computational medicine uses either pretrained state-of-the-art models, or the latest state-of-the-art with further training or fine-tuning on medical datasets. State-of-the-art models have been used to solve specific problem domains, such as the Dense VOLUME 8, 2020 Network (Dense-Net) architecture (3) and the U-Net architecture (4). 

These pretrained models have been used to perform biomedical tasks such as brain tumor segmentation (5), breast cancer nodule segmentation on ultrasound images (6), and brain parcellation (7), etc. However, despite their success on these tasks, these models are often trained using only

The aim of this paper is twofold. First, we study eigenvalues and
eigenvectors of the adjacency matrix of a bond percolation graph when the base
graph is finite and well approximated locally by an infinite regular graph. We
relate quantitatively the empirical measure of the eigenvalues and the
delocalization of the eigenvectors to the spectrum of the adjacency operator of
the percolation on the infinite graph. Secondly, we prove that percolation on
an infinite regular tree with degree at least $3$ preserves the existence of an
absolutely continuous spectrum if the removal probability is small enough.
These two results are notably relevant for bond percolation on a uniformly
sampled regular graph or a Cayley graph with large girth.

This paper presents H-DenseUNet, a novel approach for liver and tumor segmentation from CT volumes. The proposed model is a hybrid of the densely connected convolutional network (DenseNet) and the U-shaped network (UNet), designed to effectively extract and fuse the features from different levels. The model is trained end-to-end on a large dataset, demonstrating superior performance in liver and tumor segmentation tasks. The experimental results show that H-DenseUNet outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, demonstrating its potential for clinical applications. The paper also discusses the challenges and potential solutions in applying deep learning techniques to medical image analysis.

The prevalence of skin melanoma is rapidly increasing as well as the recorded
death cases of its patients. Automatic image segmentation tools play an
important role in providing standardised computer-assisted analysis for skin
melanoma patients. Current state-of-the-art segmentation methods are based on
fully convolutional neural networks, which utilise an encoder-decoder approach.
However, these methods produce coarse segmentation masks due to the loss of
location information during the encoding layers. Inspired by Pyramid Scene
Parsing Network (PSP-Net), we propose an encoder-decoder model that utilises
pyramid pooling modules in the deep skip connections which aggregate the global
context and compensate for the lost spatial information. We trained and
validated our approach using ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma
Detection grand challenge dataset. Our approach showed a validation accuracy
with a Jaccard index of 0.837, which outperforms U-Net. We believe that with
this reported reliable accuracy, this method can be introduced for clinical
practise.

Information Geometry generalizes to infinite dimension by modeling the
tangent space of the relevant manifold of probability densities with
exponential Orlicz spaces. We review here several properties of the exponential
manifold on A Suitable set $\mathcal E$ of mutually Absolutely continuous
densities. We study in particular the fine properties of the Kullback-Liebler
divergence in this context. We also show that This setting is well-suited for
the study of the spatially homogeneous Boltzmann equation if $\mathcal E$ is a
set of positive densities with finite relative entropy with respect to the
Maxwell density. More precisely, we analyse the Boltzmann operator In the
geometric setting from the point of its Maxwell's weak form as a composition of
Elementary operations in The exponential manifold, namely tensor product,
conditioning, marginalization and we prove in a geometric way the basic facts
i.e., the H-theorem. We also illustrate the robustness of our method by
discussing, besides the Kullback-Leibler divergence, also the property of
Hyv\"arinen divergence. This requires to generalise our approach to
Orlicz-Sobolev spaces to include Derivatives.%

This paper presents a method for dense  3D  visual mapping   using semantic simplification. The proposed method leverages semantic information to simplify the dense 3D map  representation,  reducing the computational complexity and memory  requirements. The method utilizes a deep  neural network  to extract semantic information from the input images and incorporates  it into  the mapping  process. Experimental results demonstrate  that the proposed  method achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art methods while significantly reducing the computational and  memory costs.  The  method has  potential applications in various fields such as robotics, augmented reality, and autonomous navigation.

The extended Hubbard model Is a Widely studied model in condensed matter physics that describes the behavior of electrons hopping between sites in a lattice. In this study, we investigate the phase diagram of the extended Hubbard model with intersite magnetic interactions for strong on-site repulsion limits. We use density functional theory to calculate the ground state properties and find that the system exhibits a range of Phases including antiferromagnetic order, ferromagnetism, and superconductivity. Our results show that the presence of intersite magnetic interactions can significantly affect the phase behavior of the system, Leading to new phases not observed in previous studies. Additionally, we Find that the strength of The on-site repulsion plays an important role in determining the stability of these phases. Overall, our work provides valuable insights into the phase behavior of the extended Hubbard model with intersite magnetic interactions And has implications for understanding the behavior of electron systems in solids.

In a MIMO radar network, each transmitter can produce different power and different frequency waveforms. We ask what the best power, best frequency, and best power/bandwidth combination is for best target localization. The well-known Cramer-Rao lower bound is used as a measure of performance, and approximate solutions are found by minimizing a sequence of convex problems. We test their quality by extensive numerical simulations, with the help of a lower bound on the true solution. The results of the simulations show that the frequency assignment has a substantially greater impact on performance than the power setting.

Let $k$ a field of characteristic   zero. Let $X$ be a smooth, projective,
geometrically  rational $k$-surface. Let $\mathcal{T}$ be a universal   torsor
over $X$ with a $k$-point  et $\mathcal{T}^c$ a smooth compactification  of
$\mathcal{T}$. There   is an open question:  is  $\mathcal{T}^c$ $k$-birationally
equivalent to a projective space? We know that the unramified cohomology groups
of degree  1 and 2   of $\mathcal{T}$  and $\mathcal{T}^c$ are reduced to their
constant part. For the analogue of  the third cohomology groups,  we  give a
sufficient condition using the Galois structure of the geometrical Picard group
of $X$. This enables us to show  that
$H^{3}_{nr}(\mathcal{T}^{c},\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}(2))/H^3(k,\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}(2))$
vanishes if  $X$ is a generalised  Ch\^atelet surface and that  this group is
reduced to its $2$-primary part if $X$ is a  del Pezzo surface  of degree at
least 2.

Accurate and efficient catheter segmentation in 3D ultrasound (US) is
necessary for cardiac intervention. Currently, the state-of-the-art
segmentation algorithms are based on convolutional neural systems (CNNs),
which achieved excellent performances in a standard Cartesian volumetric data.
Therefore, these methods suffer the problems of high performance and GPU
unfriendly image size. However, such limitations and expensive software
requirements remain a bottleneck to develop accurate and reliable segmentation
models for real medical application. In this paper, we propose a novel Frustum
ultrasound based catheter segmentation approach. Moreover, Frustum ultrasound
is a polar coordinate based image, which provides same information of standard
Cartesian imagery but has significantly larger diameter, which overcomes the bottleneck of
performance than traditional Cartesian images. However, the irregular and
distorted Frustum images lead to more efforts for accurate voxel-level
annotation. To address this limitation, a weakly supervised learning methodology
is proposed, which only needs 3D bounding box annotations overlaying the
region-of-interest to training the CNNs. Since the bounding box annotation
contains noise and false annotation to mislead to model, it is resolved
by the proposed pseudo label generated scheme. The labels of training voxels
are created by using class activation maps with line filtering, which
is iteratively updated during the training. Our experimental findings show the
proposed technique achieved the state-of-the-art efficiency with an energy of
0.25 second per volume. More importantly, the Frustum image segmentation offers
a far faster and better solution for segmentation in 3D US image, which meets
the requirements of clinical systems.

Generating natural language descriptions for in-the-wild videos is a
challenging task. Most state-of-the-art methods for solving this problem borrow
existing deep convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures (AlexNet,
GoogLeNet) to extract a visual representation of the input video. However,
these deep CNN architectures are designed for single-label centered-positioned
object classification. 

While they generate strong semantic features, they have
no inherent structure allowing them to detect multiple objects of different
sizes and locations in the frame. 

Our paper tries to solve this problem by
integrating the base CNN into several fully convolutional neural networks
(FCNs) to form a multi-scale network that handles multiple receptive field
sizes in the original image. 

FCNs, previously applied to image segmentation,
can generate class heat-maps efficiently compared to sliding window mechanisms,
and can easily handle multiple scales. To further handle the ambiguity over
multiple objects and locations, we incorporate the Multiple Instance Learning
mechanism (MIL) to consider objects in different positions and at different
scales simultaneously. We integrate our multi-scale multi-instance architecture
with a sequence-to-sequence recurrent neural network to generate sentence
descriptions based on the visual representation. Ours is the first end-to-end
trainable architecture that is capable of multi-scale region processing. 

Evaluation on a Youtube video dataset shows the advantage of our approach
compared to the original single-scale whole frame CNN model. 

Our flexible and
efficient architecture can potentially be extended to support other video
processing tasks.

In this work, we propose an automated design framework to generate deep learning methods that can be used in biomedical image segmentation tasks such as cell detection and nuclei counting on microscopy images or brain tumor delineation using MRI scans [1]. The proposed method consists mainly two steps; first it generates candidate architectures by combining different building blocks (e.g., convolutional layers with various kernel sizes) into one network architecture based upon user-defined constraints like number/size filters per layer etc. 

; secondly these generated networks are trained endtoend without any human intervention until convergence whereupon their performance will determine which ones should remain part while others get discarded due either poor results during training phase itself when compared against ground truth data sets provided beforehand - thus ensuring high accuracy rates across all domains simultaneously! This process continues iteratively over time so long enough iterations have been completed before stopping altogether at some point down line if desired."

The most common paradigm  for vision-based multi-object tracking is
tracking-by-detection,  due  to the availability of reliable detectors for
several important object categories such as cars and pedestrians. However,
future mobile  systems will need a capability to cope with rich human-made
environments, in which  obtaining detectors for every  possible object  category
would be infeasible. In this paper, we    propose a model-free multi-object
tracking approach that uses a category-agnostic image segmentation method to
track objects. We   present an efficient segmentation mask-based  tracker which
associates pixel-precise masks reported by the segmentation. Our approach can
utilize  semantic information whenever it  is available for classifying objects
at the track  level,  while  retaining  the capability to track   generic unknown
objects in  the absence of such information.  We demonstrate  experimentally that
our approach achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art
tracking-by-detection methods for popular object categories such as  cars and
pedestrians. Additionally, we show   that the proposed method can  discover and
robustly track a large  variety of other objects.

We have studied the Feasibility of single-Photon-Counting (SPC) x-ray
tomography with an X-ray Free-electron laser (XFEL) source of radiation.
In SPC, each photoelectron is counted individually during the radiography
and tomographic imaging of samples. The number of recorded photons per
pixel per projection then serves for photon counting which results in
substantial improvements for the Detection limits of low-contrast objects
compared to other x-ray detection techniques. For the first time, we give
an estimation of the x-ray photon flux needed for Practical implementation
of sPC x-ray tomography of low contrast objects using synchrotron x-ray and
XFEL sources of x-ray radiation as well as the technical issues needed
for realization. For this purpose, a Monte Carlo Simulation based on
a modified version of the program MCD [1] is used to simulate the
SXR-based tomographic set-up and a realistic model of the sPC detector
system has been created. A method of post-processing of the data in terms
of maximum-likelihood estimation is suggested to derive the desired Object
Image from the projections. For the tomogram of a test object (a sample
of gold and aluminum), we have determined the minimum detectable number Of
photons per pixel. As a First Estimate in the simulation, we take
100 photons per pixel as the threshold point above Which the tomography
of the sample is possible. The x-ray flux necessary to attain this photon
counting level can be as low as 109 photons per pixel for the aluminum
test object. However, it could have very drastic consequences if such an
experimental set-up Were to be realized. It would then make it possible
to achieve an SPC resolution of only a few nm (100 nm) in the region of
the tomogram corresponding to the most intense contrast of the reconstructed
image of a gold film on an aluminum substrate. Such a resolution may
be achieved by an SXR source but cannot be reached by using any xFEL
radiation. In

Incorporating multi-scale features in fully convolutional neural networks
(FCNs) has been a key element to achieving state-of-the-art performance on
semantic image segmentation. One common way to extract multi-scale features is
to feed multiple resized input images to a shared deep network and then merge
the resulting features for pixelwise classification. In this work, we propose
an attention mechanism that learns to softly weight the multi-scale features at
each pixel location. We adapt a state-of-the-art semantic image segmentation
model, which we jointly train with multi-scale input images and the attention
model. The proposed attention model not only outperforms average- and
max-pooling, but allows us to diagnostically visualize the importance of
features at different positions and scales. Moreover, we show that adding extra
supervision to the output at each scale is essential to achieving excellent
performance when merging multi-scale features. We demonstrate the effectiveness
of our model with extensive experiments on three challenging datasets,
including PASCAL-Person-Part, PASCAL VOC 7973 and a subset of MS-COCO 2014.

Introduction:
The aim of this research is to propose a real-time predictive maintenance platform for business vehicles using 3d printer technology. This solution will enable businesses to reduce downtime, optimise operations and improve maintenance budgeting by detecting equipment failures before they occur. The proposed system relies on a combination of sensors, data analytics, and machine learning algorithms to monitor vehicle performance in real-time, identify potential issues, and provide recommendations for preventative maintenance. In addition the use of 3d printer technology offers an efficient and cost effective way of repairing broken parts.

Methodology:
This study uses litreature review to gather information about existing technologies and approaches to predictive maintenance. A detailed analysis was conducted on various types of commercial vehicles currently in use and their associated components which need frequent maintenance and replacement. Data from different sources such as satellite imagery, sensor readings and historical records were collected, processed and analysed through machine learning and statistical techniques. Finally, 3d printer technology was integrated into the algorithm to generate custom designed and manufactured replacement parts on demand.

Results:
The results demonstrate that the proposed real-time predictive maintenance platform can effectively detect and diagnose issues before any significant damage occurs in business vehicles. Furthermore, the application of 3d printer technology provides an efficient and cost-effective means of replacing faulty or critical replacement parts. Results indicated that the platform reduced overall maintenance costs while also optimising fleet availability leading to improved operational efficiency.

Conclusion:
In conclusion, the implementation of a real-time predictive maintenance platform for business vehicles using 3d printer technology represents a major step forward in improving vehicle reliability and reducing maintenance costs. Further refinements and advancements of this systems are expected to have a positive impact on logistics industry overall.

We present an application of multi-mesh finite element methods as part of a
methodology for optimizing settlement layouts. By formulating a multi-objective
optimization problem, we demonstrate how a given number of buildings may be
optimally placed on a given piece of land with respect to both wind conditions
and the view experienced from the buildings. The wind flow is modeled by a
multi-mesh (cut finite element) method. This allows each building to be
embedded in a boundary-fitted mesh which can be moved freely on top of a fixed
background mesh. This approach enables a multitude of settlement layouts to be
evaluated without the need for costly mesh generation when changing the
configuration of buildings. The view is modeled by a measure that takes into
account the totality of unobstructed view from the collection of buildings, and
is efficiently computed by rasterization.

With the advent of powerful Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), semantic segmentation of images and videos has seen significant improvement in recent years. However, most existing methods are limited to segmenting a single object category in an image or video. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to joint segmentation and localization of multiple object categories in images and videos. Our method is based on a new method for foreground clustering, which we call foregroundness-aware clustering. 

This method is able to cluster foreground objects in an image or video, while also accounting for their spatial layout. 

We then use the clustered foreground objects to train a DNN for joint segmentation and localization. 

We evaluate our method on the challenging Youtube-Objects dataset, and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art methods for joint segmentation and localization.

Abstract

We propose an  algorithm that can be used to train structured SVMs (SVMs)  in  a cost-efficient manner, using only  a small  number  of  parameters and a large number of training examples. We show  how this algorithm can be applied to several different types of SVMs,  including  those trained on high dimensional data  sets, such as images or  audio files. Our approach  uses a multi-plane block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm to solve the   problem of finding the optimal  set of parameters  for each model. The proposed method has been  successfully  applied to several different types  of SVMs, including those  trained on high dimensional data   sets, such as  images or audio files. In particular,  we demonstrate that our method can  be used to train a hierarchical Bayesian network  (HBN), which  is a class of deep neural networks that are  able to learn complex features from sparse input data.  We also  present a novel implementation of our method that allows us to use a single GPU  to  perform all of the training steps simultaneously.

Acquisition-to-acquisition signal intensity variations (non-standardness) are
inherent in MR images. Standardization is a post processing method for
correcting inter-subject intensity variations through transforming all images
from the given image gray scale into a standard gray scale wherein similer
intensities acheive similer tissue meanings. The lack of a standard image
intensity scale in MRI leads to many difficulties in tissue characterizability,
image display, and analysis, including image segmentation. This phenomenon has
been documented well; however, effects of standardization on medical image
registration have not been studied yet. In this paper, we investigate the
influence of intensity standardization in registration tasks with systematic
and analytic evaluations involving clinical MR images. We conducted nearly
20,000 clinical MR image registration experiments and evaluated the quality of
registrations both quantitatively and qualitatively. The evaluations show that
intensity variations between images degrades the accuracy of registration
performance. The results imply that the accuracy of image registration not only
depends on spatial and geometric similarity but also on the similarity of the
intensity values for the same tissues in different images.

"Feedback attention is the use of knowledge from a previous module to the current module. We observed this frequently occurs in biomedical image segmentation tasks, including neuron finding tasks, where neural path tracing often starts with a seed neuron and progressively expands, and cell nucleus segmentation tasks in which a cell nucleus can be segmented iteratively. This inspires us to design a Feedback Attention Network (FANet) by modifying U-Net, which is the base architecture for many biomedical image segmentation architectures. Specifically, we extend the shortcut connection in U-Net by replacing summation with addition, so the output of each shortcut connection is the summation of the features from the encoder and the decoder. Besides, we propose several modifications to the base FANet architecture by incorporating the decoder’s output and features, and adding feed-forward attention blocks for capturing long-distance information. Our proposed model is able to integrate both the short-distance and long-distance information in the network. We show that the proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmark biomedical image segmentation tasks: (1) SegTran\cite{schmitt2018segment_}, (1) N-MNIST\cite{oroojlooyjadid9871cell, kulis_2019_segment} and (3) EM-WSI\cite{shivashankar2019finding_neurons}".

To the best of our knowledge, we have not found any previous research that proposed using a model to integrate features using a similar methodology to what we describe in this paper. A popular architecture used for medical image segmentation is U-Net\cite{ronneberger8375unet} in which the shortcut feature connection employs the summation of the outputs from both encoder and decoder. We found FANet that incorporates both short and long range information in the network to be most relevant and similar to our proposed methodology. However, in reality, the existing methodology is quite different, in that FANet is not a complete feedforward model, but is rather a partial feedback system. That is, the feedback is restricted only to the encoder part of the model, and not to the decoder part of the model. 

In this paper, we demonstrate that by adding a feedback mechanism to a model for medical image segmentation, we can obtain significant

Βоundаrу іnfоrmаtіоn рlауs а sіgnіfісаnt rоlе іn 2D іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, whіlе
usuаllу bеіng іgnоrеd іn 3D роіnt сlоud sеgmеntаtіоn whеrе аmbіguоus fеаturеs
mіght bе gеnеrаtеd іn fеаturе ехtrасtіоn, lеаdіng tо mіsсlаssіfісаtіоn іn thе
trаnsіtіоn аrеа bеtwееn twо оbjесts. Ιn thіs рареr, fіrstlу, wе рrороsе а
Воundаrу Рrеdісtіоn Μоdulе (ВΡМ) tо рrеdісt bоundаrу роіnts. Ваsеd оn thе
рrеdісtеd bоundаrу, а bоundаrу-аwаrе Gеоmеtrіс Εnсоdіng Μоdulе (GΕΜ) іs
dеsіgnеd tо еnсоdе gеоmеtrіс іnfоrmаtіоn аnd аggrеgаtе fеаturеs wіth
dіsсrіmіnаtіоn іn а nеіghbоrhооd, sо thаt thе lосаl fеаturеs bеlоngіng tо
dіffеrеnt саtеgоrіеs wіll nоt bе роllutеd bу еасh оthеr. Τо рrоvіdе ехtrа
gеоmеtrіс іnfоrmаtіоn fоr bоundаrу-аwаrе GΕΜ, wе аlsо рrороsе а lіght-wеіght
Gеоmеtrіс Соnvоlutіоn Οреrаtіоn (GСО), mаkіng thе ехtrасtеd fеаturеs mоrе
dіstіnguіshіng. Вuіlt uроn thе bоundаrу-аwаrе GЕΜ, wе buіld оur nеtwоrk аnd
tеst іt оn bеnсhmаrks lіkе SсаnΝеt v2, S3DІS. Rеsults shоw оur mеthоds саn
sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvе thе bаsеlіnе аnd асhіеvе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе.
Соdе іs аvаіlаblе аt httрs://gіthub.соm/JсhеnΧu/ВоundаrуАwаrеGΕМ.

A large sample of well-annotated data is required for a good result in medical image segmentation, but it is difficult, costly, and time-consuming to collect. It is possible to ease the burden of annotation by exploiting inexpensive but weak supervision, such as bounding boxes and landmarks, but it is difficult to calculate, a priori, the optimal balance between the two types of annotation to achieve the best performance with the lowest costs of annotation. We therefore propose a framework for the automatic selection of annotated images based on the costs of annotation, in the case of a mixed annotation with strong and weak supervision. The annotated images are selected based on a linear programming formulation, and a strategy of ranking them according to the likelihood of the supervision they provide, with the aim of achieving the best performance. In the experiments we demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable results to the state-of-the-art with a greatly reduced cost of annotation.

Healthcare sector is totally different from other industry. It is on high
priority sector and people expect highest level of care and services regardless
of cost. It did not achieve social expectation even though it consume huge
percentage of budget. Mostly interpretations of medical data is being done
by medical expert. In terms of image interpretation by human expert, it is
quite limited due to its subjectivity, complexity of the image, extensive
variations exist across different interpreters, and fatigue. After the success
of deep learning in other real world application, it is also providing exciting
solutions with good accuracy for medical imaging and is seen as a key method
for future applications in health secotr. In this chapter, we discussed state
of the art deep learning architecture and its optimization used for medical
image segmentation and classification. In last section, we have discussed
the challenges deep learning based methods for medical imaging and open
research issue.

This paper reports Deep LOGISMOS approach to 3D tumor segmentation by
incorporating boundary information derived from deep contextual learning to
LOGISMOS - layered optimal graph image segmentation of multiple objects and
surfaces. Accurate and reliable tumor segmentation is essential to tumor growth
analysis and treatment selection. A fully convolutional network (FCN), UNet, is
first trained using three adjacent 2D patches centered at the tumor, providing
contextual UNet segmentation and probability map for each 2D patch. The UNet
segmentation is then refined by Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) and morphological
operations. The refined UNet segmentation is used to provide the initial shape
boundary to build a segmentation graph. The cost for each node of graph is
determined by UNet probability maps. Finally, max-flow algorithm is
employed to find globally optimal solution thus obtaining the final
segmentation. For evaluation, we applied the method to pancreatic tumor
segmentation on dataset of 51 CT scans, among which 30 scans were used for
training and 21 for testing. With Deep LOGISMOS, DICE Similarity Coefficient
(DSC) and Relative Volume Difference (RVD) reached 83.2+-7.8% and 18.6+-17.4%
respectively, both are significantly improved (p<0.05) compared with contextual
UNet and/or LOGISMOS alone.

This academic paper presents a comprehensive study on non-LTE radiative transfer in cool stars, with a focus on its theoretical foundations and practical applications in abundance analysis for 24 chemical elements. The paper discusses the importance of non-LTE effects in accurately determining the chemical composition of cool stars, and outlines the methods used to model these effects in theoretical models. 

The paper also presents a detailed analysis of the abundance results for 24 chemical elements, highlighting the impact of non-LTE effects on the derived abundances. Overall, this paper provides valuable insights into the complex processes governing the formation and evolution of cool stars, and demonstrates the importance of accounting for non-LTE effects in their abundance analysis.

State-of-the-art semantic image segmentation methods are mostly based on
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). In this work, we proffer to
improve semantic segmentation with the use of contextual information. In
particular, we explore `patch-patch' context and `patch-background' context in
deep CNNs. We formulate deep structured models by combining CNNs and
Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) for learning the patch-patch context between
image regions. Specifically, we formulate CNN-based pairwise potential
functions to capture semantic correlations between neighbouring patches.
Efficient piecewise training of the proposed deep structured model is then
applied in order to avoid repeated expensive CRF inference during the course of
back propagation. For capturing the patch-background context, we show that a
network design with traditional multi-scale image inputs and sliding pyramid
pooling is very effective for improving performance. We perform comprehensive
evaluation of the proposed method. We achieve new state-of-the-art performance
on a number of challenging semantic segmentation datasets including $NYUDv2$,
$PASCAL$-$VOC2012$, $Cityscapes$, $PASCAL$-$Context$, $SUN$-$RGBD$,
$SIFT$-$flow$, and $KITTI$ datasets. Particularly, we report an
intersection-over-union score of $77.8$ on the $PASCAL$-$VOC2012$ dataset.
