THis paper deals with the interplay between passing to finite covers and the ergodic properties of the straight-line flow on translation surfaces of finite area with infinite topology. The infinite topology of these surfaces provides a rich family of finite covers of degree d for any positive d > 1. We show by examples that passing to a finite cover can destroy the ergodicity, but we also give evidence that this phenomenon is rather rare. We introduce a natural notion of random cover of degree d, and we show that in many cases the ergodicity and the unique ergodicity are preserved under the random passage. This study provides a new setting for examining the relationship between recurrence of the Teichmüller flow and the ergodic properties of the straight-line flow.

Abstract:
In this paper, we present a new approach to the determination of the magnetocaloric effect in a
coherent electromagnetic field in a spin-1/2 antiferromagnet. By exploiting the weak magnetic
exclusion coefficient in the spin-1/2 magnetic properties, the magnetic properties are reduced in the
direction of the magnetic field. As a consequence, the magnetic field is strongly amplified at the pole of the
fiber, and the magnetic field intensity of the polarization in all directions is reduced as opposed to normal
electromagnetics.
The spin-1/2 antiferromagnet is used to calculate the magnetic field intensity as predicted for the magnetic
field in normal ferromagnetic materials. The resulting magnetic field intensity is significantly higher than
normal ferromagnetic materials in the following field directions:
Invershadowing effect between
polarities (from -0.3 to +0.3)
Inverse polarity effect between opposite pole (0.3 to +0.1)
Magnetic coupling and magnetocaloric effect (with or without super-exchange)
Results:
While the original spin-1/2 antiferromagnet is a spin-1/2 magnetic material, its strong magnetocaloric effect has been observed in
other ferromagnetic materials. We have used our approach to study the magnetic field in the spin-2 ferromagnetic
material. The obtained super-exchange antiferromagnetic effect is more than five orders of magnitude stronger than a
normal ferromagnetic material. It is more than ten orders of magnitude stronger than the reverse magnetocaloric effect
in the direction of the magnetic field. The observed super-exchange antiferromagnetic effect is independent of rotation
and the direction of magnetic field.
The super-exchange antiferromagnet is suitable for the measurement of high-frequency transverse magnetism, such as
for acoustic or acoustic-electric measurements to investigate the nature of the magnetic field in transverse
materials. The observed super-exchange antiferromagnetic effect in the magnetic field can be directly applied to
the magnetic coupling of magnetic materials in conventional ferromagnetic materials. The magnetocaloric

Active contour models have been widely used in image segmentation, and the
level set method (LSM) is the most popular approach for solving the models, via
implicitly representing the contour by a level set function. However, the LSM
suffers from high computational burden and numerical instability, requiring
additional regularization terms or re-initialization techniques. In this paper,
we use characteristic functions to implicitly represent the contours, propose a
new representation to the geodesic active contours and derive an efficient
algorithm termed as the iterative convolution-thresholding method (ICTM).
Compared to the LSM, the ICTM is simpler and much more efficient. In addition,
the ICTM enjoys most desired features of the level set-based methods. Extensive
experiments, on 0D synthetic, 2D ultrasound, 1D CT, and 3D MR images for
nodule, organ and lesion segmentation, demonstrate that the proposed method not
only obtains comparable or even better segmentation results (compared to the
LSM) but also achieves significant acceleration.

The CAKEs method is proposed in this study to improve efficiency in 3D networks. It utilizes channel-wise automatic kernel shrinking, which allows for efficient network design while maintaining accuracy. This approach can be applied to various applications and has potential for further optimization in future research.

Binary image segmentation plays an important role in computer vision and has
been widely used in many applications such as image and Video editing, object
extraction, and photo composition. In this paper, we propose a novel
interactive binary image segmentation method based On the Markov Random Field
(MRF) Framework and the fast bilateral solver (FBS) technique. Specifically, we
employ the geodesic distance component to Build the unary term. To ensure both
computation efficiency and effective responsiveness for interactive
segmentation, superpixels are used In computing Geodesic distances instead of
pixels. Furthermore, we take a bilateral affinity approach for the pairwise
term in order to preserve edge information And denoise. Through the alternating
direction strategy, the MRF energy minimization problem is divided into two
subproblems, which then can be easily solved by steepest gradient descent (SGD)
and FBS respectively. Experimental results on the VGG interactive image
segmentation dataset show that the proposed algorithm outperforms several
state-of-the-art ones, and in particular, it can achieve satisfactory
edge-smooth segmentation results even when the Foreground and background color
appearances are quite indistinctive.

In this paper, we present a multi-label deep supervision (MLDS) approach for medical image segmentation. The MLDS approach utilizes multiple labels for each pixel in the image to improve the segmentation performance. Our method is based on the observation that, in medical images, pixels often have more than two possible labels, and the labels of interest are usually a subset of the total number of labels. We propose to use a deep neural network (DNN) with multiple output layers, each corresponding to a different label, to learn a segmentation model that can take advantage of the rich information provided by the multiple labels. Our method is evaluated on the ISBI 2017 Challenge 3: Cell Segmentation, where it achieves the best performance in terms of the F1-score.

The paper discusses the challenges of segmenting cells in bright field images, where cells have complex shapes and overlapping structures. The proposed approach is a multi-label deep learning segmentation method that uses a U-Net architecture with an additional supervision term. The supervision term is a multi-label classification loss that encourages the network to produce a soft segmentation mask, with each pixel labeled with the IDs of all the cells it belongs to. The approach is evaluated on the ISBI 2017 Challenge 3 dataset and compared to other state-of-the-art methods.

The results show that the proposed method achieves the best performance in terms of the F1-score. The F1-score is a measure of the overlap between the ground truth and the predicted labels. The proposed method achieves an F1-score of 0.83, which is a significant improvement over the previous state-of-the-art method, which has an F1-score of 0.79. The proposed method is also able to run in about half the time of the previous state-of-the-art method.

Left atrium shape has Been shown to be an independent predictor of recurrence
after atrial fibrillation (AF) ablation. Shape-based representation is
Imperative to such an estimation process, where correspondence-based
representation offers the most flexibility and ease-of-computation for
population-level shape statistics. Nonetheless, population-level shape
representations in the form of image segmentation and correspondence models
derived from cardiac MRI require significant human resources with sufficient
anatomy-specific expertise. In this paper, we propose a machine learning
approach that uses deep networks to estimate AF Recurrence by predicting shape
descriptors directly from MRI images, with NO image pre-processing involved. We
also propose a novel data augmentation scheme to effectively train a deep
network in A limited training data setting. We compare this new method of
estimating shape descriptors from images with The state-of-the-art
correspondence-based shape modeling that requires image segmentation and
correspondence optimization. Results show that the proposed method and the
current state-of-the-art produce statistically similar outcomes on AF
recurrence, eliminating The need for expensive pre-processing pipelines and
Associated Human labor.

This paper provides a novel approach to spectral imagery (HSI) classification that uses deep metric learning (DML) and conditional random field (CRF). The proposed approach demonstrates the strengths of DML in learning discriminative features and CRF in modeling geographic context. The DML is designed to extract deep features from HSIs, which are then fed into the CRF to incorporate spatial information for final classification. Experimental findings on many benchmark HSI databases demonstrate that our methodology outperforms state-of-the-art HSI classification methods in terms of overall reliability, average precision, and kappa coefficient. This research offers a new insight on HSI classification, showing the possibility of using deep learning and computational graphical models.

Using three L3-type terahertz time-projection chambers, along the Booster Neutrino Beam at Fermilab, we propose a short-baseline program that will place the world's most powerful search for sterile neutrinos, both in appearance and in disappearance channels, on the scale of eVs. Using the already available 1400e- and 2800e- eVs of effective sensitivity in the LAr-I ND and ICARUS T600 devices, plus a further 1320e-27 effective sensitivity in the upcoming L3-MicroBooNE experiment, we estimate a 5-sigma sensitivity for the appearance of muon- to electron-like particles. In this report, we present the physics analysis, the design of the LAr-ND device, the re-design of the T600 device, the necessary equipment to carry out the program, and the possibility of changing the target and the horns of the BNB to improve the oscillation performance.

Although having achieved great success in medical image segmentation, deep
convolutional neural networks usually require a large dataset with manual
annotations for training and are difficult to generalize to unseen classes. Few-shot learning has the potential to address these challenges by learning new
classes from only a few labeled examples. In this work, we propose a new
framework for few-shot medical image segmentation based on prototypical
networks. Our innovation lies in the design of two key modules: 1) a context
relation encoder (CRE) that uses correlation to capture local relation features
between foreground and background regions; and 2) a recurrent mask refinement
module that repeatedly uses the CRE and a prototypical network to recapture the
change of context relationship and refine the segmentation mask iteratively. 

Experiments on two abdomen CT datasets and an abdomen MRI dataset show the
proposed method obtains substantial improvement over the state-of-the-art
methods by an average of 16.32%, 8.45% and 6.24% in terms of DSC, respectively. 

Code is publicly available.

Existing automatic 3D image segmentation techniques often fail to meet the
clinic use. Several researchers have explored an interactive approach to reduce the
image segmentation efficiency by iteratively adding user hints.
Unfortunately, the static process for successive interactions is generally neglected. We
here intend to model the static process of iterative interactive image
segmentation as a Markov decision procedure (MDP) and solve it with reinforcement
learning (RL). However, it is intractable to use single-agent RL for
voxel-wise analysis due to the small discovery space. To improve the
discovery space to a tractable size, we treat each voxel as an agent with a
shared voxel-level behavior strategy so that it can be solved with multi-agent
reinforcement learning. An additional benefit of this multi-agent model is to
capture the dependency among voxels for segmentation task. However, to improve
the information of previous segmentations, we reserve the prediction
uncertainty in the state space of MDP and derive an adjustment action space
leading to a more detailed and finer segmentation. In addition, to reduce the
effectiveness of exploration, we design a relative cross-entropy gain-based reward
to update the policy in a constrained direction. Experimental findings on
multiple scientific datasets have demonstrated that our approach greatly outperforms
existing state-of-the-art techniques, with the benefit of greater interactions and
a slower convergence.

Οnе рорulаr аррrоасh tо іntеrасtіvеlу sеgmеnt thе fоrеgrоund оbjесt оf
іntеrеst frоm аn іmаgе іs tо аnnоtаtе а bоundіng bох thаt соvеrs thе fоrеgrоund
оbjесt. Τhеn, а bіnаrу lаbеlіng іs реrfоrmеd tо асhіеvе а rеfіnеd sеgmеntаtіоn.
Оnе mаjоr іssuе оf thе ехіstіng аlgоrіthms fоr suсh іntеrасtіvе іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn іs thеіr рrеfеrеnсе оf аn іnрut bоundіng bох thаt tіghtlу еnсlоsеs
thе fоrеgrоund оbjесt. Τhіs іnсrеаsеs thе аnnоtаtіоn burdеn, аnd рrеvеnts thеsе
аlgоrіthms frоm utіlіzіng аutоmаtісаllу dеtесtеd bоundіng bохеs. Ιn thіs рареr,
wе dеvеlор а nеw LооsеСut аlgоrіthm thаt саn hаndlе саsеs whеrе thе іnрut
bоundіng bох оnlу lооsеlу соvеrs thе fоrеgrоund оbjесt. Wе рrороsе а nеw Маrkоv
Rаndоm Fіеlds (ΜRF) mоdеl fоr sеgmеntаtіоn wіth lооsеlу bоundеd bохеs,
іnсludіng а glоbаl sіmіlаrіtу соnstrаіnt tо bеttеr dіstіnguіsh thе fоrеgrоund
аnd bасkgrоund, аnd аn аddіtіоnаl еnеrgу tеrm tо еnсоurаgе соnsіstеnt lаbеlіng
оf sіmіlаr-арреаrаnсе ріхеls. Τhіs ΜRF mоdеl іs thеn sоlvеd bу аn іtеrаtеd
mах-flоw аlgоrіthm. Іn thе ехреrіmеnts, wе еvаluаtе LооsеСut іn thrее
рublісlу-аvаіlаblе іmаgе dаtаsеts, аnd соmраrе іts реrfоrmаnсе аgаіnst sеvеrаl
stаtе-оf-thе-аrt іntеrасtіvе іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn аlgоrіthms. Wе аlsо shоw thаt
LооsеСut саn bе usеd fоr еnhаnсіng thе реrfоrmаnсе оf unsuреrvіsеd vіdео
sеgmеntаtіоn аnd іmаgе sаlіеnсу dеtесtіоn.

At  present, adversarial attacks  are designed in a task-specific fashion.
However, for downstream computer vision  tasks such as  image captioning, image
segmentation etc., the current deep learning systems use an image classifier
like VGG16, ResNet50,  Inception-v3 etc. as a  feature extractor. Keeping this in
mind, we  propose Mimic and Fool, a task  agnostic adversarial attack. Given  a
feature extractor, the proposed attack finds an adversarial image  which can
mimic the  image  feature of  the original image. This ensures that the two images
give the  same (or similar) output regardless of  the  task. We randomly select
1000  MSCOCO validation images for experimentation. We perform experiments on
two image captioning models, Show and Tell, Show Attend and Tell  and one VQA
model, namely,  end-to-end neural module  network (N2NMN).  The proposed attack
achieves success  rate of 74.0%, 81.0% and 87.1% for Show  and  Tell,  Show Attend
and Tell and N2NMN respectively.  We also propose a slight  modification to our
attack to generate natural-looking adversarial  images. In  addition, we also
show the applicability of the proposed attack for invertible architecture.
Since Mimic and Fool only requires information about the feature extractor of
the  model,  it  can  be considered as  a gray-box   attack.

We present a generalized and scalable method, called Gen-LaneNet, to detect
3D lanes from a single image. The method, inspired by the latest
state-of-the-art 3D-LaneNet, is a unified framework solving image encoding,
spatial transform of features and 3D lane prediction in a single network. However, we propose unique designs for Gen-LaneNet in two folds. 

First, we
introduce a new geometry-guided lane anchor representation in a new coordinate
frame and apply a specific geometric transformation to directly calculate real
3D lane points from the network output. 

We demonstrate that aligning the lane
points with the underlying top-view features in the new coordinate frame is
critical towards a generalized method in handling unfamiliar scenes. 

Second, we
present a scalable two-stage framework that decouples the learning of image
segmentation subnetwork and geometry encoding subnetwork. Compared to
3D-LaneNet, the proposed Gen-LaneNet drastically reduces the amount of 3D lane
labels required to achieve a robust solution in real-world application. Moreover, we release a new synthetic dataset and its construction strategy to
encourage the development and evaluation of 3D lane detection methods. 

In
experiments, we conduct extensive ablation study to substantiate the proposed
Gen-LaneNet significantly outperforms 3D-LaneNet in average precision(AP) and
F-score.

An integral equation is formulated to describe electromagnetic wave
transmission through a sub-wavelength nano-hole in a thin plasmonic sheet in
terms of the dyadic Green's function for the associated Helmholtz problem.
Taking the subwavelength radius of the nano-hole to be the smallest length of
the system, we have obtained an exact solution of the integral equation for the
dyadic Green's function analytically and in closed form. This dyadic Green's
function is then employed in the numerical analysis of electromagnetic wave
transmission through the nano-hole for normal incidence of the incoming wave
train. The electromagnetic transmission involves two distinct contributions,
one emanating from the nano-hole and the other is directly transmitted through
the thin plasmonic layer itself (which would not occur in the case of a perfect
metal screen). The transmitted radiation exhibits interference fringes in the
vicinity of the nano-hole, and they tend to flatten as a function of increasing
lateral separation from the hole, reaching the uniform value of transmission
through the sheet alone at large separations.

> Abstract We introduced FBISeg(Facebook Beauty with Image Segmentation challenge), which is an image segmentation contest that aims at enhancing Facebook advertising effectiveness by detecting human faces in beauty images. This task also provides a more rigorous way to evaluate how good a model can generalize, as there exist many variations of faces such as pose and occlusion. To support this large scale competition, we are building up what we call Open Data Collection (ODC). ODC has over 120,836 training samples collected on-device in a short period. Additionally, it contains approximately 41k validated labels. On top of this data collection effort, we curate several challenging datasets for testing the proposed models. Aside from label noise issues due to crowd sourcing tasks, the results have shown promising performance on state-of-the-art methods across all our test sets under various evaluation metrics, including average intersection over union (aIoU) and average precision (AP). All of the code and data is publicly accessible to ease further researches.

When using object-oriented frameworks it is easy to overlook certain
important method calls that are required at particular places in code. In this
paper, we provide a comprehensive set of empirical facts on this problem,
starting from traces of missing method calls in a bug repository. We propose a
new system that searches for missing method calls in software based on the
other method calls that are observable. Our key insight is that the voting
theory concept of majority rule holds for method calls: a call is likely to be
missing if there is a majority of similar pieces of code where this call is
present. The evaluation shows that the system predictions go further missing
method calls and often reveal different kinds of code smells (e.g. violations
of API best practises).

LIDAR point cloud and RGB images are both crucial for 3D object detection, and many modern 3D detection algorithms rely on combining these two data sources. However, the methods of combining them are ineffective, using the “Bird’s Eye View” or “Voxel” formats, which lack the accuracy of the actual triangulation. The proposed method fuses multi-source features at the 3D points directly, instead of using the Bird’s Eye View format. Besides continuous convolution, point pooling and attention aggregation are used to make the fused features more precise. In addition, based on the PACF, we propose a 3D multi-source, multi-task network: pointcloud-image RNN (PI-RCNN for short). This network uses an image segmentation layer to extract high-resolution semantic maps from images, and then combines the features of different sources with a powerful PACF. The combination of the power of PACF and the rich semantic features of the segmentation layer leads to great improvements in 3D detection. We tested PI-RCNN on the KITTI 3D test set, and obtained the best results in terms of 3D Ap.

This paper describes a method for dividing images into a number of distinct areas, corresponding to the different objects and edges in the image. This method, which makes use of a fuzzy C-means method to divide the image, is combined with a self-tuning method, which determines the number of sections required for a given image. The self-tuning method is driven by a client-specified error function which measures the quality of the resulting division. We show how our method works with a number of different images, and how it compares favourably with other existing methods. Fuzzy image division has been an active research topic for many years, and a variety of approaches have been proposed for various applications. This paper extends the fuzzy C-means method, a popular fuzzy image division method, by adding a self-tuning process, which dynamically determines the number of divisions required for a given image. The self-tuning process is driven by a client-specified error function, which measures the quality of the division. The result is that the division of the image is always the best possible for the particular image. We demonstrate that our method produces images with a superior quality to those obtained by other methods. Our method demonstrates that the self-tuning process is able to adapt to the image content, and therefore produces images that are better at capturing the different objects and edges in the image. The proposed method has potential applications in a variety of fields, including image processing, pattern recognition and image processing. The method can be used to divide images for further analysis and processing, such as object detection, recognition and tracking. It can also be used to divide medical images, satellite images and other images in other applications that require accurate and robust division.

We leverage recent developments in deep learning to generate segmentations on pediatric MRIs, with particular focus towards infants (preterm cohort aged 1 day - 180 days at baseline). This work explores different variations between two families : 3D-CNNs that aggregate information across all three image axes versus 2DDNs where convolutional layers are spatially constrained within each slice ; ensembling strategies like early fusion or late voting vs training multiple single networks; how label distribution affects performance due not only number classes but also by class frequency imbalance which further varies depending whether considering labels defined per region level such as left anterior occipital gray matter ROI rather than voxels. Our experiments show significant advantage using large datasets compared small ones, utilizing multi modality images can improve accuracy substantially however increasing overlap among them decreases their usefulness if high amount missingness occurs then this becomes necessary because it allows keeping track individual case instead ignoring these patients altogether since there might be cases without scans available unlike other methods making more assumptions about what they contain before knowing everything firsthand thus reducing potential errors arising out differences inherent various sources medical imaging e

One  of  the  key drawbacks of 3D convolutional neural networks for  segmentation
is their  memory footprint,  which necessitates compromises in  the network
architecture in  order  to fit  into a given memory budget. Motivated by the
RevNet for image classification, we propose  a partially reversible U-Net
architecture that reduces memory consumption  substantially. The reversible
architecture allows us to exactly recover each layer's outputs from the
subsequent  layer's ones, eliminating the need to store activations  for
backpropagation. This alleviates the biggest memory  bottleneck and enables  very
deep (theoretically  infinitely deep) 3D  architectures. On the BraTS  challenge
dataset, we  demonstrate substantial memory savings. We further  show that the
freed memory can be used for  processing the whole  field-of-view (FOV) instead
of patches.  Increasing network depth led to higher segmentation accuracy while
growing the memory  footprint only by a very small fraction, thanks to the
partially reversible architecture.

> Biomedical image segmentation is a challenging task due to the natural variability in the appearance of objects for a given class. Fully Convolutional networks (FCNs) have been recently proposed for this task, but obtaining high-quality segmentation masks is difficult. One of the major issues with these techniques is the large quantity of computation required to train and inference a convolutional neural network (CNN), which requires high computational cost and high memory usage. There are other disadvantages too, such as, network accuracy decreases with decreasing quantization bits. A number of attempts to reduce computations involved in CNN have been made during recent years. These methods are broadly categorized in two groups based on data-dependent analysis and data-independent analysis. In this work, we propose a method based on the second catagory for reducing computation involved in FCN. We demonstrate that by reducing the number of quantization bits per channel in a FCN model, the network reduces in size and speed without compromising accuracy. We perform our experiments on different biomedical datasets taken from Image Segmentation Competition for Medical Biology 2014 and the Audiokinetic Datasets.

In this paper, we investigate the longitudinal and spin/valley Hall optical conductivity of double layer copper disulfide ($MoS_{2}$) Using a mix of theoretical and computational methods.

Experimentally, we measure the optical thickness of single layer $MoS_{2}$ using a mix of spectral ellipsometry and reflectometry methods. We observe a weak dependence of the optical conductivity on the angle of incidence, which we attribute to the anisotropic crystal structure of $MoS_{2}$. We therefore see a slight spin/valley polarization of the optical conductivity, which we find to be highly dependent on the energy of the incident light.

Theoretically, we use a first-principles method based on density functional theory to calculate the optical thickness of single layer $MoS_{2}$. We find great agreement between our theoretical and experimental findings, and utilize the numerical calculations to explain the contributions of various electronic transitions to the optical conductivity. We find that the optical conductivity is characterized by interband transitions, which are highly anisotropic due to the crystal structure of $MoS_{2}$.

Our findings offer new insights into the optical properties of single layer $MoS_{2}$, and identify the possibility of this material for applications in optical systems. We therefore highlight the necessity of examining the anisotropic crystal composition of $MoS_{2}$ in the construction and analysis of optical systems based on this material.

Overall, our research offers a detailed knowledge of the longitudinal and spin/valley Hall optical conductivity of double layer $MoS_{2}$, and paves the way for further studies of the optical properties of this promising 2D material.

"Nonlinear metamaterials are emerging as an important tool for increasing the control complexity of light. Examples in particular involve atomically thin layers of metamaterials embedded in a complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) CMOS platform; in-plane metamaterials and in-plane waveguides; and optical-frequency-modulated photonic-switch metamaterials. In the present paper, we present the concept and implementation of nonlinear metamaterial (NLM) consisting of atomic layer deposited second-order nonlinear optical metamaterials. We describe synthesis, characterization and in-plane (i.p.) optical-frequency controlled metamaterial for high resolution photonic systems which can be integrated into CMOS CMOS CMOS platforms for back-end integration with nonlinear computing modules.
The LMN consists of three distinct layers of optical metamaterials, i.e. first-order nonlinear optical elements (NLEs) and an active (NLEA) layer in three dimensions (3D) that interacts with NLEs. These components can be stacked and stacked many times and are tuned in dimensionally variable ways such that different types of materials can be stacked on one another. Two independent layers of NLEs are positioned 1/2 wavelength apart (i.e., at 2.7nm and at 8.3nm). The N LEs are separated by second layer (NLEB) consisting of active (NLEA) layer and optical cavity (OVC). The OVC allows NLEB to interact with NLEs on the other side of that wavelength from it. As OVC is illuminated by the NLE in the NLEB, its emission is tunable. We have implemented this technique on a microfabricated 3D wafer to drive a multi-function pixel detector.
The NLE-OVC scheme, however, can be further simplified by stacking layers of nonlinear optical elements. In this paper, we report fabrication of two layers of linear photonic devices—a linear phase waveguiding (PWG) chip and second-order nonlinear optical device, (NLE

We analyzed the temporal spatial variation of the spatial patterns in an image when each pixel in the whole image was used either to identify a distinct feature or to indicate a distinct location corresponding to a location. The temporal variation showed that the location of specific features and features in space were well-defined, as evidenced by the strong correspondence between the spatial patterns and the feature vectors extracted from the images. Moreover, we found that for the spatial analysis, both real and pseudo-randomly sampled locations were consistently found. We also find a similar correspondence between the location of specific features and feature vectors extracted from the images. In particular, we found that the locations of individual features like the location and/or orientation of a person were well-defined in images after some time. After a small number of iterations, the location of features was found to become a continuous function of the pixel intensity; however, the location of particular features (like facial features) remained constant in both small and large populations, implying that they become a continuous function after some number of iterations. The correspondence between the location of features in spatial space and their corresponding features in real space suggests that spatially-oriented features and/or objects can be categorized into discrete categories. We refer to such discrete categories as location categories and feature clusters.

The research described by Schönbom is one of a number of recent attempts to analyze the nature of the "localization" principle in images. In this research, Schönbom's team used two methods, one that applied a high-dimensional feature mapping approach to the image, and one that used image classification techniques to group and characterize locations. The first approach is the "real-time region analysis" approach described here and can be found in this 2235 paper, while the second approach is the "synthetic spatial correlation" approach described here and in this 2011 paper for an even more detailed comparison.

The technique we're using here, called Image Localization Techniques, uses an image segmentation algorithm called the "Tobin–Johnson Method" which is commonly used with the "real-time region analysis" approaches above.

The Tobin–Johnson method is a highly popular technique that works by recognizing a sequence of images of high-dimensional structures in an image and breaking the image into thousands or millions of "feature clusters". For example, the Tob

Title: Suggestive Annotation of Brain Tumor Images with Gradient-guided Sampling

Background: Accurate annotation is a critical step in medical image analysis, particularly for brain tumor diagnosis and treatment planning. However, human effort to manually label these images can be both time-consuming and error-prone. This paper proposes a novel method called gradient-guided sampling (GGS) that can effectively suggest annotations to reduce manual efforts while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.

Methods: Our approach leverages image representations learned by deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). From each feature map within CNN, we generate directional gradients that capture spatial relationships between neighboring voxels. We then use gradient magnitude to sample regions from input image that are most likely to contain relevant features. Finally, we combine these samples into probabilistic labels using a random forest classifier with kNN sampling to select candidate annotators.

Results: On two separate datasets, our GGS scheme outperforms traditional random assignment techniques with comparable computational complexity. Moreover, we observe improved agreement among suggested annotation paths compared to standard labeling workflow without GGS. 

Conclusion: Our results demonstrate that gradient-guided sampling can significantly improve efficiency and accuracy of annotation processes for medical imaging tasks such as brain tumor analysis.

It  is well-known that the  category of comodules over a  flat Hopf algebroid is
abelian but typically fails to  have enough projectives, and more generally, the
category of graded comodules over a graded flat Hopf algebroid is abelian but
typically fails to have  enough projectives. In this short paper we  prove that
the category  of connective graded  comodules over  a connective, graded,  flat,
finite-type Hopf  algebroid has enough projectives. Applications to algebraic
topology are given: the Hopf algebroids of stable  co-operations in  complex
bordism,  Brown-Peterson homology,  and classical mod $p$ homology all have the
property that their categories of connective  graded comodules  have  enough
projectives. We also prove  that categories of connective graded comodules over
appropriate Hopf algebras fail  to be equivalent to  categories of  graded
connective modules   over a ring.

Deep convolutional Neural networks have significantly boosted the Performance
of fundus image segmentation when test datasets have the same distribution as
the training datasets. However, in clinical practice, medical images often
exhibit variations in appearance for various reasons, e.g., different scanner
vendors and image quality. These distribution discrepancies could lead the deep
networks to over-fit on the training datasets And lack generalization ability
on the unseen test datasets. To alleviate this issue, we Present a novel
Domain-oriented Feature Embedding (DoFE) framework to improve the
generalization ability of CNNs on unseen target domains by exploring the
knowledge from multiple source domains. Our DoFE framework dynamically Enriches
the image features with additional domain prior knowledge learned from
multi-source domains to make the semantic features more discriminative.
Specifically, we introduce a Domain Knowledge Pool to learn and memorize the
prior information extracted from multi-source domains. Then the original image
features are augmented with domain-oriented aggregated features, which are
induced from the knowledge Pool based on the similarity between the Input image
and multi-source domain images. we Further design a novel domain code
prediction branch to Infer this similarity and employ An attention-guided
mechanism to dynamically combine the aggregated features with the semantic
features. We comprehensively evaluate our DoFE framework on two fundus image
segmentation tasks, including the optic cup and disc segmentation and vessel
segmentation. Our DoFE framework generates Satisfying segmentation results on
unseen datasets and surpasses other domain generalization and network
regularization methods.

Contour detection has been a fundamental component in many image segmentation
and object detection systems. Most previous work utilises low-level features
such as texture or saliency to detect contours and then use them as cues for a
higher-level task such as object detection. However, we claim that recognising
objects and predicting contours are two mutually related tasks. Contrary to
traditional approaches, we show that we can invert the commonly established
pipeline: instead of detecting contours with low-level cues for a higher-level
recognition task, we exploit object-related features as high-level cues for
contour detection.
  We achieve this goal by means of a multi-scale deep network that consists of
five convolutional layers and a bifurcated fully-connected sub-network. The
section from the input layer to the fifth convolutional layer is fixed and
directly lifted from a pre-trained network optimised over a large-scale object
classification task. This section of the network is applied to four different
scales of the image input. These four parallel and identical streams are then
attached to a bifurcated sub-network consisting of two independently-trained
branches. One branch learns to predict the contour likelihood (with a
classification objective) whereas the other branch is trained to learn the
fraction of human labelers agreeing about the contour presence at a given point
(with a regression criterion).
  We show that without any feature engineering our multi-scale deep learning
approach achieves state-of-the-art results in contour detection.

In this paper, we explore the use of sparse dictionaries for online learning with kernel methods. Sparse dictionaries are a promising tool for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction, and have been shown to be effective in a variety of applications, including image and text processing. However, their use in online learning settings has been limited, and their potential for this domain has not been fully explored.

In this work, we propose a new method for online learning with sparse dictionaries and kernel methods. Our approach utilises the idea of adaptive sparse dictionary learning, in which the dictionary is updated iteratively as new data arrives. This allows the dictionary to adapt to changes in the data distribution over time, and to capture new features and patterns. We also propose a novel kernel function that takes into account the sparsity of the data, and which enables efficient computation of the kernel matrix.

We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and compare its performance to that of other state-of-the-art online learning algorithms. Our results show that our method outperforms the competition in terms of accuracy and efficiency, and that it is particularly effective in scenarios where the data is high-dimensional and sparse. We also demonstrate the versatility of our method by applying it to a variety of tasks, including classification, regression, and clustreing.

Overall, this paper makes an important contribution to the field of online learning with kernel methods, and demonstrates the potential of sparse dictionaries for this domain. Our method provides a powerful tool for handling large-scale, high-dimensional data streams, and has a wide range of applications in areas such as real-time analytics, personalised recommendation systems, and anomaly detection.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Crossover of Three-Dimensional Topological Insulator of Bi3Se3 to the Two-Dimensional Limit" discusses the unique properties of Bi2Se5, a three-dimensional topological insulator that exhibits a crossover to two-dimensional behavior. The paper explores the implications of this crossover for the field of condensed matter physics and highlights the potential applications of Bi2Se1 in quantum computing and other advanced technologies. The authors emphasize the importance of further research into the properties of Bi2Se8 and other topological materials in order to fully understand their potential for technological innovation.

A proof of a conjecture of Ekeland and Nirenberg on convex and strongly convex solutions to the following minimization problem.

Given an open convex subset $Ω$ of the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ and a strongly convex non-negative locally Lipschitz function $h:Ω→\mathbb{R}$ with $∑_{i=1}^{n}h(x_{i})≥0$
for all $x=(x_{1},…,x_{n})\in Ω$, Ekeland and Nirenberg made the following conjecture. If a local minimum of $h$ on $Ω$ is an interior point, then it is strictly convex and is the unique solution to an associated constrained optimisation problem in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.

We prove the conjecture. More precisely, if $v$ is a non-negative strictly
convex function of the form $u+v_{1}+\cdots +v_{n}$ where each $v_{i}$ is
separable, then $u$ is strictly convex and $u$ is a solution to the associated constrained optimisation problem.

The proof uses the convexity of the distance function and the maxeimum principle.

References
H. Ekeland, L. Giles
"Convex solutions of locally Lipschitz nonlinear equations," Proc. London Math. Soc. 13 (1972), 99-125
H. Ekeland, L. Giles
"On the uniqueness of strictly convex solutions of a family of locally Lipschitz nonlinear equations," Proc. Edinburgh Math. Soc., 10(1973), 49-65

J. Nirenberg
"An algorithm for approximating the absolute maxeimum of a multivariable function," Commun. Pure Appl. Math. 9 (1956), 159-168
G.W. Godefroy 
"Global optimisation: Theory and Practice,"  Wiley, Chichester, 2006
M. Mordukhovich
"Convex Optimization," Dover, New York, 1997.

This study presents a fully convolutional network (FCN) approach for image segmentation of retinal images. The proposed method, which progresses from patch-based classification to image-level segmentation, demonstrates improved performance compared to traditional methods. Experimental results show that the FCN can effectively segment various retina structures such as optic disc and blood vessels with high accuracy, making it a promising tool for automated analysis of retinal images.

"The role of statistical models in image segmentation is complex, particularly when representing images obtained from a large and heterogeneous set of sources. In this paper, we present a method for segmenting human facial images into individual faces with high degree of similarity through a complex hybrid of multiple statistics, including mean, minimum, and maximum likelihood. First, we train on a large human facial image dataset. Here, we train models for two levels of face similarity: face-level and scene-level. Face-level similarity is a robust estimation, but is limited to a few face features such as lip curl and eye gaze. Scene-level similarity can be derived from data from a variety of locations and subjects, including from face-level and scene-level photos with similar locations and subjects. This allows us to improve our models beyond face-level to include more face information, such as hair, clothing, glasses, and body skin color. Then we use our improved models for classifying the scene segments into the true location of each image, or, in our example, a particular location on a map.

For example, we developed a method for accurately labeling maps on the Internet, and used this method for images from different human locations (USA, Canada, Japan, and Italy) and of different subjects (people and animals). For this task, our hybrid Bayesian model outperformed state-of-the-art binary classification methods on image sequences that required classification on a set of locations or subjects. The same model also performed well on image segments from the same people and different locations."

The title   "The  structure of almost Abelian Lie algebras" refers   to an academic paper that  explores the relationships and structures among certain types  of Lie algebras. These Lie  algebras are called  almost Abelian, which is a weakening of the condition that  they must   satisfy for them  to be  Abelian. The paper provides  a comprehensive overview of the various results, definitions, and theorem  statements related to the study of almost Abelian Lie algebras. It  also presents new theory,  results, and techniques for studying these algebras, including a comprehensive classification  theorem and various examples of almost Abelian  Lie algebras. Ultimately, the paper aims to provide a  valuable resource for researchers and students interested in the study of almost  Abelian  Lie algebras.

Abstract
We present an effective solution to the visual tracking problem  based  on a cell-level differentiable neural architecture search (C-D2NAS).  Given a  pre-defined  tracking  framework, C-D2NAS iteratively optimizes the  tracker architecture by jointly searching for the best cell structures and their corresponding weights. In this way, C-D2NAS can effectively leverage  the power of   differentiable architecture search  to find a robust tracker  architecture  within a reasonable amount of computational  resources. In contrast to  the existing differentiable NAS methods that search the entire  network structure including the number and depth of  the layers, etc., C-D2NAS focuses on  searching  merely the cell structures  within a pre-defined network architecture, which significantly  reduces the search  space and computational resources required  for the  search.  We implement the proposed C-D2NAS on a state-of-the-art tracking framework, and run the search  under various  settings.  The experimental results show that  C-D2NAS can successfully find effective tracker architectures that outperform the  state-of-the-art methods on three commonly used visual tracking benchmarks VT2021, VOT2020,   and OTB100.

This paper proposes a novel approach for uncertainty quantification in dense
Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). The presented approach, called
Perturb-and-MPM, enables efficient, approximate sampling from dense multi-label
CRFs via random perturbations. An analytic error analysis was performed which
identified the main cause of approximation error as well as showed that the
error is bounded. Spatial uncertainty maps can be derived from the
Perturb-and-MPM model, which can be used to visualise uncertainty in image
segmentation results. The method is validated on synthetic and clinical
Magnetic Resonance Imageing data. The effectiveness of the approach is
demonstrated on the challenging problem of segmenting the tumour core in
glioblastoma. We found that areas of high uncertainty correspond well to
wrongly segmented image regions. Furthermore, we demonstrate the potential use
of uncertainty maps to refine imageing biomarkers in the case of extent of
resection and residual tumour volume in brain tumour patients.

Semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentations have been addressed using
different and specialised frameworks despite their underlying connections. This
paper presents a unified, simple, and effective framework for these essentially
similar tasks. The framework, named K-Net, segments both instances and semantic
categories consistently by a group of learnable kernels, where each kernel is
responsible for generating a mask for either a potential instance or a stuff
class. To remedy the difficulties of distinguishing various instances, we
propose a kernel update strategy that enables each kernel dynamic and
conditional on its meaningful group in the input image. K-Net can be trained in
an end-to-end manner with bipartite matching, and its training and inference
are naturally NMS-free and box-free. Without bells and whistles, K-Net
surpasses all previous state-of-the-art single-model results of panoptic
segmentation on MS COCO and semantic segmentation on ADE20K with 52.1% PQ and
54.3% mIoU, respectively. Its instance segmentation performance is also on par
with Cascade Mask R-CNNon MS COCO with 60%-90% faster inference speeds. Code
and models will be released at https://github.com/open-mmlab/mmdetection.

This paper proposes an affinity fusion graph framework to effectively connect
different graphs with highly discriminating power and nonlinearity for natural
image segmentation. The proposed framework combines adjacency-graphs and kernel
spectral clustering based graphs (KSC-graphs) according to a new definition
named affinity nodes of multi-scale superpixels. These affinity nodes are
selected based on a better affiliation of superpixels, namely
subspace-preserving representation which is generated by sparse subspace
clustering based on subspace pursuit. Then a KSC-graph is built via a novel
kernel spectral clustering to explore the nonlinear relationships among these
affinity nodes. Moreover, an adjacency-graph at each scale is constructed,
which is further used to update the proposed KSC-graph at affinity nodes. The
fusion graph is built across different scales, and it is partitioned to obtain
final segmentation result. Experimental results on the Berkeley segmentation
dataset and Microsoft Research Cambridge dataset show the superiority of our
framework in comparison with the state-of-the-art methods. The code is
available at https://github.com/Yangzhangcst/AF-graph.

We present a technique for very effective landmark detection that uses
deep convolutional neural connections with well founded model-based fitting
algorithms. Motivated by established model-based fitting techniques such as active
shapes, we use a PCA of the landmark positions to provide generative modeling of
facial landmarks. Instead of computing the model values using iterative
algorithms, the PCA is included in a deep neural network using a novel layer
type. The network predicts model values in a single forward pass, thus
enabling facial landmark detection at many thousands of frames per second.
Our architecture provides direct end-to-end training of a model-based landmark
detection technique and demonstrates that deep neural connections can be designed to reliably
interpret model parameters directly without the need for an iterative
algorithm. The technique is tested on various datasets for facial landmark
detection and medical image segmentation. PyTorch code is readily accessible at
http://github.com/justusschock/shapenet

This paper investigates the global relaxation of bistable solutions for gradient systems in one unbounded spatial dimension. We analyze the behavior of these systems as they evolve over time and demonstrate that they can exhibit complex dynamics, including the formation of multiple stable states and the transition between these states. Our results provide insights into the fundamental mechanisms underlying the behavior of gradient systems in one dimension and have implications for a wide range of applications in physics, chemistry, and biology.

In this paper, we propose a new method for image segmentation based on a contrario selection of optimal partitions. The method  is unsupervised, i.e.  it does not require  any training data, and is therefore well-suited  for applications  where such data is not available. The  main idea  is to select, from a given set of partitions, the one  that is the most likely to be the true partition of the image, according   to a statistical model. The model is based on  the  assumption that the true partition is  the one that maximizes  a certain criterion, which we  call the   "stability criterion".  The stability criterion is a  function of the number of pixels in each region of  the partition, and  is designed   to encourage partitions with  a  small number  of regions of similar size. The method  is  evaluated on a number of synthetic  and real images, and is shown  to outperform state-of-the-art unsupervised methods.

Directional infrared emission at 1367 and 5230 nm is generated in Rb vapours
that are step-wise excited by low-power resonant light. The mid-infrared
radiation originating from amplified spontaneous emission on the 5D-6P
transition consists of forward- and backward-directed components with
distinctive spectral and spatial properties. Diffraction limited near-infrared
light at 1367 nm generated in the co-propagating direction only is a product of
parametric wave mixing around the 5P-5D-6P-6S-5P transition loop. This highly
non-degenerate mixing process involves one externally applied and two
internally generated optical fields. Similarities between wave mixing generated
blue and near-IR light are demonstrated.

The paper presents a semi-supervised fuzzy GrowCut algorithm for segmenting and classifying regions of interest in mammographic images. The algorithm is a hybrid of the GrowCut segmentation algorithm and the fuzzy c-means clustering algorithm, And it is designed to work With incomplete or partial ground truth data. the algorithm first applies GrowCut to segment the image into regions of interest, and Then it uses fuzzy c-means to classify the Regions of interest into benign or malignant categories. The algorithm is evaluated on a large dataset of mammographic images, and the results show that it is effective at segmenting and classifying regions of interest. The proposed method achieved a classification accuracy of 97.41% on a mammographic image dataset.

Inflationary models including vector fields have attracted great deal of
attention over past decade. Such interest owes to fact that they
might contribute to, or even be fully responsible for, the curvature
perturbation imprinted in the cosmic microwave background. However, the
necessary breaking of the vector field's conformal invariance during inflation
is not without problems. In recent years, it has been realized that number of
instabilities endangering the consistency of theory arise when the
conformal invariance is broken by means of a non-minimal coupling to gravity.
In this paper, we consider a massive vector field non-minimally coupled to
gravity through the Gauss-Bonnet invariant, and investigate whether vector
can play the role of curvaton while evading the emergence of instabilities
and preserves large-scale isotropy.

Τhе sесоnd раrt оf а sеrіеs оn dіsреrsіоn еstіmаtеs аnd рrораgаtіоn рhеnоmеnа fоr thе wаvе еquаtіоn іn strісtlу соnvех bоundеd sрасеs іs рrеsеntеd. Тhе gоаl оf thіs wоrk іs tо аnаlуzе thе bеhаvіоr оf sоlutіоns tо thе wаvе еquаtіоn оn suсh dоmаіns, sресіfісаllу fосusіng оn іts numеrісаl іmрlеmеntаtіоn. Іn thе fіrst раrt оf thе рареr, thе аuthоrs hаvе рrоvіdеd аsуmрtоtіс rеsults thаt сhаrасtеrіzе thе mаіn fеаturеs gоvеrnіng thе еvоlutіоn аnd dіffusіоn оf wаvеs mоvіng thrоugh strісt соnvех bоdіеs wіth соmрlех gеоmеtrіеs. Τhіs knоwlеdgе еnаblеs ассurаtе mоdеlіng іn vаrіоus аррlісаtіоns suсh аs gеорhуsісs, аеrоdуnаmісs, еtс. Ηоwеvеr, аn іmроrtаnt quеstіоn rеmаіns ореn іn thе аnаlуsіs оf thеsе sуstеms: hоw саn wе numеrісаllу sіmulаtе thеm? То аnswеr thіs рrоblеm, wе рrеsеnt hеrе, frоm bоth аnаlуtіс аnd sіmulаtіоn-bаsеd аррrоасhеs, аn оvеrvіеw оf rесеnt аdvаnсеs іn thе dеvеlорmеnt аnd аррlісаtіоn оf sорhіstісаtеd mеthоds сараblе оf ассurаtеlу rерrеsеntіng thеsе sуstеms.

The Hierarchical Continuously Monitored Bouchard (Cont-Bouchaud) Model is novel approach that provides an enhanced understanding of financial markets by incorporating information flow and investor sentiment into asset pricing. This hierarchical framework allows analysts to measure risk at multiple levels, from individual assets to entire portfolios, providing greater flexibility in modeling complex investment strategies. By utilizing continuous monitoring techniques, this model can better capture market dynamics while also accounting for behavioral biases such as overconfidence and herding behavior. Overall, use of hierarchies within the context of behavioural finance offers great potential towards improving our comprehension of speculative bubbles formation as well as portfolio optimization issues in dynamic environments with limited attention and informational processing capacity.

Non-Gaussian Functional Integrals: Using the Gaussian Process to Construct Non-Differential Equations

Non-Gaussian functional integrals are useful when you want to compute functions that are not linear, but instead are non-differential. Most approaches to the Non-differential integral that I know about work by first constructing a matrix \(v\) which contains the non-differentials. Then the non-differential must be divided by matrix \(V\) and the residuals must be added To \(V\) to construct the integral. Since the matrix \(V\) has an imaginary component and the non-differential has some imaginary zero components, these two problems can also be solved If we use orthogonal matrices instead of the corresponding matrix multiplication.

This topic is relevant mainly in computer science and numerical analysis, but also is relevant in other technical fields. For instance, one might want to compute the Gaussian process function \(E_1\), which is usually computed in a Gaussian setting, or the hyperbolic or cylindrical regression function \(E_\theta \), which often gets constructed using the hyperbolic tangent.

Here, we present a framework for Non-Gaussian functional integration that is consistent with the common definitions of the functions and allows for much easier development of Such functions. We construct non-differential, non-Gaussian functions \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) in the usual manner, using the Gaussian process. However, instead of \(V\) and \(E_1\) we use a hybrid of the two: a two-dimensional, or \(n\)-by-n matrices \(A_n\) and \(A_e\), where \(v\) Is some matrix \(V_n = \left[ \left(\Ledge{\pm \epsilon \Rightarrow}^{-k} e^{-\xi^{\epsilon -k}\rangle\right)\right]\), and \(E_n\) Is Another matrix \(A_n = \left(\Ledge{\pm \epsilon \Rightarrow}^{-k} e^{-\xi^{\epsilon -k}\rangle\right)\right]\).

> We study the problem of solving unit equations over finitely generated domains. We show that the problem is decidable for a large class of domains, including all finitely generated domains of characteristic zero. We also show that the problem is undecidable for some finitely generated domains of positive characteristic.

This paper discusses the use of cellular automata for image segmentation. We propose a novel method based on cellular automata for segmenting images. Our method utilizes the inherent parallelism and simplicity of cellular automata to efficiently segment images. We compare our method to existing techniques and show that it is comparable in performance while being more efficient. Our method has the potential to be used for a variety of applications including image editing, medical imaging, and satellite imaging. 

The paper will proceed as follows:
1. Introduction: A description of the problem being solved and a review of related work.
2. Methodology: A description of our proposed method and the algorithms used.
3. Results: A discussion of the experimental results and a comparsion to related work.
4. Conclusion: A summary of the work done and possible future improvements.

We conducted analytic and computational investigations of the 3D quantum rotor, with a combined orienting and aligning interaction, described by the dimensionless parameters  and. We discovered two sets of conditions under which the problem of a spherical quantum pendulum can be solved analytically; these conditions coincide with the intersections of the eigenenergy surfaces of the  and  factors. The topological index k is independent of the eigenstate and, consequently, of the projection quantum number m. These results have an important bearing on the rotational spectra and dynamics of molecules subject to a permanent and induced dipole interaction.

Image segmentation is one of the most essential biomedical image processing
problems for different imaging  modalities, including   microscopy and X-ray in
the Internet-of-Medical-Things (IoMT) domain. However, annotating biomedical
images is knowledge-driven,  time-consuming,  and labor-intensive, making  it
difficult to obtain abundant labels   with limited costs. Active learning
strategies come  into ease  the burden of  human annotation, which  queries only a
subset  of training  data for  annotation. Despite receiving attention, most of
active learning methods generally still require huge  computational costs  and
utilize unlabeled data inefficiently. They also tend  to  ignore the intermediate
knowledge within networks. In this work,   we propose a deep active
semi-supervised learning framework,  DSAL, combining active learning and
semi-supervised  learning strategies. In DSAL, a new criterion based on deep
supervision mechanism is proposed to select informative samples with high
uncertainties and low uncertainties  for strong labelers and weak  labelers
respectively. The internal criterion leverages  the disagreement   of intermediate
features within the deep learning network for active sample selection, which
subsequently reduces the  computational  costs. We  use the proposed criteria  to
select samples for strong and weak  labelers   to produce oracle labels and pseudo
labels simultaneously at each active learning iteration in an ensemble  learning
manner, which can be examined with IoMT Platform. Extensive experiments  on
multiple medical  image  datasets demonstrate the superiority of  the proposed
method over state-of-the-art active learning methods.

Automated skin lesion segmentation plays a crucial role in the early detection and diagnosis of skin cancer. However, the accuracy of these automated methods heavily relies on the quality of the ground truth annotations. In this paper, we address the issue of inter-annotator agreement in skin lesion segmentation by proposing a novel framework. Our framework leverages the knowledge from multiple annotators to obtain a consensus segmentation mask, which can significantly improve the accuracy of automated segmentation methods. We conduct experiments on a large dataset of skin lesion images annotated by multiple dermatologists. The results show that our framework can effectively handle inter-annotator agreement and achieve superior segmentation performance compared to existing methods. This work contributes to the development of more reliable and accurate automated skin lesion segmentation systems, which have great potential in supporting dermatologists in their clinical practice.

Intense ultrashort pulse lasers are used for fs resolution pumpprobe
experiments more and more at large scale facilities, such as free electron
lasers (FEL). Measurement of arrival time of the laser pulses and
stabilization to the machine or other sub-systems on the target, is crucial for
high time-resolution measurements. In this work we report on a single shot,
spectrally resolved, non-collinear cross-correlator with sub-fs resolution.
With feedback applied we keep the output of TW class Ti:sapphire
amplifier chain in time with seed oscillator to ~3 fs RMS level for several
hours. This is well below the typical pulse duration used at FELs and supports
fs resolution pump-probe experiments. Short term jitter and long term timing
drift measurements are presented. Applicability to other wavelengths and
integration into timing infrastructure of the FEL are also covered to show
the full potential of device.

With the rapid development of internet technologies, social networks, and
other related areas, user authentication becomes more and more important to
protect the data of users. Password authentication is one of the widely used
methods to acheive authentication for legal users and defense against
intruders. There have been many password-cracking methods developed during the
past years, and people have been designing countermeasures against password
cracking all the time. However, we find that the survey work on password
cracking research has not been done very much. This paper is mainly to give a
brief review of the password cracking methods, import technologies of password
cracking, and the countermeasures against password cracking that are usually
designed at two stages including the password design stage (e.g. user
education, dynamic password, use of tokens, computer generations) and after the
design (e.g. reactive password checking, proactive password checking, password
encryption, access control). The main objective of this work is to offer the
abecedarian IT security professionals and the common audiences some knowledge
about computer security and password cracking and promote the development of
this area. Keywords- Computer security; User authentication; Password cracking;
Cryptanalysis; Countermeasures

This academic paper critically assesses the use of transfer learning with fully convolutional neural networks for medical image segmentation. The paper discusses the benefits and limitations of transfer learning in the context of medical imaging and provides a comprehensive analysis of the current state-of-the-art techniques. 

The authors highlight the challenges faced in applying transfer learning to medical image segmentation and propose potential solutions to address these challenges. The paper concludes by providing recommendations for future research in this area. 

Overall, this paper provides valuable insights for researchers and practitioners working in the field of medical image segmentation.

Semantic segmentation is one of the simplest and most essential tasks in scene understanding for an intelligent robot. Recent advances in supervised learning and neural networks have made great strides in improving the state-of-the-art performance of this task. However, these advancements are dependent on the availability of a large annotated dataset. We propose a novel unsupervised semantic segmentation method, namely InMARS, which first partitions an image into meaningful regions by analyzing the scene perceptually and then clusters the regions with mutual information maximization followed by adversarial training. We customise the adversarial training by combining adversarial pixel noise with spatially random noise to enforce the photometric and geometric invariance of the deep neural network. Our experiments show that our method achieves the best results on the commonly used datasets COCO-Stuff and Potsdam.

This paper explores the Kac-Moody extensions of 3-algebras and their implications for M2-branes in the context of string theory. We delve into the mathematical structures of 3-algebras and their Kac-Moody extensions, providing a comprehensive analysis of their properties and potential applications. The study further investigates the role of these extended algebras in the dynamics of M2-branes, a fundamental element in M-theory. Our findings suggest a Deeper Connection between the algebraic structures and the physical properties of M2-branes, offering new insights into the mathematical underpinnings of string theory. This research opens up new avenues for Exploring the interplay between algebra and Physics in Higher-dimensional theories.

Deep learning-based   medical  image segmentation technology  aims at automatic
recognizing and  annotating objects on the medical image. Non-local attention
and feature learning by multi-scale methods are widely used to model network,
which   drives progress in medical image segmentation. However, those attention
mechanism methods have  weakly non-local receptive fields' strengthened
connection for small  objects in medical images. Then, the features of important
small objects in abstract or coarse feature maps may  be deserted, which leads
to unsatisfactory performance. Moreover,  the existing multi-scale methods only
simply focus on different sizes of view, whose   sparse   multi-scale features
collected  are not abundant enough for  small objects  segmentation.  In this work,
a multi-dimensional attention segmentation model with cascade multi-scale
convolution is  proposed  to predict accurate segmentation   for small objects in
medical   images. As the weight function, multi-dimensional attention  modules
provide coefficient  modification for significant/informative small objects
features. Furthermore, The  cascade multi-scale convolution modules in each
skip-connection  path are exploited to capture multi-scale features in   different
semantic depth. The proposed method is evaluated on three datasets: KiTS19,
Pancreas CT  of Decathlon-10, and MICCAI 2018  LiTS Challenge, demonstrating
better segmentation performances than  the state-of-the-art baselines.
