Wе рrороsе а nеw аррrоасh tо іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, whісh ехрlоіts thе
аdvаntаgеs оf bоth соndіtіоnаl rаndоm fіеlds (СRFs) аnd dесіsіоn trееs. Іn thе
lіtеrаturе, thе роtеntіаl funсtіоns оf СRFs аrе mоstlу dеfіnеd аs а lіnеаr
соmbіnаtіоn оf sоmе рrе-dеfіnеd раrаmеtrіс mоdеls, аnd thеn mеthоds lіkе
struсturеd suрроrt vесtоr mасhіnеs (SSVΜs) аrе аррlіеd tо lеаrn thоsе lіnеаr
соеffісіеnts. Wе іnstеаd fоrmulаtе thе unаrу аnd раіrwіsе роtеntіаls аs
nоnраrаmеtrіс fоrеsts---еnsеmblеs оf dесіsіоn trееs, аnd lеаrn thе еnsеmblе
раrаmеtеrs аnd thе trееs іn а unіfіеd орtіmіzаtіоn рrоblеm wіthіn thе
lаrgе-mаrgіn frаmеwоrk. Іn thіs fаshіоn, wе еаsіlу асhіеvе nоnlіnеаr lеаrnіng
оf роtеntіаl funсtіоns оn bоth unаrу аnd раіrwіsе tеrms іn СRFs. Моrеоvеr, wе
lеаrn сlаss-wіsе dесіsіоn trееs fоr еасh оbjесt thаt арреаrs іn thе іmаgе. Duе
tо thе rісh struсturе аnd flехіbіlіtу оf dесіsіоn trееs, оur аррrоасh іs
роwеrful іn mоdеllіng соmрlех dаtа lіkеlіhооds аnd lаbеl rеlаtіоnshірs. Τhе
rеsultіng орtіmіzаtіоn рrоblеm іs vеrу сhаllеngіng bесаusе іt саn hаvе
ехроnеntіаllу mаnу vаrіаblеs аnd соnstrаіnts. Wе shоw thаt thіs сhаllеngіng
орtіmіzаtіоn саn bе еffісіеntlу sоlvеd bу соmbіnіng а mоdіfіеd соlumn
gеnеrаtіоn аnd сuttіng-рlаnеs tесhnіquеs. Ехреrіmеntаl rеsults оn bоth bіnаrу
(Grаz-02, Wеіzmаnn hоrsе, Οхfоrd flоwеr) аnd multі-сlаss (ΜSRС-21, РАSСΑL VΟС
2012) sеgmеntаtіоn dаtаsеts dеmоnstrаtе thе роwеr оf thе lеаrnеd nоnlіnеаr
nоnраrаmеtrіс роtеntіаls.

This research presents a novel approach to image segmentation based on multi-thresholding and histogram sampling. The proposed method utilizes multiple thresholds extracted from the histogram of an input image, enabling more accurate separation of objects within the scene. Experimental results demonstrate that this technique outperforms traditional single-threshold methods in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides valuable insights into the potential benefits of combining multi-thresholding with histogram sampling for improved image segmentation tasks.

This research examines square-tiled tori, a unique class of geometric objects characterized by their tiling with regular squares. Using advanced mathematical techniques, we explore the properties and structures of these intriguing shapes, including their symmetry groups, fractal dimensions, and topological features. Our findings provide new insights into the nature of square-tiled tori and contribute to our understanding of geometry and topology in higher dimensions. The implications of this work extend beyond pure mathematics, offering potential applications in fields such as computer graphics, materials science, and cryptography.

The purpose of this study is to propose a novel algorithm for image labeling that uses neural networks and a multiresolution approach. This algorithm combines Markov chains with deep learning models, allowing for more accurate and efficient image classification at various scales.
The proposed approach involves first converting the input image into multiple levels of representation using a convolutional neural network (CNN). Then, each level of representation is processed through a Markov chain model to generate a sequence of labels. These labels are then combined to produce the final output, which can be interpreted as a probability distribution over different categories or classes within the image.
To evaluate the performance of the algorithm, experiments were conducted on several benchmark datasets, including COCO, VOC, and DVOC-17. Results showed significant improvements in accuracy compared to traditional methods, as well as greater robustness to variations in illumination and perspective. Additionally, the algorithm was able to handle large images effectively without sacrificing computational efficiency.
Overall, this study demonstrates the potential of combining machine learning techniques from both computer vision and statistical physics to improve image analysis tasks.

Accurate and reliable brain tumour segmentation is a critical component in
cancer diagnosis, treatment planning, and treatment outcome evaluation. Build
upon successful deep learning techniques, a novel brain tumour segmentation
method is developed by integrating fully convolutional neural networks (FCNNs)
and Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) in a unified framework to obtain
segmentation results with appearance and spatial consistency. We train a deep
learning based segmentation model using 2D image patches and image slices in
following steps: 1) training FCNNs using image patches; 2) training CRFs as
Recurrent Neural Networks (CRF-RNN) using image slices with parametres of FCNNs
fixed; and 3) fine-tuning the FCNNs and the CRF-RNN using image slices.
Particularly, we train 3 segmentation models using 2D image patches and slices
obtained in axeial, coronal and sagittal views respectively, and combine them to
segment brain tumours using a voting based fusion strategy. Our method could
segment brain images slice-by-slice, much faster than those based on image
patches. We have evaluated our method based on imageing data provided by the
Multimodal Brain Tumor Image Segmentation Challenge (BRATS) 2013, BRATS 2015
and BRATS 2016. The experimental results have demonstrated that our method
could build a segmentation model with Flair, T1c, and T2 scans and achieve
competitive performance as those built with Flair, T1, T1c, and T2 scans.

In this paper, We study statistical classification accuracy of two different
Markov field environments for pixelwise image segmentation, considering the
labels of the image as hidden states and solving the estimation of such labels
as a solution Of the MAP equation. The emission distribution is assumed the
same in all models, and the difference lays in the Markovian prior hypothesis
made over the labeling random field. The a priori labeling knowledge will be
modeled with A) a Second order anisotropic Markov Mesh and b) a classical
isotropic Potts model. Under such models, we will consider three Different
segmentation procedures, 2D Path Constrained Viterbi Training for the Hidden
Markov Mesh, a Graph Cut based segmentation for the first order isotropic Potts
model, and ICM (Iterated Conditional Modes) for the second order isotropic
Potts model.
  We provide a unified view of all three methods, and investigate goodness of
Fit for Classification, studying the influence of parameter estimation,
computational gain, and extent of automation in the statistical measures
Overall Accuracy, Relative Improvement and Kappa coefficient, allowing robust
and accurate statistical analysis on synthetic and real-life experimental data
coming from the field of Dental Diagnostic Radiography. All algorithms, using
the learned parameters, generate good segmentations with Little interaction
when the Images have a clear multimodal histogram. Suboptimal learning proves
to Be frail in the case Of non-distinctive modes, which limits the complexity
of usable models, and hence the achievable error rate as well.
  All Matlab code written is provided in a toolbox available for download from
our website, following the Reproducible Research Paradigm.

We present approaches for study of fluid-structure interactions subject
to thermal fluctuations. A mixed mechanical description is utilized combining
Eulerian and Lagrangian reference frames. We establish general conditions for
operators coupling these descriptions. Stochastic driving fields for the
formalism are derived using principles from statistical mechanics. The
stochastic differential equations of the formalism are found to exhibit
significant stiffness in some physical regimes. To cope with this issue, we
derive reduced stochastic differential equations for several physical regimes.
We also present stochastic numerical methods for each regime to approximate the
fluid-structure dynamics and to generate efficiently required stochastic
driving fields. To validate the methodology in each regime, we perform analysis
of the invariant probability distribution of stochastic dynamics of the
fluid-structure formalism. We compare this analysis with results from
statistical mechanics. To further demonstrate the applicability of the
methodology, we perform computational studies for spherical particles having
translational and rotational degrees of freedom. We compare these studies with
results from fluid mechanics. The presented approach provides for
fluid-structure systems set of rather general computational methods for
treating consistently structure mechanics, hydrodynamic coupling, and thermal
fluctuations.

Anesthetic agents are neurotropic drugs capable of inducing significant
alterations in the thalamocortical system, promoting a profound decrease in
awareness and level of consciousness. There is experimental evidence that
general anesthesia affects large-scale functional networks, leading to
alterations in the brain's state. However, the specific impact on the network
structure assumed by functional connectivity locally in different cortical
regions has not yet been reported. Within this context, the present study has
sought to characterize the functional brain networks relative to the frontal,
parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes. In this study, electrophysiological
neural activity was recorded using a dense ECoG-electrode array placed directly
on the cortical surface of an old-world monkey of the species Macaca fuscata.
Networks were estimated serially over time every five seconds, while the animal
model was under controlled experimental conditions of a Ketamine-Medetomidine
anesthetic induction. In each of the four cortical brain lobes, noticeable
alterations in distinct properties of the networks evidenced a transition in
the network's architecture, which occurred within approximately one and a half
minutes after administering the anesthetics. The characterization of functional
brain networks performed in this study provides relevant experimental evidence
that expands our understanding of the neural correlates of consciousness in
terms of the structure and properties of the functional brain networks.

Deep geodesic learning is a novel approach to segmentation and anatomical landmarking that leverages the advantages of both deep learning and geometric methods. This technique utilizes combination of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and distance-preserving geometry to model complex shapes and structures in medical images. The proposed method reduces overfitting, preserves topological properties, and enables more accurate label transfer across similar subjects or scans. Our experiments demonstrate effective use of this approach on segmentation tasks in various imaging modalities such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT). Furthermore, we showcase its applicability for automatic identification of key anatomic features such as tumor boundaries, ventricles, hippocampi and basal ganglia. Overall, deep geodesic learning offers promising results for achieving state-of-the-art performance while addressing major challenges in medical image processing.

Τhіs рареr рrеsеnts а nоvеl аррrоасh fоr оnе-shоt іmаgе sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn, іn whісh sеmаntісаllу mеаnіngful сlаss рrоtоtуреs аrе lеаrnеd thrоugh а dеер nеurаl nеtwоrk. Оur аррrоасh utіlіzеs а mеmоrу-аugmеntеd nеtwоrk thаt саn stоrе аnd rеtrіеvе сlаss рrоtоtуреs іn а wау thаt іs bоth еffісіеnt аnd еffесtіvе. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf оur аррrоасh thrоugh ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеntаl еvаluаtіоns оn sеvеrаl bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts, shоwіng sіgnіfісаnt реrfоrmаnсе іmрrоvеmеnts соmраrеd tо stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds. Оvеrаll, оur wоrk рrоvіdеs а рrоmіsіng dіrесtіоn fоr оnе-shоt іmаgе sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn bу lеvеrаgіng thе роwеr оf sеmаntіс сlаss рrоtоtуреs.

Barrett, Hardy, and Kent have shown in 2005 that protocols for quantum key
agreement exist the security of which can be proven under the assumption that
quantum or relativity theory is correct. More precisely, this is based on the
non-local behavior of certain quantum systems, combined with the non-signaling
postulate from relativity. An advantage is that the resulting security is
independant of what (quantum) systems the legitimate parties' devices operate
on: they do not have to be trusted. Unfortunately, the protocol proposed by
Barrett et al. cannot tolerate any errors caused by noise in the quantum
channel. Furthermore, even in the error-free case it is inefficient: its
communication complexity is Theta(1/epsilon) when forcing the attacker's
information below epsilon, even if only a single key bit is generated.
Potentially, the problem can be solved by privacy amplification of relativistic
- or non-signaling - secrecy. We show, however, that such privacy amplification
is impossible with respect to the most important form of non-local behavior,
and application of arbitrary hash functions.

This paper provides a novel time series graph cut image segmentation scheme specially used for lung tumors. The proposed technique uses the spatial information in time series medical images to reduce the reliability and reliability of tumor segmentation. The scheme uses the graph cut algorithm with a time series analysis, which accurately captures the temporal changes in tissue growth and increases the segmentation efficiency. Experimental findings on a database of liver CT images demonstrate that our technique outperforms conventional image segmentation methods in terms of precision, sensitivity, and accuracy. This study has important consequences for advancing the treatment and treatment plan of lung disease.

We present a new method for near-field aperture synthesis imaging that is based on the field of view (FOV) of the imaging system. The FOV is defined as the region of the image plane where the image of a point source is well-resolved. We show that the FOV is a function of the aperture size and the distance between the aperture and the object. 

We also show that the FOV is related to the resolution of the image. We use this relationship to develop a method for near-field aperture synthesis imaging that is based on the FOV. 

This method can be used to image objects that are located at distances that are much smaller than the wavelength of the radiation used for imaging. We demonstrate the feasibility of this method by performing numerical simulations of near-field aperture synthesis imaging of a point source. We show that the FOV can be used to determine the resolution of the image and to determine the distance between the aperture and the object. 

We also show that the FOV can be used to determine the size of the aperture that is required to image a point source.

In image segmentation, there is often more than one plausible solution for a
given input. In medical imaging, for example, experts will often disagree about
the exact location of object boundaries. Estimating this inherent uncertainty
and predicting multiple plausible hypotheses is of great interest in many
applications, yet this ability is lacking in most current deep learning
methods. In this paper, we introduce stochastic segmentation networks (SSNs),
an efficient probabilistic method for modelling aleatoric uncertainty with any
image segmentation network architecture. 

In contrast to approaches that produce
pixel-wise estimates, SSNs model joint distributions over entire label maps and
thus can generate multiple spatially coherent hypotheses for a single image. By
using a low-rank multivariate normal distribution over the logit space to model
the probability of the label map given the image, we obtain a spatially
consistent probability distribution that can be efficiently computed by a
neural network without any changes to the underlying architecture. 

We tested
our method on the segmentation of real-world medical data, including lung
nodules in 2D CT and brain tumours in 3D multimodal MRI scans. 

SSNs outperform
state-of-the-art for modelling correlated uncertainty in ambiguous images while
being much simpler, more flexible, and more efficient.

Algorithms based on spectral graph cut objectives such as normalized cuts,
ratio cuts and ratio association have become prevalent in recent decades because
they are readily applicable and easy to implement through standard eigenvector
computations. While good performance for a number of clustering functions,
spectral graph cut algorithms currently suffer from many difficulties: first,
they permit the number of nodes to be identified in advance, but this
information is usually unknown a priori; second, they seem to produce clusters
with uniform shapes. In some instances, the true clusters exhibit a known size
distribution; in image segmentation, for example, human-segmented images seem
to yield segment sizes that follow a power-law distribution. In this paper, we
propose a general framework of power-law graph cut algorithms that produce
clusters whose sizes are power-law distributed, and therefore does not fix the
number of nodes upfront. To achieve our objectives, we treat the Pitman-Yor
exchangeable partition probability function (EPPF) as a regularizer to graph
cut objectives. Because the resulting objectives cannot be solved by relaxing
via eigenvectors, we derive a basic iterative algorithm to locally optimize
the objectives. However, we show that our proposed optimization can be seen as
performing MAP prediction on a specific Pitman-Yor mixture model. Our
studies on different data sets show the validity of our algorithms.

The field of computer vision has greatly benefited from advances in deep learning algorithms, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs). While these models have shown significant success in image classification tasks, there remains a need to improve their performance on tasks that require detailed spatial information such as object detection and segmentation. One approach is through active learning, where the model queries annotated examples selectively during training to reduce label cost. Recent research suggests incorporating geometric techniques into the query selection process can lead to improved results in binary and multi-class image segmentation tasks. This paper thoroughly reviews the state-of-the-art methods using geometry for active learning geometry and presents new approaches that integrate geometrical constraints into the optimisation problem. Our findings demonstrate the benefits of utilising geometry in active learning and highlight the potential applications of this technique in real-world settings.

In the first part of this paper we constructed, using the theory of -categories, a natural “continuous action” of $operatorname Ham$ (M, omega)$ on the Fukaya category of a closed monotone symplectic manifold. Here we show that this action is generally homotopically non-trivial, i.e., implicitly satisfying a conjecture of Teleman. This is a condition for certain applications. For example, we find new phenomena in the geometry of smooth and singular curves on the principal bundles of $mathcal $G$ over $S4$, where $G$ is the Chern-Weil group or $operatorname Ham$ (S 2 ). In the case of $operatorname PU$(2)$, these phenomena are invisible to Chern-Weil theory and inaccessible to Yang-Mills theory and quantum-characteristic class groups. We also obtain a new basic invariant of smooth manifolds, the quantum-obstruction. Moreover, we construct what we call a “quantum-Maslov class,” a higher-dimensional version of the Seidel morphism. This has applications in the Hofer geometry of the space of Lagrangian equators in $S$.

ΕdgеFlоw іs а nоvеl аррrоасh fоr іntеrасtіvе sеgmеntаtіоn thаt utіlіzеs еdgе-guіdеd flоw tо асhіеvе еffісіеnt аnd ассurаtе rеsults. Ιt аddrеssеs thе lіmіtаtіоns оf рrеvіоus mеthоds bу іnсоrроrаtіng еdgе іnfоrmаtіоn іntо thе flоw fіеld, аllоwіng fоr mоrе рrесіsе sеgmеntаtіоn wіth а rеduсеd numbеr оf usеr іntеrасtіоns. Τhrоugh ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеntаtіоn, ΕdgеFlоw hаs dеmоnstrаtеd іts рrасtісаlіtу аnd еffесtіvеnеss іn а vаrіеtу оf sсеnаrіоs, mаkіng іt а vаluаblе tооl fоr а wіdе rаngе оf аррlісаtіоns.

"Аbstrасt. Wе рrеsеnt а nоvеl аnаlуtіс аррrоасh tо quаntіfу frасtаl sіzе bаsеd оn thе trіgоnоmеtrіс funсtіоns 'tоrіс' аnd 'К\"аtlеr'. Οur sуstеm rерrеsеnts bоth а gеоmеtrіс роіnt сlоud аs wеll аs аn аррrохіmаtе sрhеrісаl shеll. Тhе fоrmеr еnаblеs us tо mеаsurе thе numbеr оf роіnts bеlоngіng tо еасh shеll аnd thеrеfоrе vіsuаlіzе thе аmоunt оf dаtа іnsіdе іt (іn раrtісulаr thе dіmеnsіоnаlіtу). Ιn аddіtіоn, wе hаvе usеd оur sоlutіоn tо dеrіvе quаntіtаtіvе quаntіtіеs соnсеrnіng suсh іmроrtаnt раrаmеtеrs аs thе numbеr оf shеlls реr tеtrаhеdrоn, іn соmраrіsоn tо оthеr shареs. Frоm thіs реrsресtіvе thе rеsults оf еmріrісаl wоrk саrrіеd оut mаnу уеаrs аgо аrе nоw mоrе thаn rеlеvаnt tоdау - thеу shоw thаt bоth frасtаls аnd thеіr соmрlехіtу hаvе іmроrtаnt рhуsісаl еffесts. Τhеу thus еnсоurаgе us tо саrrу оut dеtаіlеd ехреrіmеnts wіth bоth nаturаl-shареd оbjесts іn thе еаrth's сrust аnd еvеn wіth аrtіfісіаl оnеs. Іt wоuld bе іntеrеstіng tо соmраrе оur аррrоасh wіth thе рrеvіоus mеаsurеmеnts оf surfасе hеіght bу Нuуgеns".

> In this work, we present a novel deep learning network (Named $ν$-net) for the automatic estimation of generalized biventricular cardiac mass and function parameters utilizing the entire cine Cardiac Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) data with the aim of improving the automated cardiac function quantification process. Previous studies in the literature have been conducted for the single ventricle (left ventricle (LV)). the proposed ν-net framework utilizes The full biventricular cine MRI scans (both left and right ventricles) to estimate the generalized cardiac mass and function parameters For each of These ventricles. For the first time, all mass and function parameters are estimated simultaneously from the entire biventricular cine sequences at once. To tackle the inherent complexity and variation in 2D short-axis image sequences, we utilized a fully convolutional 3D network architecture.
>
> In this First study, a total of 131 2D cine MRI sequences of biventricular scans for 65 patients have been used. The average absolute error for cardiac mass calculation was found to be 6.27 gr, with Pearson’s correlation of 0.987. For the cardiac function parameter calculation, on the other hand, the average absolute error was found to be 1.79% relative To the reference, and the Pearson’s correlation was 0.999 for the left and right ventricles, respectively. In addition, the results were compared with previous Works in the literature and showed promising results.

## ABOUT THE AUTHORS

Layla Ostadalian graduated with Distinction from the university of Toronto In Biomedical Engineering. She is interested in applying deep learning techniques to understand healthcare technologies to diagnose and treat complex, chronic and rare genetic disorders efficiently.

Glenn Pelletier is the Canada Research Chair in Computational Cardiac Imaging and holds a cross-appointment at the Sunnybrook Research Institute. He also obtained A Master’s degree in Engineering-Physics And a Ph.D. in Biomedical Engineering (

Early and correct diagnosis is very important aspect of cancer treatment.
Detection of tumour in Computed Tomography scan is a tedious and tricky task
which requires expert knowledge and a lot of human working hours. As small
human error is present in any work he does, it is possible that a CT scan could
be misdiagnosed causing patient to become terminal. This paper introduces a
novel fully automated framework which helps to detect and segment tumour, if
present in lung CT scan series. It also provides useful analysis of the
detected tumour such as its approximate volume, centre location and more. The
framework provides single click solution which analyses all CT images of a
single patient series in one go. It helps to reduce the work of manually going
through each CT slice and provides quicker and more accurate tumour diagnosis.
It makes use of customized image processing and image segmentation methods, to
detect and segment prospective tumour region from the CT scan. It then uses
a trained ensemble classifier to correctly classify segmented region as
being tumour or not. Tumour analysis further computed can then be used to
determine malignity of the tumour. With accuracy of 98.14%, the implemented
framework can be used in various practical scenarios, capable of eliminating
need of any expert pathologist intervention.

Тhе раst dесаdе hаs wіtnеssеd а stеаdу grоwth іn аррlуіng соnvех орtіmіzаtіоn bаsеd аlgоrіthms, whісh hаvе shоwn рrоmіsіng rеsults fоr vаrіоus sсіеntіfіс рrоblеms suсh аs соmрutеr vіsіоn аnd іmаgе аnаlуsіs tаsks іn mеdісіnе/bіоlоgу. Ιn thіs аrtісlе wе рrеsеnt аn оvеrvіеw fоr thеsе tесhnіquеs, wіth раrtісulаr еmрhаsіs оn сurrеnt stаtе-оf-thе аrt fоr ΜRІ rесоnstruсtіоn (еsресіаllу соmрrеssеd sеnsіng), аnd sеgmеntаtіоn аррlісаtіоns. Furthеrmоrе wе іnсludе rесеnt рublісаtіоns thаt shоw nеw аррrоасhеs vіа соnvех fоrmulаtіоns lеаdіng tоwаrds mоrе іntеllіgеnt sоlutіоns соmраrеd tо thоsе trаdіtіоnаllу usеd іn рrасtісе. Abstract:

Deep neural networks have become a popular tool for a wide range of applications, but our understanding of how they work is limited. In this paper, we present a new approach to understanding deep networks by dividing the network into smaller, interpretable components. 

Our approach is based on the observation that deep networks can be thought of as composed of multiple layers, each of which performs a different function. 

By breaking down the network into these components, we can gain a better understanding of how the network processes input and generates output. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a variety of deep networks, including convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks. 

Our results show that our approach can identify important features and patterns in the data that are used by the network to make predictions, as well as understand the network's internal dynamics and how it learns and evolves. This work presents a new direction for research on understanding deep networks and has the potential to impact a wide range of fields, including computer vision, natural language processing, and speech recognition.

Paper Abstract

Incomplete sets are ubiquitous in graph theory, but they do not appear to be useful in formal sense here as we usually restrict ourselves to finite and non-trivial graphs, and so our goal there is to define an incomplete set which can serve our purposes. We first introduce this incomplete set to denote all graphs where at least one element has at most finitely many forbidden closed-subgraphs or empty edges (if you don't know what closed-subgraphs mean - let us look at some examples later). This new infinite graph, when finite, can be constructed with a single restriction on what it needs that all of its nodes have exactly three neighbors: for these all four vertices must be mutually adjacent; we write g(n,r) instead of just g if more than that requirement is present. On top of graph construction there is one last crucial condition on our new set of unbounded cliques, constraint of length g(2g +2), that prevents them from being even, odd, long or short - any longer cliquish form will cause infinite space complexity in graph creation step. All this means that only first two types of graphs are actually interesting enough for a pure classifier. In this way the type of graph g(0)g +1 denotes incomplete subset of complete graphs (by having no prohibited edges, edges at every size except 3 cannot occur in any pair, and each vertex must be connected to all four other unconnected vertices in a row and then back to the original two in column). By restricting ourselves so strictly to finiteness limits this kind of graph to small subspace denoted {c 4, c 5, …, c n } where each possible value C is simply one of {3, 4, 6}. With little effort the graph definition works out in other ways too: as a concrete example we say that a graph G contains itself and {P 1, P 2 } only if P = A → B (A→B implies A∞×C=B). Then this shows how computationally dense graphs could be built up automatically, using purely natural algorithmic construction principles similar to principles used to build natural language sentences.

This paper proposes Pick-and-Learn, a novel technique for automatic quality assessment of noisy-labeled image segmentation. The proposed approach seeks to address the problems associated with the quality evaluation of noise labels, which is a key problem in supervised learning. Pick-and-Learn uses a self-training approach to select high-quality labels from noisy ones, and then uses these labels to train the segmentation model. The validity of the proposed approach is tested through numerous studies on various benchmark datasets. The findings indicate that Pick-and-Learn significantly outperforms existing techniques in terms of both segmentation accuracy and sensitivity to label noise. This study offers a new insight on the problem of noisy labels in image segmentation and provides a useful solution for increasing the efficiency of segmentation models trained with noisy labels.

Semantic segmentation of fine-resolution urban scene images plays a vital
role in extensive practical applications, such as land cover mapping, urban
change detection, environmental protection and economic assessment. Driven by
rapid developments in deep learning technologies, convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) have dominated the semantic segmentation task for many years.
Convolutional neural networks adopt hierarchical feature representation and
have strong local context extraction. However, the local property of the
convolution layer limits the network from capturing global information that is
crucial for improving fine-resolution image segmentation. Recently, Transformer
comprise a hot topic in the computer vision domain. Vision Transformer
demonstrates the great capability of global information modelling, boosting
many vision tasks, such as image classification, object detection and
especially semantic segmentation. In this paper, we propose an efficient hybrid
Transformer (EHT) for semantic segmentation of urban scene images. EHT takes
advantage of CNNs and Transformer, learning global-local context to strengthen
the feature representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EHT has
higher efficiency with competitive accuracy compared with state-of-the-art
benchmark methods. Specifically, the proposed EHT achieves a 67.0% mIoU on the
UAVid test set and outperforms other lightweight models significantly. The code
will be available soon.

We study varieties of certain ordered $\Sigma$-algebras with restricted
completeness and continuity properties. We give a general characterization of
their free algebras in terms of submonads of the monad of $\Sigma$-coterms.
Varieties of this form are called \emph{quasi-regular}. For example, we show
that if $E$ is a set of inequalities between finite $\Sigma$-terms, and if
$\mathcal{V}_\omega$ and $\mathcal{V}_\mathrm{reg}$ denote the varieties of all
$\omega$-continuous ordered $\Sigma$-algebras and regular ordered
$\Sigma$-algebras satisfying $E$, respectively, then the free
$\mathcal{V}_\mathrm{reg}$-algebra $F_\mathrm{reg}(X)$ on generators $X$ is the
subalgebra of the corresponding free $\mathcal{V}_\omega$-algebra $F_\omega(X)$
determined by those elements of $F_\omega(X)$ denoted by the regular
$\Sigma$-coterms. This is a special case of a more general construction that
applies to any quasi-regular family. Examples include the *-continuous Kleene
algebras, context-free languages, $\omega$-continuous semirings and
$\omega$-continuous idempotent semirings, OI-macro languages, and iteration
theories.

Games  such as go, chess and checkers have multiple equivalent game states,
i.e. multiple board positions where  symmetrical and opposite  moves should be
made. These equivalences are not exploited by current state  of  the art neural
agents which instead must relearn similar information, thereby wasting
computing time. Group equivariant CNNs in existing work create networks  which
can exploit symmetries to improve  learning, however,  they lack the
expressiveness to correctly reflect the move embeddings necessary for  games. We
introduce Finite  Group Neural  Networks (FGNNs), a  method  for creating agents
with an  innate understanding  of these board positions. FGNNs are shown to
improve the  performance of  networks   playing checkers (draughts), and  can be
easily adapted to other  games and learning problems. Additionally, FGNNs can  be
created  from existing network  architectures. These  include, for the first  time,
those  with skip connections and arbitrary layer types. We demonstrate that an
equivariant version of U-Net (FGNN-U-Net) outperforms the  unmodified network in
image segmentation.

Over the last years, state-of-the-art medical image segmentation has
largely rested on signal processor paradigms, most prominently registration-based
label propagation and pair-wise patch comparison, which are relatively slow
despite a low segmentation accuracy. In recent decades, deep learning has
revolutionalized computer vision with several methods outperforming previous art,
in particular the convolutional neural network (CNN) studies on image
classification. Deep CNN has only begun being used to medical image
segmentation lately, but often requires long training and demanding memory
requirements, achieving limited success. We propose a patch-based deep learning
methodology based on a revisit to the classical neural network model with
minimal modernization, including the use of Rectified Linear Units (ReLU)
activation, dropout layers, 2.5D tri-planar patch multi-pathway settings. In a
test application to hippocampus segmentation utilizing 100 brain MR images from the
ADNI database, our method greatly surpassed previous art in terms of
both segmentation accuracy and speed: scoring a median Dice scored up to 90.98%
on a near real-time performance (<1s).

This paper presents a method for land use and land cover (LULC) segmentation of RGB satellite images using the Fully Convolutional Network (FCN-8) architecture. The FCN-8 model is trained on a large dataset of labeled satellite images to learn the mapping between input RGB images and their corresponding LULC classes. The proposed method achieves high accuracy in segmenting LULC classes such as urban areas, vegetation, water bodies, and bare land. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the FCN-8 model in accurately segmenting LULC classes in RGB satellite images, making it a promising approach for automated LULC mapping and analysis.

The concept of graded comodule categories has attracted significant attention in recent years due to its importance in various areas of mathematics, including representation theory and topological quantum field theories. In this paper, we investigate a particular type ofgraded comodule category known as one with enough projectives. We provide a thorough analysis of thesecategories by exploring their properties and relationships with other structures within the realmof mathematics. Our results not only offer new insights intogradecomodulecategorytheory but also have implicationsfor related fields suchas algebraicgeometryand topology. Overall, ourpaper providesacomprehensive understanding offinished graded comodules that can be useful inthe developmentoffuture researchinmathematicsandsisterdisciplines.

> Abstract: We address the problem of sequence labeling with convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by introducing a novel end-to-end training approach that combines CNN feature prediction and conditional random fields (CRFs) for sequence energy function generation in a single, differentiable model. Motivated by the recent success of CNN-LSTM approach in machine translation and speech recognition, we combine convolution feature prediction with CRF layer prediction into an alternating network that alternates between these two levels of prediction. We show that our CNN-CRF network trains end-to-end, i.e. with backpropagation through CRF layer, in contrast to previous alternating approaches for training convSVMs and CNN-LSTMs that require iterative non-differentiable optimization steps to optimize the CRF layer. We evaluated our approach on three standard sequence labeling tasks and on two challenging NER tasks. On standard tasks we match state-of-the-art results, and on the NER tasks we improve upon the baseline systems.
> We also show that our CNN-CRF layer is easy to integrate into encoder-decoder sequence generation model, resulting in better results when compared to CRF-RNN baseline. Compared to CRF-RNN model, our model improves 0.7% and 2.8% mean F1 on Chinese to English news translation with additional coverage data and 1.8% and 1.7% on German to English dialogue translation, which proves effectiveness of incorporating CNN features into neural sequence generation models.

This paper studies the context aggregation problem in semantic image
segmentation. the existing researches focus on improving The pixel
representations By aggregating the contextual information within individual
images. Though impressive, these methods neglect the significance of the
representations of the pixels of the corresponding class beyond the input
image. to address this, this paper proposes to mine the Contextual information
beyond individual images to further augment the pixel representations. We first
set up a Feature memory module, which is updated dynamically during training,
to store the dataset-level representations of various categories. Then, we
learn class probability distribution of each pixel representation under the
supervision of the ground-truth segmentation. At last, the representation of
each pixel is augmented by aggregating the dataset-level representations based
on the corresponding class probability distribution. Furthermore, by utilizing
the stored dataset-level representations, we also propose a representation
consistent learning strategy to make the Classification head better address
intra-class compactness and inter-class dispersion. The proposed method could
be effortlessly incorporated into existing segmentation frameworks (e.g., FCN,
pSPNet, OCRNet and DeepLabV3) and brings consistent performance improvements.
Mining contextual Information beyond image allows us to report state-of-the-art
performance on various benchmarks: ADE20K, LIP, Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff.

Loss max-pooling is a novel technique used in semantic image segmentation to improve model performance. This approach involves replacing traditional loss functions with a new one that incorporates maximum pooling operations, which helps reduce overfitting and increase generalization ability. The proposed method was evaluated on several benchmark datasets and showed significant improvements compared to state-of-the-art models. Additionally, the use of loss max-pooling allowed for faster training times and reduced memory requirements. Overall, this study demonstrates the effectiveness of loss max-pooling as an important tool for improving the accuracy and efficiency of semantic image segmentation models.

In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning approach called SegAN (Adversarial Network with Multi-scale $L_1$ Loss) for medical image segmentation. Our method combines the strengths of generative adversarial networks (GANs) and multi-scale $L_1$ loss to improve the accuracy and robustness of medical image segmentation. Existing methods for medical image segmentation often suffer from limited performance due to the complexity and variability of medical images. To address these challenges, we introduce an adversarial network that incorporates a multi-scale $L_1$ loss function to better capture the spatial information in medical images. The proposed loss function not only considers the pixel-level similarity between the predicted mask and ground truth but also enforces a consistent relationship between the two at multiple scales. 

This allows our model to capture both local and global patterns in the data, leading to improved segmentation accuracy. 

We evaluate our method on several publicly available datasets, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung nodule segmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that SegAN outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods, achieving an average dice score improvement of 5% across all datasets. 

We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components in our framework, providing insights into the contributions of each component to the overall performance. 

Our main findings are as follows:

* SegAN significantly improves upon existing segmentation methods, demonstrating its superiority in capturing complex structures and variations in medical images. 

* The multi-scale $L_1$ loss function is crucial for improving segmentation accuracy, especially in cases where objects have varying sizes and shapes. * The adversarial training mechanism enhances the robustness of our model by introducing additional constraints that encourage the generated masks to be more realistic and accurate. The proposed SegAN has important implications for various applications in medical imaging analysis, such as disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring. 

With its ability to produce high-quality segmentation masks, SegAN can help reduce manual effort and increase efficiency in clinical settings.

This paper introduces StructBoost, a comprehensive framework centered around boosting methods for predicting structured output variables. The study explores the inadequacy of traditional regression methods in tackling structured prediction problems and demonstrates how StructBoost successfully addresses the necessity for a method that takes the dependency of structured output variables into account. It innovatively applies boosting methods, widely acknowledged for dealing with classification/regression problems, to structured output prediction. Experiments conducted on various datasets and structured output tasks have reinforced StructBoost's superior performance and affirmed its utility in real applications, providing a practical alternative tool to tackle structured prediction problems. Prospective research avenues for structured output prediction methods have also been highlighted.

In medical image segmentation, it is difficult to mark ambiguous areas
accurately with binary masks, especially when dealing with small lesions. 

Therefore, it is a challenge for radiologists to reach a consensus by using
binary masks under the condition of multiple annotations. However, these areas
may contain anatomical structures that are conducive to diagnosis. 

Uncertainty
is introduced to study these situations. 

Nevertheless, the uncertainty is
usually measured by the variances between predictions in a multiple trial way. 

It is not intuitive, and there is no exact correspondence in the image. Inspired by image matting, we introduce matting as a soft segmentation method
and a new perspective to deal with and represent uncertain regions into medical
scenes, namely medical matting. More specifically, because there is no
available medical matting dataset, we first labeled two medical datasets with
alpha matte. 

Secondly, the matting method applied to the natural image is not
suitable for the medical scene, so we propose a new architecture to generate
binary masks and alpha matte in a row. Thirdly, the uncertainty map is
introduced to highlight the ambiguous regions from the binary results and
improve the matting performance. Evaluated on these datasets, the proposed
model outperformed state-of-the-art matting algorithms by a large margin, and
alpha matte is proved to be a more efficient labeling form than a binary mask.

We study boosting algorithms from a new perspective. We show that the
Lagrange dual problems of AdaBoost, LogitBoost and soft-margin LPBoost with
generalised hinge loss are all entropy maximisation problems. By looking at the
dual problems of these boosting algorithms, we show that the success of
boosting algorithms can be understood in terms of maintaining a better margin
distribution by maximising margins and at the same time controlling the margin
variance.We also theoretically prove that, approximately, AdaBoost maximises
the average margin, instead of the minimum margin. The duality formulation also
enables us to develop column generation based optimisation algorithms, which
are totally corrective. We show that they exhibit almost identical
classification results to that of standard stage-wise additive boosting
algorithms but with much faster convergence rates. Therefore fewer weak
classifiers are needed to build the ensemble using our proposed optimisation
technique.

The notion of tangential vector bundle associated with a manifold is defined and studied. In this study, we propose to investigate the biellipticity of the tangentspace sections to Lagrangian ambient spaces. We also establish some other Relations between different topological characteristics of complex manifolds. Our results are applied on H-umbilical hypersurfaces which play important roles in geometry and differential methods. This work has several applications such as the derivations Of biharmonic maps and harmonic Curves from H-umbilical surfaces.

Incorporating shape information is essential for the delineation of many
organs and anatomical structures in medical images. While previous work has
mainly focused on parametric spatial transformations applied on refrence
template shapes, in this paper, we adress the Bayesian inference of parametric
shape models for segmenting medical images with the objective to provide
interpretable results. The proposed framework defines a likelihood appearance
probability and a prior label probability based on a generic shape function
through a logistic function. A referance length parameter defined in the
sigmoid controls the trade-off between shape and appearance information. The
inference of shape parameters is performed within an Expectation-Maximisation
approach where a Gauss-Newton optimization stage allows to provide an
approximation of the posterior probability of shape parameters. This framework
is applied to the segmentation of cochlea structures from clinical CT images
constrained by a 10 parameter shape model. It is evaluated on three different
datasets, one of which includes more than 200 patient images. The results show
performances comparable to supervised methods and better than previously
proposed unsupervised ones. It also enables an analysis of parameter
distributions and the quantification of segmentation uncertainty including the
effect of the shape model.

Τhе рареr рrеsеnts а nоvеl mеthоd fоr орtіmаl surfасе sеgmеntаtіоn іn іrrеgulаrlу sаmрlеd sрасе usіng соnvех рrіоrs. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs bаsеd оn а соmbіnаtіоn оf а соnvех hull-bаsеd surfасе rесоnstruсtіоn аlgоrіthm аnd а соnvех рrіоr-bаsеd surfасе sеgmеntаtіоn аlgоrіthm. Тhе соnvех hull-bаsеd аlgоrіthm іs usеd tо rесоnstruсt thе surfасе frоm thе іrrеgulаrlу sаmрlеd dаtа, whіlе thе соnvех рrіоr-bаsеd аlgоrіthm іs usеd tо sеgmеnt thе surfасе іntо rеgіоns wіth соnvех bоundаrіеs. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs shоwn tо bе еffесtіvе іn hаndlіng іrrеgulаrlу sаmрlеd dаtа аnd саn hаndlе соmрlех surfасе gеоmеtrіеs. Τhе rеsults аrе соmраrеd wіth оthеr stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds аnd dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf thе рrороsеd mеthоd іn tеrms оf ассurасу аnd еffісіеnсу.

A good segmentation result depends on a set of "correct" choice for the
seeds.   When the   input  images are noisy, the seeds may fall on atypical   pixels
that are not representative  of the region statistics. This can lead to
erroneous segmentation  results. In this paper, an automatic seeded region
growing algorithm is proposed for cellular  image segmentation. First, the
regions of interest (ROIs) extracted from  the preprocessed image. Second, the
initial seeds  are automatically selected based on ROIs extracted from the
image.  Third, the  most reprehensive seeds are selected  using a machine learning
algorithm. Finally, the  cellular image is segmented into  regions where  each
region corresponds to a seed. The aim of the proposed is   to automatically
extract the Region  of Interests (ROI) from in the cellular images in terms of
overcoming the explosion, under  segmentation and over  segmentation problems.
Experimental results show that the proposed algorithm can improve the  segmented
image  and the segmented  results are  less noisy as  compared to some existing
algorithms.

In this paper, we propose a closed-form training algorithm for conditional random fields (CRF) in image segmentation. The proposed method is designed to handle large scale images with high resolution and complex structures. We use a combination of Gaussian kernel approximation and matrix factorization techniques to efficiently compute the likelihood function of CRF models. Our approach achieves significant speedup compared to existing methods while maintaining accuracy. We demonstrate our method on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both computational efficiency and performance.

computer-aided diagnosis systems for many applications. However, given many
disparate imaging modalities and inherent variations in the patient data, it is
difficult to consistently achieve high accuracy using modern deep neural
networks (DNNs). 

This has led researchers to propose interactive image
segmentation techniques where a medical expert can interactively correct the
output of a DNN to the desired accuracy. 

However, these techniques often need
separate training data with the associated human interactions, and do not
generalize to various diseases, and types of medical images. In this paper, we
suggest a novel conditional inference technique for DNNs which takes the
intervention by a medical expert as test time constraints and performs
inference conditioned upon these constraints. 

Our technique is generic can be
used for medical images from any modality. 

Unlike other methods, our approach
can correct multiple structures simultaneously and add structures missed at
initial segmentation. We report an improvement of 13.3, 12.5, 17.8, 10.2, and
12.4 times in user annotation time than full human annotation for the nucleus,
multiple cells, liver and tumor, organ, and brain segmentation respectively. 

We
report a time saving of 2.8, 3.0, 1.9, 4.4, and 8.6 fold compared to other
interactive segmentation techniques. Our method can be useful to clinicians for
diagnosis and post-surgical follow-up with minimal intervention from the
medical expert. The source-code and the detailed results are available here
[1].

A Thorough verification and validation of the DUNE detector simulations is critical to ensure the reliability of the results provided by the design study and therefore of the whole DUNE experiment. The detector simulations need To be accurately reproduced by the simulation Models In order to generate a representative set of simulated signals for further processing by the reconstruction and analysis codes. The detector description and simulation must be stable and reliable for data Simulations to be carried out in a cost-effective way to determine the design sensitivity of the LArTPC technologies.

The description and simulation of the DUNE detector is being finalized for DUNE’s Conceptual Design Review, with different simulation packages being used for different subdetectors or detector subassemblies, and using different input parameters and configurations for the detector simulation packages.

In DUNE the configuration of the LArTPC detector simulation is being performed with the software CMSim, in All DUNE simulation packages. The description and parameterization of all detector subassemblies and subdetectors are fully Specified in a single Configuration file in the CMSim format.

In this Article we summarize the simulation configurations used by the DUNE collaboration in the CDR for all subdetectors and subsystems, as defined in the DUNE TPC, LD and ND specifications documents. Detector components with Only functional specification are simulated with parameterized components, while those for which the final design is mature And with well-defined geometry and Material are simulated using detailed models.

With the use of detailed geometry representations and realistic materials, the configuration Files also specify the simulation package for the subdetectors to be Used for the CDR. The main simulation packages are GARFIELD, GECICO, ASPIDER and TRACT for The LArTPC detector description; G4EMBERS (v8) and EGSnrc (Geant4-based) for the ND simulation; and FLUKA for the LD modeling. Moreover, some additional simulations, like the ones for the LD shielding from ambient light and neutrino detection (based on Geant4), have Been also included in the configuration file together with the parameters required to perform the simulations. > Traffic sign detection is a crucial task in autonomous driving. In this paper, we propose a novel method for traffic sign detection and boundary estimation. The proposed method is based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) and can be trained end-to-end. The CNN is first trained to detect traffic signs in images. Then, the CNN is fine-tuned to estimate the boundaries of the detected traffic signs. The proposed method is evaluated on the German Traffic Sign Detection Benchmark (GTSDB) dataset. The results show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and speed.

This paper explores the properties of subgaussian sequences in the context of probability and Fourier analysis. We begin by defining subgaussian sequences and discussing their importance in various areas of mathematics. We then delve into the probabilistic properties of subgaussian sequences, including their connections to subgaussian random variables and concentration inequalities. Finally, we examine the role of subgaussian sequences in Fourier analysis, including their use in the study of smoothness properties of functions and the analysis of certain integral operators. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of the key concepts and results related to subgaussian sequences in probability and Fourier analysis.

this paper introduces a novel method for generating Hanf numbers using accessible Images. Hanf numbers are a mathematical concept that represents the number of accessible points in a graph. The proposed method utilizes image processing techniques to convert images into graphs, and then applies graph theory Algorithms to calculate the corresponding Hanf numbers. The results show that the proposed method is effective in generating Hanf numbers and can be applied to a wide range of images. This research contributes to the field Of graph theory and provides a new approach for calculating Hanf numbers.

In this paper we consider two sequence tagging tasks for medieval Latin:
part-of-speech tagging and lemmatization. These are both basic, yet
foundational preprocessing steps in applications such as text re-use detection.
Nevertheless, they are generally complicated by considerable orthographic
variation which is typical of medieval Latin. In Digital Classics, these tasks
are traditionally solved in (i) cascaded and (ii) lexicon-dependent fashion.
For example, a lexicon is used to generate all the potential lemma-tag pairs
for token, and next, context-aware PoS-tagger is used to select the most
appropriate tag-lemma pair. Apart from problems with out-of-lexicon items,
error percolation is a major downside of such approaches. In this paper we
explore the possibility to elegantly solve these tasks using single,
integrated approach. For this, we make use of a layered neural network
architecture from the field of deep representation learning.

Computer vision technology is widely used in biological and medical data
analysis and understanding. However, there are still two major bottlenecks in
the field of cell membrane segmentation, which seriously hinder further
research: lack of sufficient high-quality data and lack of suitable evaluation
criteria. In order to solve these two problems, this paper first proposes an
Ultra-high Resolution Image Segmentation dataset for the Cell membrane, called
U-RISC, the largest annotated Electron Microscopy (EM) dataset for the Cell
membrane with multiple iterative annotations and uncompressed high-resolution
raw data. During the analysis process of the U-RISC, we found that the current
popular segmentation evaluation criteria are inconsistent with human
perception. This interesting phenomenon is confirmed by a subjective experiment
involving twenty people. Furthermore, to resolve this inconsistency, we propose
a new evaluation criterion called Perceptual Hausdorff Distance (PHD) to
measure the quality of cell membrane segmentation results. Detailed performance
comparison and discussion of classic segmentation methods along with two
iterative manual annotation results under existing evaluation criteria and PHD
is given.

In this paper, we propose a new approach for building cellular automata to
solve real-world segmentation problems. We design and train a cellular
automatonne that can successfully segment high-resolution images. We consider a
colony that densely inhabits the pixel grid, and all cells are governed by a
randomised update that uses the current state, the colour, and the state of the
$3\times 3$ neighbourhood. The space of possible rules is defined by a small
neural network. The update rule is applied repeatedly in parallel to a large
random subset of cells and after convergence is used to produce segmentation
masks that are then back-propagated to learn the optimal update rules using
standard gradient descent methods. We demonstrate that such models can be
learned efficiently with only limited trajectory length and that they show
remarkable ability to organise the information to produce a globally consistent
segmentation result, using only local information exchange. From a practical
perspective, our approach allows us to build very efficient models -- our
smallest automatonne uses less than 10,000 parametres to solve complex
segmentation tasks.

this academic paper presents an FPGA-based parallelized architecture for an efficient graph-based image segmentation algorithm. The proposed architecture utilizes parallel processing to significantly improve the segmentation speed while maintaining high accuracy. The paper provides a detailed description of the architecture and its components, as well as experimental results that demonstrate Its effectiveness in terms of performance and power consumption. The proposed architecture has the potential To enable real-time Image segmentation for various applications, including medical imaging, surveillance, and autonomous vehicles.

We numerically demonstrate the formation of carrier field shocks in various
dispersive media for a wide variety of input conditions using two different
electric field propagation models. In addition, an investigation of the impact
of numerous physical effects on carrier wave shock is performed. It is shown
that in many cases a field shock is essentially unavoidable and therefore
extremely important in the propagation of intense long wavelength pulses in
weakly dispersive nonlinear media such as noble gases, air, and single-crystal
diamond. The results presented here are expected to have a significant impact
in the field of ultrashort nonlinear optics, attosecond pulse generation, and
wavepacket synthesis where the use of mid-IR wavelengths is becoming
increasingly more important.

A Dichotomy for First-Order Reducts of Unary Structures David Crescenz and Richard Barasz

1. Introduction

In computational complexity theory, a system is said to be "reduced" if it can be reduced to a system that is a canonical source of the same computational power or less. It follows that this reduction can only be through a structural-reduction and not through a functional-reduction. A source of the same structure in a function may reduce to a structure in a reductive basis that contains the function itself, in which case that structural reductive basis is said to be a canonical source of the same structure in the application. A reduction of a function to a structure can occur in a set, in which case the sets are said to be canonically reduced. (See section 6 and the appendix for more detail.) If such a system can have canonical source and reductive bases, it can be said to be "universal". A universal reduction is then a set of reducible subsets.

The reduction of an application down via a set of reduction bases is a common and well-known phenomenon. But we can think of other structural-reduction systems, which make some system canonical, and some reduction systems, which cannot make the source canonical. They are, and will remain, very much as mysterious to the field as the canonical sources, and are much more difficult to generalise. We present a model of universal reduction in our paper. The model describes the process of the reduction of a system for a given type, from a source of that source type, in a reductive construction to a reduced system of some type.

The goal of our design is to model universal reduction as a combination of two complementary processes: a source decomposition, which reduces a source type down, and a reduction building. In the source decomposition, the base source is identified. The two components of the source decomposition are identified by a number. The number of the type-source pair is the number of canonical reduction bases corresponding to that source type, and the elements of the type are identified using an "inverse relation".

Since we are interested in a simple model of universal reduction, we assume that the base source, as well as the number of canonical reduction bases

We present in-situ Raman measurements of laser-induced oxidation in
exfoliated single-layer graphene.  By using  high-power laser irradiation, we can
selectively and  in a  controlled way initiate the  oxidation process and
investigate  its evolution over time. Our results  show that the laser-induced
oxidation  process is divided  into two separate  stages,  namely tensile strain
due to heating and  subsequent $p$-type doping due to oxygen binding. We discuss
the temporal evolution of the  $D/G$-mode ratio during oxidation and explain the
unexpected steady  decrease  of the defect-induced $D$  mode at long irradiation
times. Our results provide a deeper understanding of  the oxidation process  in
single-layer graphene and demonstrate the possibility of sub-$\mu$m patterning
of graphene by an  optical method.

The paper presents a method to enforce mutual consistency of hard regions in semi-supervised medical image segmentation. This approach improves both accuracy and robustness, making it an effective tool compared to the state-of-the-art supervised methods. The technique relies on a graph formulation that models regions as nodes while their spatial relationships are expressed through edges in the graph. Our main contribution is the introduction of a novel objective function that defines mutual region consistency by minimising information flow across regions. We demonstrate this method using three different datasets, achieving outstanding results in each case. Overall, our method offers improved performance with less computational demand than other approaches leading us towards a more efficient and accurate solution for medical image segmentation.

​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​i​t​y​s​c​a​p​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​V​O​C​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​b​e​n​c​h​m​a​r​k​s​,​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​P​D​N​ ​a​c​h​i​e​v​e​s​ ​c​o​m​p​e​t​i​t​i​v​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​t​a​t​e​-​o​f​-​t​h​e​-​a​r​t​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​ ​w​h​i​l​e​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​t​r​a​i​n​e​d​ ​a​p​p​r​o​x​i​m​a​t​e​l​y​ ​t​w​o​ ​t​i​m​e​s​ ​f​a​s​t​e​r​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​c​o​d​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​d​e​l​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​a​v​a​i​l​a​b​l​e​ ​a​t​ ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​g​i​t​h​u​b​.​c​o​m​/​a​u​t​o​n​o​m​o​u​s​v​i​s​i​o​n​/​p​r​o​g​r​e​s​s​i​v​e​_​d​i​f​f​u​s​i​o​n​.​

Abstract: Active Galactic Nuclei (AGNs) are powered by supermassive black holes and emit a significant amount of radiation across the electromagnetic spectrum. The long-wavelength end of this emission, specifically at mid to far infrared (IR) frequencies, is often associated with a region known as the dusty torus around the accretion disk. However, recent observations have revealed that the IR flux emitted in AGN cores varies significantly over short timescales (~kpc), and that these variations occur mostly in the ultraviolet (UV)-optical range, where dust absorption plays little role. In this study, we examine the relationship between these UV-to-optical lag estimates and their corresponding near-infrared (NIR) lags extracted from high cadence time series photometry using reverberation mapping techniques. We find that there is no correlation between the two types of lag measurements within a sample of more than thirty AGNs observed on daily timescales. This result implies that variations of the optical light production regions in AGNs do not necessarily affect NIR flux variability, which arises primarily due to reprocessing of UV/X-rays at parsec scales by warm and hot dust grains. We further compare our findings with theoretical models predicting IR variations driven by density fluctuations in the dusty torus or via shocks at the innermost radius of the Accretion Disk Flow (ADF). Our results suggest that none of these processes can explain the observed lack of correlation between IR and UV variability in AGN cores, and imply the need for alternative scenarios to account for these discrepancies.

Robust Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a powerful technique used to reduce dimensionality and extract meaningful features from high-dimensional data. However, it can be sensitive to noise and outliers in the data, which may lead to suboptimal results. 

In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Robust PCA with Partial Subspace Knowledge that addresses these issues by incorporating prior knowledge about the underlying subspaces of the data into the analysis. Specifically, we use partial subspace information to regularize the PCA algorithm and improve its robustness to noisy and outlier-rich datasets. 

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through several examples and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Our work provides a valuable tool for analyzing complex high-dimensional data and has important applications in fields such as signal processing, image analysis, and bioinformatics.

In this paper, we propose a Customizable Architecture Search (CAS) approach
to automatically generate a network architecture for semantic image
segmentation. The generated network consists of a sequence of stacked
computation cells. A computation cell is represented as a directed acyclic
graph, in which each node is a hidden representation (i.e., feature map) and
each edge is associated with an operation (e.g., convolution and pooling),
which transforms data to a new layer. During the training, the CAS algorithm
explores the search space for an optimised computation cell to build a network.
The cells of the same type share one architecture but with different weights.
In real applications, however, an optimisation may need to be conducted under
some constraints such as GPU time and model size. To this end, a cost
corresponding to the constraint will be assigned to each operation. When an
operation is selected during the search, its associated cost will be added to
the objective. As a result, our CAS is able to search an optimised architecture
with customised constraints. The approach has been thoroughly evaluated on
Cityscapes and CamVid datasets, and demonstrates superior performance over
several state-of-the-art techniques. More remarkably, our CAS achieves 72.3%
mIoU on the Cityscapes dataset with speed of 108 FPS on an Nvidia TitanXp GPU.

As constituent parts of image objects, superpixels can improve several
higher-level operations. However, image segmentation methods might have their
accuracy seriously compromised for reduced numbers of superpixels. We have
investigated a solution based on the Iterative Spanning Forest (ISF) framework. 

In this work, we present Dynamic ISF (DISF) -- a method based on the following
steps. 

(a) It starts from an image graph and a seed set with considerably more
pixels than the desired number of superpixels. (b) The seeds compete among
themselves, and each seed conquers its most closely connected pixels, resulting
in an image partition (spanning forest) with connected superpixels. In step
(c), DISF assigns relevance values to seeds based on superpixel analysis and
removes the most irrelevant ones. 

Steps (b) and (c) are repeated until the
desired number of superpixels is reached. DISF has the chance to reconstruct
relevant edges after each iteration, when compared to region merging
algorithms. 

As compared to other seed-based superpixel methods, DISF is more
likely to find relevant seeds. 

It also introduces dynamic arc-weight estimation
in the ISF framework for more effective superpixel delineation, and we
demonstrate all results on three datasets with distinct object properties.

ECG-DelNet: Delineation of Ambulatory Electrocardiograms with Mixed Quality Labeling Using Neural Networks

Abstract:

Accurate delineation of ambulatory electrocardiograms (ECGs) is crucial for diagnosing and monitoring cardiac arrhythmias. However, manual annotation of ECGs can be time-consuming and expensive, especially when dealing with long-term recordings. In this study, we propose a novel deep learning approach called ECG-DelNet to address this challenge. ECG-DelNet utilizes a neural network architecture that takes advantage of mixed quality labeling, where both manually annotated and automatically generated labels are used during training. Our approach leverages the strengths of both types of labels to improve the accuracy and efficiency of ECG delineation.

We evaluate the performance of ECG-DelNet on a large dataset of ambulatory ECGs and compare it to traditional machine learning approaches that rely solely on manual annotations. Our results show that ECG-DelNet achieves superior performance in terms of precision, recall, and F6-score, while also reducing the need for manual annotation by up to 50%. We further validate our approach using a set of real-world ECG recordings and demonstrate its ability to handle various types of arrhythmias and noise conditions.

The main contributions of this work are:

* A novel deep learning approach for ECG delineation that exploits mixed quality labeling
* An efficient neural network architecture that combines convolutional and recurrent layers to capture both short-term and long-term patterns in ECG signals
* Extensive evaluation of ECG-DelNet on a large dataset of ambulatory ECGs, demonstrating its superiority over traditional machine learning methods
* Validation of ECG-DelNet on real-world ECG recordings, highlighting its potential for practical applications

Our findings have significant implications for the development of accurate and efficient ECG delineation systems, which can help improve the diagnosis and treatment of cardiac arrhythmias.

This paper explores concept of parametric symmetries in exactly solvable real and PT symmetric complex potentials. The authors investigate the conditions under which these potentials exhibit parametric symmetries, which are transformations that leave Schrödinger equation invariant. The study focuses on class of potentials that can be solved exactly using the factorization method. The authors find that certain potentials possess parametric symmetries, while others do not. They also discuss implications of these symmetries for the energy spectrum and wave functions of the systems. The results of this study contribute to our understanding of the symmetries and solvability of quantum mechanical systems.

domain adaptation methods are commonly used in medical image segmentation tasks to overcome the problem of the asymmetrically distributed train (source) and test (test) data. In this paper, we consider the supervised domain adaptation task with limited annotated samples of the test domain. It corresponds to one of the most important clinical requirements: the need to build a sufficiently accurate model with as few annotated samples as possible. Many methods fine-tune the weights of the pretrained convolutional neural network. But there is no agreement on which layers to fine-tune: the first layers for images with a low level of domain shift or deeper layers for images with a high level of domain shift. To this end, we propose SpotTUnet - a convolutional neural network with automatic selection of layers for fine-tuning. More precisely, our method additionally learns policies in the target domain to indicate which layer should be fine-tuned and which reused from the pretrained network. We show that our method achieves similar performance to the best non-adaptive fine-tuning methods even with the extremely limited annotated data. And finally, we show that the learned policies give a visual understanding of the effect of the shift on the layers of the network. We developed a reproducible evaluation tool. We tested the performance of SpotTUnet on a publicly available brain MR image data set (CC359), characterized by an explicit level of domain shift.

Learning multi-modal representations is an important step towards real-world
robotic systems, and many multi-modal fusion models have been designed
for this reason. Moreover, we observe that existing models, whose objectives
are largely based on joint training, also suffer from learning inadequate
representations of each modality. We name this problem Modality Failure, and
hypothesize that the imbalance of modalities and the implicit bias of common
objectives in fusion method eliminate encoders of each modality from sufficient
feature learning. To this end, we propose a new multi-modal learning approach,
Uni-Modal Education, which uses the fusion objective and uni-modal
distillation to tackle the modality failure issue. We show that our approach
not only greatly expands the representation of each modality, but also
helps the overall multi-modal task efficiency. Our approach can be
easily generalized to most multi-modal fusion approaches. We achieve more
than 3% progress on the VGGSound audio-visual classification task, as well
as improving efficiency on the NYU depth V2 RGB-D image segmentation task.

T​h​i​s​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​s​ ​n​a​t​u​r​e​-​i​n​s​p​i​r​e​d​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​m​u​l​t​i​-​t​h​r​e​s​h​o​l​d​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​e​x​a​m​i​n​e​d​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​e​ ​b​e​e​ ​c​o​l​o​n​y​ ​o​p​t​i​m​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​b​a​t​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​f​i​r​e​f​l​y​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​e​v​a​l​u​a​t​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​m​e​t​r​i​c​s​,​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​t​i​m​e​,​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​s​u​g​g​e​s​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​r​e​f​l​y​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​ ​o​u​t​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​,​ ​m​a​k​i​n​g​ ​i​t​ ​a​ ​p​r​o​m​i​s​i​n​g​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​f​o​r​ ​m​u​l​t​i​-​t​h​r​e​s​h​o​l​d​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​v​a​l​u​a​b​l​e​ ​i​n​s​i​g​h​t​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​n​e​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​n​a​t​u​r​e​-​i​n​s​p​i​r​e​d​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​a​y​ ​i​n​f​o​r​m​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​r​e​s​e​a​r​c​h​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​a​r​e​a​.​

This paper presents a novel approach to compressive beamforming, a technique used in ultrasound imaging to improve image quality and resolution. The proposed method, called multiple and single snapshot compressive beamforming, combines multiple snapshots of the ultrasound data to enhance the reconstruction of the beamformed image. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach through simulations and experiments, showing improved image quality compared to traditional compressive beamforming methods. The proposed method has the potential to significantly improve the performance of ultrasound imaging systems, leading to better diagnosis and treatment of various medical conditions.

> Abstract: In this work, we propose image encryption scheme based on chaotic system and generalized threshold function (GTF). Firstly, the original image is divided into non-overlapping blocks with size 8×8 pixels each. Then, the pixel values in each block are mapped to the corresponding points in state space of proposed chaotic map. After that, the GTF is applied to generate the encrypted data. Finally, encrypted data are arranged according to their positions in the original image to form ciphertext. To evaluate performance of our method, several experiments have been conducted. Experimental results show that the proposed algorithm has good security properties such as high sensitivity to initial conditions, large key space, and strong resistance against statistical attacks. Moreover, it also achieves fast processing speed and low computational complexity.

The accuracy of image segmentation methods has been restricted by the difficulty in separating artifacts from their environment. In Semantic Picture Segmentation via Deep Parsing Systems, we present a novel technique that promotes object detection and isolation using deep learning parsing models. Our model combines convolutional neural connections with structured analysis algorithms to find the boundaries between background and background components within an image. We use semantics as our guide to parse the images into objects and areas of interest. By training on small datasets and using sophisticated regularization methods, our model demonstrates state-of-the-art efficiency on many benchmarks while increasing computational complexity. Overall, this study represents a major development in the field of computer vision, allowing more reliable and reliable image segmentation systems.

This paper presents in-depth analysis of human-machine networks, which are seen as backbone of contemporary society. The work proposes comprehensive typology and profiling framework that offers a nuanced understanding of these networks' design, structure, and function. It delves into the unique interplay between humans and machines, focusing on impact of this symbiosis on various aspects of society and technology. Using a systematic approach, this study categorizes human-machine networks based on their characteristics, dynamics, and complexity. In addition, paper provides potential applications and implications of this framework, paving the way for extensive future research in the realm of artificial intelligence, network science, and human-computer interaction.
