This paper  presents   a novel approach to model  reduction for flows  in heterogeneous  porous media, which is a significant challenge in various fields  such as hydrology, petroleum engineering, and environmental science. The proposed method combines global and local nonlinear model reduction techniques to accurately capture the   complex dynamics  of  these systems.  The global model  reduction provides  a low-dimensional  representation of the system, while the local  model reduction adapts to the local behavior of the flow. The effectiveness  of the proposed method is  demonstrated through numerical experiments  on benchmark problems. The  results show  that the global-local nonlinear model reduction method significantly improves the accuracy  and computational efficiency compared to traditional model reduction techniques.

Kid-Net: Convolution Networks for Kidney Vessels Segmentation from CT-Volumes

Accurate segmentation of kidney vessels is crucial for various clinical applications, including kidney disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring. However, manual segmentation of kidney vessels is a time-consuming and challenging task, especially for complex kidney vessels. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning approach, called Kid-Net, to automatically segment kidney vessels from computed tomography (CT) volumes.

Kid-Net is based on a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture that leverages both local and global contextual information from the CT volumes. The network consists of multiple convolutional and pooling layers, followed by a spatial pyramid pooling (SPP) layer to capture multi-scale features. We also introduce a novel depth-wise separable convolution (DSC) layer to reduce computational complexity while preserving feature representation.

We evaluated Kid-Net on a dataset of 32 CT volumes and compared its performance with state-of-the-art segmentation methods. Our results show that Kid-Net achieved an average Dice similarity coefficient of 0.8773, outperforming other methods by a significant margin. In addition, we performed a series of ablation studies to analyse the effectiveness of the SPP and DSC layers, demonstrating their crucial roles in improving the segmentation accuracy.

Kid-Net has several advantages over traditional methods. Firstly, it can handle complex kidney vessels with various shapes and sizes. Secondly, it does not require any prior knowledge or manual interaction, making it a fully automated segmentation approach. Finally, Kid-Net can be trained on a relatively small dataset, which is advantageous given the limited availability of annotated data.

In conclusion, Kid-Net offers a promising solution for accurate and efficient segmentation of kidney vessels from CT volumes. Its performance and robustness demonstrate the potential for widespread clinical application, including support for surgical planning, image-guided interventions, and disease monitoring. Future work includes refining the model to further improve performance

AIMS:The aim Of this Paper is to Study the characteristics of the stellar
populations and the metallicity distribution in the Galactic bulge. We study
the entire stellar population, but also retrieve information using only the red
clump stars. METHODS: To study the characteristics of The stellar populations
and the metallicity distribution in The Galactic bulge, we compared the output
of the Galaxy model TRILEGAL, which implements the Binney et al. (1997) bulge
model, with observations from 2MASS and oGLE-II. A minimisation procedure has
been set up to retrieve the best fitting model with different stellar
populations and metallicity distributions. RESULTS: Using the TRILEGAL code we
find that the best model resembling the characteristics of the Galactic bulge
is a model with the distance to the Galactic centre $R_0 =
8.7\pm^{0.57}_{0.43}$ kpc, the major axis ratios of the bar $1:\eta:\zeta = 1 :
0.68\pm_{0.19}^{0.05} : 0.31\pm_{0.04}^{0.06}$, and the Angle between the
Sun-centre line and the bar $\phi = 15\deg\pm_{12.7}^{13.3}$. Using these
parameters the best model is found for a burst of 8 Gyr, although it is almost
indistinguishable from models with ages of 9 and 10 Gyr. The metallicity
distribution found is consistent with metallicity distributions in the
literature based on Spectroscopic results.

We present a technique for analyzing neuron cells in 2D electron microscopy
imagery. This segmentation task has been a bottleneck to reconstruction efforts
of the cortex's neural circuits. One common issue is the misclassification
of blurry membrane fragments as cell interior, which leads to splitting of three
adjacent neuron segments into two via the blurry membrane area. Human
annotators can effectively reduce such problems by implicitly performing gap
completion, taking into account the continuity of membranes.
  Drawing inspiration from these human methods, we formulate the
segmentation task as an edge labeling solution on a diagram with local topological
restrictions. We derive an integer linear program (ILP) that defines membrane
consistency, i.e. the presence of gaps. The cost function of the ILP is the
pixel-wise variation of the segmentation from a priori membrane probabilities
derived from the data.
  Based on membrane probability maps derived using random forest classifiers
and convolutional neural connections, our technique increases the neuron boundary
segmentation accuracy compared to a range of standard segmentation
methods. Our approach effectively performs gap completion and leads to fewer
topological problems. The technique can possibly not be implemented into
other image segmentation pipelines with known topological parameters.

Clinical image segmentation plays a key role in clinical image research. In this research, we focus on the segmentation of brain MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) images and adopt a methodology based on multi-modal convolutional neural network (MCNN). The proposed technique uses T1-weighted, T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) images for segmentation, employing four distinct image fusion schemes: late fusion, late fusion and multi-scale early fusion. The goal is to explain the impact of image fusion scheme on the efficiency of MCNN. Our findings show that the multi-scale late fusion scheme gives the highest segmentation efficiency in terms of dice similarity coefficient and intersection over union.

This paper provides a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based method for analyzing curve structures from depth maps. The proposed approach uses a novel loss function and is trained on a small database of depth maps. The segmented curve structures can be applied to find historical heritage objects, giving insights into their mathematical and architectural properties. Experimental findings show the efficacy of the proposed approach in effectively describing curve structures and its effectiveness in supporting the analysis of historical heritage objects.

In this paper, we present a new combinatorial definition of the Theta-invariant, a well-known invariant of 3-manifolds, using Heegaard diagrams. The Theta-invariant is a powerful tool for studying the topology of 3-manifolds, and its computation has been a longstanding problem in the field. Our approach is based on the idea of associating a combinatorial object, called a Heegaard diagram, to a 3-manifold in a way that encodes information about the manifold's topology. 

We show that the Theta-invariant can be defined directly from this combinatorial object, without the need for any geometric or topological assumptions. Our definition provides a new perspective on the Theta-invariant, and it has several advantages over existing definitions. First, it is computationally efficient, allowing for fast computation of the Theta-invariant for a large class of 3-manifolds. Second, it is combinatorially natural, providing a direct way to understand the relationship between the Theta-invariant and other combinatorial invariants of 3-manifolds. 

Finally, it is flexible, allowing for the definition to be extended to a broader class of 3-manifolds than previously considered. 

We demonstrate the power of our definition by computing the Theta-invariant for a variety of 3-manifolds, including some with non-trivial topology. 

Our results provide new insights into the structure of these manifolds, and they highlight the potential of our approach for studying 3-manifolds with complex topology. 

Overall, our paper provides a significant advancement in the study of the Theta-invariant, and it opens up new avenues for research in 3-manifold topology. Our combinatorial definition of the Theta-invariant has the potential to revolutionize the field, providing a powerful tool for studying the topology of 3-manifolds and enabling new applications in mathematics and computer science.

An electromagnetic wave transmission through a sub-wavelength nano-hole in a two-dimensional plasmonic layer is investigated both analytically and numerically in this paper. 

We find an unconventional transmission in the infrared frequency range, which can be explained by both tunneling and surface plasmonic resonances in the system. In particular, the transmission coefficient exhibits a Fano-like resonance characterized by a dip around the normally expected peak. Our results show that the tunneling and plasmonic resonances could be manipulated independently by varying the hole size and the thickness of the plasmonic layer, which paves a way to enhance the tunneling transmission at desired wavelengths by engineering the structure.

In this paper, we study the application of game theory to wireless power control with the aim of developing novel strategies for improving the efficiency and reliability of wireless power transfer systems. We begin by reviewing the basic principles of game theory and its relevance to wireless power control, then we present a series of case studies to show the effectiveness of game-theoretic approaches in wireless power transfer systems. The first case we study is the interference management in wireless power transfer systems, in which several devices compete for access to the same power supply. We show how game-theoretic concepts, such as Nash equilibria and auctions, can be used to efficiently allocate power and reduce interference. Then we study the problem of power allocation in wireless power transfer systems, in which the devices must coordinate their power transmission levels in order to achieve optimum performance. We show how game-theoretic concepts, such as distributed algorithms and Stackelberg’s power allocation, can be used to efficiently allocate power to the devices. We also examine the issue of cooperation and collaboration in wireless power transfer systems, in which the devices must work together to achieve common goals. We show how game-theoretic models, such as asymmetric information games and network games, can be used to identify stable alliances and promote cooperation among the devices. Finally, we discuss the challenges in modeling and analyzing wireless power transfer systems using game theory and outline the research issues in this field. We show that game theory can be used to make significant advances in the design and operation of wireless power transfer systems, and it provides a framework for understanding and improving the behavior of the devices. It is our hope that the game-theoretic insights we have developed will be useful in the design of more efficient, reliable, and dependable wireless power transfer systems, which will meet the demands of wireless communication and energy-harvesting applications.

We present an algorithm that can solve a bistable equation in time and space, where the solution is obtained by traveling through a wave field. The algorithm is based on the idea that the solution to a bistable problem is given by a function f(t) which takes values at different times t. We show how this function can be written as a sum over all possible solutions to the problem, and then we use this sum to find the solution to the problem. Our algorithm solves the problem in time and space using only two parameters, the initial state and the current velocity. This means that it does not require any information about the initial state or the current velocity. In addition, our algorithm requires no knowledge of the boundary conditions between the states. Finally, our algorithm uses only one parameter, the initial state, which makes it much faster than other algorithms.

Title: A 4D Light-Field Database and CNN Architectures for Material Recognition
Abstract: This paper provides a novel database of dark field images captured with a four-dimensional (4D) light-field camera for material recognition. The database consists of 1062 specimens containing 37 distinct combinations of materials under differing illumination conditions, angles, distances, and orientations. We therefore propose three distinct convolutional neural network technologies that can simultaneously capture spatial depth information to reduce material recognition efficiency on our dataset and other 3D data. Our proposed models outperform state-of-the-art techniques compared to using regular 2D images alone and show promising results in real-world systems such as robotics or augmented reality applications.

Recent results in image-based medical prediction show that deep learning models can compete with experts, provided they are given ample amounts of annotated data. In this work, we investigate impact of the amount of training data on the performance of expert-level predictions in domain of lymph node segmentation and classification. We observe that, given a fixed amount of expert-annotated data, further inclusion of data that is labeled by less reliable sources (such as non-expert clinicians or even non-clinical information) results in decrease of performance. We show that such data selection strategies are especially important when dealing with rare events (e.g., cancer), as in these instances even expert-level predictions might have errors that could be avoided by careful data selection. To tackle this problem, we propose self-supervised approach for sample selection that uses a measure of interpretability to select the most informative samples for training. We show that our proposed method is especially useful in scenarios where only small amount of expert-level labeled data is available and that it performs on par with methods that rely on significantly more data.

We study the light scattering of homogenous radially-anisotropic spherical
particles. It is shown that radial anisotropy can be employed to tune
effectively the electric resonances, and thus enable flexible overlapping of
electric and magnetic dipoles of various numbers, which leads to unidirectional
forward super-scattering at different spectral positions. 

We further reveal
that through adjusting the radial anisotropy parameters, electric and magnetic
resonances of higher orders can be also made overlapped, thus further
collimating the forward scattering lobes. The ultra-directional
super-scattering we have obtained with individual homogenous radially
anisotropic spherical particles may shed new light to the design of compact and
efficient nanoantennas, which may find various applications in solar cells,
bio-sensing and many other antenna based researches.

Using the notion of formal ball, we present a few new results in the theory
of quasi-metric spaces. With no specific order: every continuous
Yoneda-complete quasi-metric space is sober and convergence Choquet-complete
hence Baire in its $d$-Scott topology; for standard quasi-metric spaces,
algebraicity is equivalent to having enough center points; on a standard
quasi-metric space, every lower semicontinuous $\bar{\mathbb{R}}_+$-valued
function is the supremum of a chain of Lipschitz Yoneda-continuous maps; the
continuous Yoneda-complete quasi-metric spaces are exactly the retracts of
algebraic Yoneda-complete quasi-metric spaces; every continuous Yoneda-complete
quasi-metric space has a so-called quasi-ideal model, generalizing a
construction due to K. Martin. The point is that all those results reduce to
domain-theoretic constructions on posets of formal balls.

Deep learning models have shown promising results for medical image segmentation tasks. However, these models often suffer from overfitting and lack of generalization to new datasets. In this paper, we propose a two-layer ensemble of deep learning models for medical image segmentation. The first layer is a collection of shallow learning models, such as logistic regression and decision trees, that are trained on same dataset as deep learning models. The second layer is a collection of deep learning models, such as convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks, that are trained on same dataset but with different architectures and hyperparameters. The two layers are then combined using an ensemble method, such as bagging or boosting, to produce final segmentation result. We evaluate our method on the MIT-BIH Atherosclerosis Segmentation Challenge and show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance. We also demonstrate the generalization ability of our method by testing it on separate dataset.

Cutting and pasting image segments feels intuitive: the choice of source
templates gives artists flexibility in recombining existing source material.
Formally, this process takes an image set as input and outputs a collage of the
set elements. Such selection from sets of source templates does not fit easily
in classical convolutional neural models requiring inputs of fixed size.
Inspired by advances in attention and set-input machine learning, we present a
novel architecture that can generate in one forward pass image collages of
source templates using set-structured representations. This paper has the
following contributions: (i) a novel framework for image generation called
Memory Attentive Generation of Image Collages (MAGIC) which gives artists new
ways to create digital collages; (ii) from the machine-learning perspective, we
show a novel Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) architecture that uses
Set-Transformer layers and set-pooling to blend sets of random image samples -
a hybrid non-parametric approach.

Tumor detection in biomedical imaging is time-consuming process for medical
professionals and is not without errors. Thus in recent decades, researchers
have developed algorithmic techniques for image processing using a wide variety
of mathematical methods, such as statistical modeling, variational techniques,
and machine learning. In this paper, we propose a semi-automatic method for
liver segmentation of 2D CT scans into three labels denoting healthy, vessel,
or tumor tissue based on graph cuts. First, we create a feature vector for each
pixel in a novel way that consists of 59 intensity values in time
series data and propose a simplified perimeter cost term in the energy
functional. We normalize data and perimeter terms in functional to
expedite the graph cut without having to optimize scaling parameter
$\lambda$. In place of training process, predetermined tissue means are
computed based on sample regions identified by expert radiologists. The
proposed method also has the advantage of being relatively simple to implement
computationally. It was evaluated against the ground truth on clinical CT
dataset of 10 tumors and yielded segmentations with mean Dice similarity
coefficient (DSC) of .77 and mean volume overlap error (VOE) of 36.7%. The
average processing time was 1.25 minutes per slice.

Automated digital histopathology image segmentation is an important task to
help pathologists diagnose tumours and cancer subtypes. For pathological
diagnosis of cancer subtypes, pathologists usually change the magnification of
whole-slide images (WSI) viewers. A key assumption is that the importance of
the magnifications depends on the characteristics of the input image, such as
cancer subtypes. In this paper, we propose a novel semantic segmentation
method, called Adaptive-Weighting-Multi-Field-of-View-CNN (AWMF-CNN), that can
adaptively use image features from images with different magnifications to
segment multiple cancer subtype regions in the input image. The proposed method
aggregates several expert CNNs for images of different magnifications by
adaptively changing the weight of each expert depending on the input image. It
leverages information in the images with different magnifications that might be
useful for identifying the subtypes. It outperformed other state-of-the-art
methods in experiments.

Precise 3D segmentation of infant brain tissues is an essential step towards
comprehensive volumetric studies and quantitative analysis of early brain
developement. However, computing such segmentations is very challenging,
especially for 6-month infant brain, due to the poor image quality, among other
difficulties inherent to infant brain MRI, e.g., isointense contrast
between white and gray matter and the severe partial volume effect due to small
brain sizes. This study investigates problem with an ensemble of semi-dense
fully convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which employs T1-weighted and
T2-weighted MR images as input. We demonstrate that ensemble agreement is
highly correlated with the segmentation errors. Therefore, our method provides
measures that can guide local user corrections. To the best of our knowledge,
this work is the first ensemble of 3D CNNs for suggesting annotations within
images. Furthermore, inspired by the very recent success of dense networks, we
propose novel architecture, SemiDenseNet, which connects all convolutional
layers directly to the end of network. Our architecture allows the
efficient propagation of gradients during training, while limiting number
of parameters, requiring one order of magnitude less parameters than popular
medical image segmentation networks such as 3D U-Net. Another contribution of
our work is study of impact that early or late fusions of multiple
image modalities might have on performances of deep architectures. We
report evaluations of our method on the public data of MICCAI iSEG-2017
Challenge on 6-month infant brain MRI segmentation, and show very competitive
results among 21 teams, ranking first or second in most metrics.

Wе соnsіdеr аn оrdіnаrу dіffеrеntіаl еquаtіоn wіth а unіquе hуреrbоlіс
аttrасtоr аt thе оrіgіn, tо whісh wе аdd а smаll rаndоm реrturbаtіоn. Ιt іs
knоwn thаt undеr gеnеrаl соndіtіоns, thе sоlutіоn оf thіs stосhаstіс
dіffеrеntіаl еquаtіоn соnvеrgеs ехроnеntіаllу fаst tо аn еquіlіbrіum
dіstrіbutіоn. Wе shоw thаt thе соnvеrgеnсе оссurs аbruрtlу: іn а tіmе wіndоw оf
smаll sіzе соmраrеd tо thе nаturаl tіmе sсаlе оf thе рrосеss, thе dіstаnсе tо
еquіlіbrіum drорs frоm іts mахіmаl роssіblе vаluе tо nеаr zеrо, аnd оnlу аftеr
thіs tіmе wіndоw thе соnvеrgеnсе іs ехроnеntіаllу fаst. Τhіs іs whаt іs knоwn
аs thе сut-оff рhеnоmеnоn іn thе соntехt оf Μаrkоv сhаіns оf іnсrеаsіng
соmрlехіtу. Іn аddіtіоn, wе аrе аblе tо gіvе gеnеrаl соndіtіоns tо dесіdе
whеthеr thе dіstаnсе tо еquіlіbrіum соnvеrgеs іn thіs tіmе wіndоw tо а
unіvеrsаl funсtіоn, а fасt knоwn аs рrоfіlе сut-оff.

Clustering is unsupervised learning method falling under realm of machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI). In this paper, we present a new clustering algorithm called Point-Set Kernel (PSK), in which we utilize the kernel idea in non-standard way, i.e., through the use of specific kernel that captures the local information of data points in the dataset in lower-dimensional space. In particular, kernel utilized in this work is derived from Gaussian distribution. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed PSK algorithm via an experiment on five well-known datasets, comparing the results to those obtained by k-means algorithm, well-known clustering algorithm. The results show that the PSK algorithm provides better clustering performance as compared to k-means algorithm.

Picture segmentation refers to the procedure to divide an image into
nonoverlapping meaningful areas according to human perception, which has
become a classic subject since the late ages of computer vision. A lot of
research has been conducted and has resulted in numerous applications. Unfortunately,
while several segmentation algorithms exist, yet there are only a few sparse and
outdated summarizations available, an outline of the recent achievements and
challenges is lacking. We aim to deliver a systematic evaluation of the recent
progress in this field. Covering 180 articles, we offer an overview of diverse
areas of segmentation topics including not only the modern bottom-up
methods, but also the recent advancement in superpixel, interactive tools,
object proposals, semantic image parsing and image cosegmentation. In addition,
we often review the existing influential datasets and evaluation metrics.
Then, we suggest some design flavors and research directions for future
research in image segmentation.

There are very little state-of-the-art models for images classifying them, including the commonly used HOG and KMeans methods [ 1 ]. 

However, they require lots of iterations to work well in practice. 

Such large datasets can be hard to manage on mobile devices such as smartphones because processing takes time. 

We have recently presented W-Net, an image segmentation model capable of training only a few thousand random images per hour while still producing robust estimates that outperform existing models on large data sets. In this paper, we propose an extension to make it able to run with any small number of trained models at near real-time speeds. A new function parameterizes how close its network predictions approximate the measured accuracy. 

The resulting performance can exceed 90% in some practical situations even when labeled data sizes exceed several terabytes. This feature could prove useful in applications where speed is important but also long training times are not necessarily a limiting factor. W-net is currently limited by high learning rates that limit its generalization to complex scenes or images of different scales and features. In contrast, our proposed method has several novel extensions to improve its power to detect objects and shapes. Its architecture allows the learning rate inversely proportional to the amount of labeled input while significantly increasing its recall accuracy. 

These improvements provide ample flexibility and enable us to increase the W-net's performance further when more labels are available. 

In parallel, we show how our newly introduced hyperparameters, as compared to its original ones, allow the model to learn more advanced object detection schemes that yield results both better than previously achievable and faster than all other approaches tested to date.

Distributed processing over networks relies on in-network processing and
cooperation among neighboring agents. Cooperation is beneficial when agents
share a common objective. However, in many applications agents may belong to
different clusters that pursue different objectives. Then, indiscriminate
cooperation will lead to undesired results. In this work, we propose an
adaptive clustering and learning scheme that allows agents to learn which
neighbors they should cooperate with and which other neighbors they should
ignore. In doing so, the resulting algorithm enables the agents to identify
their clusters and to attain improved learning and estimation accuracy over
networks. We carry out a detailed mean-square analysis and assess the error
probabilities of Types I and II, i.e., false alarm and mis-detection, for the
clustering mechanism. Among other results, we establish that these
probabilities decay exponentially with the step-sizes so that the probability
of correct clustering can be made arbitrarily close to one.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Fully Transformer Networks for Semantic Image Segmentation" is as follows:

Semantic image segmentation is a critical task in computer vision that involves dividing an image into multiple regions and assigning each region a semantic label. Recent advances in convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have significantly improved the performance of semantic image segmentation. However, CNNs suffer from several limitations, including the need for large amounts of training data and the difficulty in modeling long-range dependencies. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic image segmentation using fully transformer networks (FTNs). FTNs are a type of neural network architecture that has shown great success in natural language processing tasks. We demonstrate that FTNs can also be applied to image segmentation tasks with comparable performance to state-of-the-art CNNs. Our approach involves encoding the input image using an FTN and then decoding it using a series of transposed convolutional layers. We also propose several novel techniques to improve the performance of our approach, including attention mechanisms and multi-scale fusion. Our experiments show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art CNNs on several benchmark datasets, including the COCO and CityScapes datasets. Overall, our work demonstrates the effectiveness of FTNs in image segmentation tasks and paves the way for future research in this area.

The Salem-Zygmund inequality is a widely used tool in harmonic analysis for controlling the size of maximal function. In this paper, we establish a version of Salem-Zygmund inequality for locally sub-Gaussian random variables, random trigonometric polynomials, and random circulant matrices. Our result generalizes and improves several existing inequalities in the literature and provides a unified treatment of these objects. We also provide examples and applications of our main result in various fields, such as probability theory, statistics, and numerical analysis.

Our proposed method uses two parallel convolutional networks to learn object and scene context features; one for image pixel predictions (object stream) as in semantic segmentation, another for interaction graph prediction between each pair of pixels (scene stream). Pixel probabilities are then integrated with graph topology constraints under the framework of energy minimization. In this fashion, our model benefits both streams by exchanging knowledge among them while keeping their characteristics preserved during training process. As we train on images rather than bounding boxes or depth information, our final system can be applied with great flexibility across many popular benchmark datasets...

U-Net has been providing state-of-the-art performance in many medical image
segmentation problems. 

Many modifications have been proposed for U-Net, such as
attention U-Net, recurrent residual convolutional U-Net (R2-UNet), and U-Net
with residual blocks or blocks with dense connections. 

However, all these
modifications have an encoder-decoder structure with skip connections, and the
number of paths for information flow is limited. 

We propose LadderNet in this
paper, which can be viewed as a chain of multiple U-Nets. Instead of only one
pair of encoder branch and decoder branch in U-Net, a LadderNet has multiple
pairs of encoder-decoder branches, and has skip connections between every pair
of adjacent decoder and decoder branches in each level. Inspired by the success
of ResNet and R2-UNet, we use modified residual blocks where two convolutional
layers in one block share the same weights. A LadderNet has more paths for
information flow because of skip connections and residual blocks, and can be
viewed as an ensemble of Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN). 

The equivalence to
an ensemble of FCNs improves segmentation accuracy, while the shared weights
within each residual block reduce parameter number. Semantic segmentation is
essential for retinal disease detection. We tested LadderNet on two benchmark
datasets for blood vessel segmentation in retinal images, and achieved superior
performance over methods in the literature. 

The implementation is provided
\url{https://github.com/juntang-zhuang/LadderNet}

Wе соnsіdеr thе wаvе еquаtіоn оn а mаnіfоld $(\Оmеgа,g)$ оf dіmеnsіоn $d\gеq
2$ wіth smооth strісtlу соnvех bоundаrу $\раrtіаl\Оmеgа\nеq\еmрtуsеt$, wіth
Dіrісhlеt bоundаrу соndіtіоns. Wе соnstruсt а shаrр lосаl іn tіmе раrаmеtrіх
аnd thеn рrосееd tо оbtаіn dіsреrsіоn еstіmаtеs: оur fіхеd tіmе dесау rаtе fоr
thе Grееn funсtіоn ехhіbіts а $t^{1/4}$ lоss wіth rеsресt tо thе bоundаrу lеss
саsе. Wе рrесіsеlу dеsсrіbе whеrе аnd whеn thеsе lоssеs оссur аnd rеlаtе thеm
tо swаllоwtаіl tуре sіngulаrіtіеs іn thе wаvе frоnt sеt, рrоvіng thаt оur dесау
іs орtіmаl. Μоrеоvеr, wе dеrіvе bеttеr thаn ехресtеd Strісhаrtz еstіmаtеs,
bаlаnсіng lоssу lоng tіmе еstіmаtеs аt а gіvеn іnсіdеnсе wіth shоrt tіmе оnеs
wіth nо lоss: fоr $d=3$, іt hеurіstісаllу mеаns thаt, оn аvеrаgе thе dесау lоss
іs оnlу $t^{1/6}$.

In this paper, we propose an unsupervised cross-modality domain adaptation method for convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to improve the performance of biomedical image segmentations. our approach utilizes Adversarial loss to learn a mapping between the source and target domains, allowing the CNN to adapt to the new domain without requiring any labeled Data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two different biomedical Image segmentation tasks, and show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and generalization ability. Our work contributes to the development of more robust and transferable CNNs for biomedical image analysis.

Many diffusion MRI studies, including the Human Connectome Program (HCP),
obtain data using multishell (e.g., WU-Minn consortium) and diffusion spectrum
imaging (DSI) schemes (e.g., USC-Harvard consortium). Unfortunately, these data sets
are not easily available to high angular resolution diffusion imaging (HARDI)
analysis techniques that are popular in connectomics research. Here we introduce a
scheme conversion method that takes multishell and DSI data into their
respective HARDI representations, thus enabling HARDI-based statistical
tools to make use of data acquired using non-HARDI methods. This approach
was implemented on both phantom and in-vivo human data sets by utilizing
multishell, DSI, and HARDI data simultaneously, and using the converted
HARDI, from non-HARDI methods, with the original HARDI database. Analysis on the
phantom indicates that the converted HARDI from DSI and multishell data strongly
predicts the original HARDI (similarity coefficient > 0.9). Our in-vivo analysis
demonstrates that the converted HARDI can be constructed by constrained spherical
deconvolution, and the fiber orientation distributions are inconsistent with
those from the original HARDI. We further demonstrate that our scheme conversion
technique can be applied to HCP data, and the converted HARDI do not tend to
sacrifice angular resolution. However this novel method can benefit all
HARDI-based research methods, providing better analytical accessibility to
non-HARDI databases, excluding data from the HCP.

The phenomenon of Anderson localization of waves in elastic systems is
studied. We analyze this phenomenon in two different set of systems: disordered
linear chains of harmonic oscillators and disordered Rods which oscillate with
torsional waves. The first set is analyzed numerically whereas the second One
is studied both Experimentally and theoretically. In particular, we discuss the
localization properties Of the waves as a function of the frequency. In doing
that we have used the inverse participation ratio, which is related to the
localization length. We find that the normal modes localize exponentially
according to Anderson theory. In the elastic systems, the localization length
decreases With frequency. This behavior is in contrast With what happens in
analogous quantum mechanical systems, for which the localization length grows
with energy. This difference is explained by means of the Properties of the re
ection coefficient of a single scatterer in each case.

The deformable image registration problem is a basic problem in medical image analysis, which aims to determine the corresponding deformable transformation of a moving image with respect to a fixed image. It has been widely used in many medical image analysis tasks, such as image-guided radiotherapy, image-guided surgery, and image-guided intervention. >  In  this  work, we propose an unsupervised segmentation method based on clustering and deep representation learning to automatically  extract regions of interest  (ROIs) in medical images.  We first use    a convolutional neural  network (CNN)  to learn a feature space  that can represent the image content well. Then, we apply  k-means algorithm to cluster the  features  into several groups according to their similarity. Finally, each group represents one ROI. Our  proposed approach does not require any  manual labeling  or supervision during training  process. It only needs some prior knowledge about the number of ROIs in  advance. Experiments are conducted on two public datasets including CT scans  and MR brain images. Results show that our  method achieves better performance than other state-of-the-art  methods without using any  labeled  data.

​W​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​ ​a​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​r​e​-​u​s​i​n​g​ ​a​d​v​e​r​s​a​r​i​a​l​ ​m​a​s​k​ ​d​i​s​c​r​i​m​i​n​a​t​o​r​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​e​s​t​-​t​i​m​e​ ​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​u​n​d​e​r​ ​d​i​s​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​ ​s​h​i​f​t​s​.​ ​O​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​i​s​ ​b​a​s​e​d​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​o​b​s​e​r​v​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​a​s​k​s​ ​l​e​a​r​n​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​s​c​r​i​m​i​n​a​t​o​r​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​e​ ​n​e​w​ ​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​d​a​t​a​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​i​e​r​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​r​e​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​a​t​i​v​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​d​i​s​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​.​ ​W​e​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​o​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​i​e​r​ ​o​n​ ​a​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​s​e​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​s​e​t​.​ ​W​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​o​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​c​l​a​s​s​i​f​i​e​r​ ​o​n​ ​a​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​s​e​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​s​e​t​ ​w​i​t​h​o​u​t​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​a​n​y​ ​l​a​b​e​l​e​d​ ​d​a​t​a​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​s​e​t​.​

In this paper, we  present  a novel method for enforcing mutual consistency of hard regions for semi-supervised  medical image segmentation. Our   approach utilizes  a fully-connected conditional  random field  (CRF) model that is trained  using a semi-supervised learning (SSL) technique.  We introduce a novel hard region consistency loss that  ensures that the CRF model produces consistent segmentations across all images in the  training set.  Our approach is evaluated on the ISBI 2017 segmentation challenge dataset  and demonstrates significant improvements over  the baseline method. We achieve  a dice similarity coefficient  (DSC) of 0.9143 and a Hausdorff distance (HD) of 3.43 pixels on the test  set, outperforming the baseline method by 0.0803 in DSC  and 2.41 pixels in HD. Our approach  also achieves comparable performance  to  fully-supervised segmentation  methods in the  literature.

In this paper, we study the  most basic   domination invariants in  graphs, in
which number 2 is intrinsic part of their definitions. We classify them upon
three criteria, two of which give the following previously  studied invariants:
the  weak $2$-domination number, $\gamma_{w2}(G)$, the $2$-domination number,
$\gamma_2(G)$, the $\{2\}$-domination number, $\gamma_{\{2\}}(G)$, the double
domination number, $\gamma_{\times 2}(G)$, the total $\{2\}$-domination number,
$\gamma_{t\{2\}}(G)$, and  the total double domination number, $\gamma_{t\times
2}(G)$, where $G$ is a graph in  which a corresponding invariant  is  well
defined. The third criterion yields rainbow versions  of the mentioned six
parameters, one of   which has already  been well studied, and three other  give
new interesting parameters. Together  with  a special, extensively studied Roman
domination, $\gamma_R(G)$, and two classical  parameters, the  domination  number,
$\gamma(G)$, and the  total domination number, $\gamma_t(G)$, we consider 13
domination invariants in graphs $G$. In the  main  result of the paper we present
sharp upper and  lower bounds   of each   of  the  invariants in terms   of every other
invariant, large majority of  which are new results proven in this paper. As a
consequence of the main theorem we obtain some complexity results for  the
studied invariants, in particular regarding  the existence of  approximation
algorithms  and inapproximability bounds.

We prove that every graph of maximum degree at most $d$ contains an irreversible 3-conversion set, i.e. a set of at most $8d$ vertices whose removal makes the graph 2-colorable. This improves a recent result of the first author, who proved the same result for graphs of maximum degree at most $d-1$.

This paper maps the national UK web presence on the basis of an analysis of
the .uk domain from 1996 to 2010. It reviews previous attempts to use web
archives to understand national web domains and describes the dataset. Next, it
presents an analysis of the .uk domain, including the overall number of links
in the archive and changes in the link density of different second-level
domains over time. We then explore changes over time within a particular
second-level domain, the academic subdomain .ac.uk, and compare linking
practises with variables, including institutional affiliation, league table
ranking, and geographic location. We do not detect institutional affiliation
affecting linking practises and find only partial evidence of league table
ranking affecting network centrality, but find a clear inverse relationship
between the density of links and the geographical distance between
universities. This echoes prior findings regarding offline academic activity,
which allows us to argue that real-world factors like geography continue to
shape academic relationships even in the Internet age. We conclude with
directions for future uses of web archive resources in this emerging area of
research.

This paper provides a new method for very accurate brain area of
interest (ROI) detection from dynamic susceptibility contrast (DSC) perfusion
magnetic resonance (CS) images of a human head with normal brain anatomy.
Such photographs produce challenges for automatic brain segmentation algorithms, and
as a consequence, poor perfusion ROI detection affects both statistical
measurements and visual analysis of perfusion data. In the proposed method
image segmentation is based on CUSUM filter usage that was designed to be
applicable to process DSC perfusion MR images. The result of segmentation is a
binary mask of brain ROI that is created through usage of brain boundary
location. Each point of the boundary between the cortex and surrounding cells
is detected as a change-point by CUSUM filter. Proposed adopted CUSUM filter
operates by accumulating the deviations between the observed and expected
intensities of image points at the moment of turning on a trajectory. Motion
velocity is formed by the iterative shift of movement path inside the
background area in order to reach brain area, and vice versa after boundary
crossing. Proposed segmentation method was implemented with Dice index
comparing obtained results to the reference standard. Manually marked brain
area pixels (reference standard), as well as visual inspection of detected
with CUSUM filter usage brain ROI, were provided by experienced physicians.
The findings showed that proposed technique is feasible to be applied for brain ROI
detection from DSC perfusion MR images of a human head with normal brain
anatomy and can, thus, be used in the DSC perfusion data study.

We studied a feature engineering algorithm at work in cluster algorithms that results in high throughput, even at a modest level of computational complexity and scale. We have provided our approach via code sharing. Using an interesting form of clustering known as Point-based kernel or kernel aggregation we show how to reduce the computational cost of clusters by clustering large number of points in a set (a kernel) in an efficient manner, with up to 70% improvement across multiple runs within 60 samples. The result has been previously reported under similar optimization issues but under much more stringent constraints while achieving comparable speedups between several different implementations.

This paper investigates sub-propositional fragments of modal logic, which are fragments of modal logic that do not include the full range of propositional operators. The authors examine the expressive power and computational complexity of various sub-propositional fragments, focusing on two well-known fragments: the basic fragment and the conjunctive fragment. They provide a comprehensive analysis of the expressivity and computational complexity of these fragments, highlighting their limitations and potential applications. The results obtained in this study contribute to the understanding of sub-propositional fragments of modal logic and provide valuable insights for further research in this area.

We propose a method for the construction of image segmentation models, based on Gaussian mixtures whose parameters are grouped in a vectorial representation through sparse regularization. This regularization enforces geometric constraints on the model, making it applicable to image segmentation tasks. Our method allows the constraints to be smoothly weakened, contrary to previous approaches that combine sparsity with mixture models. We illustrate the interest of this flexibility on a public image segmentation benchmark.

Deep neural networks have been a prevailing technique in the field of medical
image processing. However, the most popular convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) based methods for medical image segmentation are imperfect because they
model long-range dependencies by stacking layers or enlarging filters.
Transformers and the self-attention mechanism are recently proposed to
effectively learn long-range dependencies by modeling all pairs of word-to-word
attention regardless of their positions. The idea has also been extended to the
computer vision field by creating and treating image patches as embeddings.
Considering the computation complexity for whole image self-attention, current
transformer-based models settle for a rigid partitioning scheme that
potentially loses informative relations. Besides, current medical transformers
model global context on full resolution images, leading to unnecessary
computation costs. To address these issues, we developed a novel method to
integrate multi-scale attention and CNN feature extraction using a pyramidal
network architecture, namely Pyramid Medical Transformer (PMTrans). The PMTrans
captured multi-range relations by working on multi-resolution images. An
adaptive partitioning scheme was implemented to retain informative relations
and to access different receptive fields efficiently. Experimental results on
three medical image datasets (gland segmentation, MoNuSeg, and HECKTOR
datasets) showed that PMTrans outperformed the latest CNN-based and
transformer-based models for medical image segmentation.

We introduce Post-DAE, a post-processing method based on denoising
autoencoders (DAE) to improve the anatomical plausibility of arbitrary
biomedical image segmentation algorithms. Some of the most popular segmentation
methods (e.g. based on convolutional neural networks or random forest
classifiers) incorporate additional post-processing steps to ensure that the
resulting masks fulfil expected connectivity constraints. These methods
operate under the hypothesis that contiguous pixels with similar aspect should
belong to the same class. Even if valid in general, this assumption does not
consider more complex priors like topological restrictions or convexity, which
cannot be easily incorporated into these methods. Post-DAE leverages the latest
developments in manifold learning via denoising autoencoders. First, we learn a
compact and non-linear embedding that represents the space of anatomically
plausible segmentations. Then, given a segmentation mask obtained with an
arbitrary method, we reconstruct its anatomically plausible version by
projecting it onto the learnt manifold. The proposed method is trained using
unpaired segmentation mask, what makes it independent of intensity information
and image modality. We performed experiments in binary and multi-label
segmentation of chest X-ray and cardiac magnetic resonance images. We show how
erroneous and noisy segmentation masks can be improved using Post-DAE. With
almost no additional computation cost, our method brings erroneous
segmentations back to a feasible space.

In this paper, we present an innovative approach to improve the segmentation of the left atrium in cardiac MRIs by integrating global shape priors and local intensity priors. The method combines the mixture modeling of global shape priors, which exploits a database of atlas, and an autoencoder-based local intensity prior to accommodate intensity variations across different acquisitions. Experiments were conducted on a dataset of 200 MRIs from various clinical centers, with results demonstrating significant improvements in both accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. This integrated approach presents promising potential towards the efficient and reliable segmentation of the left atrium, thereby advancing cardiac MRI analysis and diagnosis.
